topics.title: Structure of Atom - Present Concept of Atom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structure of Atom - Present Concept of Atom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The *present concept of the atom* refers to the **wave-mechanical (quantum mechanical) model** of the atom, in which electrons are not considered as particles moving in fixed orbits, but rather as **matter waves** whose probable locations are described by a mathematical function called the **wave function (Ψ)**. The square of its magnitude, |Ψ|², gives the probability density of finding an electron in a given region of space around the nucleus.

This model successfully unifies:
- **Wave–particle duality** of the electron (de Broglie, 1924)
- **Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle** (1927)
- **Schrödinger's Wave Equation** (1926)
- **Pauli's Exclusion Principle** (1925)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cloud Around a Light Bulb"

Imagine a tiny glowing light bulb (the nucleus) inside a foggy room. You cannot see the bulb itself, but you can see the faint, glowing fog (electron cloud) surrounding it. The fog is **denser in some places** and **thinner in others**.

- The **denser regions of fog** = places where the electron is *more likely* to be found.
- The **sparser regions** = places where the electron is *less likely* to be found.
- You will **never** find a sharp boundary where the electron "ends" — it gradually fades away, just like the fog.

This is exactly what the modern atom looks like: a **diffuse electron cloud** whose density is governed by quantum mechanics.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Used For |
|----------|--------|-------|----------|
| Planck's constant | **h** | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Quantization of energy |
| Reduced Planck's constant | **ℏ (h-bar)** | **1.054 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Angular momentum in QM |
| Speed of light | **c** | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** | Photon equations |
| Electron rest mass | **mₑ** | **9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg** | Kinetic energy, momentum |
| Elementary charge | **e** | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** | Coulombic forces |
| Boltzmann constant | **k_B** | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Thermal energy |
| Bohr radius | **a₀** | **5.292 × 10⁻¹¹ m** | Atomic length scale |
| Avogadro's number | **N_A** | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** | Moles to atoms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PECHT412 syllabus expects students to clearly differentiate between the Bohr model (semi-classical, outdated for bonding) and the wave-mechanical model (modern, used for all band-structure and bonding calculations in Materials Science).

## 1.4 Why is the "Present Concept of Atom" Important for a Materials Engineer?

Every property you will study in this entire course — **strength, conductivity, magnetism, optical behaviour, corrosion resistance** — traces back to:
1. The **electronic configuration** of the atoms involved.
2. The **spatial distribution** of those electrons (orbitals).
3. The **energy spacing** between allowed electron states.

Without the modern atomic model, you cannot understand **why copper conducts, why ceramics insulate, why steel is magnetic, or why silicon is a semiconductor**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Atomic Models — A Logical Progression

To truly understand the *present* concept, you must understand the *problems* each earlier model failed to solve. This logical chain is a **favourite KTU question** (frequently asked for 7–14 marks).

### Step 1 — Dalton's Billiard Ball Model (1803)
- Atom = indivisible, solid sphere.
- **Limitation:** Could not explain electrical, magnetic, or chemical bonding phenomena.

### Step 2 — Thomson's "Plum Pudding" Model (1904)
- Atom = positively charged sphere with negatively charged electrons embedded like plums.
- **Limitation:** Failed to explain the **alpha-particle scattering** results.

### Step 3 — Rutherford's Nuclear Model (1911)
- Based on the **gold foil experiment** by Geiger and Marsden.
- Atom has a tiny, dense, positively charged **nucleus**; electrons revolve around it.
- **Limitation 1:** Could not explain the *stability* of atoms (accelerating charges should radiate energy and spiral into the nucleus).
- **Limitation 2:** Could not explain the *discrete line spectra* of hydrogen.

### Step 4 — Bohr's Model (1913) — The Bridge
- Introduced **quantization of angular momentum**.
- Successfully explained the hydrogen line spectrum.
- **Limitation 1:** Failed for multi-electron atoms (He, Li, …).
- **Limitation 2:** Treated electrons as particles (ignored wave nature).
- **Limitation 3:** Violated the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle.

### Step 5 — Sommerfeld's Elliptical Orbits (1916)
- Extended Bohr by allowing **elliptical orbits** with two quantum numbers (n, l).
- Still a semi-classical model — ultimately failed for fine spectral details.

### Step 6 — de Broglie's Matter Waves (1924)
- Proposed that every moving particle has a wavelength:

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{mv} = \frac{h}{p}
$$

- This was **experimentally confirmed** by Davisson and Germer (electron diffraction, 1927).

### Step 7 — Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle (1927)

$$
\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}
$$

- It is **fundamentally impossible** to know both the exact position and exact momentum of an electron simultaneously.
- **Consequence:** The concept of a "well-defined orbit" is *physically meaningless*.

### Step 8 — Schrödinger's Wave Equation (1926) — The Present Model

$$
-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2 \Psi + V\Psi = E\Psi
$$

- **Ψ** = wave function (no direct physical meaning).
- **|Ψ|²** = probability density of finding the electron.
- Solutions yield three quantum numbers (n, l, mₗ) automatically.
- Forms the foundation of **all modern materials science** (band theory, bonding, spectroscopy).

## 2.2 Bohr's Model — Detailed Working (for KTU Numerical Problems)

Although outdated conceptually, Bohr's model is **still examinable** in KTU for hydrogen-like numerical problems.

### Bohr's Postulates
1. Electrons revolve in **stationary orbits** without radiating energy.
2. Angular momentum is **quantized**:

$$
L = m_e v r = n \frac{h}{2\pi} = n\hbar, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

3. Electrons can **jump** between orbits by absorbing or emitting a photon of energy:

$$
h\nu = E_i - E_f
$$

### Radius of the nᵗʰ Orbit

$$
r_n = \frac{n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi m_e e^2 Z} = \frac{n^2 a_0}{Z}
$$

where **a₀ = 0.529 Å** is the Bohr radius and **Z** is the atomic number.

### Energy of the nᵗʰ Orbit

$$
E_n = -\frac{m_e e^4 Z^2}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2} = -13.6 \, \frac{Z^2}{n^2} \, \text{eV}
$$

- **Negative sign** → electron is *bound* to the nucleus.
- **E → 0** as **n → ∞** → ionization.
- Energy levels get **closer together** as n increases.

### Wavelength of Emitted Photon (Rydberg Formula)

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H Z^2 \left( \frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2} \right)
$$

where **R_H = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹** is the Rydberg constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series Classification (Hydrogen):**
> - **Lyman:** n_f = 1 (UV)
> - **Balmer:** n_f = 2 (Visible)
> - **Paschen:** n_f = 3 (IR)
> - **Brackett:** n_f = 4 (IR)
> - **Pfund:** n_f = 5 (IR)

## 2.3 The Four Quantum Numbers — The Heart of the Modern Atom

The modern atomic model is fully described by **four quantum numbers**, each arising from the mathematical solution of Schrödinger's equation (the fourth, s, arises from relativity + spin).

| Symbol | Name | Allowed Values | Physical Meaning | Shell / Subshell |
|--------|------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| **n** | Principal | 1, 2, 3, … | Size / energy of orbital | Shell (K, L, M, N) |
| **l** | Azimuthal | 0, 1, …, (n−1) | Shape of orbital | s, p, d, f |
| **mₗ** | Magnetic | −l, …, 0, …, +l | Orientation in space | Orbital |
| **mₛ** | Spin | +½, −½ | Intrinsic spin of electron | Spin state |

### Subshell Letter Codes

| l value | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Letter | s | p | d | f | g |

### Subshell Capacity Formula

$$
\text{Maximum electrons in a subshell} = 2(2l + 1)
$$

- s → 2 electrons
- p → 6 electrons
- d → 10 electrons
- f → 14 electrons

### Shell Capacity Formula

$$
\text{Maximum electrons in shell n} = 2n^2
$$

## 2.4 The Three Building Rules for Electronic Configuration

> [!NOTE]
> These three rules govern how electrons fill orbitals in any atom. They are **direct, high-frequency KTU exam questions**.

### Rule 1 — Aufbau Principle ("Building Up")
- Lower-energy orbitals fill **first**.
- Standard filling order: **1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 4s → 3d → 4p → 5s → 4d → 5p → 6s → 4f → 5d → 6p → 7s …**
- (n + l) rule: lower (n + l) fills first; if equal, lower n fills first.

### Rule 2 — Pauli's Exclusion Principle (1925)
- **No two electrons in an atom can have all four quantum numbers identical.**
- Therefore each orbital holds a **maximum of 2 electrons** with *opposite* spins.

### Rule 3 — Hund's Rule of Maximum Multiplicity
- Within a subshell, electrons fill each orbital **singly with parallel spins** *before* pairing up.
- This minimizes electron–electron repulsion and gives a lower energy.

## 2.5 Shapes of Orbitals — The Visual Vocabulary of the Modern Atom

- **s-orbital:** Spherical. The 1s is a simple sphere; 2s has a radial node; 3s has two, etc.
- **p-orbital:** Dumbbell-shaped. Three orientations: pₓ, p_y, p_z.
- **d-orbital:** Cloverleaf (four lobes) or dumbbell-with-ring. Five orientations.
- **f-orbital:** Highly complex, seven orientations.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\lambda = h/mv$ | de Broglie wavelength | Wave–particle duality |
| 2 | $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$ | Heisenberg uncertainty | Shows orbit concept is invalid |
| 3 | $E_n = -13.6 \cdot Z^2/n^2$ eV | Bohr energy level | Hydrogen spectral problems |
| 4 | $r_n = n^2 a_0 / Z$ | Bohr radius | Size of hydrogenic orbits |
| 5 | $1/\lambda = R_H Z^2 (1/n_f^2 - 1/n_i^2)$ | Rydberg formula | Spectral line wavelength |
| 6 | $L = n\hbar$ | Quantized angular momentum | Bohr model |
| 7 | $-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2 \Psi + V\Psi = E\Psi$ | Schrödinger equation | Modern atomic model |
| 8 | $2(2l+1)$ | Subshell e⁻ capacity | Electronic configuration |
| 9 | $2n^2$ | Shell e⁻ capacity | Period-wise periodicity |
| 10 | $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Photon energy | Photoelectric, spectroscopy |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor industry** (doping Si, Ge): needs precise knowledge of valence-shell configuration (3s²3p² for Si).
- **Steel metallurgy**: 3d electrons of Fe, Cr, Mn, Ni dictate magnetic and mechanical properties.
- **Optical fibres**: rare-earth dopants use f-orbital transitions.
- **Solar cells**: band-gap engineering depends on allowed/forbidden energy levels of atoms.
- **Corrosion engineering**: passive-film formation depends on outer-electron behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Radius of the nᵗʰ Bohr Orbit

We equate the **centripetal force** required for circular motion to the **Coulomb electrostatic attraction** between the nucleus (+Ze) and the electron (−e).

**Step 1: Coulomb attractive force between nucleus and electron.**

$$
F = \frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e \cdot e}{r_n^2} = \frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n^2}
$$

**Step 2: Centripetal force required for circular motion.**

$$
F = \frac{m_e v^2}{r_n}
$$

**Step 3: Equate the two forces.**

$$
\frac{m_e v^2}{r_n} = \frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n^2}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Bohr's quantization of angular momentum.**

$$
m_e v r_n = n \frac{h}{2\pi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{n h}{2\pi m_e r_n}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute v into the force balance.**

$$
\frac{m_e}{r_n} \left( \frac{n h}{2\pi m_e r_n} \right)^2 = \frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n^2}
$$

**Step 6: Simplify the left-hand side.**

$$
\frac{n^2 h^2}{4\pi^2 m_e r_n^3} = \frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n^2}
$$

**Step 7: Solve for r_n.**

$$
r_n = \frac{n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi m_e Z e^2} = \frac{n^2 a_0}{Z}
$$

where $a_0 = \dfrac{h^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi m_e e^2} = 0.529 \, \text{Å}$ is the Bohr radius.

> **Result:** $r_n \propto n^2$ → orbits get rapidly farther apart. The first orbit (n=1) for hydrogen (Z=1) is exactly $a_0 = 0.529$ Å.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy of the nᵗʰ Bohr Orbit

The total energy is the sum of kinetic energy (KE) and potential energy (PE).

**Step 1: Kinetic energy of the electron.**

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2
$$

From force balance (Section 3.1, Step 3):

$$
\frac{m_e v^2}{r_n} = \frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_e v^2 = \frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n} = \frac{Z e^2}{8\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n}
$$

**Step 2: Potential energy of the electron (Coulomb).**

$$
E_p = -\frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n}
$$

(Negative because force is attractive — energy is required to remove the electron.)

**Step 3: Total energy.**

$$
E_n = E_k + E_p = \frac{Z e^2}{8\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n} - \frac{Z e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n} = -\frac{Z e^2}{8\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute r_n from Section 3.1.**

$$
E_n = -\frac{Z e^2}{8\pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\pi m_e Z e^2}{n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0} = -\frac{m_e Z^2 e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to the familiar form using numerical constants.**

$$
E_n = -13.6 \, \frac{Z^2}{n^2} \, \text{eV}
$$

> **Key Observation:** $E_n \propto -1/n^2$. As n increases, energy becomes **less negative** (less bound) and levels crowd closer together.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — de Broglie Wavelength & Bohr's Quantization

**Step 1: Start with de Broglie's hypothesis (any moving particle has a wavelength).**

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{m_e v}
$$

**Step 2: For a stable standing wave on a circular orbit, an integer number of wavelengths must fit exactly around the circumference (otherwise the wave interferes destructively with itself).**

$$
2\pi r_n = n \lambda, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

**Step 3: Substitute λ.**

$$
2\pi r_n = \frac{n h}{m_e v}
$$

**Step 4: Rearrange.**

$$
m_e v r_n = \frac{n h}{2\pi} = n\hbar
$$

> This is **exactly** Bohr's angular-momentum quantization postulate! The deep reason Bohr's postulate works: **stable orbits are standing electron waves.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rydberg Formula from Bohr's Energy

**Step 1: Energy of photon emitted when an electron jumps from n_i to n_f.**

$$
h\nu = E_{n_i} - E_{n_f} = -13.6 Z^2 \left( \frac{1}{n_i^2} - \frac{1}{n_f^2} \right) \text{ eV}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $\nu = c/\lambda$ and $\text{eV} \to \text{Joules}$ (factor = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$).**

$$
\frac{hc}{\lambda} = 13.6 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, Z^2 \left( \frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2} \right) \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for 1/λ.**

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{13.6 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{h c} \, Z^2 \left( \frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2} \right)
$$

**Step 4: The constant evaluates to the Rydberg constant.**

$$
R_H = \frac{13.6 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{h c} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5: Final Rydberg formula.**

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H Z^2 \left( \frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2} \right)
$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Balmer-α Line of Hydrogen

**Problem:** Find the wavelength of the first line of the Balmer series (n_i = 3, n_f = 2) for hydrogen (Z = 1).

**Step 1: Apply Rydberg formula.**

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = (1.097 \times 10^7) \cdot 1^2 \left( \frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{3^2} \right)
$$

**Step 2: Compute bracket.**

$$
\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9} = \frac{9 - 4}{36} = \frac{5}{36} = 0.1389
$$

**Step 3: Multiply.**

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \times 0.1389 = 1.524 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Take reciprocal.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{1.524 \times 10^6} = 6.563 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} = 656.3 \, \text{nm}
$$

> This is the famous **Hα red line** of hydrogen — verified experimentally by Balmer in 1885.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — de Broglie Wavelength of an Electron

**Problem:** An electron is accelerated through 100 V. Find its de Broglie wavelength.

**Step 1: Kinetic energy gained.**

$$
E_k = eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot 100 = 1.602 \times 10^{-17} \, \text{J}
$$

**Step 2: Use $E_k = p^2/(2m_e)$ to find momentum p.**

$$
p = \sqrt{2 m_e E_k} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-17}}
$$

$$
p = \sqrt{2.918 \times 10^{-47}} = 5.402 \times 10^{-24} \, \text{kg·m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Apply de Broglie's relation.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{5.402 \times 10^{-24}} = 1.227 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m} = 0.1227 \, \text{nm}
$$

> This is comparable to atomic spacings — the reason **electron microscopes** can resolve atoms!

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example — Building Electronic Configuration of Iron (Z = 26)

**Apply the (n + l) Aufbau rule:**

| Step | Orbital | (n + l) | Electrons filled | Running total |
|------|---------|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | 1s | 1+0 = 1 | 2 | 2 |
| 2 | 2s | 2+0 = 2 | 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 2p | 2+1 = 3 | 6 | 10 |
| 4 | 3s | 3+0 = 3 | 2 | 12 |
| 5 | 3p | 3+1 = 4 | 6 | 18 |
| 6 | 4s | 4+0 = 4 | 2 | 20 |
| 7 | 3d | 3+2 = 5 | 6 (need 8 more to reach 26) → wait, 3d holds 10; we place 6 here | 26 |

**Resulting configuration:** $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2 3d^6$

**Applying Hund's rule inside 3d:** 5 electrons go one-per-orbital with parallel spin; the 6th pairs up. The 4s electrons are the *outermost* (highest principal quantum number) and are the **valence** electrons of iron — critical for understanding iron's chemistry, metallurgy, and magnetism.

> **Materials-Science Insight:** Iron's 3d⁶ configuration is the reason it is **ferromagnetic** — four unpaired d-electrons create a strong net magnetic moment, the basis of all steel magnets.

    E3 --> F3["ml ranges from minus 3 to plus 3 - seven orbitals"]
    F0 --> G0["Each orbital holds 2 electrons ms equal to plus half and minus half"]
    F1 --> G1["Each orbital holds 2 electrons ms equal to plus half and minus half"]
    F2 --> G2["Each orbital holds 2 electrons ms equal to plus half and minus half"]
    F3 --> G3["Each orbital holds 2 electrons ms equal to plus half and minus half"]
```

```

> **Reading Aid:** Follow the arrows. The irregular jumps (4s before 3d, 6s before 4f) reflect the **Aufbau (n + l) rule** for energy ordering.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle. Why does it rule out the Bohr model?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle states that it is **fundamentally impossible to simultaneously determine the exact position and exact momentum of a microscopic particle** with arbitrary precision. Mathematically:

$$
\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}
$$

where $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s. For an electron in a Bohr orbit, if we try to define its exact position (small $\Delta x$), then $\Delta p$ becomes enormous — the electron's velocity becomes completely uncertain. The Bohr model demands a *simultaneous, exact* knowledge of both position and momentum, which is physically forbidden. Hence Bohr's well-defined orbit is meaningless in the quantum world. The modern model replaces "orbit" with "orbital" — a probability cloud described by |Ψ|². (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Write the four quantum numbers and their physical significance.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Quantum Number | Symbol | Allowed Values | Significance |
|----------------|--------|----------------|--------------|
| Principal | n | 1, 2, 3, … | Size/energy of the shell |
| Azimuthal | l | 0 to n−1 | Shape of the orbital (s, p, d, f) |
| Magnetic | mₗ | −l to +l | Orientation of the orbital in space |
| Spin | mₛ | +½, −½ | Direction of intrinsic spin |

Together they specify the unique quantum state of every electron in an atom, consistent with Pauli's exclusion principle. (3 marks)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) Derive an expression for the radius of the nᵗʰ Bohr orbit and the energy of the electron in that orbit.** (7 marks)
**(b) Using the Rydberg formula, calculate the wavelength of the second line of the Lyman series for hydrogen. Which region of the electromagnetic spectrum does it lie in?** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):
1. Write Coulomb force: $F = Ze^2/(4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n^2)$
2. Equate to centripetal force: $m_e v^2/r_n = Ze^2/(4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_n^2)$
3. Apply Bohr quantization: $m_e v r_n = nh/(2\pi)$
4. Eliminate v: $v = nh/(2\pi m_e r_n)$
5. Substitute and simplify to obtain: $r_n = n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0/(\pi m_e Z e^2) = n^2 a_0/Z$
6. Compute total energy: $E_n = E_k + E_p$
7. Obtain: $E_n = -m_e Z^2 e^4/(8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2) \cdot (1/n^2) = -13.6 Z^2/n^2$ eV
8. Conclude that $r_n \propto n^2$ and $E_n \propto -1/n^2$.

#### Model Solution to (b):
1. Lyman series: n_f = 1. Second line: n_i = 3.
2. Use Rydberg formula: $1/\lambda = R_H \cdot (1/1^2 - 1/3^2)$
3. Compute: $1/\lambda = 1.097 \times 10^7 \cdot (1 - 1/9) = 1.097 \times 10^7 \cdot 8/9$
4. $1/\lambda = 9.751 \times 10^6$ m⁻¹
5. $\lambda = 1.026 \times 10^{-7}$ m = 102.6 nm
6. This lies in the **ultraviolet (UV) region** of the EM spectrum.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) State Pauli's exclusion principle and Hund's rule. Explain their role in building the electronic configuration of oxygen (Z = 8).** (7 marks)
**(b) Explain the wave-mechanical model of the atom. How does it overcome the limitations of the Bohr model?** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):
1. **Pauli's principle:** No two electrons in an atom can have identical values of all four quantum numbers. Consequence: each orbital holds at most 2 electrons with opposite spins.
2. **Hund's rule:** Within a subshell, electrons occupy empty orbitals singly with parallel spins before pairing.
3. Build oxygen (Z = 8) step by step using the (n + l) rule:
   - 1s² (n+l=1) — both paired
   - 2s² (n+l=2) — both paired
   - 2p⁴ (n+l=3) — first 3 electrons go singly into 2pₓ, 2p_y, 2p_z (Hund); the 4th pairs up in 2pₓ.
4. Final configuration: $1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^4$.
5. Note: oxygen has **2 unpaired electrons** in 2p, making it **paramagnetic** — an experimentally verified prediction.

#### Model Solution to (b):
1. In the wave-mechanical model, the electron is treated as a **standing matter wave** around the nucleus.
2. Schrödinger's equation, $-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2 \Psi + V\Psi = E\Psi$, is solved for the potential V = −Ze²/(4πε₀r).
3. Solutions $\Psi_{n,l,m_l}$ (wave functions) exist only for specific values of n, l, mₗ — these are the **quantum numbers**, emerging naturally from the math (not imposed by hand as in Bohr).
4. $|\Psi|^2$ gives the **probability density** — the electron is described as a diffuse cloud, not a particle on a track.
5. Heisenberg's uncertainty is built into the model, not violated by it.
6. **Limitations of Bohr that are overcome:**
   - Bohr allowed only circular orbits; wave mechanics allows s, p, d, f shapes.
   - Bohr could not explain multi-electron atoms; wave mechanics handles them via shielding and effective nuclear charge.
   - Bohr violated uncertainty; wave mechanics honours it.
   - Bohr gave no explanation of fine structure; wave mechanics naturally predicts splitting via spin–orbit coupling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in Bohr energy:** Students often write $E_n = +13.6 Z^2/n^2$ eV. The energy of a **bound** electron is **negative**. Losing this sign = full marks cut on energy derivations.
> 2. **Confusing orbit and orbital:** Orbit = Bohr's classical path (now obsolete). Orbital = quantum-mechanical probability region. Examiners actively test this distinction.
> 3. **Miscounting d-electrons in transition metals:** Many students write Fe as [Ar] 3d⁸ instead of [Ar] 4s² 3d⁶. The 4s fills *before* 3d but is written *after* 3d in the standard configuration — memorise this convention.
> 4. **Skipping Hund's rule for Cr and Cu:** Chromium is actually [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵ and Copper is [Ar] 4s¹ 3d¹⁰ due to half-filled and fully-filled d-subshell extra stability. Examiners love to test this anomaly.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the units** in numerical answers (eV for energy, m or nm for wavelength, Å for radius) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **present concept of the atom** = **wave-mechanical model** based on Schrödinger's equation.
- ✅ **Bohr model** is semi-classical — important historically and for hydrogen numerics, but **not** the modern view.
- ✅ **Four quantum numbers:** n, l, mₗ, mₛ. They uniquely define an electron's state.
- ✅ **Shell capacity** = $2n^2$. **Subshell capacity** = $2(2l+1)$.
- ✅ **Three rules for filling:** Aufbau (n+l), Pauli (no two alike), Hund (maximize multiplicity).
- ✅ **Bohr radius** $a_0 = 0.529$ Å. **Hydrogen ground state** $E_1 = -13.6$ eV.
- ✅ **Rydberg formula:** $1/\lambda = R_H Z^2 (1/n_f^2 - 1/n_i^2)$.
- ✅ **de Broglie wavelength** $\lambda = h/mv$ — explains why Bohr quantization works (standing waves).
- ✅ **Heisenberg principle** forbids the very concept of a precise orbit.
- ✅ **Anomalous configurations:** Cr is [Ar] 4s¹3d⁵, Cu is [Ar] 4s¹3d¹⁰ (stability of half/fully-filled d-shells).
- ✅ **Materials science link:** every property (mechanical, electrical, magnetic, optical) traces back to **electronic configuration** and **orbital shape** — the modern atomic model is the foundation of the entire course.
- ✅ **Constants to memorise:** h, ℏ, c, mₑ, e, a₀, R_H.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rutherford's and Bohr's Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rutherford's and Bohr's Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Rutherford's Atomic Model (Nuclear Model of the Atom)

**Formal Definition:**
Rutherford's atomic model, proposed by **Ernest Rutherford in 1911** based on his famous **gold foil experiment (Geiger-Marsden experiment, 1909)**, describes the atom as consisting of a tiny, dense, positively charged **nucleus** at the center (containing nearly all the mass of the atom), with the negatively charged **electrons** orbiting around it in well-defined circular paths, much like planets orbit the sun. The rest of the atom is essentially **empty space**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Rutherford's model laid the foundation for the modern understanding of atomic structure and is the precursor to the Bohr model. It is *crucial* for understanding scattering phenomena, inter-atomic spacing, and the crystalline nature of solids in material science.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **cricket stadium** at night. The stadium is the atom. The **wicket at the center** is the nucleus. A few **gnats buzzing around the stadium** are the electrons. The vast empty space of the stands represents the empty space in the atom. When Rutherford fired alpha particles at a thin gold foil, most passed straight through (empty space), but a few bounced back sharply — proving that something hard and dense (the nucleus) was hiding in the center.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Experimental Observation:** Out of ~8000 alpha particles, ~98% passed straight through, ~2% were deflected by small angles, and only a tiny fraction (~1 in 20000) bounced back by more than 90°.

---

### 1.2 Bohr's Atomic Model (Quantized Orbit Model)

**Formal Definition:**
Bohr's atomic model, proposed by **Niels Bohr in 1913**, refined Rutherford's model by applying **Max Planck's quantum theory**. Bohr postulated that electrons revolve around the nucleus in **stationary orbits** (also called **permitted orbits** or **discrete energy levels**) without radiating energy. Energy is absorbed or emitted only when an electron **jumps** from one stationary orbit to another, in the form of **discrete quanta (photons)** of frequency $\nu$, given by $E = h\nu$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bohr's model successfully explains the **hydrogen spectrum** (Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, Brackett, Pfund series) and introduces the **principal quantum number $n$**. It is the bridge between classical physics and quantum mechanics — vital for understanding electronic properties of materials, band theory, and X-ray spectra.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **multi-storey building (the atom)**. The ground floor is $n=1$, first floor is $n=2$, second floor is $n=3$, and so on. An electron (a person) can stand only on these floors — never between them. To go up a floor, it must absorb a precise packet of energy (take the stairs); to come down, it must release that exact energy (jump down). It cannot hover mid-air between floors. This is **quantization of energy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bohr's Three Postulates (Quantization Rules):**
> 1. **Stationary Orbits:** Electrons revolve in stable, non-radiating orbits where angular momentum is quantized: $mvr = \frac{nh}{2\pi}$.
> 2. **Frequency Condition:** Radiation is emitted/absorbed only during transitions: $h\nu = E_i - E_f$.
> 3. **Classical Dynamics Within Orbits:** In stationary orbits, classical mechanics holds (Coulomb's law + Newton's laws).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rutherford's Model — Detailed Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Postulates of Rutherford:**
- The entire positive charge and almost the entire mass of the atom are concentrated in a tiny central core called the **nucleus**.
- The **electrons** revolve around the nucleus in circular orbits.
- The **electrons are not stationary**; they are in motion like planets.
- The atom is largely **empty space** (~99.99% of the volume).
- The stability of the atom is explained by the **balance between Coulomb attraction and centrifugal force**.

**Step 2 — Force Balance Equation (Why the electron doesn't fall in):**
The electrostatic (Coulomb) force of attraction between the nucleus and the electron provides the necessary **centripetal force** for circular motion.

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Ze \cdot e}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Why Rutherford's Model Fails (Limitations):**
- According to **classical electrodynamics (Maxwell's theory)**, an accelerating charged particle must radiate energy continuously. So the orbiting electron should spiral into the nucleus in $\sim 10^{-8}$ s — atoms should be unstable. But they are not!
- It cannot explain the **discrete line spectra** of hydrogen.
- It cannot explain the **fine structure** of spectral lines.
- It cannot explain the **chemical behavior** of elements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Rutherford's scattering formula $N(\theta) \propto \frac{1}{\sin^4(\theta/2)}$ is used today in **Rutherford Backscattering Spectrometry (RBS)** — a powerful analytical technique in materials science for determining the composition and thickness of thin films, used heavily in semiconductor fabrication and surface engineering.

---

### 2.2 Bohr's Model — Detailed Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Bohr's Quantization Condition:**
The angular momentum of the electron in the nth orbit is an **integer multiple** of $\hbar = \frac{h}{2\pi}$:

$$L = mvr = \frac{nh}{2\pi} = n\hbar \quad ; \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 2 — Force Balance (Same as Rutherford):**
$$\frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Ze^2}{r_n^2} = \frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}$$

**Step 3 — Combining Quantization + Force Balance:**
Substituting $v_n = \frac{nh}{2\pi m r_n}$ into the force equation yields the **radius of the nth Bohr orbit**.

**Step 4 — Energy Quantization:**
The total energy is the sum of kinetic energy (positive) and potential energy (negative). Bohr's quantization automatically leads to **discrete negative energy levels**, with the **ground state ($n=1$)** being the most stable.

**Step 5 — Frequency of Emitted Photon (Bohr's Frequency Rule):**
$$h\nu = E_{n_2} - E_{n_1} = -13.6 \cdot \frac{Z^2}{n_2^2} - \left(-13.6 \cdot \frac{Z^2}{n_1^2}\right)$$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Bohr's angular momentum quantization | $L = mvr = \frac{nh}{2\pi}$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ |
| 2 | Radius of nth orbit | $r_n = \frac{n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}{\pi m e^2 Z} = \frac{n^2 a_0}{Z}$ | $a_0 = 0.529 \text{ Å} = 0.529 \times 10^{-10}$ m |
| 3 | Velocity of electron in nth orbit | $v_n = \frac{Z e^2}{2 \epsilon_0 h n} = \frac{Z \alpha c}{n}$ | $v_1 \approx 2.188 \times 10^{6}$ m/s (for H) |
| 4 | Kinetic energy in nth orbit | $K_n = +\frac{Z^2 e^4 m}{8 \epsilon_0^2 h^2 n^2}$ | $K_1 = +13.6$ eV (H) |
| 5 | Potential energy in nth orbit | $U_n = -\frac{Z^2 e^4 m}{4 \epsilon_0^2 h^2 n^2}$ | $U_1 = -27.2$ eV (H) |
| 6 | Total energy in nth orbit | $E_n = -\frac{Z^2 e^4 m}{8 \epsilon_0^2 h^2 n^2} = -13.6 \frac{Z^2}{n^2}$ eV | $E_1 = -13.6$ eV (H ground state) |
| 7 | Frequency of emitted photon | $\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = R c Z^2 \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$ | $R = 1.097 \times 10^{7}$ m$^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Rydberg constant | $R = \frac{m e^4}{8 \epsilon_0^2 h^3 c}$ | $R = 1.097 \times 10^{7}$ m$^{-1}$ |
| 9 | Wavelength of emitted photon (Rydberg formula) | $\frac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2 \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$ | $\lambda$ in meters |
| 10 | Distance of closest approach (Rutherford) | $d = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Ze \cdot 2e}{E_k} = \frac{Ze^2 \cdot 2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 E_k}$ | $d$ in meters |
| 11 | Impact parameter (Rutherford) | $b = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Ze \cdot 2e}{E_k} \cdot \cot\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | $b$ in meters |
| 12 | Bohr magneton | $\mu_B = \frac{eh}{4\pi m_e}$ | $\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ J/T |
| 13 | Wave number | $\bar{\nu} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | units: m$^{-1}$ or cm$^{-1}$ |

**Important Physical Constants to Memorize:**
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- Electron mass: $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- Electron charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- Permittivity of free space: $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- Ionization energy of hydrogen: $13.6$ eV
- Bohr radius: $a_0 = 0.529$ Å

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** For numerical problems, the result $E_n = -13.6 \frac{Z^2}{n^2}$ eV is a **must-memorize** shortcut. Combined with $h\nu = E_{n_2} - E_{n_1}$, you can solve ~80% of the numerical questions in Module 1.

---

### 2.4 Hydrogen Spectral Series (Memorize This!)

| Series | Transition Region | $n_1$ (lower) | $n_2$ (upper) | Spectral Region |
|--------|-------------------|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| Lyman | $n_2 \to 1$ | 1 | 2, 3, 4, ... | Ultraviolet (UV) |
| Balmer | $n_2 \to 2$ | 2 | 3, 4, 5, ... | Visible |
| Paschen | $n_2 \to 3$ | 3 | 4, 5, 6, ... | Infrared (IR) |
| Brackett | $n_2 \to 4$ | 4 | 5, 6, 7, ... | Infrared (IR) |
| Pfund | $n_2 \to 5$ | 5 | 6, 7, 8, ... | Far Infrared |

**Real-World Utility in Engineering:**
- **Balmer series** → used in **astronomy** to detect hydrogen in stars (spectroscopic analysis).
- **Lyman series** → used in **UV spectroscopy** and **plasma diagnostics**.
- **Paschen series** → used in **infrared spectroscopy** and **night-vision technology**.
- Bohr's model concepts directly lead to **band theory of solids**, the foundation of **semiconductor physics**, **optoelectronics**, and **photovoltaic cells**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Radius of the nth Bohr Orbit ($r_n$)

**Starting Equation — Coulomb force = Centripetal force:**

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r_n^2} = \frac{m v_n^2}{r_n}$$

**Step 1 — Simplify the force balance equation:**
Multiply both sides by $r_n$:

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r_n} = m v_n^2 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bohr's Quantization of Angular Momentum:**

$$m v_n r_n = \frac{n h}{2\pi}$$

Solving for $v_n$:

$$v_n = \frac{n h}{2 \pi m r_n} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Square equation (2):**

$$v_n^2 = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r_n^2} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (3) into (1):**

$$\frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r_n} = m \cdot \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m^2 r_n^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$\frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r_n} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m r_n^2}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-multiply and solve for $r_n$:**

$$Z e^2 \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot r_n = n^2 h^2 \cdot \epsilon_0$$

$$r_n = \frac{n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}{\pi m Z e^2}$$

**Step 7 — Express in terms of Bohr's first radius $a_0$ (for $Z=1$, $n=1$):**

$$a_0 = \frac{h^2 \epsilon_0}{\pi m e^2} = 0.529 \text{ Å}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{r_n = \frac{n^2}{Z} \cdot a_0 = \frac{n^2}{Z} \times 0.529 \text{ Å}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $r_n \propto n^2$ — the radius increases as the **square of the principal quantum number**. This is why higher orbits are spaced farther apart.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Velocity of the Electron in the nth Orbit ($v_n$)

**Step 1 — From the quantization condition, substitute $r_n$ into (2):**

$$v_n = \frac{n h}{2 \pi m r_n} = \frac{n h}{2 \pi m} \cdot \frac{\pi m Z e^2}{n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel common terms:**

$$v_n = \frac{Z e^2}{2 \epsilon_0 h n}$$

**Step 3 — Express using fine structure constant $\alpha = \frac{e^2}{2\epsilon_0 h c} \approx \frac{1}{137}$:**

$$v_n = \frac{Z \alpha c}{n}$$

For the first Bohr orbit ($Z=1$, $n=1$):

$$\boxed{v_1 = \alpha c \approx \frac{c}{137} \approx 2.188 \times 10^{6} \text{ m/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $v_n \propto \frac{1}{n}$ — higher orbits have **lower** electron speeds. The first orbit has the highest velocity, but even this is only $\sim 0.7\%$ of the speed of light, justifying the non-relativistic treatment.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Energy of the Electron in the nth Orbit ($E_n$)

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy from Force Balance (1):**

$$K_n = \frac{1}{2} m v_n^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r_n}$$

**Step 2 — Potential Energy (Coulomb potential):**

$$U_n = -\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{r_n}$$

**Step 3 — Total Energy $E_n = K_n + U_n$:**

$$E_n = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r_n} - \frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r_n} = -\frac{Z e^2}{8 \pi \epsilon_0 r_n}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $r_n = \frac{n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}{\pi m Z e^2}$:**

$$E_n = -\frac{Z e^2}{8 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\pi m Z e^2}{n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{E_n = -\frac{m Z^2 e^4}{8 \epsilon_0^2 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2} = -13.6 \, \frac{Z^2}{n^2} \text{ eV}}$$

For hydrogen ($Z=1$):
- $E_1 = -13.6$ eV (ground state)
- $E_2 = -3.40$ eV
- $E_3 = -1.51$ eV
- $E_\infty = 0$ eV (ionized state)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The negative sign indicates a **bound state**. The electron must absorb **13.6 eV** of energy to be completely freed from the hydrogen atom (ionization energy). The energy gap between adjacent levels **decreases** as $n$ increases — this is why spectral lines converge at the series limit.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Rydberg Formula for Hydrogen Spectrum

**Step 1 — Bohr's Frequency Rule:**

$$h \nu = E_{n_2} - E_{n_1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $E_n$ formula:**

$$h \nu = -13.6 \frac{Z^2}{n_2^2} - \left(-13.6 \frac{Z^2}{n_1^2}\right) = 13.6 Z^2 \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) \text{ eV}$$

**Step 3 — Convert frequency to wavelength using $\nu = c/\lambda$:**

$$\frac{h c}{\lambda} = 13.6 Z^2 \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) \text{ eV}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\frac{1}{\lambda}$:**

$$\frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{13.6}{h c} \cdot Z^2 \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Define the Rydberg constant $R$:**

$$R = \frac{13.6 \text{ eV}}{h c} = \frac{13.6 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2 \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Distance of Closest Approach (Rutherford)

**Step 1 — When an alpha particle is fired directly at the nucleus, all its kinetic energy converts to electrostatic potential energy at closest approach $d$:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Z e \cdot 2 e}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d$:**

$$\boxed{d = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 Z e^2}{E_k}}$$

**For gold ($Z=79$) with $E_k = 7.7$ MeV:**
$$d = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{7.7 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 3 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m} = 30 \text{ fm}$$

This is ~$\frac{1}{100000}$ of the atomic radius ($\sim 10^{-10}$ m), confirming the nucleus is incredibly small.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Rutherford vs. Bohr Model

| Feature | Rutherford Model (1911) | Bohr Model (1913) |
|---------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| **Year & Scientist** | 1911, Ernest Rutherford | 1913, Niels Bohr |
| **Atomic structure** | Tiny dense positive nucleus + orbiting electrons | Nucleus + electrons in **quantized** orbits |
| **Basis** | Gold foil experiment (Geiger-Marsden) | Planck's quantum theory + classical mechanics |
| **Electron motion** | Classical circular orbits | Stationary (non-radiating) orbits |
| **Energy emission** | Continuous (predicts collapse in $10^{-8}$ s) | Discrete quanta ($h\nu = E_i - E_f$) |
| **Stability of atom** | Unstable (violates Maxwell's EM theory) | Stable (explains ground state) |
| **Angular momentum** | Any value allowed | Quantized: $L = \frac{nh}{2\pi}$ |
| **Energy levels** | Continuous | Discrete: $E_n \propto -\frac{1}{n^2}$ |
| **Spectra explained** | None | Hydrogen line spectra (Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, etc.) |
| **Limitations** | Cannot explain discrete spectra, stability | Fails for multi-electron atoms, no fine structure, ignores wave nature of electron |
| **Engineering relevance** | Foundation of nuclear physics, RBS technique | Foundation of atomic spectroscopy, lasers, semiconductor physics |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Rutherford's Gold Foil Experiment — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alpha Source: Radioactive Polonium-212] -->|emits alpha particles| B[Lead Slit: Collimates beam into narrow stream]
    B -->|directed at| C[Thin Gold Foil: ~10^-7 m thick, ~1000 atoms thick]
    C -->|most pass straight through| D[Detector at 0 deg: ~98% straight]
    C -->|some deflect by small angles| E[Detector at 30-60 deg: ~2% deflected]
    C -->|few bounce back at >90 deg| F[Detector at 150-180 deg: ~1 in 20000]
    D --> G[Conclusion: Atom is mostly empty space]
    E --> H[Conclusion: Nucleus is small, positively charged, massive]
    F --> I[Conclusion: Nucleus is dense, concentrated at center]
    G --> J[Rutherford Model: Tiny nucleus + orbiting electrons]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Foundation for Bohr Model 1913]
```

### 4.2 Bohr Model — Energy Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph n1["n = 1 Ground State: E = -13.6 eV"]
        L1[Electron at closest stable orbit, r = 0.529 A]
    end
    subgraph n2["n = 2 Excited State: E = -3.40 eV"]
        L2[Electron at r = 2.116 A]
    end
    subgraph n3["n = 3 Excited State: E = -1.51 eV"]
        L3[Electron at r = 4.761 A]
    end
    subgraph n4["n = 4 Excited State: E = -0.85 eV"]
        L4[Electron at r = 8.464 A]
    end
    subgraph nInf["n = infinity: E = 0 eV Ionized"]
        L0[Electron freed from atom]
    end
    L4 -->|Absorption 10.20 eV| L3
    L3 -->|Absorption 12.09 eV| L2
    L2 -->|Absorption 10.20 eV| L1
    L1 -->|Absorption 13.6 eV| L0
    L0 -->|Emission 13.6 eV| L1
    L1 -->|Emission 10.20 eV| L2
    L2 -->|Emission 12.09 eV| L3
    L3 -->|Emission 10.20 eV| L4
```

### 4.3 Hydrogen Spectral Series — Transition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Initial Higher Level n2] -->|emission| P2[Final Lower Level n1]
    P2 --> Q1{Which series?}
    Q1 -->|n1 = 1| R1[Lyman Series: UV region, 91-122 nm]
    Q1 -->|n1 = 2| R2[Balmer Series: Visible, 365-656 nm]
    Q1 -->|n1 = 3| R3[Paschen Series: IR, 820-1875 nm]
    Q1 -->|n1 = 4| R4[Brackett Series: IR, 1458-4051 nm]
    Q1 -->|n1 = 5| R5[Pfund Series: Far IR, 2279-7458 nm]
    R1 --> S1[Engineering: UV spectroscopy, plasma diagnostics]
    R2 --> S2[Engineering: Astronomy, H alpha line at 656 nm]
    R3 --> S3[Engineering: IR spectroscopy, night vision]
    R4 --> S4[Engineering: Astronomical IR observations]
    R5 --> S5[Engineering: Far IR atmospheric studies]
```

### 4.4 Force Balance in a Bohr Orbit — Vector Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electron mass m moving with velocity v in circular orbit of radius r] --> B[Two competing forces act on electron]
    B --> C1[Centripetal Force: F_c = m v squared over r, directed toward nucleus]
    B --> C2[Coulomb Attractive Force: F_e = Ze squared over 4 pi epsilon_0 r squared, directed toward nucleus]
    C1 --> D[For stable circular orbit: F_e = F_c]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E1[Equation 1: Classical force balance]
    D --> E2[Equation 2: Bohr quantization L = n h over 2 pi]
    E1 --> F[Solve simultaneously for r and v]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G1[Result: r_n proportional to n squared]
    F --> G2[Result: v_n proportional to 1 over n]
    F --> G3[Result: E_n proportional to minus 1 over n squared]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the postulates of Bohr's atomic model. Mention any two limitations of Rutherford's model.**

**Model Answer:**

**Bohr's Postulates:**
1. **Stationary Orbits:** An electron in a hydrogen-like atom revolves around the nucleus in certain definite circular orbits called *stationary* or *permitted orbits* without radiating energy. In these orbits, the angular momentum of the electron is quantized:
   $$L = mvr = \frac{nh}{2\pi} \quad ; \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$
2. **Frequency Condition:** The radiation of energy occurs only when an electron jumps from a higher energy orbit ($E_{n_2}$) to a lower energy orbit ($E_{n_1}$). The frequency of the emitted photon is given by:
   $$h\nu = E_{n_2} - E_{n_1}$$
3. **Classical Dynamics:** Within stationary orbits, the electron obeys classical mechanics, i.e., Coulomb's law provides the centripetal force:
   $$\frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

**Two Limitations of Rutherford's Model:**
1. **Atomic Stability:** According to classical electrodynamics, an accelerating charged particle must radiate energy. The orbiting electron (centripetally accelerated) should spiral into the nucleus in $\sim 10^{-8}$ seconds, making the atom unstable. This contradicts reality.
2. **Discrete Spectra:** Rutherford's model cannot explain the observed **line spectrum** of hydrogen. It predicts a **continuous spectrum**, which is experimentally false.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Derive the expression for the radius of the nth Bohr orbit.**

**Model Answer:**

**Starting Equation — Coulomb's force = Centripetal force:**

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Ze^2}{r_n^2} = \frac{mv_n^2}{r_n} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Bohr's Quantization Condition:**

$$m v_n r_n = \frac{nh}{2\pi} \implies v_n = \frac{nh}{2\pi m r_n} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Substituting (2) into (1):

$$\frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r_n^2} = \frac{m}{r_n} \cdot \frac{n^2 h^2}{4\pi^2 m^2 r_n^2}$$

$$\frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4\pi^2 m r_n}$$

$$\boxed{r_n = \frac{n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0}{\pi m Z e^2} = \frac{n^2}{Z} \cdot a_0}$$

where $a_0 = 0.529$ Å is the Bohr radius.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) (7 marks) Derive the expression for the total energy of an electron in the nth orbit of a hydrogen atom using Bohr's postulates. Hence calculate the energy of the electron in the ground state and the first excited state.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy:**
From force balance:
$$K_n = \frac{1}{2} m v_n^2 = \frac{Ze^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0 r_n}$$

**Step 2 — Potential Energy:**
$$U_n = -\frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r_n}$$

**Step 3 — Total Energy:**
$$E_n = K_n + U_n = \frac{Ze^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0 r_n} - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r_n} = -\frac{Ze^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0 r_n}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $r_n$ from Bohr's derivation:**
$$E_n = -\frac{Ze^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\pi m Z e^2}{n^2 h^2 \epsilon_0} = -\frac{m Z^2 e^4}{8 \epsilon_0^2 h^2 n^2}$$

**Step 5 — In SI units (Z = 1 for hydrogen):**
$$E_n = -\frac{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^4}{8 \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12})^2 \times (6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2 \times n^2}$$

$$\boxed{E_n = -13.6 \cdot \frac{1}{n^2} \text{ eV}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical values:**
- **Ground state ($n=1$):** $E_1 = -13.6$ eV
- **First excited state ($n=2$):** $E_2 = -\frac{13.6}{4} = -3.40$ eV

---

**(b) (7 marks) The wavelength of the first line of the Balmer series of hydrogen is 6563 Å. Calculate the Rydberg constant using Bohr's theory. Also find the wavelength of the first line of the Lyman series.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Rydberg formula for Balmer series first line ($n_1 = 2$, $n_2 = 3$):**

$$\frac{1}{\lambda} = R \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) = R \left(\frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{3^2}\right) = R \left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9}\right) = R \cdot \frac{5}{36}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\lambda = 6563$ Å = $6.563 \times 10^{-7}$ m:**

$$\frac{1}{6.563 \times 10^{-7}} = R \cdot \frac{5}{36}$$

$$R = \frac{36}{5 \times 6.563 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.097 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{R = 1.097 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Lyman series first line ($n_1 = 1$, $n_2 = 2$):**

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_L} = R \left(\frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2}\right) = R \cdot \frac{3}{4}$$

$$\lambda_L = \frac{4}{3R} = \frac{4}{3 \times 1.097 \times 10^7} = 1.215 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_L = 1215 \text{ Å}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) (7 marks) Describe the Rutherford scattering experiment. Derive the expression for the distance of closest approach of an alpha particle to the nucleus.**

**Model Solution:**

**Rutherford Scattering Experiment (Geiger-Marsden, 1909):**

A collimated beam of **alpha particles** (emitted from a radioactive source like Polonium-212) was directed at a **thin gold foil** (~10$^{-7}$ m thick, ~1000 atoms thick). The scattered alpha particles were detected using **zinc sulfide (ZnS) screens** placed at various angles around the foil, observed as flashes of light (scintillations) through a microscope.

**Observations:**
- ~98% of alpha particles passed straight through with negligible deflection.
- ~2% were deflected by small angles (< 90°).
- A tiny fraction (~1 in 20,000) bounced back by angles > 90° (some nearly 180°).

**Conclusions:**
- The atom is **mostly empty space** (most particles pass through).
- A tiny, dense, **positively charged nucleus** exists at the center.
- The nucleus contains nearly all the atomic mass.

**Derivation of Distance of Closest Approach:**

At the distance of closest approach $d$, the alpha particle momentarily comes to rest. All its **kinetic energy** is converted into **electrostatic potential energy**.

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{(Ze)(2e)}{d}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$\boxed{d = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2Ze^2}{E_k} = \frac{2Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 E_k}}$$

**Numerical example:** For gold ($Z = 79$) with $E_k = 7.7$ MeV:
$$d = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{7.7 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 3 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m} = 30 \text{ fm}$$

This is $\sim 10^{-5}$ times the atomic radius, confirming the nucleus is extremely tiny.

---

**(b) (7 marks) Using Bohr's theory, derive the expression for the wavelength of radiation emitted during a transition between two energy levels in a hydrogen atom. A hydrogen atom is excited from the ground state to the 4th energy level. Calculate the wavelengths of all possible emission lines.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derive wavelength formula:**
From Bohr's frequency rule $h\nu = E_{n_2} - E_{n_1}$ and $\nu = c/\lambda$:

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda} = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{\lambda} = R \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)}$$

where $R = 1.097 \times 10^7$ m$^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Identify all possible transitions from $n=4$:**
The number of spectral lines is $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$ lines.

The 6 transitions are:
1. $4 \to 3$ (Paschen)
2. $4 \to 2$ (Balmer)
3. $4 \to 1$ (Lyman)
4. $3 \to 2$ (Balmer)
5. $3 \to 1$ (Lyman)
6. $2 \to 1$ (Lyman)

**Step 3 — Calculate each wavelength using $\lambda = \frac{1}{R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)}$:**

1. **$4 \to 3$:** $\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left(\frac{1}{9} - \frac{1}{16}\right) = 5.33 \times 10^5$ m$^{-1}$ $\Rightarrow \lambda = 1.875 \times 10^{-6}$ m = **18750 Å** (IR)
2. **$4 \to 2$:** $\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{16}\right) = 2.057 \times 10^6$ m$^{-1}$ $\Rightarrow \lambda = 4862$ Å (Visible - blue-green, Balmer $H_\beta$)
3. **$4 \to 1$:** $\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left(1 - \frac{1}{16}\right) = 1.028 \times 10^7$ m$^{-1}$ $\Rightarrow \lambda = 972.5$ Å (UV, Lyman series limit)
4. **$3 \to 2$:** $\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9}\right) = 1.524 \times 10^6$ m$^{-1}$ $\Rightarrow \lambda = 6563$ Å (Visible - red, Balmer $H_\alpha$)
5. **$3 \to 1$:** $\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left(1 - \frac{1}{9}\right) = 9.751 \times 10^6$ m$^{-1}$ $\Rightarrow \lambda = 1025.7$ Å (UV, Lyman)
6. **$2 \to 1$:** $\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) = 8.228 \times 10^6$ m$^{-1}$ $\Rightarrow \lambda = 1215.7$ Å (UV, Lyman)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of energy:** Students often write $E_n = +13.6/n^2$ eV. The total energy of a bound electron is **always negative**. The negative sign indicates a bound state. Losing this sign = losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $n_1$ and $n_2$ in the Rydberg formula:** $n_1$ is the **lower** level (final state for emission), $n_2$ is the **higher** level (initial state for emission). Reversing them leads to negative wavelengths.
> 3. **Forgetting $Z^2$ in formulas:** For hydrogen-like atoms (He$^+$, Li$^{2+}$), all formulas acquire a $Z^2$ factor. Skipping this in numericals = full mark loss.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Using Å in some places and meters in others without conversion. Standardize: either everything in meters, or use the handy $E_n = -13.6 Z^2/n^2$ eV shortcut.
> 5. **Rutherford's closest approach formula:** Many students forget the factor of **2** (for the alpha particle's charge $2e$) and write $d = \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 E_k}$ instead of $\frac{2Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 E_k}$.
> 6. **Counting spectral lines:** Always use $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — for $n=4$, it's 6, not 4!

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Rutherford (1911):** Atom has a tiny, dense, positive **nucleus**; electrons orbit around it; mostly empty space.
- [x] **Rutherford's key failure:** Cannot explain stability and line spectra.
- [x] **Bohr (1913):** Applied **Planck's quantum theory** to atomic structure.
- [x] **Three Bohr postulates:** Stationary orbits + frequency rule + classical dynamics inside orbits.
- [x] **Quantization condition:** $L = mvr = \frac{nh}{2\pi}$ — **memorize this!**
- [x] **Bohr radius:** $a_0 = 0.529$ Å (ground state of H).
- [x] **Radius formula:** $r_n = \frac{n^2}{Z} \cdot a_0 \implies r_n \propto n^2$.
- [x] **Velocity formula:** $v_n = \frac{Z e^2}{2\epsilon_0 h n} \implies v_n \propto \frac{1}{n}$; $v_1 \approx 2.188 \times 10^6$ m/s for H.
- [x] **Energy formula:** $E_n = -13.6 \frac{Z^2}{n^2}$ eV — **THE most important formula of Module 1!**
- [x] **Ground state energy of H:** $-13.6$ eV = ionization energy.
- [x] **Rydberg formula:** $\frac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2 \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$; $R = 1.097 \times 10^7$ m$^{-1}$.
- [x] **Hydrogen spectral series:** Lyman (UV, $n_1=1$), Balmer (visible, $n_1=2$), Paschen (IR, $n_1=3$), Brackett (IR, $n_1=4$), Pfund (far IR, $n_1=5$).
- [x] **Counting spectral lines from level $n$:** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- [x] **Distance of closest approach:** $d = \frac{2Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 E_k}$ (note the factor 2 for alpha particle).
- [x] **Bohr's magneton:** $\mu_B = \frac{eh}{4\pi m_e} = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}$ J/T.
- [x] **Bohr model limitations:** Fails for multi-electron atoms, no fine structure, ignores Heisenberg uncertainty, ignores wave-particle duality.
- [x] **Engineering relevance:** Band theory of solids, lasers, X-rays, semiconductors, spectroscopy, plasma diagnostics, photovoltaics.
- [x] **Constants to memorize:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg; $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C; $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- [x] **Key conversion:** $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
- [x] **Fine structure constant:** $\alpha = \frac{1}{137}$ — appears in $v_n = \frac{Z \alpha c}{n}$.
- [x] **Comparison mantra:** Rutherford = classical + unstable; Bohr = quantized + stable + explains spectra.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bonding in Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bonding in Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bonding in Solids** refers to the fundamental interatomic and intermolecular forces that hold constituent atoms, ions, or molecules together in a solid material, thereby determining its mechanical, thermal, electrical, magnetic, and chemical properties. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PECHT412 (Material Science), bonding is classified into **Primary Bonds** (strong, ~100–1000 kJ/mol) and **Secondary Bonds** (weak, ~1–40 kJ/mol).

The five principal bonding mechanisms covered in Module 1 are:

1. **Ionic Bonding** – Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions.
2. **Covalent Bonding** – Sharing of valence electron pairs between adjacent atoms.
3. **Metallic Bonding** – Delocalized "sea" of valence electrons surrounding positive ion cores.
4. **Van der Waals (London Dispersion) Bonding** – Transient induced dipole interactions.
5. **Hydrogen Bonding** – Strong dipole-dipole interaction involving H bonded to N, O, or F.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to **derive the Born–Landé equation** for ionic lattice energy, sketch the **interatomic force–separation curve (F vs r)**, and compute the **equilibrium bond length r₀** and **bond energy E₀** from first principles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine atoms as **magnets in a workshop**. They "click" together when the attractive pull is greater than the repulsion — at exactly the right distance where both forces balance. Move them too close, and the magnets violently push apart (electron-cloud repulsion). Move them too far, and the grip weakens (no attraction). The sweet spot — the **equilibrium separation (r₀)** — is where the bond is strongest, just like finding the perfect tool-shelf distance in a workshop.

| Bond Type | Workshop Analogy |
|---|---|
| Ionic | North–South poles of two powerful magnets locked together |
| Covalent | Two hands clasping each other firmly |
| Metallic | Tools floating in a magnetic fluid that glues them as one |
| Van der Waals | Two sticky notes barely clinging to each other |
| Hydrogen | A small clamp holding a wet piece in place |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Remember:** The vacuum permittivity **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** and the elementary charge **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** appear in virtually every bonding energy derivation for KTU problems.

### 1.3 Significance in Materials Engineering

Bonding dictates the **stress–strain response**, **melting point**, **electrical conductivity**, and **optical behavior** of every engineering material. For example:
- **Ceramics** (ionic/covalent) → high melting point, brittle, insulating.
- **Metals** (metallic) → ductile, conductive, ductile.
- **Polymers** (covalent + secondary) → low melting point, flexible, insulating.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Bonding Mechanisms — Structured Logic

#### 2.1.1 Ionic Bonding
- **Why it forms:** Large electronegativity difference (ΔEN > 1.7) between atoms (typically a metal + non-metal). Electron transfer produces cations (+) and anions (−).
- **How it works:** Coulombic attraction F = (1/4πε₀)(q⁺q⁻/r²) pulls ions together. Short-range electron-cloud repulsion (Born repulsion) sets the equilibrium.
- **Engineering example:** NaCl, MgO, Al₂O₃ — used as refractories and insulators.

#### 2.1.2 Covalent Bonding
- **Why it forms:** Atoms with similar electronegativity share valence electrons to complete their octet.
- **How it works:** Localized electron-pair bonds form directional hybrid orbitals (sp³, sp², sp).
- **Engineering example:** Diamond, Si, SiC, GaAs, polymer chains.

#### 2.1.3 Metallic Bonding
- **Why it forms:** Metal atoms contribute their weakly-held valence electrons to a delocalized electron "gas."
- **How it works:** Free electrons screen the positive ion cores; the cohesive energy is the sum of attractive (electron–ion) and repulsive (ion–ion) terms.
- **Engineering example:** Cu, Al, Fe, Ni — conductors and structural alloys.

#### 2.1.4 Van der Waals (London Dispersion) Bonding
- **Why it forms:** Instantaneous fluctuations in electron distribution create temporary dipoles, inducing dipoles in neighboring atoms.
- **How it works:** Attractive potential scales as U ~ −A/r⁶ (London).
- **Engineering example:** Solidified noble gases, graphite interlayer sliding, polymer crystallites.

#### 2.1.5 Hydrogen Bonding
- **Why it forms:** A hydrogen atom covalently bonded to N, O, or F develops a strong partial positive charge.
- **How it works:** This H⁺ attracts a lone pair on a neighboring N, O, or F atom.
- **Engineering example:** Water (ice), Nylon, Kevlar, DNA double helix.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coulombic (Madelung) Energy | $U_C = -\dfrac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}$ | Electrostatic attraction per mole; J/mol |
| 2 | Born Repulsion Energy | $U_R = \dfrac{B}{r^n}$ | Short-range repulsion; B is empirical constant |
| 3 | **Born–Landé Equation** (Ionic Lattice Energy) | $U_L = -\dfrac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}\left(1-\dfrac{1}{n}\right)$ | Total lattice energy; J/mol |
| 4 | Equilibrium Condition | $\left.\dfrac{dU}{dr}\right\rvert_{r=r_0} = 0$ | Force balance at equilibrium |
| 5 | Born Exponent (typical) | n ≈ 9 (for Na⁺–Cl⁻) to 12 | Dimensionless; depends on ion size |
| 6 | Madelung Constant (NaCl) | M = 1.7476 | Geometry-dependent |
| 7 | Madelung Constant (CsCl) | M = 1.7627 | Geometry-dependent |
| 8 | Madelung Constant (ZnS) | M = 1.6381 | Geometry-dependent |
| 9 | Bond Energy at Equilibrium | $E_0 = U(r_0)$ | Minimum of U–r curve; eV/atom or kJ/mol |
| 10 | Interatomic Force | $F = -\dfrac{dU}{dr}$ | N; zero at r = r₀ |
| 11 | Lennard-Jones Potential | $U(r) = 4 \varepsilon \left[\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6}\right]$ | Van der Waals modeling |
| 12 | London Dispersion | $U \approx -\dfrac{A}{r^6}$ | Attractive; A = polarizability-dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, every magnitude/absolute value such as the derivative condition has been written with the word "evaluated" or rendered as a textual condition. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify **Madelung constants** correctly for different crystal geometries (NaCl, CsCl, ZnS).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Material selection:** Knowing the bond type tells the engineer whether the material is ductile (metallic), brittle (ionic/covalent), or soft (van der Waals).
- **Semiconductor design:** Covalent Ge/Si with controlled dopants underpins all modern electronics.
- **High-temperature ceramics:** Strong ionic + covalent bonds (e.g., Si₃N₄) survive >1500 °C for turbine blades.
- **Polymer processing:** Secondary bonds govern crystallinity, glass transition, and melting in thermoplastics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Born–Landé Equation for Ionic Lattice Energy

The total potential energy of an ionic crystal consists of two competing terms: the **Coulombic (Madelung) attractive energy** and the **Born short-range repulsive energy**. The equilibrium bond length and lattice energy are found by minimizing the total energy with respect to interionic separation.

#### Step 1: Define the total potential energy

The total potential energy per ion pair is the sum of attractive and repulsive contributions:

$$
U(r) = U_C(r) + U_R(r)
$$

where $U_C(r)$ is the Coulombic (electrostatic) attraction and $U_R(r)$ is the Born repulsion.

#### Step 2: Write the Coulombic energy for an ion pair

For a single ion pair with charges $z^+ e$ and $z^- e$ separated by distance $r$, the electrostatic potential energy is given by Coulomb's law:

$$
U_{\text{pair}}(r) = -\dfrac{(z^+ e)(z^- e)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r}
$$

The negative sign indicates attraction between oppositely charged ions.

#### Step 3: Extend to the entire crystal lattice using the Madelung constant

In a real crystal, each ion interacts with all other ions in the lattice. The geometric summation of all these Coulombic interactions yields the **Madelung constant M**, a dimensionless number that depends on the crystal geometry (NaCl, CsCl, ZnS, etc.). The total Coulombic energy per mole becomes:

$$
U_C(r) = -\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r}
$$

where $N_A$ = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹ is Avogadro's number.

#### Step 4: Model the Born repulsive energy empirically

The short-range repulsion arises from overlap of filled electron shells when ions approach too closely. It is modeled as:

$$
U_R(r) = \dfrac{B}{r^n}
$$

where **B** is a positive empirical constant and **n** is the **Born exponent** (typically 9 ≤ n ≤ 12), determined from measured compressibility data.

#### Step 5: Combine into the total lattice potential energy

$$
U(r) = -\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r} + \dfrac{B}{r^n}
$$

#### Step 6: Apply the equilibrium condition

At the equilibrium interionic separation $r = r_0$, the total potential energy is at a minimum. Therefore, the first derivative with respect to r must vanish:

$$
\dfrac{dU}{dr} = 0 \quad \text{at} \quad r = r_0
$$

Differentiating term-by-term:

$$
\dfrac{dU}{dr} = \dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r^2} - \dfrac{n B}{r^{n+1}}
$$

Setting this equal to zero at $r = r_0$:

$$
\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r_0^2} = \dfrac{n B}{r_0^{\,n+1}}
$$

#### Step 7: Solve for the empirical constant B

Rearranging the equilibrium equation to isolate B:

$$
B = \dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2 \, r_0^{\,n-1}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, n}
$$

#### Step 8: Substitute B back into U(r₀)

Insert this expression for B into the total energy expression evaluated at $r = r_0$:

$$
U(r_0) = -\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r_0} + \dfrac{B}{r_0^{\,n}}
$$

Substituting the value of B:

$$
U(r_0) = -\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r_0} + \dfrac{1}{r_0^{\,n}} \cdot \dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2 \, r_0^{\,n-1}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, n}
$$

#### Step 9: Simplify algebraically

The second term simplifies as follows:

$$
\dfrac{r_0^{\,n-1}}{r_0^{\,n}} = \dfrac{1}{r_0}
$$

So:

$$
U(r_0) = -\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r_0} + \dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, n \, r_0}
$$

#### Step 10: Factor out the common term

Factor out the common Coulombic prefactor:

$$
U(r_0) = -\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r_0} \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)
$$

#### Final Result — The Born–Landé Equation

$$
\boxed{\,U_L = -\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r_0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)\,}
$$

The negative sign indicates that energy must be released to form the crystal (exothermic process). This is the **lattice energy per mole** of the ionic solid.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** Calculate the lattice energy of NaCl given:
- M (Madelung constant) = 1.7476
- z⁺ = z⁻ = 1
- r₀ = 0.282 nm = 2.82 × 10⁻¹⁰ m
- n (Born exponent) = 9
- N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹
- e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m

**Solution:**

$$
U_L = -\dfrac{(6.022 \times 10^{23})(1.7476)(1)(1)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2}{4 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2.82 \times 10^{-10})}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{9}\right)
$$

Computing the numerator:

$$
N_{\text{term}} = (6.022 \times 10^{23})(1.7476)(2.566 \times 10^{-38}) \approx 2.702 \times 10^{-14}
$$

Computing the denominator:

$$
D_{\text{term}} = 4 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2.82 \times 10^{-10}) \approx 3.137 \times 10^{-20}
$$

The ratio with the $(1 - 1/9) = 8/9$ factor:

$$
U_L \approx -\dfrac{(2.702 \times 10^{-14})}{(3.137 \times 10^{-20})} \cdot \dfrac{8}{9} \approx -7.66 \times 10^{5} \; \text{J/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{U_L \approx -766 \; \text{kJ/mol}}
$$

The accepted experimental value for NaCl is ≈ **−787 kJ/mol**, confirming the model's accuracy.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bond Property Comparison

| Property | Ionic | Covalent | Metallic | Van der Waals | Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bond Energy (kJ/mol) | 600–1500 | 200–1000 | 100–800 | 1–40 | 10–40 |
| Directionality | None | High | None | None | Moderate |
| Electrical Conductivity (solid) | Insulator | Insulator (mostly) | Conductor | Insulator | Insulator |
| Mechanical Behavior | Brittle | Hard & Brittle | Ductile | Soft | Soft |
| Melting Point | High | High | Moderate–High | Very Low | Low |
| Example | NaCl | Diamond | Cu | Solid Ar | Ice |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define ionic bonding. Explain how the Born–Landé equation is used to compute the lattice energy of an ionic crystal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Ionic bonding* is the electrostatic force of attraction between positively and negatively charged ions, formed when one atom (typically a metal with low ionization energy) transfers its valence electron(s) to another atom (typically a non-metal with high electron affinity), producing ions that attract each other Coulombically. The *Born–Landé equation* gives the lattice energy of an ionic solid as:

$$
U_L = -\dfrac{N_A \, M \, z^+ z^- \, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \, r_0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)
$$

where M is the Madelung constant (geometry-dependent), z⁺ and z⁻ are ionic charges, r₀ is the equilibrium interionic distance, and n is the Born exponent. The factor (1 − 1/n) accounts for the short-range Born repulsion, reducing the pure Coulombic energy by ~10–20 %.

*(Examiner's note: Full marks require stating the equation, naming all symbols, and explaining the (1 − 1/n) correction term.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between primary and secondary bonds with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Primary bonds* are strong interatomic bonds (100–1000 kJ/mol) involving electron transfer, sharing, or delocalization. Examples: (i) *Ionic* — NaCl, (ii) *Covalent* — Diamond, (iii) *Metallic* — Copper.

*Secondary bonds* are weak intermolecular forces (1–40 kJ/mol) arising from dipolar or induced-dipolar interactions. Examples: (i) *Van der Waals* — Solidified Argon, (ii) *Hydrogen bonds* — Ice (H₂O).

Primary bonds dictate bulk mechanical and thermal properties; secondary bonds govern intermolecular phenomena such as polymer crystallinity, graphite lubrication, and biological molecular recognition.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the **Born–Landé equation** for the lattice energy of an ionic crystal, starting from Coulombic and Born repulsive potential terms. Clearly state the equilibrium condition and explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For **NaCl**, given: M = 1.7476, z⁺ = z⁻ = 1, r₀ = 2.82 × 10⁻¹⁰ m, n = 9, N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, and ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m. Compute the lattice energy and compare with the experimental value of −787 kJ/mol. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **State total energy** (1 mark): $U(r) = U_C(r) + U_R(r)$
2. **Write Coulombic energy per ion pair** (1 mark): $U_C = -N_A M z^+ z^- e^2 / (4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r)$
3. **Write Born repulsion** (1 mark): $U_R = B/r^n$
4. **Combine and differentiate** (1 mark): $dU/dr = 0$ at $r = r_0$
5. **Solve for B** (1 mark): $B = N_A M z^+ z^- e^2 r_0^{n-1} / (4 \pi \varepsilon_0 n)$
6. **Substitute and simplify** (1 mark): Obtain Born–Landé equation.
7. **Explain significance** (1 mark): The factor (1 − 1/n) is the **Born repulsion correction**; it reduces the pure Madelung energy by ~10–20 %.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Compute the Coulombic energy prefactor (2 marks):
$$
U_C = -\dfrac{(6.022 \times 10^{23})(1.7476)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2}{4 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2.82 \times 10^{-10})}
$$
$$
U_C \approx -8.61 \times 10^5 \; \text{J/mol} \approx -861 \; \text{kJ/mol}
$$

Step 2: Apply the Born correction (2 marks):
$$
(1 - 1/n) = (1 - 1/9) = 8/9 \approx 0.8889
$$

Step 3: Final lattice energy (2 marks):
$$
U_L = -861 \times 0.8889 \approx -765 \; \text{kJ/mol}
$$

Step 4: Comparison (1 mark): Calculated value ≈ −765 kJ/mol vs. experimental ≈ −787 kJ/mol. The discrepancy (~2.8 %) arises from neglecting zero-point energy, covalent contributions, and polarization effects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B 14-Mark Questions:**
> 1. Students frequently **forget to substitute the Madelung constant**, losing 2 marks instantly.
> 2. The **unit conversion for r₀** (Å → m or nm → m) is a common 1-mark deduction. Always write units explicitly.
> 3. In derivation questions, examiners award marks **per logical step**. A skipped step (e.g., "set dU/dr = 0") loses at least 1 mark.
> 4. The factor (1 − 1/n) is **NOT** a typo — omitting it results in a 3-mark penalty.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch of the **interatomic force vs. interatomic separation (F vs. r)** curve, explain the concepts of **equilibrium bond length (r₀)**, **bond energy (E₀)**, and the nature of attractive and repulsive forces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the five primary and secondary bonding types in materials with respect to (i) bond energy, (ii) directionality, (iii) electrical conductivity, and (iv) two engineering examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) F vs. r Curve (7 marks):**

1. **Sketch the F–r curve** (2 marks): F_r (repulsive) > 0 for small r, F_a (attractive) < 0 for larger r, F_net = F_r + F_a = 0 at r = r₀.
2. **Identify r₀** (1 mark): The separation at which attractive and repulsive forces exactly balance, defining the equilibrium bond length (typically 0.1–0.3 nm).
3. **Identify E₀** (1 mark): The minimum of the U(r) potential well, equal to the energy required to separate the atoms to infinity. Typical values: 1–10 eV/atom.
4. **Discuss attractive forces** (1.5 marks): Coulombic (ionic), exchange (covalent), electron-sea (metallic), dispersion (van der Waals).
5. **Discuss repulsive forces** (1.5 marks): Pauli exclusion at small r causing exponential Born-type repulsion ~B/r^n.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Property | Ionic | Covalent | Metallic | VdW | Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Bond Energy (kJ/mol) | 600–1500 | 200–1000 | 100–800 | 1–40 | 10–40 |
| (ii) Directionality | None | High | None | None | Moderate |
| (iii) Conductivity | Insulator | Insulator (mostly) | High | Insulator | Insulator |
| (iv) Examples | NaCl, MgO | Diamond, Si | Cu, Al | Solid Ar, N₂ | Ice, Nylon |

(Each row correctly filled and explained = ~1.5 marks; final example row with 2 examples each = 1 mark.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Comparison Table:**
> 1. Students often **label VdW as covalent** in error — they are fundamentally different (electron sharing vs. induced dipoles).
> 2. For semiconductors like **Si and Ge**, bond type is covalent with a minor ionic component — examiners accept "predominantly covalent."
> 3. Always state **both examples** for each bond type to secure the full example-row mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Five bond types:** Ionic, Covalent, Metallic, Van der Waals, Hydrogen.
- ✅ **Primary bonds** are strong (100–1000 kJ/mol); **secondary bonds** are weak (1–40 kJ/mol).
- ✅ **Ionic bond energy** is given by the **Born–Landé equation**: $U_L = -\dfrac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$.
- ✅ **Madelung constant** depends on crystal geometry: NaCl (1.7476) < CsCl (1.7627); ZnS = 1.6381.
- ✅ **Born exponent n** typically 9–12; determined from compressibility data.
- ✅ **Equilibrium bond length r₀** is where dU/dr = 0 (force balance).
- ✅ **Bond energy E₀** is the minimum of U(r); corresponds to cohesive energy per atom pair.
- ✅ **Metallic bonding** gives ductility and conductivity due to the delocalized electron sea.
- ✅ **Covalent bonds** are **directional** → control hardness and brittleness in ceramics and semiconductors.
- ✅ **Van der Waals** bonds govern polymer crystallinity, graphite lubrication, and noble-gas solids.
