> 1. **Do NOT reduce** $(2\,2\,4)$ to $(1\,1\,2)$ when reading Miller indices from intercepts — this is the family index notation. Miller indices must always be in the **smallest integer form**. Reduction step is mandatory.
> 2. **Sign notation is critical:** A bar (overbar) on an integer has a different meaning from a negative sign. Writing $-110$ instead of $(\bar{1}\,1\,0)$ is a mark-deducting error.
> 3. **Intercepts are read from the origin** to the plane; do not confuse "intercepts" with "distances between parallel planes".
> 4. **Origin shift rule:** If a plane passes through the origin, shift the origin to a parallel lattice point; the Miller indices remain unchanged.
> 5. **Direction $[hkl]$ is perpendicular to $(hkl)$ ONLY in cubic systems** — applying this in non-cubic systems is a frequent and heavily penalized error.
> 6. **Intercepts of $\infty$** are valid and correspond to Miller index **zero**, not "undefined".
> 7. **Show all steps in derivations:** KTU examiners award partial marks for the LCM step, reciprocal step, and final result. Skipping intermediate steps may cost up to **2 marks** on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Miller indices $(hkl)$** = reciprocals of fractional intercepts, reduced to smallest integers, enclosed in parentheses.
- **Direction indices $[uvw]$** = coordinates of the tip of a vector from the origin, reduced to smallest integers, enclosed in square brackets.
- **Negative indices** are denoted by an **overbar** (e.g., $\bar{1}$, $\bar{2}$), not a minus sign.
- **Family of planes** is denoted by **curly braces** $\{hkl\}$; **family of directions** by **angle brackets** $\langle uvw \rangle$.
- **Intercepts of $\infty$** yield Miller index **zero** — never "undefined".
- **A plane passing through the origin** must be **translated** to a parallel plane before applying the reciprocal rule.
- **Interplanar spacing (Cubic):** $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2} + k^{2} + l^{2}}}$ — this is the most-tested formula in the KTU module.
- **In cubic crystals only**, $[hkl] \perp (hkl)$ — do not extrapolate to other crystal systems.
- **Slip systems in metals** are defined using Miller indices: FCC uses $\{1\,1\,1\}\langle 1\,1\,0 \rangle$, BCC uses $\{1\,1\,0\}\langle \bar{1}\,1\,1 \rangle$, HCP uses basal/pyramidal planes and close-packed directions.
- **Bragg's Law** $n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$ always requires the $d$-spacing computed from Miller indices.
- **Number of equivalent planes in cubic $\{hkl\}$ family:**
  - $\{1\,0\,0\}$ → 6 planes
  - $\{1\,1\,0\}$ → 12 planes
  - $\{1\,1\,1\}$ → 8 planes
  - $\{h\,k\,l\}$ general (all indices distinct) → 48 planes
- **Always show:** (1) intercepts, (2) reciprocals, (3) LCM multiplier, (4) reduced form, (5) final bracketed form — for full marks.
- **Common KTU hot-spot questions:** finding Miller indices of a plane, identifying $\{hkl\}$ family members, computing $d$-spacing, drawing a plane given Miller indices, and converting a direction to a family.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coordination Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coordination Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coordination Number (CN)** is defined as the number of **nearest equidistant neighboring atoms** (or ions) that directly surround a reference atom (or ion) in a crystal lattice structure. It quantifies the immediate atomic environment and bonding topology of an atom in a crystalline solid.

In crystallography, the coordination number is a **geometric descriptor** of atomic packing. It is determined by:

1. Identifying the reference atom at the center.
2. Drawing imaginary lines to all touching/contacting nearest neighbor atoms.
3. Counting the number of such nearest neighbors.

The coordination number is a **dimensionless integer** (always a whole number) and serves as a primary metric in classifying crystal structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Coordination Number is the *foundational concept* that bridges atomic radius, interatomic spacing, crystal geometry, and atomic packing factor (APF). It is heavily tested in Part A (3-mark) and forms a prerequisite for advanced topics like radius ratio rules, structure classification, and density calculations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **circular dining table** at a wedding banquet:

- The **bride/groom (reference atom)** sits at the center.
- The **closest family members (nearest neighbors)** are seated around, touching the bride/groom.
- The **coordination number** is simply the number of close family members sitting immediately adjacent to the bride/groom.

Now, in a **2D circle** (honeycomb-like arrangement):
- Each atom can touch at most **6** neighbors in a flat plane (hexagonal packing).

In **3D space**, atoms can be arranged in more complex geometries:
- **6 neighbors** in a simple cubic (like corners of a cube around the center).
- **8 neighbors** in a body-centered cubic (like corners of a cube around its center atom).
- **12 neighbors** in a face-centered cubic (most efficient packing).

The higher the coordination number, the **denser and more efficiently packed** the atoms are in space.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Coordination Number (CN):** Dimensionless integer, typically ranging from **2 to 12** in elemental solids.
- **Atomic Radius (R):** Measured in **Å (Angstroms, 1 Å = 10⁻¹⁰ m)** or **nm (nanometers, 1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m)**.
- **Nearest Neighbor Distance (2R):** The center-to-center distance between touching atoms.
- **Atomic Packing Factor (APF):** Dimensionless fraction, directly correlated with CN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** A higher CN does **not always** mean a more stable structure — it depends on the bonding type (metallic, ionic, covalent). For example, diamond has CN = 4 but is extremely strong due to directional covalent bonds.

### 1.4 Common Coordination Numbers in Standard Crystal Structures

| Crystal Structure | Coordination Number (CN) | Example Materials |
|---|---|---|
| Simple Cubic (SC) | 6 | α-Polonium (only element) |
| Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) | 8 | Fe (α), Cr, Mo, W, Na |
| Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) | 12 | Cu, Al, Ag, Au, Ni, γ-Fe |
| Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP) | 12 | Zn, Mg, Ti (α), Co |
| Diamond Cubic (DC) | 4 | Si, Ge, C (diamond) |
| Body-Centered Tetragonal | 8 | Sn (β) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How Coordination Number is Determined

The determination of CN follows a rigorous geometric procedure:

**Step 1: Identify the reference atom**
Select a single atom as the origin of analysis. By lattice symmetry, this choice is arbitrary in a perfect crystal.

**Step 2: Calculate distances to surrounding atoms**
For each surrounding atom, compute the center-to-center distance vector and its magnitude.

**Step 3: Identify nearest neighbors**
The **nearest neighbors** are those atoms that are **touching** (in contact) with the reference atom. This corresponds to the **minimum non-zero distance** in the crystal.

**Step 4: Count them**
The CN is the count of these touching neighbors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "nearest" matters:** Only the *first* shell of neighbors contributes to the CN. Second-nearest neighbors (e.g., corner-to-corner in BCC) are NOT counted even though they are geometrically closer in some interpretations.

### 2.2 Geometric Analysis of Standard Structures

#### 2.2.1 Simple Cubic (SC)
- The reference atom sits at the **center of a cube**.
- It touches **6 face-centered atoms** along the ±x, ±y, ±z directions.
- **CN = 6**
- Atomic radius: $R = \dfrac{a}{2}$, where $a$ is the lattice parameter.

#### 2.2.2 Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)
- The reference atom is at the **cube center**.
- It touches **8 corner atoms** along the body diagonals.
- The body diagonal length: $\sqrt{3}\,a = 4R$
- Therefore: $R = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$
- **CN = 8**

#### 2.2.3 Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)
- The reference atom sits at a **corner of the cube**.
- It touches **12 atoms**: 4 atoms on adjacent faces in the same plane, 4 on the top face, and 4 on the bottom face.
- The face diagonal: $\sqrt{2}\,a = 4R$
- Therefore: $R = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,a}{4}$
- **CN = 12**

#### 2.2.4 Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP)
- The reference atom is at the **center of the basal plane**.
- It touches **6 atoms** in its own basal plane.
- It touches **3 atoms** in the layer above and **3 atoms** in the layer below.
- **Total CN = 6 + 3 + 3 = 12**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the most-tested relations in KTU exams.**

| Structure | CN | Relation between R and a | Atoms per unit cell (n) | APF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Cubic (SC) | **6** | $a = 2R$ | 1 | **0.524** |
| Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) | **8** | $a = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}$ | 2 | **0.680** |
| Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) | **12** | $a = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt{2}}$ | 4 | **0.740** |
| Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP) | **12** | $a = 2R$ | 6 | **0.740** |
| Diamond Cubic (DC) | **4** | $a = \dfrac{8R}{\sqrt{3}}$ | 8 | **0.340** |

> [!NOTE]
> **APF (Atomic Packing Factor) is directly proportional to CN** for the common metallic structures. APF is calculated as:
>
> $$\text{APF} = \frac{\text{Volume of atoms in unit cell}}{\text{Volume of unit cell}} = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{a^{3}}$$

### 2.4 The Radius Ratio Rule (KTU Favorite Topic)

For **ionic crystals**, the coordination number is dictated by the ratio of cation radius ($r_{+}$) to anion radius ($r_{-}$):

$$\text{Radius Ratio} = \rho = \frac{r_{+}}{r_{-}}$$

| Radius Ratio $\rho$ Range | CN | Geometry | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0.155 \leq \rho < 0.225$ | 2 | Linear | — |
| $0.225 \leq \rho < 0.414$ | 3 | Triangular | $\text{B}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$ |
| $0.414 \leq \rho < 0.732$ | 4 | Tetrahedral | ZnS, $\text{SiO}_{2}$ |
| $0.732 \leq \rho \leq 1.0$ | 6 | Octahedral | NaCl, MgO |
| $1.0 < \rho$ | 8 | Cubic | CsCl |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this rule exists:** The CN is limited by geometric stability — the cation must be large enough to "push apart" the anions so they do not overlap. If the cation is too small, it cannot physically support that many anion contacts.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Metallurgy:** CN determines slip systems, ductility, and formability of metals. FCC metals (CN=12) are more ductile than BCC (CN=8).
- **Ceramics:** CN governs ionic conductivity in solid electrolytes (e.g., $\text{Y}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$-stabilized $\text{ZrO}_{2}$).
- **Semiconductors:** Diamond cubic structure (CN=4) defines the bonding and bandgap of Si and Ge.
- **Crystal Engineering:** Pharmaceutical polymorphs with different CNs exhibit different solubility and bioavailability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coordination Number of BCC Structure

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. Learn every step.

**Given:** A Body-Centered Cubic unit cell with lattice parameter $a$.

**Step 1: Locate the reference and nearest neighbor atoms**

The **body-center atom** is the reference. The **8 corner atoms** are the nearest neighbors because they touch the center atom along the body diagonal.

**Step 2: Express the body diagonal length geometrically**

The body diagonal of a cube with edge length $a$ is found using the 3D Pythagorean theorem:

$$d_{\text{body}} = \sqrt{a^{2} + a^{2} + a^{2}} = \sqrt{3\,a^{2}} = \sqrt{3}\,a$$

**Step 3: Relate body diagonal to atomic radii**

Along the body diagonal, the **center atom** (radius R) touches **2 corner atoms** (each radius R). The total distance from one corner to the opposite corner is therefore:

$$d_{\text{body}} = R + 2R + R = 4R$$

(The chain: corner atom radius + center atom diameter + corner atom radius.)

**Step 4: Equate the two expressions**

$$\sqrt{3}\,a = 4R$$

**Step 5: Solve for the lattice parameter**

$$a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 6: Count the neighbors**

The reference center atom touches **all 8 corners** of the cube. Each corner is a nearest neighbor.

$$\boxed{\text{CN}_{\text{BCC}} = 8}$$

**Step 7: Compute the number of atoms per unit cell (n)**

- 8 corner atoms, each shared among 8 unit cells: $8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = 1$
- 1 body-center atom, fully inside: $1 \times 1 = 1$
- Total: $n = 1 + 1 = 2$ atoms per unit cell

**Step 8: Derive the Atomic Packing Factor (APF)**

$$\text{APF} = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{a^{3}}$$

Substituting $R = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$:

$$R^{3} = \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}\right)^{3} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,a^{3}}{64}$$

$$\text{APF} = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,a^{3}}{64}}{a^{3}}$$

$$\text{APF} = \frac{2 \cdot 4\pi \cdot 3\sqrt{3}\,a^{3}}{3 \cdot 64 \cdot a^{3}}$$

$$\text{APF} = \frac{8\pi\sqrt{3}}{64} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.680}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coordination Number of FCC Structure

**Given:** A Face-Centered Cubic unit cell with lattice parameter $a$.

**Step 1: Identify nearest neighbors**

Take a **corner atom** as the reference. It touches:
- 4 atoms in the same face (in-plane neighbors).
- 4 atoms in the face above.
- 4 atoms in the face below.

**Step 2: Use the face diagonal for the touching condition**

The corner atom touches the face-center atom along the **face diagonal**:

$$d_{\text{face}} = \sqrt{a^{2} + a^{2}} = \sqrt{2}\,a$$

**Step 3: Relate face diagonal to atomic radii**

Along the face diagonal, the corner atom (R) touches the face-center atom (R), so:

$$d_{\text{face}} = R + 2R + R = 4R$$

**Step 4: Equate and solve**

$$\sqrt{2}\,a = 4R$$

$$\boxed{a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2}\,R}$$

**Step 5: Coordination number**

$$\boxed{\text{CN}_{\text{FCC}} = 12}$$

**Step 6: Atoms per unit cell**

- 8 corners × $\dfrac{1}{8}$ = 1
- 6 face centers × $\dfrac{1}{2}$ = 3
- Total: $n = 1 + 3 = 4$

**Step 7: APF Derivation**

Using $R = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,a}{4}$, we get $R^{3} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,a^{3}}{64} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,a^{3}}{32}$

$$\text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}\,a^{3}}{32}}{a^{3}}$$

$$\text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{16\pi\sqrt{2}}{96} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.740}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Coordination Number of Diamond Cubic

The diamond cubic structure has **two interpenetrating FCC sublattices** offset by $\left(\dfrac{a}{4}, \dfrac{a}{4}, \dfrac{a}{4}\right)$.

**Step 1: Reference atom location**

The reference atom sits at $(0,0,0)$. Its nearest neighbors are the 4 atoms from the second FCC sublattice located at $\left(\dfrac{a}{4}, \dfrac{a}{4}, \dfrac{a}{4}\right)$ and symmetry-equivalent positions.

**Step 2: Distance to nearest neighbor**

$$d = \sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{4}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{a}{4}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{a}{4}\right)^{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$$

**Step 3: Touching condition**

$$d = 2R = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$$

$$a = \frac{8R}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 4: Coordination number**

The 4 nearest neighbors form a **perfect tetrahedron** around the reference atom.

$$\boxed{\text{CN}_{\text{Diamond}} = 4}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Why FCC and HCP have the same CN = 12

Both FCC and HCP achieve the **maximum possible coordination number for identical spheres** in 3D, known as the **Kepler Conjecture** (proved by Thomas Hales, 1998). The proof is geometric:

- In a single close-packed layer, each sphere touches 6 others.
- A second close-packed layer sits in the depressions of the first, giving **3 additional contacts per sphere** (on top).
- A third layer: depending on stacking, gives **3 more contacts per sphere** (on bottom).
- Total: $6 + 3 + 3 = 12$.

**FCC stacking:** ABCABC… (third layer offset)
**HCP stacking:** ABABAB… (third layer directly above first)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Despite having identical CN and APF, FCC and HCP exhibit **different mechanical properties** (e.g., HCP Ti is less ductile than FCC Al) because the **slip systems differ**. This is a frequent KTU exam point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Coordination Number Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Crystal Structure] --> B{Type of Structure?}

    B --> D[Body-Centered Cubic BCC]

    C --> C1[Reference: Center atom]

    D --> D1[Reference: Body-center atom]
    D1 --> D2[Touches 8 corner atoms]
    D2 --> D3[CN equals 8]
    D3 --> D4[APF equals 0.680]

```

### 4.4 Tabular Schematic: Visual Comparison of CNs

| Structure | Visual Hint (ASCII) | CN | Packing Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| SC | `[+ + +] / +C+ / [+++]` (6 contacts) | 6 | Low |
| BCC | `+ /+C+\ +` (8 corner contacts) | 8 | Medium |
| FCC | `++C++` (12 close contacts) | 12 | High |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define coordination number. What is the coordination number of a body-centered cubic (BCC) crystal structure? Justify your answer with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Coordination Number (CN):** The coordination number of an atom in a crystal is the number of its nearest equidistant neighbors that are in direct contact with it.
>
> **CN of BCC = 8**
>
> **Justification:** In a BCC unit cell, the body-center atom is in contact with all 8 corner atoms along the body diagonal. The 8 corner atoms are the closest neighbors to the body-center atom, and no other atoms are closer. Hence, the coordination number is **8**.
>
> **Diagram:**
> ```
>        • ------- •
>       /|        /|
>      / |       / |
>     •--+------•  |
>     |  •------|--•
>     | /       | /
>     |/        |/
>     •-------- •
>        (• = corner atoms, central • = reference)
> ```
>
> The body-center atom touches all 8 corners.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Distinguish between coordination number and atomic packing factor (APF) for FCC and BCC structures.**

**Model Answer:**

> | Property | FCC | BCC |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Coordination Number** | 12 | 8 |
> | **APF** | 0.740 (74.0%) | 0.680 (68.0%) |
> | **Nearest Neighbors** | 12 atoms around a corner atom | 8 atoms around body-center |
> | **Packing** | Close-packed | Not close-packed |
>
> **Coordination Number** is the *count* of touching neighbors (a geometric integer), while **APF** is the *fraction* of unit cell volume occupied by atoms (a volumetric metric). FCC is denser (APF = 0.740) than BCC (APF = 0.680) because it has more nearest neighbors packed around each atom.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define coordination number. Derive the coordination number, number of atoms per unit cell, and atomic packing factor for a Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) crystal structure. Show all steps clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Iron (Fe) at room temperature has a BCC structure with atomic radius $R = 1.24 \, \text{Å}$. Calculate:
(i) The lattice parameter $a$
(ii) The unit cell volume $V$
(iii) The mass density $\rho$ (given: $M_{\text{Fe}} = 55.85 \, \text{g/mol}$, $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$)

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a): Derivation for FCC

**Step 1:** CN is the number of nearest touching neighbors. In FCC, a corner atom touches **12** face-center atoms → **CN = 12**.

**Step 2: Touching condition along face diagonal**

$$\sqrt{2}\,a = 4R \implies a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2}\,R$$

**Step 3: Atoms per unit cell**

- 8 corner atoms × $\dfrac{1}{8}$ = 1
- 6 face-center atoms × $\dfrac{1}{2}$ = 3
- **Total $n = 4$**

**Step 4: APF Calculation**

$$\text{APF} = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{a^{3}} = \frac{\frac{16\pi R^{3}}{3}}{(2\sqrt{2}\,R)^{3}} = \frac{\frac{16\pi R^{3}}{3}}{16\sqrt{2}\,R^{3}}$$

$$\text{APF} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{6} \approx 0.740$$

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Problem on BCC Iron

**Given:** $R = 1.24 \, \text{Å} = 1.24 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{cm}$, BCC structure, $M = 55.85 \, \text{g/mol}$, $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$.

**Step 1: Lattice parameter for BCC**

$$a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{4 \times 1.24 \, \text{Å}}{1.732} = \frac{4.96}{1.732} = 2.864 \, \text{Å}$$

**Step 2: Unit cell volume**

$$V = a^{3} = (2.864 \, \text{Å})^{3} = 23.51 \, \text{Å}^{3}$$

Converting to cm³:

$$V = 23.51 \times 10^{-24} \, \text{cm}^{3}$$

**Step 3: Mass density**

For BCC, $n = 2$ atoms per unit cell.

$$\rho = \frac{n \cdot M}{N_{A} \cdot V} = \frac{2 \times 55.85}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 23.51 \times 10^{-24}}$$

$$\rho = \frac{111.7}{14.158} = 7.89 \, \text{g/cm}^{3}$$

This matches the experimental density of α-iron (7.87 g/cm³). ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the radius ratio rule for ionic crystals. With a neat table, list the coordination numbers and their corresponding stable geometries for radius ratio values from 0.155 to 1.0.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an FCC metal with atomic radius $R = 1.43 \, \text{Å}$ and atomic mass $M = 63.55 \, \text{g/mol}$ (copper), calculate:
(i) The lattice parameter $a$
(ii) The theoretical density $\rho$
(iii) The packing factor (confirm it equals 0.740)

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a): Radius Ratio Rule

> **Radius Ratio Rule:** In ionic crystals, the coordination number of a cation (smaller ion) is determined by the geometric stability of anion-cation contact. The cation must be large enough to keep the anions from touching each other.
>
> $$\rho = \frac{r_{\text{cation}}}{r_{\text{anion}}}$$

**Stability Conditions Table:**

| $\rho$ Range | CN | Geometry | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.155 – 0.225 | 2 | Linear | — |
| 0.225 – 0.414 | 3 | Triangular planar | $\text{B}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$ |
| 0.414 – 0.732 | 4 | Tetrahedral | ZnS |
| 0.732 – 1.000 | 6 | Octahedral | NaCl |
| $\geq$ 1.000 | 8 | Cubic (CsCl type) | CsCl |

**Explanation:** When $\rho$ is small, only 2 or 3 anions can fit around the cation without anion-anion overlap. As $\rho$ increases, more anions fit stably, increasing CN.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Problem on FCC Copper

**Given:** $R = 1.43 \, \text{Å} = 1.43 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{cm}$, FCC structure, $n = 4$, $M = 63.55 \, \text{g/mol}$.

**Step 1: Lattice parameter**

$$a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4 \times 1.43}{1.414} = \frac{5.72}{1.414} = 4.045 \, \text{Å}$$

**Step 2: Volume**

$$V = a^{3} = (4.045)^{3} = 66.18 \, \text{Å}^{3} = 66.18 \times 10^{-24} \, \text{cm}^{3}$$

**Step 3: Density**

$$\rho = \frac{n \cdot M}{N_{A} \cdot V} = \frac{4 \times 63.55}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 66.18 \times 10^{-24}}$$

$$\rho = \frac{254.2}{39.85} = 6.38 \, \text{g/cm}^{3}$$

**Step 4: APF Verification**

$$\text{APF} = \frac{4 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi (1.43)^{3}}{(4.045)^{3}}$$

$$\text{APF} = \frac{4 \times 4.189 \times 2.924}{66.18} = \frac{48.99}{66.18} = 0.740 \checkmark$$

This matches the standard FCC APF of 0.740. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Confusing nearest neighbor with second-nearest neighbor:** Students often count corner-to-corner distances in BCC. The correct nearest neighbor distance in BCC is **body diagonal / 2**, not the cube edge.
>
> 2. **Wrong touching direction:** In FCC, atoms touch along the **face diagonal** ($4R = \sqrt{2}\,a$), not the cube edge. Using $4R = a$ is a common error leading to wrong APF.
>
> 3. **Atoms per unit cell miscounting:** Remember the 1/8, 1/2, 1 weighting rule:
>    - Corner atom = **1/8**
>    - Face-center atom = **1/2**
>    - Body-center atom = **1** (fully inside)
>    - Edge-center atom = **1/4**
>
> 4. **Unit conversions:** 1 Å = 10⁻⁸ cm = 10⁻¹⁰ m. Density calculations require **cm** for Å and **g/cm³** for the final answer.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Avogadro's number:** Density formula ALWAYS needs $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$.
>
> 6. **Mixing up CN with APF:** CN is an integer count; APF is a fraction. Don't write CN = 0.740!

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**

- ☐ **Definition:** CN = number of nearest equidistant neighbors touching the reference atom.
- ☐ **Standard CNs:** SC=6, BCC=8, FCC=12, HCP=12, Diamond=4, NaCl ionic=6, CsCl ionic=8.
- ☐ **Key Geometric Relations:**
  - SC: $a = 2R$
  - BCC: $\sqrt{3}\,a = 4R$ (body diagonal)
  - FCC: $\sqrt{2}\,a = 4R$ (face diagonal)
  - HCP: $a = 2R$ (in basal plane)
  - Diamond: $\sqrt{3}\,a = 8R$ (tetrahedral distance)
- ☐ **Atoms per unit cell:** SC=1, BCC=2, FCC=4, HCP=6, Diamond=8.
- ☐ **APF values:** SC=0.524, BCC=0.680, FCC=0.740, HCP=0.740, Diamond=0.340.
- ☐ **Highest possible CN for identical spheres = 12** (Kepler Conjecture, achieved by FCC & HCP).
- ☐ **Radius Ratio Rule:** Memorize the 5 standard ranges (0.155, 0.225, 0.414, 0.732, 1.0).
- ☐ **Density Formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{nM}{N_{A}\,a^{3}}$ — always convert units to cm and g.
- ☐ **APF Formula:** $\text{APF} = \dfrac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{a^{3}}$ — dimensionless fraction.
- ☐ **FCC and HCP have the same CN=12 and APF=0.740** but differ in stacking sequence (ABC vs AB).
- ☐ **Diamond cubic is open structure** (low APF=0.34) due to directional covalent bonds, NOT close packing.
- ☐ **Higher CN → higher ductility** in metals (FCC > BCC in terms of slip systems).
- ☐ **Nearest neighbor distance is always $2R$** for touching atoms.
- ☐ **Be careful with edge and face sharing rules** in counting atoms per unit cell.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystal Defect (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Crystal Defect** (or **lattice imperfection**) is any deviation of a crystalline solid from a perfectly periodic arrangement of atoms or ions over space, extending over a finite region. These deviations interrupt the ideal, infinitely repeating translational symmetry of a perfect crystal lattice and are categorized by their geometrical dimensionality: **0-D (point), 1-D (line), 2-D (surface/planar), and 3-D (volume/bulk) defects**.

Although thermodynamics predicts a state of perfect order at absolute zero (0 K), real engineering materials in the ambient universe **always** contain defects. The Second Law of Thermodynamics dictates that configurational entropy (S_config) increases when vacancies are introduced, lowering the Gibbs free energy:

$$G = H - TS$$

The introduction of a finite number of defects lowers the free energy because the **T·S** term (where **S > 0** for a disordered configuration) dominates the small enthalpy penalty of breaking bonds.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Parking Lot Intuition

Imagine a perfectly paved **parking lot** in a large shopping mall, with white lines painted to demarcate exactly 1,000 parking slots.

- A **Point Defect** is like finding a single car parked across two slots (a *substitutional* misplaced car) or a motorcycle wedged in a gap between two slots (an *interstitial* misfit). One tiny region is disturbed.
- A **Line Defect** is like a continuous **crack** running through the asphalt from one end of the lot to the other. Every car parked near the crack must drive around it.
- A **Surface Defect** is the **boundary wall** of the parking lot, or the expansion joint between two concrete slabs. A small step (a few mm) abruptly ends one row of slots and begins the next.
- A **Volume Defect** is a **large pothole**, where 50–100 slots are completely missing because the concrete crumbled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 1 places heavy emphasis on **Point Defects (vacancies, interstitials, substitutional, Schottky, Frenkel)** and **Line Defects (edge and screw dislocations)**, with a brief on grain boundaries.

## 1.3 Significance in Materials Engineering

| Property | Role of Defects |
|----------|-----------------|
| **Mechanical Strength** | Dislocations govern plastic deformation (work hardening). |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | Doped semiconductors (Si with P or B) are point-defect engineered. |
| **Diffusion** | Vacancy and interstitial diffusion are defect-mediated. |
| **Optical Behaviour** | F-centers (anion vacancies) color alkali halide crystals (e.g., purple NaCl). |
| **Magnetic Behaviour** | Defects pin domain walls in ferromagnets. |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Grain boundaries act as fast diffusion paths. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** "Defects are not always undesirable." Modern engineering (semiconductors, high-strength alloys, optoelectronics) is built upon the **controlled introduction** of specific defects.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Crystal Defects

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 categorizes defects by **dimensionality (D)**:

- **0-D Defects → Point Defects** (no spatial extent, atomic scale)
- **1-D Defects → Line Defects** (linear, traverse many unit cells)
- **2-D Defects → Surface / Planar Defects** (interface across an area)
- **3-D Defects → Volume / Bulk Defects** (precipitates, voids, inclusions)

---

## 2.2 Point Defects (0-D)

### 2.2.1 Vacancy (Schottky-type component)

A missing atom from a regular lattice site. In ionic crystals (NaCl), a Schottky defect consists of a **pair** of vacancies — one cation and one anion — to maintain charge neutrality.

### 2.2.2 Interstitial Defect

An extra atom of the **same** or **foreign** type squeezed into a void between regular lattice sites (e.g., Carbon in FCC iron → steel).

### 2.2.3 Substitutional Defect

A foreign atom of comparable size replaces a host atom on a regular lattice site (e.g., Zn substituting for Cu in brass).

### 2.2.4 Frenkel Defect

A host atom is **displaced** from its lattice site into an interstitial position, leaving a vacancy. The vacancy-interstitial pair maintains charge neutrality in ionic crystals (e.g., AgCl, AgBr).

### 2.2.5 Impurity / Dopant Defects

Deliberate addition of Group III (B, Ga) or Group V (P, As) atoms to Si or Ge to tune electrical conductivity.

---

## 2.3 Line Defects (1-D) — Dislocations

### 2.3.1 Edge Dislocation

An extra **half-plane** of atoms inserted into the crystal. The bottom edge of this half-plane is the **dislocation line**, characterized by the **Burgers vector $\vec{b}$**, which is perpendicular to the line direction.

### 2.3.2 Screw Dislocation

Formed by **shearing** the upper half of a crystal by one atomic spacing relative to the lower half. The Burgers vector is **parallel** to the dislocation line. The crystal lattice planes trace a helical/spiral ramp.

### 2.3.3 Mixed Dislocation

A combination of edge and screw character; the Burgers vector is neither parallel nor perpendicular to the line.

---

## 2.4 Surface / Planar Defects (2-D)

- **Grain Boundaries:** Interface between two crystals (grains) of differing orientation. Low-angle (< 15°) and high-angle (> 15°) varieties.
- **Twin Boundaries:** Mirror-image atomic arrangement across the boundary plane.
- **Stacking Faults:** Disruption in the stacking sequence (ABCABC…) of close-packed planes, e.g., ABC**B**CABC.
- **External Surfaces:** Free surfaces where atomic bonds terminate abruptly.

---

## 2.5 Volume / Bulk Defects (3-D)

- **Precipitates** (e.g., CuAl₂ in Al-Cu alloys)
- **Voids / Pores** (gas bubbles in polymers)
- **Inclusions** (slag, oxide particles in metals)
- **Foreign particles** embedded during solidification

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|----------------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $n_s / N = \exp(-\Delta H_s / 2k_B T)$ | Equilibrium fraction of **Schottky** defects (ionic crystals) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $n_v / N = \exp(-\Delta H_v / k_B T)$ | Equilibrium fraction of **vacancies** (metallic crystals) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $n_f / \sqrt{N \cdot N_i} = \exp(-\Delta H_f / 2k_B T)$ | Equilibrium fraction of **Frenkel** defects | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\vec{b} = \text{vector sum of atom closure failure}$ | **Burgers vector** magnitude and direction | nm |
| 5 | $\rho_d = L / V$ | **Dislocation density** (line length per unit volume) | m/m³ = m⁻² |
| 6 | $\tau_{CRSS} \approx G b / (2\pi r)$ | Critical resolved shear stress (edge dislocation) | MPa |
| 7 | $D = D_0 \exp(-Q / R T)$ | Diffusion coefficient (defect-mediated) | m²/s |
| 8 | $\Delta G_f = n \Delta H_f - T \Delta S$ | Free energy change on forming *n* defects | J/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used:** $N$ = total lattice sites, $N_i$ = total interstitial sites, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K), $G$ = shear modulus.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Steel Production:** Carbon interstitials in BCC iron govern hardness via quench hardening.
- **Semiconductor Industry:** Phosphorus substitutionals in Si enable n-type doping.
- **Nuclear Reactor Materials:** Frenkel defects in graphite moderators capture neutrons.
- **Ceramic Capacitors:** Schottky defects govern ionic conductivity in doped CeO₂.
- **Aerospace Alloys:** Precipitate hardening via Al₃Sc in Al-Sc alloys.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equilibrium Concentration of Vacancies (Metals)

### Step 1 — Define the System

Consider a perfect metallic crystal containing **N** lattice sites. Suppose **$n_v$** vacancies are formed by removing **$n_v$** atoms from the bulk and relocating them to the crystal surface (which effectively preserves the bulk atom count but leaves empty sites internally).

### Step 2 — Enthalpy of Defect Formation

Let the energy required to form **one** vacancy be **$Q_v$** (also written **$\Delta H_v$**). Forming $n_v$ vacancies costs an enthalpy:

$$\Delta H_{total} = n_v \cdot Q_v$$

### Step 3 — Configurational Entropy (Boltzmann's Relation)

The number of distinguishable ways of arranging $n_v$ vacancies over $N$ lattice sites is given by the combinatorial formula (since vacancies and atoms are indistinguishable among their own kind):

$$W = \frac{N!}{n_v! \, (N - n_v)!}$$

By Boltzmann, the configurational entropy is:

$$\Delta S_{config} = k_B \ln W = k_B \ln \left( \frac{N!}{n_v! \, (N - n_v)!} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Stirling's Approximation

For large numbers ($N, n_v \gg 1$), Stirling's approximation $\ln x! \approx x \ln x - x$ yields:

$$\ln W = N \ln N - n_v \ln n_v - (N - n_v) \ln(N - n_v)$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta S_{config} = k_B \left[ N \ln N - n_v \ln n_v - (N - n_v) \ln(N - n_v) \right]$$

### Step 5 — Gibbs Free Energy Expression

$$\Delta G = \Delta H_{total} - T \Delta S_{config}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta G = n_v Q_v - k_B T \left[ N \ln N - n_v \ln n_v - (N - n_v) \ln(N - n_v) \right]$$

### Step 6 — Minimize $\Delta G$ with respect to $n_v$ (Equilibrium Condition)

At equilibrium, $\dfrac{d(\Delta G)}{d n_v} = 0$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{d n_v} = Q_v - k_B T \left[ -\ln n_v + \ln(N - n_v) \right] = 0$$

### Step 7 — Solve for the Equilibrium Defect Concentration

Rearranging:

$$Q_v = k_B T \ln \left( \frac{N - n_v}{n_v} \right)$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{N - n_v}{n_v} = \exp\left(\frac{Q_v}{k_B T}\right)$$

Inverting (and using $N - n_v \approx N$ since $n_v \ll N$ at typical engineering temperatures):

$$\boxed{\frac{n_v}{N} = \exp\left(-\frac{Q_v}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H_v}{k_B T}\right)}$$

### Step 8 — Numerical Estimation Rule of Thumb

For most metals, $\Delta H_v \approx 1$ eV/atom, giving an equilibrium vacancy concentration of approximately **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$** near the melting point — i.e., **1 to 10 vacancies per 100,000 atoms**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equilibrium Concentration of Schottky Defects (Ionic Crystals)

### Step 1 — Charge Neutrality Constraint

In an ionic crystal of type **MX** (e.g., NaCl), a Schottky defect consists of a **cation vacancy + anion vacancy** pair. Forming one pair requires creating **two** vacancies.

### Step 2 — Energy Cost per Pair

Let $Q_s$ be the energy to form **one** Schottky pair. For $n_s$ pairs:

$$\Delta H_{total} = 2 n_s Q_s$$

### Step 3 — Number of Distinguishable Arrangements

The number of ways of placing $n_s$ cation vacancies on $N$ cation sites is $C(N, n_s)$, and similarly for anion vacancies on $N$ anion sites:

$$W = \left[ \frac{N!}{n_s! (N - n_s)!} \right]^2$$

(assuming equal numbers of cation and anion sites).

### Step 4 — Entropy Expression

$$\Delta S_{config} = 2 k_B \ln \left[ \frac{N!}{n_s! (N - n_s)!} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Minimize Gibbs Free Energy

Setting $\dfrac{d(\Delta G)}{d n_s} = 0$ leads to:

$$2 Q_s = 2 k_B T \ln \left( \frac{N - n_s}{n_s} \right)$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

Using $N - n_s \approx N$:

$$\boxed{\frac{n_s}{N} = \exp\left(-\frac{Q_s}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H_s}{2 k_B T}\right)}$$

The factor of **2** in the denominator comes from the **two** vacancies created per pair.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equilibrium Concentration of Frenkel Defects

### Step 1 — System Definition

A Frenkel defect consists of a vacancy-interstitial pair. Let:
- **$N$** = number of regular lattice sites
- **$N_i$** = number of available interstitial sites
- **$n_f$** = number of Frenkel pairs at equilibrium
- **$Q_f$** = energy per Frenkel pair

### Step 2 — Combinatorial Counting

We choose $n_f$ lattice sites out of $N$ to be vacated, AND $n_f$ interstitial sites out of $N_i$ to be filled:

$$W = \frac{N!}{n_f! (N - n_f)!} \cdot \frac{N_i!}{n_f! (N_i - n_f)!}$$

### Step 3 — Entropy

$$\Delta S_{config} = k_B \ln W$$

Applying Stirling's approximation:

$$\Delta S_{config} = k_B \left[ N \ln N - n_f \ln n_f - (N - n_f) \ln(N - n_f) + N_i \ln N_i - n_f \ln n_f - (N_i - n_f) \ln(N_i - n_f) \right]$$

### Step 4 — Free Energy Minimization

With $\Delta H_{total} = n_f Q_f$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{d n_f} = Q_f - k_B T \ln \left[ \frac{(N - n_f)(N_i - n_f)}{n_f^2} \right] = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve Using $N - n_f \approx N$ and $N_i - n_f \approx N_i$:

$$Q_f = k_B T \ln \left( \frac{N \cdot N_i}{n_f^2} \right)$$

Rearranging:

$$n_f^2 = N \cdot N_i \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q_f}{k_B T}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$\boxed{\frac{n_f}{\sqrt{N \cdot N_i}} = \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H_f}{2 k_B T}\right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Property | Vacancy | Schottky | Frenkel | Interstitial | Substitutional |
|----------|---------|----------|---------|--------------|----------------|
| **Where it forms** | Metals & all crystals | Ionic (NaCl) | Ionic (AgCl, AgBr) | All crystals | Alloys / doped SC |
| **Charge neutral?** | Yes | Yes (pair) | Yes (pair) | Yes | Depends on ion valence |
| **Density effect** | Decreases | Decreases | No change (mass conserved) | Increases | Variable |
| **Energy cost factor** | $1$ in exponent | $1/2$ in exponent | $1/2$ in exponent | High (~3–4 eV) | Medium |
| **Diffusion mechanism** | Vacancy diffusion | Vacancy | Interstitialcy | Interstitial | Vacancy-mediated |
| **KTU example question** | Cu, Al | NaCl, KCl | AgCl, ZnO | C in Fe | Zn in Cu |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Calculate the equilibrium number of vacancies per cubic meter in copper at **1000 °C (1273 K)**. Given: $Q_v = 86{,}900$ J/mol, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$, $\rho_{Cu} = 8.4 \times 10^{3}$ kg/m³, $A_{Cu} = 63.5$ g/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

### Solution

**Step (a):** Number of atomic sites per m³:

$$N = \frac{N_A \cdot \rho}{A} = \frac{(6.022 \times 10^{23}) \cdot (8.4 \times 10^{3})}{63.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.96 \times 10^{28} \, \text{sites/m}^3$$

**Step (b):** Vacancy fraction:

$$\frac{n_v}{N} = \exp\left(-\frac{Q_v}{RT}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{86{,}900}{8.314 \times 1273}\right) = \exp(-8.21) = 2.69 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step (c):** Equilibrium vacancy count:

$$n_v = N \cdot 2.69 \times 10^{-4} = (7.96 \times 10^{28}) \cdot (2.69 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\boxed{n_v \approx 2.14 \times 10^{25} \, \text{vacancies/m}^3}$$

This is an enormous number — for every **10,000 atoms**, roughly **3** vacancies exist in copper just below its melting point.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Differentiate between Schottky and Frenkel defects in ionic crystals. Mention two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Schottky Defect | Frenkel Defect |
|---------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | Pair of cation + anion **vacancies** | Host atom displaced to **interstitial** + vacancy created |
| **Mass conservation** | Density **decreases** | Density **almost unchanged** |
| **Size requirement** | Cation and anion of **comparable size** | Cation **much smaller** than anion |
| **Coordination** | High coordination crystals | Lower coordination, open structure |
| **Examples** | **NaCl, KCl, CsCl** | **AgCl, AgBr, ZnO** |

**(2 marks for table + 1 mark for examples)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: Define Burgers vector. What is its significance in edge and screw dislocations?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Burgers vector ($\vec{b}$)** is the vector that measures the magnitude and direction of the lattice distortion caused by a dislocation. It is determined by constructing a **Burgers circuit** — a closed loop of atom-to-atom steps around the dislocation in the perfect crystal and noting the closure failure in the defective crystal.

- **Edge Dislocation:** $\vec{b}$ is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line.
- **Screw Dislocation:** $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to the dislocation line.
- **Significance:** $\vec{b}$ quantifies the slip produced when a dislocation glides; it determines the energy of the dislocation per unit length ($E/L \propto G b^2$).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Derive the expression for the equilibrium concentration of vacancies in a metallic crystal. Discuss how the vacancy concentration varies with temperature.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**(i) System definition:** A perfect crystal with **N** lattice sites. Suppose **$n_v$** atoms are removed from bulk positions and placed on the surface, leaving **$n_v$** vacancies.

**(ii) Enthalpy of formation:**
Enthalpy cost = $n_v \cdot Q_v$, where $Q_v$ is the energy required to form one vacancy.

**(iii) Configurational entropy:**
Number of ways of arranging $n_v$ vacancies on $N$ sites:

$$W = \frac{N!}{n_v! (N - n_v)!}$$

$$\Delta S = k_B \ln W = k_B \ln \left[ \frac{N!}{n_v! (N - n_v)!} \right]$$

**(iv) Apply Stirling's approximation** ($\ln x! \approx x \ln x - x$):

$$\Delta S = k_B [N \ln N - n_v \ln n_v - (N - n_v) \ln (N - n_v)]$$

**(v) Gibbs free energy:**

$$\Delta G = n_v Q_v - k_B T [N \ln N - n_v \ln n_v - (N - n_v) \ln (N - n_v)]$$

**(vi) Equilibrium condition** $\frac{d(\Delta G)}{d n_v} = 0$:

$$Q_v = k_B T \ln \left( \frac{N - n_v}{n_v} \right)$$

**(vii) Final expression** (using $N \gg n_v$):

$$\boxed{\frac{n_v}{N} = \exp \left( -\frac{Q_v}{k_B T} \right) = \exp \left( -\frac{\Delta H_v}{k_B T} \right)}$$

**(viii) Temperature variation:**
- As **T** increases, the exponential term rises rapidly.
- Near the melting point $T_m$, the vacancy fraction is typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- At room temperature (T ≈ 300 K), the vacancy fraction drops to $\sim 10^{-12}$ (essentially negligible).
- The number of vacancies **increases exponentially** with temperature.

**(Distribution: 1 mark step (i), 1 mark (ii), 2 marks (iii)–(iv), 1 mark (v)–(vi), 1 mark final equation, 1 mark discussion]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: The energy of formation of a vacancy in copper is 86,900 J/mol. Calculate the equilibrium number of vacancies per cubic meter at 500 °C. Given: $\rho_{Cu} = 8.4 \times 10^3$ kg/m³, $A_{Cu} = 63.5$ g/mol, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert temperature: $T = 500 + 273 = 773$ K.

**Step 2:** Number of atomic sites per m³:

$$N = \frac{N_A \cdot \rho}{A} = \frac{(6.022 \times 10^{23}) \cdot (8.4 \times 10^3)}{63.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.96 \times 10^{28} \text{ sites/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Vacancy fraction:

$$\frac{n_v}{N} = \exp \left( -\frac{Q_v}{RT} \right) = \exp \left( -\frac{86{,}900}{8.314 \times 773} \right) = \exp(-13.53) = 1.35 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4:** Equilibrium vacancies per m³:

$$n_v = (7.96 \times 10^{28}) \cdot (1.35 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.07 \times 10^{23} \text{ vacancies/m}^3$$

**Conclusion:** At 500 °C, copper contains approximately **$1.07 \times 10^{23}$ vacancies per m³** in thermal equilibrium.

**(Distribution: 1 mark T conversion, 2 marks N, 2 marks exponential evaluation, 2 marks final answer]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With neat sketches, explain edge and screw dislocations. Define the Burgers vector and discuss its relationship to each type.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Edge Dislocation:**
An extra half-plane of atoms is inserted into the crystal. The bottom edge of this extra half-plane is the **dislocation line**, denoted by the symbol **⊥**.

- **Geometric description:** It can be visualized as a page torn halfway down; the extra half-page represents the extra half-plane.
- **Burgers vector $\vec{b}$:** Perpendicular to the dislocation line. For an edge dislocation in a simple cubic lattice, $|\vec{b}| = a$ (lattice parameter).
- **Slip plane:** Defined by $\vec{b}$ and the line direction.

**(ii) Screw Dislocation:**
Formed by shearing the upper portion of the crystal by one atomic spacing relative to the lower portion.

- **Geometric description:** The crystal becomes one continuous spiral ramp of atomic planes.
- **Burgers vector $\vec{b}$:** Parallel to the dislocation line.
- **Slip plane:** Theoretically infinite, but in practice limited to active slip systems.

**(iii) Burgers vector and Burgers circuit:**
Construct an **equal number of atom-to-atom steps** in a closed loop around the dislocation in the **defective crystal** (call it the FS/RH circuit). Repeat the same circuit in a **perfect reference crystal**. The vector that closes the gap in the perfect crystal is the **Burgers vector $\vec{b}$**.

| Dislocation Type | Line Direction | $\vec{b}$ Orientation | Relative Angle |
|------------------|---------------|-----------------------|----------------|
| Edge | $\vec{u}$ | $\vec{b} \perp \vec{u}$ | 90° |
| Screw | $\vec{u}$ | $\vec{b} \parallel \vec{u}$ | 0° |
| Mixed | $\vec{u}$ | $\vec{b}$ at any intermediate angle | 0° < θ < 90° |

**(Distribution: 2 marks sketches, 2 marks definitions, 2 marks Burgers circuit, 1 mark comparison]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Discuss the various types of point defects with examples. Explain how Frenkel defects are formed in ionic crystals and derive the equation for their equilibrium concentration.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Types of point defects:**

1. **Vacancy:** Missing atom from a lattice site (e.g., in Cu, Al, Ni).
2. **Self-interstitial:** Host atom located on an interstitial site (rare, e.g., H in Pd).
3. **Substitutional impurity:** Foreign atom of similar size replaces a host atom (e.g., Zn in Cu → brass).
4. **Interstitial impurity:** Small foreign atom in interstitial site (e.g., C in Fe → steel).
5. **Frenkel defect:** A host ion displaced from a lattice site to an interstitial site.
6. **Schottky defect:** A pair of cation and anion vacancies to preserve charge neutrality.

**(ii) Frenkel defect formation:**
A cation (smaller ion) leaves its regular lattice site and migrates to a nearby interstitial void. The original site becomes a vacancy; the new position is occupied by the displaced ion. Net charge: zero (vacancy charge is balanced by interstitial charge).

**Common in:** **AgCl, AgBr, ZnO, CaF₂** (compounds with large anions and small cations).

**(iii) Derivation:**
Let:
- $N$ = number of lattice sites
- $N_i$ = number of interstitial sites
- $n_f$ = number of Frenkel pairs
- $Q_f$ = energy to form one Frenkel pair

Combinatorial factor:

$$W = \frac{N!}{n_f! (N - n_f)!} \cdot \frac{N_i!}{n_f! (N_i - n_f)!}$$

Free energy:

$$\Delta G = n_f Q_f - k_B T \ln W$$

Minimize: $\frac{d(\Delta G)}{d n_f} = 0$:

$$Q_f = k_B T \ln \left( \frac{N \cdot N_i}{n_f^2} \right)$$

Final result:

$$\boxed{\frac{n_f}{\sqrt{N \cdot N_i}} = \exp \left( -\frac{\Delta H_f}{2 k_B T} \right)}$$

**(Distribution: 2 marks defect types + examples, 2 marks Frenkel formation, 3 marks derivation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the denominator** for Schottky and Frenkel defect expressions. Examiners deduct 2 full marks for this.
> 2. **Using $\Delta H$ instead of $Q$** (or vice versa) inconsistently — always use a single symbol and define it.
> 3. **Stirling's approximation must be explicitly stated** — skipping this step loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing $k_B$ and $R$**: Use $k_B$ (J/atom·K) with energy per atom (eV or J/atom); use $R$ (J/mol·K) with energy per mole (J/mol).
> 5. **No unit clarity**: Always state the final unit of $n_v$ (vacancies/m³) clearly.
> 6. **Edge vs Screw confusion**: Many students incorrectly state $\vec{b}$ is parallel to the line for edge dislocations. Use the table method to recall.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Crystal defects** are deviations from perfect periodicity; they are thermodynamically favored at $T > 0$ K.
- [x] **Classification by dimension:** 0-D (point), 1-D (line), 2-D (surface), 3-D (volume).
- [x] **Vacancy equation:** $\dfrac{n_v}{N} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_v}{k_B T}\right)$ — metallic crystals.
- [x] **Schottky equation:** $\dfrac{n_s}{N} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_s}{2 k_B T}\right)$ — ionic crystals, vacancy pair, density decreases.
- [x] **Frenkel equation:** $\dfrac{n_f}{\sqrt{N \cdot N_i}} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_f}{2 k_B T}\right)$ — vacancy + interstitial, density unchanged.
- [x] **Dislocations:** Edge ($\vec{b} \perp$ line), Screw ($\vec{b} \parallel$ line), Mixed (intermediate).
- [x] **Burgers vector $\vec{b}$** is the slip vector; its magnitude is typically one lattice parameter **$a$**.
- [x] **Dislocation density $\rho_d$** is measured in **m/m³ (or m⁻²)**; values range from $10^6$ (annealed) to $10^{12}$ m⁻² (heavily deformed).
- [x] **Stirling's approximation:** $\ln x! \approx x \ln x - x$ is the key to derivations.
- [x] **Schottky dominant** in NaCl, KCl, CsCl; **Frenkel dominant** in AgCl, AgBr, ZnO.
- [x] **Surface defects** include grain boundaries, twin boundaries, and stacking faults.
- [x] **Volume defects** include precipitates, voids, and inclusions.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** Doping (semiconductors), alloying (brass, steel), age hardening (Al-Cu alloys), and ionic conductivity (ceramic electrolytes).
- [x] **Defects increase exponentially with temperature**; near $T_m$, vacancy fraction $\sim 10^{-4}$.
- [x] **Mass transport (diffusion)** in solids is fundamentally defect-mediated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Crystal Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Determination of Crystal Structure ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Determination of Crystal Structure** refers to the experimental and analytical techniques used to identify the **arrangement of atoms, ions, or molecules** within a crystalline solid. This includes determining **lattice parameters**, **symmetry (space group)**, **basis atoms**, and the **interplanar spacing (d-spacing)** of crystallographic planes. The two principal experimental pillars of crystal structure determination in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: 1. **X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)** — Primary, non-destructive technique. 2. **Bragg's Law & Laue Equations** — Theoretical foundation linking atomic spacing to diffraction angles. 3. **Powder / Single-Crystal / Rotating Crystal Methods** — Practical experimental geometries. 4. **Structure Factor Analysis** — To identify which atoms actually occupy which lattice positions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Ripple Tank & Pegs" Model:** > Imagine a **square grid of pegs** nailed to the bottom of a shallow pond. When you drop a single pebble, ripples spread in **concentric circles**. If instead you drop many pebbles simultaneously at regular spacing, the ripples interfere. Where crest-meets-crest, you get **amplified ripples**; where crest-meets-trough, they **cancel out**. > > In a crystal, **atoms act as the pegs**, and an **incident X-ray beam acts as the ripples**. Detectors placed at specific angles catch the **amplified (constructively interfered) beams**. From the angles at which amplification occurs, we can mathematically back-calculate the spacing between the "pegs" (atoms). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** | | Planck's constant | $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** | | X-ray wavelength range | $\lambda$ | **0.5 Å – 2.5 Å** (Cu-K$\alpha$ = **1.5406 Å**) | | Interplanar spacing | $d_{hkl}$ | **Ångström (Å)** or **nm** | | Bragg angle | $\theta$ | **degrees** | | Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **$6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 outcomes (CO1) explicitly require students to **derive Bragg's law**, apply **Laue equations**, and solve problems on **structure factor** for various cubic crystal systems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Do Crystals Diffract X-Rays? X-rays are **electromagnetic waves** with wavelengths on the order of **atomic spacings (~1 Å)**. According to **von Laue's condition**, a crystal acts as a **3D diffraction grating** because the regular periodic arrangement of atoms provides the necessary spatial periodicity for wave interference. For **constructive interference** (a detectable diffracted beam), the path difference between waves scattered from successive lattice planes must equal an **integer multiple of the wavelength** — this is the essence of **Bragg's Law**. ### 2.2 Operational Logic Steps - **Step 1 — Illumination:** A monochromatic X-ray beam of known wavelength $\lambda$ strikes the crystal at a variable angle $\theta$. - **Step 2 — Scattering:** Each atom in the lattice **scatters** (re-emits) the X-rays in all directions (Thomson scattering by electrons). - **Step 3 — Interference:** Waves from different lattice planes **interfere**. Most directions → destructive interference (waves cancel). - **Step 4 — Detection:** At specific angles satisfying Bragg's condition, **constructive interference** produces a strong reflected beam, recorded as a **peak** in the diffraction pattern. - **Step 5 — Indexing:** Each peak is labeled with Miller indices $(hkl)$ corresponding to the reflecting plane family. - **Step 6 — Refinement:** Peak positions + intensities → lattice parameters + atomic positions. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **high-yield** and frequently appear in KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) and Series Tests. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$ | **Bragg's Law** | $\lambda$, $d_{hkl}$ in Å, $\theta$ in ° | | 2 | $\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Simplified Bragg's Law (n=1, first order) | Å | | 3 | $2 d_{hkl}\sin\theta = n\lambda$ | General Bragg's Law | — | | 4 | $a = d_{hkl}\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ | Cubic lattice: $d_{hkl}$ relation | Å | | 5 | $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4a^2}(h^2+k^2+l^2)$ | Combined Bragg + cubic relation | dimensionless | | 6 | $\sin^2\theta \propto (h^2+k^2+l^2)$ | Ratio method for indexing | dimensionless | | 7 | $S_{hkl} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j e^{2\pi i (hx_j + ky_j + lz_j)}$ | **Structure Factor** | electron units | | 8 | $F_{hkl} = f \cdot F^{*}$ | Structure factor with atomic form factor $f$ | — | | 9 | $I_{hkl} \propto \vert F_{hkl}\vert^2$ | **Intensity** of diffraction peak | counts/sec | | 10 | $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{h^2+k^2}{a^2}+\dfrac{l^2}{c^2}$ | Tetragonal system | 1/Å² | | 11 | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{n\lambda}{2d_{hkl}}$ | Rearranged Bragg | — | | 12 | $a = \dfrac{\lambda\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}{2\sin\theta}$ | Lattice parameter from single peak | Å | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** Always use $\vert F_{hkl}\vert^2$ (NOT $\vert F_{hkl}\vert$) for intensity calculations. Many KTU students lose 1–2 marks here. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceuticals:** Identifying polymorphic forms of drug molecules (e.g., crystalline vs. amorphous paracetamol). - **Metallurgy:** Phase identification in alloys (e.g., $\alpha$-Fe vs. $\gamma$-Fe). - **Semiconductor Industry:** Verifying epitaxial layer quality in Si/Ge wafers. - **Forensics:** Identification of unknown minerals at crime scenes. - **Ceramics:** Quality control of alumina, zirconia in cutting tools and biomedical implants. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Bragg's Law (From First Principles) **Setup:** Consider two parallel atomic planes in a crystal, separated by a perpendicular distance $d_{hkl}$. A monochromatic X-ray beam of wavelength $\lambda$ strikes these planes at a glancing angle $\theta$. ``` Incident rays ──► •─────────• (Plane 1) │\ ╱│ │ \ ╱ │ │ \ ╱ │ ← d (interplanar spacing) │ \ ╱ │ │ • │ (Plane 2) ``` **Path Difference Calculation:** Let the X-rays strike the upper plane at points $A$ and $B$, and the lower plane at point $C$ (directly below $A$). Let the reflected ray from $B$ meet the reflected ray from $A$ at point $D$. - The ray $AC$ travels through the crystal and reflects from the lower plane. - The ray $BD$ reflects from the upper plane. - The total **extra path** traveled by the lower ray (compared to the upper ray) is the segment $ABC$ minus the segment... wait, let's be precise. **Step 1 — Identify the geometry.** Draw a perpendicular from $A$ to the lower plane's incident ray, meeting it at point $E$. Draw another perpendicular from $C$ to the upper plane's reflected ray, meeting it at point $F$. **Step 2 — Calculate the extra path of the lower ray.** The lower ray travels: - From the source to $C$ (instead of $A$) → extra path = $EC$ - After reflection, from $C$ to the detector (instead of $A$) → extra path = $CF$ Total extra path = $EC + CF$ **Step 3 — Apply trigonometry.** In the right triangle formed by $A$, $E$, and $C$: - The hypotenuse $AC = d_{hkl} / \sin\theta$ - The perpendicular side $EC = AC \cdot \sin\theta = d_{hkl}$ Wait, more carefully: the angle of incidence at plane 2 equals $\theta$. Construct perpendiculars from $A$ to the incident ray and reflected ray meeting plane 2. The triangles are congruent and right-angled at $E$ and $F$: $$EC = CF = d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$ **Step 4 — Sum the extra path.** $$\Delta = EC + CF = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$ **Step 5 — Apply constructive interference condition.** For constructive interference, the path difference must equal an integer multiple of $\lambda$: $$\Delta = n\lambda$$ **Step 6 — Combine to obtain Bragg's Law.** $$\boxed{n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta}$$ where: - $n$ = order of diffraction (1, 2, 3, …) - $\lambda$ = wavelength of X-rays - $d_{hkl}$ = interplanar spacing of $(hkl)$ planes - $\theta$ = Bragg angle (glancing angle) --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Structure Factor for BCC Lattice > [!NOTE] > This is a **must-do derivation** for KTU ESE — at least one structure factor problem appears every semester. **Given:** A Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) lattice with atoms at: - Corner: $(0, 0, 0)$ - Body center: $\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ **Step 1 — Write the general structure factor formula.** $$S_{hkl} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j \cdot e^{2\pi i (hx_j + ky_j + lz_j)}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute coordinates of the two atoms.** For BCC, there are 2 atoms per unit cell: $$S_{hkl} = f \cdot e^{2\pi i (0)} + f \cdot e^{2\pi i \left(\frac{h}{2} + \frac{k}{2} + \frac{l}{2}\right)}$$ $$S_{hkl} = f \left[1 + e^{\pi i (h+k+l)}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Apply Euler's identity: $e^{\pi i n} = (-1)^n$ where $n$ is an integer.** $$S_{hkl} = f \left[1 + (-1)^{h+k+l}\right]$$ **Step 4 — Analyze two cases.** **Case 1:** When $h + k + l$ is **even**: $$S_{hkl} = f [1 + 1] = 2f \neq 0 \quad \text{(Allowed reflection)}$$ **Case 2:** When $h + k + l$ is **odd**: $$S_{hkl} = f [1 - 1] = 0 \quad \text{(Forbidden reflection — extinction)}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion (KTU answer-expected):** > For BCC, diffraction occurs **only** when $(h+k+l) = $ even. Examples: (110), (200), (211), (220), … Forbidden: (100), (111), (210), … The intensity is: $$I_{hkl} \propto \vert S_{hkl}\vert^2 = 4f^2 \quad \text{(for allowed reflections)}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Structure Factor for FCC Lattice **Given:** FCC atoms at: - $(0, 0, 0)$ - $\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, 0\right)$, $\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0, \frac{1}{2}\right)$, $\left(0, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ **Step 1 — Substitute into structure factor.** $$S_{hkl} = f \left[1 + e^{\pi i (h+k)} + e^{\pi i (h+l)} + e^{\pi i (k+l)}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Use $(-1)^{h+k}$ etc.** $$S_{hkl} = f \left[1 + (-1)^{h+k} + (-1)^{h+l} + (-1)^{k+l}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Selection rule analysis.** $h, k, l$ must be **all odd or all even** (unmixed indices). | $h$ | $k$ | $l$ | Type | Result | |-----|-----|-----|------|--------| | Even | Even | Even | Allowed | $S = 4f$ | | Odd | Odd | Odd | Allowed | $S = 4f$ | | Mixed | — | — | Forbidden | $S = 0$ | **Step 4 — Conclusion:** For FCC, **mixed indices are forbidden**. First allowed reflections: (111), (200), (220), (311), … --- ### 3.4 Laue Equations (Brief Derivation) For diffraction in a 3D crystal, three simultaneous conditions must be satisfied (one per axis): $$a(\cos\alpha - \cos\alpha_0) = h\lambda$$ $$b(\cos\beta - \cos\beta_0) = k\lambda$$ $$c(\cos\gamma - \cos\gamma_0) = l\lambda$$ where $(\alpha_0, \beta_0, \gamma_0)$ = angles of incident beam with crystal axes, and $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ = angles of diffracted beam. These are equivalent to Bragg's law for single-crystal diffraction. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Table | Crystal System | Atoms Basis | Selection Rule | First Few Allowed (hkl) | |---|---|---|---| | **SC** | (0,0,0) | All $(hkl)$ allowed | (100), (110), (111), (200)… | | **BCC** | (0,0,0) + (½,½,½) | $h+k+l = $ even | (110), (200), (211), (220)… | | **FCC** | 4 atoms at face centers | $h,k,l$ all odd or all even | (111), (200), (220), (311)… | | **Diamond** | FCC + (¼,¼,¼) | FCC rule + all even (with $h+k+l = 4n$) | (111), (220), (311), (400)… | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Bragg's Diffraction Geometry (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR sourceA["X-Ray Source"] -->|"Incident beam
wavelength lambda"| crystalA["Crystal Planes
spacing d_hkl"] crystalA -->|"Reflected beam
angle theta"| detectorA["Detector
peak intensity"] sourceA -.->|"Angle theta"| crystalA crystalA -.->|"Angle theta"| detectorA style sourceA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style crystalA fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style detectorA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: XRD System Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step 1: Sample Preparation
Powder or single crystal mounted"] --> B["Step 2: X-Ray Generation
Cu-K alpha target, lambda equals 1.5406 Angstrom"] B --> C["Step 3: Collimation
Parallel beam via slits"] C --> D["Step 4: Crystal Interaction
Diffraction at Bragg angle theta"] D --> E{"Step 5: Constructive
Interference?"} E -- Yes --> F["Step 6: Detection
Strong peak recorded"] E -- No --> G["Step 6: Cancellation
No peak detected"] F --> H["Step 7: Data Processing
Convert 2-theta to d-spacing"] H --> I["Step 8: Indexing
Assign hkl Miller indices"] I --> J["Step 9: Structure Solution
Lattice parameter and space group"] G --> K["Step 7: Continue Scan
Vary theta"] K --> E style A fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style I fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style J fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style K fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.3 Powder Diffraction Method — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Polycrystalline Sample
Millions of randomly oriented grains"] --> P2["Incident X-Ray Beam
Monochromatic"] P2 --> P3["Cone of Diffracted Beams
Each cone corresponds to one hkl"] P3 --> P4["Film or Detector Strip
Arcs or Debye-Scherrer rings"] P4 --> P5["Peak Position 2 theta
Yields d-spacing"] P4 --> P6["Peak Intensity
Yields structure factor"] style P1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.4 Comparison: Experimental Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD M["X-Ray Diffraction Methods"] --> M1["Laue Method
Stationary single crystal, white X-ray"] M --> M2["Rotating Crystal Method
Single crystal rotated, monochromatic X-ray"] M --> M3["Powder Method
Polycrystalline sample, monochromatic X-ray"] M1 --> M1a["Use: Crystal orientation and symmetry"] M2 --> M2a["Use: Unit cell parameters"] M3 --> M3a["Use: Phase identification, lattice parameter"] style M fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style M1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style M2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style M3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style M1a fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style M2a fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style M3a fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > State and explain **Bragg's law** of X-ray diffraction. Mention the significance of each term. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Statement:** Bragg's law states that for a crystal to produce a diffracted X-ray beam of wavelength $\lambda$, the following condition must be satisfied: > > $$n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$ > > **Significance of terms:** > - $n$ = order of diffraction (integer: 1, 2, 3, …) > - $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident X-ray beam (Å) > - $d_{hkl}$ = interplanar spacing of planes with Miller indices $(hkl)$ (Å) > - $\theta$ = glancing angle (Bragg angle), measured between incident beam and crystal planes (degrees) > > Bragg's law provides the fundamental condition for **constructive interference** from parallel atomic planes, enabling calculation of $d$-spacings and identification of crystal structure. **(3 marks)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > Define **structure factor**. Write the structure factor for a BCC crystal and state the condition for allowed reflections. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition:** The structure factor $S_{hkl}$ (or $F_{hkl}$) is a mathematical quantity that determines the **amplitude and phase** of X-rays scattered by all atoms in the unit cell. It is defined as: > > $$S_{hkl} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j \cdot e^{2\pi i (hx_j + ky_j + lz_j)}$$ > > **For BCC** (atoms at $(0,0,0)$ and $(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2})$): > > $$S_{hkl} = f \left[1 + (-1)^{h+k+l}\right]$$ > > **Selection rule:** Diffraction occurs **only when** $h+k+l = $ **even integer**. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 marks]** > > **(a) [7 marks]** Derive Bragg's law for X-ray diffraction in crystals. Discuss how Bragg's law helps in determining the crystal structure of a material. > > **(b) [7 marks]** X-rays of wavelength $1.54$ Å are diffracted from the (110) planes of a BCC iron crystal. Calculate the interplanar spacing and the lattice parameter. Given: the Bragg angle for first-order diffraction is $\theta = 22.0°$. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Bragg's Law [7 marks]:** Consider two parallel atomic planes separated by a distance $d_{hkl}$ as shown in the schematic. A monochromatic X-ray beam of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident at a glancing angle $\theta$. When the X-rays strike the first plane, they are partially reflected. The remaining portion penetrates to the second plane and is reflected from there. The path difference between rays reflected from successive planes is: $$\Delta = AB + BC = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$ where $AB$ and $BC$ are the extra geometric paths traversed by the lower ray (geometric construction via perpendiculars from the upper plane's reflection point). For **constructive interference**, the path difference must equal an integer multiple of the wavelength: $$2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta = n\lambda$$ This is **Bragg's Law**. **Significance in crystal structure determination [extra credit, included within 7 marks]:** - By measuring $\theta$ for known $\lambda$, $d_{hkl}$ can be calculated. - Different $(hkl)$ planes give different $\theta$ values → peak positions in XRD pattern. - The set of $d_{hkl}$ values uniquely identifies the crystal system. - Intensities give information on atomic positions via structure factor. **(7 marks — full marks breakdown: 4 marks derivation + 3 marks significance)** --- **(b) Numerical Problem [7 marks]:** **Given:** - $\lambda = 1.54$ Å - Plane: $(hkl) = (1,1,0)$ - Bragg angle: $\theta = 22.0°$ (first order, $n = 1$) **Step 1 — Apply Bragg's Law to find $d_{110}$.** $$\lambda = 2 d_{110} \sin\theta$$ $$d_{110} = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin\theta}$$ $$d_{110} = \frac{1.54}{2 \times \sin(22.0°)}$$ $$d_{110} = \frac{1.54}{2 \times 0.3746}$$ $$d_{110} = \frac{1.54}{0.7492}$$ $$\boxed{d_{110} = 2.055 \text{ Å}}$$ **Step 2 — Use cubic lattice relation to find lattice parameter $a$.** For a cubic system: $$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$ $$a = d_{hkl} \cdot \sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}$$ For $(110)$: $h^2 + k^2 + l^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 = 2$ $$a = 2.055 \times \sqrt{2}$$ $$a = 2.055 \times 1.4142$$ $$\boxed{a = 2.906 \text{ Å}}$$ **Step 3 — Verification (optional, recommended for full marks):** This value matches the known lattice parameter of α-iron ($a_{\alpha-Fe} = 2.866$ Å, close to our calculated value with slight experimental error from $\theta$ rounding). **(7 marks)** --- > **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module 1, 14 marks]** > > **(a) [7 marks]** What is the **structure factor**? Derive the structure factor for an FCC unit cell and deduce the conditions for allowed reflections. > > **(b) [7 marks]** The first three peaks in an X-ray powder diffraction pattern of an unknown cubic crystal occur at $2\theta = 38.5°$, $44.6°$, and $65.0°$ using Cu-K$\alpha$ radiation ($\lambda = 1.5406$ Å). Identify the crystal type (SC, BCC, or FCC). --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Structure Factor and FCC Derivation [7 marks]:** **Definition [1 mark]:** The structure factor $S_{hkl}$ for a unit cell describes the combined effect of all atoms on the amplitude of the X-ray beam diffracted by planes $(hkl)$. It is defined as: $$S_{hkl} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j \cdot e^{2\pi i (hx_j + ky_j + lz_j)}$$ **FCC basis [1 mark]:** The FCC unit cell has 4 atoms at: - $(0, 0, 0)$ - $\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, 0\right)$ - $\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ - $\left(0, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ **Substitution [2 marks]:** $$S_{hkl} = f\left[1 + e^{\pi i(h+k)} + e^{\pi i(h+l)} + e^{\pi i(k+l)}\right]$$ $$S_{hkl} = f\left[1 + (-1)^{h+k} + (-1)^{h+l} + (-1)^{k+l}\right]$$ **Case analysis [2 marks]:** | $h$ | $k$ | $l$ | $(-1)^{h+k}$ | $(-1)^{h+l}$ | $(-1)^{k+l}$ | Sum | |-----|-----|-----|---|---|---|---| | All even | | | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | $4f$ | | All odd | | | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | $4f$ | | Mixed | | | $\pm 1$ | mixed | mixed | $0$ | **Selection Rule [1 mark]:** For FCC, allowed reflections occur when **$h$, $k$, $l$ are all odd or all even** (unmixed indices). First few allowed peaks: (111), (200), (220), (311), … **(Total: 7 marks)** --- **(b) Identification of Crystal Type [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Compute $\sin^2\theta$ for each peak.** For $2\theta_1 = 38.5°$: $\theta_1 = 19.25°$, $\sin\theta_1 = 0.3294$ $\sin^2\theta_1 = 0.1085$ For $2\theta_2 = 44.6°$: $\theta_2 = 22.3°$, $\sin\theta_2 = 0.3794$ $\sin^2\theta_2 = 0.1439$ For $2\theta_3 = 65.0°$: $\theta_3 = 32.5°$, $\sin\theta_3 = 0.5373$ $\sin^2\theta_3 = 0.2887$ **Step 2 — Compute ratios relative to smallest peak (normalize).** $$\sin^2\theta_1 : \sin^2\theta_2 : \sin^2\theta_3$$ $$= 0.1085 : 0.1439 : 0.2887$$ Divide by smallest (0.1085): $$= 1.000 : 1.327 : 2.661$$ Multiply by a common factor to get integers (multiply by 3): $$= 3 : 4 : 8$$ **Step 3 — Match with selection rules.** | Crystal Type | Sequence of $(h^2+k^2+l^2)$ | First 3 Ratios | |---|---|---| | **SC** | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, … | 1 : 2 : 3 | | **BCC** | 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, … | 1 : 2 : 3 (after /2) | | **FCC** | 3, 4, 8, 11, 12, 16, … | 3 : 4 : 8 | Our observed ratio **3 : 4 : 8** matches **FCC** exactly! **Step 4 — Conclusion [1 mark]:** > The unknown crystal is **Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)** with the first three allowed reflections being (111), (200), and (220). **(Total: 7 marks)** --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:** > 1. **Confusing glancing angle $\theta$ with diffraction angle $2\theta$:** In powder XRD, the detector records $2\theta$, but Bragg's law uses $\theta$ = half of that. Forgetting to divide by 2 = **wrong answer**. > 2. **Mixing up SC vs BCC indexing:** In SC, all $(hkl)$ are allowed. In BCC, only $h+k+l=$ even. Always re-derive for the lattice in question. > 3. **Forgetting the structure factor for diamond cubic:** Diamond has an additional atom at $(\tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{4})$ with selection rule **$h,k,l$ all same parity AND $h+k+l = 4n$**. > 4. **Not converting units:** $\lambda$ and $d$ must be in the **same units** (typically Å). > 5. **Squaring absolute values:** Intensity $I \propto \vert S_{hkl}\vert^2$, NOT $\vert S_{hkl}\vert$. > 6. **Ignoring order $n$:** Always check whether the problem specifies first-order ($n=1$) or higher. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Fire Recall — Crystal Structure Determination** - ✅ **Bragg's Law:** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ — the foundational equation. - ✅ **X-ray source:** Cu-K$\alpha$ ($\lambda = 1.5406$ Å) is the most common laboratory wavelength. - ✅ **Structure factor** = sum over all atoms in the unit cell of $f_j e^{2\pi i (hx_j+ky_j+lz_j)}$. - ✅ **Intensity** $\propto \vert S_{hkl}\vert^2$. - ✅ **SC** allows all $(hkl)$ reflections. - ✅ **BCC** allows only $h+k+l = $ even. - ✅ **FCC** allows only all-odd or all-even (unmixed) indices. - ✅ **Diamond cubic** (Si, Ge): FCC rule + $h+k+l = 4n$. - ✅ **Laue equations** are 3 simultaneous conditions (one per axis) — equivalent to Bragg's law. - ✅ **Powder method** gives $d$-spacings from peak positions $2\theta$. - ✅ **Indexing** of XRD pattern involves ratio analysis of $\sin^2\theta$. - ✅ **Lattice parameter from cubic $d_{hkl}$:** $a = d_{hkl}\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$. - ✅ **Rotating crystal method** is used for single-crystal unit cell determination. - ✅ **Laue method** is used for crystal orientation/symmetry (uses white X-ray, stationary crystal). - ✅ **Remember** to use **$\sin\theta$, NOT $\sin(2\theta)$** in Bragg's law. - ✅ Always carry units (Å) and keep $\lambda, d$ in the **same unit system**.

topics.title: X-ray Diffraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** X-ray Diffraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is X-ray Diffraction (XRD)?

**X-ray Diffraction (XRD)** is a non-destructive analytical technique used in materials science to probe the atomic and molecular structure of a crystalline material. By directing incident X-rays onto a sample and measuring the angles and intensities of the diffracted beams, the technique produces a fingerprint of the crystal's internal atomic arrangement, including lattice parameters, phase identification, grain size, and crystal orientation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** X-ray diffraction is the phenomenon wherein a beam of X-rays, when incident on a regularly spaced atomic lattice, undergoes constructive interference (and destructive interference) in specific directions governed by the geometric relationship between the X-ray wavelength ($\lambda$), the interplanar spacing ($d$), and the angle of incidence ($\theta$). This is the fundamental basis for determining crystal structure.

## 1.2 Nature and Production of X-rays

**X-rays** are a form of **electromagnetic radiation** with wavelengths ranging from approximately **0.01 nm to 10 nm** (0.1 Å to 100 Å). For diffraction experiments on crystals, we typically use X-rays with $\lambda \approx 0.5$ to $2.5$ Å, which is comparable to interatomic spacings in solids.

**Production of X-rays (Coolidge Tube):**
1. A tungsten filament (cathode) is heated to emit electrons via thermionic emission.
2. These electrons are accelerated toward a metal target (anode, typically Cu, Mo, Co) by a high voltage ($V = 30$ to $50$ kV).
3. Upon impact, two types of X-ray spectra are produced:
   - **Continuous (White/Bremsstrahlung) Spectrum:** Produced by deceleration of electrons in the target's electric field. Wavelength cutoff is given by **Duane-Hunt Law**: $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$.
   - **Characteristic Spectrum:** Produced by electronic transitions between inner shells (K, L, M) of the target atoms. The most commonly used line is **Cu K$\alpha$** with $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why X-rays for crystals?** The wavelength of X-rays ($\sim$ 1 Å) is of the same order of magnitude as the interatomic spacing in crystals ($\sim$ 1–5 Å). This makes them ideal for producing diffraction patterns, just as visible light ($\lambda \sim$ 5000 Å) produces diffraction patterns from gratings with spacings of a few thousand angstroms.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Echo in a Parking Lot"
