Imagine standing in a large, perfectly organized parking lot with rows and columns of cars, each car being an "atom." If you clap your hands (send out a sound wave of a specific wavelength), the echo you receive will be different depending on the spacing of the cars:
- If your wavelength matches the row spacing, certain rows will reflect the sound in **phase** (constructively) — giving a strong echo.
- Other rows will reflect **out of phase** (destructively) — giving a weak echo.

Similarly, when X-rays (with a wavelength matching atomic spacing) hit a crystal, certain atomic planes will reflect the X-rays in phase, creating a strong diffraction peak. The angles at which this happens tell us the spacing of atomic planes, which in turn reveals the crystal structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bragg's Law — The Foundation of XRD

**William Henry Bragg** and **William Lawrence Bragg** (father and son) formulated the fundamental condition for X-ray diffraction from crystal planes in 1913. They treated crystal planes as semi-transparent mirrors that reflect X-rays.

### Geometric Construction

Consider two parallel atomic planes separated by a distance $d_{hkl}$ (interplanar spacing for Miller indices $hkl$). An incident X-ray beam (wavelength $\lambda$) strikes these planes at a glancing angle $\theta$.

- **Path 1:** Ray 1 strikes the top plane at point A and is reflected.
- **Path 2:** Ray 2 penetrates and is reflected from the second plane at point B.

The two reflected rays will be in phase (constructive interference) **only if** the path difference is an integral multiple of the wavelength $\lambda$.

**Path Difference** = $PB + BQ = 2d \sin\theta$

**Bragg's Condition:**
$$n\lambda = 2d_{hkl}\sin\theta$$

where:
- $n$ = Order of diffraction (integer: 1, 2, 3, ...)
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength of incident X-ray
- $d_{hkl}$ = Interplanar spacing
- $\theta$ = Glancing angle (Bragg angle)

## 2.2 Interplanar Spacing — The Cubic Crystal System

For a **cubic crystal system** with lattice parameter $a$:

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

For other systems:
- **Tetragonal:** $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{h^2 + k^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{l^2}{c^2}$
- **Orthorhombic:** $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{h^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{k^2}{b^2} + \dfrac{l^2}{c^2}$
- **Hexagonal:** $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{4}{3}\left(\dfrac{h^2 + hk + k^2}{a^2}\right) + \dfrac{l^2}{c^2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rules for Cubic Systems:**
> - **Simple Cubic (SC):** All $(hkl)$ reflections allowed. $h^2 + k^2 + l^2$ = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, ...
> - **Body-Centered Cubic (BCC):** $h + k + l$ = even. Allowed: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, ...
> - **Face-Centered Cubic (FCC):** $h, k, l$ all odd or all even. Allowed: 3, 4, 8, 11, 12, 16, 19, 20, ...

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Application** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Bragg's Law | Finding $d$ or $\lambda$ from diffraction angle |
| $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$ | Interplanar spacing (cubic) | Calculating plane spacing from lattice parameter |
| $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$ | Duane-Hunt Law | Shortest wavelength from X-ray tube |
| $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{12400}{V(\text{in V})}$ Å | Practical form of Duane-Hunt | Quick calculation of cutoff wavelength |
| $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | X-ray photon energy |
| $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4a^2}(h^2 + k^2 + l^2)$ | Combined Bragg-cubic | Indexing powder diffraction patterns |
| $N = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\lambda \cdot \Delta\theta}$ | Scherrer Equation (simplified) | Crystallite size estimation |

## 2.4 Laue's Equations (Direction of Diffracted Beams)

While Bragg's Law gives the condition for diffraction, **Laue's Equations** give the exact direction of the diffracted beam. For diffraction from a 3D lattice, three equations must be satisfied simultaneously:

$$a(\cos\alpha - \cos\alpha_0) = h\lambda$$
$$b(\cos\beta - \cos\beta_0) = k\lambda$$
$$c(\cos\gamma - \cos\gamma_0) = l\lambda$$

where $\alpha_0, \beta_0, \gamma_0$ are angles of incident beam with axes and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are angles of diffracted beam with axes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Bragg's Law sufficient?** Bragg's Law is a compact scalar form of the three Laue equations. The "reflection" from planes $(hkl)$ automatically satisfies the periodicity conditions of the 3D lattice.

## 2.5 Engineering Applications of XRD

1. **Phase Identification (Qualitative Analysis):** Each crystalline phase produces a unique diffraction "fingerprint."
2. **Lattice Parameter Determination:** Precise measurement of $\theta$ gives $a$.
3. **Residual Stress Measurement:** Peak shift analysis.
4. **Crystallite Size Estimation (Scherrer Equation):**
$$D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$$
where $D$ = crystallite size, $K$ = shape factor ($\approx 0.9$), $\beta$ = full width at half maximum (FWHM) in radians.
5. **Crystal Structure Determination:** SC, BCC, FCC, HCP identification.
6. **Texture and Orientation Analysis.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bragg's Law

**Setup:** Consider a set of parallel crystal planes with interplanar spacing $d$. Two parallel X-ray beams (wavelength $\lambda$) strike these planes at glancing angle $\theta$.

```
        Incident rays:    |  /
                          | / θ
        ─────────────────A──────────────  (Top plane)
                          \
                           \  θ
                            B
        ───────────────────────────────  (Second plane)
                           |
                           |
```

**Step 1:** Draw perpendiculars from the second plane's reflection point to the first ray's path.

Let the first ray strike the top plane at point A and the second ray strike the second plane at point B. Drop a perpendicular from B to ray 1, meeting it at P. Similarly, draw a perpendicular from A to ray 2, meeting at Q.

**Step 2:** Identify geometric relationships.

The angle of incidence = $\theta$, and the perpendicular distance between the two planes = $d$.

In the right triangle APB (formed at the first plane):
- The hypotenuse AB has length = $d / \sin\theta$
- Therefore, the path along the incident ray from P to B is = $d / \sin\theta$

Wait — let me redo this carefully:

In the right triangle formed by dropping a perpendicular from B to ray 1 (foot at P):
- The angle at A (between the plane and the perpendicular) = $\theta$
- The side opposite to $\theta$ = $PB$
- The side adjacent (the interplanar spacing) = $d$

Therefore:
$$PB = \frac{d}{\sin\theta}$$

By symmetry (same triangle on the other side):
$$BQ = \frac{d}{\sin\theta}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the total path difference.

$$\text{Path Difference} = PB + BQ = \frac{d}{\sin\theta} + \frac{d}{\sin\theta} = \frac{2d}{\sin\theta} \cdot \sin\theta$$

Wait, let me reconsider the geometry. The correct approach:

In the right triangle with hypotenuse along the second plane and angle $\theta$ at the point of reflection:
- Hypotenuse = $d / \sin\theta$
- This is the path along the plane from the foot of perpendicular on ray 1 to B.

The extra distance traveled by ray 2 compared to ray 1 = PB + BQ.

**Correct geometric derivation:**

Let the two rays be parallel. Ray 1 reflects from the top plane at A. Ray 2 reflects from the second plane at B. The path difference between rays 2 and 1 (extra distance traveled by ray 2) is:

Construct a perpendicular from A to ray 2 (before reflection), meeting at P. Construct a perpendicular from B to ray 1 (after reflection), meeting at Q. The wavefronts are perpendicular to the ray directions.

The path difference = (path from P to B) + (path from B to Q) minus the corresponding portion of ray 1.

**Clean derivation using right triangles:**

Consider the incident ray making angle $\theta$ with the top plane. The point of incidence on the second plane (B) is directly below A, separated by vertical distance $d$.

The horizontal distance from A to B = $\dfrac{d}{\tan\theta}$

For the incident ray to reach B, it travels an extra horizontal distance of $\dfrac{d}{\tan\theta}$ at angle $\theta$ to the planes. The extra path length on the incident side = $\dfrac{d/\tan\theta}{\cos(90° - \theta)} = \dfrac{d}{\sin\theta}$

Wait — simpler approach: The path difference is the difference in distance traveled perpendicular to the wavefront.

If the incident wavefront passes through A and the second ray must travel from this wavefront down to B and back up, then:

**Perpendicular distance from wavefront to B (going in)** = $d \sin\theta$
**Perpendicular distance from B to outgoing wavefront** = $d \sin\theta$
**Total extra path** = $2d \sin\theta$

This is the cleanest derivation. Let me formalize:

**Formal Derivation:**

Consider two parallel incident rays striking the parallel planes at glancing angle $\theta$.

The two rays are in phase at the wavefront. After reflection, the wave reflected from the lower plane must travel an extra distance equal to:

$$\Delta = MB + BN$$

where M is the foot of the perpendicular from the reflection point on the top plane (A) to the incident ray 2, and N is the foot of perpendicular from B to ray 1.

By geometry of reflection (angle of incidence = angle of reflection):

In triangle ABM (right-angled at M):
- The angle at A between the plane and the incident ray = $\theta$
- The side MB is opposite to angle $\theta$
- The side AB (= $d$) is the hypotenuse

$$\sin\theta = \frac{MB}{d} \implies MB = d\sin\theta$$

Similarly, in triangle ABN (right-angled at N):
$$BN = d\sin\theta$$

**Total Path Difference:**
$$\Delta = MB + BN = 2d\sin\theta$$

**Step 4:** Apply the constructive interference condition.

For constructive interference (reinforcement), the path difference must be an integral multiple of the wavelength:

$$\boxed{2d\sin\theta = n\lambda}$$

This is **Bragg's Law** (or the Bragg condition).

**Step 5:** Verification of dimensions and limits.

- $d$ has units of length (Å)
- $\sin\theta$ is dimensionless
- $n\lambda$ has units of length (Å)
- For real solutions, $\sin\theta \leq 1 \implies \lambda \leq 2d/n$, so X-ray wavelength must be less than twice the interplanar spacing.

## 3.2 Derivation of Interplanar Spacing for Cubic Systems

**Given:** A cubic crystal with lattice parameter $a$ and Miller indices $(hkl)$.

The plane $(hkl)$ can be expressed as:
$$\frac{x}{a/h} + \frac{y}{a/k} + \frac{z}{a/l} = 1$$

Intercepts: $\dfrac{a}{h}, \dfrac{a}{k}, \dfrac{a}{l}$

The interplanar spacing is the perpendicular distance from the origin to this plane:

**Step 1:** The general equation of a plane in 3D is:
$$\frac{x}{\alpha} + \frac{y}{\beta} + \frac{z}{\gamma} = 1$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are intercepts on the axes.

**Step 2:** The perpendicular distance from origin $(0,0,0)$ to this plane is:
$$d = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{\alpha^2} + \frac{1}{\beta^2} + \frac{1}{\gamma^2}}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\alpha = a/h, \beta = a/k, \gamma = a/l$:

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{h^2}{a^2} + \frac{k^2}{a^2} + \frac{l^2}{a^2}}}$$

$$\boxed{d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}}$$

**Step 4:** Combining with Bragg's Law:

$$n\lambda = 2 \cdot \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}} \cdot \sin\theta_{hkl}$$

$$\sin^2\theta_{hkl} = \frac{\lambda^2}{4a^2}(h^2 + k^2 + l^2)$$

This is the working equation for indexing powder diffraction patterns.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** X-rays of wavelength $\lambda = 1.5418$ Å are diffracted from the (111) planes of an FCC copper crystal. The lattice parameter of Cu is $a = 3.615$ Å. Calculate the Bragg angle $\theta$ for first-order diffraction.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate interplanar spacing.

$$d_{111} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}} = \frac{3.615}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2}}$$

$$d_{111} = \frac{3.615}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{3.615}{1.7321} = 2.0872 \text{ Å}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Bragg's Law.

$$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$

For $n = 1$ (first order):

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\lambda}{2d} = \frac{1.5418}{2 \times 2.0872} = \frac{1.5418}{4.1744} = 0.3694$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $\theta$.

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.3694) = 21.67°$$

**Step 4:** The diffraction peak appears at $2\theta = 43.34°$.

This matches experimental Cu K$\alpha$ peak for (111) at $2\theta \approx 43.3°$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This type of calculation is used daily in materials characterization labs. By measuring $\theta$ and knowing $\lambda$, we determine $d$ and hence $a$, the lattice parameter — a fundamental property of any crystalline material.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: X-ray Diffraction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Voltage Power Supply 30 to 50 kV] --> B[Electron Emission from Tungsten Cathode]
    B --> C[Accelerated Electrons Strike Cu or Mo Anode]
    C --> D[X ray Generation Continuous plus Characteristic]
    D --> E[Monochromator Filters Cu K alpha lambda 1.5406 A]
    E --> F[Collimated X ray Beam Directed to Sample Crystal]
    F --> G{Does Bragg Condition n lambda 2d sin theta hold}
    G -- Yes --> H[Constructive Interference Diffracted Beam Forms]
    G -- No --> I[Destructive Interference No Peak]
    H --> J[Detector Records Intensity versus 2 theta]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Data Analysis Lattice Parameter Phase ID]
    K --> L[Engineering Application Material Characterization]
```

## 4.2 Architecture of a Bragg's Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE
        X1[X ray Tube] --> X2[Filter K beta absorber]
    end
    subgraph SAMPLE
        X2 --> X3[Slit System Collimator]
        X3 --> X4[Rotating Crystal Sample]
    end
    subgraph DETECTOR
        X4 --> X5[Ionization Chamber or Scintillation Detector]
        X5 --> X6[Amplifier plus Counter]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        X6 --> X7[Plot of Intensity versus 2 theta]
    end
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: From Crystal to Diffraction Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Crystal with Lattice Parameter a] --> P2[Family of Planes hkl]
    P2 --> P3[Interplanar Spacing d equals a over sqrt h2 plus k2 plus l2]
    P3 --> P4[Apply Bragg n lambda 2d sin theta]
    P4 --> P5[Diffraction Peak at Specific 2 theta]
    P5 --> P6[Plot Intensity versus 2 theta]
    P6 --> P7[Phase Identification via ICDD Database]
    P6 --> P8[Lattice Parameter Calculation]
    P6 --> P9[Crystal Structure SC BCC FCC HCP]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: X-ray Diffraction Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[XRD Techniques] --> Q2[Laue Method]
    Q1 --> Q3[Rotating Crystal Method]
    Q1 --> Q4[Powder Diffraction Method]
    Q2 --> Q5[Single Crystal Stationary Polychromatic X ray]
    Q3 --> Q6[Single Crystal Rotating Monochromatic X ray]
    Q4 --> Q7[Polycrystalline Powder Monochromatic X ray]
    Q5 --> Q8[Used for Crystal Orientation]
    Q6 --> Q9[Used for Unknown Structure Determination]
    Q7 --> Q10[Most Widely Used in Materials Labs]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Bragg's Law and state its significance.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Bragg's Law states that for constructive interference (diffraction) of X-rays from crystal planes:

$$n\lambda = 2d_{hkl}\sin\theta$$

where $n$ is the order of diffraction, $\lambda$ is the wavelength of X-rays, $d_{hkl}$ is the interplanar spacing, and $\theta$ is the glancing angle.

**Significance:**
1. It is the fundamental equation for X-ray diffraction analysis.
2. It enables determination of interplanar spacing $d$ from measured $\theta$ values.
3. Combined with Miller indices, it allows calculation of lattice parameters.
4. It forms the basis for crystal structure identification, phase analysis, and stress measurement.
5. It relates microscopic atomic arrangement to macroscopic measurable diffraction angles.

---

### **Question 2: Distinguish between continuous and characteristic X-ray spectra.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Continuous Spectrum (Bremsstrahlung)** | **Characteristic Spectrum** |
|------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Produced by deceleration of electrons in target material | Produced by electronic transitions between inner atomic shells |
| Has a minimum cutoff wavelength given by Duane-Hunt law | Has discrete, fixed wavelengths characteristic of target element |
| Wavelength depends on applied voltage | Wavelength depends on target material |
| Broad, polychromatic | Sharp, monochromatic lines (K$\alpha$, K$\beta$) |
| Used for Laue method | Used for powder and rotating crystal methods |
| No specific energy levels involved | Involves K, L, M shell transitions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse K$\alpha$ and K$\beta$ lines. **K$\alpha$** results from L → K transition (longer wavelength, more intense). **K$\beta$** results from M → K transition (shorter wavelength, less intense). Use a Ni filter to remove K$\beta$ when using Cu target.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Bragg's Law for X-ray diffraction from crystal planes. Explain the geometric construction with a clear diagram. (7 Marks)

**(b)** X-rays of wavelength 1.5418 Å are diffracted by (200) planes of an FCC metal with lattice parameter 4.05 Å. Calculate the Bragg angle for first-order diffraction. (7 Marks)

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Consider two parallel crystal planes separated by distance $d$. A monochromatic X-ray beam (wavelength $\lambda$) is incident at glancing angle $\theta$.

**Step 2:** The first ray reflects from the top plane at point A. The second ray penetrates to the second plane and reflects from point B.

**Step 3:** Drop perpendiculars from A to ray 2 (foot at P) and from B to ray 1 (foot at Q).

**Step 4:** In right triangle APB, the angle at A is $\theta$ (since ray 2 is parallel to ray 1 and the plane is horizontal):
$$PB = d\sin\theta$$

**Step 5:** By symmetry:
$$BQ = d\sin\theta$$

**Step 6:** Total path difference:
$$\Delta = PB + BQ = 2d\sin\theta$$

**Step 7:** For constructive interference:
$$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta \quad \text{(Bragg's Law)}$$

This is the **fundamental condition** for X-ray diffraction. *(See geometric construction in Section 4 of these notes.)*

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate $d_{200}$ for cubic system with $a = 4.05$ Å.

$$d_{200} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}} = \frac{4.05}{\sqrt{2^2 + 0^2 + 0^2}} = \frac{4.05}{2} = 2.025 \text{ Å}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Bragg's Law with $n = 1$:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{n\lambda}{2d} = \frac{1 \times 1.5418}{2 \times 2.025} = \frac{1.5418}{4.050} = 0.3807$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $\theta$:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.3807) = 22.37°$$

**Answer:** Bragg angle $\theta = 22.37°$; diffraction peak at $2\theta = 44.74°$.

---

### **Question B: (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Bragg's X-ray spectrometer. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for interplanar spacing in a cubic crystal system. (7 Marks)

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.2 for block diagram):**

A Bragg's spectrometer consists of:
1. **X-ray source:** Coolidge tube with Cu/Mo target producing monochromatic K$\alpha$ radiation.
2. **Collimator:** System of slits to produce a narrow parallel beam.
3. **Crystal mount (Goniometer):** Rotatable sample holder on a precisely calibrated circular scale.
4. **Detector:** Ionization chamber or Geiger-Müller counter mounted on the rotating arm.
5. **Amplifier and recorder:** Converts detector signals to plot of intensity vs $2\theta$.

**Working:**
- The crystal is mounted on the goniometer and rotated by known angles.
- X-rays from the source strike the crystal at varying angles.
- The detector is rotated at twice the angular speed of the crystal to maintain the $\theta$–$2\theta$ geometry.
- When Bragg's condition $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ is satisfied, a sharp peak in intensity is recorded.
- Knowing $\theta$ and $\lambda$, the interplanar spacing $d$ is calculated.
- Lattice parameter $a$ is then determined using the cubic system formula.

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1:** The Miller indices $(hkl)$ define a family of planes with intercepts $\dfrac{a}{h}, \dfrac{a}{k}, \dfrac{a}{l}$ on the crystallographic axes.

**Step 2:** The equation of the plane is:
$$\frac{hx}{a} + \frac{ky}{a} + \frac{lz}{a} = 1$$

**Step 3:** The perpendicular distance from origin $(0,0,0)$ to this plane is given by the standard formula:
$$d_{hkl} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{h}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{k}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{l}{a}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplifying:
$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

**Step 5:** Combining with Bragg's Law:
$$\sin^2\theta = \frac{\lambda^2}{4a^2}(h^2 + k^2 + l^2)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Bragg's angle vs detector angle:** Many students confuse $\theta$ (glancing angle on crystal) with $2\theta$ (detector position). The detector always moves at **twice the rate** of the crystal.
> 2. **Miller indices in formula:** Students forget to **square** the indices ($h^2 + k^2 + l^2$), not add linearly.
> 3. **Units:** Always keep wavelength in Å when $a$ is in Å. Do not mix units.
> 4. **Order of diffraction:** $n$ is an integer. Higher orders occur at larger $\theta$.
> 5. **FCC vs BCC selection rules:** Wrong indexing is the most common error. Always verify allowed reflections.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Bragg's Law:** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ — foundation of all XRD analysis.
- ✅ **Interplanar spacing (cubic):** $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$
- ✅ **Duane-Hunt Law:** $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{12400}{V(\text{V})}$ Å for X-ray tube cutoff.
- ✅ **X-ray production:** Bremsstrahlung (continuous) + Characteristic (K$\alpha$, K$\beta$).
- ✅ **Crystal selection rules:**
  - SC: All $(hkl)$ allowed
  - BCC: $h + k + l$ = even
  - FCC: $h, k, l$ all odd or all even
- ✅ **Spectrometer geometry:** Crystal rotates at $\theta$, detector at $2\theta$.
- ✅ **Applications:** Phase ID, lattice parameter, crystallite size, residual stress.
- ✅ **Cu K$\alpha$ wavelength:** 1.5406 Å (most common laboratory source).
- ✅ **Order of peaks:** Higher $n$ → larger $\theta$ → smaller $\sin\theta$ requirements.
- ✅ **Laue's Equations:** Three simultaneous conditions for 3D lattice diffraction; equivalent to Bragg's Law in vector form.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for Selection Rules:** **"BCC = Body sum Even, FCC = Family Fully same parity"** — recall that for FCC all three indices are either odd or all even (e.g., 111, 200, 220, 311 all allowed).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Diffraction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electron Diffraction Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electron Diffraction** is a crystallographic technique that exploits the **wave-particle duality** of accelerated electrons to probe the periodic atomic arrangement in crystalline solids. When a beam of high-velocity electrons (typically accelerated through 50–400 kV) is incident upon a thin crystalline specimen, the electrons are elastically scattered by the periodic lattice planes and produce a characteristic diffraction pattern (spots, rings, or Kikuchi lines) that can be used to determine crystal structure, lattice parameter, orientation, and defects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Module 1 Focus:** The emphasis is on understanding *why* electrons behave as waves in a crystal, the relationship between accelerating voltage and wavelength, Bragg's Law in reciprocal space (Ewald construction), and the comparison with X-ray diffraction.

**Two Principal Geometries in Electron Diffraction:**
1. **LEED (Low-Energy Electron Diffraction)** — electrons of 20–500 eV, used for surface crystallography (penetration ~1 nm).
2. **HEED / SAED (High-Energy Electron Diffraction / Selected Area Electron Diffraction in TEM)** — electrons of 50–400 keV, used for bulk thin-film and transmission analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Sound Echoes in a Marble Hall"

Imagine standing in a grand hall whose floor and walls are tiled with a perfectly repeating **marble mosaic pattern**. If you clap your hands (your "incident wave"), the sound bounces off the tiles. Most of the time, the echoes are weak and scattered. But at certain **specific standing positions and angles**, all the echoes arrive back at your ear **in perfect synchrony**, producing an intense, ringing boom.

The marble tiles = **crystal lattice planes**; your clap = the **electron wave**; the synchronised boom = the **diffracted beam**. The synchronisation condition is precisely **Bragg's Law**. Electron diffraction is essentially "listening" to the geometry of the marble hall by analysing where the echoes add up constructively.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are essential for deriving and solving electron diffraction problems:

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $h$ | Planck's constant | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}}$ |
| $m_0$ | Rest mass of electron | $\mathbf{9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}}$ |
| $e$ | Electron charge | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}}$ |
| $c$ | Speed of light | $\mathbf{3.0 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}}$ |
| $1\ \text{Å}$ | Ångström | $\mathbf{10^{-10}\ \text{m}}$ |
| $1\ \text{nm}$ | Nanometre | $\mathbf{10^{-9}\ \text{m}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why electrons?** Because their wavelength (≈ 0.0025–0.05 Å for TEM voltages) is comparable to atomic spacings (≈ 1–4 Å) — the same regime that gives X-ray diffraction its power, but with the added ability to *focus* electrons using electromagnetic lenses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Electrons Diffract

The diffraction phenomenon rests on three pillars:

1. **de Broglie's Hypothesis (1924):** A moving particle of momentum $p$ has an associated wavelength
   $\lambda = h/p$. For an electron accelerated through a potential $V$, $p = \sqrt{2m_0 eV}$ (non-relativistic), so
   $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2m_0 eV}$.

2. **Bragg's Law:** Constructive interference from lattice planes of spacing $d_{hkl}$ occurs only when
   $n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the glancing angle and $n$ is the diffraction order.

3. **Reciprocal-Lattice Interpretation (Ewald Construction):** A diffraction condition is satisfied when a reciprocal-lattice point $\mathbf{g}_{hkl}$ lies exactly on the **Ewald sphere** of radius $1/\lambda$ centred at the tip of the incident wave vector $\mathbf{k}_0$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Diffraction Pattern Formation

- **Step 1 — Electron Generation:** A thermionic (W or LaB₆ filament) or field-emission gun emits electrons that are accelerated through a high potential $V$ (e.g., 100 kV, 200 kV).
- **Step 2 — Wavelength Selection:** The accelerating voltage fixes $\lambda$. For 100 kV, the relativistic wavelength is ≈ 0.0037 nm, far smaller than typical $d_{hkl}$ values (≈ 0.2 nm).
- **Step 3 — Interaction with Specimen:** Because $\lambda \ll d_{hkl}$, the Bragg angle $\theta$ is very small (typically < 2°), so the diffracted beams are tightly clustered around the direct beam.
- **Step 4 — Pattern Recording:** A phosphor screen or photographic plate placed at distance $L$ (the **camera length**) records the diffracted beams as a pattern of spots (single crystal) or rings (polycrystalline).
- **Step 5 — Indexing:** The geometry of the pattern ($R_d$ = ring radius) is related to $d_{hkl}$ by the **camera-constant equation** $R_d \cdot d_{hkl} = L \lambda$.

## 2.3 Relativistic Correction — Why It Is Essential

At accelerating voltages above ~50 kV, the electron's velocity approaches a significant fraction of $c$, so the non-relativistic formula overestimates the wavelength. The full relativistic wavelength is

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}}\right)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For 100 kV electrons, the relativistic correction lowers $\lambda$ from 0.00388 nm (non-relativistic) to **0.00370 nm** — a ~5% difference that is critical for accurate lattice-parameter determination in TEM.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Set** — Memorise these equations. They appear in nearly every Part-B (14-mark) question on electron diffraction.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2 m_0 e V}$ | Non-relativistic de Broglie wavelength | Quick calc for $V < 50$ kV |
| 2 | $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2 m_0 e V(1 + eV/2m_0 c^2)}$ | Relativistic de Broglie wavelength | $V \ge 50$ kV (TEM) |
| 3 | $2 d \sin\theta = n\lambda$ | **Bragg's Law** | Core diffraction condition |
| 4 | $R_d \cdot d = L\lambda$ | **Camera-constant equation** | Indexing SAED rings |
| 5 | $R_{hkl}^{2} = \dfrac{L^{2} \lambda^{2}(h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2})}{a^{2}}$ | Ring radius for cubic crystal | Lattice parameter $a$ |
| 6 | $\vert \mathbf{k} \vert = 1/\lambda$ | Ewald-sphere radius | Reciprocal-space geometry |
| 7 | $\mathbf{k} - \mathbf{k}_0 = \mathbf{g}_{hkl}$ | Diffraction vector condition | Ewald construction |
| 8 | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}$ | $d$-spacing for cubic system | Indexing diffraction spots |
| 9 | $S_g = \mathbf{g} \cdot \left(\mathbf{s} + \dfrac{\mathbf{g}}{2}\right)$ | Deviation parameter $S_g$ | Two-beam condition |
| 10 | $\xi_g = \dfrac{\pi V_c \cos\theta}{\lambda \vert F_g \vert}$ | **Extinction distance** | TEM contrast / thickness fringes |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $L\lambda$ is called the **camera constant** (units: mm·Å) and is the most important experimental parameter in SAED analysis. It is calibrated using a known standard (e.g., polycrystalline Au or ThCl₂).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Materials Characterisation (TEM/SAED):** Identifying phases, measuring lattice parameters to ±0.001 nm, determining Burgers vectors of dislocations.
- **Semiconductor Industry:** LEED verifies epitaxial growth of GaAs/Si, monitors surface reconstruction during MBE.
- **Thin-Film Technology:** Reflection HEED (RHEED) is used in situ during MBE growth of superconductors and 2D materials.
- **Failure Analysis:** Determining unknown inclusions or precipitates in failed engineering components.
- **Nanotechnology:** Probing the crystallinity of nanoparticles and quantum dots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — de Broglie Wavelength of an Accelerated Electron

**Given:** An electron of charge $e$ accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$.

**Step 1 — Energy conservation.** The work done by the electric field equals the kinetic energy gained:

$$
eV = \tfrac{1}{2} m_0 v^{2}
$$

This gives the momentum magnitude

$$
p = m_0 v = \sqrt{2 m_0 e V}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply de Broglie's relation.** Every particle of momentum $p$ has a wavelength

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V}}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in numerical constants.** Substituting $h = 6.626\times10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_0 = 9.109\times10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602\times10^{-19}$ C, and $V$ in volts:

$$
\lambda\ (\text{in nm}) = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}
$$

**Step 4 — Quick check:** For $V = 150\ \text{V}$, $\lambda = 1.227/\sqrt{150} \approx 0.100\ \text{nm} = 1.0\ \text{Å}$ — exactly the interatomic spacing regime. This is why LEED (20–500 eV) is ideal for surface crystallography.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relativistic de Broglie Wavelength

**Step 1 — Relativistic energy.** The total energy of an electron accelerated through $V$ is

$$
E = m_0 c^{2} + eV = \gamma m_0 c^{2}
$$

where $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{1 - v^{2}/c^{2}}$.

**Step 2 — Relativistic momentum.** From $E^{2} = (m_0 c^{2})^{2} + (pc)^{2}$:

$$
p = \frac{\sqrt{(m_0 c^{2} + eV)^{2} - (m_0 c^{2})^{2}}}{c} = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV + (eV/c)^{2} \cdot c^{2}}}{1}
$$

Factoring $2 m_0 eV$:

$$
p = \sqrt{2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}}\right)}
$$

**Step 3 — de Broglie wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}}\right)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical compact form** (with $\lambda$ in nm and $V$ in volts, including relativistic correction):

$$
\lambda\ (\text{nm}) = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \left(1 + 0.978\times 10^{-6}\, V\right)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify at 100 kV.** $V = 10^{5}$ V → correction factor $\sqrt{1+0.0978} = 1.0476$ → $\lambda = 1.227/\sqrt{100000 \times 1.0476} = 0.00370$ nm = 0.0370 Å. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Camera-Constant Equation $R_d d = L \lambda$

**Setup:** A polycrystalline (or randomly oriented) thin foil produces a **conical** diffracted beam from each set of $\{hkl\}$ planes. The half-angle of the cone equals the Bragg angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Geometry.** Let $L$ = distance from specimen to recording screen (camera length). The diffracted cone intersects the screen as a ring of radius $R_d$.

For small $\theta$ (always the case for fast electrons), $\tan 2\theta \approx 2\theta$ and $\sin\theta \approx \theta$. From the geometry:

$$
R_d = L \tan 2\theta \approx 2L \theta
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Bragg's Law** $2d\sin\theta = \lambda \Rightarrow 2\theta \approx \lambda/d$:

$$
R_d = L \cdot \frac{\lambda}{d}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange** to obtain the **camera-constant equation**:

$$
R_d \cdot d = L \lambda \equiv K
$$

The product $K = L\lambda$ is the **camera constant**, an instrumental quantity independent of the sample.

**Step 4 — For a cubic crystal of lattice parameter $a$**, $d_{hkl} = a/\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}$, giving the **ring-radius equation**:

$$
R_{hkl}^{2} = \frac{K^{2} (h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2})}{a^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Use:** Measure $R_{hkl}$ for at least three rings, compute the ratios $R_{i}^{2}/R_{1}^{2}$, and compare with the sequence $(h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2})$ for allowed reflections of suspected cubic structures (FCC, BCC, etc.) to identify the phase and compute $a$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Electron vs. X-ray Diffraction

| Property | Electron Diffraction | X-ray Diffraction |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength ($\lambda$) | 0.003–0.05 Å (TEM); 0.5–3 Å (LEED) | 0.5–2.5 Å (typical Cu K$\alpha$ = 1.54 Å) |
| Penetration | 10–100 nm (TEM) | 1–100 µm |
| Specimen form | Thin foil (≤ 200 nm) | Bulk powder / single crystal |
| Focusing | Electromagnetic lenses → imaging + diffraction | No simple lens — collimation only |
| Scattering factor | Coulombic — strong Z-dependence (electron-nucleus) | Form factor — $f$ depends on scattering angle |
| Bragg angle $\theta$ | Very small ($< 2°$) — small-angle approx valid | Moderate ($5$–$30°$) |
| Information volume | Local (sub-µm, even nm) | Average over mm³ |
| Best for | Defects, individual particles, thin films | Phase ID, lattice parameter (bulk) |
| Sample damage | Possible (knock-on, heating) | Minimal |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

> **Problem:** A TEM operates at 200 kV. Compute (a) the relativistic electron wavelength, and (b) the expected ring radius on a SAED pattern for the (111) planes of an Al sample ($a = 0.4049$ nm) if the camera length is 800 mm.

**Solution:**

**(a) Wavelength:** $V = 2\times 10^{5}$ V. Using the compact form,

$$
\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{2\times 10^{5}\,(1 + 0.978\times 10^{-6} \times 2\times 10^{5})}} \text{ nm}
$$

Correction factor: $1 + 0.1956 = 1.1956$, so denominator $= \sqrt{2.391\times 10^{5}} = 489.0$.

$$
\lambda = \frac{1.227}{489.0} = 2.51\times 10^{-3}\ \text{nm} = 0.0251\ \text{Å}
$$

**(b) Ring radius:** First compute $d_{111}$:

$$
d_{111} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{0.4049}{1.732} = 0.2338\ \text{nm}
$$

Camera constant: $K = L\lambda = 800\ \text{mm} \times 0.00251\ \text{nm} = 2.008\ \text{mm·nm}$.

Ring radius:

$$
R_{111} = \frac{K}{d_{111}} = \frac{2.008}{0.2338} = 8.59\ \text{mm}
$$

**Answer:** $\lambda \approx 0.0251$ Å, $R_{111} \approx 8.6$ mm. ✓ Realistic values for a 200 kV TEM.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Generation and Use of a Diffraction Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electron Gun W or LaB6 Filament] --> B[Accelerating Voltage 50 to 400 kV]
    B --> C[Electromagnetic Condenser Lens]
    C --> D[Thin Crystalline Specimen 100 to 200 nm]
    D --> E{Elastically Scattered Electrons}
    E --> F[Direct Beam k0]
    E --> G[Diffracted Beams kg]
    F --> H[Objective Aperture and Lens]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Phosphor Screen or CCD]
    I --> J[SAED Pattern Spots or Rings]
    J --> K[Indexing hkl Planes]
    K --> L[Lattice Parameter a]
    K --> M[Phase Identification]
    K --> N[Defect Analysis Burgers Vector]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Ewald Sphere Construction in Reciprocal Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Reciprocal Space Geometry]
    O[Origin of Reciprocal Lattice] --> P[Reciprocal Lattice Points g_hkl]
    C[Centre of Ewald Sphere] --> R[Radius equals 1 by lambda]
    P --> Q[Point g touching sphere equals active reflection]
    end
    C --> O
    Q -->|k outgoing| S[Diffracted beam direction]
    Q -->|k0 incident| T[Incident beam direction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A reciprocal-lattice point $\mathbf{g}_{hkl}$ lying exactly on the Ewald sphere of radius $1/\lambda$ (centred at the tip of $\mathbf{k}_0$) means the Bragg condition is satisfied for that set of planes. Tilting the crystal moves different $\mathbf{g}$ points onto the sphere — this is the principle of **two-beam condition** in TEM.

## 4.3 Functional Block — Comparison of LEED, HEED, and SAED Setups

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diffraction Technique] --> B[LEED Low Energy 20 to 500 eV]
    A --> C[HEED High Energy Reflection 50 to 100 keV]
    A --> D[SAED Selected Area in TEM 100 to 400 keV]
    B --> B1[Surface crystallography]
    B --> B2[Pattern on fluorescent screen behind hemispherical grids]
    C --> C1[In situ thin film growth MBE]
    C --> C2[Grazing incidence geometry]
    D --> D1[Internal bulk defects]
    D --> D2[Post specimen lenses project diffraction onto screen]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Table — Specimen Preparation for TEM/SAED

| Stage | Procedure | Final Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slicing with a diamond saw | ~ 500 µm |
| 2 | Mechanical grinding on SiC papers | ~ 100 µm |
| 3 | Dimpling (cup grinding) to ~ 20 µm at the centre | ~ 20 µm |
| 4 | Ion-beam milling (Ar⁺ at 3–5 kV) | **< 100 nm — electron-transparent** |
| Alternative | Electropolishing (chemical etch) | **< 100 nm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why thin?** The mean free path of a 100–200 keV electron in a solid is ~ 50–150 nm. If the foil is thicker, the electron undergoes multiple scattering and the diffraction pattern blurs out into a diffuse background (Kikuchi-line regime).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State Bragg's Law and explain the de Broglie hypothesis as it applies to electron diffraction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Bragg's Law (1 mark):** $n\lambda = 2 d \sin\theta$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of the incident wave, $d$ is the interplanar spacing, $\theta$ is the glancing angle, and $n$ is an integer representing the order of diffraction.

- **de Broglie Hypothesis (1 mark):** Every moving particle of momentum $p$ exhibits wave-like behaviour with wavelength $\lambda = h/p$. For an electron accelerated through potential $V$, $p = \sqrt{2m_0 eV}$, giving $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2m_0 eV}$.

- **Application (1 mark):** When the de Broglie wavelength of the electron (≈ 0.0025–0.05 Å at TEM voltages) is comparable to interatomic spacings (≈ 1–4 Å) and Bragg's condition is satisfied, the electron beam is diffracted by the crystal lattice, producing a measurable diffraction pattern.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between LEED and SAED techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | LEED | SAED |
|---|---|---|
| **Electron energy** | 20–500 eV (low energy) | 50–400 keV (high energy) |
| **Penetration depth** | ~ 1 nm (surface only) | 10–100 nm (thin foil) |
| **Geometry** | Back-scattering (reflection) | Transmission through thin foil |
| **Information** | Surface structure, reconstruction | Bulk structure, defects |
| **Setup** | Inside UHV chamber, hemispherical grids | Inside TEM column, post-specimen lenses |
| **Sample** | Bulk single-crystal surface | Thin electron-transparent foil |

*(2 marks for any 4 correct comparisons, 1 mark for stating that both rely on Bragg's condition for electron waves.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the de Broglie wavelength of an electron in both non-relativistic and relativistic forms. Calculate the wavelength of an electron accelerated through (i) 100 V, and (ii) 100 kV. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the Ewald sphere construction in reciprocal space. How does it help to predict which lattice planes will diffract? **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation**

**Non-relativistic form (3 marks):** Energy conservation gives $eV = \tfrac{1}{2}m_0 v^{2}$, hence $p = m_0 v = \sqrt{2 m_0 eV}$. Substituting into de Broglie's relation:

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV}}
$$

Using numerical values ($h = 6.626\times10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_0 = 9.109\times10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602\times10^{-19}$ C):

$$
\lambda\ (\text{nm}) = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}
$$

**Relativistic form (2 marks):** Using $E = m_0 c^{2} + eV$ and $E^{2} = (m_0 c^{2})^{2} + (pc)^{2}$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^{2}}\right)}}
$$

**Numerical calculations (2 marks):**

- **(i) 100 V:** $\lambda = 1.227/\sqrt{100} = 0.1227$ nm = **1.227 Å** (non-relativistic is sufficient).
- **(ii) 100 kV = $10^{5}$ V:** Correction factor = $1 + 0.978\times10^{-6}\times10^{5} = 1.0978$.
  $\lambda = 1.227/\sqrt{10^{5}\times 1.0978} = 1.227/331.3 = 3.70\times10^{-3}$ nm = **0.0370 Å**.

This relativistic value is used in TEM.

**Part (b) — Ewald Sphere (7 marks)**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** The Ewald sphere is a geometric construction in reciprocal space, with radius $1/\lambda$, centred at the tip of the incident wave vector $\mathbf{k}_0$. The origin of the reciprocal lattice sits on the sphere.

2. **Diffraction condition (2 marks):** A reciprocal-lattice point $\mathbf{g}_{hkl}$ lying **on** the sphere satisfies $\mathbf{k} - \mathbf{k}_0 = \mathbf{g}$, i.e., the Laue condition. The corresponding family of crystal planes $\{hkl\}$ is in the Bragg-diffracting orientation.

3. **Predicting reflections (2 marks):** Tilting the crystal rotates the reciprocal lattice about its origin, sweeping different $\mathbf{g}$ points across the sphere. Only those momentarily in contact produce a reflection — this is the basis of systematic-row and two-beam TEM imaging.

4. **Ewald-sphere radius comparison (1 mark):** For X-rays ($\lambda \approx 1$ Å), the sphere radius is small, so few reflections satisfy the condition at once. For electrons ($\lambda \approx 0.04$ Å), the radius is huge, and the sphere approximates a plane — many reflections are nearly satisfied simultaneously.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice — Alternative]`

**(a)** Describe the principle and construction of an electron diffraction experiment (TEM/SAED mode). With a neat diagram, explain the formation of spot and ring patterns. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A polycrystalline copper thin film is examined in a TEM at 200 kV. The (111) diffraction ring is observed at a radius of 10.5 mm. Given the camera length $L = 1000$ mm and lattice parameter of Cu, $a = 0.3615$ nm, verify that the wavelength is consistent with the relativistic value and compute the expected radius for the (220) ring. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — SAED Setup and Patterns (7 marks)**

**Principle (2 marks):** A parallel beam of high-energy electrons is incident on a thin (< 200 nm) crystalline specimen. Bragg-diffracted beams are focused by the intermediate and projector lenses of a TEM onto a phosphor screen, producing a **diffraction pattern** in the back-focal plane.

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. Electron gun → condenser lens system → forms a parallel beam of diameter selectable by condenser aperture.
2. Specimen in objective lens pre-field.
3. Selected-area aperture restricts the region contributing to diffraction (typically 100 nm – 1 µm).
4. Intermediate lens focuses the back-focal plane of the objective lens (where the diffraction pattern forms) onto the screen.
5. Projector lens magnifies the pattern onto a phosphor screen or CCD.

**Spot vs. Ring patterns (2 marks):**
- **Single crystal:** Each set of $\{hkl\}$ planes produces **one** sharp spot per plane family. The pattern is a regular array of spots whose symmetry reflects the crystal symmetry and zone axis.
- **Polycrystalline:** Thousands of randomly oriented crystallites simultaneously satisfy Bragg's law. Each ring corresponds to one $\{hkl\}$ family, appearing as a circle (Debye–Scherrer-like) on the flat screen because the cone of diffracted rays intersects the screen as a ring of radius $R_d = L\lambda/d_{hkl}$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Verification (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Relativistic wavelength for 200 kV (2 marks):**
$\lambda = 1.227 / \sqrt{2\times10^{5} \times 1.1956} = 1.227/489.0 = 2.51\times10^{-3}$ nm = 0.0251 Å.

**Step 2 — Compute $d_{111}$ for Cu (1 mark):**
$d_{111} = a/\sqrt{3} = 0.3615/1.732 = 0.2087$ nm.

**Step 3 — Predicted radius for (111) (2 marks):**
Camera constant: $K = L\lambda = 1000 \times 0.00251 = 2.51$ mm·nm.
$R_{111}^{\text{calc}} = K/d_{111} = 2.51/0.2087 = 12.03$ mm.

> Note: The observed 10.5 mm vs. calculated 12.03 mm suggests either (i) a slightly different camera length, or (ii) a different effective $K$ — typical experimental uncertainty is 5–10 %.

**Step 4 — Radius for (220) (2 marks):**
$d_{220} = a/\sqrt{8} = 0.3615/2.828 = 0.1278$ nm.
$R_{220} = K/d_{220} = 2.51/0.1278 = 19.64$ mm.

**Answer:** The expected $R_{220} \approx 19.6$ mm; the slight discrepancy for (111) suggests recalibration of the camera constant is required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Electron-Diffraction Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting relativistic correction** at voltages ≥ 50 kV — costs 1–2 marks. Always state "non-relativistic valid for V ≲ 50 kV."
> 2. **Mixing up $R_d$ and $d_{hkl}$** — $R_d$ is the ring radius on the screen (mm), $d_{hkl}$ is the interplanar spacing (Å or nm). Their product is the camera constant.
> 3. **Wrong selection rule for cubic systems** — FCC shows only reflections with $h, k, l$ all odd or all even. BCC shows only $h+k+l$ = even. Writing random $\{hkl\}$ sets loses marks.
> 4. **Ewald-sphere diagram mistakes** — the centre of the sphere is at the **tip of $\mathbf{k}_0$**, not at the origin. The origin of the reciprocal lattice lies on the sphere.
> 5. **Units inconsistency** — $\lambda$ in nm with $d$ in nm and $R, L$ in mm gives $K$ in mm·nm. Mixing Å and nm is a common fatal error.
> 6. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation** in $R_d = L\tan 2\theta \approx 2L\theta$. The full expression should be used only if $\theta > 5°$, which essentially never happens in TEM.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and tick each box during your final revision pass.**

- [ ] **de Broglie wavelength** — $\lambda = h/p$; for electrons $\lambda(\text{nm}) = 1.227/\sqrt{V}$ (non-rel.).
- [ ] **Relativistic form** — includes the factor $(1 + eV/2m_0c^{2})$ inside the square root.
- [ ] **Bragg's Law** — $2d\sin\theta = n\lambda$; for fast electrons, $\theta$ is very small (< 2°).
- [ ] **Camera-constant equation** — $R_d \cdot d_{hkl} = L\lambda = K$ (instrument constant).
- [ ] **Ring-radius for cubic crystals** — $R_{hkl}^{2} = K^{2}(h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2})/a^{2}$.
- [ ] **Ewald sphere radius** — $1/\lambda$; for TEM electrons this is very large (≈ 270 nm⁻¹), approximating a flat plane.
- [ ] **Laue condition in reciprocal space** — $\mathbf{k} - \mathbf{k}_0 = \mathbf{g}_{hkl}$.
- [ ] **LEED vs SAED vs RHEED** — energies, geometries, applications (surface vs bulk).
- [ ] **Specimen requirement** — thin foil (< 200 nm) to avoid multiple scattering for TEM/SAED.
- [ ] **Selection rules** — FCC: $h, k, l$ unmixed; BCC: $h+k+l$ even.
- [ ] **Comparison with XRD** — electrons have stronger interaction, smaller $\lambda$, smaller $\theta$, need thin samples; X-rays penetrate bulk, need larger $\theta$, no focusing lenses.
- [ ] **Extinction distance $\xi_g$** — proportional to $\cos\theta / (\lambda \vert F_g \vert)$ — controls TEM contrast.
- [ ] **Camera constant calibration** — done with a known standard (Au, ThCl₂, Al).
- [ ] **Indexing procedure** — measure $R_d$ for several rings, compute ratios, match to allowed $(h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2})$ sequences for candidate cubic structures.
- [ ] **Deviation parameter** $S_g$ — zero at exact Bragg condition, $\neq 0$ otherwise; controls diffraction intensity.

> **End of Module 1 — Electron Diffraction Methods Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Engineering Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Engineering Materials**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT412 – Material Science)**, *Properties of Engineering Materials* are defined as the quantitative and qualitative characteristics that govern the response of a material when subjected to external forces, thermal gradients, electrical fields, chemical environments, or magnetic influences during service.

These properties are the bridge between the **internal microstructure** of a material (atomic arrangement, bonding, defects) and its **external macroscopic performance** in a designed engineering component.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Properties of Engineering Materials* is the systematic study of how materials behave under mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical, and magnetic stimuli, classified broadly into **Mechanical, Physical, Chemical, and Technological (Manufacturing) Properties**.

### 1.2 Broad Classification

The properties of engineering materials are classified into six major families:

| **Category** | **Examples** |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Properties** | Strength, Hardness, Toughness, Ductility, Brittleness, Stiffness, Fatigue, Creep |
| **Physical Properties** | Density, Melting Point, Boiling Point, Colour, Specific Gravity |
| **Thermal Properties** | Thermal Conductivity, Specific Heat, Thermal Expansion |
| **Electrical Properties** | Electrical Resistivity, Conductivity, Dielectric Strength |
| **Magnetic Properties** | Permeability, Coercivity, Hysteresis |
| **Chemical Properties** | Corrosion Resistance, Oxidation Resistance, Chemical Stability |
| **Technological/Manufacturing Properties** | Castability, Weldability, Machinability, Formability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Board Examinations, **Mechanical Properties** carry the highest weightage (typically 60–70% of Module 1 questions). Always draw the **Stress–Strain Curve** whenever a question mentions "ductile material behaviour" or "yield point" – this is a mandatory diagram in the KTU valuation key.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **newspaper delivery boy** in Kochi who must cycle 20 km every morning carrying a heavy bag of papers. His "properties" as a "human engineering material" would include:

- **Strength (Mechanical):** How much weight can he carry? (Like *tensile strength* of a steel rod)
- **Hardness:** Can he resist being poked? (Like *surface hardness* of hardened steel)
- **Toughness:** If he falls off the cycle, can he absorb the impact without breaking a bone? (Like *impact toughness* of copper)
- **Ductility:** Can he stretch his arm to ring the doorbell? (Like a metal that can be drawn into a wire)
- **Brittleness:** Like a glass tumbler – shatters suddenly without warning. (Like cast iron)
- **Elasticity (Stiffness):** A rubber band stretches and returns. A steel ruler is stiffer. (Modulus of Elasticity, *E*)
- **Fatigue:** After 1000 days of cycling, his knees wear out even if each day's load was small. (Like metal fatigue in aircraft wings)
- **Creep:** If he stands still holding the bag for hours, his body slowly deforms (sags). (Like lead pipes sagging on rooftops over years)

This analogy, while humorous, captures the essence of **why we study material properties** – to *predict failure, ensure safety, and select the right material* for the right job.

### 1.4 The Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The selection of a material for a specific application is entirely driven by its properties. For example:
> - Aircraft fuselage → **High strength-to-weight ratio** (Al alloys, Ti alloys)
> - Pressure vessels → **High toughness** (low carbon steel)
> - Cutting tools → **High hardness** (Tungsten carbide, ceramics)
> - Electrical wiring → **High electrical conductivity** (Copper, Aluminium)
> - Chemical plant → **High corrosion resistance** (Stainless steel, PTFE)

**Standard Metric Conventions in KTU Examinations:**
- Stress: **MPa** (or N/mm²) — *1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa*
- Strain: dimensionless (mm/mm or m/m)
- Toughness/Energy: **Joules (J)**
- Hardness numbers: **dimensionless** (BHN, RHN, VHN)
- Density: **kg/m³** or **g/cm³**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Properties — The Heart of Module 1

The mechanical properties describe how a material deforms, resists, or fractures under applied **forces (loads)**. They are determined experimentally using standardized tests (ASTM/IS standards).

#### A. Stress ($\sigma$) and Strain ($\epsilon$)

**Stress** is the internal resistance offered by a material per unit area when an external load is applied.

**Strain** is the relative deformation (change in dimension) per unit original dimension.

For a cylindrical rod of original length $L_0$ and cross-sectional area $A_0$ subjected to an axial tensile load $P$:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A_0}$$

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Stress** uses the **original** area $A_0$.
> **True Stress** uses the **instantaneous** area $A_i$ (after deformation).
> In KTU exams, "stress" without qualification usually means *engineering stress*.

#### B. Elasticity and Plasticity

- **Elasticity:** The ability of a material to regain its original shape and size after the removal of load (e.g., rubber, spring steel).
- **Plasticity:** The ability to undergo permanent deformation without fracture (e.g., clay, lead, mild steel).

The **Elastic Limit** is the maximum stress up to which the material behaves elastically. Beyond this, deformation becomes permanent.

#### C. Stiffness (Modulus of Elasticity, $E$)

Stiffness is the resistance of a material to elastic deformation. Mathematically, it is the slope of the linear (elastic) portion of the stress–strain curve.

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}}$$

Unit: **GPa** (Gigapascals) — typically $E_{\text{steel}} = 200$ GPa, $E_{\text{Al}} = 70$ GPa, $E_{\text{cast iron}} = 100$ GPa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **high $E$** means the material is **stiff** (deforms less under load). A **low $E$** means the material is **flexible** (deforms more). Note: $E$ is **NOT** the same as strength!

#### D. Strength (Yield, Ultimate, Breaking)

- **Proportional Limit:** Stress up to which stress ∝ strain (Hooke's Law valid).
- **Elastic Limit:** Max stress for full recovery of shape.
- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$ or $\sigma_{0.2}$):** Stress at which material begins to deform plastically. For mild steel, an upper and lower yield point exist.
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_u$):** Maximum stress the material can withstand.
- **Breaking/Fracture Strength:** Stress at which the material actually fractures.

#### E. Ductility and Malleability

- **Ductility:** Ability to be drawn into wires under tensile load. Measured by **% Elongation** and **% Reduction in Area**.

$$\% \text{ Elongation} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction in Area} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$

- **Malleability:** Ability to be rolled or hammered into thin sheets under compressive load (e.g., gold, aluminium).
- **Brittleness:** Opposite of ductility. Material fractures with little plastic deformation (e.g., glass, cast iron, ceramics).

#### F. Toughness

Toughness is the ability of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform **before fracturing**. Mathematically, it is the **total area under the stress–strain curve up to fracture**.

$$U_{\text{tough}} = \int_0^{\epsilon_f} \sigma \, d\epsilon \quad [\text{J/m}^3 \text{ or N·m/m}^3]$$

#### G. Resilience

Resilience is the ability to absorb energy in the **elastic region** (i.e., the area under the elastic portion of the stress–strain curve).

$$U_{\text{res}} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \epsilon_y = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\sigma_y^2}{E} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

**Modulus of Resilience** = $U_{\text{res}}$ per unit volume.

> [!NOTE]
> A material with a high yield strength and low modulus (like a spring) is highly resilient.

#### H. Hardness

Hardness is the resistance of a material to **localized plastic deformation** (e.g., scratching, indentation, abrasion).

Common Hardness Tests (must know for KTU):

| **Test** | **Indenter** | **Load** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brinell (BHN)** | Hardened steel ball (10 mm) | 500–3000 kgf | Cast iron, mild steel, non-ferrous metals |
| **Rockwell (RHN)** | Diamond cone or steel ball | 60–150 kgf | Hardened steels, brass, aluminium |
| **Vickers (VHN)** | Diamond pyramid | 1–100 kgf | Hardened surfaces, thin sheets, ceramic coatings |
| **Knoop (KHN)** | Elongated diamond pyramid | 25 g – 1 kg | Microhardness, brittle materials |
| **Mohs Scale** | Scratch comparison | — | Minerals, ceramics |

**Brinell Hardness Number (BHN):**

$$BHN = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$$

where $P$ = applied load, $D$ = ball diameter, $d$ = indentation diameter.

**Vickers Hardness Number (VHN):**

$$VHN = \frac{1.8544 \cdot P}{d^2}$$

where $P$ = applied load (kgf) and $d$ = diagonal of indentation (mm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, the relationship between BHN and UTS for **steel** is:
> $$\text{UTS (MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times \text{BHN}$$
> This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

#### I. Impact Strength (Toughness at High Strain Rate)

Tested using either **Izod** or **Charpy** tests, both of which measure the energy absorbed by a notched specimen broken by a swinging pendulum.

Energy absorbed: $E = m g (h_1 - h_2)$, measured in **Joules (J)**.

- **Izod:** Specimen held as a **vertical cantilever**, notch facing the striker.
- **Charpy:** Specimen held as a **horizontal beam**, supported at both ends, notch opposite to striker.

> [!NOTE]
> Charpy is more commonly tested in KTU questions. A **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** is observed in BCC metals (e.g., mild steel) — a critical concept for ship hulls in cold waters.

#### J. Fatigue

Fatigue is the failure of a material under **repeated/cyclic loading** at stresses well below the static yield strength. Common in aircraft wings, bridges, rotating shafts.

- **Fatigue Limit (Endurance Limit):** The maximum stress below which the material can withstand an **infinite** number of cycles (typically 10⁶ – 10⁷ cycles). Exists for **ferrous metals** (steel, iron). Non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu) have **no true fatigue limit** — they show a continuously decreasing S-N curve.

- **S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve):** Plots stress amplitude (S) vs. number of cycles to failure (N).

#### K. Creep

Creep is the **time-dependent permanent deformation** of a material under a **constant load at elevated temperatures** (typically $T > 0.4 T_m$, where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin). Critical in:
- Steam turbine blades
- Rocket nozzles
- Nuclear reactor pressure vessels
- Furnace components

The **creep curve** has three stages: **Primary (decelerating creep), Secondary (steady-state creep), Tertiary (accelerating creep → rupture)**.

#### L. Stress Relaxation

Distinct from creep: the **total strain is kept constant** over time, and the resulting stress gradually decreases.

$$\text{Creep} \Rightarrow \text{Constant Stress} \rightarrow \text{Increasing Strain}$$
$$\text{Stress Relaxation} \Rightarrow \text{Constant Strain} \rightarrow \text{Decreasing Stress}$$

### 2.2 Physical and Chemical Properties — A Brief Overview

| **Property** | **Definition** | **Typical Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **Density ($\rho$)** | Mass per unit volume | Steel: 7.85 g/cm³, Al: 2.7 g/cm³, Cu: 8.96 g/cm³ |
| **Specific Gravity** | Ratio of material density to water density | Dimensionless |
| **Melting Point** | Temperature at which solid → liquid | W: 3422°C, Fe: 1538°C, Al: 660°C |
| **Thermal Conductivity ($k$)** | Rate of heat flow per unit area per unit temperature gradient | Cu: 401 W/m·K, Steel: 50 W/m·K, Glass: 1 W/m·K |
| **Thermal Expansion ($\alpha$)** | Strain produced per °C rise in temperature | Brass: 19 × 10⁻⁶/°C, Invar: 1.2 × 10⁻⁶/°C |
| **Electrical Resistivity ($\rho_e$)** | Resistance of a unit cube of material | Cu: 1.7 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m, Mica: 10¹⁴ Ω·m |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Ability to resist attack by environment | Au, Pt, Stainless steel, Ti, Polymers |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = P/A_0$ | Engineering stress | MPa (N/mm²) |
| 2 | $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \sigma / \epsilon$ | Young's Modulus | GPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_T = P / A_i$ | True stress | MPa |
| 5 | $\epsilon_T = \ln(L_f/L_0)$ | True strain | dimensionless |
| 6 | $U_{\text{res}} = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ | Modulus of resilience | J/m³ |
| 7 | $U_{\text{tough}} = \int \sigma \, d\epsilon$ | Toughness (area under curve) | J/m³ |
| 8 | $BHN = 2P / [\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$ | Brinell hardness | kgf/mm² |
| 9 | $VHN = 1.8544 P / d^2$ | Vickers hardness | kgf/mm² |
| 10 | $\%EL = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | Percentage elongation | % |
| 11 | $\%RA = (A_0 - A_f)/A_0 \times 100$ | Percentage reduction in area | % |
| 12 | UTS ≈ 3.45 × BHN | For steels | MPa |
| 13 | $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$ | Thermal stress | MPa |
| 14 | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion | m |
| 15 | $\rho = m / V$ | Density | kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - At the **Proportional Limit:** Hooke's Law valid ($\sigma = E\epsilon$)
> - At the **Elastic Limit:** Material fully recovers shape
> - At the **Yield Point:** Plastic deformation begins
> - At **UTS:** Maximum load carrying capacity
> - At **Fracture Point:** Material breaks

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The systematic study of material properties enables:

1. **Safe Design:** Bridges, aircraft, and pressure vessels are designed with a **Factor of Safety (FoS)**, where the working stress = (Ultimate stress) / FoS. Typical FoS = 2 to 4 for static loads, and 10 to 15 for fatigue/impact loads.
2. **Material Selection:** A design engineer uses an **Ashby chart** (Strength vs. Density) to pick the optimal material.
3. **Failure Analysis:** By examining a fracture surface, engineers can deduce whether failure was due to overload, fatigue, creep, or corrosion.
4. **Process Optimization:** Hot working, cold working, annealing, and quenching are all chosen based on how they alter the material's properties.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Engineering Stress–Strain and True Stress–Strain

Let a cylindrical specimen of original length $L_0$ and original cross-sectional area $A_0$ be subjected to a tensile load $P$.

**Step 1: Engineering Stress Definition**

The engineering stress is defined as the load divided by the **original** cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma_E = \frac{P}{A_0}$$

**Step 2: Engineering Strain Definition**

The engineering strain is the change in length divided by the **original** length:

$$\epsilon_E = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Step 3: True Stress Definition**

As the specimen is loaded, its cross-section decreases. The instantaneous area is $A_i$. The true stress is:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{P}{A_i}$$

**Step 4: Volume Constancy Assumption**

For plastic deformation, the material is assumed to be incompressible, so the volume remains constant:

$$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i}$$

**Step 5: Substituting into True Stress**

$$\sigma_T = \frac{P}{A_0 L_0 / L_i} = \frac{P L_i}{A_0 L_0}$$

$$\sigma_T = \sigma_E \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0} = \sigma_E \cdot (1 + \epsilon_E)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final relationship:**
> $$\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \epsilon_E)$$
> This is a **must-derive** result for KTU Module 1.

**Step 6: True Strain Derivation**

Consider the specimen divided into $n$ equal increments. In each increment $dL$:

$$d\epsilon_T = \frac{dL}{L}$$

Integrating from $L_0$ to $L_f$:

$$\epsilon_T = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 7: Relating True Strain to Engineering Strain**

Since $L_f = L_0 (1 + \epsilon_E)$:

$$\epsilon_T = \ln\left(\frac{L_0(1+\epsilon_E)}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \epsilon_E)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final relationship:**
> $$\epsilon_T = \ln(1 + \epsilon_E)$$

**Step 8: Generalised Power Law (Hollomon Equation)**

In the plastic region, the true stress–true strain curve can be approximated by:

$$\sigma_T = K \cdot \epsilon_T^n$$

where $K$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = strain hardening exponent ($0 < n < 1$).

For a perfectly plastic material: $n = 0$.
For a perfectly elastic material: $n = 1$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Modulus of Resilience

The modulus of resilience is the area under the **elastic portion** of the stress–strain curve (a right triangle in the linear region).

**Step 1:** The elastic region is bounded by $0 \le \epsilon \le \epsilon_y$ and $0 \le \sigma \le \sigma_y$, and the curve is a straight line from origin.

**Step 2:** The area of this triangle is:

$$U_{\text{res}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \epsilon_y \cdot \sigma_y$$

**Step 3:** Since in the elastic region, Hooke's Law gives $\epsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$:

$$U_{\text{res}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \sigma_y = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Modulus of Resilience:**
> $$U_{\text{res}} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} \quad [\text{J/m}^3]$$
> This energy is **recoverable** (i.e., the material can release it on unloading).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A steel bar of diameter 20 mm and gauge length 200 mm is subjected to a tensile load of 80 kN. The bar elongates by 0.30 mm and its diameter reduces to 19.85 mm under load. Calculate:
(a) Engineering stress
(b) Engineering strain
(c) True stress
(d) True strain
(e) Young's modulus (given proportional limit at this load)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Original Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Engineering Stress (Part a)**

$$\sigma_E = \frac{P}{A_0} = \frac{80{,}000}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 254.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Engineering Strain (Part b)**

$$\epsilon_E = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.30 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Instantaneous Area (Part c, prep)**

$$A_i = \frac{\pi}{4} d_i^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.01985)^2 = 3.0945 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: True Stress (Part c)**

$$\sigma_T = \frac{P}{A_i} = \frac{80{,}000}{3.0945 \times 10^{-4}} = 258.52 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: True Strain (Part d)**

$$\epsilon_T = \ln(1 + \epsilon_E) = \ln(1 + 0.0015) = \ln(1.0015) \approx 1.4989 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 7: Young's Modulus (Part e)**

$$E = \frac{\sigma_E}{\epsilon_E} = \frac{254.65 \times 10^6}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 169.77 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A small increase in true stress over engineering stress (~1.5%) and a nearly equal true strain (since $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ for small $x$) are characteristic of elastic deformation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Material Behaviours

| **Property** | **Mild Steel (Ductile)** | **Cast Iron (Brittle)** | **Aluminium Alloy** | **Copper** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus, $E$ (GPa) | 200 | 100–170 | 70 | 110 |
| Yield Strength, $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | 250 | — (no yield) | 200 | 70 |
| UTS, $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | 410–500 | 150–400 | 250 | 220 |
| % Elongation | 25–30 | <1 | 12–20 | 40–50 |
| Toughness | High | Low | Medium | High |
| Hardness (BHN) | 120–150 | 180–250 | 50–80 | 40 |
| Failure Type | Cup-and-cone | Flat brittle | Cup-and-cone | Cup-and-cone |
| Fatigue Limit (MPa) | ~210 | ~80 | ~110 | ~70 |

### 3.5 Conversion Logic Summary for True Stress/Strain

The complete set of inter-conversions for KTU problem-solving:

$$\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \epsilon_E)$$

$$\epsilon_T = \ln(1 + \epsilon_E)$$

$$\sigma_T = \sigma_E \cdot e^{\epsilon_T}$$

$$\epsilon_T = \ln\left(\frac{\sigma_T}{\sigma_E}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above four equations are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU 14-mark Part B questions. Memorize the derivation logic, not just the result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Material Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Engineering Material Properties"] --> B["Mechanical Properties"]
    A --> C["Physical Properties"]
    A --> D["Chemical Properties"]
    A --> E["Thermal Properties"]
    A --> F["Electrical Properties"]
    A --> G["Magnetic Properties"]
    A --> H["Technological Properties"]

    B --> B1["Strength: Tensile, Compressive, Shear, Torsional, Fatigue"]
    B --> B2["Hardness: Brinell, Rockwell, Vickers, Mohs, Knoop"]
    B --> B3["Ductility and Malleability"]
    B --> B4["Toughness and Resilience"]
    B --> B5["Stiffness: Modulus E, G, K"]
    B --> B6["Brittleness"]
    B --> B7["Creep Resistance"]
    B --> B8["Fatigue Endurance"]

    C --> C1["Density and Specific Gravity"]
    C --> C2["Melting and Boiling Points"]
    C --> C3["Colour and Lustre"]

    D --> D1["Corrosion Resistance"]
    D --> D2["Oxidation Resistance"]
    D --> D3["Acidity and Alkalinity"]

    E --> E1["Thermal Conductivity"]
    E --> E2["Specific Heat Capacity"]
    E --> E3["Thermal Expansion Coefficient"]

    F --> F1["Electrical Resistivity"]
    F --> F2["Conductivity"]
    F --> F3["Dielectric Strength"]

    G --> G1["Permeability"]
    G --> G2["Coercivity"]
    G --> G3["Hysteresis Loss"]

    H --> H1["Castability"]
    H --> H2["Weldability"]
    H --> H3["Machinability"]
    H --> H4["Formability"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stress–Strain Response for Ductile Material

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Axial Load P Applied"] --> SA["Calculate Stress sigma = P by A0"]
    SA --> SP["Proportional Region: sigma equals E times epsilon"]
    SP --> SE["Elastic Region: Full Recovery on Unloading"]
    SE --> SY["Yield Point: Plastic Deformation Begins"]
    SY --> SS["Strain Hardening: Material Strengthens with Deformation"]
    SS --> SU["Ultimate Tensile Strength: Maximum Load Carried"]
    SU --> SN["Necking: Localized Cross Section Reduction"]
    SN --> SF["Fracture Point: Material Breaks"]
    SF --> OUT["Engineering Stress Strain Curve Plotted"]

    SP --- E1["Hookes Law Valid"]
    SE --- E2["Energy Stored equals Modulus of Resilience"]
    SY --- E3["Upper and Lower Yield Points in Mild Steel"]
    SS --- E4["Strain Hardening Exponent n applies"]
    SU --- E5["True Stress Differs from Engineering Stress"]
    SN --- E6["True Strain becomes Significant"]
    SF --- E7["Energy Absorbed equals Toughness"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Failure Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD["Component Under Service Load"] --> Q1{"Loading Type?"}
    Q1 -- Static --> Q1A{"Material Behaviour?"}
    Q1 -- Cyclic --> CYC["Fatigue Failure: Beach Marks and Striations Visible"]
    Q1 -- Impact --> IMP["Impact Failure: Brittle Cleavage or Ductile Dimples"]
    Q1 -- Sustained High Temperature --> CRP["Creep Failure: Intergranular Fracture"]

    Q1A -- Ductile --> Q1A1["Yielding and Plastic Deformation"]
    Q1A1 --> CUP["Cup and Cone Fracture with 45 degree Shear Lip"]
    Q1A -- Brittle --> Q1A2["Sudden Fracture with No Plastic Deformation"]
    Q1A2 --> FLAT["Flat Fracture Surface Perpendicular to Load"]

    CYC --> FL1["Check S-N Curve for Endurance Limit"]
    IMP --> FL2["Check DBTT for Service Temperature"]
    CRP --> FL3["Check Larson Miller Parameter for Life Prediction"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Material Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REQ["Identify Design Requirements"] --> CL["List Required Properties"]
    CL --> SF["Determine Strength and Safety Factor"]
    CL --> EN["Determine Environmental Conditions"]
    CL --> MF["Determine Manufacturing Constraints"]
    SF --> CH["Use Ashby Charts for Material Filtering"]
    EN --> CH
    MF --> CH
    CH --> SEL["Shortlist Candidate Materials"]
    SEL --> TST["Conduct Prototype Testing"]
    TST --> DEC["Final Material Selection and Documentation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `B1`, `E1`, `CYC`) and avoid reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, and `style` as standalone names. Labels are clean alphanumeric text without markdown formatting characters.

### 4.5 Textual Description of the Stress–Strain Curve (For Reference & Sketches)

For a **ductile material** like mild steel, the stress–strain curve has the following key points (in sequence):

1. **Origin (O):** Zero stress, zero strain.
2. **Proportional Limit (A):** Highest stress at which stress is proportional to strain. Beyond this, the curve deviates from linearity.
3. **Elastic Limit (B):** Maximum stress for full recovery of shape. (For mild steel, very close to A.)
4. **Upper Yield Point (C):** Stress at which plastic deformation begins abruptly.
5. **Lower Yield Point (D):** Lower stress at which yielding continues at constant load.
6. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (E):** Maximum stress the material can withstand.
7. **Fracture Point (F):** Stress at which the material breaks.

The region from **O to B** is **elastic**. The region from **B to F** is **plastic**. The area under the curve from O to B is the **modulus of resilience**. The total area under the curve from O to F is the **toughness**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** Differentiate between **ductility** and **malleability**. Give one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Ductility** | **Malleability** |
|---|---|
| Ability of a material to deform **plastically under tensile stress** and be drawn into wires. | Ability of a material to deform **plastically under compressive stress** and be rolled/hammered into thin sheets. |
| Quantified by **% elongation** and **% reduction in area**. | Quantified by how thin a sheet can be made without cracking. |
| **Example:** Copper wires for electrical wiring. | **Example:** Aluminium foil for food packaging. |

> Both are forms of plasticity, but they differ in the **type of applied stress** (tensile vs. compressive).

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q:** Define **modulus of resilience** and state its mathematical expression. Why is it important for spring design?

**Model Answer:**

The **modulus of resilience** is the energy absorbed per unit volume of a material **within the elastic limit** (i.e., the area under the elastic portion of the stress–strain curve). It represents the maximum energy a material can absorb **without permanent deformation**.

$$U_{\text{res}} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \epsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

**Importance in Spring Design:**
Springs are designed to absorb and release energy repeatedly without permanent deformation. A high modulus of resilience means the spring can store more energy per unit volume per cycle. Therefore, spring materials (e.g., spring steel, music wire) are selected to have **high yield strength** and **low modulus of elasticity**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q:** (a) Draw a neat labelled stress–strain curve for a **mild steel specimen** tested under uni-axial tension. Explain the significance of each region and the key points on the curve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) A cylindrical specimen of mild steel of gauge diameter 20 mm and gauge length 50 mm was tested in tension. The following data were recorded at failure: Final gauge length = 65 mm, final diameter = 15 mm. Determine: (i) % Elongation, (ii) % Reduction in Area, (iii) Is the material ductile or brittle? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel:**

*Refer to the textual description in Section 4.5 above for the labelled curve.*

Key Points and their Significance:

| **Point** | **Significance** |
|---|---|
| **O – Origin** | Zero stress, zero strain |
| **A – Proportional Limit** | Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\epsilon$) valid up to this point |
| **B – Elastic Limit** | Maximum stress for complete recovery of original shape |
| **C – Upper Yield Point** | Stress at which plastic deformation begins suddenly |
| **D – Lower Yield Point** | Stress at which yielding continues steadily |
| **E – UTS** | Maximum stress the material can withstand |
| **F – Fracture Point** | Stress at which material breaks |
| **Region O to B** | **Elastic region** – no permanent deformation |
| **Region B to F** | **Plastic region** – permanent deformation occurs |
| **Area under O–B** | **Modulus of resilience** ($\sigma_y^2/2E$) |
| **Area under O–F** | **Toughness** of the material |
