The mild steel shows **yielding phenomenon** (sharp drop from C to D) due to **dislocation pinning by interstitial carbon/nitrogen atoms (Cottrell atmospheres)**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Given Data**

- Original diameter, $d_0 = 20$ mm, hence $A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$
- Original gauge length, $L_0 = 50$ mm
- Final gauge length, $L_f = 65$ mm
- Final diameter, $d_f = 15$ mm, hence $A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(15)^2 = 176.71 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 2: % Elongation (i)**

$$\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{65 - 50}{50} \times 100 = \frac{15}{50} \times 100 = 30\%$$

**Step 3: % Reduction in Area (ii)**

$$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.16 - 176.71}{314.16} \times 100 = \frac{137.45}{314.16} \times 100 = 43.75\%$$

**Step 4: Ductility Assessment (iii)**

Since $\%EL = 30\%$ (which is well above the typical threshold of 5% for ductile materials) and $\%RA = 43.75\%$, the material is **clearly ductile**. It underwent substantial plastic deformation before fracture, characteristic of mild steel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Rule (KTU Standard):**
> - $\%EL \geq 5\%$ → Ductile
> - $\%EL < 5\%$ → Brittle

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Q:** (a) What is **hardness**? Compare the **Brinell**, **Rockwell**, and **Vickers** hardness tests in terms of indenter, load, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) A Brinell hardness test was performed on a steel specimen using a 10 mm diameter hardened steel ball and a load of 3000 kgf. The diameter of the indentation was measured as 4.5 mm. Calculate the BHN. Also, estimate the ultimate tensile strength of the steel. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Hardness and Comparison:**

**Definition of Hardness:**
Hardness is the resistance of a material to **localized plastic deformation** caused by mechanical indentation, scratching, or abrasion. It is a surface property and is closely related to the material's wear resistance.

**Comparison of Hardness Tests:**

| **Feature** | **Brinell** | **Rockwell** | **Vickers** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Indenter** | Hardened steel ball (10 mm) or tungsten carbide ball | Diamond cone (Brale) for hard materials, steel ball for soft | Diamond pyramid (square base) |
| **Load** | 500–3000 kgf | 60, 100, 150 kgf (major) | 1–100 kgf |
| **Hardness Number** | BHN = $2P / [\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$ | Direct reading on dial (RH_A, RH_B, RH_C scales) | VHN = $1.8544 P / d^2$ |
| **Applications** | Cast iron, mild steel, non-ferrous metals in bulk | Hardened steels, surface-hardened parts, production line testing | Hardened surfaces, thin sheets, ceramic coatings, microhardness |
| **Specimen** | Large, rough surfaces acceptable | Small, finished surfaces required | Very small or thin samples, brittle materials |
| **Indentation size** | Large (visible to naked eye) | Small | Very small (precise) |
| **Disadvantage** | Slow, not for hard materials (>450 BHN) | Different scales needed for different materials | Requires careful surface preparation and measurement |

> [!NOTE]
> Rockwell is the **fastest and most widely used** in industry due to direct digital readout. Vickers is preferred for **research and microhardness**. Brinell is preferred for **rough, coarse-grained materials** like cast iron.

**Part (b) — Brinell Hardness Calculation:**

**Step 1: Given Data**

- Ball diameter, $D = 10$ mm
- Applied load, $P = 3000$ kgf
- Indentation diameter, $d = 4.5$ mm

**Step 2: Apply the Brinell Formula**

$$BHN = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $D^2 - d^2$**

$$D^2 - d^2 = (10)^2 - (4.5)^2 = 100 - 20.25 = 79.75 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Calculate $\sqrt{D^2 - d^2}$**

$$\sqrt{79.75} = 8.9306 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Calculate $D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}$**

$$D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = 10 - 8.9306 = 1.0694 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Calculate BHN**

$$BHN = \frac{2 \times 3000}{\pi \times 10 \times 1.0694} = \frac{6000}{33.59} = 178.6 \approx 179$$

**Step 7: Estimate UTS using empirical relation for steel**

$$UTS = 3.45 \times BHN = 3.45 \times 179 = 617.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity check:** For Brinell test, the indentation diameter $d$ should be in the range $0.25D < d < 0.5D$, i.e., $2.5 \text{ mm} < d < 5.0 \text{ mm}$. Here $d = 4.5$ mm ✓, so the test result is valid.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m, kgf with N, MPa with GPa. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per unit error. Always convert all quantities to a **single consistent system (SI preferred)** before calculation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Draw the Stress-Strain Curve:** In any 7-mark question on ductile material behaviour, the **labelled diagram is mandatory** and carries **at least 2 marks**. A correct answer without the diagram is capped at ~5/7.
>
> 3. **Confusing Stress vs. Strain Notations:** Writing $E = \sigma / \epsilon$ but then calling it "stiffness" without clarifying it's the **Young's Modulus**.
>
> 4. **True Stress vs. Engineering Stress:** Many students write the engineering stress formula and label it as "true stress." Remember: **True stress uses the *instantaneous* (reduced) area**, not the original area.
>
> 5. **BHN Formula Errors:** A common error is forgetting the factor of 2 in the numerator, or using $d$ instead of $D$ in the term $(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})$. Both errors lead to **wrong answers**.
>
> 6. **Misinterpreting Ductility vs. Malleability:** Ductility = tensile deformation (wires); Malleability = compressive deformation (sheets). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 7. **Skipping Boundary Conditions:** Failing to verify that the indentation diameter $d$ lies in the valid range $(0.25D$ to $0.5D)$ for Brinell test, or that the strain is in the valid range for stress–strain analysis.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for Module 1 – Properties of Engineering Materials:**

- [x] **Stress** = Load / Original Area; **Strain** = Change in Length / Original Length.
- [x] **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\epsilon$ (valid only up to the proportional limit).
- [x] **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Slope of the linear elastic region of the stress–strain curve. Unit: GPa. Higher $E$ = stiffer material.
- [x] **Modulus of Resilience** = Area under elastic region = $\sigma_y^2 / (2E)$. Energy stored elastically.
- [x] **Toughness** = Total area under stress–strain curve up to fracture. Energy absorbed before breaking.
- [x] **Ductility** = % Elongation, % Reduction in Area. Material stretches under tension. Example: copper.
- [x] **Malleability** = Compressive plasticity. Material rolled into sheets. Example: aluminium.
- [x] **Brittleness** = Fracture with little plastic deformation. Example: cast iron, glass, ceramics.
- [x] **Hardness Tests:** Brinell (steel ball, BHN), Rockwell (diamond cone/steel ball, RHN), Vickers (diamond pyramid, VHN), Knoop (elongated diamond, KHN), Mohs (scratch scale).
- [x] **BHN Formula:** $BHN = 2P / [\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$. Validity: $0.25D < d < 0.5D$.
- [x] **VHN Formula:** $VHN = 1.8544 P / d^2$.
- [x] **UTS ≈ 3.45 × BHN** for steels (very frequently asked 3-mark question).
- [x] **Fatigue Limit (Endurance Limit):** Exists for ferrous metals (steel), absent for non-ferrous (Al, Cu).
- [x] **Creep:** Time-dependent deformation at high temperature ($T > 0.4 T_m$). Three stages: Primary, Secondary, Tertiary.
- [x] **Izod vs. Charpy:** Both measure impact energy. Charpy uses horizontal beam, Izod uses vertical cantilever.
- [x] **True Stress** = $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \epsilon_E)$; **True Strain** = $\epsilon_T = \ln(1 + \epsilon_E)$.
- [x] **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT):** Critical for BCC metals; design must keep service temperature above DBTT.
- [x] **Thermal Stress:** $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$ — develops when thermal expansion is constrained.
- [x] **Engineering materials are selected** based on mechanical, physical, chemical, and manufacturing properties using **Ashby charts**.
- [x] **Hardness correlates with strength** — a hard material is generally a strong material (e.g., ceramics, hardened steels).
- [x] **Stiffness ≠ Strength:** Diamond is very stiff and very strong; rubber is not stiff but can be tough; mild steel is both stiff and strong.
- [x] **Mild steel shows yielding** due to Cottrell atmospheres pinning dislocations; this is why it has an upper and lower yield point.
- [x] **Strain Hardening Exponent ($n$):** $0 < n < 1$. Higher $n$ = better formability (e.g., $n \approx 0.2$ for steel, $n \approx 0.33$ for austenitic stainless steel).
- [x] **Ceramics and glasses** are typically brittle (low toughness) but very hard, with high strength in compression and very low in tension.
- [x] **Polymers** have low $E$ (1–5 GPa), low strength, and high ductility compared to metals.
- [x] **Composites** (e.g., CFRP, GFRP) have high strength-to-weight ratio, used in aerospace and sports goods.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Properties - Isotropy and Anisotropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties - Isotropy and Anisotropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of **Material Science** and **Solid Mechanics**, the directional dependence of physical and mechanical properties of a material is described by two fundamental concepts:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISOTROPY** — A material is said to be **isotropic** if its physical and mechanical properties (such as Young's Modulus, Yield Strength, Thermal Conductivity, Electrical Resistivity, Refractive Index) are **identical in all directions** of measurement. The property tensor reduces to a scalar, meaning the response of the material is independent of the orientation of the applied load or measurement axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ANISOTROPY** — A material is said to be **anisotropic** (also called *aeolotropic*) if its physical and mechanical properties **vary with the direction of measurement**. The property must be described by a tensor rather than a scalar, and the material response depends explicitly on the orientation of the applied stimulus relative to the internal crystal structure.

In formal continuum mechanics terms:

$$\text{Isotropic Material} \Rightarrow \text{Property} = f(\text{Magnitude only, not direction})$$

$$\text{Anisotropic Material} \Rightarrow \text{Property} = f(\text{Magnitude AND direction})$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are eating a **chocolate bar** 🍫:

- **Isotropic Material Analogy** — A completely **homogeneous, randomly-mixed fruit cake** 🍰. Whether you press it from the top, side, or bottom, it gives the same softness. There is no preferred direction — every bite feels identical in texture.

- **Anisotropic Material Analogy** — A **stack of paper sheets or plywood**. When you pull along the plane of the sheets (parallel to the grain), the material is strong. But if you try to pull perpendicular to the sheets (across the layers), the sheets easily separate. The strength DEPENDS on the direction of your pull.

### 1.3 Standardized Property Metrics (KTU Board Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terms You Must Memorize:**
> - **Isotropy Index (Aᵢ)** — Quantitative measure used to compare degree of anisotropy in materials. A value of **Aᵢ = 1** indicates a perfectly isotropic material; deviations from unity indicate anisotropy.
> - **Elastic Symmetry** — The number of independent elastic constants required to fully describe the material's stress-strain response.
> - **Degree of Anisotropy** — Ratio of maximum to minimum property value measured along different crystallographic directions.

### 1.4 Real-World Examples Relevant to KTU 2024 Scheme

| Material Type | Behavior | Example |
|---------------|----------|---------|
| Amorphous Glass | **Isotropic** | Window glass, fused silica |
| Random Polycrystalline Metal (annealed) | **Quasi-Isotropic** | Annealed aluminium, copper |
| Single Crystal | **Strongly Anisotropic** | Single crystal silicon, sapphire |
| Natural Wood | **Highly Anisotropic** | Wood (strong along grain, weak across) |
| Fiber-Reinforced Composites | **Engineered Anisotropic** | CFRP, GFRP laminates |
| Cubic Crystal Polycrystalline | **Isotropic (averaged)** | Tungsten, α-iron (BCC) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Microstructural Origin of Isotropy vs Anisotropy

The root cause of isotropy/anisotropy lies in the **atomic arrangement**:

1. **Amorphous Materials** (e.g., glass, polymers) → Random atomic arrangement → **Macroscopically Isotropic**
2. **Polycrystalline Materials with Random Grain Orientation** → Statistical averaging of randomly-oriented crystals → **Quasi-Isotropic** (only when grains are small compared to specimen and randomly oriented)
3. **Single Crystals** → Ordered, periodic atomic arrangement → **Strongly Anisotropic**
4. **Textured Polycrystals** (preferred grain orientation) → **Anisotropic**
5. **Composite Materials** (fibers in a matrix) → **Anisotropic by design**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pearl**: A polycrystalline metal with randomly oriented grains behaves as an **isotropic** aggregate because the random orientation averages out the directional dependence of individual crystals. This is why annealed copper and aluminium are treated as isotropic in most engineering calculations.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation — Hooke's Law Generalization

For an **isotropic** material under 3D loading, generalized Hooke's Law simplifies to two independent constants (E and ν, or equivalently λ and μ):

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = **Lamé's first constant** (units: Pa)
- $\mu$ = **Shear modulus (G)** (units: Pa)
- $\delta_{ij}$ = **Kronecker delta** ($\delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i = j$, else $0$)

For a fully **anisotropic** material, the most general form of Hooke's Law is a fourth-order tensor equation:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{3}\sum_{l=1}^{3} C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$

Or in compact tensor notation:

$$\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$

The stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$ has, in principle, $3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 81$ components.

### 2.3 Reduction of Independent Elastic Constants (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry arguments reduce the number of independent elastic constants dramatically:**

| Material Symmetry Class | Independent Elastic Constants | Engineering Examples |
|------------------------|------------------------------:|----------------------|
| **General Triclinic** (no symmetry) | **21** | Highly unusual crystals |
| **Monoclinic** | 13 | Gypsum, orthoclase |
| **Orthorhombic** | 9 | Uranium, α-Uranium |
| **Tetragonal** | 6 | Indium, tin (β-Sn) |
| **Hexagonal** | **5** | Zinc, titanium, ice |
| **Cubic** (single crystal) | **3** | Copper, aluminium, α-iron, tungsten |
| **Isotropic** | **2** (E and ν) | Glass, amorphous polymers |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE 2024**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Isotropic Young's Modulus (relation to G, K) | $E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Pa, single scalar |
| 2 | Poisson's ratio (isotropic) | $-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | General anisotropic Hooke's law | $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$ | 4th order tensor |
| 4 | Number of independent Cᵢⱼₖₗ (general) | $\leq 21$ | After symmetry |
| 5 | Isotropic case independent constants | **2** (E, ν) | Drastic reduction |
| 6 | Anisotropy ratio (definition) | $A_r = \dfrac{E_{\max}}{E_{\min}}$ | Ratio $\geq 1$ |
| 7 | Elastic anisotropy factor (Zener) | $A_Z = \dfrac{2C_{44}}{C_{11} - C_{12}}$ | For cubic crystals |
| 8 | Isotropic Zener ratio | $A_Z = 1$ | Perfect isotropy |
| 9 | Bulk modulus (isotropic) | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Pa |
| 10 | Shear modulus (isotropic) | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Pa |
| 11 | Shear modulus from Lamé constants | $G = \mu$ | Pa |
| 12 | Lamé λ relation | $\lambda = \dfrac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | Pa |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Composite Design (Aerospace)** — Engineers exploit anisotropy to design CFRP laminates that are stiff along load-bearing directions and compliant in others, achieving 30–50% weight savings over aluminium.
- **Sheet Metal Forming** — Anisotropy in deep-drawing steels (Lankford coefficient / r-value) governs drawability and earing behavior.
- **Geophysics & Oil Exploration** — Seismic wave propagation depends on elastic anisotropy of rock strata.
- **Crystal Plasticity (FEA)** — Modern simulations (e.g., crystal plasticity FEM) explicitly account for grain-level anisotropy to predict formability and failure.
- **Optoelectronics** — Sapphire (anisotropic) used as substrate; refractive index varies with crystal direction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Reduction from 81 to 2 Elastic Constants

We will derive, step-by-step, how the fourth-order stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$ reduces from 81 components to just 2 independent scalars for an isotropic material.

#### Step 1 — Start with the most general fourth-order tensor form

For any linear elastic material, the most general stress-strain relationship is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$

The stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$ has $3^4 = 81$ components in 3D space.

#### Step 2 — Apply Stress Symmetry

By definition of the stress tensor (balance of angular momentum):

$$\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$$

This symmetry means:

$$C_{ijkl} = C_{jikl}$$

The pair $(i,j)$ is symmetric, so the stiffness tensor is symmetric under swap of the first two indices. This reduces independent components from 81 to:

$$\text{After stress symmetry} = \frac{3 \times (3+1)}{2} \times 3 \times 3 = 6 \times 9 = 54 \text{ components}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Strain Symmetry

By definition of the small-strain tensor:

$$\varepsilon_{kl} = \varepsilon_{lk}$$

This means:

$$C_{ijkl} = C_{ijlk}$$

The pair $(k,l)$ is symmetric, reducing further:

$$\text{After both symmetries} = 6 \times 6 = 36 \text{ components}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Existence of Strain Energy Density (Major Symmetry)

For a hyperelastic material (conservative elastic response), there exists a strain energy density function $U(\varepsilon)$ such that:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \frac{\partial U}{\partial \varepsilon_{ij}}$$

This requires that second partial derivatives commute, giving the **major symmetry**:

$$C_{ijkl} = C_{klij}$$

Combined with previous symmetries, the stiffness tensor now has the full symmetry of a $6 \times 6$ symmetric matrix:

$$\text{Independent components} = \frac{6 \times (6+1)}{2} = \frac{42}{2} = 21 \text{ constants}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **At this stage, the most general anisotropic linear elastic material has exactly 21 independent elastic constants.** This is the maximum number for any crystalline material.

#### Step 5 — Apply Cubic Crystal Symmetry (For Comparison)

A cubic crystal has three orthogonal 4-fold rotation axes along $x$, $y$, $z$. Rotation by 90° about $x$-axis requires:

$$C_{11} = C_{22} = C_{33}$$
$$C_{12} = C_{13} = C_{23}$$
$$C_{44} = C_{55} = C_{66}$$
$$C_{14} = C_{15} = C_{16} = C_{24} = C_{25} = C_{26} = C_{34} = C_{35} = C_{36} = 0$$

This leaves only **3 independent constants** for cubic crystals: $C_{11}$, $C_{12}$, and $C_{44}$.

#### Step 6 — Apply Isotropic Symmetry (All Rotations Invariant)

For an **isotropic** material, the stress-strain relation must be invariant under ALL rotations (not just 90° rotations). The only tensor that is invariant under arbitrary rotation and has the full index symmetry of $C_{ijkl}$ is the combination of the Kronecker delta products:

$$C_{ijkl} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \delta_{kl} + \mu (\delta_{ik}\delta_{jl} + \delta_{il}\delta_{jk})$$

Substituting back into Hooke's Law:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \delta_{kl} \, \varepsilon_{kl} + \mu (\delta_{ik}\delta_{jl} + \delta_{il}\delta_{jk}) \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$

#### Step 7 — Simplify the First Term

Using the property $\delta_{kl} \, \varepsilon_{kl} = \varepsilon_{kk} = \varepsilon_{11} + \varepsilon_{22} + \varepsilon_{33}$ (the trace of strain, which is the volumetric strain):

$$\lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk}$$

#### Step 8 — Simplify the Second Term

Using $\delta_{ik}\delta_{jl} \varepsilon_{kl} = \varepsilon_{ij}$ and $\delta_{il}\delta_{jk} \varepsilon_{kl} = \varepsilon_{ji} = \varepsilon_{ij}$:

$$\mu (\varepsilon_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}) = 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

#### Step 9 — Combine to Obtain the Isotropic Hooke's Law

$$\boxed{\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The isotropic material requires exactly **2 independent constants** — namely $\lambda$ and $\mu$ (the two Lamé constants). Engineering practice replaces these with the more intuitive **E (Young's Modulus)** and **$\nu$ (Poisson's Ratio)**.

#### Step 10 — Verify the Reduction Count

| Material Class | Independent Constants | Reduction Ratio |
|----------------|----------------------:|----------------:|
| General anisotropic | 21 | 1.00× |
| Cubic crystal | 3 | 7.00× |
| **Isotropic** | **2** | **10.5×** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pearl**: Many board exam questions test whether students know the sequence: 81 → 54 → 36 → 21 → 3 → 2, and WHY each step is valid. Memorize the symmetry arguments.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Young's Modulus Along [111] Direction in a Cubic Crystal

**Problem:** For a cubic single crystal with stiffness constants $C_{11}$, $C_{12}$, and $C_{44}$, calculate the effective Young's Modulus along the [111] body diagonal direction. Show that for $2C_{44} = C_{11} - C_{12}$ (Zener isotropy condition), the result becomes direction-independent.

#### Step 1 — Direction Cosines for [111]

The unit vector along [111] in a cubic system has components:

$$n_1 = n_2 = n_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

#### Step 2 — Use the Inverse Compliance Formula

For a cubic crystal, the Young's modulus along direction $\vec{n}$ is given by:

$$\frac{1}{E(\vec{n})} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right)\left(n_1^2 n_2^2 + n_2^2 n_3^2 + n_1^2 n_3^2\right)$$

Where $S_{ij}$ are the compliance tensor components. Equivalently, in stiffness form:

$$\frac{1}{E(\vec{n})} = \frac{(C_{11} + 2C_{12})(C_{11} - C_{12}) - (C_{11}-C_{12}-2C_{44}) \Gamma}{(C_{11}+2C_{12})(C_{11}-C_{12}) + (C_{11}-C_{12}-2C_{44})(C_{11}+C_{12}) \Gamma}$$

Where $\Gamma = n_1^2 n_2^2 + n_2^2 n_3^2 + n_1^2 n_3^2$ is the **direction index**.

#### Step 3 — Evaluate $\Gamma$ for [111]

$$\Gamma = n_1^2 n_2^2 + n_2^2 n_3^2 + n_1^2 n_3^2$$

$$\Gamma = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Zener Isotropy Condition

The Zener anisotropy ratio is:

$$A_Z = \frac{2C_{44}}{C_{11} - C_{12}}$$

When $A_Z = 1$ (isotropic limit), the term $(C_{11} - C_{12} - 2C_{44}) = 0$, and the $\Gamma$-dependent term vanishes. Therefore $E(\vec{n})$ becomes independent of direction, confirming isotropy.

#### Step 5 — Numerical Check for Real Materials

| Material | $C_{11}$ (GPa) | $C_{12}$ (GPa) | $C_{44}$ (GPa) | $A_Z$ | Behavior |
|----------|---------------:|---------------:|---------------:|------:|----------|
| Aluminium (FCC) | 108 | 62 | 28 | **1.22** | Near-isotropic |
| Copper (FCC) | 168 | 121 | 75 | **3.21** | Anisotropic |
| α-Iron (BCC) | 237 | 141 | 116 | **2.42** | Anisotropic |
| Tungsten (BCC) | 522 | 204 | 161 | **1.01** | Near-isotropic |
| Hypothetical $A_Z=1$ | — | — | — | **1.00** | Perfectly isotropic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Isotropy vs Anisotropy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Material Under Test] --> B{Atomic Structure Type?}
    B -->|Amorphous| C[Random Atomic Arrangement]
    B -->|Polycrystalline| D{Grain Orientation?}
    B -->|Single Crystal| E[Ordered Crystal Lattice]

    C --> F[ISOTROPIC]
    D -->|Random| F
    D -->|Textured| G[ANISOTROPIC]
    E --> G

    F --> H[Independent Constants: 2]
    F --> I[Examples: Glass, Polymers, Annealed Random Polycrystal]

    G --> J[Independent Constants: 3 to 21]
    G --> K[Examples: Wood, CFRP, Single Crystal Silicon]

    H --> L[Engineering Design: Use scalar E and nu in FEA]
    J --> M[Engineering Design: Use full stiffness tensor C_ijkl]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Elastic Constant Reduction

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[General Anisotropic Material: 81 components] --> N2[Apply Stress Symmetry: 54]
    N2 --> N3[Apply Strain Symmetry: 36]
    N3 --> N4[Apply Energy Symmetry: 21]
    N4 --> N5[Orthorhombic Symmetry: 9]
    N5 --> N6[Hexagonal Symmetry: 5]
    N6 --> N7[Cubic Symmetry: 3]
    N7 --> N8[ISOTROPIC: 2]

    style N1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style N4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style N7 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style N8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Property Comparison Map — Directional Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ISO[ISOTROPIC MATERIAL]
        A1[Property: Scalar Value] --> A2[Same in ALL directions]
        A2 --> A3[Stiffness Matrix: 2 Constants]
    end

    subgraph ANISO[ANISOTROPIC MATERIAL]
        B1[Property: Tensor Value] --> B2[DIFFERENT in different directions]
        B2 --> B3[Stiffness Matrix: Up to 21 Constants]
    end

    ISO --> C[Engineering Implication: Simple FEA]
    ANISO --> D[Engineering Implication: Tensor FEA Required]
```

### 4.4 Zener Anisotropy Index Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[Calculate Zener Index: AZ = 2C44 / C11 - C12] --> Q{AZ equals 1?}
    Q -->|YES| R[ISOTROPIC behavior]
    Q -->|NO| S[ANISOTROPIC behavior]
    Q -->|CLOSE to 1| T[Quasi-isotropic polycrystal]
    S --> U[Direction-dependent E, G, nu]
    R --> V[Direction-INDEPENDENT E, G, nu]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** Physical drawings of 3D stress tensors are difficult to render in plain text. Block-level architecture flows are used as a professional substitute to convey the logical structure of the property classification system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]
Define **isotropy** and **anisotropy** in materials. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

> **Isotropy** is the property of a material where its mechanical and physical responses are identical in all directions of measurement. Examples: (i) Glass, (ii) Annealed copper with randomly oriented grains.
>
> **Anisotropy** is the property of a material where its mechanical and physical responses vary with the direction of measurement. Examples: (i) Wood (strong along grain, weak across), (ii) Single crystal silicon.
>
> **[3 Marks: Definition 1.5 + Examples 1.5]**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
A cubic single crystal has $C_{11} = 168$ GPa, $C_{12} = 121$ GPa, and $C_{44} = 75$ GPa. Calculate the Zener anisotropy index and state whether the material is isotropic or anisotropic.

**Model Answer:**

$$A_Z = \frac{2 C_{44}}{C_{11} - C_{12}} = \frac{2 \times 75}{168 - 121} = \frac{150}{47} = 3.19$$

> Since $A_Z \neq 1$, the material is **anisotropic** (this corresponds to copper).
>
> **[3 Marks: Formula 1 + Substitution 1 + Conclusion 1]**

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Starting from the most general fourth-order stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$ in Hooke's Law, derive step-by-step the reduction of independent elastic constants from 81 to 21 for a general anisotropic material. State all symmetry arguments used. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A polycrystalline copper specimen has randomly oriented grains. Explain, with a labeled sketch, why the macroscopic elastic response of this aggregate is treated as **isotropic**, even though the individual grains (single crystals) are anisotropic. Use the Zener anisotropy index to support your explanation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step derivation of 81 → 21:**

Step 1: General 4th order tensor: $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \varepsilon_{kl}$, giving $3^4 = 81$ components.

Step 2: **Stress symmetry** ($\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$) gives $C_{ijkl} = C_{jikl}$, reducing to 54 components.

Step 3: **Strain symmetry** ($\varepsilon_{kl} = \varepsilon_{lk}$) gives $C_{ijkl} = C_{ijlk}$, reducing to 36 components.

Step 4: **Major symmetry** (from existence of strain energy density $U$ such that $\sigma_{ij} = \partial U / \partial \varepsilon_{ij}$ and equality of mixed partials) gives $C_{ijkl} = C_{klij}$.

Step 5: Combined symmetry makes $C_{ijkl}$ behave as a $6 \times 6$ symmetric matrix:

$$\text{Independent constants} = \frac{6(6+1)}{2} = \frac{42}{2} = 21$$

> **[7 Marks: 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5]**

**(b) Why polycrystalline copper with random grains is macroscopically isotropic:**

Individual copper grains are single crystals with cubic symmetry, having 3 independent elastic constants ($C_{11}, C_{12}, C_{44}$). In a polycrystalline aggregate with **N >> 1 randomly oriented grains**, the elastic response is a **Voigt-Reuss-Hill average** over all grain orientations.

For an aggregate with N randomly oriented grains, the effective stiffness tensor is:

$$C_{ijkl}^{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} a_{im} a_{jn} a_{kp} a_{lq} C_{mnpq}^{(n)}$$

Where $a_{ij}$ are rotation matrices for each grain. After averaging over all possible orientations, all directional dependence cancels by symmetry, leaving:

$$C_{ijkl}^{\text{eff}} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \delta_{kl} + \mu (\delta_{ik}\delta_{jl} + \delta_{il}\delta_{jk})$$

This is the **isotropic form**, depending only on the 2 Lamé constants. Therefore:
- Macroscopic Young's modulus becomes a **scalar** E.
- Zener index $A_Z$ of the effective aggregate → 1.

**Sketch (describe in words for exam):**
```
[Single grain cubic]    [Polycrystal random]    [Macroscopic isotropy]
   A_Z = 3.21         -->   averaged  -->          A_Z -> 1
   3 constants             bulk behavior           2 constants
   (Anisotropic)            (Isotropic)            (Isotropic)
```

> **[7 Marks: Microstructure explanation 2 + Voigt average 2 + Resultant isotropy 2 + Sketch 1]**

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the difference between **isotropy** and **anisotropy** with reference to (i) Young's modulus, (ii) thermal conductivity, and (iii) refractive index. Provide one engineering example where anisotropy is exploited to achieve a specific design goal. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For an isotropic material, derive the relation $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ starting from the two Lamé constants $\lambda$ and $\mu$. Show all intermediate steps. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Differences with reference to specific properties:

| Property | Isotropic Material | Anisotropic Material |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| (i) Young's Modulus E | **Same** in all directions (single scalar E) | **Different** along different crystal axes (tensor) |
| (ii) Thermal Conductivity k | **Same** in all directions (single scalar k) | **Direction-dependent** (tensor with 2 to 6 independent components) |
| (iii) Refractive Index n | **Same** for all polarization directions (glass) | **Birefringent** — light splits into ordinary and extraordinary rays (calcite) |

**Engineering example where anisotropy is exploited:** **Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** used in Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 fuselage. Carbon fibers are aligned along the load direction, giving very high stiffness/strength along fibers but lower stiffness transversely. This **engineered anisotropy** achieves:
- 30–50% weight reduction vs aluminium
- Tailorable stiffness in specific directions
- Excellent fatigue resistance along the fiber direction

> **[7 Marks: Each property explanation 1.5 × 3 = 4.5 + Example 2.5]**

**(b) Derivation of E = 2G(1 + ν) from Lamé constants:**

**Step 1:** Start with the isotropic Hooke's Law:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

**Step 2:** Consider a uniaxial tension test along x-direction:

$$\sigma_{11} = \sigma, \quad \sigma_{22} = \sigma_{33} = 0$$

**Step 3:** By symmetry, the lateral strains are equal:

$$\varepsilon_{22} = \varepsilon_{33} = -\nu \varepsilon_{11}$$

And the volumetric strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{kk} = \varepsilon_{11} + \varepsilon_{22} + \varepsilon_{33} = \varepsilon_{11} - 2\nu\varepsilon_{11} = (1 - 2\nu)\varepsilon_{11}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the isotropic Hooke's law for the x-direction:

$$\sigma_{11} = \lambda (\varepsilon_{11} + \varepsilon_{22} + \varepsilon_{33}) + 2\mu \varepsilon_{11}$$

$$\sigma_{11} = \lambda (1 - 2\nu)\varepsilon_{11} + 2\mu \varepsilon_{11}$$

$$\sigma_{11} = \left[\lambda(1 - 2\nu) + 2\mu\right] \varepsilon_{11}$$

**Step 5:** By definition of Young's modulus:

$$E = \frac{\sigma_{11}}{\varepsilon_{11}} = \lambda(1 - 2\nu) + 2\mu$$

**Step 6:** Use the relation $\lambda = \dfrac{2\mu\nu}{1 - 2\nu}$ (derived from the lateral strain condition in uniaxial tension):

$$E = \frac{2\mu\nu(1 - 2\nu)}{1 - 2\nu} + 2\mu = 2\mu\nu + 2\mu = 2\mu(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 7:** Since $\mu = G$ (shear modulus):

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> **[7 Marks: Setup 1 + Lateral strain 1 + Volumetric strain 1 + Substitution 1 + Definition 1 + λ relation 1 + Final result 1]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "isotropic" with "homogeneous"** — Homogeneous means property is the same at every POINT; isotropic means same in every DIRECTION. A material can be homogeneous but anisotropic (e.g., a single crystal).
> 2. **Forgetting to state the symmetry argument** when reducing constants. Examiners allocate 1.5 marks specifically for naming each symmetry (stress, strain, energy).
> 3. **Writing "81" instead of "21"** as the number of independent constants for a general anisotropic material. The reduction to 21 is critical.
> 4. **In Zener formula**, students often forget the factor of 2 in the numerator. Correct: $A_Z = 2C_{44}/(C_{11} - C_{12})$.
> 5. **Polycrystal vs single crystal confusion** — Always clarify that random polycrystalline aggregates are macroscopically isotropic even if individual grains are anisotropic.
> 6. **Units** — Always state **GPa** or **Pa** explicitly. Omitting units loses 0.5 mark in numerical problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION POINTS — READ BEFORE EXAM:**

- ✅ **Isotropy** = Same properties in all directions. **Anisotropy** = Direction-dependent properties.
- ✅ The most general anisotropic linear elastic material requires **21 independent elastic constants**.
- ✅ The reduction sequence is: **81 → 54 → 36 → 21** (after stress, strain, and energy symmetries).
- ✅ **Cubic crystals** have **3** independent constants ($C_{11}, C_{12}, C_{44}$).
- ✅ **Isotropic materials** have **2** independent constants (E, ν) or equivalently ($\lambda, \mu$).
- ✅ Isotropic Hooke's Law: $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}$.
- ✅ General anisotropic Hooke's Law: $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$ (4th order tensor).
- ✅ **Zener anisotropy index**: $A_Z = 2C_{44} / (C_{11} - C_{12})$.
- ✅ For **perfect isotropy**, $A_Z = 1$, which requires $2C_{44} = C_{11} - C_{12}$.
- ✅ **Amorphous materials** (glass) → isotropic; **Single crystals** → strongly anisotropic.
- ✅ **Random polycrystalline aggregates** → macroscopically isotropic (Voigt-Reuss-Hill averaging).
- ✅ **Wood, CFRP, single crystal Si** → classic anisotropic engineering materials.
- ✅ **Isotropic** ≠ **Homogeneous**. Homogeneous = same at every point; Isotropic = same in every direction.
- ✅ Key relations for isotropic materials: $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ and $-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$.
- ✅ Anisotropy is **exploited** in CFRP aerospace structures and **avoided** in pressure vessel design.
- ✅ Engineering examples of **near-isotropic** cubic metals: Tungsten ($A_Z \approx 1.01$), Aluminium ($A_Z \approx 1.22$).
- ✅ Engineering examples of **strongly anisotropic** cubic metals: Copper ($A_Z \approx 3.21$), α-Iron ($A_Z \approx 2.42$).
- ✅ For 14-mark KTU questions, always (i) state the symmetry argument, (ii) show the tensor reduction mathematically, (iii) connect to a real engineering example.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Elasticity** is the fundamental mechanical property of a material that describes its ability to resist deformation under an applied external load and to **recover its original shape and dimensions completely** once the load is removed. The branch of mechanics that quantifies this reversible, time-independent response of solids to applied forces is termed the **Theory of Elasticity**, and the maximum stress up to which a material exhibits this fully reversible behavior defines the **Elastic Limit** of that material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Elasticity encompasses the study of **Stress, Strain, Hooke's Law, Young's Modulus, Shear Modulus, Bulk Modulus, Poisson's Ratio, and the inter-relationships between these elastic constants.** Mastery of this topic is mandatory as it forms the foundation for all subsequent modules including plastic deformation, fracture mechanics, and failure analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Elastic deformation is **reversible** (the material returns to its original state). This is fundamentally different from **plastic deformation**, which is permanent and irreversible. The transition between these two regimes occurs at the elastic limit and is governed by the atomic bonding characteristics of the material.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **spring** attached between a wall and a wooden block. When you pull the block slightly, the spring stretches. The moment you release it, the spring snaps back to its exact original length. That is elasticity in its purest form.

Now translate this to engineering materials:

- **A rubber band** being stretched and released → perfect everyday example of elastic behavior.
- **A steel cable** in a suspension bridge** → deforms microscopically under vehicle load but recovers instantly.
- **A piston in a hydraulic cylinder** → undergoes elastic compression and recovers when pressure is released.

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Anchor:** Think of elasticity as a material's "memory." As long as you don't stress it beyond its elastic limit, the material "remembers" its original shape perfectly and returns to it once forces are removed. Beyond the elastic limit, this memory is partially or fully erased (leading to plastic/permanent deformation).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Unit of Stress:** **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**. The practical engineering unit is **MPa** (10⁶ Pa) or **GPa** (10⁹ Pa).
- **Strain** is **dimensionless** (a ratio of lengths), often expressed as a pure number or in microstrain (με = 10⁻⁶).
- **Young's Modulus (E)** for steels: **~200 GPa**, for aluminium alloys: **~70 GPa**, for polymers: **~1–5 GPa**.
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** for most metals: **0.25 – 0.35**; for cork: **~0** (nearly zero); for rubber: **~0.5** (nearly incompressible).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Stress (σ)

**Stress** is defined as the internal resisting force per unit area developed within a material when an external load is applied. It is a measure of the intensity of the internal force distribution.

### Types of Stress:

**a) Normal Stress (σ):**
- Acts **perpendicular** to the cross-sectional area.
- **Tensile stress:** σₜ = P/A (causes elongation, considered positive).
- **Compressive stress:** σc = P/A (causes shortening, considered negative).

**b) Shear Stress (τ):**
- Acts **parallel (tangential)** to the cross-sectional area.
- Causes one plane of the material to slide relative to another.
- **τ = P/A** where P is the tangential force and A is the area parallel to it.

**c) Hydrostatic / Volumetric Stress (P):**
- Uniform pressure acting on all surfaces of a body from within or without.
- Causes a change in volume without a change in shape.

## 2.2 The Concept of Strain (ε)

**Strain** is the measure of deformation of a material relative to its original size. It is a dimensionless geometric quantity.

### Types of Strain:

**a) Normal / Longitudinal Strain (ε):**
- Ratio of change in length to original length.
- ε = ΔL / L₀

**b) Lateral Strain (ε_lat):**
- Ratio of change in lateral dimension to original lateral dimension.
- ε_lat = Δd / d₀

**c) Shear Strain (γ):**
- Angular deformation resulting from shear stress.
- γ = tan θ ≈ θ (for small angles, in radians).

**d) Volumetric Strain (εᵥ):**
- Ratio of change in volume to original volume.
- εᵥ = ΔV / V₀

## 2.3 Hooke's Law (The Foundation of Elasticity)

**Robert Hooke's Law (1660)** states that, within the elastic limit, **stress is directly proportional to strain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Statement of Hooke's Law:**
> σ = E · ε (for normal stress-strain in tension/compression)
> τ = G · γ (for shear stress-strain)

The proportionality constant **E** is the **Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus)** and **G** is the **Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity)**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Stress | σ | σ = P / A | N/m² (Pa) | Force per unit area on a normal plane |
| Tensile Strain | ε | ε = ΔL / L₀ | dimensionless | Fractional change in length |
| Young's Modulus | E | E = σ / ε | N/m² (Pa) | Stiffness in tension/compression |
| Shear Stress | τ | τ = P / A | N/m² (Pa) | Tangential force per unit area |
| Shear Strain | γ | γ = tan θ ≈ θ | dimensionless (rad) | Angular deformation |
| Shear Modulus | G | G = τ / γ | N/m² (Pa) | Resistance to shape change |
| Bulk Modulus | K | K = -P / (ΔV/V₀) | N/m² (Pa) | Resistance to volume change |
| Poisson's Ratio | ν | ν = -ε_lat / ε_long | dimensionless | Lateral to axial strain ratio |
| Volumetric Strain | εᵥ | εᵥ = ΔV / V₀ | dimensionless | Fractional change in volume |
| Relationship 1 | E,G,ν | E = 2G(1 + ν) | — | Links E, G, and ν |
| Relationship 2 | E,K,ν | E = 3K(1 - 2ν) | — | Links E, K, and ν |
| Relationship 3 | G,K,ν | G = 3K(1 - 2ν) / [2(1+ν)] | — | Links G, K, and ν |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Tip:** The three relationships above are **highly recurring** in KTU university exams. Memorize them — they carry direct 3-mark and 7-mark question potential almost every semester.

## 2.5 The Four Elastic Constants and Their Physical Meaning

1. **Young's Modulus (E):** Measures the material's **stiffness in tension/compression**. Higher E → stiffer material (e.g., diamond has E ≈ 1000 GPa, rubber has E ≈ 0.01 GPa).

2. **Shear Modulus (G):** Measures the material's **resistance to shape change** (without volume change). Critical in torsional loading of shafts.

3. **Bulk Modulus (K):** Measures the material's **resistance to uniform volume change** under hydrostatic pressure. Critical in fluid containment and deep-subsea applications.

4. **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** Measures the **lateral contraction that accompanies axial extension** (or vice versa). For stable, isotropic materials: **-1 < ν < 0.5**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Calculation of deflections in beams, columns, and slabs.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of pressure vessels (uses K), springs (uses G), and rotating shafts (uses G).
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Selection of lightweight stiff materials (high E/ρ ratio) for aircraft structures.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Design of implants and prosthetics requiring biomimetic stiffness.
- **Polymer Engineering:** Understanding viscoelastic behavior of rubbers and plastics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between E, G, and Poisson's Ratio (ν)

**Objective:** To establish the equation **E = 2G(1 + ν)**.

**Step 1 — Setup and assumptions:**
Consider a small cubic element of side length **L** of a homogeneous, isotropic material. Apply a tensile force **P** along the x-axis, producing axial stress σₓ = P / A.

**Step 2 — Compute axial strain (Hooke's Law):**
The axial strain along the x-direction is:
εₓ = σₓ / E

**Step 3 — Apply Poisson's effect (lateral strains):**
Due to Poisson's ratio, the cube contracts laterally along y and z axes:
ε_y = ε_z = -ν · εₓ

**Step 4 — Apply pure shear equivalent:**
This uniaxial tension is equivalent to the superposition of:
- A hydrostatic (volumetric) component of stress = σₓ/3 on each axis.
- A pure shear component of magnitude σₓ/2 acting on the diagonal planes.

**Step 5 — Resolve shear stress and shear strain:**
On the 45° inclined plane, the shear stress is:
τ = σₓ / 2

The diagonal elongation along the 45° direction, using the strain transformation principle, yields a shear strain:
γ = 2(1 + ν) · εₓ

**Step 6 — Apply Hooke's Law in shear:**
Using the shear modulus definition:
τ = G · γ

Substituting:
σₓ / 2 = G · 2(1 + ν) · εₓ

**Step 7 — Substitute εₓ = σₓ / E:**
$$\frac{\sigma_x}{2} = G \cdot 2(1+\nu) \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for E in terms of G and ν:**
$$E = 4G(1+\nu) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Uniaxial tension was decomposed into hydrostatic + pure shear components. The shear stress on the 45° plane equals half the applied normal stress, and the corresponding shear strain is enhanced by the factor (1+ν) due to lateral contraction. Setting τ = Gγ and substituting εₓ = σₓ/E yields the famous **E = 2G(1+ν)** relationship.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between E, K, and Poisson's Ratio (ν)

**Objective:** To establish the equation **E = 3K(1 - 2ν)**.

**Step 1 — Apply equal triaxial tension (hydrostatic pressure reversed):**
Apply uniform tensile stress σ on all three perpendicular faces of a cubic element. The hydrostatic pressure equivalent is P = -σ (negative because tensile stress reduces volumetric compression).

**Step 2 — Compute each normal strain using generalized Hooke's Law:**
The generalized Hooke's Law for normal strain along x-direction:
εₓ = (1/E)[σₓ - ν(σ_y + σ_z)]

For hydrostatic case: σₓ = σ_y = σ_z = σ
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma - \nu(\sigma + \sigma)] = \frac{\sigma(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry: ε_y = ε_z = εₓ = σ(1 - 2ν) / E

**Step 3 — Compute volumetric strain:**
The volumetric strain is the sum of three normal strains:
εᵥ = εₓ + ε_y + ε_z = 3 · σ(1 - 2ν) / E

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3\sigma(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Use Bulk Modulus definition:**
By definition: K = -P / εᵥ where P = -σ (since tensile σ corresponds to negative pressure for bulk modulus convention).

$$K = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{\sigma}{\frac{3\sigma(1-2\nu)}{E}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for E in terms of K and ν:**
$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Hydrostatic loading produces equal stresses on all three axes. The sum of strains gives volumetric strain. Applying K = σ/εᵥ and solving algebraically yields the result. This relationship tells us that **as ν → 0.5, E → 0 (rubber-like incompressible behavior)**, and **as ν → 0, E → 3K** (cork-like behavior).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Expression for Volumetric Strain Under Three Orthogonal Stresses

**Step 1 — Apply three mutually perpendicular stresses σₓ, σ_y, σ_z on a cubic element.**

**Step 2 — Generalized Hooke's Law gives:**
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Add all three strains to obtain volumetric strain:**
$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E}\left[(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) - 2\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1 - 2\nu)}{E} \cdot (\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Adding the three generalized strain equations and factoring yields a beautifully symmetric result. Note that the mean stress is σₘ = (σₓ + σ_y + σ_z)/3, and the relationship can also be written as εᵥ = 3σₘ(1 - 2ν)/E, which when combined with K = σₘ/εᵥ recovers E = 3K(1-2ν) as a consistency check.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Types

| **Problem Type** | **Approach** | **Key Formula** | **Common Mistake** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Find elongation under axial load | δL = PL / AE | Hooke's Law | Forgetting to convert units (N, mm, MPa) |
| Find lateral strain given axial strain | ε_lat = -ν·ε_long | Poisson's Ratio | Sign convention error |
| Find E from G and ν | E = 2G(1+ν) | Elastic constant relation | Using wrong relationship |
| Find K from E and ν | K = E / [3(1-2ν)] | Elastic constant relation | Forgetting the factor of 2 in (1-2ν) |
| Find stress in composite bar | Force equilibrium + compatibility | σ = E·ε | Not accounting for equal strain (parallel) |
| Volumetric strain in triaxial stress | εᵥ = (1-2ν)(σₓ+σ_y+σ_z)/E | Generalized Hooke's Law | Mixing up sign conventions |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress-Strain Diagram for a Ductile Material (Mild Steel)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level architecture of the elastic regime** within a typical stress-strain curve, mapping the key regions, transitions, and their corresponding physical significance for KTU examination purposes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OA["Origin O: Zero Load Zero Deformation"] --> A1["Region OA: Linear Elastic Zone"]
    A1 --> A2["Proportional Limit: Hookes Law Valid"]
    A2 --> A3["Elastic Limit: Maximum Reversible Deformation"]
    A3 --> A4["Yield Point: Onset of Plastic Flow"]
    A4 --> A5["Ultimate Tensile Strength: Maximum Load"]
    A5 --> A6["Fracture Point: Material Failure"]
    OA --> B1["Stress Axis: Sigma in MPa"]
    OA --> B2["Strain Axis: Epsilon dimensionless"]
    A1 --> C1["Young Modulus E: Slope of OA Line"]
    A1 --> C2["Strain Energy Stored: Area Under Curve"]
    A3 --> C3["Elastic Recovery Zone"]
    A3 --> C4["Permanent Set Begins Beyond This Point"]
    C1 --> D1["High E: Stiff Material Steel 200 GPa"]
    C1 --> D2["Low E: Compliant Material Rubber 0.01 GPa"]
    C2 --> E1["Resilience Modulus: Energy per unit volume"]
    C2 --> E2["Toughness Modulus: Total Energy to Fracture"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow proceeds from **Origin (O)** through the **linear elastic zone (OA)** where Hooke's Law (σ = Eε) holds. Beyond the **Proportional Limit**, the curve begins to deviate from linearity, but the material still recovers elastically until the **Elastic Limit**. Beyond this, **plastic deformation** begins, culminating in fracture. **Young's Modulus (E) is the slope of region OA**, and the area under the elastic portion represents the **modulus of resilience**.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Elastic Response Flow

This diagram maps the **sequence of physical and mathematical operations** that govern a material's elastic response under load, designed specifically for KTU Module 1 elasticity concepts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["External Load P Applied"] --> N1["Stress Generation sigma = P slash A"]
    IN2["Geometry Definition L A"] --> N2["Strain Generation epsilon = delta L over L"]
    N1 --> N3["Hookes Law sigma equals E times epsilon"]
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4["Elastic Constant Determination"]
    N4 --> O1["Young Modulus E for Tension"]
    N4 --> O2["Shear Modulus G for Shear"]
    N4 --> O3["Bulk Modulus K for Volume"]
    N4 --> O4["Poissons Ratio nu for Lateral Effect"]
    O1 --> OUT1["Engineering Design: Stiffness Selection"]
    O2 --> OUT2["Engineering Design: Torsion Shafts"]
    O3 --> OUT3["Engineering Design: Pressure Vessels"]
    O4 --> OUT4["Engineering Design: Multi axial Loading"]
    OUT1 --> FB["Load Removal"]
    FB --> RES["Complete Recovery: Elastic Regime"]
    FB --> RES2["Permanent Deformation: Plastic Regime"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** This functional flow is the conceptual backbone of any 7-mark or 14-mark elasticity question. When asked to "explain the elastic behavior of materials," trace the path: **Load → Stress → Strain → Hooke's Law → Elastic Constants → Engineering Application → Recovery on Unloading**. This guarantees complete marks.

---

## 4.3 Three-Dimensional State of Stress Schematic (Textual)

A point inside a loaded body experiences a **3D stress state** represented by a **stress tensor** with nine components:

- **Three normal stresses:** σₓ, σ_y, σ_z (acting perpendicular to faces).
- **Six shear stresses:** τₓy, τ_yx, τ_yz, τ_zy, τ_xz, τ_zx.
- **Equilibrium condition:** τᵢⱼ = τⱼᵢ (symmetry), reducing independent components to **six**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 1 Focus:** The syllabus primarily addresses **uniaxial** and **biaxial** states of stress. The full 3D tensor treatment is reserved for higher semesters in Mechanics of Materials. For PECHT412, focus on σₓ, σ_y, σ_z and the volumetric strain derivation shown in Section 3.3.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following terms: (i) Young's Modulus, (ii) Bulk Modulus, (iii) Poisson's Ratio. Mention their units.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Young's Modulus (E):** It is defined as the ratio of longitudinal (tensile or compressive) stress to the corresponding longitudinal strain within the elastic limit.
**Formula:** E = σ / ε
**Unit:** N/m² or Pascal (Pa); practically MPa or GPa.

**(ii) Bulk Modulus (K):** It is defined as the ratio of hydrostatic (volumetric) pressure to the corresponding volumetric strain, with a negative sign indicating that an increase in pressure causes a decrease in volume.
**Formula:** K = -P / (ΔV/V₀)
**Unit:** N/m² or Pascal (Pa).

**(iii) Poisson's Ratio (ν):** It is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain when a material is subjected to uniaxial loading.
**Formula:** ν = -ε_lateral / ε_longitudinal
**Unit:** Dimensionless (no unit).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Hooke's Law. What are the limits of its validity?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement of Hooke's Law:** Within the elastic limit, the stress developed in a material is directly proportional to the strain produced.
**Mathematical Form:** σ = E · ε (for normal loading) and τ = G · γ (for shear loading), where E and G are constants of proportionality.

**Limits of Validity:**
1. The material must be stressed **below the proportional limit**, not merely below the elastic limit.
2. The material should be **homogeneous and isotropic**.
3. The load must be **static** (time-independent) — not applicable for dynamic/impact loading.
4. The **temperature** must remain constant (isothermal conditions).
5. The strains must be **small** (typically ε < 0.001 for metals).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's Ratio (ν)**. Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel rod of length **2 m** and cross-sectional area **400 mm²** is subjected to a tensile load of **80 kN**. If Young's Modulus of steel is **200 GPa**, determine:
(i) The stress developed in the rod
(ii) The elongation of the rod
(iii) The lateral strain, given Poisson's ratio = 0.30
(iv) The change in diameter, assuming original diameter = 20 mm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Refer to **Section 3.1 (Derivation 1)** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final result is:
$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

**Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- L = 2 m = 2000 mm
- A = 400 mm²
- P = 80 kN = 80,000 N
- E = 200 GPa = 200,000 N/mm² = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²
- ν = 0.30
- d₀ = 20 mm

**(i) Stress developed:**
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80{,}000}{400} = 200 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Elongation of the rod:**
Using Hooke's Law: σ = E·ε, so ε = σ/E
$$\varepsilon = \frac{200}{2 \times 10^5} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2000 = 2 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Lateral strain:**
$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.30 \times (1 \times 10^{-3}) = -3 \times 10^{-4}$$

The negative sign indicates contraction.

**(iv) Change in diameter:**
$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d_0 = (-3 \times 10^{-4}) \times 20 = -0.006 \text{ mm}$$

The diameter decreases by **0.006 mm** (final diameter = 19.994 mm).

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **Young's Modulus (E), Bulk Modulus (K), and Poisson's Ratio (ν)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a given metal, Young's Modulus is **210 GPa** and Poisson's Ratio is **0.28**. Calculate:
(i) Shear Modulus
(ii) Bulk Modulus
(iii) Volumetric strain when the metal is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of **150 MPa**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Refer to **Section 3.2 (Derivation 2)** of these notes. The final result is:
$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- E = 210 GPa = 210 × 10³ MPa
- ν = 0.28
- P = 150 MPa (hydrostatic)

**(i) Shear Modulus (G):**
Using E = 2G(1 + ν), solve for G:
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^3}{2 \times 1.28} = \frac{210{,}000}{2.56}$$
$$G = 82{,}031.25 \text{ MPa} \approx 82.03 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Bulk Modulus (K):**
Using E = 3K(1 - 2ν), solve for K:
$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^3}{3 \times (1 - 0.56)} = \frac{210{,}000}{3 \times 0.44} = \frac{210{,}000}{1.32}$$
$$K = 159{,}090.91 \text{ MPa} \approx 159.09 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Volumetric strain:**
By definition of Bulk Modulus:
$$K = \frac{P}{\varepsilon_v} \Rightarrow \varepsilon_v = \frac{P}{K}$$
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{150}{159{,}090.91} = 9.43 \times 10^{-4}$$

The volumetric strain is **9.43 × 10⁻⁴** (compressive, since pressure causes volume decrease).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Elasticity Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Mixing up N/mm², MPa, and GPa. Always convert to a consistent system first. **1 GPa = 1000 MPa = 1000 N/mm².**
>
> 2. **Sign Convention Error in Poisson's Ratio (–1 mark):** Forgetting the negative sign in ν = -ε_lat/ε_long. The negative sign is integral; the formula's physical meaning demands it.
>
> 3. **Wrong Elastic Constant Relationship (–3 marks):** Confusing E = 2G(1+ν) with E = 3K(1-2ν). The first involves G, the second involves K. **Memory trick:** "**2**G" for shear and "**3**K" for bulk.
>
> 4. **Skipping Derivation Steps (–2 to –4 marks):** Writing only the final formula in derivation questions. **Always show every algebraic step** — KTU examiners allot marks for each transition.
>
> 5. **Using σ = P/A without specifying whether A is original or instantaneous area:** For Module 1 (small strains), use original area. Mention this assumption explicitly.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Assumption of Isotropy in derivations:** All elastic constant relationships (E = 2G(1+ν), E = 3K(1-2ν)) are **strictly valid only for isotropic, homogeneous materials.** State this assumption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Elasticity (Module 1):**
>
> - **Stress (σ) = Force / Area** — Units: N/m² (Pa). Tensile is positive, compressive is negative.
> - **Strain (ε) = Change in length / Original length** — Dimensionless, no units.
> - **Hooke's Law:** σ = Eε (valid only below the proportional limit).
> - **Young's Modulus (E):** Stiffness against tensile/compressive deformation. **Steel ≈ 200 GPa, Aluminium ≈ 70 GPa, Copper ≈ 110 GPa.**
> - **Shear Modulus (G):** Resistance to shape change. **G < E/2** for most metals.
> - **Bulk Modulus (K):** Resistance to volume change. Critical for pressure vessel design.
> - **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** Always between **-1 and 0.5** for stable isotropic materials. **Most metals: 0.25–0.35.**
> - **E = 2G(1 + ν)** — memorize this (relates E, G, ν).
> - **E = 3K(1 - 2ν)** — memorize this (relates E, K, ν).
> - **G = 3K(1 - 2ν) / [2(1 + ν)]** — derived from the two above; rarely asked directly.
> - **Volumetric strain:** εᵥ = (1 - 2ν)(σₓ + σ_y + σ_z) / E.
> - **Elastic limit ≠ Proportional limit:** Proportional limit is the end of linear (Hookean) behavior; elastic limit is the maximum stress for full recovery on unloading.
> - **Material "memory":** Elastic deformation is fully reversible (like a stretched spring).
> - **For uniaxial loading:** Lateral strain = -ν × longitudinal strain.
> - **All elastic constant relations are valid ONLY for isotropic, homogeneous materials.**

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — Elasticity Notes.** These notes cover the complete KTU 2024 syllabus for the elasticity topic of PECHT412 - Material Science, including definitions, derivations, formulas, diagrams, and examination-grade question bank. Practice the derivations and numerical problems thoroughly — elasticity forms the conceptual foundation for **all subsequent modules** in this course.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plasticity** is the property of a solid material that allows it to undergo **permanent, irreversible deformation** when subjected to applied stresses that exceed the material's elastic limit. Unlike elastic deformation (which is fully recoverable upon load removal), plastic deformation involves the breaking and reforming of atomic bonds, dislocation motion, and grain boundary sliding, resulting in a shape change that remains even after the load is removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"Plasticity is the ability of a material to deform continuously and permanently without rupture when subjected to a stress greater than its yield stress. The deformation is time-independent and is primarily governed by dislocation glide, slip, and twinning mechanisms within the crystal lattice."*

The phenomenon is fundamentally governed by the **shear stress** acting on specific crystallographic planes and directions, known as **slip systems**. Plasticity is observed only in materials possessing a **crystalline structure** (metals, certain ceramics, and polymers above their glass transition temperature).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **paper clip** made of steel wire:

- If you bend it **slightly** and release, it returns to its original shape → This is **elastic deformation** (Hooke's Law region).
- If you bend it **too far** (past a critical point), it stays bent → This is **plastic deformation**.

Another classic analogy: **wet modeling clay** vs. **a rubber band**.
- The rubber band snaps back (elastic).
- The wet clay, when pressed, holds its new shape (plastic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Elastic Limit**: Maximum stress up to which the body regains its original shape.
> - **Yield Point**: Stress at which plastic deformation begins to occur noticeably.
> - **Proportional Limit**: End of the linear stress-strain relationship.
>
> All three are close in value for ductile materials, but they are *conceptually distinct* in the KTU Board marking scheme.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shear Modulus** | $G$ | 25 – 110 GPa | Resistance to shear deformation |
| **Yield Strength** | $\sigma_y$ | **MPa** or **GPa** | Onset of plasticity |
| **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)** | $\tau_{CRSS}$ | 0.5 – 50 MPa | Minimum shear stress to initiate slip |
| **Strain Hardening Exponent** | $n$ | 0.0 – 0.5 | Capacity for work hardening |
| **BCC / FCC / HCP Slip Systems** | – | 48 / 12 / 3 | Number of independent slip systems |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanistic Breakdown of Plasticity

Plastic deformation in crystalline solids occurs through four primary mechanisms, listed in order of decreasing temperature dominance:

1. **Slip (Dislocation Glide)**
   - The dominant mechanism in crystalline materials at low-to-moderate temperatures.
   - Involves the movement of **dislocations** (line defects) along specific crystallographic planes (**slip planes**) and directions (**slip directions**).
   - The combination of a slip plane + slip direction = **Slip System**.

2. **Twinning**
   - A coordinated atomic rearrangement where a portion of the crystal mirrors across a **twin plane**.
   - Common in **HCP metals** (e.g., Mg, Zn, Ti) at low temperatures and in **BCC** metals at high strain rates.
   - Produces a characteristic mirror-image lattice orientation.

3. **Grain Boundary Sliding**
   - At high temperatures ($\geq 0.4 T_m$, where $T_m$ is the melting point), adjacent grains slide past each other.
   - Dominant in **superplastic forming** operations.

4. **Diffusion-Based Creep**
   - Vacancy diffusion and atomic migration under stress.
   - Significant only at very high homologous temperatures ($T \geq 0.5 T_m$).

## 2.2 Slip Systems in Common Crystal Structures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Table** — Memorize the slip systems:

| Crystal Structure | Slip Plane | Slip Direction | Number of Slip Systems | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCC** | $\{111\}$ | $\langle 110 \rangle$ | **12** | Cu, Al, Ni, Au, Ag |
| **BCC** | $\{110\}, \{112\}, \{123\}$ | $\langle 111 \rangle$ | **48** | $\alpha$-Fe, Cr, Mo, W |
| **HCP** | Basal $\{0001\}$ | $\langle 11\bar{2}0 \rangle$ | **3** | Mg, Zn, Ti, Co |

> The high number of slip systems in **FCC (12)** and **BCC (48)** explains why these metals are highly ductile. **HCP** metals, with only 3 basal slip systems, are typically brittle at room temperature.

## 2.3 Schmid's Law — The Foundation of Plasticity

When a single crystal is loaded along an axis at an arbitrary angle, the resolved shear stress on a slip plane is given by **Schmid's Law** (1924):

$$\tau_R = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Applied tensile (axial) stress
- $\phi$ = Angle between the **normal to the slip plane** and the **loading direction**
- $\lambda$ = Angle between the **slip direction** and the **loading direction**

**Slip initiates** when the resolved shear stress $\tau_R$ equals the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)**, $\tau_{CRSS}$, which is a material constant for a given temperature and strain rate.

$$\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma_y \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$

This is the **yield criterion for a single crystal**.

## 2.4 Macroscopic Yield Criteria (Polycrystalline Materials)

For polycrystalline aggregates, KTU examiners frequently test the following two yield criteria:

### 2.4.1 Tresca Criterion (Maximum Shear Stress Theory)
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2\tau_y = \sigma_y$$

### 2.4.2 Von Mises Criterion (Distortion Energy Theory)
$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Tresca** is **simpler** and more **conservative** (predicts yielding earlier).
> - **Von Mises** is **more accurate** for ductile materials and is the standard in finite element analysis (FEA) software like ANSYS and Abaqus.
> - In KTU Board papers, if no criterion is specified, the **default is Von Mises**.

## 2.5 Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)

Once plastic deformation begins, the material's resistance to further deformation **increases**. This phenomenon is called **strain hardening** or **work hardening**, modeled by the **Hollomon equation**:

$$\sigma_T = K \varepsilon_T^n$$

Where:
- $\sigma_T$ = **True stress**
- $\varepsilon_T$ = **True strain**
- $K$ = **Strength coefficient** (MPa)
- $n$ = **Strain hardening exponent** (0 to 0.5 for metals)

## 2.6 True Stress – True Strain vs. Engineering Stress – Strain

The conversion relationships are critical for KTU numerical problems:

$$\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E)$$

$$\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| Equation | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Schmid's Law** | $\tau_R = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ | Single-crystal slip initiation |
| **CRSS Yield Criterion** | $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma_y \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ | Yield in single crystal |
| **Tresca Yield** | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$ | Conservative yield prediction |
| **Von Mises Yield** | $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2$ | Ductile material yielding |
| **Hollomon Equation** | $\sigma_T = K \varepsilon_T^n$ | Strain hardening |
| **True Stress** | $\sigma_T = \sigma_E(1+\varepsilon_E)$ | Conversion from engineering |
| **True Strain** | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1+\varepsilon_E)$ | Conversion from engineering |
| **Maximum Ductility** | $\%\text{EL} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Ductility measure |
| **Reduction in Area** | $\%\text{RA} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Ductility measure |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Schmid's Law** is the basis of single-crystal superalloy design (e.g., jet engine turbine blades).
> - **Von Mises** is used in **pressure vessel** and **bridge** design codes (ASME, IS 800).
> - **Hollomon's equation** is essential for **sheet metal forming** simulations in the automotive industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Schmid's Law

**Objective:** To derive the resolved shear stress on a slip plane in terms of the applied axial stress and the orientation angles $\phi$ and $\lambda$.

### Step 1: Define the Geometry

Consider a cylindrical single crystal of cross-sectional area $A_0$ with its axis oriented at angles $\phi$ and $\lambda$ relative to a uniaxial tensile force $F$. The slip plane has an area $A_0 / \cos\phi$, and the slip direction lies within the slip plane.

### Step 2: Compute the Force Components

The component of the applied force $F$ acting **along the slip direction** is:

$$F_{\parallel} = F \cos\lambda$$

The component of the applied force acting **normal to the slip plane** is:

$$F_{\perp} = F \cos\phi$$

### Step 3: Compute the Resolved Shear Stress

The resolved shear stress is the shear force divided by the area of the slip plane (not the cross-section):

$$\tau_R = \frac{F_{\parallel}}{A_{\text{slip}}} = \frac{F \cos\lambda}{A_0 / \cos\phi}$$

Substituting $F = \sigma A_0$:

$$\tau_R = \frac{\sigma A_0 \cos\lambda \cos\phi}{A_0}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_R = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda}$$

### Step 4: Schmid Factor

The product $\cos\phi \cos\lambda$ is termed the **Schmid Factor**, $m$:

$$m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$

The **maximum value** of $m$ is **0.5** (when $\phi = \lambda = 45°$). This is why slip is most easily activated at $45°$ orientations.

### Step 5: Yield Criterion for a Single Crystal

Slip initiates when the resolved shear stress equals the critical value:

$$\tau_R = \tau_{CRSS}$$

$$\sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda = \tau_{CRSS}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \cos\lambda}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: True Stress from Engineering Stress

**Objective:** Convert engineering stress to true stress, accounting for the reduction in cross-sectional area during plastic deformation.

### Step 1: Assume Volume Constancy

Plastic deformation conserves volume (incompressibility assumption):

$$V_0 = V_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$$

### Step 2: Express Engineering Stress

$$\sigma_E = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

### Step 3: Express True Stress

True stress uses the **instantaneous** (deformed) area:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_f}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $A_f$ Using Volume Constancy

From $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$:

$$A_f = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_f} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_E}$$

where $\varepsilon_E = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$.

### Step 5: Final Expression

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_f} = \frac{F}{A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon_E)} = \frac{F}{A_0} (1 + \varepsilon_E)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E)}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: True Strain from Engineering Strain

### Step 1: Define Instantaneous Strain

For an incremental change in length $dL$:

$$d\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L}$$

### Step 2: Integrate from $L_0$ to $L_f$

$$\varepsilon_T = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Engineering Strain

Since $\varepsilon_E = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$, we have $\dfrac{L_f}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon_E$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A single crystal of copper (FCC) is loaded in tension. The slip plane normal makes an angle of $60°$ with the load axis, and the slip direction makes an angle of $30°$ with the load axis. The CRSS for copper is **20 MPa**. Calculate the applied stress required to initiate plastic deformation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the angles:
- $\phi = 60°$
- $\lambda = 30°$
- $\tau_{CRSS} = 20$ MPa

**Step 2:** Compute $\cos\phi$ and $\cos\lambda$:

$$\cos 60° = 0.5$$

$$\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

**Step 3:** Apply Schmid's Law:

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \cos\lambda} = \frac{20}{0.5 \times 0.866}$$

$$\sigma_y = \frac{20}{0.433}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_y \approx 46.19 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4:** Verify using Schmid Factor:
- $m = 0.5 \times 0.866 = 0.433$
- $\sigma_y = 20 / 0.433 = 46.19$ MPa ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress–Strain Curve for a Ductile Material

The following Mermaid block illustrates the complete deformation regions from elastic loading to necking and fracture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Origin: Zero Stress and Strain"] --> B["Elastic Region: Linear Hookes Law"]
    B --> C["Proportional Limit: End of Linearity"]
    C --> D["Elastic Limit: Fully Recoverable Limit"]
    D --> E["Yield Point: Onset of Plasticity"]
    E --> F["Strain Hardening Region: Dislocation Multiplication"]
    F --> G["Ultimate Tensile Strength: Maximum Load"]
    G --> H["Necking Region: Localized Cross Section Reduction"]
    H --> I["Fracture Point: Final Failure"]
    J["Elastic Recovery Line: Unloading Path"] -.-> B
    K["Plastic Strain: Permanent Offset"] -.-> E
```

## 4.2 Schmid's Law — Geometric Configuration

The following block diagram captures the geometric relationships between the loading axis, slip plane normal, and slip direction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Applied Tensile Force F along Load Axis"] --> B["Component along Slip Direction: F times cos lambda"]
    A --> C["Component Normal to Slip Plane: F times cos phi"]
    B --> D["Resolved Shear Stress tau_R"]
    C --> E["Resolved Normal Stress sigma_n"]
    D --> F{"tau_R greater than or equal to tau_CRSS ?"}
    E --> G["Does Not Initiate Slip"]
    F -->|Yes| H["SLIP INITIATED: Plastic Deformation Begins"]
    F -->|No| I["Elastic Deformation Only"]
```

## 4.3 Slip System Architecture in FCC Crystals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FCC Single Crystal"] --> B["Four Unique Slip Planes: Family 111"]
    A --> C["Three Unique Slip Directions per Plane: Family 110"]
    B --> D["Plane 1: 111"]
    B --> E["Plane 2: 1 1 1 bar"]
    B --> F["Plane 3: 1 bar 1 1"]
    B --> G["Plane 4: 1 bar 1 bar 1"]
    C --> H["Direction 1: 0 1 bar 1"]
    C --> I["Direction 2: 1 0 1 bar"]
    C --> J["Direction 3: 1 bar 1 0"]
    D --> H
    D --> I
    D --> J
    E --> H
    E --> I
    E --> J
    F --> H
    F --> I
    F --> J
    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J
    H --> K["TOTAL SLIP SYSTEMS = 4 x 3 = 12"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Cold Working vs. Hot Working — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Incoming Metal Stock"] --> B{"Homologous Temperature T over Tm ?"}
    B -->|Below 0.4| C["COLD WORKING"]
    B -->|Between 0.4 and 0.5| D["WARM WORKING"]
    B -->|Above 0.5| E["HOT WORKING"]
    C --> F["Dislocation Density Increases"]
    F --> G["Strength Increases, Ductility Decreases"]
    G --> H["Annealing Required for Recrystallization"]
    E --> I["Dynamic Recrystallization Occurs"]
    I --> J["No Strain Hardening Retained"]
    J --> K["Lower Final Strength, Higher Ductility"]
    D --> L["Partial Recrystallization"]
    L --> M["Balanced Properties"]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block: Tresca vs. Von Mises in 2D Principal Stress Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Principal Stress State: sigma_1, sigma_2, sigma_3"] --> B["Apply Tresca Criterion"]
    A --> C["Apply Von Mises Criterion"]
    B --> D["Hexagonal Yield Surface in 3D"]
    C --> E["Cylindrical Yield Surface in 3D"]
    D --> F["Conservative: Inner Envelope"]
    E --> G["Accurate: Smooth Surface"]
    F --> H["Used in: Pressure Vessel Conservative Design"]
    G --> I["Used in: FEA Software ANSYS ABAQUS"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define **plasticity**. Differentiate between **elastic** and **plastic** deformation in crystalline solids.

**Model Answer:**

**Plasticity** is the property of a material to undergo **permanent, non-recoverable deformation** when the applied stress exceeds its yield point, primarily through dislocation motion along specific crystallographic slip systems.
