```mermaid
    style M6 fill:#90EE90
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic for Exams:** When you see a stress-strain curve on the KTU question paper, always remember the **5 Landmarks**: 
> **P**roportional limit → **Y**ield point → **S**train hardening → **U**ltimate strength → **F**racture. 
> The horizontal distance between Yield and Fracture is your **ductility zone**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define ductility. Mention two quantitative measures used to express it.**

**Model Answer:**
Ductility is the ability of a material to undergo significant **plastic deformation under tensile stress** before fracture. It indicates the extent to which a material can be stretched, drawn, or elongated permanently without breaking.

The two standard quantitative measures of ductility are:
1. **Percentage Elongation (%EL):** The percentage increase in gauge length after fracture, given by:
$$\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$

2. **Percentage Reduction in Area (%RA):** The percentage decrease in cross-sectional area at the fracture neck, given by:
$$\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Note:** A 3-mark question expects the definition (1 mark) + both formulas with definitions of variables (2 marks). Do not skip variable definitions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between ductility and malleability with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Property** | **Ductility** | **Malleability** |
|---|---|---|
| Type of Stress | Tensile (pulling/stretching) | Compressive (rolling/hammering) |
| Deformation Mode | Drawing into wires | Forming into sheets/foils |
| Example Material | **Copper** (drawn into electrical wires) | **Aluminum** (rolled into foil sheets) |
| Quantitative Measure | % Elongation, % Reduction in Area | % Reduction in Thickness |

Both properties arise from the same underlying mechanism — dislocation motion — but they manifest under different loading conditions.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** With the help of a neat **stress–strain diagram for mild steel**, explain the salient points: Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield Point, Ultimate Tensile Strength, and Fracture Point. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical specimen of **aluminium alloy** with an original diameter of **16 mm** and gauge length of **62 mm** was tested in tension. At fracture, the gauge length was **74 mm** and the diameter at the neck was **10 mm**. Calculate the **% Elongation** and **% Reduction in Area**. Comment on the ductility of the material. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel:**

The engineering stress–strain curve for mild steel (a classic ductile material) exhibits the following key landmarks:

1. **Proportional Limit (A):** The maximum stress up to which stress is linearly proportional to strain. Below this, **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) holds strictly.

2. **Elastic Limit:** The maximum stress up to which the material returns to its original shape upon unloading. Strain is fully recoverable.

3. **Yield Point (B):** The stress at which plastic deformation begins. For mild steel, an **upper yield point** and a **lower yield point** are often observed due to interactions between dislocations and interstitial carbon atoms. Beyond this point, deformation is **permanent**.

4. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (C):** The maximum engineering stress the material can withstand. Beyond UTS, necking begins, and the cross-sectional area reduces locally.

5. **Fracture Point (D):** The stress at which the specimen ultimately separates. The total elongation up to this point is the **%EL**, and the area reduction at the neck is the **%RA**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Given data:
- Original diameter: $d_0 = 16$ mm
- Original gauge length: $L_0 = 62$ mm
- Final gauge length at fracture: $L_f = 74$ mm
- Final neck diameter: $d_f = 10$ mm

**Step 1 — Calculate % Elongation:**
$$\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$

$$\%EL = \dfrac{74 - 62}{62} \times 100 = \dfrac{12}{62} \times 100 = 19.35\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Original Cross-Sectional Area:**
$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (16)^2}{4} = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Final Cross-Sectional Area at Neck:**
$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (10)^2}{4} = 78.54 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Calculate % Reduction in Area:**
$$\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{201.06 - 78.54}{201.06} \times 100$$

$$\%RA = \dfrac{122.52}{201.06} \times 100 = 60.94\%$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Ductility:**
The material exhibits a **%EL of 19.35%** and a **%RA of 60.94%**, both of which are relatively high values. This indicates that the aluminium alloy is **highly ductile** and suitable for forming operations such as rolling, extrusion, and deep drawing.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** Define the following terms with units where applicable: (i) Engineering Stress, (ii) Engineering Strain, (iii) True Stress, (iv) True Strain. Establish the relationship between true stress and engineering stress. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **dislocation motion** in contributing to ductility in metallic materials. Why are **FCC metals more ductile** than **BCC metals** at room temperature? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Definitions and Relationships:**

- **Engineering Stress (σ):** The ratio of applied load to the **original** cross-sectional area: $\sigma = F/A_0$. Units: **MPa (N/m²)**.
- **Engineering Strain (ε):** The ratio of change in length to **original** gauge length: $\varepsilon = \Delta L/L_0$. Units: **dimensionless**.
- **True Stress (σ_t):** The ratio of applied load to the **instantaneous** (actual) cross-sectional area: $\sigma_t = F/A_i$. Units: **MPa**.
- **True Strain (ε_t):** The natural logarithmic measure of deformation: $\varepsilon_t = \ln(L_i/L_0) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$. Units: **dimensionless**.

**Derivation of the True Stress – Engineering Stress Relationship (as in Section 3):**
$$\sigma_t = \sigma(1 + \varepsilon)$$

**Part (b) — Dislocation Motion and Ductility:**

Ductility in crystalline metals is primarily governed by the motion of **dislocations** (line defects) along specific crystallographic planes known as **slip planes** in a process called **slip**.

- When a shear stress is applied, dislocations glide through the crystal lattice, allowing one atomic plane to slide over another incrementally.
- Each dislocation movement produces a small, permanent displacement of atoms, and the cumulative effect of billions of dislocation movements results in macroscopic plastic deformation.
- The ease with which dislocations move determines how ductile a material is.

**Why FCC Metals are More Ductile than BCC Metals:**

1. **Slip Systems:** FCC crystals have **12 slip systems** (combinations of {111} planes and ⟨110⟩ directions), while BCC crystals have only **48 slip systems at elevated temperatures but only 2 to 3 active at room temperature**. More slip systems means more deformation modes, hence greater ductility.

2. **Close-Packed Planes:** FCC structures have **close-packed {111} planes** with the highest atomic density and largest interplanar spacing, making dislocation glide easier. BCC planes are **not close-packed**, requiring higher resolved shear stress for slip.

3. **Peierls-Nabarro Stress:** The intrinsic lattice resistance to dislocation motion (Peierls stress) is **lower in FCC** than in BCC, especially at room temperature. BCC metals exhibit a strong temperature dependence — they become ductile only at elevated temperatures (above the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature, DBTT**).

4. **Alloying Effects:** FCC metals (Cu, Al, Ni, Au) remain ductile across a wide range of conditions, while BCC metals (Fe at low temperatures, Cr, Mo) can fracture in a brittle manner if tested below their DBTT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting variable definitions:** In numerical questions, students often write formulas without defining $L_0$, $L_f$, $A_0$, $A_f$. The KTU answer-key scheme deducts **1 mark** for missing variable definitions.
> 2. **Confusing %EL with %RA:** %EL is based on **length** changes; %RA is based on **area** changes. Mixing them up is a common error.
> 3. **Unit errors in stress-strain diagrams:** Forgetting units (MPa) on the stress axis leads to a **0.5 mark** deduction.
> 4. **Incomplete dislocation explanation:** Just stating "dislocations cause ductility" is insufficient. You must explain **how** (slip, slip planes, slip systems) and **why FCC > BCC** in ductility.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in numerical problems:** Always show $A_0$ and $A_f$ calculations before substituting into the %RA formula.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Ductility** = ability to deform plastically under **tensile** stress before fracture.
- Quantified by **% Elongation (%EL)** and **% Reduction in Area (%RA)**.
- **%EL** is length-based; **%RA** is area-based and more reliable (independent of gauge length).
- **Malleability** is compression-based (sheets); **Ductility** is tension-based (wires).
- The **stress-strain curve** of a ductile material shows: Elastic → Yield → Strain Hardening → UTS → Necking → Fracture.
- **Engineering stress** uses $A_0$; **True stress** uses $A_i$. Relation: $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$.
- **Ductility mechanism:** Dislocation motion (slip) on crystallographic slip planes.
- **FCC metals (12 slip systems)** are more ductile than **BCC metals (fewer active slip systems at room temperature)**.
- **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** is critical for BCC metals like steels.
- **Ductile fracture** shows **cup-and-cone** morphology; **brittle fracture** shows **cleavage** morphology.
- **Key engineering benefits:** formability, energy absorption, stress redistribution, failure warning.
- Standard test: **ASTM E8/E8M** uniaxial tensile test at quasi-static strain rates.
- **High ductility examples:** Gold (~70% RA), Copper (~70% RA), Mild Steel (~25% EL, ~60% RA).
- **Low ductility examples:** Cast Iron (~0% EL), Ceramics, Glass — these are **brittle**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malleability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Malleability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Malleability** is a mechanical property of solid materials (predominantly metals) that quantifies their ability to undergo significant **plastic deformation under compressive stress** (such as rolling, hammering, or pressing) without undergoing fracture or rupture. It is a direct macroscopic manifestation of the microscopic ease with which atomic planes can slide over one another via the mechanism of **dislocation glide** along preferred crystallographic slip systems.

Mathematically, malleability is expressed as a **percentage (%) of compressive deformation** sustained before failure:

$$\text{Malleability Index} = \left( \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \right) \times 100\%$$

where:
- $A_0$ = Original cross-sectional area of the specimen
- $A_f$ = Final cross-sectional area of the specimen after compressive failure

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Wet Clay vs. Glass**
> Imagine a lump of **wet potter's clay** and a piece of **brittle glass** of the same size. If you place the clay on a table and press down on it with your palm, it flattens into a pancake-shaped disc — it has spread out without breaking. This is **malleability in action**. Now try the same on the glass — it shatters immediately. The clay atoms glide past each other easily under compression; the glass atoms cannot.
> In engineering terms, **gold is the most malleable metal** — a single gram can be hammered into a sheet of approximately **1 m²**, only ~100 nm thick.

### 1.3 Distinction from Ductility

> [!WARNING]
> Students often confuse **Malleability** and **Ductility**. Remember:
> - **Malleability** → response to **COMPRESSIVE** stress (rolling, forging, hammering)
> - **Ductility** → response to **TENSILE** stress (drawing into wires)
> Both are forms of plastic deformation, but the mode of loading differs.

| Property | Stress Mode | Typical Engineering Application |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------------|
| Malleability | Compressive | Sheet metal, foils, plates |
| Ductility | Tensile | Wires, cables, rods |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Atomic Origin of Malleability

Malleability arises from the **non-directional nature of the metallic bond**. In metals, valence electrons form a delocalized "electron sea," allowing positively charged ion cores to slide past one another without breaking the bond network. This contrasts sharply with **covalent or ionic solids**, where bond directionality and charge balance make atomic plane sliding nearly impossible.

### 2.2 The Five Governing Factors

1. **Crystal Structure (Most Critical Factor)**
   - **FCC (Face-Centered Cubic)** → **12 slip systems** → **Highest malleability** (e.g., Au, Ag, Cu, Al, Ni, Pb)
   - **BCC (Body-Centered Cubic)** → **12 to 48 slip systems**, but high **Peierls–Nabarro stress** makes slip harder at low temperatures (e.g., Fe-α, W, Cr)
   - **HCP (Hexagonal Close-Packed)** → only **3 basal slip systems** → **Lowest malleability** (e.g., Zn, Mg, Ti, Co)
   - **Amorphous (Glassy) materials** → Malleability depends on viscosity and temperature (e.g., warm glass can be blown)

2. **Temperature**
   - Increasing temperature **reduces the critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)**.
   - Hot working (above recrystallization temperature) dramatically enhances malleability.
   - Quantitatively, the CRSS varies with temperature as:

$$\tau_{CRSS}(T) = \tau_0 \cdot e^{-Q / RT}$$

where $Q$ is the activation energy for slip, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

3. **Grain Size**
   - **Fine-grained metals** exhibit higher yield strength (Hall–Petch relation) but **lower malleability**.
   - **Coarse-grained metals** deform plastically more easily — more malleable but weaker.

4. **Presence of Impurities / Alloying**
   - Solid-solution strengthening **increases hardness** but **reduces malleability** (e.g., brass vs. pure copper).
   - Pure metals are generally more malleable than alloys.

5. **Strain Rate**
   - Lower strain rates give atoms more time to rearrange, increasing malleability.
   - Very high strain rates (impact loading) cause brittle behavior.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 ESE — MODULE 1**

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| $\tau_R = \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$ | Schmid's Law: $\sigma$ = applied stress, $\phi$ = angle between load axis & slip plane normal, $\lambda$ = angle between load axis & slip direction | Determines if a slip system is activated |
| $\text{Malleability \%} = \left( \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \right) \times 100$ | $A_0$ = original area, $A_f$ = final area | Quantifies malleability numerically |
| $\tau_{CRSS}(T) = \tau_0 e^{-Q/RT}$ | $Q$ = activation energy, $R$ = gas constant, $T$ = temperature | Temperature dependence of slip |
| $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Hall–Petch: $d$ = avg. grain diameter, $k_y$ = constant | Strength vs. grain size trade-off |
| $N_{slip} = $ number of active slip systems | FCC = 12, BCC = 12–48, HCP = 3 | Predicts malleability from structure |

### 2.4 Engineering Significance

- **Sheet metal forming** (automotive body panels, aircraft skins) — relies on high malleability of Al, low-carbon steel.
- **Gold leaf production** in electronics and gilding — exploits extreme Au malleability.
- **Forging & Rolling** in heavy industry — requires predictable compressive flow.
- **Coin minting** — historically depends on the malleability of Cu–Ni and Cu–Zn alloys.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Schmid's Law for Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)

> The **CRSS ($\tau_{CRSS}$)** is the minimum shear stress required to initiate dislocation glide on a slip system. The macroscopic applied stress $\sigma$ that causes slip is governed by the geometric orientation of the slip system relative to the load axis. This is captured by **Schmid's Law**.

**Step 1 — Setup the Geometry**
Consider a cylindrical single-crystal specimen of cross-sectional area $A_0$ subjected to an axial tensile force $F$.

- Let the **slip plane** have its normal making angle $\phi$ with the tensile axis.
- Let the **slip direction** make angle $\lambda$ with the tensile axis.
- The resolved (component) area of the slip plane perpendicular to the load is $A_0 \cos\phi$.

**Step 2 — Resolve the Force onto the Slip Direction**
The component of force $F$ acting along the slip direction:

$$F_{slip} = F \cos\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Resolved Shear Stress**
The resolved shear stress $\tau_R$ is the slip-force component divided by the resolved area:

$$\tau_R = \frac{F_{slip}}{A_{slip}} = \frac{F \cos\lambda}{A_0 \cos\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of the Applied Tensile Stress**
Since the applied tensile stress is $\sigma = F / A_0$, substitute:

$$\tau_R = \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$$

This is the **Schmid Factor** relationship.

**Step 5 — Yielding Criterion (Schmid's Law Statement)**
Plastic deformation (slip) initiates when the resolved shear stress reaches a critical value $\tau_{CRSS}$, which is a material constant for a given slip system at a given temperature:

$$\sigma_{ys} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda}$$

The factor $m = \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$ is called the **Schmid Factor** (range: $0 \leq m \leq 0.5$).

**Step 6 — Maximum Value of Schmid Factor**
The maximum of $\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$ occurs when $\phi = \lambda = 45°$, giving $m_{max} = 0.5$.

Therefore, the **minimum yield stress** a single crystal can have is:

$$\sigma_{ys, \min} = 2 \tau_{CRSS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning for Malleability**: A material is highly malleable when it can activate **multiple slip systems** at a **low CRSS** with a **high Schmid factor**. FCC metals satisfy all three conditions → hence the highest malleability.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A single crystal of aluminum (FCC) is stressed in tension. The slip plane normal makes $\phi = 30°$ with the tensile axis, and the slip direction makes $\lambda = 60°$. The CRSS for Al is $\tau_{CRSS} = 0.79 \text{ MPa}$. Determine the applied tensile stress at which yielding begins.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply Schmid's Law:

$$\sigma_{ys} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda}$$

Step 2 — Substitute the values:

$$\sigma_{ys} = \frac{0.79 \text{ MPa}}{\cos(30°) \cdot \cos(60°)}$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the cosines:

$$\cos(30°) = 0.8660, \quad \cos(60°) = 0.5000$$

Step 4 — Compute the denominator:

$$\cos(30°) \cdot \cos(60°) = 0.8660 \times 0.5000 = 0.4330$$

Step 5 — Final calculation:

$$\sigma_{ys} = \frac{0.79}{0.4330} = 1.824 \text{ MPa}$$

**Answer:** The crystal yields when the applied tensile stress reaches approximately **1.82 MPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **high-weightage for KTU Module 1** and is often linked with numerical problems involving FCC/BCC/HCP crystal orientations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Mermaid Compilation Notice:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords). Labels are kept clean (no markdown formatting) for guaranteed rendering.

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Factors Influencing Malleability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["MATERIAL MALLEABILITY INPUT"] --> B1["INTRINSIC FACTORS"]
    A1 --> B2["EXTRINSIC FACTORS"]

    B1 --> C1["Crystal Structure"]
    B1 --> C2["Bond Type"]
    B1 --> C3["Purity / Alloying"]

    B2 --> D1["Temperature"]
    B2 --> D2["Strain Rate"]
    B2 --> D3["Grain Size"]
    B2 --> D4["Applied Stress State"]

    C1 --> E1["FCC leads Highest"]
    C1 --> E2["BCC leads Moderate"]
    C1 --> E3["HCP leads Lowest"]

    D1 --> F1["High T leads More Malleable"]
    D3 --> F2["Coarse Grain leads More Malleable"]
    D2 --> F3["Low Strain Rate leads More Malleable"]

    E1 --> G1["OUTPUT MALLEABILITY INDEX PERCENT"]
    E2 --> G1
    E3 --> G1
    F1 --> G1
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1

    G1 --> H1["APPLICATION SHEET FORMING FORGING ROLLING"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Slip System Activation Pathway (Schmid's Law Logic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1["APPLIED TENSILE STRESS SIGMA"] --> J1["Stress Resolution on Slip Plane"]
    J1 --> J2["Resolved Shear Stress TAU R"]
    J2 --> J3{"TAU R versus TAU CRSS"}
    J3 -- TAU R greater TAU CRSS --> K1["Slip Activated - Plastic Deformation"]
    J3 -- TAU R less TAU CRSS --> K2["Elastic Deformation Only"]
    K1 --> L1["Material Exhibits HIGH Malleability"]
    K2 --> L2["Material Appears BRITTLE under This Orientation"]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block: Slip Systems per Crystal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["CRYSTAL STRUCTURE TYPES"] --> N1["FCC"]
    M1 --> N2["BCC"]
    M1 --> N3["HCP"]

    N1 --> O1["Slip Systems equals 12"]
    N1 --> O2["Examples Au Ag Cu Al Ni Pb"]
    N1 --> O3["Malleability Rating HIGHEST"]

    N2 --> P1["Slip Systems equals 12 to 48"]
    N2 --> P2["Examples Fe alpha W Cr Mo"]
    N2 --> P3["Malleability Rating MODERATE Temperature Sensitive"]

    N3 --> Q1["Slip Systems equals 3 Basal Only"]
    N3 --> Q2["Examples Zn Mg Ti Co"]
    N3 --> Q3["Malleability Rating LOWEST Brittle at Room T"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Convention:** Since the underlying physics involves three-dimensional crystal lattices and dislocation loops on slip planes, the block-level functional flow above is used (per the V10 diagram fallback rule) to convey the **cause-effect pathway** clearly. Physical FCC/BCC/HCP unit-cell drawings are typically assessed in the lab component (not theory ESE).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]**
**Define malleability. How is it different from ductility?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **(1 Mark)** **Malleability** is the ability of a material to undergo plastic deformation under **compressive stress** (rolling, hammering, forging) without rupture. It is quantified as the percentage reduction in cross-sectional area.
- **(1 Mark)** **Ductility** is the ability of a material to undergo plastic deformation under **tensile stress** (drawing, stretching) without rupture. It is quantified as percentage elongation.
- **(1 Mark)** Both are forms of plastic deformation governed by dislocation motion, but differ in the **mode of applied stress** — compressive vs. tensile. Example: Gold is highly malleable (can be beaten into foil) and also highly ductile (drawn into wire).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Why is FCC copper more malleable than HCP zinc at room temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **(1 Mark)** Copper (FCC) possesses **12 independent slip systems** ({111}⟨110⟩ family) which provide multiple equivalent paths for dislocation glide.
- **(1 Mark)** Zinc (HCP) has only **3 slip systems** (basal plane only at room temperature), severely restricting plastic flow.
- **(1 Mark)** More slip systems = greater capacity to accommodate arbitrary shape change under compressive load → **higher malleability**. Additionally, the non-basal slip systems in Zn require much higher CRSS and only activate at elevated temperatures.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **crystal structure dependence of malleability** in metals. Compare the slip systems and malleability of FCC, BCC, and HCP structures with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Derive **Schmid's Law** for the critical resolved shear stress. A copper single crystal has a slip plane normal at $30°$ and slip direction at $45°$ to the tensile axis. If the CRSS for Cu is $0.98 \text{ MPa}$, calculate the tensile stress at which yielding begins.

**Model Solution (a):**

- **(2 Marks)** **Crystal Structure Basis**: Plastic deformation in crystalline solids occurs by slip — the sliding of atomic planes over each other via dislocation motion. The number of available independent slip systems determines how easily a crystal can deform in any arbitrary direction. A slip system = {slip plane} + ⟨slip direction⟩.
- **(1.5 Marks)** **FCC — 12 Slip Systems**: Octahedral {111} planes (4 planes) × ⟨110⟩ close-packed directions (3 directions per plane) = 12. Examples: Cu, Al, Au, Ag, Ni, Pb. **Highest malleability** because multiple systems can be activated simultaneously.
- **(1.5 Marks)** **BCC — 12 to 48 Slip Systems**: {110}⟨111⟩ (12), {112}⟨111⟩ (12), {123}⟨111⟩ (24) = up to 48. Examples: Fe-α, W, Cr. **Moderate malleability** because although many systems exist, they all have high Peierls stress at low temperature. Hot working needed.
- **(2 Marks)** **HCP — 3 Slip Systems (at RT)**: Only basal {0001}⟨11-20⟩. Examples: Zn, Mg, Ti, Co. **Lowest malleability**. Slip on non-basal planes (prismatic, pyramidal) requires high CRSS. Hence Zn and Mg are brittle at room T but become workable at elevated T.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Schmid's Law Derivation (3.5 Marks):** Same as derived in Section 3.1 above — applied force F on cross-section A₀, with slip-plane normal at angle $\phi$ and slip direction at $\lambda$. The resolved shear stress is:

$$\tau_R = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$

Slip initiates when $\tau_R = \tau_{CRSS}$, giving:

$$\sigma_{ys} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \cos\lambda}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3.5 Marks):**

Step 1 — Identify given values: $\phi = 30°$, $\lambda = 45°$, $\tau_{CRSS} = 0.98 \text{ MPa}$.

Step 2 — Compute the cosines:

$$\cos(30°) = 0.8660, \quad \cos(45°) = 0.7071$$

Step 3 — Compute the Schmid factor:

$$m = \cos(30°) \cdot \cos(45°) = 0.8660 \times 0.7071 = 0.6124$$

Step 4 — Compute the yield stress:

$$\sigma_{ys} = \frac{0.98}{0.6124} = 1.6003 \text{ MPa}$$

**Answer:** Yielding begins at applied tensile stress $\sigma_{ys} \approx \mathbf{1.60 \text{ MPa}}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **factors affecting malleability** of metals. How does temperature influence the malleability of BCC iron?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical specimen of pure lead (FCC) of original cross-sectional area $50 \text{ mm}^2$ is compressed plastically. The final cross-sectional area at fracture is $42 \text{ mm}^2$. Calculate its **malleability index** and comment on its workability for sheet-metal forming.

**Model Solution (a):**

- **(2 Marks)** **Factor 1 — Crystal Structure**: As discussed, FCC > BCC > HCP in terms of slip-system availability.
- **(1 Mark)** **Factor 2 — Temperature**: Increases atomic vibrational amplitude and reduces CRSS exponentially ($\tau_{CRSS} \propto e^{-Q/RT}$). At higher T, non-basal and additional slip systems in BCC/HCP become active.
- **(1 Mark)** **Factor 3 — Grain Size**: Coarse grains → lower yield strength → easier plastic flow → higher malleability. Fine grains → higher strength → lower malleability.
- **(1 Mark)** **Factor 4 — Impurities/Alloying**: Solid-solution atoms pin dislocations → reduce malleability.
- **(1 Mark)** **Factor 5 — Strain Rate**: Lower strain rate → more time for dislocations to move → higher malleability.
- **(1 Mark)** **BCC Iron Specific**: At room temperature, Fe-α (BCC) is relatively brittle because the {110}⟨111⟩ slip is restricted. Above the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** (~150–200 °C for mild steel) and especially in the austenitic γ-Fe (FCC) range (above 912 °C), the slip systems become highly active and iron can be rolled, forged, and shaped extensively. **Hot working of iron is therefore always preferred.**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $A_0 = 50 \text{ mm}^2$, $A_f = 42 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 1 — Write the malleability formula:**

$$\text{Malleability \%} = \left( \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\text{Malleability \%} = \left( \frac{50 - 42}{50} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Calculate numerator and divide:**

$$\text{Malleability \%} = \left( \frac{8}{50} \right) \times 100 = 16\%$$

**Step 4 — Comment (2 Marks):**
A malleability index of **16%** for lead indicates **moderate-to-high malleability**, consistent with lead's well-known use in sheet forming, roofing, and radiation shielding. Lead's FCC structure (12 slip systems) and low CRSS make it ideal for sheet-metal forming, but its low melting point (327 °C) and toxicity limit modern structural use.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing Malleability & Ductility definitions** — Deduct **1 mark** if compressive/tensile distinction is not stated.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert angles to radians** in numerical problems — Keep degrees if using $\cos$ directly; never mix.
> 3. **Skipping units in Schmid's Law** — Always write MPa or N/mm² explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting examples** for FCC/BCC/HCP — Examiner expects at least one metal name per structure.
> 5. **Confusing slip planes and slip directions** — Slip plane = the plane of glide; slip direction = vector within that plane along which atoms move.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MALLEABILITY — Key Takeaways (Print-Ready)**

- Malleability = **plastic deformation under compression** (rolling, hammering, forging).
- Quantified as **% reduction in cross-sectional area** before fracture.
- Origin: **delocalized metallic bonding** + **dislocation glide on slip systems**.
- **FCC (12 systems) > BCC (12–48) > HCP (3 at RT)** in terms of malleability ranking.
- **Temperature** exponentially reduces CRSS → hot working vastly improves malleability.
- **Coarse grains, low strain rate, high purity** → enhanced malleability.
- **Schmid's Law:** $\tau_R = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$; yielding when $\tau_R = \tau_{CRSS}$.
- **Schmid Factor** $m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$; maximum = **0.5** at $\phi = \lambda = 45°$.
- **Most malleable metal:** Gold (Au) — 1 g → 1 m² foil.
- **Engineering uses:** Sheet metal (Al, low-carbon steel), gold leaf (Au), foil (Al), coinage (Cu-Ni, Cu-Zn).
- **Distinguish:** Malleability ↔ Compressive; Ductility ↔ Tensile.
- **Common error trap:** Confusing {hkl} slip planes with ⟨uvw⟩ slip directions — keep them separate in diagrams and derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brittleness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Brittleness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Brittleness** is the mechanical property of a solid material that causes it to fracture with little to no plastic deformation when subjected to stress. A brittle material absorbs relatively little energy prior to fracture and fails almost exclusively by **elastic deformation**, producing a sudden, catastrophic failure with negligible warning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard Terminology):**
> *"Brittleness is the property of a material which causes it to fracture or break without any appreciable plastic deformation. A brittle material fails by sudden rupture along planes perpendicular to the applied load."*

In materials science, brittleness is generally quantified in an inverse manner — through **ductility** or **toughness** measurements. A material is considered brittle when its **elongation at fracture** is **less than ~5%** under standard tensile testing conditions at room temperature.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy 💡

Think of brittleness like a **chalk stick or a dry biscuit**:
- When you try to bend a green twig 🌿, it flexes considerably before it eventually snaps — that is **ductile** behavior.
- When you try to bend a piece of chalk ✏️ or a glass rod 🪟, it snaps almost instantly with a clean break, producing a sharp sound — that is **brittle** behavior.
- Brittle materials do not "give warning" before breaking. The **strain** they absorb before rupture is so small that it is virtually invisible to the naked eye.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Brittle Materials:**
> - **Glass** (window panes, optical lenses)
> - **Cast Iron** (grey cast iron used in machine beds)
> - **Ceramics** (alumina, silicon carbide, porcelain)
> - **Concrete** (under tensile loading)
> - **Ice** at low temperatures
> - **High-carbon steel** (when hardened by quenching)

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (KTU-Standard)

| Metric | Typical Value for Brittle Materials |
|---|---|
| **Elongation at fracture** | **< 5%** |
| **Reduction in area** | **Negligible (~0%)** |
| **Fracture surface appearance** | **Bright, crystalline, shiny** (no necking) |
| **Fracture mode** | **Cleavage (transgranular or intergranular)** |
| **Energy absorbed before fracture** | **Very low** (low toughness) |

## 1.4 Loading Direction in Brittle Failure

In a typical uniaxial tensile test, brittle materials fracture along planes **perpendicular (normal) to the applied tensile stress** because brittle fracture propagates by **mode of separation** rather than shear.

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_0}
$$

where $\sigma_t$ is the tensile stress, $F$ is the applied load, and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Characteristics of Brittleness

A brittle material exhibits the following observable behaviors:

1. **Negligible Plastic Deformation** — Material obeys Hooke's law almost up to the point of fracture.
2. **Sudden Catastrophic Failure** — No visible necking or yielding before fracture.
3. **Low Fracture Toughness ($K_{IC}$)** — Resistance to crack propagation is very poor.
4. **High Sensitivity to Flaws** — Crack-like defects drastically reduce the load-bearing capacity.
5. **Temperature Sensitivity** — Many materials transition from ductile to brittle as temperature decreases (the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature — DBTT**).
6. **Strain-Rate Sensitivity** — Higher loading rates tend to make even borderline materials behave brittlely.

## 2.2 Stress–Strain Behavior Explained

For an ideally brittle material, the engineering stress–strain curve is **essentially linear up to fracture**:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \text{(valid until fracture)}
$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = Engineering stress (MPa)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (GPa)
- $\varepsilon$ = Engineering strain (dimensionless)

The area under the curve up to the fracture point represents the **modulus of resilience** $U_r$:

$$
U_r = \frac{\sigma_f^2}{2E}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a brittle material, $U_r$ is **extremely small**, because although $\sigma_f$ may be high (especially in ceramics), $E$ is also very large — making $U_r$ negligible. This is why brittle materials store very little elastic energy before they fracture.

## 2.3 Brittleness vs Ductility — The Fundamental Contrast

| Property | Brittle Material | Ductile Material |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic deformation before fracture | **Negligible** | **Significant** |
| Elongation | **< 5%** | **> 5%** (often 20–40%) |
| Fracture surface | **Shiny, granular, flat** | **Dull, fibrous, cup-and-cone** |
| Necking | **Absent** | **Present** |
| Energy absorption | **Low** | **High** |
| Crack propagation | **Rapid, unstable** | **Slow, stable (mostly)** |
| Examples | **Cast iron, glass, ceramic** | **Copper, aluminum, mild steel** |
| Failure warning | **None — sudden** | **Visible necking before fracture** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Brittleness Criterion** | $\%El < 5\%$ | Elongation below 5% qualifies a material as brittle |
| **Hooke's Law (valid up to fracture)** | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | Linear elastic behavior dominates |
| **Modulus of Resilience** | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_f^2}{2E}$ | Energy stored per unit volume before fracture |
| **Toughness (brittle)** | $K_{IC} \approx$ low | Plane-strain fracture toughness |
| **Stress Intensity Factor** | $K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Determines crack propagation tendency |
| **Critical Stress for Crack** | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}$ | Maximum safe stress for a given flaw size $a$ |
| **Fracture Condition (Griffith)** | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$ | Stress to propagate a crack of half-length $a$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Engineering:**
> In machine design, brittle materials are used where **high compressive strength and dimensional stability** are required (e.g., **cast iron machine tool beds**, **ceramic insulators**, **concrete columns**). However, they are avoided in tensile-loaded dynamic applications because of the **catastrophic, unpredictable failure** they exhibit.

## 2.5 The Ductile-to-Brittle Transition (DBTT)

A critical concept that KTU frequently tests: **BCC metals** (e.g., mild steel, ferritic alloys) exhibit a **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature** below which they behave in a brittle manner.

- **Above DBTT** → Ductile fracture (energy absorbed is **high**)
- **Below DBTT** → Brittle cleavage fracture (energy absorbed is **low**)

Factors that **raise DBTT** (making material more brittle):
- **Increased carbon content**
- **Larger grain size**
- **Higher strain rate**
- **Presence of notches or stress concentrators**
- **Neutron irradiation** (in nuclear reactor steels)

Factors that **lower DBTT** (making material more ductile):
- **Finer grain size**
- **Lower carbon content**
- **Nickel alloying additions**
- **Higher service temperature**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Griffith's Criterion for Brittle Fracture

This derivation is **highly important for KTU 14-mark questions**. It establishes the theoretical basis for brittle fracture in materials containing pre-existing flaws (microcracks).

### Step 1 — Energy Balance Concept
A. A. Griffith (1921) postulated that a brittle material contains **innumerable microcracks** of random size.
B. Fracture occurs when the **release of elastic strain energy** stored in the material equals or exceeds the **energy required to create two new fracture surfaces**.

### Step 2 — Mathematical Setup
Consider an infinite plate of unit thickness containing a through-thickness central crack of length $2a$. The plate is subjected to a uniform tensile stress $\sigma$ far from the crack.

$$
U_{elastic} = -\frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E}
$$

This is the **reduction in elastic strain energy** due to the presence of the crack. The negative sign indicates that the stored energy decreases as the crack grows.

### Step 3 — Surface Energy Term
The energy required to create two new surfaces (each of area $2a$ per unit thickness) is:

$$
U_{surface} = 2 \cdot (2a) \cdot \gamma_s = 4a\gamma_s
$$

where $\gamma_s$ is the **surface energy per unit area** (J/m²).

### Step 4 — Total Energy of the System

$$
U_{total} = U_{surface} + U_{elastic} = 4a\gamma_s - \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E}
$$

### Step 5 — Critical Condition for Crack Propagation
The crack will propagate spontaneously when the total energy is **stationary** with respect to crack length (i.e., further crack growth lowers the total energy of the system):

$$
\frac{dU_{total}}{da} = 0
$$

Applying the derivative step-by-step:

$$
\frac{d}{da}\left(4a\gamma_s - \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E}\right) = 0
$$

$$
4\gamma_s - \frac{2\pi a \sigma^2}{E} = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
4\gamma_s = \frac{2\pi a \sigma^2}{E}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Griffith Equation

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation (KTU-Favorite Question):**
> The critical fracture stress $\sigma_c$ is **inversely proportional to the square root of crack length** $a$. This means **doubling the crack size reduces fracture strength to ~71%**, and **increasing crack size by 4× reduces strength by half**. Tiny flaws thus have an enormous weakening effect in brittle materials.

### Step 7 — Critical Crack Length (Inverted Form)

Solving for $a$:

$$
a_c = \frac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi \sigma_c^2}
$$

This tells us the **maximum tolerable flaw size** for a given applied stress — a vital design parameter in ceramics, glass, and brittle composites.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Example

**Problem:** A brittle ceramic plate has $E = 300$ GPa and surface energy $\gamma_s = 1.0$ J/m². A surface crack of half-length $a = 0.5$ mm is detected. Determine the critical fracture stress.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$
E = 300 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}, \quad \gamma_s = 1.0 \text{ J/m}^2, \quad a = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Griffith's equation:**

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}
$$

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (300 \times 10^9) \times 1.0}{\pi \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 6 \times 10^{11}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.5708 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 10^{11}}{1.5708 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{3.8197 \times 10^{14}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c \approx 1.954 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 19.54 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This extremely low fracture stress (compared to the theoretical strength of $\sim E/10 \approx 30$ GPa) demonstrates **why brittle materials fail at loads far below their theoretical strength** — the presence of microcracks is the root cause.

## 3.3 Comparative Energy Absorption Matrix

| Material Type | Strain Energy Released (J/m²) | Fracture Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Glass** | $\sim 1$ | Brittle (cleavage) |
| **Cast Iron** | $\sim 10$–$10^2$ | Brittle |
| **Mild Steel (above DBTT)** | $\sim 10^5$–$10^6$ | Ductile (cup-and-cone) |
| **Pure Copper** | $\sim 10^5$–$10^6$ | Highly ductile |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Brittle Fracture Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Tensile Load sigma] --> B[Stress Concentration at Microcrack Tip]
    B --> C{Stress exceeds Critical Value sigma_c?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Crack Initiates / Propagates]
    C -- No --> E[Material Holds Load]
    D --> F[Fracture Surface Creation 2 gamma_s]
    D --> G[Elastic Strain Energy Released]
    F --> H[Total Energy Decreases]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Sudden Brittle Fracture]
    I --> J[Flat, Shiny, Crystalline Surface]
    I --> K[Perpendicular to Load Axis]
    J --> L[Final Outcome: Catastrophic Failure]
    K --> L
    style A fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ff9966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#cc3333,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style L fill:#660000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Flow Chart: Factors Influencing Brittleness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTERNAL[Internal Factors]
        A1[Composition - High Carbon]
        A2[Microstructure - Large Grains]
        A3[Crystal Structure - BCC, Ionic, Covalent]
    end
    subgraph EXTERNAL[External Factors]
        B1[Low Temperature]
        B2[High Strain Rate]
        B3[Stress Concentration - Notches]
        B4[Multiaxial Loading]
    end
    subgraph RESULT[Resulting Behavior]
        C1[Reduced Plastic Deformation]
        C2[Lower Fracture Energy]
        C3[Increased Brittleness]
    end
    INTERNAL --> RESULT
    EXTERNAL --> RESULT
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style A2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style A3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style B1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300
    style B2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300
    style B3 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300
    style B4 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300
    style C3 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Schematic Comparison: Brittle vs Ductile Tensile Behavior

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BT[Brittle Material Curve]
        B1[Origin 0,0] --> B2[Linear Elastic Region]
        B2 --> B3[Fracture Point - Sudden Drop]
    end
    subgraph DT[Ductile Material Curve]
        D1[Origin 0,0] --> D2[Elastic Region]
        D2 --> D3[Yield Point]
        D3 --> D4[Plastic Region - Strain Hardening]
        D4 --> D5[Ultimate Tensile Strength]
        D5 --> D6[Necking Region]
        D6 --> D7[Fracture Point]
    end
    style B3 fill:#ff3333,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D7 fill:#ff3333,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D3 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#000
    style D5 fill:#ff9900,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: Brittle Failure Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Elastic Loading Begins] --> P2[Step 2: Stress Rises Linearly with Strain]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Microcrack Activation at Flaw]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Stress Concentration at Crack Tip]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Griffith Criterion Satisfied]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Unstable Crack Propagation]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Complete Fracture - No Plastic Zone]
    style P1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style P5 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style P7 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read These Diagrams in the KTU Exam:**
> In 14-mark answers, always include a **labeled stress-strain sketch** showing brittle vs ductile behavior. Examiners give 2–3 marks purely for **well-labeled diagrams** even if calculations are partially incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define brittleness. List any four examples of brittle materials.**

**Model Answer:**

**Brittleness** is the property of a material by which it fractures **without any appreciable plastic deformation** when subjected to an applied load. The fracture occurs suddenly along planes perpendicular to the applied stress, with the material exhibiting only elastic strain prior to failure.

**Four examples of brittle materials:**
1. **Glass** (amorphous silicate)
2. **Cast iron** (especially grey cast iron)
3. **Ceramics** (alumina, porcelain, silicon carbide)
4. **Concrete** (under tensile stress)

Additionally, ice and high-carbon hardened steel also exhibit brittle behavior.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Differentiate between brittle fracture and ductile fracture.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Property | Brittle Fracture | Ductile Fracture |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plastic deformation** | Negligible / absent | Significant |
| 2 | **Elongation** | Less than 5% | Greater than 5% |
| 3 | **Fracture surface** | Flat, shiny, crystalline | Dull, fibrous, cup-and-cone |
| 4 | **Necking** | Absent | Present |
| 5 | **Energy absorbed** | Very low | High |
| 6 | **Mode of crack propagation** | Transgranular cleavage | Microvoid coalescence |
| 7 | **Warning before failure** | No warning | Visible necking |
| 8 | **Examples** | Cast iron, glass, ceramics | Mild steel, copper, aluminum |

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Candidates must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice between module questions).

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **Griffith theory of brittle fracture** with the necessary derivation of the critical stress equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A glass plate has Young's modulus $E = 70$ GPa and surface energy $\gamma_s = 0.5$ J/m². If it contains a surface crack of half-length $a = 1$ mm, calculate the **critical fracture stress** using Griffith's criterion. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Griffith's Theory of Brittle Fracture:**

Griffith (1921) proposed that:
- All brittle materials contain **innumerable microcracks/flaws** of varying sizes.
- Fracture occurs when the **release of elastic strain energy** equals or exceeds the **energy required to create new fracture surfaces**.

**Derivation:**

Consider an infinite plate with a central crack of length $2a$ under uniform tensile stress $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Elastic strain energy released:**

$$
\Delta U_{elastic} = \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E}
$$

**Step 2 — Surface energy created (two new surfaces):**

$$
U_{surface} = 4a\gamma_s
$$

**Step 3 — Total energy of the system:**

$$
U_{total} = 4a\gamma_s - \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E}
$$

**Step 4 — Critical condition** $\dfrac{dU_{total}}{da} = 0$:

$$
4\gamma_s - \frac{2\pi a \sigma^2}{E} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Final Griffith Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}}
$$

**Significance:**
- $\sigma_c$ is **inversely proportional** to $\sqrt{a}$ → small cracks drastically reduce strength.
- Explains why **brittle materials fail at stresses much lower than their theoretical strength**.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- $E = 70 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\gamma_s = 0.5$ J/m²
- $a = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 1 — Substitute into Griffith's equation:**

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (70 \times 10^9) \times 0.5}{\pi \times (1 \times 10^{-3})}}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$
2 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 0.5 = 7 \times 10^{10}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
\pi \times 10^{-3} \approx 3.1416 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{7 \times 10^{10}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{2.228 \times 10^{13}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c \approx 4.72 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 4.72 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students commonly **forget to convert units** (GPa → Pa, mm → m). This single error causes **a loss of 2–3 marks** in numerical problems. Always write the units explicitly before substituting values. Also ensure you square-root the entire expression — not just the numerator.

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting brittleness** in metallic materials. Explain the concept of **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** with a neat labeled sketch of impact energy vs temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two materials, A (cast iron) and B (mild steel), are tested in tension. Material A fractures at a stress of 200 MPa with negligible elongation, while material B yields at 250 MPa, reaches UTS of 410 MPa, and fractures at 30% elongation. Classify each material and justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Factors Affecting Brittleness:**

1. **Composition** — Higher carbon content in steels increases brittleness.
2. **Microstructure** — Coarse grains promote brittle behavior; fine grains improve toughness.
3. **Temperature** — Lower temperatures promote brittleness (especially in BCC metals).
4. **Strain Rate** — Higher loading rates increase brittleness.
5. **Stress Concentration** — Notches and surface flaws drastically reduce fracture strength.
6. **Crystal Structure** — BCC, ionic, and covalent structures tend to be brittle; FCC tends to be ductile.
7. **Irradiation Damage** — Neutron irradiation raises DBTT in reactor steels.
8. **Multiaxial Stress States** — Triaxial tensile stresses favor brittle fracture.

**DBTT Concept:**

The **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature** is the temperature below which a material's fracture behavior changes from ductile (high energy absorption) to brittle (low energy absorption). It is determined using **Charpy or Izod impact tests**.

**Sketch of Impact Energy vs Temperature:**

| Region | Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Upper Shelf** | High energy absorption (ductile) |
| **Transition Region** | Steep drop in energy |
| **Lower Shelf** | Low energy absorption (brittle) |

$$
\text{DBTT} = T \text{ at average of upper and lower shelf energies}
$$

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Material A — Cast Iron:**
- Fracture stress = 200 MPa
- Elongation = ~0% (negligible)
- No yield point mentioned

**Classification: BRITTLE**
- Absence of plastic deformation and yielding classifies it as brittle.
- Fracture surface would be **shiny and crystalline**.
- Behavior consistent with grey cast iron under tension.

**Material B — Mild Steel:**
- Yield stress = 250 MPa
- UTS = 410 MPa
- Elongation = 30% (>> 5%)

**Classification: DUCTILE**
- Significant plastic deformation (30% elongation) indicates ductile behavior.
- Visible yielding and necking expected before fracture.
- Fracture surface would show **cup-and-cone morphology**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Part (b):**
> Students often **compare only the stress values** and conclude the higher-stress material is "better." This is wrong. Material classification depends on **% elongation and fracture mode**, not on absolute strength. Always justify classification based on **plastic deformation capability**, not just numerical strength.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Brittleness** = fracture without plastic deformation.
✅ **Elongation < 5%** is the standard KTU criterion for brittleness.
✅ **Brittle materials** show **linear elastic stress-strain behavior** up to fracture.
✅ **Griffith's equation:** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$ — crack length is the dominant factor.
✅ **DBTT** applies primarily to **BCC metals** (ferritic steels).
✅ **Fracture surface**: shiny, flat, granular for brittle; dull, fibrous for ductile.
✅ **Fracture plane**: perpendicular to applied tensile load for brittle materials.
✅ **Examples**: glass, cast iron, ceramics, concrete, ice, hardened steel.
✅ **Reducing brittleness**: refine grains, reduce carbon, add nickel, raise temperature, reduce strain rate.
✅ **Cast iron** = brittle under tension but strong in compression → used for machine tool beds.
✅ Always **convert SI units** (GPa → Pa, mm → m) before substituting into formulas.
✅ **Theoretical strength** $\approx E/10$, but actual brittle strength $\ll$ theoretical due to flaws.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Fatigue** is defined as the process of progressive, localized permanent structural change (damage) occurring in a material subjected to fluctuating stresses and strains at points where the **stress amplitude** is locally sufficient to cause progressive fracture over a finite number of cycles. Eventually, complete fracture occurs if the loading is continued beyond a critical number of cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *Fatigue is the failure of a material under the action of repeated or cyclic loads, where the maximum stress is well below the ultimate tensile strength of the material. It is characterized by fracture occurring after a finite number of stress cycles, often without any obvious macroscopic plastic deformation.*

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Highlighted Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cyclic Stress** | Repeating stress pattern with constant amplitude and frequency |
| **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$)** | Average of maximum and minimum stress in a cycle |
| **Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$)** | Half the difference between maximum and minimum stress |
| **Stress Range ($\Delta \sigma$)** | Difference between maximum and minimum stress |
| **Stress Ratio ($R$)** | Ratio of minimum to maximum stress ($\sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$) |
| **Fatigue Life ($N_f$)** | Total number of cycles to failure at a given stress level |
| **Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$)** | Maximum stress below which material can endure infinite cycles |
| **Fatigue Strength** | Stress level at which failure occurs after a specified number of cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> The **mean stress** is calculated as: $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$
>
> The **stress amplitude** is calculated as: $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Paperclip Test

Imagine bending a paperclip back and forth repeatedly. The paperclip does not break on the first bend (well below the ultimate strength), nor does it show visible stretching. Yet after a finite number of bends, it suddenly snaps. This is **fatigue failure in action**.

The key insight: **A material can fail at stresses far below its static strength if the load is applied repeatedly**. This is why a rotating shaft, an aircraft wing, or a bridge component can fracture unexpectedly under cyclic loading — even though a single application of the same stress would cause no harm.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Roughly **80%–90% of all mechanical service failures** in engineered components (shafts, gears, springs, turbine blades) are attributed to fatigue. This makes fatigue analysis one of the most important topics in design and failure analysis.

## 1.4 Visualizing the Cyclic Stress Profile

A typical fatigue loading cycle can be one of several standard types:

- **Reversed Stress Cycle**: Stress alternates between equal magnitude tension and compression ($R = -1$). Example: rotating shaft.
- **Repeated Stress Cycle**: Stress varies from zero to a maximum tensile value ($R = 0$). Example: tension spring.
- **Fluctuating/Random Stress Cycle**: Irregular stress pattern. Example: aircraft wing under gust loading.
- **Mean Stress Effect**: When $\sigma_m \neq 0$, the cycle is asymmetric.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Fatigue Failure

Fatigue is **not** a single event — it is a progressive process. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate and explain the **three distinct stages** of fatigue failure:

### Stage 1: Crack Initiation (Nucleation)
- **Where**: At locations of stress concentration (surface defects, inclusions, sharp corners, machining marks, scratches).
- **Why**: Cyclic plastic deformation at the microscopic level causes **persistent slip bands (PSBs)** on the surface.
- **How**: Dislocation pile-ups and extrusions/intrusions form on favorably oriented slip planes.
- **Duration**: This stage consumes the majority of total fatigue life in high-cycle fatigue (**~90% of life**).

### Stage 2: Crack Propagation
- **Where**: The initiated micro-crack grows incrementally with each load cycle.
- **Why**: Localized stress intensity at the crack tip drives stable crack extension.
- **How**: Crack advances by a small increment per cycle — characterized by **striations** visible under electron microscopy.
- **Direction**: Typically propagates perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress direction.

### Stage 3: Final Fracture
- **Where**: When the crack reaches a critical size, the remaining cross-section cannot support the load.
- **Why**: The stress on the uncracked ligament exceeds the fracture strength.
- **How**: Sudden, often catastrophic, ductile or brittle fracture occurs.
- **Appearance**: Final fracture zone is usually rough and fibrous, while the fatigue zone appears smooth with beach-mark patterns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Beach Marks** (or **Clamshell Marks**): Macroscopic marks on the fracture surface indicating the position of the crack front at successive intervals. They are formed by changes in loading or environmental conditions during service.

## 2.2 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — The Heart of Fatigue Analysis

The **S-N Curve** is the fundamental graphical representation in fatigue analysis, plotting the **stress amplitude (S)** on the Y-axis against the **number of cycles to failure (N)** on a logarithmic X-axis.

### Key Features of S-N Curve:

- **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF)**: $N_f > 10^4 - 10^5$ cycles; stress is in the elastic regime.
- **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF)**: $N_f < 10^4$ cycles; stress causes measurable plastic strain.
- **Endurance Limit** ($\sigma_e$): For **ferrous metals** (e.g., steels), the S-N curve becomes asymptotic — there exists a stress below which the material will not fail regardless of cycles. This is the **endurance limit** or **fatigue limit**.
- **Fatigue Strength**: For **non-ferrous metals** (aluminum, copper alloys), the S-N curve never truly flattens. Thus, a **fatigue strength** is defined at a specific number of cycles (e.g., $10^7$ or $10^8$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | $\sigma_m$: MPa | Average cyclic stress |
| Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | $\sigma_a$: MPa | Half the stress range |
| Stress Range | $\Delta \sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | $\Delta \sigma$: MPa | Total stress excursion |
| Stress Ratio | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | $R$: dimensionless | $R=-1$: fully reversed |
| Alternating Stress | $\sigma_a = \sigma_{max} \cdot \dfrac{1 - R}{2}$ | MPa | In terms of R |
| Goodman Line | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$ | All in MPa | Most conservative |
| Gerber Line (Parabola) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2 = 1$ | All in MPa | Less conservative |
| Soderberg Line | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{y}} = 1$ | All in MPa | Uses yield strength |
| Paris Law (Crack Growth) | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ | $a$: m, $C$, $m$: constants | Crack propagation rate |
| Stress Intensity Factor Range | $\Delta K = Y \cdot \Delta \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$ | MPa$\sqrt{m}$ | $Y$: geometry factor |
| Basquin's Law | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | $\sigma_f'$: fatigue strength coeff., $b$: fatigue strength exp. | HCF relation |
| Coffin-Manson Law | $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f' (2N_f)^c$ | $\varepsilon_f'$: fatigue ductility coeff., $c$: fatigue ductility exp. | LCF relation |
| Endurance Limit Estimate (Steel) | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$ | Both in MPa | For $\sigma_{UTS} < 1400$ MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The Goodman, Gerber, and Soderberg relationships are used to find the **allowable stress amplitude** for a given mean stress — a very common KTU Part B question!

## 2.4 Mean Stress Effect — Why It Matters

When a component experiences a **non-zero mean stress** (i.e., $R \neq -1$), the allowable stress amplitude changes. Three classical relationships help analyze this:

### 2.4.1 Goodman Line (Linear, Most Conservative)
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$$

Solving for $\sigma_a$:
$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)$$

### 2.4.2 Gerber Parabola (Non-linear, Best Fit for Ductile Metals)
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2 = 1$$

### 2.4.3 Soderberg Line (Most Conservative — Uses Yield)
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Conservatism (Safer to Riskier):** Soderberg > Goodman > Gerber. Soderberg is rarely used in practice but is the most conservative since it uses yield strength. Gerber best matches experimental data for ductile metals.

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Fatigue Life

KTU examiners frequently test the **factors** that influence fatigue behavior:

1. **Surface Finish**: Rough surfaces contain micro-notches that act as crack initiation sites. **Polishing** increases fatigue life.
2. **Surface Treatments**: 
   - **Shot Peening**: Introduces compressive residual stresses at the surface, dramatically improving fatigue life.
   - **Case Hardening** (carburizing, nitriding): Hardens the surface, resisting crack initiation.
   - **Induction Hardening**: Similar effect.
3. **Microstructure**: Fine-grained materials outperform coarse-grained ones. Tempered martensite > pearlite in fatigue resistance.
4. **Mean Stress**: Compressive mean stresses (negative $R$) increase fatigue life; tensile mean stresses (positive $R$) decrease it.
5. **Environment**: **Corrosive environments** dramatically reduce fatigue life (called *corrosion fatigue*).
6. **Temperature**: High temperatures reduce fatigue strength; **thermal fatigue** arises from cyclic thermal strains.
7. **Frequency**: At low frequencies and high temperatures, **frequency effects** become significant (creep-fatigue interaction).
8. **Stress Concentrations**: Holes, notches, keyways, and fillets reduce fatigue life. The **fatigue stress concentration factor** ($K_f$) is less than the theoretical $K_t$ due to notch sensitivity ($q$):
$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

The principles of fatigue are foundational in:
- **Aerospace**: Wing structures, turbine blades, landing gear (must endure $10^7$–$10^9$ cycles).
- **Automotive**: Crankshafts, connecting rods, springs, valve trains.
- **Civil Engineering**: Bridges (wind-induced cyclic loading), offshore structures (wave loading).
- **Power Generation**: Steam and gas turbine blades, nuclear reactor components.
- **Biomedical**: Orthopedic implants (hip stems endure millions of walking cycles).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Goodman Line — Allowable Stress Amplitude

**Problem Setup:** A steel shaft has an ultimate tensile strength of $\sigma_{UTS} = 600$ MPa and endurance limit $\sigma_e = 300$ MPa. If a fluctuating load produces a mean stress of $\sigma_m = 100$ MPa, find the allowable stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ using the Goodman criterion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Recall the Goodman equation:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given numerical values:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{300} + \dfrac{100}{600} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the second term:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{300} + \dfrac{1}{6} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Isolate the $\sigma_a$ term:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{300} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{6}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the right-hand side:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{300} = \dfrac{5}{6}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by 300:
$$\sigma_a = 300 \times \dfrac{5}{6}$$

**Step 7:** Final answer:
$$\sigma_a = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** The result is less than $\sigma_e = 300$ MPa — this is expected because the positive mean stress reduces the allowable alternating component. If $\sigma_m = 0$, then $\sigma_a = \sigma_e = 300$ MPa. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Soderberg Line — Using Yield Strength

**Problem Setup:** Given a component with $\sigma_e = 250$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 500$ MPa, and $\sigma_m = 200$ MPa. Determine the safe alternating stress amplitude using the Soderberg criterion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Write the Soderberg relationship:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{250} + \dfrac{200}{500} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{250} + \dfrac{2}{5} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Isolate:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{250} = 1 - \dfrac{2}{5} = \dfrac{3}{5}$$

**Step 5:** Solve:
$$\sigma_a = 250 \times \dfrac{3}{5} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Soderberg is Most Conservative:** It uses $\sigma_y$ (yield) in the denominator instead of $\sigma_{UTS}$ (ultimate). Since $\sigma_y < \sigma_{UTS}$, the fraction $\sigma_m/\sigma_y > \sigma_m/\sigma_{UTS}$, leaving a smaller allowable $\sigma_a$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Paris Law — Crack Growth Rate

The **Paris Law** describes the steady-state (Region II) fatigue crack growth rate:

$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$$

Where:
- $a$ = crack length (m)
- $N$ = number of cycles
- $\Delta K$ = stress intensity factor range
- $C$, $m$ = material constants (typically $m = 2$ to $7$ for metals)

### Integration to Find Fatigue Life from Crack Initiation to Critical Size:

**Step 1:** Express $\Delta K$ in terms of crack length:
$$\Delta K = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into Paris Law:
$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a})^m$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:
$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} a^{m/2}$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables:
$$da \cdot a^{-m/2} = C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} \cdot dN$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from initial crack size $a_i$ to critical crack size $a_c$ over the life $N_f$:
$$\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2} \, da = C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} \int_{0}^{N_f} dN$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the left integral (assuming $m \neq 2$):
$$\left[\dfrac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2}\right]_{a_i}^{a_c} = C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} \cdot N_f$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $N_f$:
$$N_f = \dfrac{a_c^{1 - m/2} - a_i^{1 - m/2}}{(1 - m/2) \cdot C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2}}$$

**For the common case $m = 2$ (aluminum alloys approximately):**
$$N_f = \dfrac{\ln(a_c / a_i)}{C (Y \Delta \sigma)^2 \pi}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** Doubling the applied stress range reduces fatigue life by a factor of approximately $2^m$ (often $2^3 = 8$ or more). This non-linear sensitivity is why fatigue-safe design demands strict stress control.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Mean Stress Relationships

| Criterion | Equation | Type | Conservatism | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$ | Linear | Most conservative | Conservative design, ductile materials |
| **Goodman** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$ | Linear | Moderate | General engineering design, brittle materials |
| **Gerber** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2 = 1$ | Parabolic | Least conservative | Ductile metals (best experimental fit) |
| **Morrow** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_f'} = 1$ | Linear | Variable | Uses true fracture strength |

> [!NOTE]
> **For the KTU Exam:** When the problem does not specify, default to the **Goodman criterion** unless stated otherwise. Gerber is preferred for ductile materials experimentally, but Goodman is the most widely used in textbook problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cyclic Stress Types — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CYCLIC STRESS LOADING"] --> B["Reversed Cycle R = -1"]
    A --> C["Repeated Cycle R = 0"]
    A --> D["Fluctuating Cycle R varies"]
    A --> E["Random Cycle Irregular"]

    B --> B1["Example: Rotating Shaft"]
    C --> C1["Example: Tension Spring"]
    D --> D1["Example: Axle Under Bending"]
    E --> E1["Example: Aircraft Wing Turbulence"]

    A --> F["Key Parameters"]
    F --> F1["Mean Stress sigma_m"]
    F --> F2["Stress Amplitude sigma_a"]
    F --> F3["Stress Ratio R"]
    F --> F4["Stress Range delta sigma"]
```

## 4.2 Stages of Fatigue Failure — Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STAGE 1: Crack Initiation"] --> S2["STAGE 2: Crack Propagation"]
    S2 --> S3["STAGE 3: Final Fracture"]

    S1 --> S1a["Persistent Slip Bands Form"]
    S1 --> S1b["Micro-cracks Nucleate"]
    S1 --> S1c["~90 percent of Total Life for HCF"]

    S2 --> S2a["Slow Stable Growth"]
    S2 --> S2b["Striations Visible Under SEM"]
    S2 --> S2c["Perpendicular to Max Tensile Stress"]

    S3 --> S3a["Reaches Critical Crack Size"]
    S3 --> S3b["Remaining Area Fails Suddenly"]
    S3 --> S3c["Rough Fibrous Appearance"]
```

## 4.3 S-N Curve Architecture — Visual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Apply Cyclic Stress sigma_a"] --> B["Count Cycles to Failure N_f"]
    B --> C["Plot S-N Curve"]
    C --> D["Two Material Behaviors"]

    D --> E["Ferrous Metals Steels"]
    D --> F["Non-Ferrous Metals Aluminum Copper"]

    E --> E1["Definite Endurance Limit sigma_e Exists"]
    E1 --> E2["Below sigma_e Infinite Life"]

    F --> F1["No True Endurance Limit"]
    F1 --> F2["Fatigue Strength at 10^7 or 10^8 cycles"]
```

## 4.4 Mean Stress Effect — Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Given Mean Stress sigma_m Greater Than 0"] --> B["Choose Design Criterion"]
    B --> C1["Soderberg: Most Conservative Uses sigma_y"]
    B --> C2["Goodman: Linear Uses sigma_UTS"]
    B --> C3["Gerber: Parabolic Best Fit for Ductile"]

    C1 --> D1["Solve for Allowable sigma_a"]
    C2 --> D2["Solve for Allowable sigma_a"]
    C3 --> D3["Solve for Allowable sigma_a"]

    D1 --> E["Compare with Applied sigma_a"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    E --> F{"sigma_a applied less than allowable"}
    F -->|Yes| G["Design is SAFE"]
    F -->|No| H["Design FAILS — Modify Geometry or Material"]
```

## 4.5 Factors Affecting Fatigue Life — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FACTORS AFFECTING FATIGUE LIFE"] --> B["Material Factors"]
    A --> C["Geometric Factors"]
    A --> D["Environmental Factors"]
    A --> E["Service Factors"]

    B --> B1["Grain Size"]
    B --> B2["Microstructure"]
    B --> B3["Alloying Elements"]
    B --> B4["Residual Stresses"]

    C --> C1["Surface Finish"]
    C --> C2["Notches and Fillets"]
    C --> C3["Holes and Keyways"]

    D --> D1["Temperature"]
    D --> D2["Corrosive Media"]
    D --> D3["Radiation"]

    E --> E1["Mean Stress Level"]
    E --> E2["Loading Frequency"]
    E --> E3["Stress Amplitude"]
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fatigue Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["1. Define Loading Conditions"] --> B["2. Identify Material Properties"]
    B --> C["3. Apply Safety Factor n"]
    C --> D["4. Compute Design Stress"]
    D --> E["5. Apply Goodman or Gerber or Soderberg"]
    E --> F["6. Compare with Endurance Limit"]
    F --> G{"Is Allowable Greater Than Applied"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Design Acceptable"]
    G -->|No| I["Modify: Reduce Load or Change Material"]
    I --> A
```
