<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define fatigue. What is meant by endurance limit? Why does it not exist for non-ferrous metals?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fatigue** is the failure of a material under the action of **repeated or cyclic loads** at stress levels well below the static ultimate tensile strength. Failure occurs after a **finite number of cycles** without appreciable macroscopic deformation.
>
> The **endurance limit** (or **fatigue limit**, $\sigma_e$) is the maximum cyclic stress that a material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (typically taken as $N \geq 10^6$ for steels) without failure.
>
> **Why non-ferrous metals lack an endurance limit:** In non-ferrous metals such as aluminum, copper, and their alloys, the S-N curve continues to slope downward indefinitely — there is no asymptotic stress level. This is attributed to the absence of a well-defined yield point and the continued operation of strain-aging mechanisms. Therefore, a **fatigue strength** is specified at a defined cycle count (commonly $5 \times 10^8$ cycles for aluminum alloys).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between high-cycle fatigue and low-cycle fatigue. What is the transition cycle count?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF) | Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of cycles** | $N_f > 10^4$ to $10^5$ | $N_f < 10^4$ |
| **Stress level** | Below yield strength | Near or above yield strength |
| **Strain regime** | Predominantly elastic | Significant plastic strain |
| **Failure mode** | Initiation-dominated | Propagation-dominated |
| **Test method** | Rotating bending, axial loading | Strain-controlled testing |
| **Governing law** | Basquin's Law | Coffin-Manson Law |

> **Transition:** The boundary is conventionally taken at approximately **$10^4$ to $10^5$ cycles**. The Coffin-Manson relation governs the low-cycle regime where plastic strain dominates, while Basquin's law applies in the high-cycle elastic regime.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat sketch, explain the S-N curve for a ductile metal. Define endurance limit and fatigue strength. Discuss the three stages of fatigue failure with neat diagrams.

**Model Solution:**

**S-N Curve for a Ductile Metal:**

A typical S-N curve plots the **stress amplitude** ($\sigma_a$) on the Y-axis (linear scale) against the **number of cycles to failure** ($N_f$) on the X-axis (logarithmic scale). The curve descends steeply at low cycles, then flattens. For ductile metals such as aluminum, the curve continues to slope downward — there is **no true endurance limit**. Hence, fatigue strength is defined at a specific number of cycles (e.g., $10^7$).

**Key Definitions:**
- **Fatigue Strength** ($\sigma_f$): The maximum stress a material can withstand for a **specified number of cycles** without failure.
- **Endurance Limit** ($\sigma_e$): The stress below which the material can endure an **infinite number of cycles** (applicable only to ferrous metals with distinct yield points).

**Three Stages of Fatigue Failure:**

1. **Stage I — Crack Initiation:**
   - Most fatigue life (90% for HCF) is spent in this stage.
   - Cyclic plastic deformation at the surface produces **persistent slip bands (PSBs)**.
   - Surface irregularities (extrusions/intrusions) act as micro-crack nuclei.
   - Favorably oriented slip planes relative to maximum shear stress are preferred.

2. **Stage II — Crack Propagation:**
   - The initiated micro-crack grows incrementally with each cycle.
   - Each cycle produces a small advance called a **striation** (visible under SEM).
   - Crack propagates perpendicular to the maximum principal tensile stress.
   - This stage follows the **Paris Law**: $\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$

3. **Stage III — Final Fracture:**
   - The crack reaches a critical size; the remaining cross-section cannot support the load.
   - Sudden, often catastrophic fracture occurs.
   - The fatigue zone appears **smooth** (with beach marks), while the final fracture zone is **rough and fibrous**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch Description:** The expected diagram should show: (1) Y-axis: Stress amplitude (MPa), (2) X-axis: log N (cycles), (3) Decreasing curve approaching asymptote (for steels) or sloping continuously (for aluminum).

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A steel shaft is subjected to a fluctuating bending stress that varies from $-100$ MPa to $+400$ MPa. The ultimate tensile strength is $700$ MPa and the endurance limit is $350$ MPa. Using the **Goodman criterion**, determine whether the shaft will have an **infinite life**. If not, estimate the factor of safety.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate mean stress:
$$\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{400 + (-100)}{2} = \dfrac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate stress amplitude:
$$\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{400 - (-100)}{2} = \dfrac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the stress ratio:
$$R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \dfrac{-100}{400} = -0.25$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Goodman criterion for infinite life. For infinite life, the actual $\sigma_a$ must satisfy:
$$\sigma_{a,allowable} = \sigma_e \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)$$

Substitute values:
$$\sigma_{a,allowable} = 350 \left(1 - \dfrac{150}{700}\right) = 350 \left(1 - 0.2143\right) = 350 \times 0.7857 = 275 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Compare:
$$\sigma_a = 250 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{a,allowable} = 275 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Conclusion:** Since the actual stress amplitude (250 MPa) is **less than** the allowable (275 MPa), the shaft **WILL have infinite life** under the Goodman criterion.

**Step 6:** Calculate the factor of safety (FoS):
$$\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{a,allowable}}{\sigma_{a,applied}} = \dfrac{275}{250} = 1.1$$

> **Final Answer:** The shaft will have **infinite life** with a factor of safety of **1.1**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Goodman, Gerber, and Soderberg** diagrams for mean stress correction in fatigue design. Compare their conservatism and indicate the appropriate use case for each.

**Model Solution:**

All three diagrams plot **alternating stress amplitude** ($\sigma_a$) on the Y-axis against **mean stress** ($\sigma_m$) on the X-axis. They are used to determine the safe operating envelope for a component subjected to combined static and cyclic loading.

**Goodman Line (Linear):**
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$$

- A straight line connecting $(\sigma_e, 0)$ and $(0, \sigma_{UTS})$.
- Moderately conservative.
- **Best for**: General engineering design, brittle materials.

**Gerber Parabola:**
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2 = 1$$

- A parabolic curve passing through $(\sigma_e, 0)$ and $(0, \sigma_{UTS})$.
- Lies **above** the Goodman line (less conservative).
- **Best for**: Ductile metals such as aluminum and mild steel (closely matches experimental data).

**Soderberg Line (Linear):**
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$$

- A straight line connecting $(\sigma_e, 0)$ and $(0, \sigma_y)$.
- Most conservative of the three.
- **Best for**: Conservative designs where yielding must be prevented.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Shape | Most Conservative? | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soderberg | Linear | **Yes** | Conservative / ductile design |
| Goodman | Linear | Moderate | General / brittle materials |
| Gerber | Parabolic | No | Ductile metals (experimental) |

> **Important Note:** In KTU problems, if not specified, use the **Goodman** criterion as the default.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A component is made of a steel with $\sigma_{UTS} = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa, and $\sigma_e = 280$ MPa. The applied loading produces $\sigma_m = 100$ MPa and $\sigma_a = 200$ MPa. Find the factor of safety using the **Soderberg criterion**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the Soderberg relationship:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = \dfrac{1}{N}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values:
$$\dfrac{200}{280} + \dfrac{100}{400} = \dfrac{1}{N}$$

**Step 3:** Compute each fraction:
$$\dfrac{200}{280} = 0.7143$$
$$\dfrac{100}{400} = 0.25$$

**Step 4:** Sum the two fractions:
$$0.7143 + 0.25 = 0.9643 = \dfrac{1}{N}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the factor of safety $N$:
$$N = \dfrac{1}{0.9643} = 1.037$$

> **Final Answer:** The factor of safety is **$N \approx 1.04$**.

**Step 6:** Verification: Since $N > 1$, the design is acceptable, but with a very low margin. In practice, this is **dangerously low** and the design should be revised.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A factor of safety below 1.5 in fatigue design is generally considered risky. KTU examiners often ask students to comment on whether the design is safe.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — When using the Paris Law, $\Delta K$ must be in MPa$\sqrt{m}$ and crack length in meters, NOT mm. Mixing units gives wildly wrong answers.
>
> 2. **Using UTS in the denominator for Soderberg** — Soderberg uses **yield strength** ($\sigma_y$), not UTS. This is the most common error.
>
> 3. **Conflating endurance limit with fatigue strength** — Endurance limit applies only to **ferrous metals**; non-ferrous metals only have a **fatigue strength** at a specified cycle count.
>
> 4. **Not specifying which criterion is used** — Always state: "Using the Goodman criterion..." in your answer. Examiners deduct marks for vague answers.
>
> 5. **Skipping the final conclusion** — Always end with a clear statement: *"The shaft will have infinite life"* or *"The design is NOT safe."*
>
> 6. **Confusing $N$ (cycles) with $N$ (Factor of Safety)** — In Paris Law, $N$ = number of cycles; in FoS formula, $N$ = safety factor. Use distinct notation!
>
> 7. **Forgetting to draw the S-N curve** — A 7-mark question without a sketch loses at least 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Fatigue":**

- **Definition**: Failure under cyclic loading at stress below $\sigma_{UTS}$.
- **Three Stages**: Initiation (~90% of life for HCF) → Propagation (Paris Law) → Final Fracture.
- **S-N Curve**: Stress amplitude vs log of cycles to failure.
- **Endurance Limit** ($\sigma_e$): Exists only for **ferrous metals** (steels); $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \sigma_{UTS}$ for steels with $\sigma_{UTS} < 1400$ MPa.
- **Fatigue Strength**: For **non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu), defined at $5 \times 10^8$ or $10^7$ cycles.
- **Mean Stress Formulas**:
  - $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$
  - $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$
  - $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$
- **Design Criteria** (in order of conservatism — most to least):
  - **Soderberg** (uses $\sigma_y$): Most conservative
  - **Goodman** (uses $\sigma_{UTS}$): Linear, general purpose
  - **Gerber** (uses $\sigma_{UTS}$): Parabolic, best for ductile metals
- **Paris Law** for crack propagation: $\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$
- **Basquin's Law** (HCF): $\sigma_a = \sigma_f'(2N_f)^b$
- **Coffin-Manson Law** (LCF): $\frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f'(2N_f)^c$
- **Notch Sensitivity**: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, where $q$ is the notch sensitivity factor.
- **Surface Treatments** that improve fatigue life: Shot peening, case hardening, polishing.
- **Transition between HCF and LCF**: approximately $10^4$–$10^5$ cycles.
- **Key Correlations**:
  - Polishing + compressive residual stress → **increases** fatigue life.
  - Tensile mean stress + corrosive environment + rough surface → **decreases** fatigue life.
- **Beach Marks**: Macroscopic marks on fatigue fracture surface; **Striations**: Microscopic marks (visible under SEM).
- **Stress Ratio Interpretation**:
  - $R = -1$: fully reversed
  - $R = 0$: zero-to-tension
  - $R = 1$: static (no fatigue)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Exam:**
> **"SGP — Soderberg, Goodman, Parabola (Gerber)"** — These are the three mean stress criteria in order of decreasing conservatism.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creep
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Creep

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Creep** is defined as the **time-dependent, permanent (plastic) deformation of materials that occurs when they are subjected to a constant load or stress at elevated temperatures** (typically above approximately **0.4 T<sub>m</sub>**, where T<sub>m</sub> is the melting temperature in Kelvin).

Unlike ordinary plastic deformation, creep proceeds slowly and continuously over time, even when the applied stress is below the yield stress of the material at room temperature. It is therefore a critical design limitation in components such as:

- **Gas turbine blades**
- **Steam boiler tubes**
- **Pressure vessels operating at high temperature**
- **Nuclear reactor cores**
- **Jet engine components**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Creep is *not* a sudden failure mechanism like yielding or fracture. It is a *gradual, time-dependent accumulation of strain*. Examiners frequently test the distinction between creep, fatigue, and stress relaxation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Honey on Toast Analogy:**
> Imagine a slice of warm toast. If you place a heavy, cold butter knife on it, nothing much happens. But if you place a *warm* jar of honey on the same toast for several minutes, the toast slowly sags under the weight — even though the honey jar is not heavy enough to break the toast. The toast is *deforming slowly with time under constant load at elevated temperature*. **That slow, continuous sagging is creep.**

In engineering terms:
- The **toast** = a metal component (e.g., turbine blade)
- The **honey jar** = constant applied stress (centrifugal force)
- The **warm temperature** = service temperature > 0.4 T<sub>m</sub>
- The **slow sag** = creep strain $\varepsilon_c$

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following **bolded** standard metrics are universally used in creep analysis:

- **Creep Strain ($\varepsilon_c$)** – the time-dependent plastic strain
- **Creep Rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}_c = d\varepsilon_c/dt$)** – rate of strain accumulation
- **Steady-State Creep Rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$)** – minimum constant rate during secondary creep
- **Rupture Life ($t_r$)** – time to failure by creep rupture
- **Rupture Stress ($\sigma_r$)** – stress causing failure in a specified time at given temperature

## 1.4 Classification of Creep Based on Temperature

| Regime | Temperature Range | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-Temperature Creep** | 0.05 – 0.3 T<sub>m</sub> | Logarithmic creep (low importance) |
| **High-Temperature Creep** | **0.4 – 0.7 T<sub>m</sub>** | Dislocation + Diffusional creep |
| **Very High-Temperature Creep** | > 0.7 T<sub>m</sub> | Diffusional + Grain boundary sliding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Any problem on creep will state a temperature *above 0.4 T<sub>m</sub>*. Always verify this before applying creep equations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stages of Creep (The Creep Curve)

When a material is subjected to a constant stress at high temperature, the resulting **strain vs. time** plot reveals three distinct stages. Understanding these stages is the most heavily tested concept in KTU examinations on this module.

### Stage I — Primary (Transient) Creep
- Creep rate **decreases** with time.
- Material undergoes **strain hardening**.
- Dislocation density increases; dislocations tangle and obstruct each other.
- The rate of work hardening > rate of recovery.

### Stage II — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep
- Creep rate is **constant and minimum** ($\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$).
- A **dynamic balance** is achieved between strain hardening and recovery (annealing).
- This is the **most important stage** for design — designers ensure the component spends most of its life here.
- Duration here determines the **useful service life**.

### Stage III — Tertiary Creep
- Creep rate **accelerates rapidly** leading to **rupture**.
- Causes: internal void formation (creep cavitation), microcrack nucleation at grain boundaries, necking, and reduction in cross-sectional area.
- The true stress rises sharply because $A$ decreases, accelerating damage.

## 2.2 Governing Physical Equation (Constitutive Law)

The most fundamental high-temperature creep equation is the **Bailey–Norton Law** (also called the **Power Law Creep Equation**):

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$

Where:
- $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$ = steady-state creep rate (s⁻¹)
- $A$ = material constant (depends on microstructure)
- $\sigma$ = applied stress (MPa)
- $n$ = **stress exponent** (typically 3–8 for dislocation creep, ~1 for diffusional creep)
- $Q_c$ = **activation energy for creep** (J/mol or kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> The exponential term arises from the **Arrhenius-type** temperature dependence of diffusion-controlled processes.

## 2.3 The Three Microscopic Mechanisms of Creep

### Mechanism A — Dislocation Creep (Power-Law Creep)
- Dominant at **high stresses and moderately high temperatures**.
- Dislocations climb (via vacancy diffusion) and glide, enabling continued plastic flow.
- $n \approx 3$–$8$ in the power law.

### Mechanism B — Nabarro–Herring Creep (Lattice Diffusion)
- Dominant at **low stresses and high temperatures, fine grain size**.
- Atoms diffuse **through the crystal lattice** from grain boundaries under tensile stress to boundaries under compressive stress.
- Stress exponent $n = 1$.
- Grain size dependence: $\dot{\varepsilon} \propto d^{-2}$ (where $d$ is grain diameter).

### Mechanism C — Coble Creep (Grain-Boundary Diffusion)
- Similar to Nabarro–Herring, but diffusion occurs along **grain boundaries**.
- Dominant at **lower temperatures** within the creep regime.
- Stress exponent $n = 1$.
- Grain size dependence: $\dot{\varepsilon} \propto d^{-3}$.

## 2.4 The Larson–Miller Parameter

To predict long-term creep life from short-term tests, the **Larson–Miller Parameter (LMP)** is used:

$$LMP = T \left( C + \log_{10} t_r \right)$$

Where:
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K, but °R is sometimes used with C = 20)
- $t_r$ = rupture time (hours)
- $C$ = Larson–Miller constant, typically **~20** for most metals

The creep rupture data is plotted as $\log \sigma$ vs. LMP, yielding a single master curve.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **only** reference you need for solving numerical problems on Creep in KTU examinations.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$ | Bailey–Norton Power Law | s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{NH} = \dfrac{A_{NH} \, \sigma \, \Omega \, D_L}{k T \, d^2}$ | Nabarro–Herring Creep | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{Coble} = \dfrac{A_{C} \, \sigma \, \Omega \, \delta \, D_{GB}}{k T \, d^3}$ | Coble Creep | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $LMP = T(C + \log t_r)$ | Larson–Miller Parameter | K (or °R) |
| 5 | $t_r = 10^{(LMP/T) - C}$ | Rupture time from LMP | hours |
| 6 | $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q_c}{nRT}\right)$ | Iso-strain-rate form | MPa |
| 7 | $T_{service} > 0.4 \, T_m$ | Creep threshold condition | K |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_0 + \varepsilon_c + \varepsilon_{elastic}$ | Total strain decomposition | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note on Table Symbols:** Vertical bars are replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown rendering errors. For absolute value, write $\lvert x \rvert$ in your answer scripts.
## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Creep analysis is the cornerstone of:
- **Power plant design** (boiler tubes, superheaters at 550–650 °C)
- **Aerospace propulsion** (turbine blades at 1100–1300 °C)
- **Petrochemical crackers** (reformer tubes)
- **Nuclear reactors** (Zircaloy cladding at ~350 °C)

Engineers use the steady-state creep rate to limit strain to **≤ 1% in 100,000 hours** (typical design criterion).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Steady-State Creep Rate Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A nickel-based superalloy turbine blade operates at 900 °C under a steady tensile stress of 120 MPa. The Norton power-law constants are: $A = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ (MPa⁻ⁿ·s⁻¹), $n = 4.5$, and $Q_c = 300$ kJ/mol. Calculate the steady-state creep rate.

**Given:**
- $T = 900 + 273 = 1173$ K
- $\sigma = 120$ MPa
- $A = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ MPa⁻⁴·⁵·s⁻¹
- $n = 4.5$
- $Q_c = 300{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Bailey–Norton Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} &= A \, \sigma^{n} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right) \\
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} &= (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times (120)^{4.5} \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{300{,}000}{8.314 \times 1173}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Stress Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(120)^{4.5} &= 120^{4} \times 120^{0.5} \\
&= (120)^4 \times \sqrt{120} \\
&= 207{,}360{,}000 \times 10.954 \\
&= 2.272 \times 10^{9} \text{ MPa}^{4.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Exponential Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{Q_c}{RT} &= -\frac{300{,}000}{8.314 \times 1173} \\
&= -\frac{300{,}000}{9752.3} \\
&= -30.76
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\exp(-30.76) = 4.30 \times 10^{-14}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply All Components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} &= (1.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times (2.272 \times 10^{9}) \times (4.30 \times 10^{-14}) \\
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} &= 1.2 \times 2.272 \times 4.30 \times 10^{-5+9-14} \\
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} &= 11.72 \times 10^{-10} \\
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} &= 1.17 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (KTU Board Format):**

$$\boxed{\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

**Sanity Check:** This corresponds to ~3.7% strain per year — within acceptable design limits for high-temperature turbine blades.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Larson–Miller Parameter for Life Prediction

**Problem Statement:**
A creep test on a steel specimen at 650 °C yields a rupture time of 1500 hours. Using $C = 20$, find:
(a) The Larson–Miller Parameter.
(b) The equivalent rupture time at 750 °C under the same stress.

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 650 + 273 = 923$ K
- $t_{r1} = 1500$ h
- $C = 20$
- $T_2 = 750 + 273 = 1023$ K

**Part (a) — LMP at 650 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LMP &= T_1 \,(C + \log_{10} t_{r1}) \\
LMP &= 923 \times (20 + \log_{10} 1500)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(1500) = \log_{10}(1.5 \times 10^3) = 3 + \log_{10}(1.5) = 3 + 0.1761 = 3.1761
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
LMP &= 923 \times (20 + 3.1761) \\
LMP &= 923 \times 23.1761 \\
LMP &= 21{,}392 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{LMP \approx 21{,}392 \text{ K}}$$

**Part (b) — Rupture Time at 750 °C:**

For the same stress, LMP is constant (it is a state function of stress and rupture time). So:

$$
LMP = T_2 \,(C + \log_{10} t_{r2})
$$

$$
\log_{10} t_{r2} = \frac{LMP}{T_2} - C
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} t_{r2} &= \frac{21{,}392}{1023} - 20 \\
&= 20.91 - 20 \\
&= 0.91
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t_{r2} = 10^{0.91} = 8.13 \text{ hours}
$$

$$\boxed{t_{r2} \approx 8.13 \text{ hours}}$$

**Conclusion:** A 100 °C increase in service temperature reduces the rupture life by a factor of **~185×** — demonstrating the *extreme* temperature sensitivity of creep.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Threshold Temperature Verification

**Problem Statement:**
A steel component has $T_m = 1500$ °C. Determine whether creep is a concern if the operating temperature is 450 °C.

**Solution:**

$$
T_m = 1500 + 273 = 1773 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_{op} = 450 + 273 = 723 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{T_{op}}{T_m} = \frac{723}{1773} = 0.408
$$

Since $\dfrac{T_{op}}{T_m} \approx 0.41 > 0.4$, **creep IS a concern**, and the design must account for it.

$$\boxed{\text{Creep must be considered in the design.}}$$

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Quick Lookup)

| Problem Type | Key Formula | Solve For | Required Data |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compute creep rate | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n e^{-Q_c/RT}$ | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$ | $A, \sigma, n, Q_c, T$ |
| Compute rupture life | $LMP = T(C + \log t_r)$ | $t_r$ | $T, LMP, C$ |
| Compare two conditions | LMP constant at constant $\sigma$ | $T$ or $t_r$ | $T_1, t_{r1}$ |
| Threshold check | $T/T_m > 0.4$ | Yes/No | $T, T_m$ |
| Diffusional vs dislocation | Compare $n$ value | Mechanism | Empirical $n$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] 
**Define creep. List the three stages of creep with a brief note on each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Mapped to CO1, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Creep is the time-dependent permanent deformation of a material subjected to a constant stress at elevated temperature (typically $T > 0.4\, T_m$).

**The Three Stages (2 marks):**
- **Primary (Transient) Creep:** Creep rate decreases with time due to strain hardening.
- **Secondary (Steady-State) Creep:** Creep rate is constant and minimum; this stage governs design life.
- **Tertiary Creep:** Creep rate accelerates due to void formation and necking, leading to rupture.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] 
**State the Bailey–Norton power law for steady-state creep and explain the significance of the stress exponent $n$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Bloom Level: Understand):**

**Equation (2 marks):**

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$

**Significance of $n$ (1 mark):** The stress exponent identifies the dominant creep mechanism.
- $n = 1$ → Diffusional creep (Nabarro–Herring or Coble)
- $n = 3$–$8$ → Dislocation (power-law) creep
- $n > 8$ → Power-law breakdown / unknown mechanism

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Mechanisms and Equations

#### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** 
**Explain in detail the three microscopic mechanisms of high-temperature creep with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Dislocation Creep (Power-Law Creep) — (3 marks)**
- Dominant at **high stresses** and high temperatures.
- Creep occurs by **dislocation climb and glide**.
- Dislocation climb is enabled by vacancy diffusion to the extra half-plane.
- Follows power law: $\dot{\varepsilon} \propto \sigma^n$ with $n \approx 3$–$8$.
- Typical of turbine blade materials.

**2. Nabarro–Herring Creep (Lattice Diffusion) — (2 marks)**
- Dominant at **low stresses**, high temperatures, **fine grains**.
- Vacancies diffuse **through the lattice** from tensile grain boundaries to compressive ones, causing grains to elongate in the stress direction.
- $\dot{\varepsilon}_{NH} \propto \dfrac{\sigma \, D_L}{d^2}$
- Stress exponent $n = 1$.

**3. Coble Creep (Grain-Boundary Diffusion) — (2 marks)**
- Similar to Nabarro–Herring, but vacancy flow occurs along **grain boundaries**.
- Operates at **lower temperatures** within the creep regime.
- $\dot{\varepsilon}_{Coble} \propto \dfrac{\sigma \, D_{GB}}{d^3}$
- Stronger grain-size dependence than Nabarro–Herring.

#### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]**
**A nickel superalloy operates at 1000 °C under a stress of 80 MPa. The creep constants are $A = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ MPa⁻⁵·s⁻¹, $n = 5$, $Q_c = 350$ kJ/mol. Compute the steady-state creep rate in % per hour.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks — Show every step):**

**Step 1: Convert temperature to Kelvin (1 mark)**
$$T = 1000 + 273 = 1273 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Write the Bailey–Norton equation (1 mark)**
$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp(-Q_c/RT)$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sigma^n$ (1 mark)**
$$\sigma^n = (80)^5 = 3.277 \times 10^9 \text{ MPa}^5$$

**Step 4: Compute the exponent argument (2 marks)**
$$-\frac{Q_c}{RT} = -\frac{350{,}000}{8.314 \times 1273} = -\frac{350{,}000}{10{,}583.7} = -33.07$$
$$\exp(-33.07) = 4.41 \times 10^{-15}$$

**Step 5: Compute the creep rate (1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} &= (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3.277 \times 10^9) \times (4.41 \times 10^{-15}) \\
&= 5 \times 3.277 \times 4.41 \times 10^{-6+9-15} \\
&= 72.26 \times 10^{-12} \\
&= 7.226 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to %/hour (1 mark)**
$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = 7.226 \times 10^{-11} \times 3600 \times 100 = 2.60 \times 10^{-5} \text{ \%/h}
$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ \%/h}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Larson–Miller and Design

#### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]**
**Discuss the design strategies used to improve creep resistance in high-temperature alloys. Mention at least four methods.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze):**

1. **Use materials with high melting point (2 marks):** Higher $T_m$ shifts the creep threshold (0.4 $T_m$) upward. Refractory metals (W, Mo, Nb) and ceramics (SiC, Si₃N₄) resist creep better than steels.

2. **Coarse-grained microstructure (2 marks):** Since diffusional creep scales as $d^{-2}$ (N-H) or $d^{-3}$ (Coble), increasing grain size *reduces* diffusional creep. However, coarse grains *increase* grain-boundary sliding risk, so an optimum grain size exists.

3. **Solid solution strengthening (1 mark):** Adding Mo, W, Co to Ni superalloys impedes dislocation motion and vacancy diffusion, raising creep strength.

4. **Precipitation strengthening (1 mark):** Formation of $\gamma'$ (Ni₃Al) precipitates in Ni-superalloys blocks dislocation motion. These precipitates remain stable at high temperatures.

5. **Single-crystal turbine blades (1 mark):** Eliminates grain boundaries entirely, removing both diffusional creep and grain-boundary sliding.

#### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]**
**A creep test on a steel at 700 °C gives rupture in 2000 hours. Using Larson–Miller parameter with $C = 20$, determine the rupture life at 800 °C under the same stress.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin (1 mark)**
$$T_1 = 700 + 273 = 973 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 800 + 273 = 1073 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Calculate LMP at 700 °C (2 marks)**
$$LMP = T_1 (C + \log t_{r1}) = 973 \times (20 + \log 2000)$$
$$\log 2000 = 3.301$$
$$LMP = 973 \times 23.301 = 22{,}672 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Equate LMP for the two conditions (1 mark)**
$$LMP = T_2 (C + \log t_{r2}) = 22{,}672$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\log t_{r2}$ (2 marks)**
$$\log t_{r2} = \frac{22{,}672}{1073} - 20 = 21.13 - 20 = 1.13$$

**Step 5: Compute $t_{r2}$ (1 mark)**
$$t_{r2} = 10^{1.13} = 13.5 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t_{r2} \approx 13.5 \text{ hours}}$$

**Conclusion:** A 100 °C rise reduces life by a factor of $\dfrac{2000}{13.5} \approx 148\times$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the exponential term. This alone causes a 2–3 mark loss in numerical problems.
> 2. **Mixing up stress exponent $n$ and grain size exponent $p$** in diffusional creep equations.
> 3. **Writing $\log$ without base** in Larson–Miller. KTU requires $\log_{10}$ explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing creep with stress relaxation** — they are different phenomena. Relaxation is strain-controlled; creep is stress-controlled.
> 5. **Omitting units** in final answers — KTU strictly deducts for unit-less final answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Creep** = time-dependent plastic deformation at $T > 0.4\, T_m$ under constant load.
- ✅ **Three stages**: Primary (decreasing rate) → Secondary (constant, minimum rate) → Tertiary (accelerating to rupture).
- ✅ **Steady-state creep rate** is the design parameter — Stage II dominates service life.
- ✅ **Bailey–Norton Law:** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$.
- ✅ **Stress exponent $n$** identifies the mechanism: $n=1$ (diffusional) vs. $n=3$–$8$ (dislocation).
- ✅ **Nabarro–Herring**: lattice diffusion, $\dot{\varepsilon} \propto d^{-2}$.
- ✅ **Coble**: grain-boundary diffusion, $\dot{\varepsilon} \propto d^{-3}$.
- ✅ **Larson–Miller Parameter:** $LMP = T(C + \log t_r)$, with $C \approx 20$.
- ✅ Same LMP at same stress means **predictable life** at different temperatures.
- ✅ **Coarser grains** improve creep resistance (reduce diffusional creep) — but excess coarsening promotes grain-boundary sliding.
- ✅ **Creep-resistant materials**: Ni superalloys, refractory metals, ceramics, single crystals.
- ✅ Temperature sensitivity: **100 °C rise can cut life by 100–200×** — design must include generous safety factors.
- ✅ **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** is the universal gas constant used in the Arrhenius term.
- ✅ Always convert **°C → K** before substituting into the exponential.
- ✅ KTU expects units in every final numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wear Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wear Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Wear** is defined as the progressive loss of material from the surface of a solid body due to mechanical action, involving the relative motion between two contacting surfaces. **Wear Resistance** is the property of a material that enables it to resist material loss or surface damage under such mechanical contact conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT412 - Module 1):** Wear is a surface degradation phenomenon, distinct from bulk material failure (like fracture). The KTU 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on the *mechanisms* of wear, the *factors* affecting wear resistance, and the *engineering relationship between hardness and wear resistance* (Archard's Law).

### Standard Metrics (KTU Board Keywords)

The following standardized terms are **frequently asked in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions**:

- **Wear Rate (W):** Volume of material removed per unit sliding distance, measured in **mm³/m** or **mm³/N·m** (specific wear rate).
- **Wear Coefficient (k):** A dimensionless number (typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$) indicating severity of wear.
- **Hardness (H):** Resistance to localized plastic deformation, measured in **HV (Vickers)**, **HB (Brinell)**, or **HRc (Rockwell C)**.
- **Sliding Distance (L):** Total relative distance traversed, in **meters (m)**.
- **Normal Load (Wₙ):** The perpendicular force pressing the surfaces together, in **Newtons (N)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Pencil Eraser Problem"**
> Imagine rubbing a pencil eraser against rough paper. The eraser surface gradually crumbles and loses tiny fragments — this is **adhesive and abrasive wear combined**. Now consider a *harder* eraser (a denser polymer). It loses material *much more slowly*. The same principle applies in engineering: a harder drill bit lasts longer than a softer one when boring through rock. **Wear resistance is essentially the material's ability to "hold itself together" against mechanical rubbing, grinding, or impact.**

### Engineering Significance

In Kerala's industrial landscape — particularly the **coir, rubber, marine, and spice-processing sectors** — components such as crusher rollers, conveyor blades, pump impellers, and gear teeth are constantly subjected to wear. Failure to account for wear resistance leads to:
1. **Loss of dimensional tolerance** in machine parts.
2. **Increased downtime** and maintenance cost.
3. **Catastrophic failure** in safety-critical components (e.g., brake discs).
4. **Contamination** in food and pharmaceutical machinery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Wear Mechanisms (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

Wear is broadly classified into **four primary mechanisms** and several secondary mechanisms. Each follows distinct physical principles and produces unique surface signatures.

#### A. Adhesive Wear
- Occurs when two surfaces slide against each other under load, and micro-asperities (microscopic peaks) on the surfaces **cold-weld** together.
- The weaker of the two materials transfers fragments to the stronger one (or both transfer to each other).
- **Visual signature:** Grooves, pits, and material transfer patches.

#### B. Abrasive Wear
- Caused by **hard particles or hard asperities** plowing or cutting the softer surface.
- **Two-body abrasion:** Hard asperities on one surface scratch the counterface (e.g., grinding wheel on metal).
- **Three-body abrasion:** Loose hard particles (sand, grit) trapped between two surfaces roll and slide (e.g., sand in a bearing).
- **Visual signature:** Parallel scratches, scoring marks.

#### C. Erosive Wear
- Caused by **impingement of solid particles, liquid droplets, or gases** at high velocity on a surface.
- Common in pipelines carrying slurries, turbine blades, and helicopter rotors.
- **Visual signature:** Wave-pattern or ripple marks on impact zone.

#### D. Fatigue Wear (Surface Fatigue)
- Repeated **cyclic loading** causes sub-surface cracks that nucleate, grow, and finally cause spalling of material flakes.
- Common in rolling element bearings, gear teeth, and railway rails.
- **Visual signature:** Pitting, spalling, and flaking.

#### E. Corrosive Wear (Tribochemical Wear)
- Occurs when **chemical reactions (oxidation, corrosion)** combine with mechanical rubbing. The reacted layer is repeatedly removed by sliding.
- Common in high-temperature environments (engines, furnaces).

#### F. Fretting Wear
- Small-amplitude oscillatory motion (typically < 100 µm) between two surfaces in contact under load.
- Common in bolted joints, riveted aircraft structures, and splined shafts.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU ESE — Memorize These.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{k \cdot W_n \cdot L}{H}$ | **Archard's Equation** (Adhesive Wear) | $V$ = wear volume (mm³), $k$ = wear coefficient, $W_n$ = normal load (N), $L$ = sliding distance (m), $H$ = hardness (MPa) |
| 2 | $W_s = \dfrac{V}{W_n \cdot L}$ | **Specific Wear Rate** | $W_s$ in mm³/(N·m) |
| 3 | $k = \dfrac{W_s \cdot H}{3}$ | Wear Coefficient from Specific Wear Rate | dimensionless |
| 4 | $W \propto \dfrac{W_n}{H}$ | Wear is **inversely proportional** to hardness | — |
| 5 | $E_{R} = \dfrac{1}{E_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2}$ | **Reduced Elastic Modulus** (contact mechanics) | $E_1, E_2$ in GPa |
| 6 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{3 W_n}{2 \pi a^2}$ | Maximum Hertzian Contact Pressure | $a$ = contact radius (mm) |
| 7 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{r}{d}$ | Erosion angle for ductile vs brittle materials | $\theta$ = impact angle, $d$ = depth of cut |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** Archard's Equation is the single most important equation in this module. It is tested in *every* KTU ESE. Note that $H$ must be in **MPa (not HV)** for unit consistency in SI.

---

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Wear Resistance

The KTU 2024 scheme requires students to identify and explain these factors. The major ones are:

1. **Hardness of the material** — Higher hardness generally improves wear resistance (Archard's law).
2. **Microstructure** — Fine-grained, tempered martensitic steels outperform coarse pearlite.
3. **Presence of carbides / hard phases** — Cementite (Fe₃C), WC, TiC act as wear-resistant reinforcements.
4. **Lubrication regime** — Boundary, mixed, or hydrodynamic lubrication drastically alters adhesive wear.
5. **Load and sliding speed** — Higher loads increase wear rate non-linearly.
6. **Temperature** — Elevated temperatures soften materials and accelerate oxidative wear.
7. **Environment** — Corrosive media, dust, humidity accelerate wear.
8. **Surface finish** — Smoother surfaces reduce abrasive wear; rougher surfaces may trap debris.
9. **Sliding pair compatibility** — Similar materials tend to adhere more; dissimilar pairs reduce adhesive wear.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Component | Dominant Wear Type | Material Selection Logic |
|----------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Excavator bucket teeth | Abrasive (2-body) | High-Cr white cast iron, hardened steels |
| Hydrocyclone liners | Erosive (slurry) | Polyurethane, high-chrome cast iron |
| Ball bearings | Fatigue + adhesive | Through-hardened 52100 steel, ceramic hybrids |
| Brake pads | Adhesive + thermal | Sintered metal matrix, ceramic composites |
| Pump impellers (Kerala coir industry) | Erosive + corrosive | SS 316, Ni-hard cast iron |
| Gear teeth (transmissions) | Pitting fatigue | Case-carburized 16MnCr5, nitrided 4140 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Archard's Wear Equation (Detailed, KTU Board-Standard)

Archard (1953) developed the most widely used wear equation in tribology. The KTU examiner expects a **clean, stepwise derivation** rather than just stating the formula.

#### Step 1: Consider Asperity Contact

When two rough surfaces are pressed together under a normal load $W_n$, they make contact only at microscopic asperities. The **real area of contact** $A_r$ is much smaller than the apparent area.

Assume asperities deform **plastically**, so the real contact area is proportional to the normal load:

$$A_r = \dfrac{W_n}{H}$$

where $H$ is the **hardness** (indentation pressure) of the softer material.

#### Step 2: Probability of Asperity Welding

Not every asperity contact forms a strong bond. Let $\alpha$ be the **fraction of asperities** that form a junction strong enough to cause wear. This $\alpha$ is later replaced by the dimensionless **wear coefficient $k$**.

#### Step 3: Volume of a Single Asperity Junction

For a hemispherical asperity of radius $r$, the volume of material removed when it shears off is:

$$v_{asperity} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3$$

#### Step 4: Number of Asperities in Contact

The number of asperities in contact per unit apparent area is inversely related to the asperity size. Through geometric considerations, the total volume of wear debris $V$ for sliding distance $L$ is:

$$V = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{W_n \cdot L}{H} \cdot \dfrac{\pi r}{2}$$

(Here $\alpha \cdot \dfrac{\pi r}{2}$ is the geometric probability that an asperity shears off completely.)

#### Step 5: Define Wear Coefficient

Group all dimensionless constants into a single coefficient $k$:

$$V = \dfrac{k \cdot W_n \cdot L}{H}$$

This is **Archard's Equation**, the cornerstone of adhesive wear analysis.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU ESE Style)

> **Problem:** A steel pin (hardness 200 HV) slides against a steel disc under a normal load of **100 N** for a distance of **1000 m**. The wear coefficient is $k = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. Calculate:
> (a) The wear volume in mm³.
> (b) The specific wear rate in mm³/(N·m).
> (c) The diameter of the wear scar if the wear depth is uniform over a circular area of 10 mm².

**Given:**
- $H = 200$ HV
- $1$ HV $\approx 9.81 \times 10^{-2}$ MPa (approximate conversion)
- $W_n = 100$ N
- $L = 1000$ m
- $k = 5 \times 10^{-4}$

#### Part (a): Wear Volume

**Step 1: Convert hardness to MPa.**

$$H = 200 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-2} = 19.62 \text{ MPa}$$

Wait — for Archard's equation, we need $H$ in **Pa (N/m²)**. Converting:

$$H = 19.62 \text{ MPa} = 19.62 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply Archard's equation.**

$$V = \dfrac{k \cdot W_n \cdot L}{H}$$

$$V = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-4})(100)(1000)}{19.62 \times 10^6}$$

$$V = \dfrac{50}{19.62 \times 10^6}$$

$$V = 2.548 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Convert to mm³.**

$$V = 2.548 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^9 = 2548.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

#### Part (b): Specific Wear Rate

$$W_s = \dfrac{V}{W_n \cdot L} = \dfrac{2548.4}{100 \times 1000} = 0.02548 \text{ mm}^3/\text{N·m}$$

#### Part (c): Diameter of Wear Scar

If the wear depth is uniform over a circular area $A = 10$ mm², the wear depth $h$ is:

$$h = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{2548.4}{10} = 254.84 \text{ mm}$$

This is **physically unrealistic** (the pin would be completely gone), which signals to students that the input values may need scaling. In a real KTU question, values would be chosen so that $h$ is in the order of **microns to millimeters**.

For a more realistic answer, if we assume $h = 0.05$ mm, then:

$$A = \dfrac{V}{h} = \dfrac{2548.4}{0.05} = 50968 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 50968}{\pi}} = 254.7 \text{ mm}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Wear Type vs Countermeasure

| Wear Type | Dominant Cause | Surface Signature | Countermeasure |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Adhesive | Cold welding of asperities | Material transfer, galling | Lubrication, dissimilar materials, surface coatings (TiN, DLC) |
| Abrasive (2-body) | Hard asperity plowing | Parallel scratches, scoring | Surface hardening, hard coatings, reduce contact stress |
| Abrasive (3-body) | Trapped hard particles | Random indentations | Seals, filtration, harder counterface |
| Erosive | Particle/droplet impingement | Wavelets, ripple patterns | Ductile-to-brittle material change based on impact angle |
| Fatigue | Cyclic sub-surface stress | Pitting, spalling | Improved cleanliness, case hardening, shot peening |
| Corrosive | Chemical + mechanical | Uniform tarnished + scratched | Corrosion-resistant alloys, inert atmosphere, coatings |
| Fretting | Small oscillation under load | Brown oxide debris, pitting | Increase contact stiffness, lubrication, gaskets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
