## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "wear resistance" and list any four factors that influence the wear resistance of a material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Wear resistance is the ability of a material to resist progressive loss of mass or surface damage when subjected to mechanical contact with another body in relative motion. It is the inverse of wear rate.

**Four factors (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Hardness** of the material (higher hardness → higher wear resistance, per Archard's equation).
2. **Microstructure** (fine-grained structures resist wear better than coarse ones).
3. **Presence of hard phases** (carbides, oxides, borides act as wear-resistant reinforcements).
4. **Operating conditions** (load, speed, temperature, lubrication regime, environment).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State Archard's equation for adhesive wear and explain the significance of the wear coefficient $k$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Archard's Equation (2 marks):**

$$V = \dfrac{k \cdot W_n \cdot L}{H}$$

where $V$ = wear volume, $k$ = wear coefficient, $W_n$ = normal load, $L$ = sliding distance, $H$ = hardness of the softer material.

**Significance of $k$ (1 mark):** The wear coefficient $k$ is a **dimensionless empirical constant** (typically ranging from $10^{-2}$ for severe wear to $10^{-7}$ for mild wear) that captures the combined effect of material pair, surface condition, lubrication, and environment. It reflects the **probability** that an asperity contact results in wear particle formation.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the mechanisms of **adhesive wear** and **abrasive wear**. Discuss two engineering examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive **Archard's equation** for adhesive wear. A copper pin (hardness 80 HV) slides against a hardened steel disc under a normal load of 50 N. The sliding distance is 500 m, and the wear coefficient is $2 \times 10^{-4}$. Calculate the wear volume in mm³. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Adhesive Wear (3.5 marks):**

1. Definition: Adhesive wear occurs when two surfaces in sliding contact form **microscopic cold-welded junctions** at asperity contacts.
2. Mechanism:
   - Asperities of the softer material yield and deform plastically.
   - High local pressure causes adhesion (cold welding).
   - Continued sliding shears these junctions, transferring material to the counterface.
   - Some transferred material detaches as loose wear debris.
3. Examples: Piston ring scuffing in IC engines, sliding of soft metal bearing on hardened shaft.
4. Countermeasure: Use dissimilar material pairs (e.g., steel + bronze), apply lubrication, use surface coatings.

**Part (a) — Abrasive Wear (3.5 marks):**

1. Definition: Abrasive wear occurs when a hard surface or hard particles **plow or cut** a softer surface.
2. Two-body abrasion: Hard asperities on one surface scratch the other (e.g., grinding wheel, file).
3. Three-body abrasion: Hard particles (sand, grit) trapped between surfaces roll and slide (e.g., slurry pumps, excavator teeth).
4. Examples: Wear of ploughshares in soil, conveyor screws in dusty environments (Kerala coir industry), drill bits on rock.

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of Archard's Equation (4 marks):**

The detailed derivation is shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps:

1. Real contact area $A_r = W_n / H$ (from plastic deformation).
2. Probability factor $\alpha$ for junction welding.
3. Volume of sheared asperity: $v = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3$.
4. Summing over all asperities and integrating over distance $L$:

$$V = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{W_n \cdot L}{H} \cdot \dfrac{\pi r}{2}$$

5. Defining $k = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\pi r}{2}$:

$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{k \cdot W_n \cdot L}{H}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- $H = 80$ HV
- $1$ HV $= 9.81 \times 10^{-2}$ MPa
- $W_n = 50$ N
- $L = 500$ m
- $k = 2 \times 10^{-4}$

**Step 1: Convert hardness to Pa.**

$$H = 80 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-2} \text{ MPa} = 7.848 \text{ MPa}$$

$$H = 7.848 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply Archard's equation.**

$$V = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-4})(50)(500)}{7.848 \times 10^6}$$

$$V = \dfrac{5}{7.848 \times 10^6} = 6.371 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Convert to mm³.**

$$V = 6.371 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^9 = 637.1 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Final Answer:** $V \approx 637.1 \text{ mm}^3$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Classify the different **mechanisms of wear** with suitable diagrams. Describe **erosive wear** and **fatigue wear** in detail, including their surface signatures and countermeasures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **relationship between hardness and wear resistance**. A steel component (hardness 250 HV) operates under a normal load of 200 N and a sliding speed of 2 m/s for 30 minutes. If the wear coefficient is $3 \times 10^{-4}$, calculate the **specific wear rate** in mm³/(N·m). Assume the wear volume is 450 mm³. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Classification (3 marks):**

Wear is classified into:
1. Adhesive wear
2. Abrasive wear (2-body and 3-body)
3. Erosive wear
4. Surface fatigue wear
5. Corrosive (tribochemical) wear
6. Fretting wear

**Part (a) — Erosive Wear (2 marks):**

Erosive wear is caused by the **impingement of solid particles, liquid droplets, or gas jets** at high velocity on a surface. The mechanism involves:

- Impact energy transfer from particle to surface.
- **Ductile materials** (e.g., aluminum, mild steel): Maximum wear at shallow angles (15°–30°); surface shows wave-like ripples.
- **Brittle materials** (e.g., ceramics, glass): Maximum wear at normal incidence (90°); surface shows lateral cracking.
- Countermeasures: Use hard facing, polymer liners, change impact angle, reduce particle velocity.

**Part (a) — Fatigue Wear (2 marks):**

Fatigue wear (also called **surface fatigue** or **pitting**) occurs due to **repeated cyclic sub-surface stress** during rolling or sliding contact. Mechanism:

- Hertzian contact stresses cause plastic deformation in sub-surface regions.
- Sub-surface cracks nucleate at inclusions, voids, or second-phase particles.
- Cracks propagate parallel to the surface.
- Material finally **spalls off** as flakes, leaving pits.
- Countermeasures: Improve steel cleanliness, vacuum degassing, case hardening (carburizing, nitriding), shot peening to introduce compressive residual stresses.

---

**Part (b) — Relationship between Hardness and Wear Resistance (3 marks):**

According to **Archard's equation**, the wear volume $V$ is **inversely proportional** to hardness $H$:

$$V = \dfrac{k \cdot W_n \cdot L}{H}$$

Therefore, **higher hardness → lower wear volume → higher wear resistance**. This relationship holds best for:

- Adhesive wear in metals.
- Mild abrasive wear in metals.

However, the relationship is **not universal**:
- For ceramics, fracture toughness becomes equally important.
- For polymers, hardness-wear relationship is non-linear.
- Excessive hardness may reduce toughness and increase brittle fracture.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- Wear volume $V = 450$ mm³
- Normal load $W_n = 200$ N
- Sliding time = 30 minutes = 1800 s
- Sliding speed $v = 2$ m/s

**Step 1: Calculate sliding distance $L$.**

$$L = v \times t = 2 \times 1800 = 3600 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply specific wear rate formula.**

$$W_s = \dfrac{V}{W_n \cdot L}$$

$$W_s = \dfrac{450}{200 \times 3600}$$

$$W_s = \dfrac{450}{720000}$$

$$W_s = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^3/\text{N·m}$$

**Final Answer:** $W_s = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^3/\text{N·m}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Archard's equation:** Students often leave hardness in HV instead of converting to Pa or MPa. Always convert $H$ to **N/m² (Pa)** for unit consistency.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert sliding time to distance:** Many students substitute time directly into $L$ — sliding distance $L$ (in metres) = speed × time.
> 3. **Mixing up specific wear rate and wear coefficient:** Specific wear rate has units of mm³/(N·m), while wear coefficient $k$ is dimensionless.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps:** KTU examiners allocate **at least 2 marks** for the derivation in Archard's equation questions. Do not just state the final formula.
> 5. **Confusing erosion with abrasion:** Erosion is *impingement* (perpendicular impact), abrasion is *sliding contact*. This is a very common Part A trap.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1, Wear Resistance**

- ☐ **Wear** is the progressive loss of material from a surface due to mechanical contact with another body in relative motion.
- ☐ **Wear resistance** is the material's ability to resist this loss; it is the **inverse** of wear rate.
- ☐ Six major types of wear: **Adhesive, Abrasive, Erosive, Fatigue, Corrosive, Fretting.**
- ☐ **Archard's Equation:** $V = \dfrac{k \cdot W_n \cdot L}{H}$ — single most important formula in this module.
- ☐ **Specific Wear Rate:** $W_s = \dfrac{V}{W_n \cdot L}$ — measured in mm³/(N·m).
- ☐ Wear is **inversely proportional to hardness** for most metals.
- ☐ Adhesive wear produces **material transfer and galling**.
- ☐ Abrasive wear produces **scratches and scoring** (2-body) or random indentations (3-body).
- ☐ Erosive wear: ductile materials worst at **shallow angles**; brittle materials worst at **90° impact**.
- ☐ Fatigue wear: cyclic loading → sub-surface cracks → **pitting and spalling**.
- ☐ **Hardness must be in Pa (N/m²)** when used in Archard's equation.
- ☐ Sliding distance $L$ = speed × time; never confuse with time.
- ☐ Wear coefficient $k$ is **dimensionless** and typically $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-2}$.
- ☐ **Case hardening, surface coatings (TiN, Cr₂O₃, DLC), lubrication, and shot peening** are common surface-engineering methods to improve wear resistance.
- ☐ Kerala industry connections: coir processing (abrasive + erosive), marine propellers (cavitation erosion), spice grinders (3-body abrasion).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson's Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Poisson's Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** is defined as the **negative ratio of lateral (transverse) strain to longitudinal (axial) strain** in a material subjected to a uniaxial tensile or compressive load along its longitudinal axis, within the elastic limit of the material.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}}}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}$ = Strain in the direction perpendicular to the applied load
- $\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}}$ = Strain in the direction parallel to the applied load
- The **negative sign** is introduced to make $\nu$ a **positive quantity**, since lateral strain is always opposite in sign to longitudinal strain.

> [!NOTE]
> Poisson's Ratio is a **dimensionless quantity** (since it is a ratio of two strains). It is one of the most fundamental **elastic constants** in the mechanics of materials and is critical in the design of structural members, pressure vessels, and machine components.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you stretch a **rubber band** between your two hands.

- As you pull it **lengthwise** (axial direction), the rubber band becomes **longer**.
- But at the same time, it becomes **thinner** (the cross-section shrinks).

This **necking** or **contraction** in the perpendicular direction is exactly what Poisson's Ratio quantifies. The rubber tries to "conserve its volume" — what it gains in length, it loses in cross-section.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** If a material is "stretched lengthwise," its "thickness shrinks" — this dual behavior is the essence of Poisson's effect. Without the negative sign, the ratio would be negative (since the strains have opposite signs), so engineers conventionally make it positive by inserting the minus sign.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

| Material | Approximate Poisson's Ratio (ν) |
|----------|--------------------------------|
| Steel | **0.30** |
| Aluminum | **0.33** |
| Copper | **0.34** |
| Cast Iron | **0.21 – 0.26** |
| Concrete | **0.10 – 0.20** |
| Rubber | **0.45 – 0.49** |
| Cork | **≈ 0.00** |
| Glass | **0.22 – 0.25** |
| Titanium | **0.32** |
| Lead | **0.43** |

> [!NOTE]
> Theoretically, for a stable, isotropic, linear-elastic material, Poisson's ratio must lie within the bounds: **−1 < ν < 0.5**. For most engineering materials, it falls within **0 < ν < 0.5**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Poisson's Effect

When a cylindrical specimen of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is subjected to an axial tensile load $P$:

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Elongation:**
The original length $L$ increases by $\delta L$ due to tensile stress $\sigma = P/A$.

$$\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = \frac{\delta L}{L} = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

**Step 2 — Lateral Contraction:**
The original diameter $d$ decreases by $\delta d$ due to lateral strain, which is governed by Poisson's coupling.

$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = \frac{\delta d}{d} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{long}} = -\frac{\nu \sigma}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Volume Change:**
For a cylindrical bar, the volumetric strain is the sum of strains in three orthogonal directions.

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 4 — Material Classification by ν:**
- **ν ≈ 0.5** → Incompressible material (rubber, liquids)
- **0 < ν < 0.5** → Standard engineering materials
- **ν = 0** → Auxetic / Cork-like behavior (no lateral contraction)
- **ν < 0** → Auxetic materials (lateral EXPANSION on stretching — rare engineered materials)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{long}}}$ | Basic definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{long}}$ | Lateral strain from axial strain | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | E–G–ν relationship (for isotropic materials) | Same units as E |
| 4 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | E–K–ν relationship | Same units as E |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$ | G–K–ν relationship | Same units as E |
| 6 | $\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$ | Solve for ν from E and G | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\nu = \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{E}{6K}$ | Solve for ν from E and K | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$ | Volumetric strain under hydrostatic stress | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = 0$ (when $\nu = 0.5$) | Condition for volume constancy | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** The relationships $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ are derived assuming the material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**. KTU problems often give you TWO elastic constants and ask for the THIRD — these formulas are your master keys.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pressure Vessel Design:** Poisson's ratio governs the radial expansion of cylindrical shells under internal pressure.
2. **Composite Materials:** Laminated composites are designed with tailored ν values to prevent delamination.
3. **Civil Engineering:** Soil and concrete mechanics use ν to predict settlement and lateral earth pressure.
4. **Biomechanics:** Bone, cartilage, and tendon mechanics all depend heavily on Poisson's ratio.
5. **Manufacturing:** Sheet metal forming operations rely on accurate ν values to predict spring-back.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Generalized Hooke's Law in 3D

For a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material subjected to triaxial stress $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z)$, the strains in each principal direction are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

This is the **generalized form of Hooke's Law**, where Poisson's ratio explicitly couples the three orthogonal directions.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship E = 2G(1 + ν)

This is a **high-priority KTU derivation** — expect it on exams. We derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Apply Uniaxial Stress State:**
Consider a cube subjected only to $\sigma_x = \sigma$ along the x-axis. Then $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$.

Substituting into the generalized Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma - \nu(0 + 0)\right] = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[0 - \nu(\sigma + 0)\right] = -\frac{\nu \sigma}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[0 - \nu(\sigma + 0)\right] = -\frac{\nu \sigma}{E}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Pure Shear as a Superposition:**
A pure shear stress state can be created by rotating the coordinate axes of a uniaxial tension by 45°. The equivalent shear stress is:

$$\tau = \frac{\sigma}{2}$$

Under pure shear $\tau$ in the xy-plane, the principal stresses are:
- $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ (tension along 45° axis)
- $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (compression along perpendicular 45° axis)

**Step 3 — Compute Engineering Shear Strain:**
The total engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ in a pure shear state equals the difference between the principal strains:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply Generalized Hooke's Law to Principal Stresses:**
With $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (and $\sigma_z = 0$):

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau + 0)\right] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu(\tau + 0)\right] = \frac{-\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Difference:**

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left[\frac{-\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right] = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear:**
By definition, shear stress and shear strain are related through the shear modulus:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma_{xy} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

**Step 7 — Equate and Solve for G:**

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Solving:

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When deriving $E = 2G(1+\nu)$, the cleanest method is the **pure shear = 45° rotated uniaxial tension** approach. Markers will award full credit only if every algebraic step is shown.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship E = 3K(1 − 2ν)

**Step 1 — Apply Hydrostatic Pressure:**
A hydrostatic pressure $P$ produces equal stresses in all directions: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P$.

**Step 2 — Compute Volumetric Strain:**
Adding the three normal strain components:

$$\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) - 2\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}(1 - 2\nu)(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

**Step 3 — Identify Volumetric Strain:**
The left side is precisely the volumetric strain:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 4 — Define Bulk Modulus:**
By definition, hydrostatic pressure is related to volumetric strain via bulk modulus:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{-P}{K}$$

But $\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z = -3P$, so:

$$\frac{-3P}{E}(1 - 2\nu) = \frac{-P}{K}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for K:**

$$\frac{3(1 - 2\nu)}{E} = \frac{1}{K}$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A steel rod of length 2 m and diameter 30 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 90 kN. Given E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3, find:
(a) Longitudinal strain
(b) Lateral strain
(c) Change in diameter
(d) Change in volume

**Solution:**

Given: $L = 2\,\text{m}$, $d = 0.030\,\text{m}$, $P = 90{,}000\,\text{N}$, $E = 200 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$, $\nu = 0.3$

**Part (a) — Longitudinal Strain:**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$$

Axial stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90{,}000}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 127.32\,\text{MPa}$$

Longitudinal strain:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{127.32 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 6.366 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Part (b) — Lateral Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{long}} = -0.3 \times 6.366 \times 10^{-4} = -1.910 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Part (c) — Change in Diameter:**

$$\delta d = \varepsilon_{\text{lat}} \cdot d = -1.910 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.030 = -5.73 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = -5.73\,\mu\text{m}$$

**Part (d) — Change in Volume:**
For a cylinder, volumetric strain is:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_{\text{long}} + 2\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_{\text{long}}(1 - 2\nu) = 6.366 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.6) = 6.366 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = 2.546 \times 10^{-4}$$

Original volume:

$$V_0 = A \cdot L = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 1.4137 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$$

Change in volume:

$$\Delta V = 2.546 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.4137 \times 10^{-3} = 3.599 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^3 = 0.3599\,\text{cm}^3$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram: Poisson's Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Axial Tensile Load P Applied] --> nodeB[Longitudinal Stress sigma in Material]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Longitudinal Strain epsilon_long is Positive]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Lateral Response of Material}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Lateral Strain epsilon_lat is Negative]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Lateral Strain epsilon_lat is Zero for Cork]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Lateral Strain epsilon_lat is Positive for Auxetic Materials]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Compute Poisson Ratio nu equals negative epsilon_lat divided by epsilon_long]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Apply Elastic Constant Relationships]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[E equals 2 times G times 1 plus nu]
    nodeI --> nodeK[E equals 3 times K times 1 minus 2 nu]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Volumetric Strain equals sigma over E times 1 minus 2 nu]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Elastic Constant Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT ELASTIC CONSTANTS"]
        nodeIn1[Young Modulus E]
        nodeIn2[Shear Modulus G]
        nodeIn3[Bulk Modulus K]
        nodeIn4[Poisson Ratio nu]
    end

    subgraph Coupling["POISSON COUPLING EQUATIONS"]
        nodeEq1[E equals 2 G 1 plus nu]
        nodeEq2[E equals 3 K 1 minus 2 nu]
        nodeEq3[G equals 3 K 1 minus 2 nu over 2 1 plus nu]
        nodeEq4[nu equals E over 2 G minus 1]
    end

    subgraph Output["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS"]
        nodeOut1[Volume Change Prediction]
        nodeOut2[Spring Back in Sheet Metal]
        nodeOut3[Pressure Vessel Radial Expansion]
        nodeOut4[Composite Layup Design]
    end

    nodeIn1 --- nodeEq1
    nodeIn2 --- nodeEq1
    nodeIn4 --- nodeEq1
    nodeIn1 --- nodeEq2
    nodeIn3 --- nodeEq2
    nodeIn4 --- nodeEq2
    nodeIn3 --- nodeEq3
    nodeIn4 --- nodeEq3
    nodeIn1 --- nodeEq4
    nodeIn2 --- nodeEq4
    nodeEq1 --> nodeOut1
    nodeEq2 --> nodeOut1
    nodeEq3 --> nodeOut2
    nodeEq1 --> nodeOut3
    nodeEq4 --> nodeOut4
```

### 4.3 Deformation Modes Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Before["BEFORE LOADING"]
        nodeB1[Original Length L]
        nodeB2[Original Diameter d]
    end

    subgraph Loading["APPLYING TENSILE LOAD P"]
        nodeL1[Stress sigma equals P over A]
        nodeL2[Strain epsilon equals sigma over E]
    end

    subgraph After["AFTER LOADING"]
        nodeA1[New Length L plus delta L Longer]
        nodeA2[New Diameter d minus delta d Thinner]
        nodeA3[Volume change delta V computed using 1 minus 2 nu]
    end

    Before --> Loading
    Loading --> After
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Poisson's ratio. What is its significance in the design of engineering components?**

**Model Answer:**

Poisson's ratio (ν) is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain when a material is subjected to a uniaxial load within its elastic limit:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** elastic constant. Its significance lies in:

1. **Multi-axial coupling:** It governs how a material deforms in directions perpendicular to the applied load, which is critical for predicting the true 3D stress-strain state.
2. **Volume change prediction:** It determines whether a material's volume remains constant (ν = 0.5) or changes under hydrostatic loading.
3. **Design safety:** In pressure vessels, beams, and columns, ignoring Poisson's effect leads to underestimation of lateral stresses and unsafe designs.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks significance)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the relationships between the three elastic constants E, G, K and Poisson's ratio ν.**

**Model Answer:**

For a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material, the three elastic constants are inter-related through Poisson's ratio:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

Using these, the third relationship can be derived:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}}$$

These three equations allow any one elastic constant to be computed if the other two are known.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per equation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Define Poisson's ratio. Explain the physical significance of its **negative sign** and state its **theoretical range** with justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between Young's modulus $E$, shear modulus $G$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ for an isotropic material. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** Poisson's ratio is the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain in a material under uniaxial loading.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{long}}}$$

**Physical Significance of the Negative Sign:**

When a material is stretched axially (longitudinal strain is positive), the lateral dimensions contract (lateral strain is negative). Therefore, the ratio $\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{long}}}$ is intrinsically negative. The conventional negative sign is introduced so that **ν becomes a positive number** for the vast majority of engineering materials, making it convenient for use in formulas.

**Theoretical Range:**

The valid range is $-1 < \nu < 0.5$, justified as follows:

- **Lower bound (ν → −1):** If $\nu < -1$, the bulk modulus $K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ becomes negative, implying an unstable material.
- **Upper bound (ν → 0.5):** If $\nu > 0.5$, the shear modulus $G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$ becomes negative, again implying instability.

For most engineering materials, $0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition + 2 for negative sign significance + 3 for theoretical range with justification)*

##### Model Solution for (b):

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.2** above. The key steps are:

1. Apply uniaxial stress $\sigma$ along x-axis → compute $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$ from generalized Hooke's Law.
2. Express pure shear as 45° rotated uniaxial tension.
3. Compute engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$.
4. Apply shear Hooke's law $\tau = G\gamma_{xy}$.
5. Equate and solve: $\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$ or equivalently $\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$.

*(7 marks: full credit requires every algebraic transition to be written)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** A steel bar of length 1.5 m and cross-sectional area $2500\,\text{mm}^2$ is subjected to an axial tensile load of 100 kN. Given $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$, determine:
- (i) Longitudinal strain
- (ii) Lateral strain
- (iii) Change in length
- (iv) Change in volume **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a homogeneous isotropic material, prove that $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$. Discuss the special case when $\nu = 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Given:** $L = 1.5\,\text{m}$, $A = 2500 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$, $P = 100\,\text{kN} = 10^5\,\text{N}$, $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Axial Stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{10^5}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 40 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa} = 40\,\text{MPa}$$

**(i) Longitudinal Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{long}} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(ii) Lateral Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{long}} = -0.3 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = -6 \times 10^{-5}$$

**(iii) Change in Length:**

$$\delta L = \varepsilon_{\text{long}} \cdot L = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 3 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.3\,\text{mm}$$

**(iv) Change in Volume:**

For a uniform bar, volumetric strain:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_{\text{long}} + 2\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = \varepsilon_{\text{long}}(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.6) = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 8 \times 10^{-5}$$

Original volume:

$$V_0 = A \cdot L = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 = 3.75 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$$

Change in volume:

$$\Delta V = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.75 \times 10^{-3} = 3 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^3 = 0.3\,\text{cm}^3$$

*(7 marks: ~1.75 marks per sub-part)*

##### Model Solution for (b):

Refer to **Derivation 3 in Section 3.3** above. The proof proceeds as follows:

1. Apply hydrostatic pressure $P$ → equal stresses $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P$ in all directions.
2. Sum the three normal strains from generalized Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) = \frac{-3P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

3. The left side equals volumetric strain $\frac{\Delta V}{V}$.
4. By definition of bulk modulus, $\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{-P}{K}$.
5. Equating and solving:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Special Case ν = 0.5:**

If $\nu = 0.5$, then:

$$E = 3K(1 - 2 \times 0.5) = 3K(0) = 0$$

This would imply $K \to \infty$, which physically means the material is **incompressible** — its volume does not change under hydrostatic pressure. Such a material is called an **ideal incompressible material** (e.g., rubber under small strains, fluids).

*(7 marks: 5 for derivation + 2 for special case discussion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks on Poisson's Ratio Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the definition:** Writing $\nu = \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{long}}}$ (without the minus sign) costs the definition mark entirely.
> 2. **Confusing lateral strain direction:** Lateral strain under tension is **negative** (contraction). Do not write it as positive.
> 3. **Volumetric strain sign errors:** Under tensile loading, volume INCREASES (for ν < 0.5). Writing it as decreasing loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing units in worked problems:** Always convert mm² to m², kN to N, GPa to Pa before substitution. Mixed units = full credit loss.
> 5. **Deriving E = 2G(1+ν) without showing the pure shear = 45° rotation step:** This is the heart of the derivation. Skipping it = −3 marks.
> 6. **Stating that ν is always between 0 and 0.5:** The correct theoretical bound is −1 < ν < 0.5, though engineering materials lie in 0 ≤ ν ≤ 0.5.
> 7. **Using $\nu > 0.5$ in problems:** This is physically impossible for stable materials.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{long}}}$ — a **dimensionless**, **positive** (for most materials) elastic constant.
- **Sign Convention:** Axial tension → positive longitudinal strain → negative lateral strain → ν is positive due to the minus sign.
- **Theoretical Bounds:** $-1 < \nu < 0.5$; practical engineering range: $0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$.
- **Master Formula 1:** $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ — derived from pure-shear equivalence of 45°-rotated uniaxial tension.
- **Master Formula 2:** $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ — derived from hydrostatic pressure and volumetric strain.
- **Volumetric Strain Formula:** $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$; zero when $\nu = 0.5$ (incompressibility).
- **Material Reference Values:** Steel ν ≈ 0.30; Rubber ν ≈ 0.49; Cork ν ≈ 0; Concrete ν ≈ 0.1–0.2.
- **Engineering Applications:** Pressure vessels, sheet metal forming, biomechanics, composite layups.
- **KTU Exam Frequency:** Poisson's ratio appears in **every KTU Material Science module test** — both Part A (definition/significance) and Part B (derivation + numerical).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Strain Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress-Strain Relation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Stress?

**Stress ($\sigma$)** is the internal resistance offered by a material per unit area when an external load is applied. It quantifies the intensity of internal forces distributed across a cross-section.

$$\sigma = \frac{\text{Applied Force (F)}}{\text{Cross-sectional Area (A)}}$$

The SI unit of stress is **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²**, with practical engineering units being **MPa (10⁶ Pa)** or **GPa (10⁹ Pa)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** A material does not "feel" force; it feels *stress*. Stress is a tensor quantity that fully describes the internal state at a point.

### Types of Stress
| Stress Type | Symbol | Triggering Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Stress** | $\sigma_t$ | Pulling the body apart |
| **Compressive Stress** | $\sigma_c$ | Pushing the body together |
| **Shear Stress** | $\tau$ | Tangential sliding of planes |
| **True Stress** | $\sigma_{true}$ | Force divided by *instantaneous* area |
| **Engineering Stress** | $\sigma_{eng}$ | Force divided by *original* area |

## 1.2 What is Strain?

**Strain ($\varepsilon$)** is the dimensionless measure of deformation representing the change in dimension relative to the original dimension.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Change in Length } (\Delta L)}{\text{Original Length } (L_0)}$$

Since it is a ratio, strain has **no units** (often expressed in $\mu\varepsilon$ = 10⁻⁶ for practical measurement).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear strain** occurs along the loading direction, while **Lateral strain** occurs perpendicular to the load. The ratio of lateral to linear strain defines the famous **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)**.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** between your fingers:
- The harder you pull (more **Force**), the more it stretches.
- If the rubber band is **thinner** (less area), the same force stretches it more — meaning *stress* increases.
- The **stretching itself** is the *strain* — a relative measure, not absolute.

Now replace the rubber band with a **steel wire of the same size**. The steel stretches *barely visibly* under the same pull. This is because **steel is stiffer** — it has a much higher **Young's Modulus (E)**. The relationship:

$$\text{Stress} = E \times \text{Strain}$$

...is **Hooke's Law**, the cornerstone of stress-strain analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hooke's Law — The Foundation of Elasticity

For a material within the **elastic limit**, stress is directly proportional to strain. The constant of proportionality is the **Modulus of Elasticity**, commonly known as **Young's Modulus (E)**.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** Robert Hooke (1660) stated this as "*Ut tensio, sic vis*" — "as the extension, so the force." This linear relationship holds only in the **proportional limit** of the stress-strain curve.

## 2.2 The Four Elastic Constants

A material under 3D loading requires **four independent elastic constants** to fully describe its elastic behavior:

| Constant | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Young's Modulus** | $E$ | Resistance to linear deformation | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ |
| **Shear Modulus** (Modulus of Rigidity) | $G$ | Resistance to shape change | $\tau = G\gamma$ |
| **Bulk Modulus** | $K$ | Resistance to volume change | $P = K \cdot \Delta V / V$ |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu$ | Ratio of lateral to axial strain | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat} / \varepsilon_{ax}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Only **two** of these four constants are *independent* for an isotropic material. The other two can be derived from the relationships shown in Section 3.

## 2.3 The Three Stress States and Their Strains

### (a) Uniaxial / Linear Stress
When a force is applied along one axis (say, X-axis):
- **Axial strain** along X: $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x / E$
- **Lateral strain** (by Poisson's effect) along Y and Z: $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \sigma_x / E$

### (b) Shear Stress
Tangential force causing angular distortion $\gamma$ (in radians):
$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

### (c) Hydrostatic / Volumetric Stress
Equal pressure applied from all directions:
$$\text{Volumetric Strain } e_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3K} \cdot 3(1-2\nu) \text{ simplification follows}$$

## 2.4 The Engineering Stress-Strain Curve (Ductile Material Walkthrough)

A typical **mild steel** stress-strain curve contains these critical regions:

1. **Proportional Limit** — Hooke's law strictly valid; curve is linear.
2. **Elastic Limit** — Material returns to original shape upon unloading.
3. **Yield Point (Upper / Lower)** — Plastic deformation begins; **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)** is defined here.
4. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** — Maximum stress the material can withstand.
5. **Necking Region** — Localized cross-section reduction.
6. **Fracture Point** — Material physically breaks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Distinguish *Proportional Limit* from *Elastic Limit*. The first is a *graphical* boundary (linearity in $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ plot); the second is a *physical* boundary (no permanent deformation). They are *close* but not identical.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Basic stress definition |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Linear strain |
| 3 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law |
| 4 | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Shear stress-strain |
| 5 | $P = K(\Delta V / V)$ | Bulk modulus |
| 6 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat} / \varepsilon_{ax}$ | Poisson's ratio |
| 7 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | E–G relationship |
| 8 | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | E–K relationship |
| 9 | $G = 3KE / [9K - E]$ | G from E and K |
| 10 | $\nu = (3K-2G) / (6K+2G)$ | $\nu$ from K and G |
| 11 | $e_v = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)(1-2\nu)/E$ | Volumetric strain in 3D |
| 12 | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon)$ | True vs engineering stress |

**Boundary Conditions for Valid Isotropic Materials:**
- $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$ (theoretical range; most metals: 0.25–0.35; rubber ≈ 0.5; cork ≈ 0)
- $E, G, K > 0$ always
- For an **incompressible material**, $\nu = 0.5$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between E, G, and $\nu$

### Conceptual Setup
Consider a square element of side $L$ subjected to a **pure shear stress $\tau$** on its faces. The shear deformation causes the square to distort into a rhombus. We can *decompose* this shear state into two *perpendicular tensile and compressive stresses* along the diagonals (a 45° rotated uniaxial system).

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1:** Apply pure shear $\tau$ on a unit square. The element deforms by a small shear strain $\gamma$ (angular distortion in radians).

**Step 2:** Express the principal stresses on the 45° diagonal planes using Mohr's circle or stress transformation:
$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{(tension along one diagonal)}$$
$$\sigma_2 = -\tau \quad \text{(compression along the other diagonal)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Hooke's law in the principal stress directions. For an isotropic material under biaxial stress, the strain along the tension diagonal is:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2\right]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the principal stresses from Step 2:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 5:** Relate the diagonal elongation to the shear strain geometrically. The diagonal of the unit square has initial length $\sqrt{2}$. After deformation, the shear strain $\gamma$ produces an elongation of the diagonal by approximately $\gamma/2$ (small angle approximation):
$$\varepsilon_{diagonal} \approx \frac{\gamma}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Equate the two expressions for diagonal strain (from Step 4 and Step 5):
$$\frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange and recall that $\tau = G\gamma$:
$$\gamma = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 8:** Since $\tau = G\gamma$, substitute $\gamma = \tau/G$:
$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 9:** Cancel $\tau$ from both sides and solve:
$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most tested relationship** in KTU Material Science exams. Memorize it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between E, K, and $\nu$

### Conceptual Setup
Apply a **hydrostatic pressure $P$** equally in all three directions. The stress state is $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P$. The volume change of the cube depends on both the bulk modulus and the lateral Poisson contraction.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1:** The total volumetric strain for a general 3D stress state in an isotropic material is:
$$e_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 2:** Write each linear strain using the generalized Hooke's law:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Add all three equations:
$$e_v = \frac{1}{E}\left[(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) - 2\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Factor out the common sum of stresses:
$$e_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

**Step 5:** Apply the hydrostatic condition $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -P$:
$$e_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(-P - P - P) = -\frac{3P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6:** Recall the definition of bulk modulus: $K = -P / e_v$, so $e_v = -P/K$.

**Step 7:** Equate the two expressions for $e_v$:
$$-\frac{P}{K} = -\frac{3P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 8:** Cancel $-P$ from both sides:
$$\frac{1}{K} = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 9:** Invert both sides:
$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between G, E, and K (Eliminating $\nu$)

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1:** Start with the two master relations:
$$E = 2G(1+\nu) \quad \text{...(i)}$$
$$E = 3K(1-2\nu) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2:** From (i), solve for $\nu$:
$$1 + \nu = \frac{E}{2G} \implies \nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into (ii):
$$E = 3K\left[1 - 2\left(\frac{E}{2G} - 1\right)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the bracket:
$$1 - 2\left(\frac{E}{2G} - 1\right) = 1 - \frac{E}{G} + 2 = 3 - \frac{E}{G}$$

**Step 5:** So the equation becomes:
$$E = 3K\left(3 - \frac{E}{G}\right) = 9K - \frac{3KE}{G}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by $G$:
$$EG = 9KG - 3KE$$

**Step 7:** Factor $E$ on the left side of the rearranged equation:
$$3KE + EG = 9KG$$
$$E(3K + G) = 9KG$$

**Step 8:** Final boxed result:
$$\boxed{E = \frac{9KG}{3K + G}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Strain Under Triaxial Stress

**Step 1:** From Section 3.2, the volumetric strain for arbitrary 3D stress is:
$$e_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)/3 = \sigma_{mean}$ (mean stress or hydrostatic component).

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$e_v = \frac{3(1-2\nu)\sigma_{mean}}{E}$$

**Step 4:** Using $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$, the equation simplifies beautifully:
$$\boxed{e_v = \frac{\sigma_{mean}}{K} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3K}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This proves why the Bulk Modulus $K$ directly relates *mean hydrostatic stress* to *volumetric strain* — a key insight for KTU 2024 derivations.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style Practice)

**Problem:** A steel rod of 20 mm diameter and 200 mm length is subjected to a tensile load of 50 kN. Given $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$, find: (i) Stress, (ii) Strain, (iii) Elongation, (iv) Lateral strain, (v) Change in diameter.

**Solution:**

(i) Cross-sectional area: $A = \pi d^2/4 = \pi(0.020)^2/4 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{50000}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 159.15 \text{ MPa}$$

(ii) Strain: $\varepsilon = \sigma / E = 159.15 \times 10^6 / (200 \times 10^9) = 7.96 \times 10^{-4}$

(iii) Elongation: $\Delta L = \varepsilon \times L_0 = 7.96 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.200 = 1.59 \times 10^{-4}$ m $= 0.159$ mm

(iv) Lateral strain: $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon = -0.3 \times 7.96 \times 10^{-4} = -2.39 \times 10^{-4}$

(v) Change in diameter: $\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \times d = -2.39 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -4.78 \times 10^{-3}$ mm $\approx -4.78 \text{ μm}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic: Stress Element Under 3D Loading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Load F] --> B[Stress Tensor on Cube Element]
    B --> C1[Normal Stress SIGMA_X]
    B --> C2[Normal Stress SIGMA_Y]
    B --> C3[Normal Stress SIGMA_Z]
    B --> C4[Shear Stress TAU XY]
    B --> C5[Shear Stress TAU YZ]
    B --> C6[Shear Stress TAU XZ]
    C1 --> D1[Linear Strain EPSILON_X]
    C2 --> D2[Linear Strain EPSILON_Y]
    C3 --> D3[Linear Strain EPSILON_Z]
    C4 --> E1[Shear Strain GAMMA XY]
    C5 --> E2[Shear Strain GAMMA YZ]
    C6 --> E3[Shear Strain GAMMA XZ]
    D1 --> F[Generalized Hookes Law Equations]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Material Elastic Constants E G K NU]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Stress-Strain Curve Regions (Ductile Material)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Origin: No Load] --> P1[Region 1: Linear Elastic - Hookes Law Valid]
    P1 --> P2[Region 2: Proportional Limit - Boundary of Linearity]
    P2 --> P3[Region 3: Elastic Limit - Last Point of Full Recovery]
    P3 --> P4[Region 4: Yield Point - Plastic Deformation Begins]
    P4 --> P5[Region 5: Strain Hardening - Material Strengthens]
    P5 --> P6[Region 6: Ultimate Tensile Strength UTS Maximum Stress]
    P6 --> P7[Region 7: Necking - Localized Area Reduction]
    P7 --> P8[Region 8: Fracture Point - Material Breaks]
    
    P0 --> Q0[Strain Axis EPSILON]
    P1 --> Q1[Stress Axis SIGMA]
    P4 --> Q2[Yield Strength SIGMA Y]
    P6 --> Q3[Ultimate Strength SIGMA UTS]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Relationships Between Elastic Constants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Master_Relations
        R1["E equals 2G times 1 plus NU"]
        R2["E equals 3K times 1 minus 2NU"]
        R3["E equals 9KG divided by 3K plus G"]
        R4["NU equals 3K minus 2G divided by 6K plus 2G"]
    end
    
    A[Young Modulus E] --- B[Shear Modulus G]
    A --- C[Bulk Modulus K]
    B --- D[Poisson Ratio NU]
    C --- D
    
    A --> R1
    D --> R1
    B --> R2
    D --> R2
    A --> R3
    B --> R3
    C --> R3
    B --> R4
    C --> R4
    
    R1 --> E[Derive Any Constant from Other Two]
    R2 --> E
    R3 --> E
    R4 --> E
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Stress-Strain Behavior Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Material Loaded] --> Q1{Deformation Type}
    Q1 -->|Reversible| Elastic[Elastic Behavior]
    Q1 -->|Permanent| Plastic[Plastic Behavior]
    
    Elastic --> Q2{Linear or Non Linear}
    Q2 -->|Linear| Hookean[Hookean - Example Steel within Limit]
    Q2 -->|Non Linear| NonHookean[Non Hookean - Example Rubber Cast Iron]
    
    Plastic --> Q3{Ductile or Brittle}
    Q3 -->|Ductile| Duct[Ductile - Large Plastic Region Mild Steel Copper]
    Q3 -->|Brittle| Brit[Brittle - Negligible Plastic Region Glass Ceramics]
    
    Hookean --> ModE[Governed by Young Modulus E]
    NonHookean --> ModT[Tangent Modulus at Point]
    Duct --> ModY[Yield Strength is Design Criterion]
    Brit --> ModU[Ultimate Strength is Design Criterion]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Stress and Strain. State Hooke's Law.** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Stress ($\sigma$):** The internal resistive force per unit area developed inside a material when subjected to an external load.

$$\sigma = \frac{\text{Force (F)}}{\text{Area (A)}} \quad \text{[Units: N/m}^2 \text{ or Pa]}$$

**Strain ($\varepsilon$):** The ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension. It is a dimensionless quantity.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Hooke's Law:** Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain.

$$\sigma \propto \varepsilon \implies \sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where **E** is the **Young's Modulus** of the material, representing its stiffness. The law is valid only in the proportional (linear) region of the stress-strain curve. *(3 marks: 1 for each definition + Hooke's law)*

---

### **Question 2: What is Poisson's Ratio? State its limiting values for engineering materials.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

**Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** It is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal (axial) strain when a material is subjected to a uniaxial load.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_x}$$

**Limiting Values:**
- **Theoretical Range:** $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$
- **For most metals:** $0.25 \le \nu \le 0.35$ (e.g., steel ≈ 0.3)
- **For incompressible materials** (e.g., rubber): $\nu \approx 0.5$
- **For cork and concrete:** $\nu \approx 0$ (zero lateral expansion)

The negative sign is included because axial elongation causes lateral contraction (and vice versa), making the strains opposite in sign. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Stress-Strain Analysis** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A(a)** Define the four elastic constants (E, G, K, $\nu$) used to characterize the elastic behavior of an isotropic material. State the SI units and typical governing equations for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Constant | Symbol | Definition | Equation | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | E | Stiffness under uniaxial tension/compression | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Pa or N/m² |
| Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) | G | Resistance to shape change under shear | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Pa |
| Bulk Modulus | K | Resistance to volume change under hydrostatic pressure | $P = K(\Delta V / V)$ | Pa |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | Ratio of lateral to axial strain (with negative sign) | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> For a perfectly isotropic material, only **two** of these four are independent. The relationships $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ allow derivation of the others. *(7 marks with proper equation and unit explanations)*

---

**A(b)** A cylindrical steel rod of length 1.5 m, diameter 25 mm, is subjected to an axial tensile load of 90 kN. Given $E = 210$ GPa and $\nu = 0.28$:
(i) Calculate the axial stress and strain developed.
(ii) Determine the change in length and the change in diameter.
(iii) Calculate the volumetric strain in the rod. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cross-sectional Area**
$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Axial Stress (i)**
$$\sigma_x = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{90{,}000}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 183.35 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Axial Strain (i)**
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} = \frac{183.35 \times 10^6}{210 \times 10^9} = 8.73 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Change in Length (ii)**
$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_x \times L_0 = 8.73 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 1.31 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.31 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Lateral Strain (ii)**
$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -0.28 \times 8.73 \times 10^{-4} = -2.444 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6: Change in Diameter (ii)**
$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \times d = -2.444 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = -6.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} \approx -6.11 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 7: Volumetric Strain (iii)**

For a uniaxial stress state: $e_v = \varepsilon_x + 2\varepsilon_{lat} = \varepsilon_x(1 - 2\nu)$
$$e_v = 8.73 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 2 \times 0.28) = 8.73 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.44 = 3.84 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Note:** Positive $e_v$ indicates a small net *increase* in volume despite elongation — Poisson's ratio is less than 0.5. *(7 marks: 2 for stress/strain, 3 for dimensional changes, 2 for volumetric strain)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Elastic Constant Derivation & Problem** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**B(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) for an isotropic material. State the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumption:** The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linear elastic** (obeys Hooke's law).

**Derivation Steps:**

1. Consider a square element of unit side under pure shear $\tau$. The element distorts into a rhombus with shear strain $\gamma$.

2. By stress transformation at 45°, the principal stresses on the diagonal are:
   $$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

3. The principal strain along the tension diagonal using generalized Hooke's law:
   $$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

4. Geometrically, the small shear strain $\gamma$ produces a diagonal elongation of approximately $\gamma/2$:
   $$\varepsilon_{diagonal} \approx \frac{\gamma}{2}$$

5. Equating the two:
   $$\frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

6. Using $\tau = G\gamma \Rightarrow \gamma = \tau/G$:
   $$\frac{\tau}{2G} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

7. Simplifying:
   $$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

This is a fundamental result for isotropic elastic solids. *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for derivation, 2 for final result)*

---

**B(b)** For a metal, the Young's Modulus is 200 GPa and the Bulk Modulus is 160 GPa. Calculate:
(i) Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)
(ii) Shear Modulus (G)
(iii) Volumetric strain when subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 200 MPa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Find Poisson's Ratio using $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$**
$$200 = 3(160)(1-2\nu)$$
$$200 = 480(1-2\nu)$$
$$1 - 2\nu = \frac{200}{480} = 0.4167$$
$$2\nu = 1 - 0.4167 = 0.5833$$
$$\nu = 0.2917 \approx 0.292$$

**Step 2: Find Shear Modulus using $E = 2G(1+\nu)$**
$$200 = 2G(1 + 0.2917)$$
$$200 = 2G(1.2917)$$
$$G = \frac{200}{2.5833} = 77.42 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3: Volumetric Strain under hydrostatic pressure**

Using $e_v = -P/K$ (note the negative sign: pressure reduces volume):
$$e_v = \frac{-200 \text{ MPa}}{160 \text{ GPa}} = \frac{-200 \times 10^6}{160 \times 10^9} = -1.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

The negative sign confirms a **decrease** in volume. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 for steps)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ but using $E = 200 \text{ Pa}$ in the same calculation. Always convert to **consistent base units** (Pa) before substitution.
> 2. **Sign Errors in Poisson's Ratio:** Forgetting the *negative sign* in $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$, leading to negative Poisson's values in numerical problems.
> 3. **Volumetric Strain Formula Confusion:** Using $e_v = -P/K$ (with negative sign) vs $e_v = \sigma_{mean}/K$ (without sign) inconsistently. Remember: $P$ is *compressive* and inherently negative in tension-positive convention.
> 4. **Forgetting the Factor of 2:** In $E = 2G(1+\nu)$, the "2" is crucial. Students often mistakenly write $E = G(1+\nu)$ and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **True vs Engineering Stress:** When asked for *true stress*, students give *engineering stress*. True stress uses instantaneous area: $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon)$.
> 6. **Uniaxial Assumption:** Forgetting that in 3D stress, $\varepsilon_x = [\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]/E$ — the lateral stresses contribute!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ — only valid within the **proportional limit** (not the entire elastic limit).
- **Four Elastic Constants:** $E, G, K, \nu$ — only **two are independent** for isotropic materials.
- **Master Relations to Memorize:**
  - $E = 2G(1+\nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$
  - $E = 9KG/(3K+G)$
  - $\nu = (3K-2G)/(6K+2G)$
- **Poisson's Ratio Limits:** $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$; **most metals** lie in 0.25–0.35.
- **Volumetric Strain (Triaxial):** $e_v = (1-2\nu)(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)/E$
- **True Stress:** $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon)$ — always greater than engineering stress after yielding.
- **Engineering vs True Strain:** True strain accounts for *instantaneous* length, and is more accurate in plastic deformation.
- **Stress-Strain Curve Landmarks (in order):** Proportional Limit → Elastic Limit → Yield Point → UTS → Necking → Fracture.
- **SI Units:** Stress = **Pa (N/m²)**; practical use = **MPa, GPa**. Strain = **dimensionless**, but often reported in **μ-strain** for precision.
- **Isotropic Material:** Properties same in all directions. **Anisotropic materials** (e.g., wood, composites) need additional constants.
- **Quick Sanity Check:** For $\nu = 0.3$, then $E = 2.6G$ and $E = 1.2K$ — useful for verifying numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True Stress and True Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** True Stress and True Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Foundation: Engineering Stress & Engineering Strain

Before understanding **True Stress and True Strain**, we must recall the conventional (engineering) definitions taught in basic mechanics of materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Stress (σ_E or σ_nominal):** The ratio of the instantaneous applied load (F) to the **original cross-sectional area (A₀)** of the specimen before any deformation occurs.
> $$\sigma_E = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Strain (ε_E or ε_nominal):** The ratio of the change in length (ΔL) to the **original gauge length (L₀)** of the specimen.
> $$\varepsilon_E = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0}$$

The "engineering" quantities are based on **initial dimensions** (A₀ and L₀) and are extremely easy to calculate. They work well in the **elastic region** but become physically meaningless as the material enters the **plastic (permanent) deformation region**, because the cross-section continuously shrinks (necking) while the load calculation still uses the original (larger) area.

## 1.2 True Stress and True Strain — Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **True Stress (σ_T):** The ratio of the instantaneous applied load (F) to the **instantaneous (actual) cross-sectional area (Aᵢ)** of the specimen at that moment of loading.
> $$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **True Strain (ε_T):** The instantaneous (natural) ratio of the change in length to the **current length (Lᵢ)** of the specimen. When integrated over the loading history, it becomes the **natural logarithm** of the ratio of current to original length.
> $$\varepsilon_T = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Chewing Gum Stretch Test**
> Imagine pulling a piece of chewing gum. As you stretch it, the gum becomes thinner and longer. If someone asked, *"How much force per unit area is the gum experiencing?"* you would instinctively divide the pull force by the **current thin area** — not the original fat area. The stress calculated using the **current** area is the *true stress*. Similarly, the tiny incremental stretch happening **right now** (a fraction of the *current* length) sums up to give the *true strain*. True stress-strain captures the **real, instantaneous internal state** of the deforming material.

- In true stress-strain analysis, every single point on the curve represents the **actual physical state** of the material at that instant.
- True stress is always **higher** than engineering stress (because the denominator Aᵢ < A₀).
- True strain is always **lower** than engineering strain (because the denominator is the larger, instantaneous length in the true strain integration, which produces a logarithmic, decelerating value).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The engineering-to-true conversion assumes **incompressibility of plastic deformation** (volume is conserved because plastic flow happens by dislocation glide without changing density appreciably).
- **Y-axis unit:** **MPa (N/mm²)** or **GPa**.
- **X-axis unit:** **dimensionless (mm/mm)** — a pure number, often expressed as a percentage.
- **Strain-rate sensitivity (m)** and **Strain-hardening exponent (n)** are dimensionless descriptors used later in Hollomon's law.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind True Stress & True Strain

The fundamental reason true stress-strain is necessary lies in the **physics of plastic deformation**:

1. **Cross-section Shrinks Continuously:** Once the material yields, dislocations glide, and the specimen elongates while its cross-sectional area **reduces**. Engineering stress (using A₀) under-reports the actual internal stress.
2. **Strain is Path-Dependent:** A small piece of material at the neck can experience enormous local strains that engineering strain (using L₀) cannot capture.
3. **Necking Instability:** After the ultimate tensile strength (UTS), necking begins. Here, the area drops **rapidly**, and the engineering curve *falsely* falls. True stress, however, **continues to rise** because the actual material is still strain-hardening.
4. **Formability & Forging Design:** Industrial metal-forming processes (rolling, forging, extrusion) need true stress-strain data to compute the forming loads accurately.

## 2.2 Underlying Assumptions

- **Plastic deformation occurs at constant volume (Incompressibility):** The material's density does not change during plastic flow.
- This assumption allows the powerful relationship: A₀L₀ = AᵢLᵢ.
- Elastic strain is neglected in true-strain calculations for plastic-flow problems (because it is negligible compared to plastic strain in most metals).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma_E = F / A_0$ | MPa | Valid up to necking |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon_E = (L_i - L_0)/L_0 = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | Valid up to necking |
| 3 | True Stress | $\sigma_T = F / A_i$ | MPa | Valid through necking |
| 4 | True (Natural) Strain | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L_i / L_0)$ | dimensionless | Valid through necking |
| 5 | Volume Constancy | $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ | mm³ | Plastic region only |
| 6 | Stress Conversion | $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E)$ | MPa | Plastic region only |
| 7 | Strain Conversion | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)$ | dimensionless | Plastic region only |
| 8 | Hollomon's Law (Power Law Hardening) | $\sigma_T = K (\varepsilon_T)^n$ | MPa | Plastic region, $\varepsilon_T \ge 0$ |
| 9 | n = Strain Hardening Exponent | $n = d(\ln \sigma_T) / d(\ln \varepsilon_T)$ | dimensionless | $0 \le n \le 1$ |
| 10 | K = Strength Coefficient | unit of stress | MPa | from log-log plot intercept |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols to Memorize (KTU Standard):**
> - **F** → Instantaneous tensile load
> - **A₀** → Original cross-sectional area
> - **Aᵢ** → Instantaneous cross-sectional area
> - **L₀** → Original gauge length
> - **Lᵢ** → Instantaneous gauge length
> - **K** → Strength coefficient
> - **n** → Strain-hardening exponent

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Metal Forming & Forging:** True stress-strain data is fed into FEM software (e.g., DEFORM, ABAQUS) to simulate forging, rolling, and extrusion. Forming loads are computed using $\sigma_T$ because the material's actual area in the die is changing continuously.
- **Sheet Metal Stamping (Automotive):** True stress-strain curves predict formability, springback, and failure limits (Forming Limit Diagrams).
- **Ballistic Impact & Crashworthiness:** Crash simulations need true stress-strain to capture high-strain-rate plastic flow.
- **Material Selection:** The strain-hardening exponent **n** tells engineers how uniformly a material will deform — high-n materials (e.g., austenitic stainless steel) spread deformation; low-n materials localize strain (neck early).
- **Fracture Mechanics:** True stress at fracture determines the energy absorbed before failure, critical for ductile-material design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Engineering Strain and True Strain

We start from the differential definition of true strain and integrate it over the deformation history. The engineering strain is the integrated change in length divided by the **fixed** original length.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\varepsilon_T &= \frac{dL}{L} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \left[ \ln L \right]_{L_0}^{L_i} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \ln(L_i) - \ln(L_0) \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The integral of dL/L gives the natural log of the upper limit divided by the lower limit, which is the exact definition of true strain.

Now substitute the engineering strain definition $\varepsilon_E = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$ which can be rearranged to $L_i = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_E)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_T &= \ln\!\left(\frac{L_0(1 + \varepsilon_E)}{L_0}\right) \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The L₀ cancels, leaving a clean logarithmic relationship between true strain and engineering strain. This means for every engineering strain value, we can directly compute the true strain by taking the natural log of (1 + engineering strain).

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship between Engineering Stress and True Stress

This derivation uses the **principle of volume constancy** during plastic deformation. We assume the material is plastically incompressible, meaning its density does not change.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_0 &= V_i \quad \text{(Volume is constant during plastic flow)} \\[4pt]
A_0 L_0 &= A_i L_i \\[4pt]
\frac{A_0}{A_i} &= \frac{L_i}{L_0} \\[4pt]
A_i &= \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Because the volume does not change, the product of area and length at any instant equals the product of the original area and original length. Solving for the instantaneous area Aᵢ in terms of A₀, L₀, and Lᵢ gives the relationship needed to convert stresses.

Now substitute this Aᵢ into the true stress equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_T &= \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0 L_0 / L_i} = \frac{F L_i}{A_0 L_0} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0} \\[4pt]
\sigma_T &= \sigma_E \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_E) \quad \text{since } \frac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon_E
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** True stress is engineering stress multiplied by the stretch ratio Lᵢ/L₀, which is exactly (1 + ε_E). This relationship is central to all KTU problems.

## 3.3 Derivation: Hollomon's Equation (Power Law for Plastic Region)

Hollomon observed that for many metals in the plastic region (between yielding and necking), a log-log plot of true stress vs. true strain is a straight line. This is the **power-law strain-hardening relationship**.

Starting from the empirical observation that $\ln \sigma_T$ vs. $\ln \varepsilon_T$ is linear, with slope **n** and intercept **ln K**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln \sigma_T &= \ln K + n \ln \varepsilon_T \\[4pt]
\sigma_T &= e^{\ln K + n \ln \varepsilon_T} = K \cdot e^{n \ln \varepsilon_T} = K \cdot (e^{\ln \varepsilon_T})^n \\[4pt]
\sigma_T &= K (\varepsilon_T)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Exponentiating both sides of the log-log linear equation yields a power-law form. The slope n is the **strain-hardening exponent**, and K is the **strength coefficient** (extrapolated true stress at ε_T = 1).

**Boundary conditions / physical meaning:**

- $n = 0$ → Perfectly plastic material (no hardening, like an ideal rigid-plastic solid).
- $n = 1$ → Perfectly elastic material (Hookean behavior, $\sigma_T = K \varepsilon_T$).
- $0 < n < 1$ → Real metals (e.g., mild steel n ≈ 0.15–0.20, austenitic SS n ≈ 0.4–0.5).
- **Uniform elongation (ε_u)** is related to n by: $\varepsilon_u = n$ (in true strain units) or $\%\text{EL} = n \times 100$ approximately.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A cylindrical steel specimen has an original diameter of 12 mm and a gauge length of 50 mm. It is loaded to a point where the load is 50 kN, the diameter has reduced to 11.5 mm, and the gauge length has increased to 52.5 mm. Calculate the engineering stress, engineering strain, true stress, and true strain.

**Step 1: Compute original and instantaneous areas**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (12)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 144 = 113.10 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
A_i &= \frac{\pi}{4} (11.5)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 132.25 = 103.87 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Engineering Stress and Strain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_E &= \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{50\,000 \text{ N}}{113.10 \text{ mm}^2} = 442.1 \text{ MPa} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_E &= \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{52.5 - 50}{50} = 0.050 \quad (5\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: True Stress and Strain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_T &= \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{50\,000}{103.87} = 481.4 \text{ MPa} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{52.5}{50}\right) = \ln(1.05) = 0.0488
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification using conversion formulas**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_T &= \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E) = 442.1 \times 1.05 = 464.2 \text{ MPa (approx)} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E) = \ln(1.05) = 0.0488 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The small difference in σ_T (481.4 vs 464.2) is because volume constancy is an approximation; in reality, there is a tiny elastic volume change.

## 3.5 Summary of Derived Relations

| From → To | Exact Formula | Approximation for small strains |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------------------|
| ε_E → ε_T | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)$ | $\varepsilon_T \approx \varepsilon_E$ (for ε_E < 0.1) |
| ε_T → ε_E | $\varepsilon_E = e^{\varepsilon_T} - 1$ | $\varepsilon_E \approx \varepsilon_T$ |
| σ_E → σ_T | $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E)$ | $\sigma_T \approx \sigma_E$ |
| σ_T → σ_E | $\sigma_E = \sigma_T \cdot e^{-\varepsilon_T}$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Engineering vs. True Stress-Strain Curve Comparison

> The diagram below is rendered using Mermaid. It shows the stress-strain curve regions, the divergence between engineering and true curves, and the key transition points. Due to the unavailability of native plot rendering inside Mermaid, the schematic is presented as a labelled **block-architecture flow** that students can re-draw on graph paper.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ORIGINAL UNDEFORMED STATE: Area A0, Length L0"] --> B["ELASTIC REGION: Hookes Law, Both curves overlap"]
    B --> C["YIELD POINT: Material begins plastic flow"]
    C --> D["PLASTIC REGION ENGINEERING: Area A0 used, curve flattens then falls"]
    C --> E["PLASTIC REGION TRUE: Area Ai used, curve continues to rise"]
    D --> F["UTS PEAK: Engineering reaches maximum"]
    E --> G["NECKING ZONE: Ai shrinks rapidly, true stress rises steeply"]
    F --> H["FRACTURE ENGINEERING: Apparent stress drops to zero"]
    G --> I["FRACTURE TRUE: True stress at fracture is the highest value"]
    H --> J["CONVERSION BRIDGE: sigma_T = sigma_E times 1 plus epsilon_E"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["HOLLOMON POWER LAW: sigma_T = K times epsilon_T raised to n"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stress-Strain Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT DATA FROM TENSION TEST"]
        A1["Applied Load F"]
        A2["Original Area A0"]
        A3["Instantaneous Area Ai"]
        A4["Original Length L0"]
        A5["Instantaneous Length Li"]
    end

    subgraph ENGINEERING["ENGINEERING QUANTITIES"]
        B1["Engineering Stress sigma_E = F divided by A0"]
        B2["Engineering Strain epsilon_E = Li minus L0 divided by L0"]
    end

    subgraph TRUE["TRUE QUANTITIES"]
        C1["True Stress sigma_T = F divided by Ai"]
        C2["True Strain epsilon_T = ln of Li divided by L0"]
    end

    subgraph LAWS["EMPIRICAL LAWS"]
        D1["Hollomon Equation sigma_T = K times epsilon_T raised to n"]
        D2["Volume Constancy A0 times L0 equals Ai times Li"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    A3 --> A5
    A4 --> A5
    A4 --> B2
    A5 --> B2
    A5 --> C2
    A4 --> C2
    B1 --> D1
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    A2 --> D2
    A3 --> D2
    A4 --> D2
    A5 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Conversion Logic Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CollectTestData
    CollectTestData --> ComputeEngQuantities: F, A0, L0, Li
    ComputeEngQuantities --> ApplyVolumeConstancy: Plastic Region
    ApplyVolumeConstancy --> ComputeTrueQuantities: Ai derived
    ComputeTrueQuantities --> FitHollomonLaw: Log Log Plot
    FitHollomonLaw --> ExtractKandN: Slope and Intercept
    ExtractKandN --> [*]: Ready for Forming Simulation
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to sketch on paper (Step-by-Step):**
> 1. Draw X-axis = Strain (mm/mm), Y-axis = Stress (MPa).
> 2. Plot the engineering curve: linear elastic rise, yield, plastic rise to UTS, then a *falling tail* down to fracture.
> 3. Plot the true curve on the same axes: matches engineering up to yield, then continues to **monotonically rise** through UTS, necking, and up to the **true fracture stress** (which is **higher** than the engineering UTS).
> 4. Mark the points: Yield, UTS, Necking onset, Fracture.
> 5. Mark the divergence region between the two curves.
