<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define true stress and true strain. How are they different from engineering stress and engineering strain?**

**Model Answer:**
- **True stress (σ_T):** Instantaneous load divided by the instantaneous cross-sectional area. $\sigma_T = F / A_i$.
- **True strain (ε_T):** Integral of dL/L, giving $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L_i / L_0)$.
- **Engineering stress (σ_E):** Load divided by the **original** area A₀. $\sigma_E = F / A_0$.
- **Engineering strain (ε_E):** Change in length divided by the **original** length L₀. $\varepsilon_E = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$.
- **Key difference:** True quantities use **instantaneous** dimensions and represent the actual internal state of the deforming material, while engineering quantities use **original** dimensions and are valid only up to necking.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State Hollomon's equation and explain the physical significance of the strain-hardening exponent (n).**

**Model Answer:**
- **Hollomon's Equation:** $\sigma_T = K (\varepsilon_T)^n$
- **K** is the **strength coefficient** (true stress at ε_T = 1, with units of MPa).
- **n** is the **strain-hardening exponent** (dimensionless, between 0 and 1).
- **Physical significance of n:** It quantifies a material's ability to distribute plastic deformation uniformly. A higher n means the material hardens more rapidly with strain, leading to better formability and more uniform elongation before necking. For n = 0, the material is perfectly plastic; for n = 1, it is perfectly elastic. Typical values: mild steel n ≈ 0.15–0.20; austenitic stainless steel n ≈ 0.40–0.50.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Most common mistake:** Using the **wrong area** in stress calculation. Students often plug A₀ into the true-stress formula — this will lose 4–5 marks immediately.
> - **Second pitfall:** Forgetting to convert the diameter at the **current** instant. The diameter keeps changing during loading; use the value at the *specific instant* the question asks about.
> - **Third pitfall:** Mixing up ε_T and ε_E formulas. Remember: ε_T is a **logarithm**, ε_E is a **linear ratio**.
> - **Fourth pitfall:** When deriving Hollomon's equation, students write $\sigma_T = K \varepsilon_T + n$ instead of $\sigma_T = K (\varepsilon_T)^n$. The exponent n is on the strain term, NOT added linearly.
> - **Fifth pitfall:** Not stating the **volume-constancy assumption** explicitly. Examiners award 1–2 marks just for stating "assuming plastic deformation is volume-constant."

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between true stress and engineering stress. State clearly the underlying assumption. (7 marks)

**(b)** A cylindrical brass specimen of original diameter 15 mm and gauge length 60 mm is pulled in tension. At a particular instant, the load is 60 kN, the diameter has reduced to 14.7 mm, and the gauge length is 61.5 mm. Calculate the engineering stress, engineering strain, true stress, and true strain at this instant. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Assumption:** Plastic deformation occurs at **constant volume** (incompressibility of plastic flow).

**Step 1:** State volume constancy: $V_0 = V_i$, hence $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$.

**Step 2:** Solve for Aᵢ: $A_i = A_0 L_0 / L_i$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into true stress definition:
$\sigma_T = F / A_i = F L_i / (A_0 L_0)$.

**Step 4:** Recognize that $L_i / L_0 = 1 + \varepsilon_E$ (by definition of engineering strain).

**Step 5:** Also $F / A_0 = \sigma_E$ (by definition of engineering stress).

**Step 6:** Therefore, $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E)$.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Areas**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(15)^2 = 176.71 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
A_i &= \frac{\pi}{4}(14.7)^2 = 169.72 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Engineering stress and strain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_E &= \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{60\,000}{176.71} = 339.5 \text{ MPa} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_E &= \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{61.5 - 60}{60} = 0.025
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: True stress and strain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_T &= \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{60\,000}{169.72} = 353.5 \text{ MPa} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{61.5}{60}\right) = \ln(1.025) = 0.02470
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification using conversion formulas**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_T &= \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E) = 339.5 \times 1.025 = 348.0 \text{ MPa} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_T &= \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E) = \ln(1.025) = 0.02470 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define true strain. Starting from the differential definition, derive the expression $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L_i / L_0)$. (7 marks)

**(b)** The true stress-true strain data for a certain alloy in the plastic region is given by: $\sigma_T = 700 (\varepsilon_T)^{0.25}$ MPa. Find the true stress and true strain when the engineering strain is 0.20. Also, determine the load on a specimen of original cross-sectional area 200 mm² at this point. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Definition:** True strain is the natural logarithm of the ratio of instantaneous length to original length, or equivalently, the integral of incremental strain dL/L from the original to the current length.

**Step 1:** Start with the differential definition: $d\varepsilon_T = dL / L$.

**Step 2:** Integrate from the original length L₀ to the current length Lᵢ:
$\varepsilon_T = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} (dL / L)$.

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral: $\varepsilon_T = [\ln L]_{L_0}^{L_i} = \ln(L_i) - \ln(L_0)$.

**Step 4:** Apply logarithm rules: $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L_i / L_0)$.

**Step 5:** (Optional) Convert to engineering strain: Since $\varepsilon_E = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$, we have $L_i = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_E)$, so $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)$.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Convert engineering strain to true strain**

$$
\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E) = \ln(1 + 0.20) = \ln(1.20) = 0.1823
$$

**Step 2: Calculate true stress using Hollomon's equation**

$$
\sigma_T = 700 \times (0.1823)^{0.25} = 700 \times 0.6535 = 457.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the engineering stress using volume constancy**

Since $A_i = A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon_E) = 200 / 1.20 = 166.67 \text{ mm}^2$:

$$
\sigma_T = F / A_i \implies F = \sigma_T \times A_i = 457.5 \times 166.67 = 76\,250 \text{ N} \approx 76.25 \text{ kN}
$$

**Alternative check using F = σ_E × A₀:**

$$
\sigma_E = \sigma_T \times A_i / A_0 = 457.5 \times (166.67/200) = 381.2 \text{ MPa}
$$
$$
F = \sigma_E \times A_0 = 381.2 \times 200 = 76\,240 \text{ N} \approx 76.24 \text{ kN} \checkmark
$$

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **True stress uses instantaneous area Aᵢ**, not A₀.
- [x] **True strain is logarithmic:** $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L_i / L_0)$.
- [x] **Engineering quantities use ORIGINAL dimensions** A₀ and L₀.
- [x] **Volume constancy:** $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ — valid only in the **plastic** region.
- [x] **Conversion formulas (must memorize):**
  - $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E)$
  - $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)$
- [x] **Hollomon's Law:** $\sigma_T = K (\varepsilon_T)^n$ — power-law form, NOT linear.
- [x] **K** = strength coefficient (MPa); **n** = strain-hardening exponent (dimensionless, 0 to 1).
- [x] **High n → better formability and uniform elongation**; **low n → early necking**.
- [x] **True stress-strain curve monotonically rises** through UTS and necking; engineering curve falsely *falls* after UTS.
- [x] **For small strains (ε < 0.1):** ε_T ≈ ε_E and σ_T ≈ σ_E — engineering values are acceptable approximations.
- [x] **For forming simulations (forging, rolling, extrusion):** ALWAYS use true stress-strain data.
- [x] **In exam problems, always state the volume-constancy assumption explicitly** before using the conversion formulas.
- [x] **Common units to use:** Stress in **MPa** (or N/mm²), Strain in **mm/mm** (dimensionless, sometimes as %).
- [x] **n = 0** → perfectly plastic; **n = 1** → perfectly elastic (Hookean).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Solutions - Types of Solid Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solid Solutions - Types of Solid Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solid Solution** is a crystalline phase in which two or more elements are present in a single homogeneous phase, where the atoms of the solute element(s) are incorporated directly into the crystal lattice of the solvent element without forming a new distinct chemical compound. The crystal structure of the solvent is retained, but its lattice parameters may be modified depending on the size, valency, and concentration of the dissolved solute atoms.

In the context of engineering metallurgy and KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the constituents of a solid solution are classified as:

- **Solvent (Matrix Phase):** The element or compound whose crystal structure is preserved and which is present in the larger amount. It forms the host lattice.
- **Solute (Solute Atoms):** The element(s) present in smaller concentration whose atoms are dissolved into the host lattice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT412 Module 2):** Students must be able to distinguish between *interstitial*, *substitutional*, and *interstitial-substitutional* solid solutions, and must recall **Hume-Rothery's Eight Rules** governing the extent of solid solubility in metals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing a glass of **lemonade**:

- The **water** acts as the *solvent* (host).
- The **sugar and salt particles** dissolve into it as *solute*, but the water remains visibly as water — no new chemical compound is formed.

In a metallic solid solution, the situation is similar but occurs in the **solid state**:

- Think of **brass** — an alloy of copper (Cu, the solvent) and zinc (Zn, the solute). Atoms of zinc fit into the copper crystal lattice, replacing some copper atoms. The result is a single-phase, homogeneous alloy with the same FCC crystal structure as pure copper.
- Similarly, **steel** is essentially iron (Fe) as the solvent with **carbon (C)** atoms squeezed into the interstitial spaces between the iron atoms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A solid solution is a solid "mixture" at the atomic level where solute atoms are dissolved inside the host crystal — analogous to sugar dissolved in water, except the "water" is now a rigid metallic lattice.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Crystal Structure** | The arrangement of atoms in the host lattice (BCC, FCC, HCP). |
| **Lattice Parameter (a)** | The edge length of the unit cell, typically measured in **Å (Angstroms)** or **nm**. |
| **Solubility Limit** | The maximum concentration of solute that can dissolve in the solvent at a given temperature. |
| **Phase** | A chemically and structurally homogeneous portion of a system. |

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

Solid solutions are the foundation of virtually all commercial metallic alloys. They allow engineers to **tailor mechanical, electrical, magnetic, and corrosion properties** by simply adjusting composition. For example, adding nickel to copper increases strength and corrosion resistance; adding zinc to copper creates brass with superior machinability.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Application:** The wings of modern aircraft (e.g., Boeing 787 and Airbus A350) are made from **aluminium-copper solid-solution strengthened alloys** (2xxx series), where Cu atoms dissolved in Al dramatically improve yield strength.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Conditions for Solid Solubility — Hume-Rothery's Rules

Before classifying solid solutions, we must understand the conditions under which extensive solid solubility can occur. The British metallurgist **William Hume-Rothery** proposed a set of empirical rules (originally four, later extended to eight) that predict whether two elements will form a wide-ranging solid solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hume-Rothery's Eight Rules (Comprehensive List for KTU Examinations)**

### Rule 1: Atomic Size Factor
If the atomic radii of the two elements differ by **less than 15%**, extensive solid solubility is favoured. Beyond 15%, lattice strain becomes excessive and solubility is limited.

$$
\delta = \frac{\vert r_{\text{solute}} - r_{\text{solvent}} \vert}{r_{\text{solvent}}} \times 100\% \quad \text{(Should be } \delta \leq 15\% \text{)}
$$

### Rule 2: Same Crystal Structure
The solute and solvent should have **identical crystal structures** in their pure states (e.g., both FCC, both BCC). Mismatched structures lead to limited solubility.

### Rule 3: Valency Effect
A metal of **lower valency** is more soluble in a metal of **higher valency** than vice versa. For example, Zn (valency 2) is highly soluble in Cu (valency 1), but Cu has very limited solubility in Zn.

### Rule 4: Chemical Affinity (Electronegativity)
Elements that are **chemically similar** (close electronegativity values) form extensive solid solutions. Large electronegativity differences lead to compound formation (e.g., intermetallics) rather than solid solutions.

### Rule 5: Relative Valency Factor
The solute and solvent should not differ in valency by more than **±1** for good solubility (in some texts this is grouped with Rule 3).

### Rule 6: Lattice Type Compatibility
Both metals should belong to the same **lattice type family** (e.g., transition metals with transition metals, or noble metals with noble metals).

### Rule 7: Electron Concentration
In phases such as β-brass and ε-phases, solubility is governed by the **electron-to-atom ratio (e/a)**, known as the **Hume-Rothery electron compound rule**.

### Rule 8: Position in the Periodic Table
Elements close to each other in the **periodic table** (same group or adjacent groups) generally show higher mutual solubility.

## 2.2 Types of Solid Solutions

Solid solutions are classified based on the **location** of the solute atoms within the host lattice.

### Type 1: Substitutional Solid Solution
Solute atoms **directly replace** solvent atoms on the lattice sites. The crystal structure is preserved, but lattice parameters shift slightly (Vegard's Law applies for ideal cases).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** Cu-Zn (Brass), Cu-Ni (Monel), Fe-Cr, Fe-Ni (Invar)

### Type 2: Interstitial Solid Solution
Small solute atoms (typically H, C, N, B with atomic radius < 1 Å) fit into the **interstitial voids** (tetrahedral or octahedral holes) of the host lattice. Distortion of the host lattice is significant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** Fe-C (Steel), Fe-N, Ti-H, Pd-H

### Type 3: Interstitial-Substitutional (Ordered / Compound-Type)
This is a hybrid where one type of solute atom occupies substitutional sites while another occupies interstitial sites. Common in **dual-phase steels** and complex alloys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** High-speed steels (W + C in Fe), austenitic stainless steels with both Ni (substitutional) and N (interstitial)

### Type 4: Vacancy Solid Solution
This occurs in compounds (rather than pure elements) where some lattice sites are **vacant**. Example: wüstite (FeO), where the actual composition is Fe₁₋ₓO, with iron vacancies.

### Type 5: Ordered Solid Solution
At low temperatures, solute atoms may occupy **specific, regular sites** in the lattice, creating long-range order (superlattice). Example: CuAu, Cu₃Au.

### Type 6: Disordered Solid Solution (Random)
A high-entropy or random arrangement of solute and solvent atoms on identical lattice sites — typical solid solution behaviour.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Description | Application / Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta = \dfrac{\vert r_{\text{solute}} - r_{\text{solvent}} \vert}{r_{\text{solvent}}} \times 100\%$ | Atomic size factor (Hume-Rothery Rule 1) | Predict solid solubility extent |
| $a_{\text{ss}} = a_{\text{A}}(1-x) + a_{\text{B}} \cdot x$ | **Vegard's Law** (linear interpolation of lattice parameter for ideal substitutional solid solution) | Estimate lattice parameter of an alloy of composition $x$ |
| $r_{\text{octa}} = 0.414 \cdot r_{\text{host}}$ | Radius of an octahedral interstitial site in FCC | Determine maximum size of interstitial solute |
| $r_{\text{tetra}} = 0.225 \cdot r_{\text{host}}$ | Radius of a tetrahedral interstitial site in FCC | Determine maximum size of interstitial solute |
| $r_{\text{octa, BCC}} = 0.155 \cdot r_{\text{host}}$ | Radius of an octahedral interstitial site in BCC | Determine maximum size of interstitial solute |
| $r_{\text{tetra, BCC}} = 0.291 \cdot r_{\text{host}}$ | Radius of a tetrahedral interstitial site in BCC | Determine maximum size of interstitial solute |
| $(e/a)_{\text{critical}} = 1.36$ | Critical electron-to-atom ratio for α-phase FCC | Hume-Rothery electron compound rule |
| $(e/a)_{\text{critical}} = 1.48$ | Critical electron-to-atom ratio for β-phase BCC | Hume-Rothery electron compound rule |
| $(e/a)_{\text{critical}} = 1.58$ | Critical electron-to-atom ratio for ε-phase HCP | Hume-Rothery electron compound rule |
| $\Delta a / a \approx \delta \cdot C$ | Approximate lattice distortion with solute concentration $C$ | Estimate strain due to alloying |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Hint:** For FCC, octahedral void radius is **41.4%** of atomic radius; for tetrahedral, it is **22.5%**. For BCC, octahedral voids are surprisingly small at **15.5%** — this is why carbon solubility in BCC α-iron (0.022 wt%) is much lower than in FCC γ-iron (2.14 wt%).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Engineering Field | Solid Solution Used | Property Enhanced |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace | Al-Cu (2xxx), Al-Zn (7xxx) | Strength-to-weight ratio |
| Marine Engineering | Cu-Ni (Monel 400) | Corrosion resistance in seawater |
| Electrical | Cu-Be alloys | Conductivity + strength |
| Cutting Tools | WC-Co (cemented carbides) | Hardness + toughness |
| Cryogenics | Fe-Ni (Invar, 36% Ni) | Near-zero thermal expansion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Vegard's Law (Linear Variation of Lattice Parameter)

Vegard's Law is a cornerstone empirical relationship for **ideal substitutional solid solutions**. It states that the lattice parameter of a substitutional solid solution is a linear weighted average of the lattice parameters of the pure components.

### Statement of the Law

For a binary solid solution of metals A and B with solute atomic fraction $x_B$ of B:

$$
a_{\text{alloy}} = (1 - x_B) \cdot a_A + x_B \cdot a_B
$$

where $a_A$ and $a_B$ are the lattice parameters of the pure metals A and B, and $x_B$ is the atomic fraction of B.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1: Consider an ideal substitutional solid solution.** In such a solution, atoms of B occupy random lattice sites originally occupied by atoms of A. We assume that the volume change is the sum of the volume changes contributed by each atom type.

The total volume of the unit cell can be expressed as:

$$
V_{\text{alloy}} = N_A \cdot \Omega_A + N_B \cdot \Omega_B
$$

where $N_A$ and $N_B$ are the number of atoms of A and B in the unit cell, and $\Omega_A$, $\Omega_B$ are their respective atomic volumes.

**Step 2: Express in terms of mole (or atomic) fractions.** Since $x_A + x_B = 1$, and the total number of atoms per unit cell is fixed by the crystal structure (e.g., 4 for FCC, 2 for BCC), the atomic fraction of B is:

$$
x_B = \frac{N_B}{N_A + N_B}, \quad x_A = \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_B}
$$

**Step 3: For a cubic system**, $V = a^3$, so the lattice parameter is:

$$
a_{\text{alloy}}^3 = x_A \cdot a_A^3 + x_B \cdot a_B^3
$$

**Step 4: Apply the assumption of small differences.** For the ideal linear Vegard's law, we assume a small size difference (consistent with Hume-Rothery's Rule 1) and take the cube root as approximately linear. For small perturbations, the cube root can be linearized using a first-order Taylor expansion:

$$
a_{\text{alloy}} \approx x_A \cdot a_A + x_B \cdot a_B
$$

**Step 5: Substitute $x_A = 1 - x_B$:**

$$
a_{\text{alloy}} = (1 - x_B) a_A + x_B \cdot a_B = a_A + x_B(a_B - a_A)
$$

**Final Result (Vegard's Law):**

$$
\boxed{\,a_{\text{alloy}} = a_A + x_B (a_B - a_A)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Caveat:** Vegard's Law is *strictly* valid only for **ideal** solid solutions where atomic sizes are similar and chemical effects are negligible. Real alloys like Cu-Au show **positive deviations**, while Si-Ge and InSb show reasonably linear behaviour.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Atomic Size Factor (Hume-Rothery Rule 1)

### Statement
The size factor $\delta$ measures the relative atomic size mismatch between solute and solvent.

**Step 1: Define the atomic size factor.**

$$
\delta = \frac{\vert r_{\text{solute}} - r_{\text{solvent}} \vert}{r_{\text{solvent}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2: Apply the condition.** For extensive solid solubility:

$$
\delta \leq 15\%
$$

**Worked Example:** Consider Cu (FCC) as the solvent with $r_{\text{Cu}} = 0.128$ nm and Zn (HCP) as the solute with $r_{\text{Zn}} = 0.134$ nm.

Substituting:

$$
\delta = \frac{\vert 0.134 - 0.128 \vert}{0.128} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{0.006}{0.128} \times 100\% = 4.69\%
$$

Since $4.69\% \ll 15\%$, Zn is **highly soluble** in Cu — and indeed, brass is a complete solid solution up to ~39 wt% Zn.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Radius of an Octahedral Void in an FCC Lattice

This derivation shows why carbon, despite its small size, causes significant lattice distortion in γ-iron (FCC).

**Step 1: Geometry of an FCC unit cell.** In an FCC structure, the octahedral void is located at the body centre of the cube and at the edge centres. Consider the void at the cube centre (½, ½, ½). The nearest face-centred atoms are at distance $a/2$ along the cube axes.

**Step 2: Compute the void-to-atom distance.** The distance from the body-centre void to a corner atom (which does not exist in FCC) — instead, the void is equidistant from the face-centre atoms at $(½, ½, 0)$, $(½, 0, ½)$, etc.

The distance from $(½, ½, ½)$ to $(½, ½, 0)$ is:

$$
d = \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2} = \frac{a}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Relate atomic radius to lattice parameter.** In FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal:

$$
4r_{\text{host}} = a\sqrt{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{\text{host}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and solve.**

$$
d = \frac{a}{2} = \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot r_{\text{host}}}{2} = \sqrt{2} \cdot r_{\text{host}}
$$

The void radius $r_{\text{void}}$ plus the host radius $r_{\text{host}}$ must equal the void-to-atom distance:

$$
r_{\text{void}} + r_{\text{host}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot r_{\text{host}}
$$

$$
r_{\text{void}} = (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot r_{\text{host}}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\sqrt{2} - 1 \approx 1.4142 - 1 = 0.4142
$$

$$
\boxed{\,r_{\text{octa, FCC}} \approx 0.414 \, r_{\text{host}}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interstitial Site Radii (Quick Reference)

| Lattice | Octahedral Void Radius | Tetrahedral Void Radius | Number of Voids per Atom |
|---|---|---|---|
| FCC | $0.414 \, r$ | $0.225 \, r$ | Octa: 1, Tetra: 2 |
| BCC | $0.155 \, r$ (or $0.067a$ compression) | $0.291 \, r$ | Octa: 3, Tetra: 6 |
| HCP | $0.414 \, r$ | $0.225 \, r$ | Octa: 1, Tetra: 2 |

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Insight:** The atomic radius of carbon is **0.077 nm**, and the radius of γ-iron (FCC) is **0.129 nm**. Thus, the maximum radius of the octahedral void is $0.414 \times 0.129 = 0.0534$ nm. Since $r_C = 0.077$ nm > $0.0534$ nm, carbon **cannot fit without distortion**, which is why even in austenite the lattice is significantly strained.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary: Choosing Between Substitutional and Interstitial

A clear decision matrix is given below:

| Criterion | Substitutional | Interstitial |
|---|---|---|
| **Solute atom size** | Within 15% of solvent (Hume-Rothery) | Very small ($r_{\text{solute}}/r_{\text{solvent}} < 0.414$ for FCC, $< 0.155$ for BCC) |
| **Typical solutes** | Transition metals, noble metals, near-neighbour elements | H, C, N, B, O |
| **Lattice distortion** | Local, proportional to size mismatch | Often severe, non-uniform |
| **Solubility** | Can be complete (e.g., Cu-Ni) or limited | Always limited by void size |
| **Effect on properties** | Solid solution strengthening (moderate) | Significant strengthening (e.g., steel) |
| **Example alloys** | Brass, Bronze, Monel, Invar | Steel, Cast iron, Titanium hydrides |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Solid Solution and Differentiate Between Substitutional and Interstitial Types. [3 Marks]
**Simulated Tag:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A solid solution is a homogeneous crystalline phase in which two or more elements are present in a single-phase structure, with the atoms of one or more elements (solute) occupying positions in the crystal lattice of the solvent element.

**Distinguishing Table:**

| Feature | Substitutional SS | Interstitial SS |
|---|---|---|
| Site occupied | Lattice site of host | Interstitial void |
| Solute size | Within 15% of solvent | Very small (< 41.4% of host in FCC) |
| Example | Cu-Zn (Brass) | Fe-C (Steel) |
| Distortion | Moderate | Severe |

### Question 2: State Hume-Rothery's Rules Governing Solid Solubility. [3 Marks]
**Simulated Tag:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The eight rules are:
1. **Atomic Size Factor:** Difference in atomic radii must be **less than 15%**.
2. **Same Crystal Structure:** Solute and solvent should have identical crystal structures.
3. **Valency Effect:** Lower-valency metals dissolve more in higher-valency metals.
4. **Chemical Affinity:** Closer electronegativity favours solubility; large differences lead to compound formation.
5. **Valency Compatibility:** Valency difference should not exceed ±1.
6. **Lattice Family:** Both metals should belong to the same lattice family.
7. **Electron Concentration:** Solubility is governed by the electron-to-atom (e/a) ratio.
8. **Periodic Position:** Elements close in the periodic table show greater mutual solubility.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): Solid Solutions and Hume-Rothery Analysis

**Simulated Tag:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a): Explain with suitable sketches the different types of solid solutions. Discuss the role of solute atom size in determining the type of solid solution. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A **solid solution** is a single-phase crystalline alloy in which solute atoms are dissolved in the solvent's crystal lattice. The two principal types are:

**1. Substitutional Solid Solution:**
Solute atoms replace solvent atoms on lattice sites. The crystal structure of the host is preserved. Examples include **brass (Cu-Zn)** and **bronze (Cu-Sn)**. The lattice parameter shifts linearly with composition (Vegard's Law). For extensive substitutional solubility, the **atomic size factor** $\delta$ must be ≤ 15%.

**2. Interstitial Solid Solution:**
Small atoms such as H, C, N, and B occupy the **interstitial voids** of the host lattice. In FCC structures, the octahedral void has a radius of $0.414 \, r$ and the tetrahedral void has $0.225 \, r$. In BCC, the octahedral void is much smaller at $0.155 \, r$.

**3. Other Variants:**
- **Interstitial-substitutional:** Mixed occupation; e.g., tool steels with W (substitutional) + C (interstitial) in Fe.
- **Vacancy solid solutions:** E.g., FeO with iron vacancies.
- **Ordered solid solutions:** Solute atoms occupy specific lattice positions (CuAu, Cu₃Au).

**Role of Solute Atom Size:**
- If $r_{\text{solute}} \approx r_{\text{solvent}}$ (within 15%): substitutional SS forms.
- If $r_{\text{solute}} \ll r_{\text{solvent}}$ (typically < 0.4 of host): interstitial SS forms.
- If size mismatch is large (> 15%): solubility is severely limited and intermetallic compounds may form.

#### Part (b): Discuss Hume-Rothery's rules with examples. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

Hume-Rothery's rules predict the conditions for extensive solid solubility in metallic systems. They are:

1. **Atomic Size Factor (δ ≤ 15%):** Essential because large size mismatch creates excessive lattice strain. *Example:* Cu (0.128 nm) and Zn (0.134 nm) — $\delta \approx 4.7\%$ — hence brass exhibits complete solid solubility up to 39 wt% Zn.

2. **Same Crystal Structure:** Both elements must crystallize in the same structure in their pure form. Cu (FCC) and Ni (FCC) are completely miscible, but Cu (FCC) and Cr (BCC) have limited solubility.

3. **Valency Effect:** A metal of lower valency dissolves more readily in a metal of higher valency. *Example:* Zn (valency 2) is highly soluble in Cu (valency 1), giving brass. Conversely, Cu has poor solubility in Zn.

4. **Chemical Affinity:** Elements with similar electronegativity form solid solutions, while dissimilar ones form intermetallics. Mg (1.31) and Pb (1.87) have large electronegativity difference and form an intermetallic compound Mg₂Pb rather than a solid solution.

5. **Valency Compatibility:** Valency difference should not exceed ±1. Cu⁺¹ and Zn²²⁺ are within this range; Cu and Ga (valency 3) show limited solubility.

6. **Lattice Type Family:** Transition metals dissolve in other transition metals (e.g., Fe-Cr, Fe-Ni) more readily than in s-block or p-block metals.

7. **Electron Concentration:** Phases like α (FCC, e/a = 1.36), β (BCC, e/a = 1.48), and ε (HCP, e/a = 1.58) form at characteristic electron-to-atom ratios in Cu-Zn, Cu-Al, and Cu-Sn systems.

8. **Periodic Position:** Adjacent elements in the periodic table show greater solubility (e.g., K-Rb, Ca-Sr).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often state *only the first four* rules. KTU 2024 scheme expects awareness of the **extended eight-rule formulation** for full credit in 7-mark questions. Also, numerical calculation of $\delta$ must be shown explicitly — do not skip the arithmetic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Interstitial Sites, Vegard's Law, and Alloy Examples

**Simulated Tag:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a): Derive Vegard's Law. Compute the lattice parameter of a Cu–30 at% Zn alloy, given that $a_{\text{Cu}} = 0.3615$ nm and $a_{\text{Zn}} = 0.2660$ nm (assume HCP for pure Zn and use the equivalent FCC lattice parameter for Vegard's law). [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider an ideal substitutional solid solution of metals A and B with lattice parameters $a_A$ and $a_B$ respectively. The total volume of the unit cell is the sum of contributions from each atom type.

For a cubic system, $V = a^3$, and for the alloy:

$$
a_{\text{alloy}}^3 = (1 - x_B) a_A^3 + x_B a_B^3
$$

For small lattice parameter differences (consistent with Hume-Rothery's Rule 1, $\delta \leq 15\%$), the cube root can be linearized using a first-order Taylor approximation, yielding **Vegard's Law**:

$$
\boxed{\,a_{\text{alloy}} = (1 - x_B) a_A + x_B a_B = a_A + x_B(a_B - a_A)\,}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $x_B = x_{\text{Zn}} = 0.30$, $a_{\text{Cu}} = 0.3615$ nm, $a_{\text{Zn (effective)}} = 0.2660$ nm.

Substituting:

$$
a_{\text{alloy}} = (1 - 0.30)(0.3615) + (0.30)(0.2660)
$$

$$
a_{\text{alloy}} = (0.70)(0.3615) + (0.30)(0.2660)
$$

$$
a_{\text{alloy}} = 0.25305 + 0.07980
$$

$$
\boxed{\,a_{\text{alloy}} = 0.33285 \text{ nm}\,}
$$

**Result:** The Cu–30 at% Zn alloy has a predicted FCC lattice parameter of approximately **0.333 nm**, which is in good agreement with experimental data for α-brass.

#### Part (b): Explain the formation of interstitial solid solutions. Compute the maximum radius of a solute atom that can fit into an octahedral void of an FCC iron lattice, given $r_{\text{Fe (FCC)}} = 0.129$ nm. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
In an interstitial solid solution, small solute atoms (H, C, N, B) occupy the voids between solvent atoms. The size of the void determines the maximum radius of a solute atom that can fit without excessive lattice strain.

For an FCC lattice, the octahedral void radius is given by:

$$
r_{\text{octa, FCC}} = 0.414 \, r_{\text{host}}
$$

**Derivation (briefly):**
In FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal, so $4r = a\sqrt{2}$, i.e., $a = 2\sqrt{2} \, r$. The octahedral void is at the cube centre, equidistant from the face-centre atoms at distance $a/2 = \sqrt{2} \, r$. Setting $r_{\text{void}} + r = \sqrt{2} \, r$, we get $r_{\text{void}} = (\sqrt{2} - 1)r \approx 0.414 \, r$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given $r_{\text{Fe}} = 0.129$ nm:

$$
r_{\text{octa, max}} = 0.414 \times 0.129 = 0.0534 \text{ nm}
$$

**Comparison with Carbon:**
- Atomic radius of C: **0.077 nm**.
- Maximum octahedral void radius in FCC Fe: **0.0534 nm**.

Since $r_C > r_{\text{octa, max}}$, the carbon atom **forces the iron lattice to expand locally**, generating a **tetragonal distortion**. This is the structural origin of martensite formation in quenched steel and the source of steel's exceptional hardness.

**Examples of Interstitial Solid Solutions:**
- Fe-C (austenite, γ-phase): up to 2.14 wt% C at 1147°C.
- Ti-H, Pd-H: hydrogen storage materials.
- Nb-N, Ta-N: superconducting ceramics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **BCC octahedral void radius (0.155 r)** with the FCC value (0.414 r). Always explicitly state the lattice type in your derivation. In KTU valuation, missing the lattice type **costs 1–2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This for Exam Eve)**

- ✅ **Solid Solution Definition:** Single-phase crystalline alloy; solute atoms dissolved in solvent lattice without forming a new compound.
- ✅ **Two Main Types:** *Substitutional* (solute replaces solvent on lattice sites) and *Interstitial* (small solute fills voids).
- ✅ **Hume-Rothery Rule 1 (Most Tested):** Atomic size difference must be **≤ 15%**; calculate $\delta = \frac{\vert r_s - r_m \vert}{r_m} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Vegard's Law:** $a_{\text{alloy}} = a_A + x_B(a_B - a_A)$ — valid only for ideal substitutional SS.
- ✅ **FCC Octahedral Void:** $r = 0.414 \, r_{\text{host}}$ — this is why carbon dissolves well in γ-iron.
- ✅ **BCC Octahedral Void:** $r = 0.155 \, r_{\text{host}}$ — much smaller; hence carbon solubility in α-iron is very low (0.022 wt%).
- ✅ **Hume-Rothery Electron Compounds:** α (FCC, e/a = 1.36), β (BCC, e/a = 1.48), ε (HCP, e/a = 1.58).
- ✅ **Classic Examples:** Brass (Cu-Zn, substitutional), Steel (Fe-C, interstitial), Monel (Cu-Ni, substitutional), Invar (Fe-Ni, substitutional), Bronze (Cu-Sn, substitutional).
- ✅ **Ordered vs Disordered:** At high T → disordered (random); at low T → ordered (superlattice, e.g., CuAu, Cu₃Au).
- ✅ **Vacancy SS:** Common in non-stoichiometric compounds like FeO, NiO.
- ✅ **Engineering Impact:** Solid solutions enable property tailoring — strengthening, corrosion resistance, magnetic behaviour, thermal expansion control.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always begin a solid-solution question by stating the definition, identifying the type (substitutional or interstitial) with justification based on atomic size, and then evaluating solubility using the appropriate Hume-Rothery rule(s). This structured approach guarantees maximum marks under the KTU 2024 scheme valuation scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hume-Rothery Rules** (also called the **Hume-Rothery conditions** or **solid solubility rules**) are a set of empirical guidelines formulated by the British metallurgist **William Hume-Rothery** in **1934**. These rules predict the extent to which one metallic element can dissolve in another to form a **substitutional solid solution**. They form the cornerstone of binary alloy design and phase diagram interpretation in physical metallurgy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> *"Hume-Rothery rules are empirical conditions that govern the formation of substitutional solid solutions. They are based on atomic size factor, crystal structure compatibility, valence (electron concentration), and electrochemical (electronegativity) affinity between the solute and solvent atoms."*

A **substitutional solid solution** is formed when atoms of the solute element occupy positions normally held by atoms of the solvent element in the parent crystal lattice.

---

## 1.2 The Four Hume-Rothery Rules — At a Glance

| # | Rule Name | Key Parameter | Threshold / Condition |
|---|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Atomic Size Factor Rule** | Atomic radius ($r$) | $\leq \mathbf{15\%}$ difference |
| 2 | **Crystal Structure Rule** | Lattice type | Same structure for extensive solubility |
| 3 | **Valence Rule** | Number of valence electrons ($V$) | Solute–solvent valence difference $\leq \mathbf{1}$ (or more for some) |
| 4 | **Electronegativity Rule** | Electronegativity ($\chi$) | Similar values to avoid compound formation |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balls in a Box" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine trying to mix two different sizes of billiard balls inside a single, tightly-packed box:**

- **Rule 1 (Size):** If the balls differ by more than **15%**, the larger balls simply *won't fit* in the gaps left by the smaller ones — the box gets distorted. If they are close in size, packing remains orderly. ✅
- **Rule 2 (Structure):** If one set of balls was meant for a **hexagonal** box and another for a **square** box, you can't mix them well — the shapes don't match. ✅
- **Rule 3 (Valence):** If the "chemistry" (electron behavior) of one ball is wildly different (say, a metallic vs. a non-metallic ball), they refuse to dissolve uniformly and instead form distinct compounds. ✅
- **Rule 4 (Electronegativity):** If one ball strongly attracts the glue between them and the other doesn't, the mixing is unstable, and the system prefers to form a *new compound* (like a stable ionic bond) instead of a mixed solution. ✅

This analogy makes it intuitive: Hume-Rothery rules are basically the **"compatibility checklist"** for two metals before they can merge into a single, uniform crystal phase.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

- **Atomic Radius Threshold:** $\Delta r \leq \mathbf{15\%}$
- **Electronegativity Threshold (Pauling Scale):** Difference $\leq \mathbf{0.4}$ for favorable solubility
- **Valence Parity:** Same or adjacent group preference
- **Crystal Structure Families:** FCC, BCC, HCP, Diamond Cubic

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rule 1 — The Atomic Size Factor Rule

### Formal Statement
The difference in atomic radii between the solute and solvent atoms must be **less than or equal to 15%** for appreciable solid solubility.

### Mathematical Formulation

$$\Delta r \, (\%) = \frac{\vert r_{solute} - r_{solvent} \vert}{r_{solvent}} \times 100$$

For favorable solubility:

$$\Delta r \, (\%) \leq 15\%$$

### Operational Explanation
- A size mismatch above **15%** introduces excessive **lattice strain energy** in the parent crystal.
- The strain causes localized distortion, raising the **Gibbs free energy** of the solid solution above that of a two-phase mixture.
- Therefore, the system prefers **phase separation** rather than atomic mixing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **15% rule** is not absolute — it is an empirical guideline. Some systems show limited solubility beyond 15% (e.g., Pb in Cu: ~40% mismatch, with solubility near zero). Others (like Mg in Cd: ~1% mismatch) show high solubility.

---

## 2.2 Rule 2 — Crystal Structure Compatibility

### Formal Statement
For extensive solid solubility, the solute and solvent must share the **same crystal structure** at the temperature of interest.

### Why This Matters
- The lattice geometry dictates **interatomic spacing**, **coordination number**, and **slip systems**.
- Two metals with vastly different lattices (e.g., FCC and BCC) cannot easily substitute atoms without triggering **polymorphic transformation** of one phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Example: **Cu (FCC)** and **Ni (FCC)** → complete solid solubility across all compositions. ✅
> Example: **Cu (FCC)** and **Fe (BCC at room temperature)** → limited solubility. ⚠️

---

## 2.3 Rule 3 — The Valence Rule

### Formal Statement
A metal of **higher valence** tends to dissolve more of a metal of **lower valence** than vice versa.

### Reasoning
- Each solvent atom contributes a fixed electron concentration to the lattice's **Fermi surface**.
- If a higher-valent solute adds extra electrons, they can be accommodated up to a critical limit before triggering the formation of **intermediate phases** (e.g., β-brass).
- The **Hume-Rothery electron compounds** (β, γ, ε phases) form at specific electron-to-atom ratios: **3/2, 21/13, 7/4** respectively.

---

## 2.4 Rule 4 — The Electronegativity Rule

### Formal Statement
The electronegativities of the two metals should be **comparable** to avoid compound formation.

### Pauling Scale Reference
- Solute–solvent electronegativity difference: $\Delta \chi \leq \mathbf{0.4}$ (approximate threshold).
- If $\Delta \chi$ is large, the atoms prefer to form **ionic or intermetallic compounds** rather than dissolve substitutionally.

> [!NOTE]
> Example: **Cu–Zn** ($\Delta \chi \approx 0.1$) → excellent solid solution → brass. ✅
> Example: **Mg–Si** ($\Delta \chi \approx 0.6$) → forms **Mg₂Si** intermetallic compound. ❌

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| **S.No** | **Formula / Rule** | **Equation** | **Threshold** | **Unit** | **Application** |
|----------|---------------------|--------------|---------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Atomic Size Factor | $\Delta r = \frac{\vert r_{solute} - r_{solvent} \vert}{r_{solvent}} \times 100$ | $\leq 15\%$ | % | Predict lattice strain |
| 2 | Vegard's Law (lattice parameter of solution) | $a_{ss} = a_{solute} \cdot x + a_{solvent} \cdot (1-x)$ | Linear variation | Å (Angstrom) | Lattice constant prediction |
| 3 | Electron Concentration (e/a ratio) | $\frac{e}{a} = \Sigma (V_i \cdot x_i)$ | $\beta$: 3/2, $\gamma$: 21/13, $\varepsilon$: 7/4 | electrons/atom | Identify Hume-Rothery phases |
| 4 | Lattice Strain Energy (simplified) | $E_{strain} \propto G \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta r}{r}\right)^2 \cdot V_{atom}$ | $G$ = shear modulus | J/mol | Strain estimation |
| 5 | Solid Solubility Limit Condition | $\Delta G_{mix} \leq 0$ | Atoms prefer lower Gibbs energy | J/mol | Thermodynamic feasibility |
| 6 | Hume-Rothery Phase e/a Ratios | $\beta$-phase, $\gamma$-phase, $\varepsilon$-phase | 3/2, 21/13, 7/4 | Ratio | Identify intermediate phases |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always **carry the units** in your final answer, especially in derivations involving strain energy and electron concentration. Marks are awarded for dimensional clarity.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application** |
|----------------------|-----------------|
| Aerospace Alloys | Ti–Al, Ni-based superalloys — designed using Hume-Rothery principles |
| Electrical Contacts | Cu–Zn, Cu–Sn alloys — full solubility gives tunable resistivity |
| Structural Steel | Fe–Cr, Fe–Ni — controlled solubility enables stainless steel |
| Semiconductor Doping | Si–Ge (Diamond Cubic match) — perfect solubility |
| Jewelry / Coinage | Cu–Ni, Ag–Cu — single-phase ductility |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Atomic Size Factor Numerical Evaluation

### Problem Setup
Given a hypothetical binary alloy system:
- **Solvent (A):** Copper (Cu), atomic radius $r_A = 0.1278$ nm
- **Solute (B):** Zinc (Zn), atomic radius $r_B = 0.1332$ nm

Determine whether **Zn** can substitute into the **Cu lattice** based on the **15% rule**.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Identify the solvent and solute.**
By convention, the element with higher atomic concentration is the solvent. Here, Cu is the solvent (majority), Zn is the solute (minority).

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute difference in atomic radii.**

$$\Delta r = \vert r_B - r_A \vert = \vert 0.1332 - 0.1278 \vert \text{ nm}$$

$$\Delta r = 0.0054 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Hume-Rothery size formula.**

$$\Delta r \, (\%) = \frac{\Delta r}{r_A} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\Delta r \, (\%) = \frac{0.0054}{0.1278} \times 100$$

$$\Delta r \, (\%) = 0.04225 \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Final percentage.**

$$\Delta r \, (\%) \approx 4.23\%$$

**Step 6 — Compare against threshold.**

$$\Delta r \, (\%) \approx 4.23\% \leq 15\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.**
The size factor is well within the 15% rule. **Zinc can substitutionally dissolve into copper** to form a single-phase **α-brass**, consistent with experimental observation (up to ~39 wt% Zn in Cu at room temperature).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Vegard's Law — Lattice Parameter of a Substitutional Solid Solution

### Theoretical Basis
For an ideal solid solution, the lattice parameter varies **linearly** with atomic composition.

### Mathematical Statement

$$a_{ss} = x_A \cdot a_A + x_B \cdot a_B$$

where:
- $a_{ss}$ = lattice parameter of the solid solution
- $a_A$ = lattice parameter of pure solvent
- $a_B$ = lattice parameter of pure solute
- $x_A, x_B$ = atomic fractions of solvent and solute

### Sample Numerical Computation

For a **Cu–Zn** alloy with 30 at% Zn:
- $a_{Cu} = 0.3615$ nm
- $a_{Zn} = 0.2660$ nm (HCP, but conceptually if Zn were FCC)
- $x_{Cu} = 0.70$, $x_{Zn} = 0.30$

$$a_{ss} = (0.70 \times 0.3615) + (0.30 \times 0.2660)$$

$$a_{ss} = 0.25305 + 0.07980$$

$$a_{ss} = 0.33285 \text{ nm}$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

$$\begin{aligned}
a_{ss} &= x_A \cdot a_A + x_B \cdot a_B \\[4pt]
       &= (1 - x_B) \cdot a_A + x_B \cdot a_B \\[4pt]
       &= a_A - x_B \cdot a_A + x_B \cdot a_B \\[4pt]
       &= a_A + x_B \cdot (a_B - a_A)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **linear form** of Vegard's Law, showing that the lattice parameter **shifts from $a_A$ toward $a_B$** as the solute fraction increases.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Electron Concentration (e/a Ratio) for a Binary Alloy

### Definition
The electron-to-atom ratio is the average number of **valence electrons per atom** in the alloy.

### Formula

$$\left(\frac{e}{a}\right)_{alloy} = \frac{V_A \cdot x_A + V_B \cdot x_B}{x_A + x_B} = V_A \cdot x_A + V_B \cdot x_B$$

where $V_i$ is the valence of element $i$.

### Example: Cu–Zn Alloy (Brass)
- Cu valence: $V_{Cu} = 1$ (transition metal, contributes 1 conduction electron)
- Zn valence: $V_{Zn} = 2$
- Composition: 70 at% Cu, 30 at% Zn

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{e}{a} &= V_{Cu} \cdot x_{Cu} + V_{Zn} \cdot x_{Zn} \\[4pt]
            &= (1)(0.70) + (2)(0.30) \\[4pt]
            &= 0.70 + 0.60 \\[4pt]
            &= 1.30
\end{aligned}$$

This value (**1.30**) is below the **β-phase threshold of 3/2 = 1.50**, confirming the alloy remains in the **α-phase (FCC)** — consistent with experimental phase diagrams.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Predicting Solid Solubility

| **Decision Step** | **Question Asked** | **Yes →** | **No →** |
|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | $\Delta r \leq 15\%$? | Proceed to Step 2 | Solubility negligible |
| 2 | Same crystal structure? | Proceed to Step 3 | Limited solubility |
| 3 | Valence difference $\leq 1$? | Proceed to Step 4 | Possible intermediate phase |
| 4 | $\Delta \chi \leq 0.4$? | **Full solubility predicted** | Compound forms instead |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
