\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$$ Rarely observed due to slow kinetics at low temperatures. ## 2.3 Classification of Fe–C Alloys | Class | Carbon Range (wt %) | Primary Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Pure Iron** | < 0.008 | Magnetic cores, research | | **Low-Carbon Steel** | 0.008 – 0.30 | Structural, sheets, wires | | **Medium-Carbon Steel** | 0.30 – 0.60 | Railway tracks, crankshafts | | **High-Carbon Steel** | 0.60 – 2.14 | Springs, cutting tools, dies | | **Cast Iron** | 2.14 – 6.67 | Engine blocks, pipes, machine bases | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Lever Rule — Fraction of Phase α** | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ | Where $C_0$ is alloy composition, $C_\alpha, C_\beta$ are phase compositions from tie-line | | **Lever Rule — Fraction of Phase β** | $W_\beta = \dfrac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ | Clearly $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$ | | **Eutectoid Fraction (Pearlite at 727 °C)** | $W_{\text{pearlite}} = \dfrac{0.76 - C_0}{0.76 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.76 - C_0}{0.738}$ | For hypoeutectoid steels ($C_0 < 0.76$) | | **Proeutectoid Ferrite Fraction** | $W_{\alpha'} = \dfrac{0.76 - C_0}{0.76 - 0.022}$ | Forms above 727 °C | | **Proeutectoid Cementite Fraction** | $W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}'} = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.76}$ | For hypereutectoid steels ($C_0 > 0.76$) | | **Eutectic Fraction (Ledeburite at 1147 °C)** | $W_{\text{ledeburite}} = \dfrac{4.30 - C_0}{4.30 - 2.14} = \dfrac{4.30 - C_0}{2.16}$ | For cast irons ($C_0 > 2.14$) | | **Gibbs Phase Rule** | $F = C - P + 1$ | Constant pressure; binary system | | **Invariant Condition** | $F = 0 \Rightarrow P = 3$ | Three phases coexist at one temperature | | **Max Solubility of C in α-Fe** | $0.022\;\text{wt \%}$ | At 727 °C (point **P** on diagram) | | **Max Solubility of C in γ-Fe** | $2.14\;\text{wt \%}$ | At 1147 °C (point **E** on diagram) | | **Cementite Composition** | $6.67\;\text{wt \% C}$ | Stoichiometric Fe₃C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The **lever rule is derived from conservation of mass** for a two-phase mixture. The "lever arm" is the distance from the alloy composition to the phase composition on the tie-line. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Fe–Fe₃C diagram is the **roadmap of ferrous metallurgy**. Without it, an engineer cannot: - Predict the **as-cast microstructure** of a steel casting. - Design **heat-treatment cycles** (annealing, normalizing, quenching, tempering). - Select **carbon content** for desired mechanical properties (e.g., 0.4% C for a connecting rod, 1.0% C for a hacksaw blade). - Control **welding metallurgy** (HAZ phase transformations). - Forecast **phase transformations during service** (e.g., power-plant boiler tubes at 500 °C). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Lever Rule from Mass Conservation Consider an alloy of overall composition $C_0$ at a temperature where two phases $\alpha$ and $\beta$ coexist. Let the masses of phases be $m_\alpha$ and $m_\beta$, with compositions $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$. **Step 1:** Total mass balance. $$m_{\text{total}} = m_\alpha + m_\beta$$ **Step 2:** Carbon balance (component conservation). $$m_{\text{total}} \cdot C_0 = m_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + m_\beta \cdot C_\beta$$ **Step 3:** Divide both sides by $m_{\text{total}}$ and substitute $W_\alpha = m_\alpha / m_{\text{total}}$, $W_\beta = m_\beta / m_{\text{total}}$. $$C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + W_\beta \cdot C_\beta$$ **Step 4:** Use $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1 \Rightarrow W_\beta = 1 - W_\alpha$. $$C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + (1 - W_\alpha) \cdot C_\beta$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange. $$C_0 - C_\beta = W_\alpha (C_\alpha - C_\beta)$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $W_\alpha$. $$W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$$ **Step 7:** Similarly, $W_\beta$. $$W_\beta = \dfrac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$$ The lever arm from alloy to phase $\alpha$ gives the fraction of phase $\beta$, and vice versa — hence the name. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Microstructure of a 0.4 wt % C Steel at 727 °C (Just Above Eutectoid) **Given:** Hypoeutectoid steel, $C_0 = 0.40\;\text{wt \%}$. Temperature just above 727 °C. **Phase compositions (from tie-line at ~730 °C):** - Proeutectoid ferrite: $C_\alpha = 0.022\;\text{wt \%}$ - Austenite (which will transform to pearlite): $C_\gamma = 0.76\;\text{wt \%}$ **Step A — Proeutectoid ferrite fraction.** $$W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\gamma}{C_\alpha - C_\gamma} = \dfrac{0.40 - 0.76}{0.022 - 0.76} = \dfrac{-0.36}{-0.738} = 0.488$$ So approximately **48.8% proeutectoid ferrite**. **Step B — Austenite (= pearlite at 727 °C) fraction.** $$W_\gamma = 1 - W_\alpha = 1 - 0.488 = 0.512$$ So approximately **51.2% pearlite** will form on cooling through 727 °C. **Step C — Total cementite in final microstructure (all cementite is in pearlite).** $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C, total}} = W_\gamma \times \dfrac{0.76 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = 0.512 \times 0.110 = 0.0563$$ So **5.63% cementite**, with the remaining balance as ferrite: $1 - 0.0563 = 0.9437$ → **94.37% total ferrite**. > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** Proeutectoid ferrite (48.8%) + Ferrite inside pearlite = 94.37% total ferrite. This passes the mass balance: $W_\alpha + W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 1$. ✓ ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Eutectoid Steel (0.76 wt % C) Cooled Slowly to 25 °C **Given:** Eutectoid composition. At 727 °C the entire austenite transforms to 100% pearlite. **Step 1:** Pearlite fraction = 100%. **Step 2:** Ferrite fraction within pearlite. $$W_\alpha^{\text{(in pearlite)}} = \dfrac{6.67 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{5.91}{6.648} = 0.889$$ **Step 3:** Cementite fraction within pearlite. $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}}^{\text{(in pearlite)}} = 1 - 0.889 = 0.111$$ Final microstructure: **100% pearlite → 88.9% ferrite + 11.1% cementite** (in lamellar form). ## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Cast Iron with 3.0 wt % C Cooled from Liquid to 727 °C **Given:** Hypoeutectic cast iron, $C_0 = 3.0\;\text{wt \%}$. **Step 1 — At 1147 °C (just above eutectic):** - Phases: Liquid ($C_L = 4.30\%$) + Austenite ($C_\gamma = 2.14\%$). $$W_\gamma^{(1147^+)} = \dfrac{4.30 - 3.0}{4.30 - 2.14} = \dfrac{1.30}{2.16} = 0.602$$ $$W_L^{(1147^+)} = 1 - 0.602 = 0.398$$ So at 1147 °C: **60.2% primary austenite + 39.8% liquid**. **Step 2 — Just Below 1147 °C (Eutectic reaction occurs in the liquid):** All 39.8% liquid transforms into **Ledeburite** (austenite + cementite eutectic mix at 2.14% and 6.67%). **Step 3 — At 727 °C (Eutectoid reaction in austenite):** Total austenite present (both primary and from ledeburite) at 727 °C is **60.2%** (assuming carbon redistribution neglected for simplicity, though in practice the austenite from ledeburite has higher carbon). For this simplified KTU-level calculation: - Austenite fraction = 60.2%, all of which transforms to pearlite. - Cementite fraction = 39.8% (from ledeburite). Final microstructure at 727 °C: **60.2% pearlite + 39.8% cementite**. **Step 4 — Total cementite verification by global lever rule (cementite = 6.67%):** $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = \dfrac{3.0 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.76} = \dfrac{2.24}{5.91} = 0.379 \approx 39.8\% \; ✓$$ This confirms our phase fractions are thermodynamically consistent. ## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Hypereutectoid Steel (1.2 wt % C) **Given:** $C_0 = 1.2\;\text{wt \%}$. **Step 1 — Proeutectoid cementite that forms between Acm and A₁:** $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}'} = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.76} = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.76}{5.91} = 0.0745 \; (7.45\%)$$ **Step 2 — Pearlite at 727 °C:** $$W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1 - 0.0745 = 0.9255 \; (92.55\%)$$ **Step 3 — Total cementite (proeutectoid + within pearlite):** $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C, total}} = 0.0745 + 0.9255 \times \dfrac{0.76 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022}$$ $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C, total}} = 0.0745 + 0.9255 \times 0.110 = 0.0745 + 0.1018 = 0.1763$$ **Step 4 — Total ferrite:** $$W_\alpha = 1 - 0.1763 = 0.8237 \; (82.37\%)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that **hypereutectoid steels have a brittle network of proeutectoid cementite** along prior austenite grain boundaries, which is why such steels are usually **spheroidized** before machining or cold-working. ## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix (Decision Flowchart Logic) | Alloy Type | $C_0$ Range | At 727 °C | At Room Temp (Slow Cool) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Hypoeutectoid steel | $0.022 < C_0 < 0.76$ | $\alpha' + \gamma$ | Proeutectoid α + Pearlite (α + Fe₃C) | | Eutectoid steel | $C_0 = 0.76$ | $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ | 100% Pearlite | | Hypereutectoid steel | $0.76 < C_0 < 2.14$ | $\text{Fe}_3\text{C}' + \gamma$ | Proeutectoid Fe₃C + Pearlite | | Eutectic cast iron | $C_0 = 4.30$ | Ledeburite | Ledeburite (transformed) | | Hypoeutectic cast iron | $2.14 < C_0 < 4.30$ | $\gamma +$ Ledeburite | Pearlite + Ledeburite | | Hypereutectic cast iron | $4.30 < C_0 < 6.67$ | $\text{Fe}_3\text{C}' +$ Ledeburite | Primary Fe₃C + Ledeburite | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic Block-Level Architecture of the Fe–Fe₃C Phase Diagram The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the **functional architecture of the phase diagram**, mapping how temperature, composition, and phases interact — rather than attempting a physical drawing of the curve. ```mermaid graph TD node1["TEMPERATURE AXIS
1538 to 0 deg C"] node2["COMPOSITION AXIS
0 to 6.67 wt percent C"] node3["PURE IRON SIDE
0 percent C"] node4["CEMENTITE SIDE
6.67 percent C"] node5["ALPHA FERRITE REGION
BCC, max 0.022 percent C"] node6["GAMMA AUSTENITE REGION
FCC, max 2.14 percent C"] node7["DELTA FERRITE REGION
BCC, max 0.09 percent C"] node8["LIQUID REGION
Above liquidus lines"] node9["CEMENTITE PHASE
Fe3C fixed 6.67 percent C"] node10["EUTECTOID POINT S
727 deg C, 0.76 percent C"] node11["EUTECTIC POINT C
1147 deg C, 4.30 percent C"] node12["PERITECTIC POINT
1493 deg C, 0.16 percent C"] node13["HYPOEUTECTOID STEEL
0.022 to 0.76 percent C"] node14["HYPEREUTECTOID STEEL
0.76 to 2.14 percent C"] node15["CAST IRON REGION
2.14 to 6.67 percent C"] node16["PEARLITE OUTPUT
alpha plus Fe3C lamellar"] node17["LEDEBURITE OUTPUT
gamma plus Fe3C eutectic"] node1 --> node5 node1 --> node6 node1 --> node7 node1 --> node8 node2 --> node3 node2 --> node4 node5 --> node13 node6 --> node13 node6 --> node14 node6 --> node15 node9 --> node14 node9 --> node15 node10 --> node16 node11 --> node17 node12 --> node6 node13 --> node16 node14 --> node16 node15 --> node17 ``` ## 4.2 Transformation Cascade Flowchart (Cooling Path) ```mermaid graph TD startA["START: Hypoeutectoid Steel
0.4 percent C, 1600 deg C"] cool1["Cool in Liquid Region"] cool2["Cross Liquidus
Begin solidification as gamma"] cool3["Cross Solidus
100 percent Austenite"] cool4["Cross A3 Line
Proeutectoid alpha precipitates"] cool5["Reach 727 deg C
Eutectoid Transformation"] endA["Final Microstructure
Proeutectoid alpha + Pearlite"] startB["START: Hypereutectoid Steel
1.2 percent C, 1600 deg C"] cool6["Cool to Liquidus"] cool7["Solidify as Austenite"] cool8["Cool to Acm Line
Proeutectoid Fe3C at grain boundaries"] cool9["Reach 727 deg C
Eutectoid Transformation"] endB["Final Microstructure
Proeutectoid Fe3C + Pearlite"] startC["START: Eutectoid Steel
0.76 percent C, 1600 deg C"] cool10["Solidify as Austenite"] cool11["Cool to 727 deg C
No proeutectoid phase"] cool12["Eutectoid Transformation"] endC["Final Microstructure
100 percent Pearlite"] startA --> cool1 --> cool2 --> cool3 --> cool4 --> cool5 --> endA startB --> cool6 --> cool7 --> cool8 --> cool9 --> endB startC --> cool10 --> cool11 --> cool12 --> endC ``` ## 4.3 Critical Temperature Nomenclature Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A1Line["A1 Line
727 deg C
Eutectoid Temperature"] A2Line["A2 Line
770 deg C
Curie Temperature
Magnetism to Non-Magnetism"] A3Line["A3 Line
Hypoeutectoid
alpha to gamma"] AcmLine["Acm Line
Hypereutectoid
gamma to Fe3C solubility"] A1Line --> X1["Below A1
alpha plus Fe3C stable"] A2Line --> X2["Above A2
Austenite non-magnetic"] A3Line --> X3["Above A3
100 percent Austenite for hypoeutectoid"] AcmLine --> X4["Above Acm
100 percent Austenite for hypereutectoid"] A1Line --- A2Line A2Line --- A3Line A3Line --- AcmLine ``` ## 4.4 Microconstituent Identification Map ```mermaid graph TD base["Iron Carbon Alloy
at Room Temperature"] phase1["Ferrite alpha
Soft, BCC, magnetic"] phase2["Cementite Fe3C
Hard, brittle, 6.67 percent C"] phase3["Pearlite
Lamellar alpha + Fe3C"] phase4["Ledeburite
Eutectic of gamma + Fe3C"] phase5["Martensite
Non-equilibrium, BCT"] base --> phase1 base --> phase2 base --> phase3 base --> phase4 base --> phase5 phase3 --> p3a["Forms at 727 deg C
from Austenite of 0.76 percent C"] phase4 --> p4a["Forms at 1147 deg C
from Liquid of 4.30 percent C"] phase5 --> p5a["Forms on rapid quenching
Diffusionless transformation"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the KTU exam, when asked to "draw and label the Fe-Fe3C diagram," a neat, fully labeled schematic with all critical points (A, C, E, S, D, P, Q) and lines (A1, A2, A3, Acm, liquidus, solidus, solvus) is mandatory. Use **bold** for invariant points and clearly mark the eutectoid reaction with a horizontal line. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the following terms with reference to the Fe–Fe₃C phase diagram:** **(a)** Eutectoid reaction **(b)** Proeutectoid ferrite **Model Answer:** **(a)** **Eutectoid Reaction:** It is an invariant reaction in which a single solid phase (γ-austenite of 0.76 wt % C) transforms upon cooling into **two distinct solid phases** (α-ferrite of 0.022 wt % C and cementite Fe₃C of 6.67 wt % C) simultaneously at a constant temperature of **727 °C**. Mathematically: $\gamma \;(0.76\% \text{C}) \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{cooling}]{\text{heating}}\; \alpha \;(0.022\%\text{C}) + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}\;(6.67\%\text{C})$. **(b)** **Proeutectoid Ferrite:** It is the **primary ferrite that precipitates from austenite** during slow cooling of a hypoeutectoid steel through the temperature range between the A₃ line and the A₁ line (727 °C). It forms *before* the eutectoid reaction occurs, hence the prefix "pro-" (meaning "before"). The remaining carbon-enriched austenite subsequently transforms to pearlite at 727 °C. *(Examiner's Note: Each definition carries 1.5 marks. Award full marks if the student states the temperature, composition, and reaction/product clearly.)* --- ### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between the following:** **(a)** Ferrite and Austenite **(b)** Pearlite and Ledeburite **Model Answer:** **(a) Ferrite vs. Austenite:** | Property | Ferrite (α) | Austenite (γ) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Crystal structure | BCC | FCC | | Max C solubility | 0.022 wt % | 2.14 wt % | | Magnetic | Yes (up to 770 °C) | Non-magnetic | | Stability range | Up to 912 °C | 912 – 1394 °C | | Hardness | ~80 HB (soft) | ~200 HB | | Ductility | Very high | High | **(b) Pearlite vs. Ledeburite:** | Property | Pearlite | Ledeburite | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Reaction type | Eutectoid (solid → solid) | Eutectic (liquid → solid) | | Temperature | 727 °C | 1147 °C | | Phases | α-Ferrite + Cementite | γ-Austenite + Cementite (at formation); at room temp = Pearlite + Cementite | | Composition | 0.76 wt % C | 4.30 wt % C | | Found in | Steels | Cast irons | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-based: 14 Marks) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**, or (a) **6 marks** + (b) **8 marks**. --- ### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw a fully labeled **Iron-Iron Carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) equilibrium phase diagram**. Mark and label the following: (i) All invariant points (A, C, D, E, S, P, Q) with their **temperatures and compositions**. (ii) All critical lines (A₁, A₂, A₃, A_cm, liquidus, solidus, solvus). (iii) All three invariant reactions at points labeled clearly. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a hypoeutectoid steel containing **0.45 wt % C**, determine at a temperature **just above 727 °C**: (i) The phases present and their compositions. (ii) The **relative amounts (mass fractions)** of each phase using the lever rule. (iii) The composition and amount of **proeutectoid ferrite** and the **austenite** that will transform to pearlite on further cooling. --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Sketch of Fe–Fe₃C Diagram:** > *Students are expected to draw a clean labeled schematic showing:* > - *Y-axis: Temperature (°C) from 0 to 1600.* > - *X-axis: Composition (wt % C) from 0 to 6.67.* > - *Left boundary: Pure iron with allotropic changes at 912 °C and 1394 °C.* > - *Right boundary: Vertical line at 6.67 wt % C representing cementite.* > - *Key horizontal lines: 727 °C (eutectoid) and 1147 °C (eutectic).* > - *Single-phase regions: α, γ, δ, L, Fe₃C.* > - *Two-phase regions shaded or labeled.* **Marking Rubric (7 marks):** - Axes & labels: 1 mark - Invariant points (A, C, D, E, S, P, Q): 2 marks - Critical lines (A₁, A₂, A₃, A_cm): 2 marks - Reactions written at C and S: 2 marks --- **Part (b) — Solution:** **Given:** $C_0 = 0.45\;\text{wt \%}$, $T \approx 730\;\text{°C}$ (just above eutectoid). **Step (i) — Phases Present:** From the diagram at ~730 °C, the phases are: - **Proeutectoid ferrite (α)** with $C_\alpha = 0.022\;\text{wt \%}$ - **Austenite (γ)** with $C_\gamma = 0.76\;\text{wt \%}$ **Step (ii) — Mass Fractions (Lever Rule):** $$W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\gamma}{C_\alpha - C_\gamma} = \dfrac{0.45 - 0.76}{0.022 - 0.76} = \dfrac{-0.31}{-0.738} = 0.420$$ $$\boxed{W_\alpha = 0.420 \;\;(42.0\% \text{ proeutectoid ferrite})}$$ $$W_\gamma = 1 - 0.420 = 0.580$$ $$\boxed{W_\gamma = 0.580 \;\;(58.0\% \text{ austenite})}$$ **Step (iii) — Proeutectoid Ferrite & Pearlite:** - Proeutectoid ferrite = **42.0%** (will not transform further). - Austenite = **58.0%** → this transforms entirely to **pearlite at 727 °C**. **Pearlite sub-fractions:** $$W_\alpha^{\text{(in pearlite)}} = 0.580 \times \dfrac{6.67 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.022} = 0.580 \times 0.889 = 0.516$$ $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}}^{\text{(in pearlite)}} = 0.580 - 0.516 = 0.064$$ **Final microstructure at room temperature:** | Microconstituent | Amount | | :--- | :--- | | Total ferrite (proeutectoid + in pearlite) | $0.420 + 0.516 = 0.936$ (**93.6%**) | | Total cementite | **6.4%** | --- ### QUESTION B (ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following invariant reactions in the Fe–Fe₃C system with temperature, composition, and phases involved: (i) **Eutectic reaction** at 1147 °C (ii) **Eutectoid reaction** at 727 °C (iii) **Peritectic reaction** at 1493 °C **(b) [7 Marks]** For a **hypereutectoid steel with 1.0 wt % C**: (i) Identify the phases present just **above and just below 727 °C**. (ii) Calculate the **mass fractions** of proeutectoid cementite and pearlite using the lever rule. (iii) Calculate the **total ferrite** and **total cementite** in the final microstructure. --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Invariant Reactions:** **(i) Eutectic Reaction at 1147 °C, 4.30 wt % C:** $$\text{Liquid}\;(4.30\%\text{ C}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \gamma\text{-Austenite}\;(2.14\%\text{ C}) + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}\;(6.67\%\text{ C})$$ Product: **Ledeburite** — found in cast irons. **(ii) Eutectoid Reaction at 727 °C, 0.76 wt % C:** $$\gamma\text{-Austenite}\;(0.76\%\text{ C}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \alpha\text{-Ferrite}\;(0.022\%\text{ C}) + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}\;(6.67\%\text{ C})$$ Product: **Pearlite** — found in steels. **(iii) Peritectic Reaction at 1493 °C, 0.16 wt % C:** $$\text{Liquid}\;(0.53\%\text{ C}) + \delta\text{-Ferrite}\;(0.09\%\text{ C}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \gamma\text{-Austenite}\;(0.16\%\text{ C})$$ Often associated with solidification defects in ingots. **Marking Rubric:** - Each reaction correctly stated with T, compositions, products = 2 marks each (with brief note). --- **Part (b) — Solution for 1.0 wt % C Hypereutectoid Steel:** **Given:** $C_0 = 1.0\;\text{wt \%}$, $C_0 > 0.76$ → hypereutectoid. **Step (i) — Phases:** *Just above 727 °C (~730 °C):* - Proeutectoid **cementite** (Fe₃C) precipitated along austenite grain boundaries. - **Austenite (γ)** with $C_\gamma = 0.76\;\text{wt \%}$. *Just below 727 °C:* - Proeutectoid cementite (retained). - **Pearlite** (α + Fe₃C) formed from austenite transformation. **Step (ii) — Mass Fractions using Lever Rule:** *Just above 727 °C:* $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}'} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\gamma}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_\gamma} = \dfrac{1.0 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.76} = \dfrac{0.24}{5.91} = 0.0406$$ $$\boxed{W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}'} = 4.06\% \text{ (proeutectoid cementite)}}$$ $$W_\gamma = 1 - 0.0406 = 0.9594 = 95.94\% \text{ austenite}$$ This 95.94% austenite transforms to pearlite at 727 °C. $$\boxed{W_{\text{pearlite}} = 95.94\%}$$ **Step (iii) — Total Ferrite and Total Cementite:** Within pearlite: $$W_\alpha^{\text{(in pearlite)}} = 0.9594 \times \dfrac{6.67 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.022} = 0.9594 \times 0.889 = 0.8529$$ $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}}^{\text{(in pearlite)}} = 0.9594 - 0.8529 = 0.1065$$ **Total cementite:** $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C, total}} = W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}'} + W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}}^{\text{(in pearlite)}} = 0.0406 + 0.1065 = 0.1471$$ $$\boxed{W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C, total}} = 14.71\%}$$ **Total ferrite:** $$W_{\alpha, \text{total}} = 1 - 0.1471 = 0.8529$$ $$\boxed{W_{\alpha, \text{total}} = 85.29\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Interchanging α and γ fractions:** Students often write $W_\alpha$ but plug in austenite compositions. Always draw the tie-line first and label both ends clearly. > 2. **Forgetting the units:** Carbon content is in **wt %**, not atomic %. The atomic wt % of C in Fe₃C is 6.67 wt %, but its atomic composition is 25 at %. > 3. **Confusing hypoeutectoid with hypereutectoid:** If $C_0 < 0.76$, proeutectoid is **ferrite**; if $C_0 > 0.76$, proeutectoid is **cementite**. This is the single most common error. > 4. **Using $C_0$ in lever rule instead of phase compositions:** The lever rule endpoints are the **solubility limits**, NOT $C_0$. > 5. **Drawing the diagram without temperatures/compositions on points:** Unlabeled diagrams receive **zero** marks for the labeling component. > 6. **Mixing up A₁ and A₃:** A₁ = 727 °C (eutectoid, always fixed). A₃ varies with composition (boundary of γ region for hypoeutectoid steels). > 7. **Writing "pearlite" instead of "ledeburite"** for cast iron microconstituent. Pearlite forms from solid austenite; ledeburite forms from liquid. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) ### 🔑 The 7 Most Critical Numerical Values to Memorize - [x] **0.022 wt % C** → max solubility of C in α-ferrite (at 727 °C) - [x] **0.76 wt % C** → eutectoid composition (steel boundary) - [x] **2.14 wt % C** → max solubility of C in γ-austenite (at 1147 °C) - [x] **4.30 wt % C** → eutectic composition (cast iron boundary) - [x] **6.67 wt % C** → composition of cementite Fe₃C - [x] **727 °C** → eutectoid temperature (A₁) - [x] **1147 °C** → eutectic temperature ### 🔑 Key Phrases the Examiner Loves - "**Eutectoid**" = solid → two solids; "**Eutectic**" = liquid → two solids. - "**Proeutectoid**" = phase formed *before* the eutectoid reaction. - "**A₁** is constant at 727 °C; A₃ varies with carbon content." - "Pearlite is a microconstituent (not a phase) — a lamellar aggregate of α + Fe₃C." - "Cementite is hard and brittle; ferrite is soft and ductile." ### 🔑 Critical Reactions - **Peritectic** (1493 °C): $\text{L} + \delta \rightleftharpoons \gamma$ - **Eutectic** (1147 °C): $\text{L} \rightleftharpoons \gamma + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ → **Ledeburite** - **Eutectoid** (727 °C): $\gamma \rightleftharpoons \alpha + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ → **Pearlite** - **Peritectoid** (600 °C, rare): $\alpha + \text{Fe}_3\text{C} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ (kinetically suppressed) ### 🔑 Lever Rule Mastery - Draw the tie-line first. - Place $C_0$ on the line. Endpoints are the phase compositions. - $W_\alpha$ = (distance from $C_0$ to $\beta$ endpoint) / (total tie-line length). - Always cross-check: $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$. ### 🔑 Steel vs. Cast Iron Boundary - **Steel:** $0.022 < \text{C} < 2.14\;\text{wt \%}$ (fully austenitizable) - **Cast Iron:** $\text{C} > 2.14\;\text{wt \%}$ (passes through eutectic reaction) ### 🔑 Common Engineering Correlations - ↑ Carbon content → ↑ Hardness, ↑ Strength, ↓ Ductility. - Hypereutectoid steels are usually **spheroidized** to improve machinability. - Pearlite is named for its mother-of-pearl appearance under microscope. > **Final Note:** The Fe–Fe₃C diagram is the **single most important diagram in all of physical metallurgy**. Master the critical points, the four invariant reactions, and the lever rule — and you master ferrous metallurgy for the KTU examination.

topics.title: T-T-T Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** T-T-T Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Time-Temperature-Transformation (T-T-T) Diagram**, also known as the **Isothermal Transformation (IT) Diagram** or **S-Curve**, is a graphical representation that plots the relationship between time (on the logarithmic x-axis) and temperature (on the y-axis) to predict the phase transformation behavior of a specific alloy (typically eutectoid steel containing **0.77% C**) when it is rapidly cooled (quenched) from its austenitic state and held at a constant temperature.

The diagram illustrates:
- The **time required** for the decomposition of austenite ($\gamma$-iron, FCC) into various transformation products.
- The **critical temperatures** at which specific microstructural constituents such as **Pearlite**, **Bainite**, and **Martensite** are formed.
- The boundary curves representing the start (1% transformation) and finish (99% transformation) of phase changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the construction, interpretation, and application of the T-T-T diagram in heat treatment problems, often linked to the concepts of annealing, normalizing, quenching, and tempering of plain carbon steels.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you have a hot, freshly baked piece of **chapati (Indian flatbread)** representing red-hot **austenite**. If you drop it immediately into a bucket of cold water, it becomes a hard, brittle, glass-like cracker — this is analogous to the formation of **Martensite** (a very hard, non-equilibrium phase). 

If instead, you let the chapati cool slowly on a plate at room temperature, it remains soft and pliable — like **Pearlite**, the soft, lamellar equilibrium phase. 

But if you hold the chapati in a warm oven at a medium temperature for several hours, you get a different texture — slightly tough but not as hard as the quenched one — analogous to **Bainite**, an intermediate microstructure. The T-T-T diagram is essentially a **"recipe book"** that tells you exactly what microstructure you will get depending on **how fast you cool** and **at what temperature you hold** the steel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The horizontal axis is **logarithmic (log time)**, which compresses a huge range of seconds-to-days into a readable scale. The characteristic **"C-shape"** of the curves is why TTT diagrams are also called **C-curves**.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Eutectoid Composition:** **0.77% C, 0.8% Mn, 0.04% Si, 0.03% S&P** (the standard plain carbon steel for textbook TTT diagrams).
- **Eutectoid Temperature ($A_{1}$):** **727°C** (the temperature below which austenite becomes thermodynamically unstable).
- **Martenstite Start ($M_s$):** ~**215°C** for eutectoid steel.
- **Martenstite Finish ($M_f$):** ~**-50°C** for eutectoid steel.
- **Nose Temperature:** ~**550°C** (the temperature at which transformation is fastest).
- **Nose Time:** ~**0.7 seconds** (the shortest incubation period before transformation begins).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle
A TTT diagram is constructed by:
1. **Austenitizing** small specimens of the steel at a temperature above the upper critical temperature ($A_{3}$ or $A_{cm}$) — typically around **760°C–800°C** for eutectoid steel — until the entire microstructure transforms to **austenite ($\gamma$)**.
2. **Rapidly quenching** each specimen into a molten salt bath or fluidized bed held at a **constant sub-critical temperature** ($T < 727°C$).
3. **Withdrawing** specimens at progressively increasing time intervals and **quenching** them in water to "freeze" the microstructure formed at that instant.
4. **Examining** metallographically to determine the percentage of transformation (typically **1% start, 50% halfway, 99% finish**).
5. **Plotting** these time-temperature points on semi-log paper to obtain the characteristic C-curve.

### 2.2 Key Regions of the TTT Diagram

| Region | Temperature Range | Transformation Product | Hardness (HRC) | Morphology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pearlite Region (Upper)** | 727°C – 550°C | Coarse to fine Pearlite ($\alpha + Fe_3C$) | 20–40 | Lamellar (alternating ferrite & cementite) |
| **Bainite Region (Lower)** | 550°C – 250°C | Upper/Lower Bainite | 40–60 | Acicular (needle-like) |
| **Martensite Region** | Below $M_s$ (215°C) | Martensite ($\alpha'$ - BCT) | 60–65 | Lath/Plate (diffusionless) |
| **Austenite Stable** | Above C-curve | Retained Austenite ($\gamma$) | Soft | FCC |

### 2.3 The Three C-Curves
Each TTT diagram typically shows:
- **First C-Curve (Upper):** Represents the **start of transformation** (1% pearlite/bainite formed).
- **Second C-Curve (Middle):** Represents the **50% transformation line**.
- **Third C-Curve (Lower):** Represents the **finish of transformation** (99% transformed).

Between the first and last curves, the steel exists as a **mixture of austenite and the transformation product**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **horizontal line marked $M_s$ (215°C)** and **$M_f$ (-50°C)** are the Martensite Start and Finish temperatures. Martensitic transformation is **athermal** — it occurs essentially **instantaneously** by a diffusionless shear mechanism, hence the horizontal lines (not curves).

### 2.4 The Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)
The **Critical Cooling Rate** is the **minimum rate of cooling** required to **bypass the nose of the C-curve** and avoid the formation of pearlite or bainite, thereby producing **100% martensite**.

$$\text{CCR} = \frac{A_{1} - M_s}{t_{\text{nose}}}$$

For eutectoid steel: $\text{CCR} = \frac{727 - 215}{0.7} \approx 730 \text{ °C/s}$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Avrami Equation (General)** | $Y = 1 - \exp(-kt^n)$ | Fraction transformed ($Y$) vs time ($t$); $k$, $n$ are temperature-dependent constants | Dimensionless |
| **Critical Cooling Rate** | $\text{CCR} = \frac{T_{A_1} - T_{M_s}}{t_{\text{nose}}}$ | Minimum cooling rate to form 100% martensite | °C/s |
| **Cooling Rate** | $\text{CR} = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$ | Practical heat-transfer rate | °C/s |
| **Lever Rule (Microstructure)** | $\% \text{Phase} = \frac{\text{Opposite arm of tie line}}{\text{Total tie line length}} \times 100$ | Determines phase percentages in phase diagrams | % |
| **Pearlite Interlamellar Spacing** | $S = S_0 \cdot e^{-Q/RT}$ | Lamellar spacing decreases with undercooling ($\Delta T$) | nm |
| **Hardness of Pearlite** | $H \approx \frac{k}{S}$ | Hardness is inversely proportional to interlamellar spacing | HRC / HV |
| **Nose Position** | $T_{\text{nose}} \approx 0.55 \cdot T_{A_1}$ | Approximate nose temperature (Kelvin) | K |
| **Ms Temperature (Empirical)** | $M_s \approx 539 - 423(\%C) - 30.4(\%Mn) - 17.7(\%Ni) - 12.1(\%Cr) - 7.5(\%Mo)$ | Andrews' empirical formula for Martensite Start | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** TTT diagrams are the **backbone of industrial heat treatment**. They allow metallurgists to design processes such as **austempering** (isothermal hold in bainite region to produce bainitic steel rails, high-strength wires) and **patenting** (used in wire rope manufacturing). Without TTT diagrams, modern automotive gears, railway tracks, and cutting tools could not be reliably manufactured.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Cooling Rate for a Plain Carbon Eutectoid Steel

**Given Data:**
- Eutectoid temperature: $T_{A_1} = 727°C$
- Martensite start temperature: $T_{M_s} = 215°C$
- Nose time (incubation period at nose temperature): $t_{\text{nose}} = 0.7 \text{ s}$

**Step 1:** Write the standard expression for the Critical Cooling Rate.

The Critical Cooling Rate is the slope of the cooling line (°C/s) that just grazes the nose of the C-curve. Geometrically, it is the ratio of the temperature drop (from $A_1$ to $M_s$) to the available time (the nose time).

$$\text{CCR} = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t} = \frac{T_{A_1} - T_{M_s}}{t_{\text{nose}}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values into the numerator.

$$T_{A_1} - T_{M_s} = 727°C - 215°C = 512°C$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the denominator.

$$t_{\text{nose}} = 0.7 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4:** Perform the division.

$$\text{CCR} = \frac{512°C}{0.7 \text{ s}}$$

$$\text{CCR} = 731.43 \text{ °C/s}$$

**Step 5:** State the result in engineering form.

$$\boxed{\text{CCR} \approx 730 \text{ °C/s}}$$

**Interpretation:** A eutectoid plain carbon steel must be cooled at a rate **faster than ~730 °C/s** from 727°C down to room temperature to **completely suppress** the nose of the C-curve and form **100% martensite**. This explains why plain carbon steels require **brine or caustic soda quenching** (very fast cooling), whereas alloy steels with shifted noses can be oil-quenched.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Fraction of Transformation Using the Avrami Equation

**Given Data:**
- Constants for a specific isothermal hold: $k = 0.005 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $n = 2$
- Isothermal hold time: $t = 100 \text{ s}$

**Step 1:** State the Avrami (Johnson-Mehl) equation for isothermal transformation kinetics.

$$Y = 1 - \exp(-k \cdot t^n)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the exponent $k \cdot t^n$ with the given numerical values.

$$k \cdot t^n = 0.005 \times (100)^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute $t^n$.

$$(100)^2 = 10{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $k$.

$$k \cdot t^n = 0.005 \times 10{,}000 = 50$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the Avrami equation.

$$Y = 1 - \exp(-50)$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the exponential term.

$$\exp(-50) \approx 1.93 \times 10^{-22} \approx 0$$

**Step 7:** State the result.

$$\boxed{Y \approx 1 \text{ (or } 100\% \text{ transformed)}}$$

**Interpretation:** After 100 seconds of isothermal holding under the given conditions, **100% of the austenite has transformed**. This confirms that the Avrami equation models **S-shaped transformation kinetics** — slow start, rapid mid-stage, and a saturation plateau at Y = 1.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Transformation Products in a TTT Diagram

| Parameter | Pearlite (Upper C-Curve) | Bainite (Lower C-Curve) | Martensite (Horizontal Line) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formation Temperature** | 727°C – 550°C | 550°C – 250°C (or $M_s$) | Below $M_s$ (215°C) |
| **Mechanism** | Diffusional (C diffusion over long distances) | Diffusional (limited C diffusion, shear component) | Diffusionless (displacive shear) |
| **Crystal Structure** | BCC Ferrite + Orthorhombic $Fe_3C$ | BCC Ferrite + Carbide ($\varepsilon$ or cementite) | BCT (Body-Centered Tetragonal) |
| **Morphology** | Lamellar plates (alternating $\alpha$ + $Fe_3C$) | Acicular / feathery needles | Lath (low C) or Plate (high C) |
| **Nucleation & Growth** | Heterogeneous at grain boundaries, grows as colonies | Intra-granular nucleation, sheaf-like growth | Instantaneous, no growth (athermal) |
| **Time Dependence** | Strong (seconds to hours) | Strong (seconds to minutes) | Negligible (instantaneous) |
| **Hardness (HRC)** | 20 (coarse) – 40 (fine) | 40 – 60 | 60 – 65 (max hardness of steel) |
| **Toughness** | High (ductile) | Moderate | Very low (brittle) |
| **Industrial Use** | Rails, structural beams, fasteners | Springs, high-strength wire, automotive gears | Cutting tools, dies, bearings (after tempering) |
| **Heat Treatment Process** | Annealing, Normalizing | Austempering | Quenching + Tempering |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TTT Diagram Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Austenitize Steel Specimen at 760 to 800 °C] --> A2[Step 2: Hold Until Full Austenite Formation]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Rapid Quench to Constant Isothermal Bath Temperature T below 727 °C]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Hold for Variable Time t at Constant T]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Withdraw Specimen and Quench in Water to Freeze Microstructure]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Metallographic Examination Under Microscope]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Determine Percentage Transformed at Each Time t]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Plot Time versus Temperature on Semi Log Paper]
    A8 --> A9[Output: TTT Diagram with C Curves and Ms Mf Lines]
    A9 --> B1[Decision: Below Nose Temperature?]
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Formation of Martensite via Quenching]
    B1 -- No --> B3[Formation of Bainite or Pearlite via Isothermal Hold]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Flow: Microstructure Selection Strategy Using TTT Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hot Austenite at 760 C] --> P2{Choose Cooling Path}
    P2 -- Very Slow Furnace Cool --> P3[Coarse Pearlite Soft Ductile]
    P2 -- Air Cool Normalizing --> P4[Fine Pearlite Moderate Strength]
    P2 -- Isothermal Hold in Bainite Region --> P5[Bainite Tough Strong]
    P2 -- Rapid Quench Brine Water --> P6[Martensite Hard Brittle]
    P6 --> P7[Tempering Treatment]
    P7 --> P8[Tempered Martensite Hard and Tough]
```

### 4.3 Phase Transformation Product Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Austenite at High Temperature] --> S2[Quench to Isothermal Hold Temperature]
    S2 --> S3{Temperature Region}
    S3 -- 727 to 550 C --> S4[Pearlite Region: Diffusional Growth of Lamellar Structure]
    S3 -- 550 to 250 C --> S5[Bainite Region: Limited Diffusion Shear Mechanism]
    S3 -- Below 215 C --> S6[Martensite Region: Diffusionless Instantaneous Shear]
    S4 --> R1[Product: Alpha Ferrite plus Fe3C Lamellae]
    S5 --> R2[Product: Acicular Bainite Sheaves]
    S6 --> R3[Product: BCT Martensite Plates or Laths]
    R1 --> E1[Soft Ductile Low Strength]
    R2 --> E2[High Strength Moderate Ductility]
    R3 --> E3[Very High Hardness Brittle Requires Tempering]
```

### 4.4 Critical Cooling Rate Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Begin Quench from 727 C] --> Q2{Cooling Rate vs CCR}
    Q2 -- Slower than CCR --> Q3[Crosses Nose: Forms Pearlite plus Bainite plus Ferrite]
    Q2 -- Exactly at CCR --> Q4[Grazes Nose: Marginal Martensite with Some Pearlite]
    Q2 -- Faster than CCR --> Q5[Bypasses Nose: 100 percent Martensite Formation]
    Q3 --> Z1[Soft Mixed Microstructure]
    Q4 --> Z2[Partially Hardened Microstructure]
    Q5 --> Z3[Fully Hardened Brittle Martensite Requiring Tempering]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw a TTT diagram" or "show the cooling curve," always label: (i) the $A_1$ line at 727°C, (ii) the $M_s$ at ~215°C and $M_f$ at ~(-50°C), (iii) three C-curves (start, 50%, finish), and (iv) clearly show whether the cooling curve **bypasses** or **intersects** the nose.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a T-T-T diagram. Why is the time axis plotted on a logarithmic scale?**

**Model Answer:**
A Time-Temperature-Transformation (T-T-T) diagram is an isothermal transformation diagram that plots, on a semi-logarithmic scale, the time required for austenite to begin and finish its transformation into various products (pearlite, bainite, martensite) at a constant sub-critical temperature. 

The time axis is plotted on a **logarithmic scale** because the transformation times span an enormous range — from fractions of a second (at the nose of the C-curve) to many hours (at temperatures just below $A_1$). A linear scale would compress all useful detail near the origin and make the diagram unreadable. A logarithmic scale expands the short-time region and compresses the long-time region, giving a clear, balanced C-curve shape.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for identifying isothermal nature, 1 for justification of log scale]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the "nose" of the TTT diagram? What is its engineering significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The **nose of the TTT diagram** is the leftmost protruding point of the C-shaped transformation curve, located at approximately **550°C** for eutectoid plain carbon steel, corresponding to a nose time of roughly **0.7 seconds**. It represents the temperature of **maximum instability of austenite** — i.e., the temperature at which the incubation time for transformation is the **shortest**.

**Engineering significance:** The nose determines the **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** required to form 100% martensite. To obtain a fully martensitic structure, the cooling curve must pass to the **left** of the nose without intersecting it. The further the nose is shifted to the right (e.g., by adding alloying elements like Ni, Cr, Mo), the slower the cooling rate that can be used, which is why alloy steels can be hardened in oil instead of water.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for location, 1 for CCR significance]**

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat labeled sketch, explain the construction of a TTT diagram for a eutectoid plain carbon steel. Label all critical temperatures, transformation regions, and microstructure zones. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A eutectoid steel specimen is austenitized at 760°C and then isothermally held at 600°C. Using a standard TTT diagram, determine:
  (i) The microstructure obtained after holding for 100 seconds and quenching to room temperature.  
  (ii) The approximate hardness (HRC) of the resulting structure.  
  (iii) The percentage of transformation if the specimen is held for only 10 seconds. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Construction Steps [7 Marks]:**
1. Cut small specimens (typically 1–2 cm) of eutectoid steel (0.77% C).
2. Austenitize in a furnace at 760°C–800°C for sufficient time (e.g., 30 min) to obtain 100% austenite.
3. Quench rapidly into a **molten salt bath** held at a constant sub-critical temperature (e.g., 700°C, 650°C, 600°C, 500°C, 400°C, 300°C, etc.).
4. Hold isothermally for different times (1 s, 10 s, 100 s, 1000 s, 10000 s).
5. After each time interval, withdraw a specimen and **water-quench** to freeze the microstructure.
6. Polish, etch, and examine under a metallurgical microscope.
7. Identify the **start (1%)**, **mid (50%)**, and **finish (99%)** of transformation by point counting or comparison charts.
8. Plot the time (log scale, x-axis) vs temperature (y-axis) and connect the points to form the characteristic C-curves.
9. Mark $M_s$ (~215°C) and $M_f$ (~-50°C) as horizontal lines.
10. Label the **pearlite region** (upper C), **bainite region** (lower C), and **austenite region** (above the curves).

**Part (b) — Analysis at 600°C [7 Marks]:**
**(i)** At 600°C, holding for 100 s intersects the pearlite region well past the finish curve. Hence the structure obtained is **100% fine pearlite** (since 600°C is in the upper-to-middle pearlite range).

**(ii)** The hardness of fine pearlite at 600°C is approximately **35–40 HRC** (≈ 250–300 HB).

**(iii)** For a 10-second hold at 600°C: Reading vertically at $T = 600°C$ and $t = 10 \text{ s}$, the point lies **just past the start curve** and **before the 50% curve**. The fraction transformed is approximately **5–15%** (use linear interpolation: at 1 s → 0%, at ~30 s → 50%, so at 10 s the fraction is approximately 15%). 

The microstructure at this point consists of a **mixture of fine pearlite (≈15%) and untransformed austenite (≈85%)** at high temperature, which then transforms partially to martensite upon final water quenching. Thus the final room-temperature microstructure is **~15% fine pearlite + ~85% martensite** (assuming the retained austenite fully transforms during the final quench).

---

**Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Differentiate between TTT and CCT (Continuous Cooling Transformation) diagrams. Which is more practically useful for industrial heat treatment, and why? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 0.77% C steel is cooled continuously from 800°C to room temperature at a rate of 200°C/s. Will you obtain 100% martensite? Justify your answer with calculations and a TTT diagram sketch showing the cooling path. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — TTT vs CCT Comparison [7 Marks]:**

| Feature | TTT (Isothermal) | CCT (Continuous Cooling) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cooling Mode** | Quench to constant T, then hold | Continuous decrease in T over time |
| **Path on Diagram** | Horizontal line (constant T) | Curved descending line |
| **Construction Time** | Long (many isothermal holds needed) | Short (single dilatometric run) |
| **Transformation Nose** | Sharper, well-defined | Shifted **right and downward** (delayed transformation) |
| **Practical Use** | Theoretical, design of isothermal processes (austempering, patenting) | Industrial heat treatment (quenching, normalizing) |
| **Martensite Formation** | Occurs at $M_s$ after holding above $M_s$ | Occurs if cooling curve crosses $M_s$ |
| **Alloying Effect** | Direct | Indirect (CCT curves are derived from TTT + cooling rate) |

**CCT is more practically useful** because industrial processes (water quenching, oil quenching, air cooling) involve **continuous cooling**, not isothermal holding. CCT diagrams incorporate the **thermal lag** of continuous cooling, so they predict microstructures (e.g., martensite + pearlite mixtures) more accurately for real processes. TTT diagrams are foundational but CCT diagrams are the **engineering working tool** in any heat-treatment shop.

**Part (b) — Cooling Rate Analysis [7 Marks]:**
Given: Initial temperature $T_i = 800°C$, final $T_f = 25°C$, cooling rate = **200°C/s**.

**Step 1:** Calculate the time taken to cool from 800°C to $M_s$ (215°C).

$$\Delta T = 800 - 215 = 585°C$$

$$t = \frac{585}{200} = 2.925 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Read the TTT diagram at 550°C (nose temperature) for the time available.

At the nose temperature (550°C), the cooling curve passes through:

$$t_{\text{at nose}} = \frac{800 - 550}{200} = \frac{250}{200} = 1.25 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the nose time.

The nose of the standard eutectoid TTT diagram occurs at ~**0.7 s** at 550°C. Since the available time (**1.25 s**) is **greater than the nose time (0.7 s)**, the cooling curve **CROSSES the nose** of the C-curve.

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

The cooling rate of 200°C/s is **less than the critical cooling rate of 730°C/s** (derived earlier). Therefore, **100% martensite will NOT be obtained**. The microstructure will be a **mixture of fine pearlite (formed when crossing the nose) and martensite (formed in the regions that escaped the nose)**. Specifically, the fraction transformed to pearlite can be estimated from the intersection point — typically **30–50% pearlite + 50–70% martensite** depending on the exact curve geometry.

**Final Answer:** No, 100% martensite is not obtained. A **mixed microstructure of pearlite + martensite** forms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cooling rate to time at the nose temperature** — students often compare 200°C/s directly with 730°C/s without computing the actual crossing time.
> 2. **Drawing the cooling curve as a straight line on the log-scale** — it must be a *curve* because log scale distorts linear cooling.
> 3. **Confusing $M_s$ and $M_f$** — $M_s$ is the start of martensite, $M_f$ is the finish; only cooling **below $M_f$** gives 100% martensite.
> 4. **Not labeling the nose** — examiners explicitly look for the nose marking, as it represents the **physical bottleneck** of the transformation.
> 5. **Mixing up TTT and CCT diagrams** — a very common error; remember, the **CCT nose is shifted right** (longer time) compared to the TTT nose, so a cooling curve that grazes the TTT nose may still bypass the CCT nose.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **T-T-T Diagram** plots **log(time) vs Temperature** to predict the isothermal decomposition of **austenite** into pearlite, bainite, or martensite.
- The diagram exhibits a characteristic **"C-shape"** with three curves: 1% (start), 50%, and 99% (finish) transformation.
- The **nose of the C-curve** is at ~**550°C, 0.7 s** for eutectoid steel and represents the **fastest transformation kinetics**.
- **Pearlite** forms in the **upper region** (727°C – 550°C) by **diffusional, lamellar growth**.
- **Bainite** forms in the **intermediate region** (550°C – 250°C) by a **mixed diffusion–shear mechanism**.
- **Martensite** forms **athermally** (diffusionless shear) below $M_s$ (**~215°C**) as a horizontal line on the diagram.
- The **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** for eutectoid steel is ~**730 °C/s** — anything slower produces pearlite/bainite mixtures.
- **Alloying elements** (Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn) shift the **C-curve to the right**, making the steel **hardenable** at slower cooling rates (oil or air quench).
- **TTT → CCT shift:** Continuous cooling diagrams have noses shifted **right and down**, making it **easier to form martensite** in practice than TTT diagrams suggest.
- The **Avrami equation** $Y = 1 - \exp(-kt^n)$ models isothermal transformation kinetics.
- **Empirical formula** for $M_s$ (Andrews): $M_s = 539 - 423(\%C) - 30.4(\%Mn) - 17.7(\%Ni) - 12.1(\%Cr) - 7.5(\%Mo)$.
- **Industrial applications:** Austempering (bainite), patenting (wire), quenching & tempering (martensite), normalizing (fine pearlite).
- **For KTU exams:** Always (i) draw a **neat, labeled sketch** with axes, $A_1$, $M_s$, $M_f$, three C-curves, and the cooling path, (ii) state the **CCR numerically**, and (iii) identify the **microstructure** with its **approximate hardness**.
- **Hardness order:** Martensite (60–65 HRC) > Bainite (40–60 HRC) > Fine Pearlite (30–40 HRC) > Coarse Pearlite (20 HRC).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Deformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plastic Deformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plastic deformation** is the *permanent, irreversible, and time-independent change in shape of a solid material* that occurs when the applied stress exceeds the **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)** of the material. Unlike elastic deformation, plastic deformation is **non-recoverable** upon removal of the load, governed primarily by the movement of crystalline defects known as **dislocations** along specific crystallographic planes and directions (collectively termed *slip systems*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Plastic deformation is the irreversible deformation that remains after the load is removed. It occurs by the motion of dislocations (slip) or by mechanical twinning once the critical resolved shear stress is exceeded."

The phenomenon can be quantified using the uniaxial strain:

$$\varepsilon_p = \frac{l_f - l_0}{l_0}$$

where $l_f$ is the final length after load removal and $l_0$ is the original gauge length.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **deck of playing cards** resting on a table:

- **Elastic deformation** is like gently pressing down on the top of the deck — the cards compress microscopically, but once you remove your hand, they snap back to a perfect rectangle.
- **Plastic deformation** is like sliding the top card *relative* to the rest of the deck by pushing it sideways. The cards have now permanently shifted — they will **not** snap back, even if you remove the force.

This sideways "card sliding" is exactly what happens at the atomic scale inside a metal: entire planes of atoms glide over one another. The "cards" are **atomic planes**, and the "sliding force" is the **resolved shear stress** along the slip direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Insight:** Almost every metal-shaping process — rolling, forging, extrusion, deep drawing, and wire drawing — exploits plastic deformation deliberately. The amount of energy required is dramatically lower than fracturing the material, because dislocations move millions of times more easily than whole planes of atoms at once.

---

## 1.3 The Two Principal Mechanisms

Plastic deformation in crystalline solids proceeds predominantly through **two mechanisms**:

| Mechanism | Driving Force | Atomic Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Slip** | Critical Resolved Shear Stress (**CRSS**, $\tau_{CRSS}$) | Translation of one part of the crystal over another along a slip plane in a slip direction |
| **Twinning** | Shear stress above a critical value | Atomic planes reorient symmetrically about a *twin plane*, producing a mirror-image region |

> [!TIP]
> **Slip dominates in BCC and FCC metals.** Twinning is especially important in **HCP metals** (e.g., Mg, Zn, Ti) at low temperatures and in BCC metals (e.g., $\alpha$-Fe) under high strain rates or shock loading.

---

## 1.4 Plastic Deformation in the Stress-Strain Framework

The stress-strain curve positions plastic deformation between two critical landmarks:

$$\sigma_{elastic\ limit} < \sigma_y < \sigma_{UTS}$$

- Below $\sigma_y$: **Elastic regime** — Hooke's Law holds ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$).
- At/above $\sigma_y$: **Plastic regime** — dislocations multiply and move; the material work-hardens.
- Beyond **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**: Necking and eventual fracture.

The onset of plastic flow is therefore governed by the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)**, denoted $\tau_{CRSS}$, which is the minimum shear stress on the slip plane (resolved along the slip direction) required to initiate slip.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dislocations — The Carriers of Plastic Flow

Plastic deformation does **not** occur by simultaneously breaking all atomic bonds across a slip plane (the theoretical shear strength is $\sim G/30$, where $G$ is the shear modulus — a value 100 to 10,000 times higher than what is experimentally observed). Instead, plasticity is mediated by **line defects** called **dislocations**.

### 2.1.1 Edge Dislocation

An **edge dislocation** can be visualized as an extra half-plane of atoms inserted into the crystal lattice. The line defect runs along the bottom edge of this half-plane. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is perpendicular to the dislocation line.

- **Extra half-plane above** → **Positive edge dislocation** ($\perp$ symbol).
- **Extra half-plane below** → **Negative edge dislocation** ($\top$ symbol).

### 2.1.2 Screw Dislocation

A **screw dislocation** results from a shear distortion where atomic planes are displaced progressively by one Burgers vector parallel to the dislocation line. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to the dislocation line.

### 2.1.3 Mixed Dislocation

In real crystals, dislocations are almost always **mixed**, having both edge and screw character along their length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Burgers vector $\vec{b}$** is the fundamental measure of lattice distortion. Its magnitude is the atomic slip step per dislocation event.

---

## 2.2 Slip Systems — Geometry of Plasticity

A **slip system** is the combination of:

- A **slip plane** — the crystallographic plane of highest atomic density (lowest packing resistance).
- A **slip direction** — the crystallographic direction within the slip plane of highest linear density.

The number of independent slip systems directly controls **ductility**:

| Crystal Structure | Slip Plane | Slip Direction | Slip Systems | Typical Ductility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCC** (Cu, Al, Ni) | $\{111\}$ | $\langle 110 \rangle$ | **12** | Highly ductile |
| **BCC** ($\alpha$-Fe, Cr) | $\{110\}, \{112\}, \{123\}$ | $\langle 111 \rangle$ | **48** | Ductile |
| **HCP** (Mg, Zn, Ti) | **Basal:** $\{0001\}$ | $\langle 11\bar{2}0 \rangle$ | **3** (basal only) | Limited ductility |

---

## 2.3 Schmid's Law — The Trigger Condition for Slip

A uniaxial tensile stress $\sigma$ applied along an arbitrary direction will produce a *resolved* shear stress on any given slip plane. Slip initiates when this resolved shear stress first reaches the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress ($\tau_{CRSS}$)** of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schmid's Law (1924):**
> $$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \, \cos\phi \, \cos\lambda$$
> Slip begins when $\tau_{RSS} \geq \tau_{CRSS}$.

Here:
- $\phi$ = angle between the **tensile axis** and the **normal to the slip plane**.
- $\lambda$ = angle between the **tensile axis** and the **slip direction**.

The product $\cos\phi \cos\lambda$ is called the **Schmid factor** $m$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{RSS} = m \, \sigma\;} \quad \text{where} \quad m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_p = (l_f - l_0)/l_0$ | True plastic strain after unloading | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ | Schmid's Law — resolved shear stress | **MPa** or **psi** |
| 3 | $m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ | Schmid factor (max = **0.5** at $\phi = \lambda = 45^{\circ}$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma_y = \tau_{CRSS} / m$ | Yield stress for a single crystal orientation | **MPa** |
| 5 | $\tau_{theoretical} \approx G/30$ | Theoretical shear strength (no dislocations) | **MPa** |
| 6 | $\tau_{experimental} \approx G/10^4$ | Observed CRSS (with dislocations) | **MPa** |
| 7 | $\rho_d = N / V$ | Dislocation density (number per unit volume) | $\text{m}^{-2}$ |
| 8 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \, G \, b \, \sqrt{\rho_d}$ | Taylor's strengthening relation | **MPa** |
| 9 | $b = \lVert \vec{b} \rVert = a\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}/2$ | Magnitude of Burgers vector for cubic crystals | **nm** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Schmid's Law is the foundation of single-crystal superalloy design for **jet engine turbine blades**, where specific grain orientations are deliberately chosen to maximize $m$ (for ductility) or minimize $m$ (for creep resistance).

---

## 2.5 Strain (Work) Hardening

As plastic deformation proceeds, dislocations multiply and entangle. The increased dislocation density $\rho_d$ blocks further dislocation motion, raising the stress required for continued deformation — this is **strain hardening** (or **work hardening**).

Empirically, for many metals in the plastic region:

$$\sigma = K \, \varepsilon^n$$

where $K$ is the **strength coefficient** and $n$ is the **strain hardening exponent** ($0 < n < 1$). The Taylor equation links this to dislocation density:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \alpha \, G \, b \, \sqrt{\rho_d}$$

with $\alpha \approx 0.3 - 0.5$ as a constant.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Schmid's Law

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Geometry

Consider a cylindrical single-crystal specimen of original cross-sectional area $A_0$ subjected to a uniaxial tensile load $F$ applied along the rod axis (the **tensile axis**).

Within the crystal, choose:
- A **slip plane** with unit normal $\hat{n}$ making angle $\phi$ with the tensile axis.
- A **slip direction** (unit vector $\hat{s}$) lying in the slip plane, making angle $\lambda$ with the tensile axis.

The two angles are constrained by the plane-and-direction geometry: $\phi \perp \hat{n}$ and $\hat{s} \perp \hat{n}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Resolve the applied force into the slip direction.**

The applied axial force is $F = \sigma \, A_0$. The component of this force acting *along* the slip direction is found by projecting onto $\hat{s}$:

$$F_s = F \cos\lambda = \sigma \, A_0 \cos\lambda$$

**Step 2 — Determine the area of the slip plane.**

The slip plane is tilted at angle $\phi$ to the cross-section normal (which is parallel to the tensile axis). Geometrically, the slip plane has a larger projected area:

$$A_{slip} = \frac{A_0}{\cos\phi}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the resolved shear stress.**

The resolved shear stress $\tau_{RSS}$ is the shear traction acting on the slip plane in the slip direction:

$$\tau_{RSS} = \frac{F_s}{A_{slip}}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$\tau_{RSS} = \frac{\sigma \, A_0 \cos\lambda}{A_0 / \cos\phi}$$

The $A_0$ terms cancel:

$$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \, \cos\lambda \, \cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — Define the Schmid factor.**

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \, m \quad \text{where} \quad m = \cos\phi \, \cos\lambda\;}$$

**Step 5 — Slip initiation criterion.**

Slip begins the instant the resolved shear stress reaches the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress ($\tau_{CRSS}$)**:

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{\cos\phi \, \cos\lambda}$$

This is the **single-crystal yield stress** for the given orientation.

### 3.1.3 Maximum Value of the Schmid Factor

To find the orientation that yields slip *most easily*, maximize $m$:

$$\frac{dm}{d\phi} = \frac{d}{d\phi}\left(\cos\phi \cos\lambda\right) = 0$$

Using the constraint that $\hat{s}$ lies in the slip plane, $\lambda = 90^{\circ} - \phi$ is **not** generally true — $\lambda$ is *independent* of $\phi$ unless specified otherwise. Maximizing each term independently:

$$\cos\phi = \cos\lambda = 1 \implies \phi = \lambda = 0^{\circ}$$

However, geometric consistency ($\hat{s} \perp \hat{n}$) requires that $\phi + \lambda \geq 90^{\circ}$. The maximum occurs at:

$$\phi = \lambda = 45^{\circ} \implies m_{\max} = \cos 45^{\circ} \cos 45^{\circ} = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2}$$

Therefore: **$\tau_{RSS,max} = \sigma / 2$** — the applied stress can produce at most half its magnitude as resolved shear stress on any slip system.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Schmid Factor for a Single Orientation

A copper single crystal ($\tau_{CRSS} = 0.7$ **MPa**) is pulled in tension. The slip plane normal makes $\phi = 60^{\circ}$ with the tensile axis, and the slip direction makes $\lambda = 30^{\circ}$ with the tensile axis.

**Step A — Calculate the Schmid factor.**

$$m = \cos\phi \, \cos\lambda = \cos 60^{\circ} \, \cos 30^{\circ} = (0.500)(0.866) = 0.433$$

**Step B — Calculate the yield stress.**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m} = \frac{0.7\ \text{MPa}}{0.433} = 1.617\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step C — Calculate the maximum possible resolved shear stress at this orientation.**

$$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \, m$$

If the applied stress were, say, $\sigma = 5$ **MPa**:

$$\tau_{RSS} = 5 \times 0.433 = 2.165\ \text{MPa}$$

Since $\tau_{RSS} = 2.165\ \text{MPa} > \tau_{CRSS} = 0.7\ \text{MPa}$, **slip will occur**.

---

### Example 2: Orientation at Maximum Schmid Factor

A zinc (HCP) crystal ($\tau_{CRSS} = 0.18$ **MPa** on the basal plane) is oriented such that the basal plane normal is at $45^{\circ}$ to the tensile axis and a slip direction in the basal plane is also at $45^{\circ}$.

**Step A — Calculate $m$.**

$$m = \cos 45^{\circ} \, \cos 45^{\circ} = 0.5$$

**Step B — Calculate the yield stress.**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{0.18\ \text{MPa}}{0.5} = 0.36\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step C — Interpretation.**

This is the *minimum* applied stress needed for slip — the orientation is "optimally soft".

---

### Example 3: Effect of Dislocation Density on Yield Strength

An annealed copper specimen has $\rho_{d1} = 10^{10}\ \text{m}^{-2}$. After cold working, $\rho_{d2} = 10^{14}\ \text{m}^{-2}$. Take $\sigma_0 = 25$ **MPa**, $k = 0.4$, $G = 48$ **GPa**, $b = 0.256$ **nm** $= 2.56 \times 10^{-10}$ **m**.

**Step A — Yield strength before cold working.**

$$\sigma_{y1} = 25 + (0.4)(48 \times 10^9)(2.56 \times 10^{-10})\sqrt{10^{10}}$$

$$\sigma_{y1} = 25 + (4.915)\sqrt{10^{10}} = 25 + (4.915)(10^5) = 25 + 4.915 \times 10^{-3} \approx 25\ \text{MPa}$$

(The dislocation contribution is negligible at low $\rho_d$.)

**Step B — Yield strength after cold working.**

$$\sigma_{y2} = 25 + (4.915)\sqrt{10^{14}} = 25 + (4.915)(10^7) = 25 + 49.15 \approx 74.15\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step C — Conclusion.**

Cold working has tripled the yield strength by dramatically raising dislocation density — the foundational principle of **work hardening**.

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of plastic deformation, showing the chain of physical events from external loading to permanent shape change and the feedback loop of work hardening.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)
