---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`

**Define plastic deformation. Mention the two principal mechanisms by which it occurs in crystalline solids.**

**Model Answer:**

Plastic deformation is the *permanent and irreversible* change in shape of a material that remains after the applied load is removed (i.e., after the load drops below the yield stress). It occurs in crystalline solids by two principal mechanisms:

1. **Slip** — translation of one part of the crystal over another along specific crystallographic planes and directions (slip systems), driven by the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)**.
2. **Mechanical Twinning** — a coordinated shear that produces a region of the crystal which is a mirror image of the parent across a *twin plane*.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Distinguish between slip and twinning as mechanisms of plastic deformation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Slip | Twinning |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic movement | Uniform translation by integer multiples of interatomic spacing | Coordinated, fractional displacement producing mirror-image region |
| Plane involved | Slip plane (specific crystallographic) | Twin plane |
| Orientation change | No change in lattice orientation | Reorientation of twinned region |
| Stress required | CRSS on slip plane | Shear stress above a critical value |
| Preferred crystal | FCC, BCC metals | HCP metals (Mg, Zn), BCC at low T |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Module-level internal choice**. Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Answer ANY ONE full question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** State and derive **Schmid's Law** for the critical resolved shear stress in a single crystal. A zinc single crystal is deformed in tension such that the basal plane normal is $60^{\circ}$ from the tensile axis and the slip direction is $40^{\circ}$ from the tensile axis. Given $\tau_{CRSS} = 0.18$ MPa, compute the **Schmid factor** and the **yield stress**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **dislocations** with neat sketches of edge and screw dislocations. Define the **Burgers vector** and discuss its significance. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

### (a) Schmid's Law — Statement, Derivation, and Numerical Solution

**Statement of Schmid's Law:**
When a single crystal is subjected to an external tensile stress $\sigma$ along some axis, the resolved shear stress on a given slip system is:

$$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \, \cos\phi \, \cos\lambda$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between the tensile axis and the normal to the slip plane, and $\lambda$ is the angle between the tensile axis and the slip direction. Slip begins when $\tau_{RSS}$ first reaches the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress ($\tau_{CRSS}$)** of the material.

**Derivation:**

*Step 1:* Applied force on the cross-section of area $A_0$:
$$F = \sigma \, A_0$$

*Step 2:* Component of force along the slip direction (angle $\lambda$):
$$F_s = F \cos\lambda = \sigma \, A_0 \cos\lambda$$

*Step 3:* Area of the inclined slip plane (angle $\phi$ between plane normal and tensile axis):
$$A_{slip} = \frac{A_0}{\cos\phi}$$

*Step 4:* Resolved shear stress:
$$\tau_{RSS} = \frac{F_s}{A_{slip}} = \frac{\sigma \, A_0 \cos\lambda}{A_0 / \cos\phi} = \sigma \, \cos\phi \, \cos\lambda$$

This completes the derivation. Slip initiates when $\tau_{RSS} \geq \tau_{CRSS}$.

**Numerical Solution:**

Given $\phi = 60^{\circ}$, $\lambda = 40^{\circ}$, $\tau_{CRSS} = 0.18$ MPa.

*Step A — Schmid factor:*
$$m = \cos 60^{\circ} \, \cos 40^{\circ} = (0.500)(0.766) = 0.383$$

*Step B — Yield stress:*
$$\sigma_y = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m} = \frac{0.18}{0.383} = 0.470\ \text{MPa}$$

---

### (b) Dislocations — Concept, Sketches, and Burgers Vector

**Definition:**
A **dislocation** is a *line defect* in the crystal lattice along which the atomic arrangement is misaligned. Dislocations are responsible for plastic deformation at stresses orders of magnitude lower than the theoretical shear strength.

**Edge Dislocation:**
Visualized as an **extra half-plane of atoms** inserted into the lattice. The bottom edge of this extra half-plane forms the dislocation line. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line and represents the magnitude and direction of lattice distortion.

**Screw Dislocation:**
Formed by progressive shear displacement of atomic planes. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to the dislocation line. The lattice is twisted into a helical ramp around the line.

**Burgers Vector ($\vec{b}$):**
The Burgers vector is constructed by drawing a closed loop of atomic steps (the Burgers circuit) around the dislocation in the *perfect* crystal and comparing it to the corresponding loop around the dislocation in the *defective* crystal. The closure failure is the Burgers vector $\vec{b}$.

**Significance:**
- $\vec{b}$ characterizes the magnitude of lattice distortion.
- Its magnitude equals the atomic slip distance per dislocation glide event.
- The direction of $\vec{b}$ defines the slip direction.
- For FCC metals, $\lVert \vec{b} \rVert = a/\sqrt{2}$ where $a$ is the lattice constant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define **slip** and **slip system**. Tabulate the slip systems for **FCC**, **BCC**, and **HCP** crystal structures with an example for each. Why are HCP metals generally less ductile than FCC metals? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain **strain hardening (work hardening)** with the help of the **stress-strain curve** and the **Taylor equation**. A copper wire is cold-drawn such that its dislocation density increases from $10^{11}\ \text{m}^{-2}$ to $5 \times 10^{14}\ \text{m}^{-2}$. Estimate the increase in yield strength. Take $G = 48$ GPa, $b = 2.56 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $\alpha = 0.4$, $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

### (a) Slip, Slip Systems, and Ductility

**Slip:**
Slip is the process by which one part of a crystal glides over another along a specific crystallographic plane (the **slip plane**) in a specific direction (the **slip direction**) when the resolved shear stress reaches the critical value.

**Slip System:**
A slip system = slip plane + slip direction. Both must have the highest atomic density to minimize resistance to atomic motion.

**Tabulation of Slip Systems:**

| Structure | Slip Plane | Slip Direction | No. of Slip Systems | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCC** | $\{111\}$ | $\langle 110 \rangle$ | **12** | Cu, Al, Ni, Au |
| **BCC** | $\{110\}, \{112\}, \{123\}$ | $\langle 111 \rangle$ | **48** | $\alpha$-Fe, Cr, W |
| **HCP** | $\{0001\}$ (basal) | $\langle 11\bar{2}0 \rangle$ | **3** (basal only) | Mg, Zn, Ti |

**Why HCP is less ductile:**
For a polycrystal to deform plastically without cracking at grain boundaries, **at least 5 independent slip systems** are required (von Mises criterion). HCP metals have only **3 basal slip systems** at room temperature — insufficient for general plastic strain. They must rely on additional prismatic/pyramidal systems, which require much higher thermal activation. FCC metals, with **12 slip systems**, easily accommodate arbitrary strain at the grain level, hence their superior ductility.

---

### (b) Strain Hardening and Taylor Equation

**Concept:**
Strain (or work) hardening is the phenomenon in which a metal becomes stronger and harder as it is plastically deformed. On the stress-strain curve, this appears as the rising portion **between the yield point and the UTS**.

**Mechanism:**
During plastic deformation, dislocations multiply dramatically. Their density $\rho_d$ can rise from $\sim 10^{10}\ \text{m}^{-2}$ (annealed) to $\sim 10^{15}\ \text{m}^{-2}$ (heavily cold-worked). Dislocations interact, entangle, and obstruct each other's motion. Higher stress is then required to continue slip.

**Empirical Hollomon Relation:**
$$\sigma = K \, \varepsilon^n$$
where $K$ is the strength coefficient and $n$ is the strain hardening exponent.

**Taylor Equation (microstructural basis):**
$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \alpha \, G \, b \, \sqrt{\rho_d}$$
where $\sigma_0$ is the lattice friction stress, $\alpha \approx 0.3 - 0.5$, $G$ is the shear modulus, $b$ is the Burgers vector magnitude, and $\rho_d$ is the dislocation density.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given $\rho_{d1} = 10^{11}\ \text{m}^{-2}$, $\rho_{d2} = 5 \times 10^{14}\ \text{m}^{-2}$, $G = 48 \times 10^9$ Pa, $b = 2.56 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $\alpha = 0.4$, $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa.

*Step 1 — Initial yield strength:*
$$\sigma_{y1} = 25 + (0.4)(48 \times 10^9)(2.56 \times 10^{-10})\sqrt{10^{11}}$$
$$\sigma_{y1} = 25 + (4.915)(10^{5.5}) = 25 + 4.915 \times 3.162 \approx 25 + 15.54 \approx 40.5\ \text{MPa}$$

*Step 2 — Final yield strength:*
$$\sigma_{y2} = 25 + (4.915)\sqrt{5 \times 10^{14}}$$
$$\sigma_{y2} = 25 + (4.915)\sqrt{5} \times 10^7 = 25 + (4.915)(2.236)(10^7)$$
$$\sigma_{y2} \approx 25 + 10.99 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} = 25 + 10.99 \approx 35.99\ \text{MPa}$$

*Step 3 — Increase in yield strength:*
$$\Delta\sigma_y = \sigma_{y2} - \sigma_{y1} \approx 35.99 - 15.54 \approx 20.45\ \text{MPa}$$

**Conclusion:** Cold drawing raised the yield strength by approximately **20 MPa**, illustrating the substantial strengthening achievable through work hardening alone — no alloying or heat treatment required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\phi$ and $\lambda$:** Many students mix up the angles. Remember: $\phi$ = angle to **plane normal**; $\lambda$ = angle to **slip direction**. Both measured from the **tensile axis**.
> 2. **Forgetting the cosines in area projection:** A common error is to write $\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \sin\phi \cos\lambda$. The slip-plane area uses $\cos\phi$, not $\sin\phi$, because $\phi$ is measured to the *normal*.
> 3. **Schmid factor units:** $m$ is dimensionless. Watch out for slip-system counts in HCP — *only* basal systems are active at room temperature unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 4. **Burgers vector vs dislocation line:** Always specify whether $\vec{b}$ is *parallel* (screw) or *perpendicular* (edge) to the dislocation line. Mixed dislocations have $\vec{b}$ at an arbitrary angle.
> 5. **Taylor equation constants:** Examiners frequently deduct marks if $\alpha$, $G$, $b$, and $\rho_d$ are not converted to consistent SI units (Pa, m, $\text{m}^{-2}$) before calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these KTU high-yield points the night before the exam:**

- **Plastic deformation** = permanent, irreversible shape change beyond the elastic limit.
- **Two mechanisms:** Slip (dominant in FCC, BCC) and Twinning (dominant in HCP, BCC at low T / high strain rate).
- **Schmid's Law:** $\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda = \sigma \, m$. Slip when $\tau_{RSS} \geq \tau_{CRSS}$.
- **Schmid factor maximum:** $m_{\max} = 0.5$ when $\phi = \lambda = 45^{\circ}$, giving $\tau_{RSS,max} = \sigma/2$.
- **Single-crystal yield stress:** $\sigma_y = \tau_{CRSS} / m$.
- **Slip system count:** FCC = **12**, BCC = **48**, HCP basal = **3** (insufficient for polycrystal ductility at room T).
- **Dislocations** are the carriers of plastic flow; theoretical strength $\sim G/30$ vs observed $\sim G/10^4$.
- **Edge dislocation:** extra half-plane; $\vec{b} \perp$ line.
- **Screw dislocation:** shear spiral; $\vec{b} \parallel$ line.
- **Burgers vector** = magnitude and direction of lattice distortion; equals atomic slip step per event.
- **Strain hardening:** dislocation density rises during plastic deformation, raising yield stress.
- **Hollomon equation:** $\sigma = K \varepsilon^n$.
- **Taylor equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho_d}$.
- **Von Mises criterion:** ≥ 5 independent slip systems needed for polycrystalline ductility.
- **CRSS** is a *material property*; $\sigma_y$ is *orientation-dependent* via $m$.
- **Burgers vector magnitude (FCC):** $\lVert \vec{b} \rVert = a/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Work hardening application:** cold rolling, drawing, forging — all exploit dislocation multiplication.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Re-crystallisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Re-crystallisation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Re-crystallisation** is the phenomenon of formation of a new set of strain-free, equiaxed grains in a previously cold-worked metal when it is heated to a temperature above a critical value (the recrystallisation temperature). The new grains have low dislocation density, and they nucleate at sites of high stored strain energy (such as grain boundaries, deformation bands, and twin boundaries) and grow by consuming the surrounding deformed matrix. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** > Recrystallisation is the process whereby a plastically deformed cold-worked metal, on heating, replaces its distorted, dislocation-rich grain structure with a new, strain-free, equiaxed grain structure. The process is driven by the **stored strain energy** introduced during prior cold working. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you have a crumpled ball of paper (this represents the cold-worked metal with dislocations and distorted grains). If you gently heat the paper and apply slight pressure in a controlled manner (this represents thermal energy allowing atomic movement), the paper begins to unfold and form a smoother, flatter shape. The "memory" of the crumpled state is erased, and a more organised, lower-energy configuration is achieved. In the same way, when a cold-worked metal is heated: - The dislocations "unlock" and rearrange. - New, defect-free grains nucleate and grow. - The internal energy of the metal decreases. The crumpled paper = distorted, dislocation-rich grains. The smooth paper = new strain-free, equiaxed grains. ### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Critical Standard Metric:** > The recrystallisation temperature, $T_{recry}$, is empirically given by: > $$T_{recry} \approx 0.3 \, T_m \text{ to } 0.5 \, T_m$$ > where $T_m$ is the **melting temperature in Kelvin**. - For **pure iron (Fe)**: $T_{recry} \approx 450^\circ C$ - For **pure copper (Cu)**: $T_{recry} \approx 200^\circ C$ - For **pure aluminium (Al)**: $T_{recry} \approx 100^\circ C$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Sequential Stages of Annealing a Cold-Worked Metal When a cold-worked metal is heated isothermally above the recrystallisation temperature, three distinct microstructural stages occur sequentially: #### Stage 1 — Recovery - Occurs at **lower temperatures** (just below recrystallisation temperature). - Involves: - **Relief of internal residual stresses** - **Dislocation rearrangement** (annihilation and reorganisation into low-energy arrays like tilt boundaries) - **Slight decrease in dislocation density** - No observable change in microstructure under an optical microscope. - The metal becomes **softer** but only marginally. - Mechanical properties (yield strength, hardness) are nearly unchanged. #### Stage 2 — Recrystallisation - Occurs at **higher temperatures** (above $T_{recry}$). - New strain-free, equiaxed grains **nucleate** and **grow** until the entire deformed matrix is consumed. - Dislocation density drops to a low, equilibrium value. - Significant drop in yield strength, hardness, and an increase in ductility. #### Stage 3 — Grain Growth - Occurs **after** recrystallisation is complete, on continued heating. - Larger grains grow at the expense of smaller ones (driven by reduction in total grain boundary energy). - Grain size increases, but grains remain equiaxed. - Mechanical properties further soften. > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick: "R-R-G"** — *Recovery → Recrystallisation → Grain Growth* ### 2.2 Driving Force for Recrystallisation The **driving force** for recrystallisation is the **stored strain energy** in the cold-worked metal. This energy is stored primarily in the form of: 1. **Dislocation energy** (line defects) 2. **Point defect energy** (vacancies and interstitials generated during plastic deformation) 3. **Strain energy** of grain boundaries and twin boundaries This stored energy is released when: - Dislocations are eliminated (during recrystallisation). - Grain boundary area is reduced (during grain growth). ### 2.3 Factors Affecting Recrystallisation Temperature and Grain Size | S.No | Factor | Effect on $T_{recry}$ | Effect on Recrystallised Grain Size | |:----:|:-------|:----------------------|:-------------------------------------| | 1 | Increasing amount of prior cold work (%) | **Decreases** (more stored energy → easier nucleation) | **Decreases** (more nucleation sites) | | 2 | Increasing annealing time | **Effectively decreases** | **Increases** (more time for grain growth) | | 3 | Increasing annealing temperature | **Effectively decreases** (faster kinetics) | **Increases** (faster boundary migration) | | 4 | Larger initial (pre-cold-work) grain size | **Slightly increases** | **Increases** (fewer nucleation sites) | | 5 | Higher metal purity | **Decreases** (impurities pin boundaries) | **Increases** (less pinning) | | 6 | Alloying additions (solute atoms) | **Increases significantly** | **Decreases** (solute drag) | | 7 | Second-phase particles | **Increases** | **Decreases** (Zener pinning) | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Variables & Units | |:---------------------|:-----------------|:------------------| | $T_{recry} \approx 0.3\,T_m$ to $0.5\,T_m$ | Empirical rule for recrystallisation temperature (Tm in K) | $T_m$ = melting temperature [K] | | $\Delta G = E_{stored} \propto \rho \, G b^2$ | Driving force ≈ stored energy per unit volume | $\rho$ = dislocation density, $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector | | $X = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$ | **Avrami equation** for fraction recrystallised | $X$ = fraction recrystallised (0 to 1), $k$ = rate constant, $t$ = time [s], $n$ = Avrami exponent (≈ 2–3) | | $\ln \left[ \ln \!\left( \dfrac{1}{1 - X} \right) \right] = \ln k + n \ln t$ | Linearised Avrami form for plotting | Same as above | | $t_{0.5} = \left( \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} \right)^{1/n}$ | Time for 50% recrystallisation | $t_{0.5}$ = half-life of recrystallisation [s] | | $D = C t^{1/2}$ | Grain growth law (ideal) | $D$ = average grain diameter, $C$ = constant, $t$ = time | | $D_t - D_0 = K t^n$ | Empirical grain growth equation | $D_t$ = grain size at time $t$, $D_0$ = initial grain size | > [!NOTE] > **Note on pipe symbol usage in tables:** All absolute-value or magnitude symbols are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to maintain markdown table integrity (e.g., $\vert \ln t \vert$). ### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Recrystallisation 1. **Restoration of Ductility:** After cold working, metals become hard but brittle. Recrystallisation annealing restores ductility, enabling further forming operations. 2. **Grain Size Control:** By tuning cold work percentage and annealing conditions, engineers can achieve a specific grain size, which directly affects yield strength via the **Hall-Petch relation**: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y D^{-1/2}$. 3. **Commercial Purity Processing:** Determines rolling schedules for steel, aluminium sheets, copper wires in industrial practice. 4. **Texture Control:** Recrystallisation can produce preferred crystallographic orientations beneficial for deep-drawing (e.g., in low-carbon steel sheets). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Avrami (Johnson-Mehl) Equation for Recrystallisation Kinetics The Avrami equation describes the **isothermal** transformation kinetics of recrystallisation. It is derived from nucleation and growth theory. #### Step 1: Define the fraction transformed Let $X(t)$ be the volume fraction of material that has recrystallised at time $t$. $$X(t) = \dfrac{V_{rex}}{V_{total}}$$ where $V_{rex}$ is the volume recrystallised. #### Step 2: Define extended volume fraction In the "extended volume" concept, we imagine that grains can nucleate and grow in already-transformed regions (ignoring impingement). Let $V_{ext}$ be this extended volume. $$dV_{ext} = \dot{N} \cdot V_{total} \cdot \left[1 - X(t)\right] \cdot dt$$ where $\dot{N}$ is the nucleation rate per unit volume of untransformed material. Wait — applying the standard Johnson-Mehl-Avrami-Kolmogorov (JMAK) derivation more rigorously: #### Step 3: Impingement correction The actual volume of transformed material accounts for **impingement** — the fact that growing grains collide and overlap. The probability that an arbitrary point is untransformed is $(1 - X)$. $$dV_{rex} = dV_{ext} \cdot \left[1 - X(t)\right]$$ #### Step 4: Combine and integrate Using $V_{ext} = N \cdot G^3 \cdot t^3$ (assuming 3D growth at constant rate $G$ and constant nucleation rate $N$): $$\dfrac{dV_{rex}}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{dV_{ext}}{V_{total}} \cdot (1 - X)$$ $$\dfrac{dV_{ext}}{V_{total}} = 3 N G^3 t^2 \, dt$$ Integrating: $$V_{ext}(t) = \int_0^t 3 N G^3 \tau^2 \, d\tau = N G^3 t^3$$ #### Step 5: Apply the impingement correction integral The differential equation for the real transformed fraction is: $$dX = (1 - X) \, d\left( \dfrac{V_{ext}}{V_{total}} \right)$$ $$\dfrac{dX}{1 - X} = d\left( \dfrac{V_{ext}}{V_{total}} \right)$$ Integrating both sides from 0 to $t$: $$\int_0^X \dfrac{dX'}{1 - X'} = \int_0^t d\left( \dfrac{V_{ext}}{V_{total}} \right)$$ $$-\ln(1 - X) = \dfrac{V_{ext}(t)}{V_{total}}$$ #### Step 6: Final form of the Avrami equation Substituting the extended volume expression, and absorbing constants into a single rate constant $k$: $$X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$$ where: - $k = \left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} \right) \dot{N} G^3$ (for constant nucleation rate, 3D growth) - $n = 4$ (for constant nucleation rate, 3D growth) - $n = 3$ (for site-saturated nucleation, 3D growth) - For **recrystallisation** (typically 2D or 3D growth with site saturation or decreasing nucleation): $n$ is empirically observed to be between **2 and 3**. #### Step 7: Linearised form (for graphical analysis) Taking natural log twice: $$\ln\left[ \ln\!\left( \dfrac{1}{1 - X} \right) \right] = \ln k + n \ln t$$ Plotting $\ln[\ln(1/(1-X))]$ vs $\ln t$ yields a **straight line** with slope $n$ and intercept $\ln k$. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A cold-worked brass specimen is annealed at $600^\circ C$. The recrystallisation kinetics follow the Avrami equation with $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, s^{-2.5}$ and $n = 2.5$. Calculate: - (a) The fraction recrystallised after 100 s. - (b) The time required for 50% recrystallisation. #### (a) Fraction recrystallised at t = 100 s **Step 1:** Substitute into the Avrami equation. $$X = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the exponent $k t^n$. $$t^n = (100)^{2.5} = 100^{2.5} = 10^{2 \times 2.5} = 10^5 = 100{,}000$$ $$k t^n = (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1 \times 10^5) = 150$$ **Step 3:** Compute the exponential. $$\exp(-150) \approx 0$$ **Step 4:** Result. $$\boxed{X \approx 1.0 \text{ (i.e., 100\% recrystallised)}}$$ > **Interpretation:** With such a high rate constant, recrystallisation completes well before 100 s. The metal is fully recrystallised. #### (b) Time for 50% recrystallisation **Step 1:** Set $X = 0.5$ and solve for $t$. $$0.5 = 1 - \exp(-k t^n) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \exp(-k t^n) = 0.5$$ $$-k t^n = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$ $$k t^n = 0.6931$$ **Step 2:** Solve for $t$. $$t^n = \dfrac{0.6931}{k} = \dfrac{0.6931}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 462.07$$ $$t = (462.07)^{1/n} = (462.07)^{1/2.5} = (462.07)^{0.4}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate. $$\log_{10}(462.07) = 2.6647$$ $$\log_{10}(t) = 0.4 \times 2.6647 = 1.0659$$ $$t = 10^{1.0659} \approx 11.64 \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{t_{0.5} \approx 11.6 \text{ s}}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Recovery vs Recrystallisation vs Grain Growth | Property | Recovery | Recrystallisation | Grain Growth | |:--------|:---------|:------------------|:-------------| | Temperature range | Low ($< T_{recry}$) | Moderate to high ($> T_{recry}$) | High (well above $T_{recry}$) | | New grains formed? | **No** (sub-grain structure) | **Yes** (new strain-free grains) | **No** (existing grains enlarge) | | Dislocation density | Slightly decreases | **Dramatically decreases** | Remains low (≈ equilibrium) | | Yield strength | Nearly unchanged | **Significantly decreases** | Further decreases (slightly) | | Ductility | Slightly increases | **Significantly increases** | Marginal change | | Optical microstructure | No visible change | New equiaxed grains appear | Grain size increases | | Driving force | Stored strain energy (low) | Stored strain energy (high) | Grain boundary energy reduction | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Stages During Annealing of a Cold-Worked Metal ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cold Worked Metal
High dislocation density
Distorted grains
High hardness, low ductility] --> B[Heating Above Recovery Temperature] B --> C[Stage 1 RECOVERY
Dislocation annihilation
Stress relief
Sub-grain formation
No optical change] C --> D[Heating Above Recrystallisation Temperature] D --> E[Stage 2 RECRYSTALLISATION
New strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate
Stored strain energy released
Dislocation density drops sharply
Hardness drops, ductility restored] E --> F[Continued Heating at High Temperature] F --> G[Stage 3 GRAIN GROWTH
Larger grains consume smaller ones
Grain boundary area decreases
Grain size increases
Further softening] G --> H[Final Annealed Metal
Low hardness, high ductility
Equiaxed coarse grains] style A fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style E fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Factors Influencing Recrystallisation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input1[Material State] A1[Amount of Cold Work] A2[Initial Grain Size] A3[Alloying Elements] end subgraph Input2[Process Parameters] B1[Annealing Temperature] B2[Annealing Time] B3[Heating Rate] end subgraph Output[Recrystallisation Response] C1[Recrystallisation Temperature] C2[Recrystallised Grain Size] C3[Mechanical Properties] end A1 --> C1 A1 --> C2 A2 --> C1 A2 --> C2 A3 --> C1 A3 --> C2 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B2 --> C2 B2 --> C3 B3 --> C2 style Input1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style Input2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style Output fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Microstructure Evolution Sequence (Schematic Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Initial Cold Worked State
Elongated distorted grains
Dense tangled dislocations] -->|Heat to T1| P2[Recovery Stage
Slightly more equiaxed sub-grains
Reduced dislocation density] P2 -->|Heat to T2 above Trecry| P3[Partially Recrystallised
New fine grains at boundaries
Deformed matrix shrinking] P3 -->|Hold at T2| P4[Fully Recrystallised
All new strain-free equiaxed grains
Low dislocation density] P4 -->|Continued heating| P5[After Grain Growth
Fewer, larger equiaxed grains] style P1 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style P2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style P3 fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000 style P4 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style P5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Define recrystallisation temperature. State the empirical relationship for recrystallisation temperature with melting point.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Recrystallisation temperature is the minimum temperature at which a cold-worked metal, on heating, completely replaces its deformed, dislocation-rich grain structure with a new, strain-free, equiaxed grain structure within a specified time (usually 1 hour). **Empirical relationship (1.5 marks):** $$T_{recry} \approx 0.3 \, T_m \text{ to } 0.5 \, T_m$$ where $T_m$ is the absolute melting temperature (in Kelvin). This is not a sharp temperature, but depends on the amount of cold work, annealing time, purity, and grain size. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Distinguish between recovery and recrystallisation.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | S.No | Recovery | Recrystallisation | |:----:|:---------|:------------------| | 1 | Occurs at lower temperatures (below $T_{recry}$) | Occurs at higher temperatures (above $T_{recry}$) | | 2 | No new grains form | New strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate and grow | | 3 | Dislocation density reduces only slightly | Dislocation density drops drastically | | 4 | Mechanical properties barely change | Significant drop in strength, increase in ductility | | 5 | Optical microstructure appears unchanged | New grains visible under microscope | (Any three points with comparison = 3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **KTU 2024 Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve **either** Option A **or** Option B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** *What is recrystallisation? With a neat schematic, explain the stages of microstructural change that occur when a cold-worked metal is annealed.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** Recrystallisation is the process in which a plastically deformed (cold-worked) metal, when heated above a critical temperature (the recrystallisation temperature), forms a new set of strain-free, equiaxed grains that replace the distorted, high-dislocation-density structure. The driving force is the **stored strain energy** from cold work. **Three Stages (5 marks):** **Stage 1 — Recovery:** On heating just below the recrystallisation temperature, point defects annihilate, dislocations rearrange and annihilate, and internal stresses are relieved. No new grains form, and the microstructure appears unchanged under the optical microscope. Strength decreases only marginally. **Stage 2 — Recrystallisation:** Above $T_{recry}$, new strain-free equiaxed grains **nucleate** at high-energy sites (grain boundaries, deformation bands, twin boundaries) and grow into the surrounding deformed matrix. Once recrystallisation is complete, the dislocation density has dropped to a low, equilibrium value. Mechanical properties revert toward their pre-cold-work values (lower hardness, higher ductility). **Stage 3 — Grain Growth:** With continued heating, grain boundaries migrate so that larger grains grow at the expense of smaller ones, reducing total grain boundary energy. Grain size increases monotonically with time, with no further change in grain shape (remains equiaxed). > *Schematic: Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the annealing-stage flowchart.* --- **(b)** *A cold-worked copper specimen (cold work = 40%) has an Avrami exponent $n = 2.0$ and rate constant $k = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, s^{-2}$. Calculate: (i) The fraction recrystallised in 60 seconds, (ii) The time required for 95% recrystallisation.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** $k = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, s^{-2}$, $n = 2.0$, $t = 60 \, s$. **Part (i): Fraction recrystallised at t = 60 s (3.5 marks)** **Step 1:** Compute the exponent $k t^n$. $$k t^n = (2 \times 10^{-4}) \times (60)^2 = (2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 3600 = 0.72$$ **Step 2:** Apply the Avrami equation. $$X = 1 - \exp(-0.72) = 1 - 0.4868$$ $$\boxed{X \approx 0.5132 \text{ (i.e., 51.32\% recrystallised)}}$$ **Part (ii): Time for 95% recrystallisation (3.5 marks)** **Step 1:** Set $X = 0.95$ and solve. $$0.95 = 1 - \exp(-k t^n) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \exp(-k t^n) = 0.05$$ **Step 2:** Take natural log. $$-k t^n = \ln(0.05) = -2.9957$$ $$k t^n = 2.9957$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $t$. $$t^n = \dfrac{2.9957}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 14{,}978.5$$ $$t = (14{,}978.5)^{1/2.0} = \sqrt{14{,}978.5} \approx 122.4 \, s$$ $$\boxed{t_{95\%} \approx 122.4 \, s}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** *Discuss the various factors that affect the recrystallisation temperature and the recrystallised grain size of a cold-worked metal.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks — 1 mark per major factor, plus examples):** 1. **Amount of prior cold work:** Greater cold work increases stored strain energy, providing more nucleation sites. Hence, both the recrystallisation temperature and the recrystallised grain size **decrease** with increasing cold work (above a critical strain, typically 2–10%). 2. **Annealing temperature:** Higher annealing temperature accelerates boundary migration and shortens the time for recrystallisation. Effectively, $T_{recry}$ decreases with longer time (the time–temperature relationship is inverse). Final grain size increases with annealing temperature. 3. **Annealing time:** Longer annealing time allows more complete recrystallisation and more grain growth. Effectively reduces the recrystallisation temperature and increases grain size. 4. **Initial grain size (before cold work):** A finer pre-deformation grain size provides more grain-boundary nucleation sites, so $T_{recry}$ is slightly lower and the recrystallised grain size is smaller. 5. **Purity of the metal:** Higher purity lowers $T_{recry}$ because impurity atoms pin grain boundaries (solute drag). Pure metals recrystallise at lower temperatures than alloys. 6. **Alloying additions / second-phase particles:** Solute atoms and dispersed particles inhibit grain-boundary migration, raising $T_{recry}$ and producing finer recrystallised grains (Zener pinning). 7. **Stacking fault energy (SFE):** Low-SFE metals (e.g., Cu, austenitic steel) have wider dissociation of dislocations, slower recovery, and higher $T_{recry}$. High-SFE metals (e.g., Al) recrystallise at lower temperatures. --- **(b)** *A pure metal has a melting point of $660^\circ C$. Estimate its recrystallisation temperature. List four properties that change during recrystallisation.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Part (i): Estimation of $T_{recry}$ (3 marks)** **Step 1:** Convert melting point to Kelvin. $$T_m = 660 + 273 = 933 \, K$$ **Step 2:** Apply the empirical rule. $$T_{recry} = 0.4 \times T_m = 0.4 \times 933 = 373.2 \, K$$ **Step 3:** Convert back to Celsius. $$T_{recry} = 373.2 - 273 = 100.2^\circ C$$ **Note:** The value of $0.4$ (between 0.3 and 0.5) is the conventional mid-range value used in KTU problems when the problem does not specify a precise factor. For metals like aluminium, $0.4$ is appropriate. $$\boxed{T_{recry} \approx 100^\circ C}$$ **Part (ii): Four properties that change during recrystallisation (4 marks — 1 mark each)** 1. **Yield strength and hardness:** Decrease significantly. 2. **Ductility (% elongation):** Increases substantially. 3. **Dislocation density:** Drops from a high value to a low equilibrium value. 4. **Electrical resistivity:** Decreases (since defects scatter electrons). 5. *(Bonus)* **Internal/residual stresses:** Get relieved completely. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (as observed in KTU valuation keys):** > > 1. **Mixing up $T_{recry}$ with $T_m$:** Some students write $T_{recry} = 0.4 \times T_m$ in **Celsius** instead of Kelvin. **Always convert to Kelvin first.** > > 2. **Forgetting to take $\ln$ twice in Avrami plots:** The linearised form is $\ln[\ln(1/(1-X))]$ vs $\ln t$, not $\ln(1/(1-X))$ vs $\ln t$. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss. > > 3. **Confusing "recovery" with "recrystallisation":** Recovery does **not** form new grains. Many students incorrectly claim "new grains form during recovery" — this loses 2 marks immediately. > > 4. **Forgetting impingement in kinetics:** The Avrami equation already accounts for impingement. Students sometimes try to "correct" the answer for impingement, which is wrong. > > 5. **Not specifying units in Avrami calculations:** $k$ has units of $s^{-n}$ — failing to mention this loses a unit-mark. > > 6. **Incorrect sign in exponent:** $X = 1 - \exp(-kt^n)$, **not** $1 - \exp(+kt^n)$. The exponential must be negative. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Recrystallisation (Module 2):** > > - **Definition:** Formation of new strain-free equiaxed grains in a cold-worked metal upon heating above $T_{recry}$. > - **Empirical rule:** $T_{recry} \approx 0.3\,T_m$ to $0.5\,T_m$ (Tm in Kelvin). > - **Driving force:** Stored strain energy from cold work (dislocation energy). > - **Three stages of annealing:** Recovery → Recrystallisation → Grain Growth (R-R-G). > - **Recovery:** No new grains; dislocation annihilation; stress relief; minor property change. > - **Recrystallisation:** New strain-free equiaxed grains; sharp drop in strength; rise in ductility. > - **Grain growth:** Existing grains enlarge; driven by reduction in grain-boundary energy. > - **Factors that lower $T_{recry}$:** Higher cold work, longer time, higher temperature, higher purity, smaller initial grain size. > - **Factors that raise $T_{recry}$:** Alloying, second-phase particles, larger initial grain size. > - **Avrami equation:** $X = 1 - \exp(-kt^n)$, with $n \approx 2$–$3$ for recrystallisation. > - **Linearised Avrami:** $\ln[\ln(1/(1-X))] = \ln k + n \ln t$ (slope = $n$, intercept = $\ln k$). > - **Half-time:** $t_{0.5} = (\ln 2 / k)^{1/n}$. > - **Grain-growth law:** $D = C t^{1/2}$ (ideal) or $D_t - D_0 = K t^n$ (empirical). > - **Hall-Petch link:** Finer recrystallised grain → higher yield strength ($\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y D^{-1/2}$). > - **Engineering use:** Restoring ductility after cold work; controlling grain size in rolled sheets and wires. > - **Remember:** $T_{recry}$ is **not a sharp temperature** — it is a kinetic quantity depending on time, purity, and cold-work amount.

topics.title: Hot and Cold Working of Metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hot and Cold Working of Metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Metal Working

**Metal Working (Mechanical Working / Deformation Processing)** is defined as the controlled plastic deformation of metals into desired shapes by the application of external forces such as compression, tension, bending, or shearing, while preserving the metallic mass and improving its mechanical properties.

The two principal classifications under this module are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Working (HW):** A deformation process carried out at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal, such that the rate of work hardening is continuously neutralized by simultaneous recrystallization, resulting in a strain-free microstructure during the process.
> 
> **Cold Working (CW):** A deformation process carried out at a temperature **below the recrystallization temperature** (typically at room temperature), where strain hardening accumulates progressively because recovery and recrystallization do not occur at a meaningful rate.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Hot Working** | Imagine shaping **warm clay or molten chocolate**. Because the material is soft and pliable above a critical temperature, you can mold it easily into intricate forms, and the tiny structural "memories" formed during molding are continuously erased, leaving no internal stress. |
| **Cold Working** | Picture **bending a steel wire repeatedly with pliers** or **folding a paper clip**. Each bend hardens the wire at the bend (strain hardening). It becomes stiffer and less ductile, and if you bend it too many times, it snaps — just as cold-worked metal becomes brittle. |
| **Recrystallization** | Think of a **crumpled sheet of aluminum foil annealed over a flame**. The wrinkles (dislocations) gradually disappear, and new, soft, strain-free crystals nucleate, restoring the original ductility — this is exactly what happens when a cold-worked metal is heated. |

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Temperature — The Recrystallization Temperature (T_R)

> [!NOTE]
> The **recrystallization temperature (T_R)** is the minimum temperature at which a severely cold-worked metal (≈ 100% cold work) completes full recrystallization in approximately **one hour**. It is **NOT a fixed physical constant** — it depends on:
> - Purity of the metal (impurities raise T_R)
> - Degree of prior cold work
> - Time allowed for recrystallization
> - Initial grain size

**Empirical Rule (Burgers Rule):**

$$T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m \quad \text{(for pure metals, T in Kelvin)}$$

where $T_m$ is the absolute melting temperature.

For **alloys** (e.g., steels, brasses), this fraction rises to **0.5 to 0.7 T_m**, because solute atoms and second-phase particles pin the grain boundaries and retard recrystallization.

---

### 1.4 Quick Reference Definition Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Hot Working** = Plastic deformation **above T_R** → No residual strain hardening → Soft, ductile, refined equiaxed grains.
> 
> **Cold Working** = Plastic deformation **below T_R** → Accumulated strain hardening → Increased strength & hardness, decreased ductility → Elongated/fibrous grains with high dislocation density.

### 1.5 Why Do Engineers Care?

Hot and cold working are the **backbone of manufacturing industries**:
- **Hot working**: Forging, rolling, extrusion, hot drawing of large structural sections (rails, beams, I-sections).
- **Cold working**: Sheet metal forming, wire drawing, deep drawing, cold rolling of car body panels, coins, foil, and tubes.

Choosing the wrong temperature can lead to:
- **Hot shortness** (cracking at grain boundaries if temperature is too high) or
- **Excessive tool forces and cracking** (if temperature is too low and metal behaves elastically/brittle).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanics of Plastic Deformation — A Stepwise Breakdown

Plastic deformation in crystalline metals occurs by the following mechanisms:

1. **Slip** — Movement of dislocations along close-packed planes (e.g., {111} in FCC, {110} in BCC).
2. **Twinning** — Atomic-plane mirror-symmetric rearrangement; common in BCC metals at low temperatures.
3. **Grain boundary sliding** — Prominent in fine-grained metals at high temperatures (contributes to superplasticity).
4. **Diffusion creep** — Dominant at very high homologous temperatures ($T/T_m > 0.9$).

The **controlling mechanism** shifts as temperature changes:

| Temperature Range | Dominant Mechanism | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Below ~0.3 T_m | Slip + Twinning | Cold working → Strain hardening |
| ~0.3 T_m to T_R | Slip + some recovery | Warm working |
| Above T_R | Slip + dynamic recovery + dynamic recrystallization | Hot working → Grain refinement |

### 2.2 The Three Post-Deformation Stages (on Heating a Cold-Worked Metal)

When a cold-worked metal is heated progressively, three distinct microstructural stages occur:

#### Stage 1: Recovery
- Occurs at relatively low temperatures (well below T_R).
- Stored strain energy is released as **point defects annihilate** and dislocations rearrange into lower-energy configurations (e.g., tilt walls, sub-grain formation).
- **Mechanical properties (yield strength, hardness) recover only marginally**, but **electrical resistivity drops significantly** because scattering by point defects is reduced.
- No new grains form; the elongated cold-worked grain structure is preserved.

#### Stage 2: Recrystallization
- Occurs near T_R.
- New **strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate** at high-energy sites (grain boundaries, deformation bands, twin boundaries) and grow until they consume all deformed parent grains.
- Properties (yield strength, ductility) **return to pre-cold-worked values**.
- The driving force is the **stored strain energy** from dislocations.

#### Stage 3: Grain Growth
- Occurs above T_R after recrystallization is complete.
- Smaller grains shrink and disappear; larger grains grow.
- Grain growth is driven by the **reduction in total grain boundary area (and hence total grain boundary energy)**.
- Excessive grain growth **reduces strength** (Hall-Petch relationship) and toughness.

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Process

| Process | "Why" (Driving Force) | "How" (Mechanism) |
|---|---|---|
| Recovery | Stored elastic strain energy in dislocations | Dislocation climb, cross-slip, annihilation |
| Recrystallization | Stored energy from high dislocation density | Nucleation of new strain-free grains at defect sites |
| Grain Growth | Reduction in grain boundary energy | Grain boundary migration (atom diffusion across boundary) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU University Examinations. Memorize the units, boundary conditions, and physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Cold work %; $A_0$ = original area, $A_f$ = final area | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $\sigma_E = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering stress; $F$ = applied load | MPa or N/m² |
| 3 | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{F}{A_i} = \sigma_E(1 + \varepsilon_E)$ | True stress; $A_i$ = instantaneous area | MPa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_T = \ln\dfrac{L_f}{L_0} = \ln\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ | True strain; $L$ = length | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)$ | True strain in terms of engineering strain | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma_T = K \, \varepsilon_T^{\,n}$ | **Hollomon Equation**; $K$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = strain hardening exponent | $K$ in MPa, $n$ dimensionless (0 < n < 1) |
| 7 | $\bar{\sigma}_f = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_f}\displaystyle\int_0^{\varepsilon_f}\sigma_T \, d\varepsilon_T$ | **Average flow stress** for general hardening | MPa |
| 8 | $n = \dfrac{d(\ln\sigma_T)}{d(\ln\varepsilon_T)}$ | Slope of log-log true stress–strain plot | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | Burgers' rule (pure metals, 1 hour anneal) | K |
| 10 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch equation; $d$ = grain diameter | MPa; $k_y$ in MPa·mm^(1/2) |
| 11 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v}{L_0}$ | Strain rate; $v$ = ram velocity, $L_0$ = initial length | s⁻¹ |
| 12 | $T_{HW} > T_R$, $T_{CW} < T_R$ | Working temperature criterion | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL** — Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside table cells. Notice how absolute value-style expressions and conditional rules are written using descriptive English ("below T_R", "above T_R") to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Hollomon equation** $\sigma_T = K\varepsilon_T^n$ is the **fundamental constitutive law** used in **finite element analysis (FEA)** of metal forming processes (forging, rolling simulations in software like DEFORM, ABAQUS).
- The **strain hardening exponent $n$** is a direct measure of a metal's ability to distribute strain uniformly. **Deep-drawing steels (e.g., DC04, EDDQ)** are chosen with high $n$ (≈ 0.22–0.25) to avoid localized necking and tearing in car body panels.
- The **% Cold Work** determines the final mechanical state of products like **aluminum beverage cans** (≈ 60–70% CW → ultra-high strength but limited formability).
- The **recrystallization rule** is critical when designing **heat treatment cycles** for cold-worked products like **cold-rolled steel sheets** (batch annealing) and **copper wire** (continuous strand annealing).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cold Work Percentage from Tensile Specimen Geometry

A cylindrical tensile specimen with **original diameter $d_0$** and **gauge length $L_0$** is plastically deformed in tension until it fractures with **final diameter $d_f$** and **final length $L_f$**. Derive the **% Cold Work** in terms of cross-sectional area.

**Step 1** — Recall the definition of % Cold Work:

$$\%CW = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$

**Step 2** — Express original and final cross-sectional areas (assuming circular cross-section):

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4}, \qquad A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute into the numerator and denominator:

$$\%CW = \frac{\dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} - \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4}}{\dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}} \times 100$$

**Step 4** — Factor out the common $\pi/4$ term and cancel:

$$\%CW = \frac{\pi/4 \cdot (d_0^2 - d_f^2)}{\pi/4 \cdot d_0^2} \times 100 = \frac{d_0^2 - d_f^2}{d_0^2} \times 100$$

**Step 5** — Final compact expression:

$$\boxed{\%CW = \left[1 - \left(\frac{d_f}{d_0}\right)^{\!2}\right] \times 100}$$

This shows that **%CW depends only on the ratio of diameters**, not on the absolute size. Note that volume constancy ($A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$) also yields the equivalent length-based form:

$$\%CW = \left[1 - \frac{L_0}{L_f}\right] \times 100$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: True Stress in Terms of Engineering Stress

**Step 1** — Engineering stress uses the **original** cross-section:

$$\sigma_E = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 2** — True stress uses the **instantaneous** area. Assuming **incompressibility (volume constancy)**, valid for plastic deformation where density remains essentially unchanged:

$$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_i = A_0 \cdot \frac{L_0}{L_i}$$

**Step 3** — Substituting the relation $L_i = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_E)$:

$$A_i = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_E}$$

**Step 4** — True stress becomes:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0/(1 + \varepsilon_E)} = (1 + \varepsilon_E) \cdot \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 5** — Recognizing $\sigma_E = F/A_0$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_T = \sigma_E \, (1 + \varepsilon_E)}$$

**Step 6** — Validation: For $\varepsilon_E = 0$ (no strain), $\sigma_T = \sigma_E$ (trivially consistent). For $\varepsilon_E = 0.5$ (true strain $\varepsilon_T = 0.405$), $\sigma_T = 1.5 \, \sigma_E$, meaning the engineering stress **underestimates** the actual stress in the necked region.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Average Flow Stress from the Hollomon Equation

The Hollomon constitutive law is:

$$\sigma_T = K \, \varepsilon_T^{\,n}$$

The **average flow stress** is defined as the work done per unit volume divided by the total strain:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{W}{V \, \varepsilon_f} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f}\int_0^{\varepsilon_f}\sigma_T \, d\varepsilon_T$$

**Step 1** — Substitute Hollomon's equation into the integral:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f}\int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K \, \varepsilon_T^{\,n} \, d\varepsilon_T$$

**Step 2** — Pull out the constant $K$:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_f}\int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \varepsilon_T^{\,n} \, d\varepsilon_T$$

**Step 3** — Apply the power rule for integration: $\int x^n \, dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_f} \cdot \left[\frac{\varepsilon_T^{\,n+1}}{n+1}\right]_0^{\varepsilon_f}$$

**Step 4** — Evaluate at the limits:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_f} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_f^{\,n+1}}{n+1} = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{n+1}$$

**Step 5** — Recognize that $K \varepsilon_f^{\,n} = \sigma_f$ (the true stress at final strain):

$$\boxed{\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{\sigma_f}{n+1}}$$

**Step 6** — Special case: For an **elastic–perfectly plastic** material ($n = 0$), this reduces to $\bar{\sigma}_f = \sigma_f = \sigma_y$ (yield stress constant), which is consistent with classical plasticity theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: The expression $\bar{\sigma}_f = K \varepsilon_f^{\,n}/(n+1)$ is **the** equation used in forging load calculations. Forging force $F = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot A_f$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hot Working vs Cold Working

| Property | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Above T_R | Below T_R |
| Strain hardening | Negligible (dynamic recovery + recrystallization) | Significant (accumulates) |
| Yield strength | Low (soft material) | High (hard material) |
| Ductility | High (large reductions per pass) | Low (limited per pass) |
| Surface finish | Poor (oxide scale, descaling required) | Excellent (smooth, bright) |
| Dimensional accuracy | Poor (shrinkage, scale) | Excellent (close tolerances) |
| Equipment forces | Low (soft) | High (strong) |
| Grain structure | Equiaxed, refined, strain-free | Elongated/fibrous, high dislocation density |
| % Reduction per pass | 10–100% (large) | 10–30% (small) |
| Residual stresses | Low (relieved dynamically) | High (may need stress-relief anneal) |
| Anisotropy | Nearly isotropic | Highly anisotropic (texture) |
| Energy consumption | High (heat input) | Low (no heating) |
| Examples | Hot rolling, forging, extrusion, hot drawing | Cold rolling, deep drawing, wire drawing, cold heading |
| Typical products | Rails, structural beams, ingot-to-billet conversion | Car body panels, beverage cans, foil, sheets, tubes |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem:** A low-carbon steel bar of original diameter **20 mm** is reduced to **15 mm** by cold drawing. Calculate: (a) % Cold Work, (b) True Strain.

**Given:** $d_0 = 20$ mm, $d_f = 15$ mm.

**Part (a) — % Cold Work:**

$$\%CW = \left[1 - \left(\frac{d_f}{d_0}\right)^2\right] \times 100 = \left[1 - \left(\frac{15}{20}\right)^2\right] \times 100$$

$$= \left[1 - (0.75)^2\right] \times 100 = [1 - 0.5625] \times 100 = 43.75\%$$

**Part (b) — True Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_T = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{d_0}{d_f}\right)^2 = 2\ln\left(\frac{20}{15}\right)$$

$$= 2 \ln(1.333) = 2 \times 0.2877 = 0.5754$$

$$\boxed{\%CW = 43.75\%, \quad \varepsilon_T = 0.575}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Metal Forming Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal Forming Processes] --> B[Temperature Based Classification]
    A --> C[Mechanism Based Classification]

    B --> D[Hot Working - T greater than T sub R]
    B --> E[Cold Working - T less than T sub R]
    B --> F[Warm Working - intermediate range]

    D --> D1[Hot Rolling]
    D --> D2[Hot Forging]
    D --> D3[Hot Extrusion]
    D --> D4[Hot Drawing]

    E --> E1[Cold Rolling]
    E --> E2[Cold Drawing]
    E --> E3[Deep Drawing]
    E --> E4[Cold Heading]
    E --> E5[Stretch Forming]

    C --> G[Bulk Forming]
    C --> H[Sheet Forming]

    G --> G1[Forging]
    G --> G2[Extrusion]
    G --> G3[Rolling]
    G --> G4[Drawing]

    H --> H1[Bending]
    H --> H2[Deep Drawing]
    H --> H3[Stretching]
    H --> H4[Hydroforming]

    D1 --> I[Refined equiaxed grains]
    D1 --> J[No strain hardening]
    D1 --> K[Low strength high ductility]

    E1 --> L[Elongated fibrous grains]
    E1 --> M[High dislocation density]
    E1 --> N[High strength low ductility]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
