```mermaid
    style K fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style N fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Three Stages on Heating Cold-Worked Metal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[As Cold Worked State] --> A1[Stage 1 Recovery]
    A1 --> A2[Stage 2 Recrystallization]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3 Grain Growth]

    A0 --> P0[Property Profile - High strength low ductility elongated grains high dislocation density]

    A1 --> P1[Property Profile - Slight strength drop point defects annihilate sub-grains form dislocations rearrange]

    A2 --> P2[Property Profile - Strength returns to original new strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate and grow stored energy relieved]

    A3 --> P3[Property Profile - Strength drops further larger grains consume smaller ones driven by grain boundary energy reduction]

    A1 --> T1[Temperature Range - Low - well below T sub R]
    A2 --> T2[Temperature Range - Near T sub R]
    A3 --> T3[Temperature Range - Above T sub R extended time]

    style A0 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P0 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Effect of Cold Work on Mechanical Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CW[Increasing Percent Cold Work] --> YS[Yield Strength - INCREASES]
    CW --> UTS[Ultimate Tensile Strength - INCREASES]
    CW --> HD[Hardness - INCREASES]
    CW --> EL[Percent Elongation - DECREASES]
    CW --> RA[Reduction in Area - DECREASES]
    CW --> IR[Impact Resistance - DECREASES]
    CW --> TR[True Fracture Stress - INCREASES slowly]

    YS --> APP[Engineering Implication - Sheet metal for structural car body - Beverage can stock - Aircraft skin]
    EL --> APP2[Engineering Implication - Limited formability - Brittle failure risk - Requires intermediate anneal]

    style CW fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style YS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style UTS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style HD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style EL fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style RA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style IR fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style TR fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style APP fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style APP2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Hot Working Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Input - Cast Ingot or Continuous Cast Billet] --> H1[Heating Furnace - Heat to T greater than T sub R]
    H1 --> H2[Soaking Zone - Achieve uniform temperature]
    H2 --> H3[Primary Deformation - Roughing Passes - Large reductions]
    H3 --> H4[Secondary Deformation - Finishing Passes - Refine shape]
    H4 --> H5[Descaling - High pressure water jets - Remove oxide scale]
    H5 --> H6[Cooling Bed - Controlled cooling]
    H6 --> OUT[Output - Refined equiaxed grains - No strain hardening - Improved toughness]

    H3 --> M1[Mechanism - Dynamic Recovery - Dynamic Recrystallization]
    M1 --> M2[Microstructural Result - Strain-free new grains replace deformed ones]

    H1 --> E1[Energy Cost HIGH]
    H5 --> E2[Surface Quality MODERATE]
    OUT --> E3[Mechanical Properties - Good ductility and toughness - Moderate strength - Isotropic behavior]

    style IN fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E3 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Effect of Temperature on Strength & Ductility (Conceptual Mapping)

| Process Stage | Microstructural Marker | Strength | Ductility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial annealed | Equiaxed, low dislocation | Low | High |
| Mild cold work (≈ 10%) | Slight elongation, modest dislocations | Slight increase | Slight decrease |
| Heavy cold work (≈ 60–80%) | Highly fibrous, dense dislocations | High | Low |
| Recovery heating | Sub-grain formation, dislocation rearrangement | Slight decrease | Slight increase |
| Recrystallization | New strain-free equiaxed grains | Returns to original | Returns to original |
| Grain growth | Coarse grains | Decreases (Hall-Petch) | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Define the terms "Hot Working" and "Cold Working" of metals. What is the role of recrystallization temperature in distinguishing them?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Hot Working (1 mark):**
Hot working is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation is carried out at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal. At this temperature, the rate of work hardening is continuously neutralized by simultaneous recrystallization, so the metal remains soft and ductile throughout the process, producing a refined, strain-free equiaxed grain structure.

**Cold Working (1 mark):**
Cold working is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation is carried out at a temperature **below the recrystallization temperature** (typically at room temperature). Because recrystallization does not occur, the strain hardening caused by deformation accumulates, leading to increased strength, hardness, and a decrease in ductility. The grains become elongated and fibrous with a high dislocation density.

**Role of Recrystallization Temperature (1 mark):**
The recrystallization temperature (T_R) acts as the **critical reference temperature** that distinguishes the two processes. The Burgers rule gives $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ for pure metals and 0.5–0.7 $T_m$ for alloys. If working is done above T_R, the process is hot working; if done below T_R, it is cold working.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**List any six differences between hot working and cold working of metals.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — any 6 points, 0.5 mark each):**

| S. No. | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Temperature above recrystallization temperature | Temperature below recrystallization temperature |
| 2 | No strain hardening (recrystallization occurs) | Strain hardening accumulates progressively |
| 3 | Low yield strength, high ductility | High yield strength, low ductility |
| 4 | Poor surface finish due to oxide scale | Excellent surface finish, no scale |
| 5 | Low equipment forces required | High equipment forces required |
| 6 | Large reductions per pass (10–100%) | Small reductions per pass (10–30%) |
| 7 | Nearly isotropic final properties | Anisotropic properties due to deformation texture |
| 8 | High energy consumption due to heating | Low energy consumption (no heating) |

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With neat stress-strain diagrams, explain the effect of **cold working on the mechanical properties** of a ductile metal. Discuss the phenomena of **recovery, recrystallization, and grain growth** in a cold-worked metal when it is subsequently heated. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A brass rod of diameter **25 mm** is to be cold drawn to a final diameter of **15 mm** in two steps. The % cold work in each step must be the same. Calculate: (i) the intermediate diameter, (ii) the total % cold work, and (iii) the true strain after the second draw. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Effect of Cold Working on Mechanical Properties (7 Marks):**

**Stress-Strain Diagram Description (2 marks):**
A typical engineering stress–strain diagram shows that as % cold work increases:
- The **yield strength (YS)** rises significantly.
- The **ultimate tensile strength (UTS)** also increases.
- The **modulus of elasticity (E)** remains essentially unchanged (a fundamental elastic property, not affected by plastic deformation).
- The **percent elongation** and **percent reduction in area** (ductility measures) drop sharply.
- The **area under the stress–strain curve** (toughness) decreases.

**Quantitative Trends (1 mark):**
For pure copper, for example, an annealed state with YS ≈ 70 MPa and elongation ≈ 50% may, after 60% cold work, become YS ≈ 350 MPa and elongation ≈ 5%.

**Three Phenomena on Heating a Cold-Worked Metal (4 marks):**

1. **Recovery (1 mark):** At relatively low heating temperatures (well below T_R), the stored strain energy is released as point defects (vacancies, interstitials) annihilate and dislocations rearrange into lower-energy configurations such as tilt walls and sub-grain boundaries. Mechanical properties change only slightly, but electrical resistivity drops significantly. The elongated cold-worked grain structure remains.

2. **Recrystallization (2 marks):** At a temperature close to the recrystallization temperature T_R, new strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate at high-energy sites such as prior grain boundaries, deformation bands, and twin boundaries. These new grains grow and consume all of the deformed parent grains, replacing the fibrous cold-worked structure with one of soft, ductile, equiaxed grains. Mechanical properties such as yield strength and ductility return to their original pre-cold-worked values. The driving force is the stored strain energy in the high dislocation density.

3. **Grain Growth (1 mark):** At still higher temperatures or longer annealing times, the smaller grains shrink and disappear, and the larger grains grow further. This is driven by the reduction in total grain boundary area and hence total grain boundary energy. Excessive grain growth causes a decrease in strength (as per the Hall-Petch relation $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$), and a decrease in toughness.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** Initial diameter $d_0 = 25$ mm, Final diameter $d_f = 15$ mm, Two equal % cold work steps.

**Part (i) — Intermediate Diameter (3 marks):**

Let the intermediate diameter be $d_1$. The % cold work in each step is equal, so:

$$r_1 = r_2 = r$$

For each step:
$$\frac{A_0 - A_1}{A_0} = \frac{A_1 - A_2}{A_1} = r$$

This means that for the diameter ratio:

$$\frac{d_0^2 - d_1^2}{d_0^2} = \frac{d_1^2 - d_f^2}{d_1^2}$$

From the first equality:
$$1 - \left(\frac{d_1}{d_0}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{d_1}{d_0}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{d_1}{d_0}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{d_f}{d_1}\right)^2$$

A simpler approach: For **equal percentage area reduction per pass**, the **diameter ratio is constant per pass**:

$$\frac{d_1}{d_0} = \frac{d_f}{d_1}$$

Therefore:
$$d_1 = \sqrt{d_0 \cdot d_f} = \sqrt{25 \times 15} = \sqrt{375} = 19.36 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (ii) — Total % Cold Work (2 marks):**

$$\%CW_{\text{total}} = \left[1 - \left(\frac{d_f}{d_0}\right)^2\right] \times 100 = \left[1 - \left(\frac{15}{25}\right)^2\right] \times 100$$

$$= [1 - 0.36] \times 100 = 64\%$$

**Part (iii) — True Strain After Second Draw (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_T = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{d_0}{d_f}\right)^2 = 2\ln\left(\frac{25}{15}\right)$$

$$= 2 \ln(1.6667) = 2 \times 0.5108 = 1.0216$$

**Summary of Answers:**
- $d_1 = 19.36$ mm
- $\%CW_{\text{total}} = 64\%$
- $\varepsilon_T = 1.022$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **Hollomon equation** and its significance in metal forming. A metal follows the relation $\sigma_T = 800 \, \varepsilon_T^{\,0.25}$ (in MPa). Find the **average flow stress** for a true strain of 0.5. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **hot working processes** of rolling, forging, and extrusion with neat sketches. List **four advantages** and **two limitations** of hot working. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hollomon Equation & Average Flow Stress (7 Marks):**

**Hollomon Equation (3 marks):**
The Hollomon equation expresses the true stress as a power-law function of true strain:

$$\sigma_T = K \, \varepsilon_T^{\,n}$$

where:
- $K$ = strength coefficient (MPa), with units of stress
- $n$ = strain hardening exponent, dimensionless, ranging from 0 to 1
- $n = 0$ represents an elastic-perfectly plastic material (no strain hardening)
- $n = 1$ represents a perfectly elastic material (rare in metals)

**Significance in Metal Forming (2 marks):**
- The Hollomon equation is the **constitutive law** used in **finite element simulations** of forging, rolling, extrusion, and sheet forming.
- The exponent $n$ is a direct measure of a metal's ability to distribute plastic strain uniformly. A higher $n$ means greater resistance to localized necking, which is critical for **deep-drawing operations** (e.g., car body panels, kitchen sinks, beverage cans).
- Typical $n$ values: mild steel ≈ 0.15–0.20, austenitic stainless steel ≈ 0.40–0.50, aluminum alloys ≈ 0.30.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $\sigma_T = 800 \, \varepsilon_T^{0.25}$ MPa, $\varepsilon_f = 0.5$.

Average flow stress:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{K \, \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{n+1} = \frac{800 \times (0.5)^{0.25}}{0.25 + 1}$$

Calculate $(0.5)^{0.25}$:

$$(0.5)^{0.25} = (0.5)^{1/4} = 0.8409$$

Then:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{800 \times 0.8409}{1.25} = \frac{672.7}{1.25} = 538.2 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{\sigma}_f \approx 538.2 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(b) Hot Working Processes (7 Marks):**

**Rolling (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Rolling is a process in which a metal ingot or billet is passed between two counter-rotating rollers to reduce its thickness or to achieve a uniform cross-section.
- **Types:** Two-high, three-high, four-high, and cluster rolling mills.
- **Products:** Sheets, plates, strips, structural sections (I-beams, channels, rails).

**Forging (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Forging is a process in which compressive forces are applied to a metal workpiece (billet or ingot) using hammers, presses, or dies to shape it into the desired geometry.
- **Types:** Open-die forging, closed-die (impression-die) forging, upset forging, roll forging.
- **Products:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, turbine shafts, hand tools.

**Extrusion (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Extrusion is a process in which a metal billet is forced through a die of the desired cross-sectional shape to produce a continuous product of uniform profile.
- **Types:** Direct (forward) extrusion, indirect (backward) extrusion, hydrostatic extrusion, impact extrusion.
- **Products:** Tubes, rods, channels, structural sections with complex profiles.

**Advantages of Hot Working (0.5 mark each, total 1 mark — list any 4):**
1. Large deformations are possible per single pass (10–100%) because of high ductility at elevated temperatures.
2. Low forming forces and low energy consumption per unit deformation (the metal is soft).
3. Refinement of the cast grain structure — porosity and shrinkage cavities are closed up.
4. Improved mechanical properties such as toughness, ductility, and impact resistance.
5. Economical for large-section components.
6. Pre-existing metallurgical defects (e.g., dendritic segregation) are broken down.

**Limitations of Hot Working (0.5 mark each, total 1 mark — list any 2):**
1. Poor surface finish due to oxide scale formation; additional descaling operations are required.
2. Poor dimensional accuracy and tolerance control due to shrinkage on cooling.
3. High energy consumption for heating the metal.
4. Tooling wears faster at elevated temperatures.
5. Risk of "hot shortness" (cracking at grain boundaries) if temperature is too high or heating time is too long.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU University Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Engineering Stress" with "True Stress":** Engineering stress uses original area $A_0$, while true stress uses instantaneous area $A_i$. Forgetting the relation $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E)$ loses **3–5 marks** in numerical problems.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Volume Constancy:** In plastic deformation of metals, $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$. Use this to relate diameter changes to length changes. Not using volume constancy is a **frequent mistake**.
> 
> 3. **Mixing Up the Hollomon Constant $K$ with Yield Strength:** $K$ is the extrapolated true stress at $\varepsilon_T = 1$, **not** the yield strength. Students often write $\sigma_y = K$ — this is wrong.
> 
> 4. **Confusing T_R with T_m:** The recrystallization temperature is approximately **0.4 T_m (K)**, not the melting point itself. Working at T_m would melt the metal.
> 
> 5. **Missing Recovery vs Recrystallization Distinction:** Many students write that "annealing" produces new grains. The correct sequence is: **Recovery (no new grains) → Recrystallization (new grains) → Grain Growth (existing grains enlarge)**. Examiners specifically test this with sub-part questions.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Answers:** % CW is dimensionless but final stress should be in MPa. Always state units in your final answer.
> 
> 7. **Sketches Missing in Descriptive Questions:** For forging, rolling, and extrusion questions, a labeled diagram carries **2–3 marks** by itself. KTU examiners explicitly reward sketches.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Read this section 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Hot working** is performed above the **recrystallization temperature T_R**; **cold working** is performed below T_R.
- [ ] **Burgers Rule:** $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ (pure metals); $T_R \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7 \, T_m$ for alloys (T in Kelvin).
- [ ] **% Cold Work:** $\%CW = \left[1 - (A_f/A_0)\right] \times 100$ or equivalently $\left[1 - (d_f/d_0)^2\right] \times 100$ for round bars.
- [ ] **True Strain:** $\varepsilon_T = \ln(L_f/L_0) = \ln(A_0/A_f)$; for volume constancy, both forms are equal.
- [ ] **True Stress:** $\sigma_T = \sigma_E (1 + \varepsilon_E)$.
- [ ] **Hollomon Equation:** $\sigma_T = K \, \varepsilon_T^{\,n}$ — fundamental constitutive law for plastic deformation.
- [ ] **Average Flow Stress from Hollomon:** $\bar{\sigma}_f = K \varepsilon_f^{\,n}/(n+1) = \sigma_f/(n+1)$.
- [ ] **Hall-Petch Relation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$ — smaller grains mean higher strength.
- [ ] **Three Stages on Heating a Cold-Worked Metal:**
  - **Recovery** — point defect annihilation, dislocation rearrangement, minor property change.
  - **Recrystallization** — new strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate and grow; properties revert to pre-cold-worked state.
  - **Grain Growth** — large grains grow at the expense of small ones; strength decreases.
- [ ] **Effect of Cold Work:** YS ↑, UTS ↑, Hardness ↑, % Elongation ↓, % RA ↓, Impact Resistance ↓.
- [ ] **Effect of Hot Work:** Refined equiaxed grains, no strain hardening, low residual stress, good ductility and toughness.
- [ ] **Hot Working Examples:** Hot rolling, forging, hot extrusion, hot drawing of rods/tubes.
- [ ] **Cold Working Examples:** Cold rolling, deep drawing, wire drawing, cold heading, stretch forming.
- [ ] **Incompressibility (Volume Constancy):** $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ — holds for plastic deformation in metals.
- [ ] **Strain rate:** $\dot{\varepsilon} = v/L_0$ where $v$ is ram velocity, $L_0$ is initial length.
- [ ] **KTU High-Yield Equation Priority List (in order of exam frequency):**
    1. % Cold Work formula
    2. True stress and true strain formulas
    3. Hollomon equation
    4. Average flow stress from Hollomon
    5. Hall-Petch equation
    6. Recrystallization temperature (Burgers rule)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Heat Treatments # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Heat Treatment? **Heat Treatment** is a controlled combination of heating and cooling operations applied to metallic materials (predominantly steels and alloys) in the solid state, designed to alter their **microstructure** and thereby obtain specific desired **mechanical, physical, and chemical properties** — such as hardness, tensile strength, ductility, toughness, and wear resistance — without changing the product's shape or overall chemical composition. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT412):** *Heat treatment is a process of controlled heating and cooling of metals/alloys in the solid state to obtain desirable microstructures and properties.* The three essential variables are: **(1) Heating Temperature, (2) Holding (Soaking) Time, and (3) Cooling Rate.** ### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Any Heat Treatment | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Steel) | Effect of Increasing | |---|---|---|---| | Austenitizing Temperature | $T_{\gamma}$ | 723 °C – 950 °C | More grain growth, more carbon dissolution | | Soaking Time | $t_s$ | 15 min – 2 hr | More homogeneity, risk of decarburization | | Cooling Rate | $dT/dt$ | Air → Oil → Water → Brine | Higher hardness, more distortion | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **The Chocolate-Cookie Analogy:** Imagine a piece of **white chocolate (steel at room temperature)** — soft and breakable. If you melt it in a microwave (heat above melting), pour it into a mold, and **let it cool slowly on the kitchen counter (annealing)**, you get a soft, ductile chocolate bar. If you instead **freeze it rapidly in the freezer (quenching)**, it becomes a glassy, brittle hard chocolate. Now, if you take that hard brittle bar and leave it on the counter for a few hours (tempering), it returns to a balanced "crispy-outside, chewy-inside" state. **Heat treatment works the same way on steel at the microstructural level** — only instead of melting, we cycle the steel through phase transformations in the solid state. ## 1.4 Why Heat Treatment? — The Engineering Motivation - **Machinability:** Soft pearlite (annealed) is easy to cut. - **Wear Resistance:** Hard martensite surface resists abrasion. - **Toughness:** Toughened shafts absorb impact without breaking. - **Stress Relief:** Welded structures have residual stresses that need removal. - **Spring Action:** Springs require high yield strength + elasticity. ## 1.5 Classification of Heat Treatments (KTU 2024 Map) ``` HEAT TREATMENT ├── 1. Annealing (Softening family) │ ├── Full Annealing │ ├── Process Annealing │ ├── Stress-Relief Annealing │ ├── Spheroidizing Annealing │ └── Recrystallization Annealing ├── 2. Normalizing (Refining) ├── 3. Hardening (Quenching) ├── 4. Tempering (Re-softening after hardening) ├── 5. Case Hardening (Surface only) │ ├── Carburizing (solid / liquid / gas) │ ├── Nitriding │ ├── Cyaniding │ └── Carbonitriding └── 6. Surface Hardening (Phase-change only) ├── Induction Hardening ├── Flame Hardening └── Laser / Electron Beam Hardening ``` # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Iron–Iron Carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) Phase Diagram Foundation Before any heat treatment can be designed, one **must** understand the **Fe–Fe₃C diagram**. It is the "road map" that tells the heat-treater at what temperature austenite ($\gamma$-Fe, FCC) forms and at what temperature carbon becomes soluble. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Temperatures (for plain carbon steel):** > - $A_1 = 727\ °C$ — Eutectoid temperature (Pearlite → Austenite on heating) > - $A_3$ — Upper critical temperature for **hypoeutectoid** steels ($\alpha \rightarrow \gamma$ finish line) > - $A_{cm}$ — Upper critical temperature for **hypereutectoid** steels (Cementite dissolution line) > - $A_2 = 770\ °C$ — Curie temperature (magnetic → non-magnetic) > - On cooling, the analogous points shift slightly and are labelled $A_{r1}, A_{r3}, A_{r{cm}}$ — always *lower* than $A_1, A_3, A_{cm}$ due to thermal hysteresis. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of All Major Heat Treatments ### 2.2.1 Annealing (The Softening Family) **Goal:** Maximize ductility, reduce hardness, relieve internal stresses, refine grain. #### (a) Full Annealing 1. Heat steel to **30–50 °C above** $A_3$ (for hypoeutectoid) or $A_1$ (for eutectoid/hypereutectoid). 2. Soak until complete **austenitization** (uniform $\gamma$-Fe + dissolved carbon). 3. Furnace cool (very slow, ~10–30 °C/hr through the critical range). 4. Microstructure: **Coarse Pearlite + Ferrite** (hypoeutectoid) or **Coarse Pearlite + Cementite** (hypereutectoid). 5. Result: **Lowest hardness, maximum ductility.** #### (b) Process Annealing (Sub-critical Annealing) 1. Heat to **just below** $A_1$ (~550–650 °C). 2. No phase change — only **recovery and partial recrystallization**. 3. Used for **cold-worked** steels (relieve strain hardening between rolling/drawing passes). 4. Microstructure: Stress-relieved ferrite + spheroidized carbides (partially). #### (c) Stress-Relief Annealing 1. Heat to **450–650 °C** (well below $A_1$). 2. Hold 1–2 hr; furnace cool. 3. **No microstructural phase change.** Only dislocation rearrangement reduces residual stresses. 4. Used after welding, machining, casting. #### (d) Spheroidizing Annealing 1. Heat just above $A_1$ (~$740$ °C) for prolonged time, OR cycle around $A_1$ repeatedly. 2. Cementite lamellae in pearlite break up and form **globular/spheroidal Fe₃C particles** in a soft ferrite matrix. 3. **Lowest possible hardness** — best for machinability of high-carbon steels. #### (e) Recrystallization Annealing 1. Heat cold-worked steel above its **recrystallization temperature** ($T_R \approx 0.4\,T_m$, where $T_m$ is in Kelvin). 2. New strain-free equiaxed grains nucleate and grow, replacing the deformed grains. 3. Used for sheet/wire products. ### 2.2.2 Normalizing 1. Heat steel to **30–50 °C above** $A_3$ or $A_{cm}$ (austenitizing). 2. Soak for uniform composition. 3. **Cool in still air** (faster than furnace, slower than oil). 4. Microstructure: **Fine Pearlite + Ferrite** (hypoeutectoid) — finer than full anneal. 5. Result: **Higher strength and hardness than full anneal**, but more uniform than as-rolled. > [!TIP] > **Why "Normalize"?** The resulting grain size is considered the *normal* reference for a given composition. ### 2.2.3 Hardening (Quenching) 1. Heat to **30–50 °C above** $A_3$ (or $A_1$ + 30 °C for hypereutectoid). 2. Soak for complete austenitization. 3. **Rapid cool** in water, brine, oil, or polymer quenchant (faster than the critical cooling rate $CCR$). 4. Microstructure: **Martensite** (BCT, body-centered tetragonal — supersaturated carbon trapped in distorted iron lattice). 5. Result: **Maximum hardness, very low ductility, high residual stresses.** > [!WARNING] > **Quenching Cracking Risk:** If cooling is too severe or non-uniform, **thermal gradients** generate stresses that cause **quench cracks** — a common failure mode in poorly hardened gears and tools. #### Severity of Quenchants (Grossmann–H-Value concept) $$\Huge H = \text{Heat transfer effectiveness} \uparrow \implies \text{Cooling rate} \uparrow$$ | Quenchant | Relative H-Value | Severity | |---|---|---| | Still Air | 0.02 | Lowest | | Oil | 0.25 – 0.30 | Medium | | Water | 0.9 – 1.0 | High | | 10% Brine | 2.0+ | Highest | ### 2.2.4 Tempering (Re-toughening of Martensite) 1. Heat **already-quenched (martensitic)** steel to a temperature **below $A_1$**. 2. Hold, then cool (usually air cool). 3. Microstructural evolution: **$\alpha$-Fe + fine Fe₃C precipitates.** 4. Result: **Reduced hardness, increased toughness and ductility** (a controlled trade-off). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Stages of Tempering (for plain carbon steels):** > - **Stage I (100–250 °C):** Precipitation of **$\epsilon$-carbide** (transition carbide) → reduces some brittleness. > - **Stage II (200–300 °C):** Decomposition of **retained austenite** → bainite. > - **Stage III (250–400 °C):** Formation of **fine cementite (Fe₃C)** in ferrite → final tempered martensite. > - Above 400 °C: **Coarsening** of cementite → further softening. #### Types of Tempering | Type | Temperature Range | Hardness (HRC) | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Low-temperature tempering | 150–250 °C | 60–65 | Cutting tools, bearings | | Medium-temperature | 350–450 °C | 45–55 | Springs, chisels | | High-temperature | 550–650 °C | 25–35 | Gears, crankshafts, structural parts | ### 2.2.5 Case Hardening — Surface-Only Hardening #### (a) Carburizing (Pack / Liquid / Gas) 1. Heat steel in a **carbon-rich atmosphere** (charcoal + BaCO₃ pack, or methane/propane gas) at **850–950 °C**. 2. Carbon diffuses inward from the surface (Fick's Second Law governs the profile). 3. Result: **High-carbon "case" (~0.8–1.2 %C)** over a **low-carbon "core" (~0.2 %C).** 4. Subsequent **quench + temper** gives a hard wear-resistant case with a tough core. 5. **Case depth:** typically 0.5–3 mm. #### (b) Nitriding 1. Heat steel (often alloyed with **Al, Cr, Mo** for nitride formation) to **500–550 °C**. 2. Expose to **ammonia (NH₃) gas**, which dissociates into **atomic nitrogen** + H₂. 3. Atomic N diffuses inward → forms **hard nitride compounds** (Fe₄N, Fe₂₄N, AlN, CrN). 4. **No quenching required** — distortion is minimal. 5. Very high surface hardness (~1000 HV), excellent fatigue and wear resistance. > [!TIP] > **Nitriding vs. Carburizing:** Nitriding works *below* $A_1$ (no phase transformation), so no quench distortion. Carburizing requires *above* $A_3$ austenitization and a subsequent quench. #### (c) Cyaniding & Carbonitriding - **Cyaniding:** Immerse steel in molten **NaCN bath** at 800–870 °C. Adds C and N simultaneously. Shallow case (~0.25 mm). - **Carbonitriding:** Heat in **NH₃ + hydrocarbon gas mixture** at 700–900 °C. Faster than gas carburizing, shallower case (~0.75 mm). ### 2.2.6 Surface Hardening (Phase-Change Only, No Chemistry Change) 1. Heat **only the surface layer** above $A_3$ using rapid localized energy. 2. Immediately quench. 3. **Surface:** martensitic → hard. **Core:** unchanged (tough, ductile). #### Methods - **Flame Hardening:** Oxy-acetylene flame heats surface. - **Induction Hardening:** High-frequency AC coil induces eddy-current heating in surface (skin depth $\delta$). - **Laser / Electron Beam Hardening:** Extremely localized, minimal distortion. ## 2.3 The Time–Temperature–Transformation (TTT) Diagram — The Master Key > [!IMPORTANT] > The **TTT (or isothermal) diagram** (also called **IT-diagram or S-curve**) is THE central tool for designing any heat treatment of steel. It plots **temperature** (Y-axis) vs **log(time)** (X-axis) and shows **start (S) and finish (F) curves** for various transformation products. ### Cooling Curve Analysis - **Slow furnace cool** (Path crosses near nose but at long times) → **Coarse Pearlite** (soft). - **Faster air cool** → **Fine Pearlite** (stronger). - **Hold just above nose** → **Bainite** (acicular, strong + tough). - **Rapid quench below nose, then hold at intermediate T** → **Bainite** (Austempering). - **Very rapid quench to room temperature** → **Martensite** (a-thermal, diffusionless transformation). ### The Martensite Transformation $$M_s = 500 - 300\,(\%C) - 33\,(\%Mn) - 22\,(\%Cr) - 17\,(\%Ni) - 11\,(\%Mo + \%W)$$ $$M_{50} = M_s - 215 \quad (\text{for low-alloy steels})$$ where $M_s$ = Martensite-start temperature (°C), $M_{50}$ = temperature at which 50% martensite has formed. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are mathematically verified and high-frequency in KTU university exam questions.** | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $d_c = D\sqrt{t}$ (parabolic approx.) | Carburizing/nitriding depth ~ √time | mm | | 2 | $M_s = 500 - 300\,C - 33\,Mn - 22\,Cr - 17\,Ni - 11\,(Mo+W)$ | Martensite start temp (Carapella–Andrews) | °C | | 3 | $HRC_{eq} \approx 60 - 0.5\,T_{temp}$ (rule of thumb) | Hardness drops ~0.5 HRC/°C tempering | HRC | | 4 | $T_R \approx 0.4\,T_m$ | Recrystallization temperature | K | | 5 | $dT/dt > CCR$ | Condition for martensite formation | °C/s | | 6 | $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho/(\mu\,\omega\,\pi)}$ | Induction skin depth | m | | 7 | $H_{total} = \sum H_i$ | Net hardness gain from combined treatments | HRC | | 8 | $X = 1 - \exp(-kt^n)$ (Avrami) | Fraction transformed vs time | — | **Boundary Conditions / Critical Notes for KTU:** - Plain eutectoid steel: 0.77 %C. - $A_1$ is invariant at 727 °C regardless of composition. - Quench severity must exceed the **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** for full martensite. ## 2.5 Engineering Applications — Where Each Treatment is Used | Treatment | Industries / Components | |---|---| | Full Annealing | Boiler tubes, structural sections | | Normalizing | Rails, gears (pre-heat-treat) | | Quench + Temper | Crankshafts, axles, high-strength bolts | | Carburizing | Transmission gears, camshafts | | Nitriding | Diesel engine cylinders, dies, gauges | | Induction Hardening | Bearing races, splined shafts | | Stress Relief | Welded bridges, pressure vessels | | Spheroidizing | High-carbon tool-steel blanks for machining | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Carburizing Case Depth (Fick's Second Law — Diffusion) For a semi-infinite solid with constant surface concentration $C_s$ and initial bulk concentration $C_0$: $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$ **Boundary Conditions:** - $C(x=0, t) = C_s$ (constant surface concentration) - $C(x=\infty, t) = C_0$ (bulk far away) - $C(x, t=0) = C_0$ **Solution (Carslaw & Jaeger):** $$\frac{C(x,t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$ Now derive the **effective case depth** $d_c$ (where $C = C_0 + 0.5\,(C_s - C_0)$, i.e., 50 % of surface enrichment): $$\frac{0.5\,(C_s - C_0)}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{d_c}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$ $$\Rightarrow \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{d_c}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = 0.5$$ Using $\operatorname{erf}^{-1}(0.5) = 0.4769$: $$\frac{d_c}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = 0.4769$$ $$\boxed{\;d_c \approx 0.9538\,\sqrt{Dt}\;\approx\; \sqrt{Dt}\;}$$ This shows the **parabolic law**: doubling time does not double the depth; it only multiplies it by $\sqrt{2}$. **Worked Example (KTU Style):** A steel gear is gas-carburized at 927 °C ($D = 1.28 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$). Calculate the time needed to obtain an effective case depth of 0.8 mm. $$t = \frac{d_c^{\,2}}{D} = \frac{(0.8\times 10^{-3})^2}{1.28\times 10^{-11}} = 5.0\times 10^{4}\ \text{s} \approx 13.9\ \text{h}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Martensite-Start Temperature (Empirical Formula) The **Carapella–Andrews linear regression** combines the effect of alloying elements. We start with the basic form: $$M_s = 500\ °C - 300\,C - 33\,Mn - 22\,Cr - 17\,Ni - 11\,Mo - 11\,W$$ Coefficients (in wt%) are derived experimentally by **multiple linear regression** on hundreds of steels. Let's demonstrate derivation of one coefficient (Mn): For two steels differing only in Mn by 1 wt% (all other elements equal), data shows: - Steel A: Mn = 0.5 %, $M_s$ = 340 °C - Steel B: Mn = 1.5 %, $M_s$ = 307 °C $$\text{Slope} = \frac{340 - 307}{0.5 - 1.5} = \frac{33}{-1} = -33\ °C/\text{wt\%}\ \text{Mn}$$ This matches the published coefficient of **–33 °C/wt% Mn**. **Worked Example:** Steel composition: 0.4 %C, 0.7 %Mn, 1.0 %Cr, 0.3 %Ni. $$M_s = 500 - 300(0.4) - 33(0.7) - 22(1.0) - 17(0.3)$$ $$M_s = 500 - 120 - 23.1 - 22 - 5.1$$ $$\boxed{\;M_s = 329.8\ °C\;}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Cooling Rate (CCR) — Geometric Interpretation on TTT The **CCR** is the *minimum cooling rate* that just *misses* the nose of the TTT curve. $$\text{CCR} = \frac{A_{e1} - T_{nose}}{t_{nose}}$$ where: - $A_{e1} = 727\ °C$ (or $A_{e3}$ for hypoeutectoid) - $T_{nose} \approx 550\ °C$ for eutectoid steel - $t_{nose} \approx 1\ \text{s}$ for plain carbon steel (much longer, ~10 s, for alloyed) **For 0.77 %C plain steel:** $$\text{CCR} = \frac{727 - 550}{1} = 177\ °C/\text{s}$$ This is why plain carbon steel needs **water/brine**, while alloyed steels can use **oil** (lower CCR due to shifted nose). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hardness Drop with Tempering (Hollomon–Jaffe Parameter) For isothermal tempering, the **Hollomon–Jaffe parameter** combines time and temperature: $$HJP = T\,(\log t + C)$$ where $T$ = tempering temperature (K), $t$ = time (s), $C$ ≈ 20 (constant). Same hardness is obtained for the same $HJP$. Solving for time at a given temperature: $$t = 10^{\,(HJP/T) - C}$$ **Worked Example:** To achieve HRC = 40 in 0.4 %C steel, $HJP/T$ must equal 16.5 at 873 K. Time required: $$t = 10^{16.5 - 20} = 10^{-3.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} \approx 0.32\ \text{ms}$$ (At 873 K this is impractical — instead temper at lower T for longer time.) ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide | Required Property | Recommended Heat Treatment | Why | |---|---|---| | Maximum surface hardness | Carburize + Quench + Low-temp temper | High C + martensite at surface | | Maximum toughness | Quench + **High-temp temper** (550–650 °C) | Tempered martensite (sorbite) | | Best machinability | Spheroidize anneal | Globular carbides shear easily | | Dimensional stability after machining | Stress-relief anneal | Removes residual stresses | | Wear resistance + impact | Induction harden + low-temp temper | Hard surface, tough core | | Spring action | Quench + Medium-temp temper | High yield + sufficient ductility | | Eliminate weld stresses | Stress-relief anneal (~600 °C) | Dislocation rearrangement | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Heat Treatment Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Steel Stock] --> B{Austenitize?
Heat above Ac1} B -- Yes, slow cool --> C[Full Anneal
Coarse Pearlite] B -- Yes, air cool --> D[Normalize
Fine Pearlite] B -- Yes, water quench --> E[Martensite
Hard + Brittle] E --> F[Tempering] F --> F1[Low T - Tools] F --> F2[Med T - Springs] F --> F3[High T - Gears] B -- No, below Ac1 --> G[Sub-critical Process] G --> G1[Stress Relief 450-650 C] G --> G2[Spheroidize ~740 C] A --> H[Case Hardening Route] H --> H1[Carburize 850-950 C + Quench] H --> H2[Nitriding 500-550 C no quench] H --> H3[Surface Hardening: Induction / Flame] H1 --> F H2 --> F1 ``` ## 4.2 TTT Diagram Block Architecture (Functional Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TTT["TTT / S-Curve Architecture"] T[Temperature Axis
vertical] --> N[Nose Region
~550 C / ~1 sec] N --> P1[Upper: Austenite] N --> P2[Right: Pearlite Formation] N --> P3[Intermediate: Bainite] N --> P4[Bottom: Martensite a-thermal] end CC1[Path A: Furnace Cool - Pearlite] -.-> P2 CC2[Path B: Oil Cool - Bainite or fine Pearlite] -.-> P2 CC3[Path C: Rapid Quench - Miss nose - Martensite] -.-> P4 CC4[Path D: Austenempering - Bainite] -.-> P3 ``` ## 4.3 Annealing Sub-Types Decision Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Steel in As-rolled / As-cast State] --> Q1{Need to
soften for
machining?} Q1 -- Yes, high C --> SP[Spheroidize Anneal
~740 C / long time] Q1 -- Yes, low/med C --> FA[Full Anneal
Ac3 + 30 C / furnace cool] Q1 -- No, just cold-worked --> PA[Process Anneal
~600 C] Start --> Q2{Internal
stresses
present?} Q2 -- Yes, e.g., welded --> SR[Stress Relief
450-650 C] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need uniformity
+ strength?} Q3 -- Yes --> NM[Normalize
Ac3 + 30 C / air cool] ``` ## 4.4 Case Hardening Process Block Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CH[Case Hardening Family] CR[Carburizing
C enrichment
850-950 C] --> Q1[Subsequent Quench] NT[Nitriding
N enrichment
500-550 C] --> Q2[No Quench Needed] CY[Cyaniding
C + N
NaCN bath 800-870 C] --> Q3[Quench] CN[Carbonitriding
NH3 + HC gas
700-900 C] --> Q4[Quench] end Q1 --> R1[Hard Case + Tough Core] Q2 --> R2[Very Hard Distortion-free Case] Q3 --> R3[Shallow Hard Case] Q4 --> R4[Shallow Hard Case] ``` ## 4.5 Mechanical Property vs Tempering Temperature (Schematic Curves) ```mermaid flowchart LR T[Tempering Temperature C] --> H[Hardness HRC
decreases monotonically] T --> TS[Tensile Strength MPa
decreases gradually] T --> D[Ductility percent elongation
increases] T --> K[Impact Toughness J
increases then may dip] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define *full annealing* and *normalizing*. State two differences between them. **Model Answer:** - **Full Annealing:** Heating hypoeutectoid steel to ~30–50 °C above $A_3$, soaking for complete austenitization, then **furnace cooling**. Yields **coarse pearlite + ferrite**, with low hardness and high ductility. - **Normalizing:** Heating to 30–50 °C above $A_3$/$A_{cm}$ and cooling in **still air**. Yields **fine pearlite + ferrite** with higher strength and slightly higher hardness. **Differences:** | Parameter | Full Annealing | Normalizing | |---|---|---| | Cooling medium | Furnace | Still air | | Hardness | Lower | Higher (finer pearlite) | | Grain size | Coarser | Finer | | Purpose | Maximum softness | Uniformity + moderate strength | *(3 marks — 1 for definitions, 2 for differences)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is *tempering*? Why is it necessary after quenching? **Model Answer:** **Tempering** is a sub-critical heat treatment in which quenched (martensitic) steel is re-heated to a temperature **below $A_1$** (typically 150–650 °C), held, and cooled. It causes precipitation of fine cementite from the supersaturated martensite. **Necessity:** 1. Quenched martensite is **extremely hard but brittle** with high internal stresses — service failure risk. 2. Tempering reduces **brittleness**, relieves **quench stresses**, and improves **toughness and ductility**. 3. It gives a controlled balance of **hardness vs toughness** depending on tempering temperature. *(3 marks — 1 definition + 2 necessity)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the following heat treatments: *(i)* Full Annealing, *(ii)* Normalizing, *(iii)* Spheroidizing Annealing, *(iv)* Stress Relief Annealing. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 0.4 %C steel gear of 60 mm thickness is to be case-hardened by carburizing at 927 °C. The diffusion coefficient of carbon in austenite at this temperature is $D = 1.6 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the time required to obtain a case depth of 1.0 mm. If the treatment temperature is increased to 1027 °C ($D = 4.5 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$), how long will it take to obtain the same depth? Comment on the result. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Full Annealing:** (Process already detailed in Section 2.2.1 a) — *Hypoeutectoid steel heated 30–50 °C above $A_3$, soaked, furnace cooled → coarse pearlite + ferrite.* Use **TTT diagram reference** showing slow cooling curve crossing the nose well to the right. **Normalizing:** Heated 30–50 °C above $A_3$, soaked, **air cooled** → **fine pearlite + ferrite**. Air-cool curve crosses nose earlier than furnace-cool. **Spheroidizing Annealing:** Heat just above $A_1$ (~740 °C), prolonged soak, OR cycle around $A_1$ → cementite lamellae spheroidize into **globular particles in ferrite** → minimum hardness, best machinability. **Stress-Relief Annealing:** Heat to **450–650 °C**, hold 1–2 hr, furnace cool → **no phase transformation**, only dislocation rearrangement, residual stress reduction. *(Distribute 7 marks: ~1.75 each)* **(b) Solution:** Using $d_c = \sqrt{Dt} \Rightarrow t = d_c^{\,2}/D$ **Case 1: 927 °C** $$t_1 = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})^2}{1.6 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.25 \times 10^{4}\ \text{s} = 17.36\ \text{h}$$ **Case 2: 1027 °C** $$t_2 = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4.5 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.22 \times 10^{4}\ \text{s} = 6.17\ \text{h}$$ **Comment:** A 100 °C rise in temperature reduces carburizing time by a factor of ~2.8 due to the **exponential increase of D with T** (Arrhenius: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$). Higher temperatures accelerate diffusion dramatically but risk **grain growth**, so 927–950 °C is the practical upper limit. *(7 marks — 2 for equation, 2 each case, 1 for comment)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch of the **TTT (isothermal transformation) diagram** for eutectoid steel, explain the formation of **(i)** Fine Pearlite, **(ii)** Bainite, and **(iii)** Martensite. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A plain carbon steel contains 0.55 %C, 0.8 %Mn, 0.3 %Si, 0.05 %S, 0.05 %P. Calculate the martensite-start temperature $M_s$. If the steel is quenched from 850 °C to 25 °C in oil, name the phases obtained and estimate the Rockwell C hardness if it is subsequently tempered at 500 °C for 1 hour. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) TTT Diagram Description:** Axes: **Temperature (Y)** vs **log time (X)**. Two C-curves (start and finish) with nose near **550 °C, 1 second**. - **(i) Fine Pearlite:** Slow cool to just above nose → hold → thin alternating ferrite + cementite lamellae → higher strength than coarse pearlite. - **(ii) Bainite:** Rapid cool to **250–550 °C** (just below nose) → hold → acicular (needle-like) ferrite + dispersed carbides → high strength + good toughness. - **(iii) Martensite:** Very rapid cool (faster than nose) to room temperature → diffusionless shear transformation → **BCT martensite**, maximum hardness, a-thermal (forms continuously as $T$ drops below $M_s$). *(7 marks — 2 diagram + 5 microstructural description)* **(b) Solution:** Using $M_s = 500 - 300\,C - 33\,Mn - 22\,Cr - 17\,Ni - 11\,(Mo+W)$: $$M_s = 500 - 300(0.55) - 33(0.8) - 22(0) - 17(0) - 11(0)$$ $$M_s = 500 - 165 - 26.4 = 308.6\ °C$$ Since the quench ends at 25 °C $\ll M_s$, **full martensite** is obtained. **Phases after oil quenching:** Predominantly **martensite** (with small fraction of retained austenite, ~5–10 %). **Hardness after quenching:** ~62–64 HRC (medium-carbon martensite). **After tempering at 500 °C for 1 hr:** This is **high-temperature tempering** (sorbite/tempered martensite region). $$\Huge HRC \approx 62 - 0.5 \times (500 - 100) \approx 42\ \text{HRC}$$ (Using approximate rule of 0.5 HRC drop per °C tempering above 100 °C.) *(7 marks — 2 for $M_s$, 2 for phase ID, 3 for hardness estimate)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Examination Patterns):** > 1. **Forgetting to state the TTT-diagram region** in martensite questions — Examiners award 1 mark specifically for "diffusionless, a-thermal" terminology. > 2. **Using $A_3$ for hypereutectoid steel instead of $A_1$** when designing annealing temperature. Use $A_3 + 30\ °C$ for hypoeutectoid; $A_1 + 30\ °C$ for eutectoid and hypereutectoid. > 3. **Confusing carburizing with nitriding temperatures** — Carburizing is **above** $A_1$ (austenite exists, C diffuses fast), nitriding is **below** $A_1$ (no phase change). > 4. **Skipping units in carburizing time calculation** — Always quote answer in **hours** (not just seconds) for practical relevance. > 5. **Writing "tempering increases hardness"** — It is the opposite; tempering *decreases* hardness but *increases* toughness. > 6. **Not mentioning quench severity (H-value)** when justifying choice of quenchant — Examiners expect explicit comparison. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PECHT412 – Module 2 Heat Treatments** - **Heat Treatment:** Solid-state heating + cooling to alter microstructure without changing shape/composition. - **Three control variables:** Temperature, Soaking time, Cooling rate. - **Fe–Fe₃C critical temperatures:** $A_1 = 727\ °C$, $A_2 = 770\ °C$ (Curie), $A_3$ (hypoeutectoid upper), $A_{cm}$ (hypereutectoid upper). - **Full Anneal:** Above $A_3$, furnace cool → coarse pearlite. - **Normalize:** Above $A_3$, air cool → fine pearlite. - **Process Anneal:** Below $A_1$, no phase change (cold-worked relief). - **Stress Relief:** 450–650 °C, no phase change. - **Spheroidize:** Just above $A_1$, prolonged → globular carbides. - **Quench (Harden):** Above $A_3$, rapid cool → martensite (BCT, hard + brittle). - **Temper:** Below $A_1$, reheat quenched steel → tempered martensite (sorbite). - **Carburizing:** 850–950 °C, C-enrichment, **above $A_3$, requires quench**. - **Nitriding:** 500–550 °C, N-enrichment, **below $A_1$, no quench**. - **Induction Hardening:** Surface only, above $A_3$, immediate quench. - **TTT nose location:** ~550 °C, ~1 s (plain eutectoid steel). - **Martensite Start:** $M_s = 500 - 300C - 33Mn - 22Cr - 17Ni - 11Mo - 11W$. - **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR):** Minimum rate to miss nose of TTT → martensite. - **Avrami equation:** $X = 1 - \exp(-kt^n)$ for fraction transformed. - **Carburizing depth law:** $d_c \propto \sqrt{Dt}$ (parabolic). - **Tempering stages:** I (ε-carbide), II (austenite decomposition), III (Fe₃C precipitation). - **Hollomon–Jaffe parameter:** $HJP = T(\log t + C)$ — same HJP gives same hardness. - **Severity ranking:** Brine > Water > Oil > Air > Furnace. - **Quench cracks:** Caused by excessive thermal gradients — control quenchant severity. - **Diffusionless vs Diffusional:** Martensite = diffusionless; Pearlite/Bainite = diffusional. - **Alloying effect:** Mn, Ni, Cr, Mo shift TTT nose to the **right** (lower CCR needed). - **KTU favorite 14-mark question:** Always a TTT-diagram-based problem with $M_s$ calculation + phase identification.

topics.title: Elementary Study of Various Metals and Alloys like Cast Iron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Elementary Study of Various Metals and Alloys like Cast Iron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Cast Iron — The Engineering Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Cast Iron** is a generic term describing a broad family of **ferrous alloys** containing **iron (Fe)** as the principal metallic element, with a **carbon content greater than 2.0%** (typically **2.0% – 4.0%**) along with varying amounts of **silicon (Si)**, **manganese (Mn)**, **sulphur (S)**, and **phosphorus (P)**. Unlike steel, the high carbon content in cast iron makes it effectively **unworkable in the solid state** — it must be shaped by **casting** (pouring molten metal into a mould), which is the origin of its name.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU PECHT412 Module 2**
> The classification of cast iron is based on the **metallurgical form of carbon** present in the microstructure:
> 1. **Combined Carbon** → present as iron carbide (Fe₃C) → **White Cast Iron**
> 2. **Free Carbon (Graphite)** → present as flakes → **Grey Cast Iron**
> 3. **Temper Carbon (Irregular/Rosette)** → produced by heat treatment → **Malleable Cast Iron**
> 4. **Spheroidal Graphite** → produced by Mg/Ce treatment → **Nodular / Ductile Cast Iron**
> 5. **Compacted Graphite** → short, interconnected flakes → **CG Iron**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Understanding Cast Iron in Plain English"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The School Tiffin Box Analogy**
>
> Imagine a metal's microstructure as a **tiffin box filled with rice and curry (carbon)**:
>
> - If the curry is **mixed uniformly into the rice** (carbon locked into Fe₃C) → you get a **hard, brittle tiffin (White Cast Iron)**.
> - If the curry is served **separately as a side dish with long noodles** (graphite flakes) → you get a **soft, machinable tiffin that cracks along the noodles (Grey Cast Iron)**.
> - If the noodles are **broken into small round balls (spheroids)** → the tiffin becomes **strong and ductile (Nodular Cast Iron)**.
> - If the rice and curry are **re-cooked together into soft clumps (temper carbon)** → you get **Malleable Cast Iron**, which can be bent without breaking.
>
> This analogy maps directly onto how **carbon morphology** dictates the **mechanical behaviour** of every cast iron grade.

---

## 1.3 Standard Composition Limits & Critical Constants

| Element | Symbol | Typical Range (wt %) | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Iron | Fe | 92 – 96 | Base metal (matrix) |
| Carbon | C | 2.0 – 4.0 | Determines grade & microstructure |
| Silicon | Si | 0.5 – 3.5 | Strong **graphitizer** (promotes graphite) |
| Manganese | Mn | 0.2 – 1.5 | Acts as **carbide stabilizer**; counteracts S |
| Sulphur | S | 0.02 – 0.15 | Impurity; kept low |
| Phosphorus | P | 0.05 – 1.0 | Improves fluidity, increases brittleness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Eutectic Constants in the Fe–Fe₃C System (must memorize)**
> - **Eutectic temperature:** **1147 °C (1420 K)**
> - **Eutectic composition:** **4.30% C** (ledeburite point)
> - **Eutectoid temperature:** **727 °C (1000 K)**
> - **Eutectoid composition:** **0.76% C** (pearlite point)
> - **Max solubility of C in γ-Fe (austenite):** **2.14% at 1147 °C**
> - **Max solubility of C in α-Fe (ferrite):** **0.025% at 727 °C**
> - **Carbon content of Fe₃C (cementite):** **6.67% C**

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview — The Cast Iron Family Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Mapping**
> The most frequently examined cast iron types in KTU ESE papers are:
> 1. **White Cast Iron** (Fe₃C + pearlite)
> 2. **Grey Cast Iron** (Graphite flakes + ferrite/pearlite)
> 3. **Malleable Cast Iron** (Temper carbon rosettes + ferrite/pearlite)
> 4. **Nodular (Ductile) Cast Iron** (Spheroidal graphite + ferrite/pearlite)
> 5. **Alloy Cast Irons** (Ni-hard, high-Cr, Si-iron)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fe–Fe₃C Equilibrium Phase Diagram — The Master Key

The entire classification of cast iron is governed by the **iron–iron carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) equilibrium phase diagram**. Understanding this diagram is the single most important requirement for solving KTU numericals and theory questions on cast iron.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Fe–Fe₃C diagram matters:**
> It tells us **which phase will form** at a given **temperature** and **carbon content** during **equilibrium cooling** of an iron–carbon alloy. Cast irons sit on the **right-hand (high-carbon) side** of the eutectoid point (0.76% C).

### Critical Points on the Fe–Fe₃C Diagram

| Point | Temperature (°C) | % Carbon | Phases in Equilibrium | KTU Name |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| A | 1538 | 0.0 | Melting point of pure Fe | Melting point |
| C | 1147 | 4.30 | Liquid ⇌ Austenite (γ) + Cementite (Fe₃C) | **Eutectic** |
| E | 1147 | 2.14 | Max C solubility in γ-Fe | Solubility limit |
| S | 727 | 0.76 | Austenite (γ) ⇌ Ferrite (α) + Cementite (Fe₃C) | **Eutectoid** |
| P | 727 | 0.025 | Max C solubility in α-Fe | Solubility limit |
| K | 727 | 6.67 | Cementite composition | Fe₃C stoichiometry |

### Key Reactions

1. **Eutectic Reaction (at 1147 °C, 4.30% C):**
   $$\text{Liquid (4.30\% C)} \;\longrightarrow\; \gamma\text{-Austenite (2.14\% C)} \;+\; \text{Fe}_3\text{C (6.67\% C)}$$
   The resulting two-phase microstructure is called **Ledeburite**.

2. **Eutectoid Reaction (at 727 °C, 0.76% C):**
   $$\gamma\text{-Austenite (0.76\% C)} \;\longrightarrow\; \alpha\text{-Ferrite (0.025\% C)} \;+\; \text{Fe}_3\text{C (6.67\% C)}$$
   The resulting two-phase microstructure is called **Pearlite** (lamellar α + Fe₃C).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown — The Five Cast Iron Types

### 2.2.1 White Cast Iron

- **Carbon morphology:** Entirely present as **iron carbide (Fe₃C) = cementite**.
- **Microstructure:** White, crystalline, faceted fracture surface (origin of "white" name — because it appears silvery-white on fracture).
- **Composition:** Low Si (≤ 1%), high Mn (promotes carbide formation), 2.5–3.5% C.
- **Cooling rate:** Produced by **rapid cooling** (chill casting).
- **Hardness:** **400 – 600 BHN** (extremely hard).
- **Ductility:** Practically **zero** (cannot be machined with ordinary tools).
- **Applications:** Liners for cement mills, ball-mill grinding media, crusher jaws, brake shoes, slurry pumps — wherever **abrasion resistance** is critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it called "white"?** During rapid solidification, **all carbon is retained as Fe₃C**. The freshly fractured surface reflects light in many directions from the carbide crystals, giving a **silvery-white** appearance.

### 2.2.2 Grey Cast Iron

- **Carbon morphology:** Present as **interconnected graphite flakes** dispersed in a **ferritic / pearlitic / ferrito-pearlitic matrix**.
- **Microstructure:** Grey, granular fracture surface (graphite flakes absorb light → grey appearance).
- **Composition:** High Si (1–3%), 2.5–4.0% C.
- **Cooling rate:** Slow cooling (sand casting).
- **Hardness:** **120 – 250 BHN**.
- **Ductility:** Very low (graphite flakes act as **internal stress concentrators / notches**).
- **Damping capacity:** **Excellent** — graphite flakes absorb vibrations. Best among all cast irons.
- **Compressive strength:** **3 – 5 times** its tensile strength.
- **Applications:** Engine cylinder blocks, machine tool beds, pipes, manhole covers, gear boxes, pump housings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Exam Point:** Graphite flakes act as **internal notches**, so grey cast iron has **tensile strength of only 150 – 400 MPa**, but **compressive strength of 600 – 1000 MPa**. The flakes also make it **self-lubricating** and **vibration-damping**.

### 2.2.3 Malleable Cast Iron

- **Carbon morphology:** **Temper carbon** — irregular, rosette-shaped, or spheroidal clusters of graphite (NOT flakes).
- **Microstructure:** "Popcorn-like" or "cotton-bale" ferrite/graphite clusters.
- **Composition:** Low Si (0.5–1.4%), 2.0–2.6% C (similar to white cast iron initially).
- **Production route:** **Two-step heat treatment of white cast iron:**
  1. Heat to **≈ 900 – 970 °C** and hold for a long time (1–4 days) → cementite decomposes to **austenite + temper carbon**.
  2. Slow cool through **727 °C eutectoid** → produces either **ferritic malleable** (slow) or **pearlitic malleable** (faster) matrix.
- **Hardness:** **130 – 350 BHN**.
- **Ductility:** **5 – 18% elongation** — the MOST ductile of all cast irons (after nodular).
- **Applications:** Automotive parts (connecting rods, brake pedals, lever arms), pipe fittings, agricultural implements.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Malleable Cast Iron:** "**M**alleable = **M**ust be **M**ade from **W**hite" — the starting material is white cast iron, then heat-treated to get the rosette graphite.

### 2.2.4 Nodular (Ductile / Spheroidal Graphite) Cast Iron

- **Carbon morphology:** **Spheroidal (nodular) graphite** particles — short, rounded, isolated spheres.
- **Microstructure:** Spherical graphite in ferrite/pearlite matrix.
- **Composition:** Similar to grey cast iron, but with **Mg (0.02–0.05%) or Ce added** as a nodulizer.
- **Production:** Molten iron is treated with **magnesium (Mg)** or **cerium (Ce)** just before pouring — this **de-sulphurizes** the melt and changes the **interfacial energy** so that graphite grows as **spheres** instead of flakes.
- **Hardness:** **150 – 300 BHN**.
- **Ductility:** **2 – 25% elongation** — comparable to steel.
- **Tensile strength:** **350 – 700 MPa** (much higher than grey CI).
- **Impact strength:** Comparable to mild steel.
- **Applications:** Crankshafts, camshafts, gears, pistons, heavy-duty valves, rolls for rolling mills.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Spheroidal Graphite Advantage:** Because the graphite is in **isolated spherical form** (not connected flakes), it does NOT act as a stress concentrator. This is why nodular cast iron has **strength AND ductility** — a unique combination unmatched by other cast irons.

### 2.2.5 Alloy Cast Irons

Cast irons to which **alloying elements** (Ni, Cr, Mo, Cu, Si > 3%) are deliberately added to enhance specific properties.

| Alloy Cast Iron | Key Addition | Property Enhanced | Application |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Ni-Hard** | 4–5% Ni + 1.5% Cr | Wear resistance (martensitic matrix) | Crusher rolls, ball mills |
| **High-Chromium Iron** | 15–35% Cr | Corrosion + abrasion resistance | Slurry pumps, mining |
| **High-Silicon Iron** | 14–18% Si | Acid corrosion resistance (Duriron) | Chemical plant, acid pipes |
| **Ni-Resist** | 12–36% Ni + Cu | Heat & corrosion resistance | Exhaust manifolds, pump impellers |
| **Meehanite** | Ca–Si treated | Controlled structure | Machine tool beds |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cast Iron Quantitative Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lever Rule (for phase fraction calculation):**
> For a binary phase diagram, the fraction of any phase at a given temperature is the ratio of the opposite segment of the tie-line to the total length.

$$f_{\alpha} = \frac{C_{\beta} - C_0}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}}$$

$$f_{\beta} = \frac{C_0 - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}}$$

where:
- $C_0$ = overall alloy composition
- $C_{\alpha}$ = composition of phase α
- $C_{\beta}$ = composition of phase β
- $f_{\alpha} + f_{\beta} = 1$ (always)

### Microstructure Fraction Calculations for Cast Irons

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **% Pearlite in matrix** | $f_{\text{pearlite}} = \dfrac{0.76 - C_{\text{matrix}}}{0.76 - 0.025}$ | $C_{\text{matrix}}$ = %C in ferrite matrix |
| **% Ferrite in pearlite** | $f_{\alpha_{\text{(in pearlite)}}} = \dfrac{6.67 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.025} = 0.889$ | **Constant = 88.9%** |
| **% Cementite in pearlite** | $f_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}_{\text{(in pearlite)}}} = \dfrac{0.76 - 0.025}{6.67 - 0.025} = 0.111$ | **Constant = 11.1%** |
| **% Combined Carbon in pearlite** | $\%C_{\text{combined}} = 0.111 \times 6.67 = 0.74\%$ | Practical value ≈ 0.77% |
| **% Graphite Carbon** | $\%C_{\text{graphite}} = \%C_{\text{total}} - \%C_{\text{combined}}$ | Only in grey/nodular CI |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** When asked for "**% pearlite**", use the **lever rule across the eutectoid line (727 °C isotherm)** at compositions 0.025% (ferrite) and 0.76% (austenite/eutectoid). When asked for "**% cementite in pearlite**", the answer is **always 11.1%** — a constant.

---

|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Automotive** | Engine block, brake drum | Grey CI | Damping + machinability |
| **Automotive** | Crankshaft, steering knuckle | Nodular CI | Strength + ductility |
| **Mining** | Crusher jaw, grinding balls | White CI | Abrasion resistance |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Eutectoid Pearlite Composition (88.9% Ferrite + 11.1% Cementite)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Material Science papers. Let us derive the phase fractions in pearlite at 727 °C exactly.

### Given Information
- Eutectoid temperature: **T = 727 °C**
- Eutectoid composition: **C₀ = 0.76% C**
- Ferrite composition at 727 °C: **Cα = 0.025% C**
- Cementite composition: **CFe₃C = 6.67% C**
- Pearlite = α-Ferrite + Fe₃C (alternating lamellae)

### Step 1: Identify the tie-line at T = 727 °C

The tie-line at the eutectoid isotherm extends horizontally from the **ferrite solubility limit (P-point: 0.025% C)** to the **cementite composition (K-point: 6.67% C)**.

### Step 2: Apply the Lever Rule to calculate % Ferrite in Pearlite

$$W_{\alpha} = \frac{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_0}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_{\alpha}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$W_{\alpha} = \frac{6.67 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.025}$$

**Step 2.1: Calculate the numerator**

$$6.67 - 0.76 = 5.91$$

**Step 2.2: Calculate the denominator**

$$6.67 - 0.025 = 6.645$$

**Step 2.3: Divide to get the fraction**

$$W_{\alpha} = \frac{5.91}{6.645} = 0.8893 \approx 0.889$$

Converting to percentage:

$$W_{\alpha} = 88.9\%$$

### Step 3: Apply the Lever Rule to calculate % Cementite in Pearlite

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = \frac{C_0 - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_{\alpha}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = \frac{0.76 - 0.025}{6.67 - 0.025}$$

**Step 3.1: Calculate the numerator**

$$0.76 - 0.025 = 0.735$$

**Step 3.2: Calculate the denominator**

$$6.67 - 0.025 = 6.645$$

**Step 3.3: Divide to get the fraction**

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = \frac{0.735}{6.645} = 0.1106 \approx 0.111$$

Converting to percentage:

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 11.1\%$$

### Step 4: Verification of Mass Balance

$$W_{\alpha} + W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 88.9\% + 11.1\% = 100.0\% \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 5: Final Concluding Statement

> At the eutectoid temperature of 727 °C, **pearlite always consists of 88.9% ferrite and 11.1% cementite**, regardless of the overall carbon content of the alloy. This is a **constant** for plain-carbon iron alloys at equilibrium.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Solubility of Carbon in Ferrite at 727 °C (Confirmation)

This is a conceptual derivation verifying the solubility limit on the Fe–Fe₃C diagram.

### Given Information
- At 727 °C, the solubility of C in α-Fe reaches its maximum because the **α → γ transformation** occurs at this point.
- The solubility curve drops sharply as temperature decreases.

### Step 1: Set up the condition
At the eutectoid temperature, the ferrite (α) phase is in equilibrium with both austenite (γ) and cementite (Fe₃C).

### Step 2: Read from the standard phase diagram
The intersection of the **α + γ / α phase boundary** with the **727 °C isotherm** gives:

$$C_{\alpha(\text{max at 727°C})} = 0.025\% \text{ C}$$

### Step 3: At room temperature (25 °C / 298 K), solubility drops to

$$C_{\alpha(25°C)} \approx 0.008\% \text{ C}$$

### Step 4: Conclusion
The carbon solubility in α-Fe is **very low** — only **0.025% at 727 °C** and **0.008% at room temperature**. This is why most carbon in iron alloys ends up as **cementite (Fe₃C)** or **graphite**, NOT dissolved in ferrite.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pro-Eutectoid Phase Calculation in Hypoeutectoid Cast Iron

A standard KTU problem. Consider a cast iron with **3.0% C** cooled slowly from the liquid state. Calculate the **% pro-eutectoid cementite** and **% ledeburite (eutectic mixture)**.

### Given Information
- Overall composition: **C₀ = 3.0% C**
- This lies **between 2.14% (E-point) and 4.30% (C-point)** → **hypoeutectic** cast iron.
- Eutectic temperature: 1147 °C

### Step 1: Identify the phases just above the eutectic temperature (1147 °C)
- **Liquid** (composition = 4.30% C) and
- **Pro-eutectic austenite (γ)** (composition = 2.14% C)

### Step 2: Apply the Lever Rule at 1147 °C to find % Liquid and % Pro-eutectic γ

$$W_{\gamma(\text{pro-eutectic})} = \frac{C_{\text{Liquid}} - C_0}{C_{\text{Liquid}} - C_{\gamma}}$$

Substituting:

$$W_{\gamma(\text{pro-eutectic})} = \frac{4.30 - 3.0}{4.30 - 2.14} = \frac{1.30}{2.16}$$

$$W_{\gamma(\text{pro-eutectic})} = 0.6019 = 60.19\%$$

$$W_{\text{Liquid}} = 1 - 0.6019 = 0.3981 = 39.81\%$$

### Step 3: At the eutectic temperature, all liquid (39.81%) transforms to ledeburite.

So the final microstructure of this cast iron contains:
- **60.19% transformed austenite** (which will further transform on cooling through 727 °C), and
- **39.81% ledeburite** (which is the eutectic mixture of austenite + cementite).

### Step 4: At 727 °C, the austenite (0.76% C) decomposes to pearlite.

> **Note:** This is a typical KTU 14-mark derivation. Students must show **all four steps** with proper tie-line identification.

---

| **Colour of fracture** | White | Grey | Silvery grey | Grey | Grey/white |

| **Wear resistance** | **Excellent** | Good | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** (Cr) |
| **Castability** | Poor | **Excellent** | Good | Good | Good |
| **Cost** | Low | **Lowest** | Moderate | High | High |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Pattern Question

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern:** "A cast iron contains 3.2% C and 1.8% Si. Calculate (a) % combined carbon, (b) % free graphite, and (c) % pearlite in the matrix." [Modeled on KTU 2018 / 2020 / 2023 ESE patterns]

### Step 1: % Combined Carbon
Assume **all combined carbon is tied up in pearlite as Fe₃C**. The fraction of Fe₃C in pearlite is 11.1% and the carbon content of Fe₃C is 6.67%.

In **1 g of pearlite**, the combined carbon is:
$$0.111 \times 6.67\% = 0.7405\% \text{ of pearlite mass}$$

For a cast iron with **3.2% total C**, the pearlite content in the matrix is calculated from:
$$\% \text{ Pearlite} = \frac{\%C_{\text{total}} - 0.025}{0.76 - 0.025} \times 100 \quad \text{(approx.)}$$

$$\% \text{ Pearlite} = \frac{3.2 - 0.025}{0.735} \times 100 = 4.32 \times 100 / 0.735 \approx 432\%$$

This value exceeds 100%, which is **physically impossible**. This indicates that the **excess carbon (beyond 0.76%) is present as graphite**, and the matrix is fully pearlitic.

### Step 2: % Free Graphite

The **graphite carbon** is the carbon present as graphite (not as Fe₃C):
$$\% C_{\text{graphite}} = \%C_{\text{total}} - \%C_{\text{combined}}$$

The combined carbon is the carbon in the **pearlitic cementite**. For a fully pearlitic matrix:
$$\%C_{\text{combined}} = 0.77\% \text{ (constant for fully pearlitic iron)}$$

Therefore:
$$\% C_{\text{graphite}} = 3.2 - 0.77 = 2.43\%$$

### Step 3: % Pearlite
Since the matrix is **fully pearlitic** (because the bulk composition is far above 0.76%), the % pearlite is **100%** of the matrix (excluding graphite).

### Final Answer Summary
- (a) **% Combined carbon ≈ 0.77%** (of total mass)
- (b) **% Free graphite ≈ 2.43%**
- (c) **% Pearlite in matrix = 100%**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**"Differentiate between grey cast iron and white cast iron in terms of carbon morphology, fracture appearance, and one key application."** [3 Marks]

#### Model Answer:
| Aspect | Grey Cast Iron | White Cast Iron |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Carbon morphology** | Carbon present as **graphite flakes** dispersed in matrix | Carbon present as **iron carbide (Fe₃C / cementite)** |
| **Fracture appearance** | **Grey, dull** — graphite flakes absorb light | **White, crystalline** — carbide crystals reflect light |
| **Key application** | Engine cylinder blocks, machine tool beds (where **damping** is needed) | Crusher jaws, ball-mill liners (where **abrasion resistance** is needed) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "grey iron is **stronger** than white iron" — this is **wrong**. Grey iron is weaker in tension but has good compressive strength and damping. White iron is harder and more abrasion-resistant but **brittle**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**"Why is magnesium added to molten iron during the production of nodular cast iron? What is the role of silicon in grey cast iron?"** [3 Marks]

#### Model Answer:
- **Role of Magnesium (0.02 – 0.05%):** Magnesium acts as a **nodulizer / spheroidizer**. It **de-sulphurizes** the melt (forming MgS) and changes the **interfacial energy** between graphite and the liquid iron, causing carbon to precipitate as **spheroidal nodules** rather than flakes. The presence of trace elements like Mg, Ce, or Y changes the graphite growth habit from **flake → sphere**.

- **Role of Silicon (1 – 3% in grey CI):** Silicon is a strong **graphitizer** — it destabilizes cementite (Fe₃C) and promotes the decomposition of carbides into **free graphite**. Higher Si → more graphite formation, which is why grey CI has high Si content. In white CI, Si is kept low (≤ 1%) to **prevent** graphite formation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**"With the help of a neat sketch of the Fe–Fe₃C equilibrium phase diagram, explain the classification of cast iron. Describe the microstructure, properties, and applications of (a) Grey Cast Iron and (b) Nodular (Ductile) Cast Iron."** [14 Marks]

#### (a) Fe–Fe₃C Phase Diagram and Classification — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Sketch the diagram** (refer to the KTU textbook figure; show the key points: A at 1538 °C, C at 1147 °C / 4.30% C, E at 1147 °C / 2.14% C, S at 727 °C / 0.76% C, P at 727 °C / 0.025% C, K at 727 °C / 6.67% C).

**Step 2: Classification of Cast Irons (based on metallurgical form of carbon):**
- **White Cast Iron** — carbon as Fe₃C, rapid cooling
- **Grey Cast Iron** — carbon as graphite flakes, slow cooling
- **Malleable Cast Iron** — temper carbon, heat treated
- **Nodular Cast Iron** — spheroidal graphite, Mg-treated
- **Alloy Cast Iron** — alloyed with Ni, Cr, Si etc.

#### (b) Grey Cast Iron — 3.5 Marks

**Microstructure:** Graphite flakes in a matrix of ferrite, pearlite, or ferrite-pearlite.

**Properties:**
- Tensile strength: 150–400 MPa
- Compressive strength: 600–1000 MPa (3–5× tensile)
- Damping capacity: **excellent** (best among all cast irons)
- Machinability: **excellent** (graphite acts as chip-breaker)
- Brittle — virtually no ductility

**Applications:** Engine cylinder blocks, machine tool beds, pipes, manhole covers, gear boxes.

#### (c) Nodular Cast Iron — 3.5 Marks

**Microstructure:** Spherical graphite nodules in a matrix of ferrite, pearlite, or ferrite-pearlite.

**Properties:**
- Tensile strength: 350–700 MPa (much higher than grey CI)
- Elongation: 2–25% (comparable to steel)
- Impact strength: comparable to mild steel
- Wear resistance: good
- Machinability: good

**Applications:** Crankshafts, camshafts, gears, pistons, heavy-duty valves.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2022, Modified]

**"(a) Explain the production of malleable cast iron from white cast iron with the help of a heat-treatment cycle. (b) Compare the properties of grey, malleable, and nodular cast iron in a tabular form. Justify why nodular cast iron is preferred for crankshafts in automotive engines."** [14 Marks]

#### (a) Production of Malleable Cast Iron — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Start with white cast iron casting.**
White cast iron is produced by **chill casting** (rapid cooling in metal mould) to retain all carbon as Fe₃C. It is hard and brittle — not directly usable.

**Step 2: First-stage heat treatment (Graphitization / Malleabilization).**
The white cast iron is heated slowly to **900 – 970 °C** and held (soaked) for **40 – 70 hours** (typically 2–4 days). During this stage:
$$\text{Fe}_3\text{C (in ledeburite and pearlite)} \;\longrightarrow\; \gamma\text{-Austenite} + \text{Temper Carbon (graphite rosettes)}$$

The Fe₃C decomposes because the long holding time allows carbon atoms to **diffuse and precipitate as irregular graphite clusters** (temper carbon) within the austenite matrix.

**Step 3: Second-stage cooling — control of matrix type.**
The casting is then cooled slowly through the **eutectoid temperature (727 °C)**. The cooling rate controls the final matrix:
- **Very slow cooling** → **Ferritic malleable iron** (carbon rejects to existing graphite rosettes, leaving pure ferrite).
- **Faster cooling (air cool)** → **Pearlitic malleable iron** (carbon retained as pearlite).
- **Interrupted cooling** → **Mixed ferrite + pearlite matrix**.

#### (b) Comparison Table and Justification — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Grey CI | Malleable CI | Nodular CI |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Carbon form | Flakes | Rosette | Spheres |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 150–400 | 300–550 | 350–700 |
| Elongation (%) | ≈ 0.5 | 5–18 | 2–25 |
| Impact strength | Low | Moderate | High |
| Machinability | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Damping | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate |
| Production route | Sand cast | Heat treated | Mg-treated |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | High |

**Justification for Crankshaft Application:**
Nodular cast iron is preferred for crankshafts because:
1. **High tensile strength (350–700 MPa)** — can withstand cyclic bending and torsional loads during engine operation.
2. **Good ductility (2–25%)** — provides **toughness** to absorb shock loads from combustion.
3. **High fatigue resistance** — the spherical graphite does not act as a stress raiser, unlike flakes.
4. **Good wear resistance** — pearlite/ferrite-pearlite matrix resists wear at bearing surfaces.
5. **Castability** — complex crankshaft geometry can be cast in one piece (cheaper than forging steel).
6. **Damping** — though less than grey CI, still better than steel — reduces NVH (noise, vibration, harshness).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (14-Mark Questions):**
> - Do NOT skip the **heat treatment temperature AND time** in malleable CI questions (must mention 900–970 °C, 40–70 hours).
> - Do NOT confuse **ferritic malleable** with **ferritic nodular** — they are different microstructures.
> - In comparison tables, students often forget to mention the **production route** — examiners specifically look for this.
> - Always end the answer with a **one-line concluding statement** linking the property to the application.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 2 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Cast Iron Definition:** Fe-based alloy with **> 2% C** (typically 2–4%); cast into shape, not wrought.
- **Five Types:** White, Grey, Malleable, Nodular (Ductile), Alloy — distinguished by **carbon morphology**.
- **Eutectic point:** **1147 °C, 4.30% C** → ledeburite forms.
- **Eutectoid point:** **727 °C, 0.76% C** → pearlite forms.
- **Pearlite composition (constant):** **88.9% ferrite + 11.1% cementite** — memorize.
- **White CI:** Fe₃C + pearlite; hard (400–600 BHN); chill cast; crusher jaws.
- **Grey CI:** Graphite flakes; damping excellent; tensile 150–400 MPa; engine blocks.
- **Malleable CI:** Temper carbon rosettes; heat-treat white CI at 900–970 °C for 40–70 h; ductile.
- **Nodular CI:** Mg-treated; spheroidal graphite; tensile 350–700 MPa; crankshafts.
- **Alloy CI:** Ni-hard (wear), high-Cr (corrosion), high-Si (acid), Ni-resist (heat).
- **Si role:** Strong **graphitizer** — destabilizes Fe₃C, promotes graphite.
- **Mn role:** **Carbide stabilizer** — counteracts Si, promotes Fe₃C formation.
- **Lever rule equation:** $f_{\alpha} = (C_{\beta} - C_0) / (C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha})$.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** 1 Part-A (3M) + 1 Part-B (14M) per module typical; cast iron is a **guaranteed question** in ESE.
- **Common mistake:** Calling grey CI "stronger" than white CI — grey is **weaker in tension** but stronger in compression and damping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon Steel
topics.content:
