> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Carbon Steel # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Carbon Steel** is defined as a binary iron-carbon alloy (Fe–Fe₃C system) in which carbon is the principal alloying element, and no other alloying element is intentionally added in amounts exceeding the specified limits for manganese (typically **≤ 1.65 % Mn**), silicon (≤ 0.60 % Si), and copper (≤ 0.60 % Cu). Residual elements such as sulfur and phosphorus are kept strictly below **0.05 %** in standard grades. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT412 – Material Science, Module 2)**, carbon steels occupy the compositional window from **0.008 % C to 2.14 % C** on the iron-rich side of the Fe–Fe₃C (iron–cementite) metastable equilibrium phase diagram, encompassing all commercial plain-carbon engineering grades. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The "carbon" in carbon steel does **NOT** exist as free elemental carbon. It is thermodynamically locked inside the intermetallic compound **cementite (Fe₃C)**, which contains **6.67 % C by weight** (fixed stoichiometry). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plain Roti vs. The Stuffed Paratha" Imagine you are baking: - **Pure iron (Fe)** is like a soft, plain **roti** — flexible, weak, and easy to tear. - **Carbon steel** is like a **stuffed paratha** — the dough (iron matrix, called *ferrite*) is reinforced with tiny, hard, brittle filling layers (cementite). The more filling you stuff in, the harder and stronger the paratha becomes, BUT it also gets more brittle and harder to bend. This is precisely why carbon controls almost every mechanical property of steel: | More Carbon (C ↑) | Less Carbon (C ↓) | |---|---| | Harder & stronger | Softer & more ductile | | More brittle | More tough / formable | | Less weldable | Easily weldable | | Difficult to machine | Excellent machinability | --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Constants You Must Memorize for KTU Board Exams:** > - **Eutectoid composition:** **0.76 % C** (also called the "Pearlite point" or *P* point) > - **Eutectoid temperature:** **727 °C** (designated **A₁** or lower critical temperature) > - **Maximum solubility of C in α-Ferrite:** **0.022 % C** at 727 °C (room temp ≈ 0.008 %) > - **Maximum solubility of C in γ-Austenite:** **2.14 % C** at 1147 °C > - **Cementite (Fe₃C) carbon content:** **6.67 % C** (fixed by stoichiometry) > - **Upper critical temperature line A₃ / A_cm:** depends on carbon content > - **Eutectic composition:** **4.30 % C** at **1147 °C** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Carbon Steels (By Carbon Content) The KTU 2024 syllabus partitions carbon steels into **four** sub-categories based on the weight percentage of dissolved carbon: | Class | Carbon Range (wt%) | Common Names | Typical Applications | |---|---|---|---| | **Low Carbon Steel** | **0.008 – 0.25 %** | Mild steel | Structural beams, car bodies, wire, nails, sheets | | **Medium Carbon Steel** | **0.25 – 0.60 %** | Machinery steel | Rails, gears, crankshafts, couplings, axles | | **High Carbon Steel** | **0.60 – 1.0 %** | Tool steel (mild) | Springs, chisels, cutting tools, blades | | **Ultra-High Carbon Steel** | **1.0 – 2.14 %** | High-grade tool steel | Knives, punches, shear blades, cutlery | --- ## 2.2 The Five Micro-Constituents (Phases) of Carbon Steel at Room Temperature A "phase" is a physically and chemically homogeneous region. Carbon steel at room temperature is a **multi-phase** material: 1. **α-Ferrite (α-Fe)** — Body-Centered Cubic (BCC). Soft, ductile, magnetic. Solubility for C is very low (**0.022 % max**). 2. **γ-Austenite (γ-Fe)** — Face-Centered Cubic (FCC). Stable **only above 727 °C** in plain carbon steels; non-magnetic. High C solubility (**2.14 %**). 3. **δ-Ferrite (δ-Fe)** — BCC. Stable only between **1394 °C and 1539 °C**. 4. **Cementite (Fe₃C)** — Orthorhombic intermetallic. Very hard (≈ **HV 1000**), extremely brittle. Contains **6.67 % C** (fixed). 5. **Pearlite (α + Fe₃C)** — A two-phase **micro-constituent** (not a true phase) consisting of alternating lamellae of ferrite and cementite. Forms by eutectoid decomposition at **727 °C**. --- ## 2.3 The Three Critical Reactions in the Fe–Fe₃C System > [!IMPORTANT] > **The eutectoid reaction is the single most-tested concept in KTU Module 2 — memorize it cold.** ### (a) Peritectic Reaction (at 1493 °C) $$\delta\text{-Ferrite (0.53 \% C)} + \text{Liquid (0.16 \% C)} \xrightarrow{1493\,°C} \gamma\text{-Austenite (0.16 \% C)}$$ ### (b) Eutectic Reaction (at 1147 °C) $$\text{Liquid (4.30 \% C)} \xrightarrow{1147\,°C} \gamma\text{-Austenite (2.14 \% C)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C (6.67 \% C)}$$ ### (c) **Eutectoid Reaction (at 727 °C)** — ★ THE STAR REACTION ★ $$\gamma\text{-Austenite (0.76 \% C)} \xrightarrow{727\,°C} \alpha\text{-Ferrite (0.022 \% C)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C (6.67 \% C)}$$ This single reaction governs the microstructure of **every commercial carbon steel** (since they all contain < 2.14 % C). --- ## 2.4 Classification by Eutectoid Position | Type | Carbon Range | Microstructure at Room Temp | |---|---|---| | **Hypoeutectoid Steel** | 0.022 % < C < 0.76 % | **Proeutectoid Ferrite + Pearlite** | | **Eutectoid Steel** | **C = 0.76 %** | **100 % Pearlite** | | **Hypereutectoid Steel** | 0.76 % < C < 2.14 % | **Proeutectoid Cementite + Pearlite** | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The **Lever Rule** is the master equation. Every KTU numerical question in Module 2 reduces to a lever-rule application. **Lever Rule for Phase Fraction** (applied on a horizontal tie-line at any temperature T): $$W_\alpha = \frac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha} \quad ; \quad W_\gamma = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$$ where: - $C_0$ = overall alloy composition (wt % C) - $C_\alpha$ = carbon content in ferrite phase at that temperature - $C_\gamma$ = carbon content in austenite phase at that temperature - $W_\alpha + W_\gamma = 1$ **Pearlite Composition (always):** $$W_\alpha^{\text{in pearlite}} = \frac{6.67 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.022} = 0.889 \quad ; \quad W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}}^{\text{in pearlite}} = 0.111$$ **Density & Crystal Structure:** | Phase | Crystal Structure | Hardness | |---|---|---| | α-Ferrite | BCC | ≈ 80 HV | | Austenite | FCC | ≈ 200 HV | | Cementite (Fe₃C) | Orthorhombic | ≈ 1000 HV | | Pearlite | Lamellar (α + Fe₃C) | ≈ 250 HV | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Carbon steels form the **backbone of the global steel industry**, accounting for nearly **85 % of all steel produced worldwide**. Their dominance arises from: - **Low cost** (no expensive alloying elements like Ni, Cr, Mo) - **Tunable properties** via heat treatment (annealing, normalizing, quenching, tempering) - **Recyclability** (carbon steel scrap can be re-melted indefinitely without losing base properties) - **Predictable Fe–Fe₃C phase behavior** that allows precise microstructural engineering Applications range from **construction rebar (low C)**, through **automotive crankshafts (medium C)**, to **high-speed cutting tools (high C)**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Phase Fractions in a Hypoeutectoid Steel (0.40 % C) at Room Temperature We derive the relative amounts of **proeutectoid ferrite** and **pearlite** when a **0.40 % C** steel is slowly cooled to 25 °C. **Step 1 — Identify the micro-constituents present at 25 °C.** Because $0.40\,\% < 0.76\,\%$, this is a **hypoeutectoid steel**. At 25 °C it contains: - Proeutectoid α-ferrite (formed between A₃ and A₁) - Pearlite (formed at the eutectoid temperature 727 °C) **Step 2 — Apply the overall mass balance.** The total mass is the sum of proeutectoid ferrite + pearlite: $$W_{\text{total}} = W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}} + W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1$$ **Step 3 — Apply the carbon mass balance using the lever rule between α (0.022 % C) and Fe₃C (6.67 % C).** The carbon content of pearlite is exactly the eutectoid composition, **0.76 % C**. $$C_0 = W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}} \cdot C_\alpha + W_{\text{pearlite}} \cdot C_{\text{pearlite}}$$ $$0.40 = W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}} \cdot (0.022) + W_{\text{pearlite}} \cdot (0.76)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the constraint $W_{\alpha} + W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1$.** Let $W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1 - W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}}$: $$0.40 = 0.022 \cdot W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}} + 0.76 \cdot \left(1 - W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Expand the right-hand side.** $$0.40 = 0.022 \, W_\alpha + 0.76 - 0.76 \, W_\alpha$$ $$0.40 - 0.76 = (0.022 - 0.76) \, W_\alpha$$ $$-0.36 = -0.738 \, W_\alpha$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}}$.** $$W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}} = \frac{0.36}{0.738} = 0.4878 \;\; (\approx 48.8\,\%)$$ **Step 7 — Compute $W_{\text{pearlite}}$.** $$W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1 - 0.4878 = 0.5122 \;\; (\approx 51.2\,\%)$$ **Step 8 — Final Result.** $$\boxed{W_{\alpha\,\text{(proeutectoid)}} \approx 48.8\,\% \quad\text{and}\quad W_{\text{pearlite}} \approx 51.2\,\%}$$ --- ## 3.2 Verification of the Master Lever-Rule Formula (General Derivation) For any two-phase region (α + γ) at temperature *T*, with overall alloy composition $C_0$: Let $W_\alpha$ and $W_\gamma$ be the phase fractions. Conservation of mass gives: $$W_\alpha + W_\gamma = 1$$ Conservation of solute (carbon) gives: $$C_0 = W_\alpha \, C_\alpha + W_\gamma \, C_\gamma$$ Substitute $W_\gamma = 1 - W_\alpha$: $$C_0 = W_\alpha \, C_\alpha + (1 - W_\alpha) \, C_\gamma$$ $$C_0 - C_\gamma = W_\alpha (C_\alpha - C_\gamma)$$ Solving for $W_\alpha$: $$W_\alpha = \frac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$$ By symmetry, $W_\gamma$ is obtained by swapping α ↔ γ in the numerator: $$W_\gamma = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$$ These are the **canonical Lever-Rule expressions** used throughout Module 2. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Full Marks Practice) > **Question:** A plain-carbon steel contains **1.20 % C**. Compute the weight fractions of (a) proeutectoid cementite and (b) pearlite at room temperature. Also compute the weight fraction of cementite **inside the pearlite**. **Solution.** Since $1.20\,\% > 0.76\,\%$, this is a **hypereutectoid steel**. Micro-constituents: proeutectoid Fe₃C + pearlite. **Part (a) — Proeutectoid Cementite Fraction:** Lever between $\alpha$ (0.022 % C) and pearlite (0.76 % C): $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C,proeutectoid}} = \frac{1.20 - 0.76}{6.67 - 0.76} = \frac{0.44}{5.91} = 0.0744 \;\; (\approx 7.44\,\%)$$ $$W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1 - 0.0744 = 0.9256 \;\; (\approx 92.56\,\%)$$ **Part (b) — Cementite Inside Pearlite:** Apply lever rule within pearlite between α (0.022 %) and Fe₃C (6.67 %): $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C,inside pearlite}} = \frac{0.76 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \frac{0.738}{6.648} = 0.1110 \;\; (\approx 11.1\,\%)$$ **Total Cementite at Room Temperature:** $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C,total}} = 0.0744 + (0.9256 \times 0.1110) = 0.0744 + 0.1027 = 0.1771 \;\; (\approx 17.7\,\%)$$ $$\boxed{W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C,proeutectoid}} = 7.44\,\% \quad ; \quad W_{\text{pearlite}} = 92.56\,\% \quad ; \quad W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C,total}} = 17.7\,\%}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Iron-Carbon Phase Diagram — Functional Block Architecture > The Fe–Fe₃C phase diagram is one of the most important diagrams in all of materials engineering. Since Mermaid cannot reproduce thermodynamic phase plots with curved solvus lines, the block diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of the diagram — i.e., the interactions among the critical points, lines, and reactions. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Pure Iron Fe
Melting Point 1539 C] --> A2[Delta Ferrite Region
1394 C to 1539 C
BCC] A2 --> A3[Peritectic Reaction
at 1493 C
delta + Liquid yields gamma] A3 --> A4[Gamma Austenite Region
FCC High C Solubility 2.14 percent] A4 --> A5[Eutectoid Reaction
at 727 C
gamma 0.76 percent yields alpha + Fe3C] A5 --> A6[Alpha Ferrite Region
BCC Low C Solubility 0.022 percent] A4 --> A7[Eutectic Reaction
at 1147 C
Liquid 4.30 percent yields gamma + Fe3C] A7 --> A8[Fe3C Cementite
Fixed Composition 6.67 percent C] A6 --> A9[Pearlite Microstructure
Alternating Lamellae
alpha plus Fe3C] A8 --> A9 A5 --> A9 classDef region fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000; classDef reaction fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000; classDef phase fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000; classDef micro fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000; class A1,A2,A4,A6,A8 region; class A3,A5,A7 reaction; class A9 micro; ``` ### Reading the Diagram — Key Reference Lines | Line | Temperature | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **A₁** (= 727 °C) | Constant | Eutectoid isotherm — pearlite formation | | **A₃** | Variable (912 → 727 °C) | γ → α transformation start (hypoeutectoid) | | **A_cm** | Variable (727 → 1147 °C) | γ → Fe₃C transformation start (hypereutectoid) | --- ## 4.2 Microstructure Decision Tree (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[Cool carbon steel slowly to 25 C] --> B2{Carbon content?} B2 -->|C less than 0.022 percent| B3[Almost 100 percent Ferrite
Very soft and ductile] B2 -->|0.022 to 0.76 percent| B4[Hypoeutectoid Steel] B2 -->|Exactly 0.76 percent| B5[Eutectoid Steel] B2 -->|0.76 to 2.14 percent| B6[Hypereutectoid Steel] B2 -->|Greater than 2.14 percent| B7[Cast Iron Domain
Beyond Module 2 scope] B4 --> B8[Proeutectoid Ferrite plus Pearlite] B5 --> B9[100 percent Pearlite
Fully Lamellar] B6 --> B10[Proeutectoid Cementite plus Pearlite] B8 --> B11[Strength increases with C] B9 --> B11 B10 --> B11 classDef hypo fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8; classDef eut fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207; classDef hyper fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c; classDef other fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151; class B4,B8 hypo; class B5,B9 eut; class B6,B10 hyper; class B1,B2,B3,B7,B11 other; ``` --- ## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Pearlite Lamellae ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Austenite grain
gamma phase 0.76 percent C] --> P2[Eutectoid Temperature 727 C] P2 --> P3[Diffusion of Carbon atoms] P3 --> P4[Carbon-rich regions become Fe3C lamellae] P3 --> P5[Carbon-poor regions become alpha Ferrite lamellae] P4 --> P6[Pearlite colony
Alternating alpha and Fe3C] P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7[Hardness approx 250 HV
Lamellar spacing determines toughness] classDef input fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af; classDef proc fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207; classDef output fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857; class P1 input; class P2,P3,P4,P5 proc; class P6,P7 output; ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define carbon steel. How is it classified based on carbon content?** **Model Answer:** Carbon steel is an iron-carbon alloy (Fe–Fe₃C system) where carbon is the primary alloying element and no other alloying element is added in significant quantity. It is classified into four categories based on carbon content: 1. **Low carbon steel (0.008 – 0.25 % C):** Used for structural sections, wires, and automobile bodies. 2. **Medium carbon steel (0.25 – 0.60 % C):** Used for rails, gears, axles, and machinery parts. 3. **High carbon steel (0.60 – 1.0 % C):** Used for springs, cutting tools, and chisels. 4. **Ultra-high carbon steel (1.0 – 2.14 % C):** Used for knives, punches, and shear blades. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **What is the eutectoid reaction in the Fe–Fe₃C system? State the temperature and composition.** **Model Answer:** The eutectoid reaction is a solid-state transformation in which a single solid phase (γ-austenite) decomposes into two distinct solid phases (α-ferrite and cementite) on cooling. $$\gamma \text{(0.76 \% C)} \xrightarrow{727\,°C} \alpha \text{(0.022 \% C)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C (6.67 \% C)}$$ - **Eutectoid temperature:** 727 °C (designated A₁) - **Eutectoid composition:** 0.76 % C - **Resulting microstructure:** Pearlite (lamellar α + Fe₃C) --- ## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Note:** Module 2 carries internal choice. Both options below are equally weighted and appear randomly in question papers. --- ### ★ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **(a)** Draw the iron-iron carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) phase diagram and label all important phases, temperatures, and compositions. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (Diagram Description for Board Valuation):** A neat, labeled sketch should be drawn on the left page with the following key features: - **Y-axis:** Temperature (0 °C to 1600 °C) - **X-axis:** Weight % Carbon (0 % to 6.67 %) - **Three single-phase regions:** α (BCC ferrite), γ (FCC austenite), δ (BCC), Liquid (L), Fe₃C - **Peritectic point** at 1493 °C, 0.16 % C - **Eutectic point** at 1147 °C, 4.30 % C - **Eutectoid point** at 727 °C, 0.76 % C (label this prominently — examiners love this) - **A₁, A₃, A_cm lines** clearly marked - **Composition limits** 0.022 % and 2.14 % labeled on the solvus lines **Key textual labels required:** | Label | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | A₁ | 727 °C | Eutectoid temperature | | A₃ | 912 °C (pure Fe) | Curie / α-γ transition (pure Fe) | | A_cm | 727 °C (at 0.76 % C) | Hypereutectoid solvus start | | Eutectoid | 0.76 % C, 727 °C | Pearlite formation | **(b)** A plain carbon steel contains **0.45 % C**. Calculate (i) the weight fraction of proeutectoid ferrite, (ii) the weight fraction of pearlite, and (iii) the weight fraction of cementite at room temperature. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Classification:** Since $0.45\,\% < 0.76\,\%$, the steel is **hypoeutectoid** ⇒ proeutectoid α + pearlite at room temperature. **Step 2 — Apply carbon mass balance between α (0.022 %) and pearlite (0.76 %):** $$0.45 = W_\alpha \cdot (0.022) + W_{\text{pearlite}} \cdot (0.76)$$ Using $W_\alpha + W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1$: $$0.45 = 0.022 \, W_\alpha + 0.76 (1 - W_\alpha)$$ $$0.45 = 0.022 \, W_\alpha + 0.76 - 0.76 \, W_\alpha$$ $$0.45 - 0.76 = (0.022 - 0.76) \, W_\alpha$$ $$-0.31 = -0.738 \, W_\alpha$$ $$\boxed{W_\alpha \text{ (proeutectoid)} = 0.420 \;\; (42.0\,\%)}$$ **Step 3 — Weight fraction of pearlite:** $$\boxed{W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1 - 0.420 = 0.580 \;\; (58.0\,\%)}$$ **Step 4 — Cementite inside pearlite (lever rule α vs Fe₃C):** $$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C, in pearlite}} = \frac{0.76 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \frac{0.738}{6.648} = 0.111$$ **Step 5 — Total cementite (since hypoeutectoid, all Fe₃C is inside pearlite):** $$\boxed{W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C,total}} = 0.580 \times 0.111 = 0.0644 \;\; (6.44\,\%)}$$ **Final Answers:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Proeutectoid ferrite | **42.0 %** | | Pearlite | **58.0 %** | | Total cementite | **6.44 %** | --- ### ★ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **(a)** Explain the **eutectoid reaction** in the Fe–Fe₃C system. Discuss the resulting pearlite microstructure and its mechanical significance. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **eutectoid reaction** is a phase transformation in which one solid phase transforms simultaneously into two other solid phases on cooling. In the Fe–Fe₃C system: $$\gamma \text{-Austenite (0.76 \% C)} \xrightarrow{727\,°C} \alpha \text{-Ferrite (0.022 \% C)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C (6.67 \% C)}$$ **Step-by-step physical mechanism:** 1. **Cooling:** A 0.76 % C austenite is cooled slowly past 727 °C. 2. **Carbon supersaturation:** γ-FCC, which can dissolve up to 2.14 % C, can no longer retain carbon at lower temperatures. Carbon atoms diffuse out of the FCC lattice. 3. **Nucleation of ferrite:** Carbon-poor regions nucleate as α-BCC ferrite at prior γ grain boundaries (because BCC has very low carbon solubility). 4. **Carbon enrichment of remaining γ:** Carbon rejected by ferrite enriches adjacent γ regions. 5. **Cementite precipitation:** When local carbon concentration reaches 6.67 %, Fe₃C nucleates and grows as parallel plates. 6. **Cooperative growth:** Ferrite and cementite grow side-by-side as alternating lamellae — this is **pearlite**. **Mechanical significance:** - Pearlite hardness (≈ 250 HV) lies between ferrite (≈ 80 HV) and cementite (≈ 1000 HV). - **Finer lamellar spacing → harder and stronger pearlite** (Hall-Petch analog for pearlite colonies). - 100 % pearlitic eutectoid steel is used for **rails, high-strength wires, and prestressing tendons** because of its excellent combination of strength and wear resistance. **(b)** Compare **hypoeutectoid** and **hypereutectoid** steels in terms of microstructure and properties. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | **Hypoeutectoid Steel** (0.022 < C < 0.76 %) | **Hypereutectoid Steel** (0.76 < C < 2.14 %) | |---|---|---| | **Proeutectoid phase** | α-Ferrite (soft, ductile) | Fe₃C Cementite (hard, brittle) | | **Microstructure** | Proeutectoid α at grain boundaries + pearlite colonies inside | Proeutectoid Fe₃C network at grain boundaries + pearlite colonies | | **Phase formed first on cooling** | Ferrite forms between A₃ and A₁ | Cementite forms between A_cm and A₁ | | **Hardness** | Lower (softer due to soft ferrite) | Higher (Fe₃C network reinforces) | | **Ductility / Toughness** | Higher | Lower | | **Machinability** | Better | Poorer | | **Typical applications** | Structural beams, car bodies, pipelines | Cutting tools, springs, blades | | **Heat treatment response** | Excellent for case hardening (carburizing) | Excellent for through-hardening | > [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Students frequently **confuse proeutectoid phase directions** — for hypereutectoid, it is **CEMENTITE**, not ferrite. Many lose 2 marks here every year. > 2. The eutectoid temperature is **727 °C**, NOT 723 °C (some foreign textbooks use 723). Use **727 °C** for KTU. > 3. In lever-rule problems, always state which **two endpoints** you are using (e.g., "lever between α at 0.022 % and pearlite at 0.76 %"). Vague statements lose 1–2 marks. > 4. Cementite is **NOT** "carbon." Cementite is the **compound Fe₃C**. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy terminology. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Carbon steel = Fe + C only** (no other alloying elements beyond impurity limits). - ⭐ **4 classifications:** Low (≤0.25 %), Medium (0.25–0.60 %), High (0.60–1.0 %), Ultra-High (1.0–2.14 %). - ⭐ **5 phases:** α-ferrite, γ-austenite, δ-ferrite, cementite (Fe₃C), pearlite (α + Fe₃C). - ⭐ **3 critical reactions:** Peritectic (1493 °C), Eutectic (1147 °C, 4.30 % C), **Eutectoid (727 °C, 0.76 % C)**. - ⭐ **Pearlite** = lamellar (α + Fe₃C), always contains **88.9 % ferrite + 11.1 % cementite** by weight. - ⭐ **Lever rule formula:** $W_\alpha = (C_\gamma - C_0)/(C_\gamma - C_\alpha)$. - ⭐ **Hypoeutectoid ⇒ proeutectoid ferrite + pearlite.** - ⭐ **Hypereutectoid ⇒ proeutectoid cementite + pearlite.** - ⭐ **Eutectoid steel (0.76 % C) ⇒ 100 % pearlite.** - ⭐ **Cementite = Fe₃C = 6.67 % C** — fixed stoichiometry, very hard (≈ 1000 HV), brittle. - ⭐ **More carbon → harder, stronger, more brittle, less weldable.** - ⭐ **Fe–Fe₃C diagram is metastable; true equilibrium favors graphite formation, but kinetics favor cementite at room temperature.**

topics.title: Alloy Steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alloy Steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alloy Steel:** An *alloy steel* is a ferrous-based alloy in which **manganese (Mn)** is the only alloying element present in a concentration greater than **1.65 wt%**, **silicon (Si)** or **copper (Cu)** is present in a concentration greater than **0.5 wt%**, or any other alloying element (Cr, Ni, Mo, V, W, Co, etc.) is present in a concentration that is intentionally added to achieve specific mechanical, physical, or chemical properties beyond those obtainable from plain carbon steels.

According to the **AISI (American Iron and Steel Institute)** and **SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers)** four-digit classification system, alloy steels are designated by a **four-digit code (e.g., 4340, 52100, 8620)** where:
- The first two digits indicate the alloy group/principal alloying elements.
- The last two (or three) digits indicate the carbon content in **hundredths of a percent (wt%)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Threshold (KTU Standard):**
> - **Low-Alloy Steel:** Total alloying content < **5 wt%**
> - **High-Alloy Steel:** Total alloying content ≥ **5 wt%** (e.g., Stainless Steels typically contain > 10.5% Cr)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine plain carbon steel as a **plain cup of tea** — it gives you the basic refreshment (strength, hardness), but nothing extraordinary. Now imagine you start **adding ingredients**:
- Add **ginger (Cr)** → gives an extra *protective zing* (corrosion resistance).
- Add **cardamom (Ni)** → gives *smoothness* (toughness, ductility at low temperatures).
- Add **sugar (Mn)** → gives *body and strength* (improves hardenability).
- Add a *spice mix* like **Mo + V** → gives a *unique flavor* (high-temperature strength, wear resistance).

Each alloying element is like an "ingredient" that tailors the steel's properties for a specific engineering application. Just as you wouldn't add cardamom to a coffee, you wouldn't add Ni to a cheap structural beam — it would be a costly overkill.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Metric | Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Carbon content in alloy steels** | **0.1% – 1.0%** (most common: 0.2% – 0.4%) |
| **Total alloying content (Low-alloy)** | **< 5 wt%** |
| **Total alloying content (High-alloy)** | **≥ 5 wt%** |
| **Min. Cr for "stainless" classification** | **10.5 wt%** |
| **SAE 4-digit code carbon multiplier** | **0.01%** (e.g., 4340 = 0.40% C) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Steels" and not "Iron"?** The minimum iron (Fe) content must be ≥ 50% by weight, and the carbon must be ≤ 2 wt% for the material to be classified as steel (per the iron–carbon phase diagram).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Alloying Elements Added? — The "Three Pillars"

Alloying elements are added to plain carbon steel to enhance one or more of the following property classes:

1. **Mechanical Properties** → Strength, hardness, toughness, fatigue resistance, wear resistance.
2. **Physical Properties** → Magnetic behavior, electrical resistivity, thermal expansion, density.
3. **Chemical Properties** → Corrosion resistance, oxidation resistance, acid resistance.

## 2.2 Effects of Major Alloying Elements (Step-by-Step Logic)

### 2.2.1 Manganese (Mn) — "The Strength Booster"
- **Mechanism:** Mn is a strong **austenite stabilizer** and forms a substitutional solid solution with Fe.
- **Effects:**
  - Increases **hardenability** (depth of hardening during quenching).
  - Combines with **sulfur (S)** to form **MnS**, reducing hot shortness.
  - Mildly increases strength via **solid solution strengthening**.
- **Typical content:** 0.5% – 1.5% in carbon steels; up to 12% in **Hadfield's steel** (austenitic Mn-steel for crusher jaws).

### 2.2.2 Silicon (Si) — "The Spring Maker"
- **Mechanism:** Strong **ferrite stabilizer**; promotes graphitization at high Si levels.
- **Effects:**
  - Increases **yield strength** and **elastic limit**.
  - Improves **magnetic permeability** (used in electrical steels, e.g., transformer cores).
  - Enhances **oxidation resistance** at high temperatures.
- **Typical content:** 0.2% – 0.3% (deoxidizer); up to 3%–4% in **spring steels** and **electrical steels**.

### 2.2.3 Chromium (Cr) — "The Stainless Champion"
- **Mechanism:** Strong **ferrite stabilizer**; forms a passive **Cr₂O₃** oxide layer on the surface.
- **Effects:**
  - **> 10.5% Cr** → Corrosion resistance ("stainless" classification).
  - Dramatically increases **hardenability** and **wear resistance**.
  - Forms hard **carbides (Cr₂₃C₆, Cr₇C₃)** that resist abrasion.
  - Improves **high-temperature strength** (used in tool steels).
- **Typical content:** 0.5% – 2% (low-alloy); 12% – 30% (stainless/tool steels).

### 2.2.4 Nickel (Ni) — "The Toughness Enhancer"
- **Mechanism:** Strong **austenite stabilizer**; lowers the brittle-to-ductile transition temperature.
- **Effects:**
  - Promotes **toughness**, especially at sub-zero temperatures.
  - Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., **304: 18% Cr + 8% Ni**) cannot be hardened by quenching.
  - Improves **corrosion resistance** in combination with Cr.
  - Reduces **coefficient of thermal expansion** (used in **Invar alloy: 36% Ni**).
- **Typical content:** 1% – 5% (low-alloy); 8% – 20% (austenitic stainless).

### 2.2.5 Molybdenum (Mo) — "The High-Temperature Stabilizer"
- **Mechanism:** Strong **carbide former**; very strong **ferrite stabilizer**.
- **Effects:**
  - **Dramatically increases hardenability** with minimal grain coarsening.
  - Suppresses **temper brittleness** caused by P, Sn, Sb.
  - Improves **creep resistance** at elevated temperatures (boiler tubes, turbine blades).
- **Typical content:** 0.2% – 0.5% (low-alloy); up to 5% (high-speed tool steels).

### 2.2.6 Vanadium (V) — "The Grain Refiner"
- **Mechanism:** Forms very stable, hard **vanadium carbides (V₄C₃)**.
- **Effects:**
  - Inhibits **austenite grain growth** during high-temperature heat treatment.
  - Provides **secondary hardening** on tempering (V₄C₃ precipitates).
  - Increases **wear resistance** and **fatigue strength**.
- **Typical content:** 0.1% – 0.5% (low-alloy); 1% – 5% (high-speed steels).

### 2.2.7 Tungsten (W) — "The Red-Hardness King"
- **Mechanism:** Forms extremely hard, thermally stable **WC (tungsten carbide)**.
- **Effects:**
  - Retains hardness at **red-heat temperatures** (≈ 600°C) — the property of **"red hardness"**.
  - Essential ingredient of **high-speed tool steels (HSS)** like **T1 (18-4-1)**: 18% W, 4% Cr, 1% V.
- **Typical content:** 1% – 4% (low-alloy); 12% – 18% (HSS).

### 2.2.8 Cobalt (Co) — "The Hot-Hardness Promoter"
- **Mechanism:** Raises the **martensite start (Mₛ)** temperature; does not form carbides.
- **Effects:**
  - Promotes **hot hardness** in high-speed steels.
  - Used in **M-series HSS** (e.g., **M42**: 8% Co, 1.5% W, 9.5% Mo).
  - Used in **alnico permanent magnets**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon content from SAE code** | $C \,(\%) = \dfrac{\text{Last 2 digits}}{100}$ | e.g., 4340 → $0.40\%\ C$ |
| **Hardenability multiplier (Grossmann)** | $D_I = D_{I,\text{base}} \times f_{Mn} \times f_{Cr} \times f_{Ni} \times f_{Mo} \cdot \ldots$ | $D_I$ = Ideal critical diameter (inches) |
| **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$ | $1 < \text{PBR} < 2$ for **protective** oxide (e.g., Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃) |
| **Nitrogen equivalent (for austenitic stability)** | $Ni_{eq} = \%Ni + 30 \times \%C + 0.5 \times \%Mn$ | Used in **Schaeffler diagram** |
| **Chromium equivalent (for ferrite stability)** | $Cr_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \times \%Si + 0.5 \times \%Nb$ | Used in **Schaeffler diagram** |
| **Stainless condition (corrosion resistance)** | $\%Cr \geq 10.5$ | Lower limit for "stainless" classification |
| **Austenite-to-martensite start (Mₛ) — empirical** | $M_s(^{\circ}C) \approx 539 - 423(\%C) - 30.4(\%Mn) - 12.1(\%Cr) - 17.7(\%Ni) - 7.5(\%Mo)$ | Andrews' equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardenability vs. Hardness — A Subtle but Critical Distinction:**
> - **Hardness** = a material's resistance to **localized plastic deformation** (a property at a point).
> - **Hardenability** = the ability of the steel to form **martensite to depth** upon quenching (a bulk property).
> - **Alloying elements increase hardenability, NOT necessarily peak hardness.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Steel Type | Why Alloy? |
|---|---|---|
| **Aircraft landing gear** | 4340 (Ni-Cr-Mo) | Ultra-high strength + toughness |
| **Automotive gears** | 8620 (Ni-Cr-Mo) | Case-hardenable, wear-resistant |
| **Ball bearings** | 52100 (Cr) | Extreme hardness + fatigue resistance |
| **Chemical plant piping** | 304 / 316 (Cr-Ni) | Corrosion resistance |
| **Cutting tools** | M2 / T1 (HSS) | Red hardness, wear resistance |
| **Transformer cores** | 3% Si steel | Low hysteresis loss |
| **Cryogenic tanks** | 9% Ni steel | Toughness at –196°C (LNG storage) |
| **Power plant boilers** | T22 (2.25Cr-1Mo) | Creep resistance at 600°C |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Carbon Content from SAE 4340 Designation

**Problem:** Decode the **SAE/AISI 4340** alloy steel designation and find its carbon content.

**Step 1: Recall the SAE 4-digit convention.**
- Digit 1 → Principal alloy group.
- Digit 2 → Secondary modifier.
- Digits 3 & 4 → Carbon content in **hundredths of a percent (wt%)**.

**Step 2: For 4340:**
- 4 → Ni-Cr-Mo steel group.
- 3 → Approx. 1.8% Ni, 0.8% Cr, 0.25% Mo.
- 40 → Last two digits = **40**.

**Step 3: Apply the formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%C &= \frac{\text{Last 2 digits}}{100} \\
&= \frac{40}{100} \\
&= 0.40\ \%\ \text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** **4340 steel contains 0.40 wt% C** (a medium-carbon alloy steel used for aircraft and high-stress components).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Chromium Equivalent and Stainless Classification (Schaeffler Logic)

**Problem:** A steel contains **18% Cr, 8% Ni, 2% Mn, 1% Si, 0.08% C**. Determine the **Cr equivalent** and verify stainless classification.

**Step 1: Apply the Schaeffler Chromium equivalent formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cr_{eq} &= \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \times \%Si + 0.5 \times \%Nb \\
&= 18 + 0 + 1.5 \times 1 + 0 \\
&= 18 + 1.5 \\
&= 19.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Check the stainless criterion.**

$$
\%Cr = 18 \geq 10.5
$$

→ **Meets the stainless steel criterion.**

**Step 3: Apply the Schaeffler Nickel equivalent formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ni_{eq} &= \%Ni + 30 \times \%C + 0.5 \times \%Mn \\
&= 8 + 30 \times 0.08 + 0.5 \times 2 \\
&= 8 + 2.4 + 1.0 \\
&= 11.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Read the Schaeffler diagram.**
- $Cr_{eq} = 19.5$, $Ni_{eq} = 11.4$ → Falls in the **Austenitic Stainless Steel** region.
- **This is essentially AISI 304 stainless steel** (18/8 stainless).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Martensite Start Temperature (Mₛ) using Andrews' Equation

**Problem:** For a steel with **0.4% C, 0.7% Mn, 1.0% Cr, 1.8% Ni, 0.25% Mo**, estimate the martensite start temperature.

**Step 1: Substitute into Andrews' equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_s(^{\circ}C) &\approx 539 - 423(\%C) - 30.4(\%Mn) - 12.1(\%Cr) - 17.7(\%Ni) - 7.5(\%Mo)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute each term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
423 \times 0.4 &= 169.20 \\
30.4 \times 0.7 &= 21.28 \\
12.1 \times 1.0 &= 12.10 \\
17.7 \times 1.8 &= 31.86 \\
7.5 \times 0.25 &= 1.875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the subtractions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_s &\approx 539 - 169.20 - 21.28 - 12.10 - 31.86 - 1.875 \\
M_s &\approx 539 - 236.315 \\
M_s &\approx 302.7\ ^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The Mₛ temperature is approximately **303 °C**, which is typical for medium-carbon low-alloy steels and means the steel will be **fully martensitic at room temperature (RT) after quenching** since RT < Mₛ.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) for Cr₂O₃

**Problem:** Verify whether the Cr₂O₃ oxide layer on stainless steel is **protective**.

**Given:**
- Reaction: $2\text{Cr} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$
- $M_{\text{Cr}} = 52.0\ \text{g/mol}$, $M_{\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3} = 152.0\ \text{g/mol}$
- $\rho_{\text{Cr}} = 7.19\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3} = 5.22\ \text{g/cm}^3$

**Step 1: Identify constants.**
- $n = 2$ moles of Cr per mole of Cr₂O₃.
- $M_{\text{ox}} = 152.0$, $M_{\text{metal}} = 52.0$, $M_{\text{O}} = 16.0$.

**Step 2: Apply PBR formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PBR} &= \frac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}} \\
&= \frac{152.0 \times 7.19}{2 \times 52.0 \times 5.22}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator and denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 152.0 \times 7.19 = 1092.88 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 2 \times 52.0 \times 5.22 = 542.88
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PBR} &= \frac{1092.88}{542.88} \approx 2.01
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Since **1 < PBR ≈ 2.0 < 2**, the Cr₂O₃ layer is **protective** (it compresses slightly against the metal, blocking further oxygen diffusion). This is the fundamental reason **stainless steel resists corrosion** — a stable, adherent, passive oxide film forms automatically.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Effect Table

| Element | Carbide Former? | Austenite (γ) Stabilizer? | Ferrite (α) Stabilizer? | Primary Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **C** | ✓ (very strong) | ✓ (interstitial) | ✗ | Increases hardness, strength |
| **Mn** | ✓ (weak) | ✓ (strong) | ✗ | Hardenability, de-S |
| **Si** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (strong) | Springiness, deoxidation |
| **Cr** | ✓ (strong) | ✗ | ✓ (strong) | Corrosion resistance, hardness |
| **Ni** | ✗ | ✓ (strong) | ✗ | Toughness, ductility |
| **Mo** | ✓ (very strong) | ✗ | ✓ (very strong) | Creep resistance, hardenability |
| **V** | ✓ (very strong) | ✗ | ✓ | Grain refinement, secondary hardening |
| **W** | ✓ (very strong) | ✗ | ✓ | Red hardness (HSS) |
| **Co** | ✗ | ✓ (weak) | ✗ | Hot hardness, magnetic alloys |
| **Cu** | ✗ | ✓ (weak) | ✗ | Corrosion resistance, precipitation hardening |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree for Alloy Steels

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FERROUS ALLOYS] --> B[STEELS - C less than 2 percent]
    A --> C[CAST IRONS - C greater than 2 percent]

    B --> D[Plain Carbon Steels]
    B --> E[ALLOY STEELS]

    D --> D1[Low Carbon - less than 0.25 percent C]
    D --> D2[Medium Carbon - 0.25 to 0.6 percent C]
    D --> D3[High Carbon - greater than 0.6 percent C]

    E --> F[Low Alloy Steels - total alloy less than 5 percent]
    E --> G[High Alloy Steels - total alloy greater than or equal 5 percent]

    F --> F1[Ni Cr Mo Steels - e.g. 4340]
    F --> F2[Cr Mo Steels - e.g. 4140]
    F --> F3[Ni Cr Steels - e.g. 8620]
    F --> F4[Spring Steels - e.g. 9260]
    F --> F5[Bearing Steels - e.g. 52100]

    G --> H[STAINLESS STEELS - Cr greater than 10.5 percent]
    G --> I[TOOL STEELS]
    G --> J[Maraging Steels - 18 percent Ni]

    H --> H1[Ferritic SS - e.g. 430]
    H --> H2[Austenitic SS - e.g. 304 316]
    H --> H3[Martensitic SS - e.g. 410 420]
    H --> H4[Duplex SS - Ferrite plus Austenite]
    H --> H5[PH SS - e.g. 17 4 PH]

    I --> I1[Cold Work - O1 D2]
    I --> I2[Hot Work - H11 H13]
    I --> I3[High Speed Steel - M2 T1]
    I --> I4[Shock Resistant - S1 S5]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stainless Steel Corrosion Protection Mechanism

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Oxygen and Moisture in Environment] --> B[Contact with Steel Surface]
    B --> C[Chromium greater than 10.5 percent present in steel]
    C --> D[Spontaneous Formation of Cr2O3 Passive Film]
    D --> E{Is film continuous and adherent?}
    E -- PBR between 1 and 2 --> F[Protective Passive Layer]
    E -- PBR less than 1 --> G[Porous Non protective]
    E -- PBR greater than 2 --> H[Spalling and Cracking]
    F --> I[Corrosion Rate drops below 0.001 mm per year]
    I --> J[Steel classified as STAINLESS]
    D --> K[Self healing - if scratched oxygen reforms the film]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Heat Treatment of 4340 Alloy Steel (Aircraft-Quality)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[4340 Steel - 0.4 percent C, 1.8 Ni, 0.8 Cr, 0.25 Mo] --> B[Austenitizing at 830 degree C for 1 hour]
    B --> C[Quench in Oil]
    C --> D[As Quenched Martensite - 60 HRC - Brittle]
    D --> E[Tempering at 200 degree C for 2 hours]
    E --> F[Tempered Martensite - 55 HRC - Tough]
    D --> G[Tempering at 550 degree C for 2 hours]
    G --> H[Tempered Martensite plus Carbides - 35 HRC - Very Tough]
    F --> I[Application: Gears, Shafts]
    H --> J[Application: Aircraft Landing Gear, Crankshafts]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of the Schaeffler Diagram (Microstructural Regions)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Schaeffler Diagram - Cr eq on X axis, Ni eq on Y axis] --> B[Bottom Left - Cr eq less than 10, Ni eq less than 4]
    A --> C[Top Left - Cr eq less than 10, Ni eq greater than 12]
    A --> D[Top Right - Cr eq greater than 16, Ni eq greater than 8]
    A --> E[Bottom Right - Cr eq greater than 10, Ni eq less than 6]
    A --> F[Center - Cr eq 10 to 18, Ni eq 6 to 12]

    B --> B1[Martensitic SS - e.g. 410]
    C --> C1[All Austenitic - e.g. 310]
    D --> D1[Stable Austenitic SS - e.g. 304 316]
    E --> E1[Ferritic SS - e.g. 430]
    F --> F1[Duplex Ferrite plus Austenite - e.g. 2205]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between "hardness" and "hardenability" in the context of alloy steels. Why is hardenability more critical for alloy steels than for plain carbon steels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Definition | Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | Resistance of a material to *localized plastic deformation* (e.g., indentation, scratching). | Measured at a **point** (Rockwell, Vickers). |
| **Hardenability** | Ability of the steel to form **martensite to depth** upon quenching. | Measured as a **depth/profile** (Jominy end-quench test). |

> **Why more critical for alloy steels:** Alloying elements (Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo) shift the **TTT (Time-Temperature-Transformation) curve** to the right, allowing **slower cooling** to still form martensite. This means a thick alloy-steel component can be hardened **throughout** even when quenched in **oil or air**, avoiding the distortion and cracking risks of the water-quench needed for plain carbon steels.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is chromium the most important alloying element for corrosion resistance? What is the minimum chromium content for a steel to be classified as "stainless"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reason 1 — Passive film formation:** Chromium reacts with oxygen to form a thin, continuous, self-healing, **Cr₂O₃** passive oxide layer on the surface. This layer is:
- Thermodynamically stable.
- Impervious to oxygen and moisture.
- **Self-repairing** if scratched (provided oxygen is present).

**Reason 2 — Noble oxide character:** Cr₂O₃ has a **Pilling–Bedworth ratio (PBR) ≈ 2.0**, which is the ideal range (1 < PBR < 2) for a **protective, adherent** film that does not crack or spall.

**Reason 3 — Electronic structure:** The Cr³⁺ ion's small ionic radius and high charge density enable strong Cr–O bonds.

**Minimum Cr content for "stainless" classification:** **≥ 10.5 wt%** (per ISO 3506 and ASTM A240).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

### Q3. Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Adapted]`

**"(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role and effect of the following alloying elements in steels with suitable examples: **(i) Manganese (Mn), (ii) Chromium (Cr), (iii) Nickel (Ni), (iv) Molybdenum (Mo), (v) Vanadium (V).**"

**"(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **Schaeffler diagram** and discuss how it is used to predict the microstructure of stainless-steel weld deposits."

---

### Q3 Model Answer

#### Part (a) — Role of Alloying Elements (7 marks)

| Element | % in Steel | Effect | Mechanism | Example Steel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Manganese (Mn)** | 0.5 – 1.5% (low), 12% (Hadfield) | ↑ Hardenability; de-S (forms MnS) | Austenite stabilizer; forms weak carbide (Mn₃C) | **AISI 1340**, **Hadfield (12% Mn)** |
| **Chromium (Cr)** | 0.5 – 2% (low), 12 – 30% (stainless) | ↑ Corrosion resistance, ↑ hardness, ↑ wear resistance | Ferrite stabilizer; strong carbide former (Cr₂₃C₆); forms Cr₂O₃ | **AISI 52100** (1% Cr bearing steel), **AISI 304** (18% Cr) |
| **Nickel (Ni)** | 1 – 5% (low), 8 – 20% (austenitic SS) | ↑ Toughness (esp. low-T); ↓ ductile-to-brittle transition | Austenite stabilizer; does NOT form carbides | **AISI 4340** (1.8% Ni), **AISI 304** (8% Ni) |
| **Molybdenum (Mo)** | 0.2 – 0.5% (low), 5% (HSS) | ↑ Hardenability, ↑ creep resistance, suppresses temper brittleness | Strong ferrite stabilizer; strong carbide former (Mo₂C) | **AISI 4140** (0.2% Mo), **T22** (1% Mo boiler steel) |
| **Vanadium (V)** | 0.1 – 0.5% (low), 1 – 5% (HSS) | Grain refinement; secondary hardening on tempering | Very strong carbide former (V₄C₃); inhibits grain growth | **AISI 6150** (0.15% V), **M2 HSS** (2% V) |

**Synthesis (1 mark):**
- *Carbide-formers* (Cr, Mo, V, W) → wear resistance + secondary hardening.
- *Austenite-stabilizers* (Mn, Ni, Co) → toughness, low-T performance.
- *Ferrite-stabilizers* (Cr, Si, Mo) → high-T strength, magnetic behavior.

---

#### Part (b) — Schaeffler Diagram (7 marks)

**Definition:** The Schaeffler diagram is an **empirical constitution diagram** used to **predict the microstructure** of stainless-steel weld deposits based on the **chromium equivalent (Cr_eq)** and **nickel equivalent (Ni_eq)** of the weld's chemical composition.

**Axes of the Schaeffler Diagram:**

- **X-axis (horizontal):** $Cr_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5\times\%Si + 0.5\times\%Nb$
- **Y-axis (vertical):** $Ni_{eq} = \%Ni + 30\times\%C + 0.5\times\%Mn$

**Major Microstructural Regions:**

| Region | Coordinates | Microstructure | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** (Bottom-left) | $Cr_{eq} < 10$, $Ni_{eq} < 4$ | **Martensite** | AISI 410, 420 |
| **F** (Bottom-right) | $Cr_{eq} > 10$, $Ni_{eq} < 6$ | **Ferrite** | AISI 430, 446 |
| **A+F** (Center-bottom) | $Cr_{eq} \approx 12$–$18$, $Ni_{eq} \approx 4$–$8$ | **Duplex** | AISI 2205 |
| **γ** (Top-right) | $Cr_{eq} > 16$, $Ni_{eq} > 8$ | **Austenite** | AISI 304, 316 |
| **A+γ** (Center) | $Cr_{eq} \approx 18$–$22$, $Ni_{eq} \approx 8$–$12$ | **Austenite + Ferrite** | AISI 304L weld |

**Use in Welding (3 marks):**
- During welding, the weld pool is rapidly cooled and may solidify in a **non-equilibrium microstructure** (e.g., unwanted martensite in austenitic SS welds → cracking).
- The Schaeffler diagram allows **weld engineers to predict the as-welded microstructure** by computing the Cr_eq and Ni_eq of the diluted weld metal (a mix of base metal + filler + dilution).
- The filler metal is **over-alloyed** (e.g., using 308L filler for 304 base) to ensure the final weld metal composition lands in the **desired region** (austenite + 4–10% ferrite for crack resistance).

**Sketch description:** A 2D plot with the X-axis labeled "Chromium Equivalent" (0 to 30) and Y-axis labeled "Nickel Equivalent" (0 to 24), showing contour lines dividing the plane into regions labeled **M** (martensite), **F** (ferrite), **A** (austenite), and **M+F**, **A+F**, **A+M** mixtures.

---

### Q4. Question B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]`

**"(a) [7 Marks]** Classify **stainless steels** in detail. Explain the microstructure, properties, and applications of **ferritic, austenitic, and martensitic** stainless steels."

**"(b) [7 Marks]** What are **high-speed tool steels (HSS)**? Explain the **'red-hardness'** property, and compare the **T-series (tungsten-based)** with the **M-series (molybdenum-based)** HSS with suitable examples."

---

### Q4 Model Answer

#### Part (a) — Classification of Stainless Steels (7 marks)

**Definition:** Stainless steels are **Fe-Cr alloys with ≥ 10.5% Cr** that resist corrosion due to a passive Cr₂O₃ film.

**Master Classification (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    SS[STAINLESS STEELS - Cr greater than or equal 10.5 percent] --> FER[Ferritic SS]
    SS --> AUS[Austenitic SS]
    SS --> MAR[Martensitic SS]
    SS --> DUP[Duplex SS]
    SS --> PH[Precipitation Hardening SS]
```

**Detailed Table (4 marks):**

| Type | Crystal Structure | Composition | Hardenable? | Magnetic? | Corrosion Resistance | Typical Application | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferritic** | **BCC** (α-Fe) | 12 – 30% Cr, low C (< 0.1%) | No | **Yes** | Good (esp. stress corrosion) | Kitchen sinks, exhaust systems, architectural trim | **AISI 430** (16% Cr) |
| **Austenitic** | **FCC** (γ-Fe) | 16 – 26% Cr, 6 – 22% Ni, low C | No (work-harden only) | **No** | **Excellent** (best overall) | Chemical tanks, food processing, cryogenic vessels, implants | **AISI 304** (18/8), **AISI 316** (with Mo) |
| **Martensitic** | **BCT** (martensite) | 12 – 18% Cr, 0.1 – 1.0% C | **Yes** (quench + temper) | **Yes** | Moderate | Cutlery, surgical instruments, turbine blades, bearings | **AISI 410**, **AISI 420** |
| **Duplex** | BCC + FCC (50:50) | 22 – 25% Cr, 5 – 7% Ni, 3% Mo | No | **Yes** | Excellent (esp. chloride pitting) | Marine hardware, chemical piping, oil & gas | **AISI 2205** |
| **PH (Precipitation Hard.)** | FCC → martensite + precipitates | 15 – 17% Cr, 3 – 5% Ni + Al/Cu/Ti | **Yes** (aging) | **Yes** (after aging) | High | Aerospace fittings, shafts, nuclear | **17-4 PH** (AISI 630) |

---

#### Part (b) — High-Speed Tool Steels & Red Hardness (7 marks)

**Definition:** High-Speed Steels (HSS) are a class of **highly alloyed tool steels** capable of maintaining their **hardness, sharpness, and cutting edge at elevated temperatures (up to 600 °C / "red heat")** generated during high-speed machining operations.

**"Red Hardness" — The Defining Property (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Red hardness is the ability of a steel to retain **high hardness (≥ 60 HRC)** at temperatures approaching **600 °C** (when the metal glows dull red).
- **Mechanism:**
  1. During tempering, **very stable alloy carbides** (V₄C₃, Mo₂C, W₂C) **precipitate finely and uniformly** within the martensite matrix.
  2. These precipitates are **thermally stable** — they resist coarsening (Ostwald ripening) at high temperatures.
  3. They **pin dislocations** even at red heat, maintaining strength.
  4. A high **secondary hardening peak** (≈ 550 °C tempering) shifts the hardness-vs-temp curve upward.

**Comparison of T-series vs. M-series HSS (4 marks):**

| Feature | **T-series (Tungsten-based)** | **M-series (Molybdenum-based)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Key alloying element** | **Tungsten (W)** | **Molybdenum (Mo)** |
| **Typical composition** | W: 12 – 18%, Cr: 4%, V: 1 – 3%, C: 0.7 – 0.8% | Mo: 5 – 9%, W: 1.5 – 6%, Cr: 4%, V: 1 – 3%, C: 0.8 – 1.0% |
| **Classic example** | **T1 (18-4-1)**: 18% W, 4% Cr, 1% V, 0.7% C | **M2 (6-5-4-2)**: 6% W, 5% Mo, 4% Cr, 2% V, 0.85% C |
| **Co-bearing variant** | T6 (with 12% Co for hot hardness) | **M42** (with 8% Co — most popular HSS) |
| **Cost** | **High** (W is expensive) | **Lower** (Mo is cheaper than W, ~1% Mo ≈ 2% W equivalence) |
| **Performance** | Excellent red hardness | Equivalent or slightly better; better grindability |
| **Decarburization** | Lower sensitivity | **Higher** sensitivity (requires protective atmosphere heat treatment) |
| **Applications** | Heavy-duty cutting, form tools | Drills, taps, end-mills, broaches, gear cutters |

**Heat Treatment of HSS (brief):** Austenitize at **1260 – 1300 °C** → oil or air quench → triple temper at **550 – 580 °C** for 2 hours each (to transform retained austenite to martensite + precipitate carbides) → final hardness **62 – 65 HRC**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse hardness with hardenability.** Many students write "alloy steels are hard" — this earns zero marks. The key phrase is **"alloy steels can be hardened to greater depth during quenching"**.
> 2. **Cr₂O₃ (not Fe₂O₃) is the protective oxide** in stainless steel. Writing the wrong oxide is a guaranteed –2 mark deduction.
> 3. **SAE 4-digit code:** Many students forget that the last two digits represent **carbon in 0.01% (not 0.1%)**. So 4340 = 0.40% C, NOT 4.0% C.
> 4. **Austenitic SS are non-magnetic; ferritic and martensitic SS are magnetic.** This is a favorite KTU short-answer test point.
> 5. **HSS red-hardness is NOT about the steel being "red in color."** It is about retaining hardness at the **red-heat temperature (~ 600 °C)**. Be precise.
> 6. **When asked for "examples,"** always write the **AISI/SAE grade number** (e.g., 304, 4340, M2) — vague answers like "chromium steel" earn partial credit only.
> 7. **Schaeffler diagram coordinates** must be computed — students who write the **formula** for Cr_eq / Ni_eq without substitution lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — "Alloy Steels"**

### 1. Core Definitions
- **Alloy Steel:** ≥ 1.65% Mn, OR > 0.5% Si/Cu, OR any deliberate addition of Cr/Ni/Mo/V/W.
- **Stainless Steel:** ≥ **10.5% Cr**.
- **HSS:** Tool steel with **red hardness ≥ 60 HRC at 600 °C**.

### 2. "Big Five" Alloying Elements (MUST memorize)
- **Mn** → Hardenability + de-S.
- **Cr** → Corrosion resistance + carbides.
- **Ni** → Toughness + austenite stabilizer.
- **Mo** → Creep resistance + temper-brittleness suppressor.
- **V** → Grain refiner + secondary hardening.

### 3. Three Must-Know Formulae
- **Carbon from SAE code:** $\%C = \text{(last 2 digits)} / 100$.
- **Cr_eq (Schaeffler):** $Cr_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5\times\%Si + 0.5\times\%Nb$.
- **Ni_eq (Schaeffler):** $Ni_{eq} = \%Ni + 30\times\%C + 0.5\times\%Mn$.

### 4. Three Must-Know Steels
- **AISI 304** → 18% Cr + 8% Ni → **Austenitic, non-magnetic, most common SS**.
- **AISI 4340** → 0.4% C + 1.8% Ni + 0.8% Cr + 0.25% Mo → **Aircraft-grade ultra-high-strength steel**.
- **AISI M2** → 6-5-4-2 (W-Mo-Cr-V) → **Most popular HSS for drills, taps, end-mills**.

### 5. Critical Distinctions (Likely 3-mark questions)
- **Hardness vs. Hardenability** → point vs. depth.
- **Austenitic vs. Martensitic SS** → FCC/non-magnetic vs. BCT/magnetic/hardenable.
- **T-series vs. M-series HSS** → W-based vs. Mo-based, cost & decarburization difference.

### 6. Numbers to Memorize
- **10.5%** → Min Cr for "stainless."
- **5%** → Threshold between low-alloy and high-alloy.
- **1.65%** → Min Mn for "alloy steel" classification.
- **600 °C** → Operating temperature for "red hardness."
- **PBR ≈ 2.0** → Ideal protective oxide (1 < PBR < 2).

### 7. Standard SAE 4-digit groups (Quick recall)
- **1xxx** → Carbon steels
- **2xxx** → Ni steels (e.g., 2515)
- **3xxx** → Ni-Cr steels (e.g., 3310)
- **4xxx** → Mo steels (e.g., 4037), Cr-Mo (e.g., 4140)
- **5xxx** → Cr steels (e.g., 52100)
- **6xxx** → Cr-V steels (e.g., 6150)
- **7xxx** → W steels (e.g., 7260)
- **8xxx** → Ni-Cr-Mo steels (e.g., 8620, 4340)
- **9xxx** → Si-Mn steels (e.g., 9260 — spring steel)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ferrous Metals and Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ferrous Metals and Alloys # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Ferrous Metals & Alloys** are metallic materials whose principal component is **iron (Fe)**, typically combined with **carbon** and other alloying elements such as Ni, Cr, Mn, Mo, Si, and V. They form the backbone of modern engineering because of their exceptional combination of strength, ductility, hardness, and—most importantly—their ability to be tailored over an enormous property range through compositional control and heat treatment. In the KTU 2024 scheme, *Ferrous Metals and Alloys* covers the entire spectrum of **iron-based engineering materials**, namely: - **Plain Carbon Steels** (Fe + C + Mn/Si residual) - **Alloy Steels** (Fe + C + deliberate alloying additions) - **Stainless Steels** (Fe + Cr ≥ 10.5 % with optional Ni/Mo) - **Tool Steels** (High C, high alloy for cutting/forming) - **Cast Irons** (Fe + C > 2.14 %, with graphite/Fe₃C control) > [!IMPORTANT] > The dividing line between **steel** and **cast iron** is the **carbon solubility limit in austenite at 1147 °C**, which is **2.14 % C**. Below this → steel. Above this → cast iron. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron Chef's Pantry" Think of **pure iron** as a plain, soft lump of *idli dough*—malleable, weak, and dull. Now imagine a chef who can sprinkle in different "spices": - **Carbon** acts like **salt**—a tiny pinch transforms the texture dramatically (hardness, strength jump). Too much and the dough becomes brittle. - **Chromium** is the **turmeric** that gives corrosion immunity (the famous "stainless" surface). - **Nickel** is the **oil** that makes it tough, ductile, and impact-resistant even at sub-zero temperatures. - **Molybdenum** and **Tungsten** are the **hot masala**—keeping strength intact at high temperatures. - **Vanadium** is the **yeast** that refines grain size for superior strength. Just as a chef creates a thousand different dishes from one dough, the metallurgist creates thousands of grades (1020, 4140, 304, H13, D2, GG-25, SG-400, etc.) from one base: **iron**. ## 1.3 Why Ferrous Alloys Dominate Manufacturing | Property Indicator | Ferrous Alloys | Typical Range | |---|---|---| | Annual Global Tonnage (Steel) | > 1.8 billion tonnes/yr | ≈ 90 % of all metals used | | Cost per kg | **\$ 0.6 – 1.2** | Lowest of structural metals | | Recyclability | **100 %** without quality loss | Infinitely sustainable | | Strength Range (UTS) | 300 – 2500 MPa | Widest of any alloy family | > [!TIP] > KTU 2024 examiner's hint: *Any* ferrous alloy question invariably begins with locating the alloy on the **Iron–Iron Carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) phase diagram**. Always sketch the diagram in the answer sheet—even if the question does not explicitly ask for it. It earns you 1–2 free marks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Master Classification of Ferrous Alloys The complete taxonomy can be summarized in a single hierarchical picture: ```text FERROUS ALLOYS │ ├── STEELS (C ≤ 2.14 %) │ ├── Plain Carbon Steel │ │ ├── Low Carbon (< 0.25 % C) → sheets, wires, structural │ │ ├── Medium Carbon(0.25 – 0.6 %) → axles, gears, rails │ │ ├── High Carbon (0.6 – 1.0 %) → springs, chisels │ │ └── Ultra-High (> 1.0 %) → tools, dies │ │ │ ├── Alloy Steel │ │ ├── Low Alloy (total alloy < 8 %) │ │ └── High Alloy (total alloy ≥ 8 %) │ │ │ ├── Stainless Steel │ │ ├── Austenitic (304, 316) – non-magnetic │ │ ├── Ferritic (430) – magnetic │ │ ├── Martensitic (410, 420) – hardenable │ │ └── Duplex (2205) – mixed │ │ │ └── Tool Steel │ ├── Water-hardening (W-series) │ ├── Oil-hardening (O-series) │ ├── Air-hardening (A-series) │ ├── High-speed (M-, T-series) │ └── Hot-work (H-series) │ └── CAST IRONS (C = 2.14 – 6.67 %) ├── White CI (Fe₃C, hard, brittle) ├── Gray CI (Graphite flakes) ├── Malleable CI (Tempered white CI) ├── Ductile/Nodular CI (Spheroidal graphite + Mg) └── Compacted Graphite CI ``` ## 2.2 The Iron–Carbon Phase Diagram — The Master Map The Fe–Fe₃C diagram is the Rosetta Stone for ferrous metallurgy. Three critical invariant points must be memorized: | Invariant Point | Temperature (°C) | Carbon (%) | Reaction | |---|---|---|---| | **Peritectic** | 1493 | 0.16 | L + δ-ferrite → γ-austenite | | **Eutectic** | 1147 | 4.30 | L → austenite + Fe₃C (Ledeburite) | | **Eutectoid** | 727 | 0.77 | γ → α-ferrite + Fe₃C (Pearlite) | ### 2.2.1 Key Temperature Lines - **A₁ (727 °C)**: Lowest temperature at which austenite exists. - **A₃**: Upper boundary of (γ + α) field—varies with C. - **A_cm**: Upper boundary of (γ + Fe₃C) field—also varies. - **A₄ (1394 °C)**: Upper boundary of (γ + δ) field. > [!IMPORTANT] > The subscript **"c"** (A_c1, A_c3) denotes **heating** (from French *chauffage*); the subscript **"r"** (A_r1, A_r3) denotes **cooling** (from *refroidissement*). They differ because of undercooling/overheating during transformation. **KTU expects both notations** in heat-treatment problems. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical Equations) | # | Formula / Relationship | Variables | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$ | $W_\alpha$ = ferrite mass fraction; $C_0$ = alloy C; $C_\gamma$ = 0.77; $C_\alpha$ = 0.022 | mass % | Lever rule in eutectoid steel at 727 °C | | 2 | $W_{Fe_3C} = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022}$ | Fe₃C in proeutectoid region | mass % | Cementite in hypereutectoid steel | | 3 | $W_{pearlite} = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022}$ | Pearlite fraction in hypo-eutectoid | mass % | Microstructure quantification | | 4 | $W_{proeut.\alpha} = \dfrac{0.77 - C_0}{0.77 - 0.022}$ | Proeutectoid ferrite fraction | mass % | Below eutectoid only | | 5 | $n = \dfrac{\log(\text{old diam}) - \log(\text{new diam})}{\log 2}$ | Grain-size ASTM number | dimensionless | McQuaid–Ehn grain size | | 6 | $\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Cold work percentage | % | Strengthening by deformation | | 7 | $HV \approx 0.3 \times HB$ | Hardness correlation | kgf/mm² | Cross-check hardness scales | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Do not** use the vertical bar `\|` character inside the table cells (e.g., for absolute value). Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. This is mandatory for clean markdown rendering in KTU online portals. ## 2.4 Effect of Alloying Elements — The "Why" Behind Each Addition | Element | Carbide Former? | Primary Effect | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | **C** | Yes | Strength & hardness ↑, ductility ↓ | All steels | | **Mn** | Yes | Deoxidizer, ↑ hardenability, ↑ strength | HSLA, Hadfield (12 % Mn) | | **Si** | No | ↑ strength, deoxidizer, electrical resistivity | Spring steel, transformer cores | | **Cr** | Yes (strong) | ↑ corrosion resistance (≥ 12 %), ↑ hardness | Stainless steel, bearing steel | | **Ni** | No | ↑ toughness, ↑ ductility, austenite stabilizer | 300-series SS, Invar (36 % Ni) | | **Mo** | Yes (strong) | ↑ high-temp strength, reduces temper brittleness | 4140, 4340, HSLA | | **V** | Yes (strong) | Grain refiner, secondary hardening | High-speed steels (M2) | | **W** | Yes | Red hardness, wear resistance | T1 (18-4-1) HSS | | **Cu** | No | Atmospheric corrosion resistance, ↑ strength | Weathering steel (Corten) | | **Co** | No | ↑ hot hardness, magnetic | Maraging steel, AlNiCo magnets | | **Nb / Ti** | Yes | Grain pinning via carbides/nitrides | Microalloyed HSLA | > [!NOTE] > **Carbide formers** (Cr, Mo, V, W, Ti, Nb) are *ferrite stabilizers*; they raise the eutectoid temperature and shift the diagram to the right (longer time needed for transformation = **better hardenability**). **Nickel and Manganese** are *austenite stabilizers*; they lower the eutectoid temperature and expand the γ-field. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Lever-Rule Derivation: Quantitative Microstructure of a 0.4 % C Steel **Problem (KTU Standard):** A plain carbon steel contains **0.40 % C**. Just below the eutectoid isotherm (just under 727 °C), determine: - (i) Mass fraction of **proeutectoid ferrite**, - (ii) Mass fraction of **pearlite**, - (iii) Mass fraction of **total ferrite** (proeutectoid + eutectoid), - (iv) Mass fraction of **cementite**. **Given (from the Fe–Fe₃C diagram):** - $C_\alpha = 0.022 \text{ \%}$ (solubility of C in α-ferrite at 727 °C) - $C_{eutectoid} = 0.77 \text{ \%}$ - $C_{Fe_3C} = 6.67 \text{ \%}$ ### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the alloy type.** Since $0.022 < C_0 = 0.40 < 0.77$, the steel is **hypo-eutectoid**. **Step 2 — Draw the tie-line at 727 °C.** The tie-line stretches from $C_\alpha$ (α-ferrite boundary) to $C_{Fe_3C}$ (cementite). The alloy composition $C_0$ sits on this line, with **pearlite** as a point at $0.77$ %. **Step 3 — Apply the lever rule between $C_0$ and the pearlite point (0.77 %).** $$ W_{proeut.\alpha} \;=\; \frac{C_{pearlite} - C_0}{C_{pearlite} - C_\alpha} \;=\; \frac{0.77 - 0.40}{0.77 - 0.022} $$ $$ W_{proeut.\alpha} \;=\; \frac{0.37}{0.748} \;=\; 0.4947 \;\approx\; 49.5 \text{ \%} $$ **Step 4 — Pearlite is the complement.** $$ W_{pearlite} \;=\; 1 - W_{proeut.\alpha} \;=\; 1 - 0.4947 \;=\; 0.5053 \;\approx\; 50.5 \text{ \%} $$ **Step 5 — Total ferrite includes the ferrite *inside* the pearlite.** Within pearlite, the ferrite mass fraction is: $$ W_{\alpha\,\text{in pearlite}} \;=\; \frac{6.67 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{5.90}{6.648} \;=\; 0.8875 $$ **Step 6 — Total ferrite.** $$ W_{\alpha,\text{total}} \;=\; W_{proeut.\alpha} \;+\; \big(W_{pearlite} \times W_{\alpha\,\text{in pearlite}}\big) $$ $$ W_{\alpha,\text{total}} \;=\; 0.4947 \;+\; (0.5053 \times 0.8875) $$ $$ W_{\alpha,\text{total}} \;=\; 0.4947 \;+\; 0.4487 \;=\; 0.9434 \;\approx\; 94.3 \text{ \%} $$ **Step 7 — Cementite is the remaining phase.** $$ W_{Fe_3C} \;=\; 1 - 0.9434 \;=\; 0.0566 \;\approx\; 5.7 \text{ \%} $$ **Verification using direct lever rule on tie-line:** $$ W_{Fe_3C} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_{Fe_3C} - C_\alpha} \;=\; \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.378}{6.648} \;\approx\; 0.0569 $$ The two answers agree to within rounding error. ✓ ### 3.1.2 Final Tabulated Result | Phase | Mass Fraction | Volume (visual) under microscope | |---|---|---| | Proeutectoid α-ferrite | **49.5 %** | Light grains along prior-austenite boundaries | | Pearlite (α + Fe₃C) | **50.5 %** | Dark, lamellar colonies | | Total α-ferrite | **94.3 %** | All light regions | | Cementite (Fe₃C) | **5.7 %** | Thin lamellae inside pearlite | --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cast Iron Family Comparison Cast irons are characterized by the **form, distribution, and amount of carbon**. Four industrial variants dominate engineering practice. The table below is the *single most important comparison* for KTU Module 3. | Property | **White CI** | **Gray CI** | **Malleable CI** | **Ductile (Nodular) CI** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Carbon form | Cementite (Fe₃C) | Graphite flakes | Temper graphite rosettes | Spheroidal graphite | | Production | Rapid cooling of low-Si melt | Slow cooling, 1–3 % Si | Heat-treat white CI | Add Mg/Ce to gray CI melt | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | 200 – 400 | 150 – 400 | 350 – 500 | **400 – 700** | | Ductility (% El) | ≈ 0 | < 1 | 2 – 10 | **10 – 25** | | Hardness (HB) | 400 – 600 | 150 – 250 | 150 – 250 | 150 – 250 | | Compressive Strength | High | High | High | High | | Damping Capacity | Very Low | **Highest of all CI** | Good | Moderate | | Machinability | Very Poor (abrasive) | **Excellent** (flakes chip) | Good | Good | | Wear Resistance | **Highest** | Moderate | Good | Good | | Typical Use | Grinding balls, crusher liners | Engine blocks, machine beds | Pipe fittings, chain links | Crankshafts, gears, pipes | > [!TIP] > **Why Gray CI damps vibration:** The graphite flakes act like internal *crack-arresters* and *stress-relievers*. When a vibration wave hits a flake, it reflects with reduced amplitude—converting mechanical energy into microscopic frictional heat. This is why precision machine tools are almost always mounted on **Gray CI bases**. > [!TIP] > **Why Ductile CI is ductile:** In Gray CI, the flake tips are *sharp* → stress concentrators → brittle fracture. In Ductile CI, the graphite is *spheroidal* (formed because Mg desulfurizes the melt and forces graphite to nucleate as a sphere). Spheres have **no sharp tips**, so stress concentration is minimal → ductile behavior. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Selecting a Cast Iron for a Gearbox Housing > [!NOTE] > **Question (Internal Choice Style):** A design engineer must choose a cast iron for a **gearbox housing** that must (a) damp gear-meshing vibrations, (b) be machined to complex internal shapes, and (c) cost-effectively. Recommend a specific cast iron grade and justify. **Solution Path (mark-by-mark):** 1. **Vibration damping** → graphite must be in *flake* form (highest damping). This eliminates White, Malleable, and Ductile CI. **Gray CI wins.** *(2 marks)* 2. **Machinability of complex shapes** → flakes break chips short and act as a built-in lubricant. **Gray CI is the most machinable ferrous alloy in existence.** *(2 marks)* 3. **Cost** → Gray CI is the *cheapest* engineering alloy on the market (no alloy additions, simplest melt practice). *(2 marks)* 4. **Specify grade:** **ASTM A48 Class 30 (FG 300)** — minimum tensile strength 30 ksi (~207 MPa), pearlitic matrix, fine graphite (Type A, size 4–6). Used in 80 % of Indian automobile gearbox housings. *(2 marks)* 5. **Limitation to mention (for full credit):** Gray CI has *low ductility and impact strength*; if the housing must also withstand shock loads, **upgrade to Ferritic Ductile CI (ASTM A536 Grade 60-40-18)**. *(2 marks)* --- ## 3.4 Stainless Steel Decision Tree (Analytical Tool) Use the following decision rules when identifying a stainless grade in a viva or problem: ```text START → Does the grade contain ≥ 12 % Cr? ├── NO → It is NOT a stainless steel (re-classify as alloy/tool steel). └── YES → Is the microstructure magnetic? ├── NO → Check Ni content: │ ├── Ni ≥ 7 % → AUSTENITIC (e.g., 304, 316) → non-magnetic, weldable │ └── Ni < 7 % → Unstable austenite (e.g., 201) — still austenitic └── YES → Check hardenability by heat treatment: ├── Hardenable (C > 0.1 %) → MARTENSITIC (410, 420, 440C) → cutlery, surgical └── Not hardenable, Cr ≥ 17 % → FERRITIC (430, 446) → auto trim, exhausts ``` **The 18-8 Stainless Legacy (304):** 18 % Cr + 8 % Ni → fully austenitic, non-magnetic, corrosion-resistant to most organics and oxidizing acids. Adding **2–3 % Mo** upgrades to **316** for marine/chemical service (chloride resistance). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full ferrous-alloy taxonomy with their principal alloying chemistry, suitable for the first page of any 14-mark answer. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["FERROUS ALLOYS
Base Element IRON"] nodeB["STEELS
Carbon up to 2.14 percent"] nodeC["CAST IRONS
Carbon 2.14 to 6.67 percent"] nodeD["Plain Carbon Steel
C plus Mn plus Si residual"] nodeE["Alloy Steel
Added Ni Cr Mo V etc"] nodeF["Stainless Steel
Cr greater than 10.5 percent"] nodeG["Tool Steel
High C plus carbide formers"] nodeH["White Cast Iron
Carbon as Fe3C"] nodeI["Gray Cast Iron
Carbon as graphite flakes"] nodeJ["Malleable Cast Iron
Tempered white iron"] nodeK["Ductile Cast Iron
Spheroidal graphite plus Mg"] nodeL["Austenitic SS
Examples 304 316"] nodeM["Ferritic SS
Example 430"] nodeN["Martensitic SS
Examples 410 420"] nodeO["Duplex SS
Example 2205"] nodeP["Low Alloy Steel
Total alloy below 8 percent"] nodeQ["High Alloy Steel
Total alloy 8 percent or more"] nodeR["Water Hardening W series"] nodeS["High Speed M and T series"] nodeT["Hot Work H series"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeH nodeC --> nodeI nodeC --> nodeJ nodeC --> nodeK nodeE --> nodeP nodeE --> nodeQ nodeF --> nodeL nodeF --> nodeM nodeF --> nodeN nodeF --> nodeO nodeG --> nodeR nodeG --> nodeS nodeG --> nodeT ``` ## 4.2 Iron-Carbon Phase Diagram — Functional Architecture Because the Fe–Fe₃C diagram is a 2-D plot (not a process flow), we represent it as a *functional map* showing the regions, invariant points, and the corresponding microstructures observed on cooling. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Liquid Region L
Above liquidus line"] nodeB["L plus delta Region
Peritectic at 1493 C 0.16 percent C"] nodeC["Austenite gamma
FCC iron stable field"] nodeD["Austenite plus Ferrite
Hypo eutectoid region"] nodeE["Pearlite Region
Eutectoid at 727 C 0.77 percent C"] nodeF["Austenite plus Cementite
Hyper eutectoid region"] nodeG["Ledeburite Region
Eutectic at 1147 C 4.3 percent C"] nodeH["Ferrite alpha
BCC iron stable at room T"] nodeI["Cementite Fe3C
6.67 percent C hard brittle"] nodeJ["Martensite
Rapid quench of austenite"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeE nodeF --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeI nodeE --> nodeH nodeE --> nodeI nodeC --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeH ``` ## 4.3 Effect of Alloying Elements — Schematic Flow The diagram below shows the dichotomy between **ferrite stabilizers** and **austenite stabilizers**, and how each moves the Fe–Fe₃C phase boundaries. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["ALLOYING ELEMENT ADDED TO STEEL"] nodeB["Carbide Formers
Cr Mo V W Ti Nb"] nodeC["Non Carbide Formers
Ni Mn Si Cu Co P"] nodeD["FERRITE STABILIZERS
Raise A1 and A3 temperatures"] nodeE["AUSTENITE STABILIZERS
Lower A1 and A3 temperatures"] nodeF["Result Increase strength hardness wear resistance
Examples 52100 bearing 17-4 PH"] nodeG["Result Increase toughness ductility impact resistance
Examples 304 SS Hadfield 12 percent Mn"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeG ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common KTU Diagram Mistake:** Students often label the eutectoid temperature as *730 °C* or *720 °C*. The **exact KTU-accepted value is 727 °C**. Examiners do deduct marks. Memorize the precision: **A₁ = 727 °C ± 2 °C** depending on alloy purity. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Differentiate between ferrous and non-ferrous metals. Give two examples of each.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Ferrous Metals | Non-Ferrous Metals | |---|---|---| | Base metal | Contains iron (Fe) as principal element | No iron (or Fe as trace impurity) | | Magnetic | Generally yes (except austenitic SS) | Generally no | | Corrosion resistance | Low to moderate (except stainless) | High (Al, Cu, Ti) | | Density | Higher (steel ≈ 7.85 g/cm³) | Lower (Al ≈ 2.7, Ti ≈ 4.5) | | Cost | Lower | Higher | *Examples:* Ferrous — Mild steel, Cast iron, Stainless steel 304. Non-ferrous — Aluminium, Copper, Brass, Titanium. *(1 mark for table, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for one-line definition)* ### **Q2. List the four major types of cast iron. How does the form of carbon differ in each?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **White Cast Iron** — Carbon present as **iron carbide (Fe₃C / cementite)**. Hard, brittle, abrasion-resistant. *(1 mark)* 2. **Gray Cast Iron** — Carbon present as **graphite flakes** in a ferrite/pearlite matrix. Excellent machinability and damping. *(1 mark)* 3. **Malleable Cast Iron** — Carbon present as **temper graphite (irregular rosettes)** obtained by heat-treating white CI. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Ductile / Nodular Cast Iron** — Carbon present as **spheroidal graphite nodules** produced by Mg-treatment of the melt. *(0.5 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Iron–Carbon Diagram and Phase Calculations** > **`[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`** **A. (a)** Sketch the iron–iron carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) equilibrium phase diagram and label all the salient features: the **peritectic, eutectic, and eutectoid invariant points**, the A₁, A₃, A_cm lines, and the regions corresponding to α-ferrite, γ-austenite, δ-ferrite, cementite, pearlite, and ledeburite. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution Outline (7 marks):** 1. **Axes:** Temperature (y-axis, 0–1600 °C) vs Carbon % (x-axis, 0–6.67 %). Label composition scale. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Peritectic point (1493 °C, 0.16 % C):** L + δ → γ. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Eutectic point (1147 °C, 4.3 % C):** L → γ + Fe₃C (Ledeburite). *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Eutectoid point (727 °C, 0.77 % C):** γ → α + Fe₃C (Pearlite). *(0.5 mark)* 5. **A₁ horizontal line at 727 °C** through eutectoid composition. *(0.5 mark)* 6. **A₃ curve** from peritectic composition to eutectoid; separates (γ) from (α + γ). *(0.5 mark)* 7. **A_cm curve** from eutectoid to (γ + Fe₃C) region. *(0.5 mark)* 8. **A₄ line** at 1394 °C, separates (γ) from (δ + γ). *(0.5 mark)* 9. **Solid solubility lines:** α/(α+γ) at 0.022 % C; γ/(γ+Fe₃C) sloping down to 0.77 % C. *(0.5 mark)* 10. **Phases labeled** in all 7 single- and two-phase regions. *(1 mark)* 11. **Proper heading + neat sketch** with key temperatures marked. *(1 mark)* **A. (b)** A plain carbon steel contains **0.45 % C**. Calculate the mass fractions of (i) proeutectoid ferrite, (ii) pearlite, (iii) total ferrite, and (iv) cementite, just below the eutectoid isotherm. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** Given: $C_0 = 0.45$ %, $C_\alpha = 0.022$ %, $C_{eut} = 0.77$ %, $C_{Fe_3C} = 6.67$ %. Since $0.022 < 0.45 < 0.77$, alloy is **hypo-eutectoid**. **(i) Proeutectoid ferrite:** $$ W_{\alpha,\text{pro}} \;=\; \frac{0.77 - 0.45}{0.77 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.32}{0.748} \;=\; 0.4278 \;\approx\; 42.8 \text{ \%} $$ *(2 marks — formula + substitution + result)* **(ii) Pearlite:** $$ W_{pearlite} \;=\; 1 - 0.4278 \;=\; 0.5722 \;\approx\; 57.2 \text{ \%} $$ *(1 mark)* **(iii) Total ferrite:** Within pearlite, ferrite fraction $= \dfrac{6.67 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{5.90}{6.648} = 0.8875$ $$ W_{\alpha,\text{total}} \;=\; 0.4278 + (0.5722 \times 0.8875) \;=\; 0.4278 + 0.5078 \;=\; 0.9356 \;\approx\; 93.6 \text{ \%} $$ *(2 marks — recognition that pearlite itself contains ferrite + arithmetic)* **(iv) Cementite:** $$ W_{Fe_3C} \;=\; 1 - 0.9356 \;=\; 0.0644 \;\approx\; 6.4 \text{ \%} $$ *Verification:* $W_{Fe_3C} = \dfrac{0.45 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.428}{6.648} = 0.0644$ ✓ *(2 marks)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Cast Irons & Alloy Steels** > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **B. (a)** Compare the microstructure, mechanical properties, and typical applications of **Gray Cast Iron, White Cast Iron, Malleable Cast Iron, and Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** using a comparison table. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** The candidate must present a structured table covering **all three dimensions** for **all four** alloys. The expected rows (in any order) are: | Feature | White CI | Gray CI | Malleable CI | Ductile CI | |---|---|---|---|---| | Carbon form | Fe₃C | Graphite flakes | Temper graphite | Spheroidal graphite | | Production | Rapid cool, low Si | Slow cool, 1–3 % Si | Heat-treat white CI | Add Mg/Ce to melt | | Tensile (MPa) | 200–400 | 150–400 | 350–500 | 400–700 | | Elongation (%) | ≈ 0 | < 1 | 2–10 | 10–25 | | Hardness (HB) | 400–600 | 150–250 | 150–250 | 150–250 | | Damping | Lowest | **Highest** | Good | Moderate | | Machinability | Very poor | **Excellent** | Good | Good | | Wear | **Best** | Moderate | Good | Good | | Application | Crusher liners, grinding balls | Engine blocks, machine tool beds | Pipe fittings, chain links | Crankshafts, gears, pipes | *(7 marks — 1.5 marks per correct row × 4 alloy columns ≈ 6 marks; +1 mark for neatness and key bolding of extremes)* **B. (b)** With the help of suitable examples, explain the **effects of Cr, Ni, Mn, Mo, and V** as alloying elements in steels. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Chromium (Cr)** — Strong carbide former; ≥ 12 % gives **corrosion resistance** by forming a passive Cr₂O₃ film.
