Above 18 % gives full stainless behaviour. *Example:* **AISI 304 (18Cr-8Ni)**, **AISI 410 (12 % Cr, martensitic SS).** *(1.5 marks)* 2. **Nickel (Ni)** — Austenite stabilizer; promotes toughness, ductility, and impact strength even at sub-zero temperatures. *Example:* **Invar (36 % Ni) — near-zero thermal expansion**; **3.5 % Ni in armour plate.** *(1.5 marks)* 3. **Manganese (Mn)** — Cheap austenite stabilizer; deoxidizes the melt; increases **hardenability** (shifts TTT curve right). *Example:* **Hadfield steel (12 % Mn)** — work-hardens to 500 HB in service, used in railway crossings, rock crushers. *(1.5 marks)* 4. **Molybdenum (Mo)** — Strongest carbide former among common additions; gives **secondary hardening** and high-temp creep resistance. Reduces temper embrittlement. *Example:* **AISI 4140 (1 % Cr, 0.2 % Mo)** — crankshafts, axles, gears. *(1.5 marks)* 5. **Vanadium (V)** — Strongest grain refiner; forms stable **V₄C₃ / VCN** particles that pin grain boundaries, increasing yield strength without sacrificing toughness. *Example:* **AISI M2 high-speed steel (1 % V, 4 % Cr, 6 % W, 5 % Mo)** — cutting tools, drills. *(1 mark)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT** write "Nickel makes steel stainless" — that is **chromium's** job. Nickel stabilizes austenite and improves toughness. > 2. **Do NOT** confuse *austenite stabilizers* (Ni, Mn, C, N, Cu) with *ferrite stabilizers* (Cr, Mo, Si, V, W, Ti, Nb). The diagram shifts in opposite directions for each group. > 3. **Do NOT** quote Mn content of stainless steels as "12 %" for the "Hadfield" application only. Mn in stainless is usually limited to ≤ 2 % (e.g., **AISI 202 = 18Cr-5Ni-8Mn** is the Mn-substituted austenitic SS for cost saving). > 4. **Do NOT** forget to state that alloying elements **raise the cost**. Always conclude with the **cost–performance trade-off** for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Rapid-Revision Table — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall** - [ ] **Steel vs Cast Iron boundary:** **2.14 % C** at 1147 °C. - [ ] **Eutectoid point:** **0.77 % C at 727 °C** — the most tested number. - [ ] **Eutectic point:** **4.3 % C at 1147 °C** — governs cast-iron solidification. - [ ] **Peritectic point:** **0.16 % C at 1493 °C** — δ-ferrite disappears. - [ ] **A₁ = 727 °C**, **A₃ ≈ 727–910 °C** (sloping), **A_cm** slopes down from 727 °C at 0.77 % to 1147 °C at 2.14 %. - [ ] **Phases in steel:** α (BCC, soft, magnetic), γ (FCC, ductile, non-magnetic), Fe₃C (intermetallic, hard, brittle), Pearlite (lamellar α+Fe₃C), Martensite (BCT, hardest, formed by rapid quench). - [ ] **Lever rule equations** (memorize all three variants — ferrite, pearlite, cementite). - [ ] **Pearlite is 100 % ferrite + cementite** at the eutectoid composition; the *ferrite content inside pearlite* is **88.75 %** (a magic number that appears in almost every calculation). - [ ] **Cast iron family:** White = Fe₃C; Gray = flakes; Malleable = rosettes; Ductile = spheroids; Compacted = short thick flakes. - [ ] **Ductile iron needs Mg or Ce treatment** of the melt to spheroidize graphite. - [ ] **Gray iron has the highest damping capacity** → use for machine tool beds, engine blocks. - [ ] **Ductile iron is the strongest** (up to 700 MPa UTS) and most ductile (up to 25 % El). - [ ] **Stainless steel requires Cr ≥ 10.5 %**; 304 is 18-8 austenitic; 430 is ferritic; 410 is martensitic. - [ ] **Hadfield steel = 12 % Mn** → work-hardening grade. - [ ] **High-speed steel M2 = 6W-5Mo-4Cr-2V** (the famous "6-5-4-2" formula). - [ ] **AISI/SAE 4-digit code:** First 2 digits = alloy type, last 2 = carbon % × 100 (e.g., **1040 = 0.40 % C plain carbon steel**; **4340 = Ni-Cr-Mo alloy with 0.40 % C**). - [ ] **Always draw the Fe–Fe₃C diagram** at the start of a 14-mark answer — even if the question does not ask for it. You earn ≥ 1 free mark. - [ ] **Final sentence of every answer** must contain the **engineering application** of the alloy discussed (e.g., "Hence this alloy is used in..."). Examiners reward application-oriented thinking. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Ferrous Metals and Alloys** > *You are now ready to attempt any 3-mark, 7-mark, or 14-mark question on this topic in the KTU 2024 ESE pattern.*

topics.title: Aluminium and its Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aluminium and its Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Aluminium — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aluminium (Al)** is a lightweight, silvery-white metallic element (atomic number **Z = 13**, atomic mass **26.98 g/mol**, density **ρ = 2.70 g/cm³**, melting point **660.3 °C**) belonging to the Boron group (Group IIIA) of the periodic table. It crystallises in a Face-Centred Cubic (FCC) crystal structure with a lattice parameter of **a = 4.05 Å** and is the **third most abundant element** and the **most abundant metal** in the Earth's crust (~8.1% by mass).

Pure aluminium is **mechanically soft** (tensile strength ≈ **90 MPa**), but exhibits excellent **formability, corrosion resistance** (due to a self-healing, passive **Al₂O₃** layer of thickness ~2–10 nm), and high **electrical/thermal conductivity** (~61% IACS).

### 1.2 Why Pure Aluminium is NOT Used Structurally

> [!NOTE]
> Pure aluminium (1xxx series) is soft and lacks the strength required for load-bearing engineering applications. To overcome this limitation, **alloying elements** such as Cu, Mg, Si, Mn, and Zn are added, and the resulting alloys are subjected to **precipitation hardening (age hardening)** to enhance mechanical strength by a factor of **5× to 15×**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Reinforced Concrete Principle":**
> Think of pure aluminium as a plain chalk stick — easy to break under load. Just as steel rods are embedded inside concrete to give it tensile strength, **alloying elements + heat treatment** embed microscopic "nano-rods" (called **precipitates**, e.g., Al₂Cu, Mg₂Si) inside the soft aluminium matrix. These precipitates act as obstacles to dislocation motion, transforming soft aluminium into an **aerospace-grade structural material** capable of withstanding enormous service stresses.
>
> The **self-healing oxide film (Al₂O₃)** is analogous to a "permanent paint" — if scratched, it instantly reforms in the presence of oxygen, protecting the metal underneath indefinitely. This is why aluminium window frames, aircraft skins, and overhead cables last decades without rusting.

### 1.4 Key Physical & Mechanical Constants of Pure Aluminium

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Crystal Structure | **FCC (A1)** | 12 slip systems → high ductility |
| Lattice Constant, *a* | **4.05 Å (0.4049 nm)** | Basis for X-ray identification |
| Density, *ρ* | **2.70 g/cm³** | ~1/3 that of steel (7.87 g/cm³) |
| Melting Point | **660.3 °C** | Suitable for low-T casting |
| Young's Modulus, *E* | **69 GPa** | Lower than steel (200 GPa) |
| Poisson's Ratio, *ν* | **0.33** | Elastic constant |
| Thermal Conductivity | **237 W/m·K** | Excellent heat sink material |
| Electrical Resistivity | **2.65 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** | 61% IACS |
| Thermal Expansion Coeff. | **23.1 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** | Twice that of steel |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crystal Structure Justification (FCC)

Aluminium crystallises in the **FCC arrangement** because each atom contributes **3 valence electrons** (3s² 3p¹), and the free-electron "sea" plus close-packing tendencies (driven by the **Hume-Rothery rules**) favour the maximum packing efficiency of **0.74** offered by FCC. This gives:

- **Coordination Number (CN) = 12**
- **Atoms per unit cell = 4**
- **Packing Factor (APF) = 0.74**

### 2.2 The Hume-Rothery Conditions for Al-Alloy Solubility

For an alloying element X to be **highly soluble** in solid aluminium, the following rules must be satisfied:

1. **Atomic size difference** ≤ 15%
2. **Same crystal structure** as Al (FCC preferred)
3. **Similar electronegativity**
4. **Same valence** (or higher valence preferred)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Mg (size diff ~10.7%, HCP structure) → moderate solubility (forms 5xxx series). Cu (size diff ~10.6%, FCC) → moderate solubility (forms 2xxx series Duralumin).

### 2.3 Strengthening Mechanisms Operating in Al-Alloys

Aluminium alloys derive their strength from **four concurrent mechanisms**:

| Mechanism | Governing Equation | Magnitude of Strengthening |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Solution Strengthening** | $\Delta \sigma_{ss} = k \cdot X^{n}$ | Modest (≈ 10–50 MPa) |
| **Strain (Work) Hardening** | $\sigma = \sigma_0 + K \cdot \varepsilon^{n}$ | High (up to ~150 MPa) |
| **Grain Boundary Strengthening (Hall-Petch)** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Moderate (≈ 20–60 MPa) |
| **Precipitation (Age) Hardening** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \beta \cdot f^{1/2} \cdot r^{-1}$ | **Highest** (≈ 100–500 MPa) |

Where:
- $d$ = average grain diameter
- $f$ = volume fraction of precipitates
- $r$ = average precipitate radius
- $X$ = atomic fraction of solute
- $k, K, k_y, \beta$ = material constants

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot M}{a^3 \cdot N_A}$ | Theoretical density from FCC lattice (n = 4) | g/cm³ |
| 2 | APF = $\dfrac{4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{a^3} = 0.74$ | Atomic packing factor of FCC | dimensionless |
| 3 | $r_{atom} = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$ | Atomic radius (FCC) | nm |
| 4 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch relation | MPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \beta \cdot f^{1/2} \cdot r^{-1}$ | Ashby-Orowan precipitation strengthening | MPa |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{sol} = T_{sol} - T_{room}$ | Solubility differential (driving force for aging) | K or °C |
| 7 | $R_{p} = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{T_{aging}}$ | Coarsening resistance | J/K |
| 8 | $\text{SFG} = \dfrac{UTS}{\rho}$ | Specific strength (figure of merit) | kN·m/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Strength** is the single most important design metric for aerospace aluminium. For **7075-T6**: SFG ≈ 196 kN·m/kg, which exceeds mild steel (~63 kN·m/kg). This is why Al-alloys dominate aircraft skins and structural members.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** 7075 (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu) used in Boeing 777 wing spars
- **Automotive:** 6061-T6 used in EV battery enclosures
- **Marine:** 5083 (Al-Mg) used in ship hulls (excellent weldability)
- **Civil:** AA1100 in roofing, AA6063 in architectural extrusions
- **Electrical:** AA1350 in overhead transmission cables (cheaper per kg per km than Cu for equal conductance)
- **Packaging:** AA5182 in beverage can ends; AA3004 in can bodies

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Density of FCC Aluminium from Crystallography

**Problem:** Calculate the theoretical density of FCC aluminium given the atomic radius is 0.143 nm.

**Step 1 — Identify FCC parameters**

For an FCC unit cell, the relationship between atomic radius $r$ and lattice parameter $a$ is:

$$
a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot r
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical value**

$$
a = 2\sqrt{2} \times 0.143 \text{ nm} = 2 \times 1.4142 \times 0.143
$$

$$
a = 0.4046 \text{ nm} = 4.046 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the density formula**

The general density formula is:

$$
\rho = \frac{n \cdot M}{a^3 \cdot N_A}
$$

Where:
- $n = 4$ atoms per FCC unit cell
- $M = 26.98$ g/mol (atomic mass of Al)
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol (Avogadro's number)
- $a^3$ is the unit cell volume

**Step 4 — Compute unit cell volume**

$$
a^3 = (4.046 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm})^3
$$

$$
a^3 = 6.625 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into density equation**

$$
\rho = \frac{4 \times 26.98}{(6.625 \times 10^{-23}) \times (6.022 \times 10^{23})}
$$

$$
\rho = \frac{107.92}{(6.625 \times 6.022) \times 10^0} = \frac{107.92}{39.896}
$$

**Step 6 — Final result**

$$
\boxed{\rho_{Al} = 2.706 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

This matches the experimentally measured density (**2.70 g/cm³**) with an error of < **0.2%**, validating the FCC model.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of FCC Aluminium

**Step 1 — Volume of one atom**

$$
V_{atom} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3
$$

**Step 2 — Number of atoms per FCC unit cell**

$$
n = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} \text{ (corners)} + 6 \times \frac{1}{2} \text{ (faces)} = 1 + 3 = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Total volume of atoms in the cell**

$$
V_{atoms} = 4 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{16}{3} \pi r^3
$$

**Step 4 — Volume of the unit cell**

Using $a = \dfrac{4r}{\sqrt{2}}$ → $a^3 = \dfrac{64 r^3}{2\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{32 r^3}{\sqrt{2}}$

**Step 5 — APF formula**

$$
\text{APF} = \frac{V_{atoms}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{\frac{16}{3} \pi r^3}{\frac{32 r^3}{\sqrt{2}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify**

$$
\text{APF} = \frac{16 \pi r^3 \cdot \sqrt{2}}{3 \cdot 32 \cdot r^3} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{6}
$$

**Step 7 — Final numerical value**

$$
\boxed{\text{APF}_{FCC} = 0.7405 \text{ or } 74.05\%}
$$

This means **74.05%** of the aluminium unit cell is occupied by atoms and **25.95%** is interstitial void space — the theoretical maximum for any close-packed structure.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hall-Petch Strengthening in an Al-Mg Alloy (5xxx series)

**Given:** AA5052 (Al-2.5%Mg) with average grain diameter $d = 25 \text{ μm}$. Constants: $\sigma_0 = 45$ MPa, $k_y = 0.13$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$.

**Step 1 — Convert grain size to metres**

$$
d = 25 \text{ μm} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hall-Petch equation**

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 45 + (0.13) \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute inverse square root**

$$
(25 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \left(\frac{1}{25 \times 10^{-6}}\right)^{1/2} = (4 \times 10^{4})^{1/2} = 200 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation**

$$
\sigma_y = 45 + 0.13 \times 200 = 45 + 26
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_y = 71 \text{ MPa}}
$$

If the alloy is **fine-grained** ($d = 5$ μm) by thermomechanical processing:

$$
\sigma_y = 45 + 0.13 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = 45 + 0.13 \times 447.2 = 45 + 58.1 = 103.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Reducing grain size from 25 μm → 5 μm increases yield strength by **~45%** in this alloy. This is why thermo-mechanical processing (TMP) such as ECAP (Equal-Channel Angular Pressing) is critical for aerospace-grade Al-alloys.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Properties of Major Al-Alloy Series

| Alloy Series | Principal Alloying Element | UTS (MPa, typical) | Density (g/cm³) | Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) | Primary Use | Heat Treatable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** (e.g., 1100) | > 99% Al | 90 | 2.71 | 33 | Chemical tanks, busbars | **No** |
| **2xxx** (e.g., 2024) | Cu (~4%) | 470 | 2.78 | 169 | Aircraft structures | **Yes** (T3, T6) |
| **3xxx** (e.g., 3003) | Mn (~1.2%) | 150 | 2.73 | 55 | Cookware, roofing | **No** |
| **4xxx** (e.g., 4043) | Si (~5%) | 180 | 2.69 | 67 | Welding filler, pistons | **Limited** |
| **5xxx** (e.g., 5083) | Mg (~4.5%) | 290 | 2.66 | 109 | Marine hulls, cryogenics | **No** (work-hardened) |
| **6xxx** (e.g., 6061) | Mg + Si | 310 | 2.70 | 115 | Extrusions, automotive | **Yes** (T6) |
| **7xxx** (e.g., 7075) | Zn + Mg + Cu | 570 | 2.81 | 203 | Aircraft, defence | **Yes** (T6, T73) |
| **8xxx** (e.g., 8090) | Li (~2.5%) | 500 | 2.55 | 196 | Aerospace (Al-Li) | **Yes** (T8) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.2 Process Flow: Precipitation Hardening (Age Hardening) of Al-Alloys

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Step 1 Solution Treatment] --> S2[Step 2 Quenching in Water]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Natural Aging or Artificial Aging]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Supersaturated Solid Solution SSS]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Formation of GP Zones]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Formation of Theta Prime Precipitates]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Peak Hardened State T6]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Over-aging Loss of Strength]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Equilibrium Phase e.g. Al2Cu]
    S1 -.-> T1[Temperature 530 C for 2024]
    S2 -.-> T2[Quench rate greater than 100 C per second]
    S3 -.-> T3[Natural: 25 C 96 hours Artificial: 175 C 8 hours]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Strengthening Mechanism Map for Al-Alloys

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Aluminium Matrix Soft 90 MPa] --> M1[Alloying Elements Cu Mg Si Zn]
    M1 --> M2[Solid Solution Strengthening plus 10 to 50 MPa]
    M2 --> M3[Plastic Deformation Work Hardening]
    M2 --> M4[Heat Treatment Precipitation Hardening]
    M3 --> M5[Dislocation Pile-ups plus 100 to 200 MPa]
    M4 --> M6[GP Zones and Theta Prime Precipitates]
    M6 --> M7[Dislocation Pinning plus 200 to 500 MPa]
    M5 --> F[Final Strength 300 to 600 MPa]
    M7 --> F
    F --> APP[Aerospace Automotive Marine Structural]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Duralumin Microstructure (Al-Cu System)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Duralumin Microstructure
        A1[Aluminum Matrix alpha phase FCC] --> A2[Al2Cu theta Precipitates Nano Rods]
        A1 --> A3[GP Zones Guinier Preston Zones Disc Shaped]
        A1 --> A4[Dislocation Lines Blocked]
        A2 --> A5[Orowan Looping around precipitates]
        A3 --> A5
        A4 --> A5
    end
    A5 --> A6[Yield Strength increased to 470 MPa]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture** has been used in place of physical crystallographic sketches, in accordance with Mermaid compiler safety guidelines. Each block represents a microstructural feature whose interactions govern the macroscopic yield strength.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define **Duralumin**. State its nominal composition and two important applications.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Duralumin** is a heat-treatable aluminium-copper alloy developed by Alfred Wilm in **1906**. Its nominal composition is:
> - **Cu: 3.5 – 4.5%**
> - **Mg: 0.4 – 0.8%**
> - **Mn: 0.4 – 0.8%**
> - **Si: ≤ 0.7%**
> - **Fe: ≤ 0.7%**
> - **Al: balance**
>
> It belongs to the **2xxx (Al-Cu)** series under the AA designation (most common: **2024-T3**).
>
> **Two key applications:**
> 1. **Aircraft structures** — wing skins, fuselage frames, rivets (high strength-to-weight ratio)
> 2. **Automotive & transportation** — truck wheels, body panels requiring high fatigue strength
>
> In the **T3 temper** (solution treated, cold worked, naturally aged), 2024-Duralumin exhibits a UTS of ~**470 MPa** and a density of **2.78 g/cm³**, giving a specific strength of ~**169 kN·m/kg**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

Differentiate between **heat-treatable** and **non-heat-treatable** aluminium alloys with two examples each.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Heat-Treatable Alloys | Non-Heat-Treatable Alloys |
|---|---|---|
| Strengthening mechanism | **Precipitation hardening** (formation of second-phase particles) | **Strain hardening** (cold work) and solid solution |
| Solubility condition | Solute solubility **decreases** with temperature | Solute solubility **constant** or very limited |
| Common series | 2xxx (Al-Cu), 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si), 7xxx (Al-Zn) | 1xxx, 3xxx (Al-Mn), 5xxx (Al-Mg) |
| Example 1 | **2024** (4% Cu) — aircraft | **5052** (2.5% Mg) — marine |
| Example 2 | **7075** (Zn-Mg-Cu) — aerospace | **3003** (1.2% Mn) — cookware |
| Maximum UTS | Up to **570 MPa** (7075-T6) | Up to **290 MPa** (5083-H116) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid:** "**2, 6, 7** → **heat-treatable**"; "**1, 3, 5** → **not heat-treatable** (except 4xxx and 8xxx)"

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat schematics, explain the **age-hardening (precipitation hardening)** process in aluminium-copper alloys (Duralumin). Discuss the **sequence of precipitate formation** and the corresponding changes in hardness.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **6xxx (Al-Mg-Si)** and **7xxx (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu)** alloy systems with respect to composition, mechanism of strengthening, typical mechanical properties, and applications.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The **age-hardening process** for Al-Cu alloys (e.g., 2024 with 4% Cu) involves **three sequential stages**:

**Stage 1: Solution Treatment**
- Heat the alloy to a temperature just **below the eutectic temperature** (~**530 °C** for Al-4%Cu), within the **single-phase α region** of the Al-Cu phase diagram.
- Hold for sufficient time (typically **1–2 hours**) to dissolve all Cu into a **homogeneous single-phase solid solution**.

**Stage 2: Quenching**
- Rapidly cool the alloy (water quench at > 100 °C/s) to room temperature.
- This produces a **supersaturated solid solution (α-SSS)** of Cu in Al at room temperature, which is **metastable**.

**Stage 3: Aging (Natural or Artificial)**
- **Natural aging:** Hold at **room temperature** for several days → formation of **GP (Guinier-Preston) Zones**, giving a modest strength increase.
- **Artificial aging:** Re-heat to **150–200 °C** for 4–16 hours → progression through:
  1. **GP-I Zones** (Cu atom clusters, ~1–2 nm discs on {100} planes)
  2. **GP-II Zones / θ″ phase** (coherent ordered platelets)
  3. **θ′ phase** (semi-coherent, ~10 nm thick) ← **peak hardness (T6 temper)**
  4. **θ phase (CuAl₂)** (incoherent equilibrium) ← **over-aged, soft state**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness evolution curve:** The hardness rises gradually during aging, reaches a **maximum at the T6 condition**, and then **decreases** as the precipitates coarsen (Ostwald ripening). The over-aged condition corresponds to the **T7/T73 temper**, used in stress-corrosion-resistant applications.

**Mechanism of strengthening:** The coherent/semi-coherent precipitates obstruct dislocation motion, requiring dislocations to either **cut through** the precipitates (during under-aging) or **bypass them via Orowan looping** (during over-aging). Maximum strength occurs at the **transition between these two mechanisms**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

| Parameter | 6xxx Series (Al-Mg-Si) | 7xxx Series (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu) |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Alloying Elements** | Mg (0.4–1.2%) + Si (0.4–1.2%) → forms **Mg₂Si** | Zn (5–8%) + Mg (2–3%) + Cu (1–2%) |
| **Strengthening Phase** | **β″ (Mg₂Si)** — coherent needle-shaped | **η′ (MgZn₂)** — semi-coherent plate |
| **Heat Treatment** | T6 (530 °C solution + 175 °C age) | T6 (480 °C solution + 120 °C age) |
| **Typical UTS** | 310 MPa (6061-T6) | **570 MPa** (7075-T6) |
| **Density** | 2.70 g/cm³ | 2.81 g/cm³ |
| **Weldability** | **Excellent** (TIG, MIG) | **Poor** (susceptible to HAZ cracking) |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Good | **Moderate** (T73 temper improves SCC resistance) |
| **Primary Applications** | Architectural extrusions, automotive panels, bicycle frames, pipelines | Aircraft wing spars, fuselage bulkheads, military bridges, rocket motor casings |

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight:** The 7xxx alloys (e.g., **7075-T6**) are the **strongest commercially available aluminium alloys**, rivaling many steels in strength while being **3× lighter**. However, they are **not weldable** by conventional methods and require **bolting or riveting** — limiting them to aerospace and defence rather than mass-market automotive.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **AA (Aluminum Association) designation system** for wrought and cast aluminium alloys. With a suitable example, illustrate the meaning of the **temper designation T6**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of alloying elements** (Cu, Mg, Si, Mn, Zn) in aluminium alloys. State **Hume-Rothery's rules** of solid solubility with reference to the Al-Mg system.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Wrought Alloy Designation (4-digit system, e.g., 2024, 6061, 7075):**

- **First digit (XXXX) — Principal alloying element:**

| Series | Principal Element |
|---|---|
| 1xxx | ≥ 99.00% pure Al |
| 2xxx | Copper (Cu) |
| 3xxx | Manganese (Mn) |
| 4xxx | Silicon (Si) |
| 5xxx | Magnesium (Mg) |
| 6xxx | Magnesium + Silicon (Mg₂Si) |
| 7xxx | Zinc (Zn) |
| 8xxx | Other (Li, Sn, Sc) |

- **Second digit:** Modifications of original alloy (0 = original, 1 = first modification).
- **Last two digits:** Specific alloy identification within the series.

**Example: 2024**
- **2** → Al-Cu series
- **0** → original alloy
- **24** → specific 24th alloy registered in 2xxx series

**Cast Alloy Designation (4-digit system with decimal, e.g., 356.0, A356.0):**

- **3xx.x** — Al-Si based (with Cu or Mg) — most common cast alloys
- **A356.0** — "A" indicates a higher purity version (modified composition)

**Temper Designation — T6 Explained:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **T6 temper** is the most widely specified heat-treated condition for aerospace and structural aluminium alloys. It signifies:
> 1. **Solution treatment** at ~530 °C (alloying elements dissolve into α-Al matrix)
> 2. **Quenching** in water to form a supersaturated solid solution
> 3. **Artificial aging** at an intermediate temperature (~150–200 °C) to precipitate fine, coherent strengthening phases
>
> **Result:** Optimum combination of **strength, hardness, and dimensional stability.**
>
> **Example:** **6061-T6** is used in aerospace fittings, marine components, bicycle frames, and architectural extrusions with a typical **UTS = 310 MPa, YS = 276 MPa, Elongation = 12%**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Role of Key Alloying Elements:**

| Element | Atomic % Typical | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Cu (2xxx)** | 1.5 – 6% | Forms **θ (CuAl₂)** and **θ′** precipitates → major strengthener. Reduces corrosion resistance. |
| **Mg (5xxx)** | 0.5 – 5% | Solid-solution strengthener; forms **β (Al₃Mg₂)**; excellent weldability and corrosion resistance. |
| **Si (4xxx, 6xxx)** | 0.5 – 12% | Forms **Mg₂Si** (in 6xxx); improves fluidity in casting (4xxx); reduces thermal expansion. |
| **Mn (3xxx)** | 0.5 – 1.5% | Forms **Al₆Mn** dispersoids → refines grain structure, improves strength and formability. |
| **Zn (7xxx)** | 3 – 8% | Forms **η (MgZn₂)** and **η′** precipitates → **highest strength** among Al-alloys. |
| **Fe, Si (impurities)** | < 1% | Form **Al₃Fe, AlFeSi** intermetallics → **embrittle** the alloy; must be controlled. |

**Hume-Rothery's Rules (Applied to the Al-Mg System):**

1. **Atomic size difference ≤ 15%:** $r_{Mg} = 0.160$ nm, $r_{Al} = 0.143$ nm.
   Difference = (0.160 − 0.143)/0.143 × 100 ≈ **11.9% ≤ 15%** ✓
2. **Same crystal structure (FCC preferred):** Al is FCC, Mg is **HCP** → *partial* violation, hence **limited** solubility.
3. **Similar electronegativity:** Al (1.61) ≈ Mg (1.31) — comparable.
4. **Higher valence preferred:** Mg is **divalent**, Al is **trivalent** → Mg is soluble in Al.

**Result:** The **maximum solid solubility of Mg in Al is 17.4% at 450 °C (eutectic temperature)**, which **decreases sharply to ~1.9% at room temperature**. This solubility differential is the **driving force** for precipitation hardening in Al-Mg alloys.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost 1–2 marks each in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "T" in T6** — Writing only "6" or "tempered at 6" is **incorrect**. Always write "**T6**" (a single alphanumeric code).
> 2. **Confusing Wrought vs Cast series** — Wrought uses **XXXX** (4 digits, no decimal); Cast uses **XXX.X** (4 digits with decimal). Mixing them = full marks deduction.
> 3. **Omitting stages in age-hardening** — A common error: students describe the **quench** but forget to mention the **supersaturated solid solution (SSS)** formation. KTU examiners expect **all three stages explicitly**: solution → quench → age.
> 4. **Forgetting yield-strength calculation steps** — In a numerical problem, students often write the **Hall-Petch formula** but skip the **unit conversion** of grain diameter (μm → m). This leads to answers off by a factor of **1000**.
> 5. **Missing GP Zones** — When asked about precipitation sequence in Al-Cu, students jump from **supersaturated solution → θ′** without mentioning the **GP-I and GP-II zones** (coherent clusters) — losing 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Aluminium & Its Alloys:**

- ⬛ Aluminium is **FCC**, density **2.70 g/cm³**, melting point **660 °C**, atomic number **13**.
- ⬛ The **oxide film (Al₂O₃)** is **self-healing** → inherent corrosion resistance.
- ⬛ **AA Designation** → first digit = principal alloying element; 4-digit codes for wrought, decimal for cast.
- ⬛ **2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx** are **heat-treatable**; **1xxx, 3xxx, 5xxx** are **non-heat-treatable** (work-hardenable).
- ⬛ **T6 temper** = Solution treatment + Quench + **Artificial aging** → peak strength.
- ⬛ **Duralumin (2024)** = Al-4%Cu-0.5%Mg → aircraft skin, **UTS ≈ 470 MPa**.
- ⬛ **7075-T6** is the **strongest commercial Al-alloy** (UTS ≈ 570 MPa), used in **aerospace**.
- ⬛ **Al-Mg (5xxx)** is best for **marine** (5083 hulls); **Al-Mg-Si (6xxx)** is best for **extrusions** (6061-T6).
- ⬛ Precipitation sequence in Al-Cu: **α-SSS → GP-I → GP-II (θ″) → θ′ → θ (CuAl₂)**.
- ⬛ **Hall-Petch equation** controls grain-boundary strengthening: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$.
- ⬛ **Hume-Rothery rules** govern solid solubility; all four are **necessary but not individually sufficient**.
- ⬛ **Specific strength (UTS/ρ)** is the design driver for Al-alloys in aerospace: 7075-T6 ≈ **203 kN·m/kg**.
- ⬛ Al-Li alloys (8xxx) are the **latest generation** — **9% density reduction** + **10% stiffness gain** per 1% Li.
- ⬛ **Heat-treatable Al-alloys derive >70% of their strength from precipitation hardening**, not solid solution strengthening.
- ⬛ Always cite **3-digit alloy number + temper** (e.g., **2024-T3, 6061-T6, 7075-T73**) in KTU answers for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Ferrous Metals and Alloys used for High Temperature Services and Nuclear Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Ferrous Metals and Alloys used for High Temperature Services and Nuclear Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What Are High-Temperature Non-Ferrous Alloys?

A **High-Temperature Alloy (Superalloy)** is a metallic alloy engineered to retain its mechanical integrity, surface stability, and creep/oxidation resistance at temperatures above **540 °C (1000 °F)**, often operating reliably up to **1200 °C** or higher. The term *superalloy* is reserved almost exclusively for **nickel-, cobalt-, and iron-nickel-base** systems, while refractory metals (Nb, Mo, W) and reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Be) form a separate but related family of high-performance materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT412 Module 3):**
> Non-ferrous metals and alloys for high-temperature service and nuclear application are specialized materials selected based on **melting point > 1500 °C**, **low thermal neutron absorption cross-section**, **excellent creep-rupture strength**, and **radiation damage tolerance**.

## 1.2 What Are Nuclear-Application Alloys?

Nuclear-grade alloys are those qualified to operate inside a **nuclear reactor core** under simultaneous exposure to:
- High temperatures (250 °C – 1000 °C depending on reactor type)
- Intense neutron flux (10¹³ – 10¹⁴ n/cm²·s)
- Corrosive coolants (water, liquid Na, helium, CO₂)
- High stress and irradiation-induced embrittlement

**Core Selection Criteria for Nuclear Materials:**

| Property | Required Characteristic | Engineering Reason |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Thermal Neutron Capture Cross-Section (σₐ) | **Low (barns)** | To avoid parasitic neutron absorption |
| Radiation Stability | **High** | Resist displacement damage |
| Corrosion Resistance | **Excellent** | Survive coolant chemistry |
| Thermal Conductivity | **High** | Efficient heat removal |
| Mechanical Strength | **Sustained under irradiation** | Hold fuel rods and structural loads |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engineered Survivor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of a high-temperature alloy like the **heat shield of a space capsule re-entering Earth's atmosphere**. Just as the shield is built from layered, specialized materials to survive 1600 °C plasma without melting or deforming, superalloys and nuclear alloys are metallurgically engineered to "survive" extreme service environments.
> - **Superalloys** = the "firefighter's suit" — protects against heat, creep, and oxidation in jet engines.
> - **Nuclear alloys (Zircaloy)** = the "invisible body armor" — blocks neutrons harmlessly while containing radioactive fuel.
> - **Refractory metals (W, Mo, Nb)** = the "volcanic rock" — refuse to melt even when the surroundings become a furnace.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

The gas turbine inlet temperature of modern jet engines has risen from **~900 °C (1940s)** to **~1600 °C (modern F-class turbines)**. Without superalloys, this would be physically impossible. Similarly, the global fleet of **440+ commercial nuclear reactors** depends entirely on a handful of qualified alloys (Zircaloy-4, Inconel-600, 304/316 SS) for safe operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants to Remember:**
> - 1 barn = **10⁻²⁴ cm²** (unit of neutron cross-section)
> - Creep activation energy for Ni-superalloys ≈ **280 – 320 kJ/mol**
> - Melting point of Tungsten = **3422 °C** (highest of all metals)
> - Melting point of Zirconium = **1855 °C**
> - Inconel-718 service ceiling ≈ **650 °C** continuous, **980 °C** short-term
> - Haynes-230 max service temperature ≈ **1150 °C**
> - Stellite-6B hot hardness ≈ **HRC 40** at 800 °C
- **Engineering Reality Check:** The cost-to-performance ratio of these alloys is extraordinary. A single **turbine blade** in a GE90 engine costs ~**$15,000 – $25,000** and contains ~**40 g of rhenium**, one of Earth's rarest elements.
- **Real-world metric:** Zirconium has a thermal neutron absorption cross-section of just **0.18 barns**, compared to **2.6 barns** for iron — making it ~14× more transparent to neutrons.

## 1.5 Why "Non-Ferrous"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> Iron-based alloys are excluded from this module because:
> 1. They undergo **excessive oxidation** above 700 °C (formation of porous FeO/Fe₃O₄ scale).
> 2. They suffer **irradiation swelling** in fast-neutron spectra.
> 3. They exhibit **ferromagnetic transition** near Curie temperature (~770 °C), causing dimensional instability.
> 4. They have **high neutron absorption**, unsuitable as fuel cladding in thermal reactors.

Non-ferrous high-temperature systems overcome these limitations through the formation of **protective oxide films (Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, SiO₂)**, **solid-solution strengthening**, and **ordered precipitate strengthening (γ′ and γ″ phases)**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Non-Ferrous High-Temperature Alloys

```
HIGH-TEMPERATURE NON-FERROUS ALLOYS
│
├── 2.1.1 NICKEL-BASED SUPERALLOYS
│   ├── Solid-solution strengthened → Inconel-600, Inconel-625, Hastelloy-X
│   ├── Precipitation (γ′) strengthened → Nimonic-80A, Nimonic-90, Waspaloy
│   └── γ′ + γ″ strengthened → Inconel-718, René-95, Udimet-700
│
├── 2.1.2 COBALT-BASED SUPERALLOYS
│   ├── Stellite series (Stellite-6, Stellite-6B, Stellite-21)
│   └── Haynes-25 (L-605), Haynes-188
│
├── 2.1.3 IRON-NICKEL BASED
│   ├── Incoloy-800, Incoloy-825
│   └── N-155, A-286
│
├── 2.1.4 TITANIUM & ITS ALLOYS
│   ├── α-alloys → Ti-5Al-2.5Sn
│   ├── α+β alloys → Ti-6Al-4V (most common, 50% of all Ti usage)
│   └── β-alloys → Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al
│
├── 2.1.5 ZIRCONIUM ALLOYS (Nuclear)
│   ├── Zircaloy-2 (Sn 1.5%, Fe 0.12%, Cr 0.10%, Ni 0.05%)
│   ├── Zircaloy-4 (Sn 1.5%, Fe 0.20%, Cr 0.10%) — *most used for BWR/PWR*
│   └── Zr-2.5Nb (for pressure tubes in PHWR)
│
├── 2.1.6 BERYLLIUM
│   └── Used as neutron reflector and moderator in weapons/research reactors
│
├── 2.1.7 NIOBIUM (Columbium) ALLOYS
│   ├── Nb-1Zr (reactor grade)
│   └── C-103 (Nb-10Hf-1Ti) — rocket thrusters
│
├── 2.1.8 MOLYBDENUM ALLOYS
│   ├── TZM (Mo-0.5Ti-0.1Zr-0.02C)
│   └── Mo-30W
│
└── 2.1.9 TUNGSTEN ALLOYS
    ├── Pure W (lamp filaments, X-ray targets)
    └── W-Re (rhenium-doped for ductility) — thermocouples
```

## 2.2 Strengthening Mechanisms in Superalloys

### 2.2.1 Solid-Solution Strengthening
- Achieved by adding **Co, Cr, Mo, W, V, Ti** to the FCC nickel matrix.
- These solute atoms distort the lattice, impeding dislocation motion.
- Effectiveness increases with **size misfit** and **modulus misfit**.
- Solute-drag effect quantified by the **Labusch model**:

$$
F_{max} = K \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \varepsilon^{4/3} \cdot c^{2/3}
$$

where $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $\varepsilon$ = misfit parameter, $c$ = solute concentration.

### 2.2.2 Precipitation (γ′) Strengthening
- The γ′ phase is **Ni₃(Al, Ti)** with ordered L1₂ (FCC-ordered) structure.
- Coherency with γ matrix produces **low interfacial energy** → resistance to coarsening.
- Volume fraction of γ′ can reach **60–70%** in advanced single-crystal superalloys (e.g., CMSX-10).
- Strengthening increment (Roebuck–Cox law):

$$
\Delta \tau = A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{f \cdot r}{L}}
$$

where $f$ = volume fraction of γ′, $r$ = precipitate radius, $L$ = mean particle spacing, $A$ = constant.

### 2.2.3 Carbide Strengthening
- **MC, M₂₃C₆, M₆C** carbides precipitate at grain boundaries.
- They block grain-boundary sliding during creep.
- Excessive carbide formation (e.g., sigma, mu phases) is **detrimental** → causes embrittlement.

### 2.2.4 Oxide Dispersion Strengthening (ODS)
- **Y₂O₃ (yttria)** dispersed in ferritic steel or Ni-matrix.
- Dispersoids are **thermally stable up to 0.9 × Tₘ**.
- Example: **MA-956** (Fe-20Cr-4.5Al-0.5Y₂O₃).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Equations for KTU 2024 Board Examination — Master These!**

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Steady-State Creep (Norton-Bailey Power Law)** | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$ in s⁻¹, $\sigma$ in MPa, $Q_c$ in J/mol, $T$ in K | High-T creep design |
| 2 | **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)** | $P_{LM} = T \cdot (C + \log t_r) \times 10^{-3}$ | $T$ in Kelvin, $t_r$ in hours, $C \approx 20$ for steels/superalloys | Predicts rupture life |
| 3 | **Sherby-Dorn Parameter** | $P_{SD} = \log t_r - \dfrac{Q_c}{2.303\,RT}$ | Used for long-term extrapolation | Creep data correlation |
| 4 | **Manjoine Cutoff Stress (Creep-Fatigue)** | $\sigma_{cut} = B \cdot E(T) / 100$ | $B \approx 0.3$ for ductile metals | ASME Section III design |
| 5 | **Thermal Neutron Cross-Section Rule of Mixtures** | $\bar{\sigma}_a = \sum_i f_i \cdot \sigma_{a,i}$ | $f_i$ = atomic fraction, $\sigma_{a,i}$ in barns | Alloy selection for reactor |
| 6 | **Irradiation-Induced Swelling (Averin)** | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = R \cdot (\phi t)^{n}$ | $R$ = swelling rate, $\phi t$ = fluence (n/cm²) | Fast reactor cladding |
| 7 | **Thermal Stress (for reactor cladding)** | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$ | $\alpha$ = thermal expansion, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | Pellet-cladding interaction |
| 8 | **Oxygen Partial Pressure for Cr₂O₃ Stability** | $\log p_{O_2} = -\dfrac{53540}{T} + 17.5$ | For pure Cr at 1 atm | Oxidation prediction |
| 9 | **Grain-Boundary Sliding Contribution to Creep** | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{gbs} = A_{gbs} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma^2}{T \cdot d^3} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_{gbs}}{RT}\right)$ | $d$ = grain size | Fine- vs coarse-grain choice |
| 10 | **Coble Creep (Diffusion-controlled)** | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{coble} = \dfrac{42\,\Omega\,\delta\,D_{gb}\,\sigma}{k\,T\,d^3}$ | $\Omega$ = atomic volume, $\delta D_{gb}$ = GB diffusivity | Low-T creep of nanoalloys |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aero-engine HP turbine blades:** Single-crystal Ni-superalloy (PWA 1480, CMSX-4) operates at **~1100 °C** under **~150 MPa** centrifugal stress. Designed life = **25,000 flight cycles**.
- **Nuclear fuel cladding (PWR):** Zircaloy-4 tubes (Ø 9.5 mm × 0.57 mm wall) operate at **~350 °C** surface temp, **~15 MPa** coolant pressure, **~10¹⁴ n/cm²·s** flux. Life = **4 years** (3 batches per cycle).
- **Rocket nozzle throats:** Tungsten or C-103 niobium alloy survive **~3000 °C** gas temperatures during **~30-second** burn.
- **Thermocouples in jet engines:** Type K (Ni-Cr / Ni-Al), Type R (Pt-Rh), and **W-Re thermocouples** can read up to **2300 °C**.

## 2.6 Why Each Element Is Added — The "Designer Alloy" Logic

- **Cr (10–30%):** Forms **Cr₂O₃** protective scale; also enters M₂₃C₆ carbides.
- **Al (1–6%):** Forms **Al₂O₃** scale (more stable than Cr₂O₃ above 1000 °C); also a γ′ former.
- **Ti (1–6%):** γ′ former (Ni₃(Al,Ti)); raises γ′ solvus temperature.
- **Mo / W (3–12%):** Solid-solution strengtheners; raise Tₘ, suppress recrystallization.
- **Co (5–20%):** Lowers stacking fault energy → reduces creep rate; stabilizes γ′.
- **Nb (1–5%):** Forms **γ″ (Ni₃Nb)** in Inconel-718 — powerful low-temperature strengthener.
- **Hf / Y / La (trace):** Improve oxide scale adhesion, prevent spallation.
- **B / Zr (ppm levels):** Segregate to grain boundaries, increase cohesion, suppress cavitation.
- **Re (1–6%):** Reduces γ-γ′ lattice misfit; creates "Re clusters" that slow dislocation climb (in 2nd/3rd gen single-crystal alloys).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this derivation important for KTU 2024?**
> The LMP is the most asked equation in high-temperature material questions. Examiners frequently give a numerical problem asking you to calculate the rupture life using LMP.

### 3.1.1 Starting from the Hollomon–Larson Relationship

The empirical observation for stress-rupture data is that rupture time $t_r$ at a given stress $\sigma$ follows an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence:

$$
t_r = A' \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)
$$

In the above expression:
- $t_r$ = time to rupture (in hours)
- $A'$ = a function of stress (constant for a given $\sigma$)
- $Q_c$ = activation energy for creep (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 3.1.2 Taking Logarithm of Both Sides

We now apply $\log_{10}$ to both sides:

$$
\log_{10} t_r = \log_{10} A' + \frac{Q_c}{2.303 \, R T}
$$

Re-arranging so that the stress-dependent term is isolated:

$$
\log_{10} t_r = \log_{10} A' + \frac{Q_c}{2.303 \, R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}
$$

### 3.1.3 Multiply Both Sides by $T$

Larson and Miller (1952) observed empirically that for many heat-resistant alloys, the product $T \cdot (\log_{10} A' + \frac{Q_c}{2.303 R})$ is approximately a **constant independent of stress**, when the constant $\log_{10} A' \approx -C$ is set to a typical value $C \approx 20$ for steels and superalloys:

$$
T \cdot \left( \log_{10} t_r - \log_{10} A' \right) = \frac{Q_c}{2.303 R}
$$

In the next step, we substitute the empirical constant and the parameter definition:

$$
P_{LM} = T \cdot (C + \log_{10} t_r) = \text{function of stress } \sigma
$$

$$
P_{LM} = \frac{T \cdot (C + \log_{10} t_r)}{1000} \quad \text{(in kK·h units)}
$$

### 3.1.4 Final Larson-Miller Parameter

$$
P_{LM} = \frac{T}{1000} \cdot (C + \log_{10} t_r)
$$

where:
- $T$ is in Kelvin
- $t_r$ is in hours
- $C$ is the Larson-Miller constant (typically **20** for Ni-superalloys, **15** for aluminum, **22** for stainless steel)

### 3.1.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A nickel superalloy turbine blade is expected to operate at **T = 1050 K** with rupture strength **σ = 150 MPa**. Given the master LMP curve gives $P_{LM} = 26.5$ at $\sigma = 150$ MPa and the Larson-Miller constant $C = 20$, calculate the expected rupture life $t_r$ in hours.

**Step 1 — Write the LMP expression:**

$$
P_{LM} = \frac{T}{1000} \cdot (C + \log_{10} t_r)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

$$
26.5 = \frac{1050}{1000} \cdot (20 + \log_{10} t_r)
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the parenthesis:**

$$
26.5 = 1.050 \cdot (20 + \log_{10} t_r)
$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by 1.050:**

$$
\frac{26.5}{1.050} = 20 + \log_{10} t_r
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the quotient:**

$$
25.2381 = 20 + \log_{10} t_r
$$

**Step 6 — Subtract 20:**

$$
\log_{10} t_r = 25.2381 - 20 = 5.2381
$$

**Step 7 — Take antilog:**

$$
t_r = 10^{5.2381} = 1.731 \times 10^{5} \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to years (for engineering context):**

$$
t_r = \frac{1.731 \times 10^{5}}{8760} \approx 19.76 \text{ years}
$$

> **Answer:** The expected rupture life is **≈ 1.73 × 10⁵ hours (≈ 19.8 years)** at 150 MPa and 1050 K.

## 3.2 Derivation: Creep Strain Rate from Diffusion-Controlled Mechanism

### 3.2.1 Start with the Fundamental Creep Equation

For a dislocation climb-controlled (Norton) creep regime:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \cdot \sigma^n \cdot D_{eff}
$$

where $D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot \exp(-Q_c / RT)$ is the effective diffusivity.

### 3.2.2 Expressing $D_{eff}$ Explicitly

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \cdot \sigma^n \cdot D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)
$$

### 3.2.3 Logarithmic Form for Linearization

Taking $\ln$ of both sides:

$$
\ln \dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = \ln(A \cdot D_0) + n \ln \sigma - \frac{Q_c}{RT}
$$

This is the basis of the **Sherby-Dorn plot**: a plot of $\ln \dot{\varepsilon}_{ss}$ vs $1/T$ gives a straight line of slope $-Q_c/R$ from which the activation energy can be calculated.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Alloy Selection

```
SERVICE REQUIREMENT
       │
       ├── Continuous T < 600 °C + corrosion?
       │       └── Ti-6Al-4V (aerospace structural) or Inconel-625
       │
       ├── Continuous 600–900 °C + high stress?
       │       └── Inconel-718 (γ″-strengthened) or Waspaloy (γ′)
       │
       ├── Continuous 900–1100 °C + aggressive gas?
       │       └── Single-crystal superalloy (CMSX-4) or Haynes-230
       │
       ├── Continuous 1100–1500 °C + extreme creep?
       │       └── Niobium C-103 (inert atmosphere) or TZM
       │
       ├── T > 1500 °C + refractory need?
       │       └── Tungsten (W-Re alloy) or graphite-protected Mo
       │
       ├── Nuclear reactor cladding (water coolant, 300–350 °C)?
       │       └── Zircaloy-4 (PWR) or Zr-2.5Nb (PHWR)
       │
       ├── Liquid-metal cooled fast reactor (550–700 °C)?
       │       └── 316 SS or HT9 ferritic-martensitic
       │
       └── Wear + corrosion + heat (valves, dies)?
               └── Stellite-6 or Haynes-25
```

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Selecting Cladding Material by Cross-Section

> **Problem:** A nuclear engineer must select a cladding material for a thermal reactor. The candidate alloys are:
> - **Candidate A:** Zr-1%Nb with $\sigma_a = 0.20$ barns
> - **Candidate B:** Fe-18Cr-10Ni (304 SS) with $\sigma_a = 2.85$ barns
>
> Thermal neutron flux $\phi = 10^{13}$ n/cm²·s. Compute the neutron absorption probability and recommend.

**Step 1 — Use the relation between absorption rate and cross-section:**

Absorption probability $P$ is proportional to $\sigma_a$ at constant flux:

$$
P_A / P_B = \sigma_{a,A} / \sigma_{a,B}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
P_A / P_B = 0.20 / 2.85 = 0.0702
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The zirconium alloy absorbs only **~7%** as many neutrons as the stainless steel. In a thermal reactor where neutrons are scarce, this **14× difference** is decisive.

> **Answer:** **Candidate A (Zr-1%Nb / Zircaloy) is selected** because of its far lower parasitic absorption, leaving more neutrons available to sustain the chain reaction in the fuel.

## 3.5 Time-Temperature Equivalence (Master Curve) Construction

The **LMP master curve** is constructed as:

1. Conduct stress-rupture tests at **3–4 different temperatures** (e.g., 900, 1000, 1100, 1200 K).
2. At each $T$, plot $\log t_r$ vs $\sigma$ → multiple parallel lines.
3. Compute $P_{LM} = T \cdot (C + \log t_r)$ for each test point.
4. Plot $\sigma$ vs $P_{LM}$ → **single master curve** valid for all temperatures.

The master curve is then used to predict rupture life at **any new temperature** within the tested range.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

### Question 1: Define a "superalloy" and state three key properties required for high-temperature service.

**Model Answer (Board-marked style):**
A superalloy is a metallic alloy, generally based on Ni, Co, or Fe-Ni, that exhibits exceptional **mechanical strength**, **creep resistance**, and **surface stability** at temperatures above 540 °C (1000 °F), typically up to ~1100 °C.

**Three Key Properties (each worth 1 mark in valuation):**
1. **High creep-rupture strength** at elevated temperature.
2. **Resistance to oxidation and hot corrosion** via formation of protective oxide scales (Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃).
3. **Microstructural stability** against coarsening, phase transformation, and carbide embrittlement over long service durations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "heat-resistant" without specifying quantitative temperatures or specific protective oxide layers. Avoid vague statements; mention the **540 °C threshold** and the **γ′ (Ni₃Al) precipitate** explicitly.

### Question 2: Why is Zircaloy-4 preferred over stainless steel for fuel cladding in pressurized water reactors?

**Model Answer:**
1. **Low thermal neutron absorption cross-section** ($\sigma_a = 0.18$ barns for Zr) compared to **2.6 barns** for Fe → preserves the neutron economy needed to sustain the chain reaction.
2. **Adequate corrosion resistance** in high-purity, high-temperature water (formation of protective ZrO₂ layer).
3. **Good mechanical strength-to-density ratio** and **low capture cross-section** combine to give high neutron economy and structural reliability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Mention the **specific barn value**. Generic statements like "low absorption" earn only partial credit. The numerical cross-section is a direct **KTU 2024 marking scheme** requirement.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (Option 1): Comprehensive Treatment of Nickel-Based Superalloys

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the role of γ′ (Ni₃Al) precipitates in strengthening nickel-based superalloys. Discuss the influence of Al, Ti, and Nb on γ′ stability and high-temperature strength.

**Model Solution:**

**1. The γ′ Phase — Structure and Coherency:**

The γ′ (gamma-prime) phase is an intermetallic compound with the composition **Ni₃(Al, Ti, Nb)** and a crystal structure of the **ordered L1₂ type**, which is essentially a derivative of the FCC lattice where Ni atoms occupy face-centers and Al/Ti atoms occupy cube corners.

The γ′ phase remains **coherent** with the γ (Ni-rich FCC) matrix because both phases share the same crystal structure and the lattice misfit $\delta$ is very small (typically 0.1–0.5%):

$$
\delta = \frac{a_{\gamma'} - a_{\gamma}}{a_{\gamma}} \approx 0.001 \text{ to } 0.005
$$

**2. Strengthening Mechanism:**

When a dislocation glides through the γ matrix and encounters a γ′ precipitate, it must either:
- **Cut through** the ordered particle (creating an antiphase boundary — APB).
- **Loop around** it (Orowan looping).

For small coherent precipitates, **particle shearing** dominates, with the strengthening contribution given by:

$$
\Delta \tau_{shear} \propto \sqrt{\frac{f \cdot \gamma_{APB} \cdot r}{b}}
$$

where $\gamma_{APB}$ is the antiphase boundary energy, $f$ is the volume fraction of γ′, and $r$ is the precipitate radius.

**3. Role of Individual Alloying Elements:**

| Element | Effect on γ′ |
|---------|--------------|
| **Al (3–6%)** | Primary γ′ former; raises γ′ solvus temperature |
| **Ti (1–6%)** | Substitute for Al in Ni₃Al; increases γ′ volume fraction |
| **Nb (1–5%)** | Forms **γ″ (Ni₃Nb)** — body-centered tetragonal, even stronger strengthener (basis of Inconel-718) |
| **Ta** | Increases γ′ strength and creep resistance |
| **Re (1–6%)** | Reduces misfit; forms Re clusters impeding dislocation climb |

**4. Why γ′ is Stable at High Temperature:**

Because of the **low γ-γ′ lattice misfit**, the interfacial energy is low, and the driving force for Ostwald ripening is small. The γ′ solvus temperature in advanced alloys (e.g., PWA 1480) can exceed **1300 °C**, ensuring that precipitates do not dissolve during service.

**5. The Disadvantage — Stress-Aged Cracking:**

At intermediate temperatures (650–800 °C), excessive γ′ coverage combined with coherency strains can lead to **stress-age cracking** — a metallurgical problem in weld heat-affected zones.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare the hot-corrosion resistance of Stellite-6, Inconel-718, and Haynes-230. Explain the role of Cr content and protective oxide formation.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Hot Corrosion Mechanism:**

Hot corrosion is the accelerated degradation of metals in the **800–950 °C** temperature range due to deposition of molten salts (Na₂SO₄, NaCl, V₂O₅) from fuel impurities, often referred to as **Type I (high-temperature) hot corrosion**.

**2. Comparative Data:**

| Alloy | Cr (wt%) | Al (wt%) | Other Key Elements | Hot-Corrosion Resistance |
|-------|----------|----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **Stellite-6** | 28 | – | Co-base, 4W, 1C | Excellent in sulfidation |
| **Inconel-718** | 19 | 0.5 | 3Mo, 5Nb (Nb→γ″) | Moderate; Cr₂O₃ scale |
| **Haynes-230** | 22 | 0.3 | 14W, 2Mo, Ni-Co base | Excellent |

**3. Role of Cr and Al:**

- **Cr** forms a **Cr₂O₃** scale that resists sulfidation and oxidation up to ~900 °C.
- **Al** forms an **Al₂O₃** scale that is more stable than Cr₂O₃ above 1000 °C and grows more slowly.
- Alloys with **mixed Cr₂O₃ + Al₂O₃** scales (e.g., Haynes-230, Inconel-617) offer protection across a wider temperature range.

**4. Why Haynes-230 Outperforms Inconel-718 in the 1000–1150 °C Range:**

- Higher W + Mo content provides **solid-solution strengthening** at extreme temperatures.
- Co addition stabilizes γ′ and reduces stacking fault energy.
- Lattice matching between spinel-type outer scale and inner Cr₂O₃ layer improves scale adhesion.

**5. Practical Implication:**

- Stellite-6 is the **industry standard for valve trim** in chemical plants.
- Inconel-718 is unsuitable for **combustion liners** above 900 °C due to γ″ coarsening and oxidation.
- Haynes-230 is the **preferred material for combustor cans** in modern jet engines and industrial gas turbines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse γ′ (Ni₃Al) with γ″ (Ni₃Nb). Inconel-718 is **γ″-strengthened**, not γ′-strengthened. Mixing up the two costs 1–2 marks. Also, ensure the distinction between **Type I (>900 °C) and Type II (650–750 °C) hot corrosion** is clearly stated.

---

### Question B (Option 2): Nuclear Application of Non-Ferrous Alloys

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the criteria for selecting materials for nuclear reactors. Explain why Zirconium alloys are preferred for fuel cladding in PWRs.

**Model Solution:**

**1. The "Five Criteria" for Nuclear Materials:**

1. **Low Thermal Neutron Absorption Cross-Section (σₐ):** Materials in the reactor core must not "eat up" neutrons needed to sustain the chain reaction. Zr (0.18 b), Be (0.009 b), Mg (0.063 b) are excellent.
2. **Radiation Stability:** Resistance to displacement damage caused by high-energy neutrons.
3. **Corrosion Resistance:** Compatibility with coolant (water, liquid metal, gas).
4. **Mechanical Strength:** Must sustain internal pressure, fuel swelling, and thermal cycling.
5. **Fabricability:** Must be drawable into thin-walled tubes (~0.5 mm wall) with tight tolerances.

**2. Why Zirconium, Not Steel or Aluminum?**

- **Steel** (σₐ = 2.6 b) absorbs **~14× more neutrons** than Zr → would require enriched fuel to compensate, increasing cost.
- **Aluminum** (σₐ = 0.23 b) is acceptable but **melts at 660 °C** → cannot survive a Loss-Of-Coolant Accident (LOCA) where temperatures can spike to 1200 °C.
- **Zirconium** uniquely combines **low σₐ**, **high Tₘ (1855 °C)**, **good mechanical strength**, and **corrosion resistance in water**.

**3. Zircaloy-4 vs Zircaloy-2:**

- **Zircaloy-2** contains 0.05% Ni, which causes **hydrogen blistering** in BWRs.
- **Zircaloy-4** removes Ni and adjusts Fe/Cr → used in **PWRs** for superior corrosion behavior.
- **Zr-2.5Nb** is used in **PHWR pressure tubes** (CANDU design) due to higher strength and reduced creep.

**4. The LOCA Limitation:**

In a LOCA, Zr reacts exothermically with steam:

$$
\mathrm{Zr + 2\,H_2O \rightarrow ZrO_2 + 2\,H_2} \quad \Delta H \approx -585 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

The hydrogen generation is a major safety concern, motivating the development of **"accident-tolerant fuels" (ATF)** such as Cr-coated Zircaloy or SiC-SiC composites.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the role of beryllium in nuclear reactors. Why is it not used as a structural material despite its low density and high cross-section?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Beryllium — The "Neutron Specialist":**

Beryllium has the unique combination of:
- **Lowest thermal neutron cross-section of any structural metal:** $\sigma_a = 0.009$ barns (vs. 0.18 for Zr).
- **High scattering cross-section:** $\sigma_s = 7.0$ barns → excellent for **moderating and reflecting neutrons**.
- **Low density:** 1.85 g/cm³ (the lightest structural metal).
- **High modulus:** 303 GPa (5× that of aluminum).
- **High specific heat:** 1925 J/(kg·K) → excellent thermal sink.

**2. Applications in Reactors:**

- **Neutron Reflector** in research reactors and thermonuclear weapons (saves up to 50% of the fissile material).
- **Neutron Moderator** in compact reactors.
- **Plasma-facing material** in fusion reactors (ITER uses Be for the first wall).
- **Inertial fusion targets** (cryogenic Be capsule implosion).

**3. Why Not a Structural Material?**

| Issue | Consequence |
|-------|-------------|
| **Toxicity (berylliosis)** | Powder inhalation causes chronic lung disease; mandates stringent machining controls. |
| **Extreme brittleness** | Poor ductility at room temperature → failure under impact. |
| **High cost** | ~$800/kg vs. $25/kg for Zr. |
| **Anisotropic behavior** | Cold-worked Be shows severe directionality of properties. |
| **Limited high-T strength** | Strength drops above 600 °C. |

**4. The Toxicity Constraint:**

OSHA limits airborne Be to **2 μg/m³** (8-hour TWA). This alone prevents widespread use despite its excellent nuclear properties. The Be industry uses a "**beryllium worker protection model**" with full protective gear and medical surveillance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **absorption (σₐ)** and **scattering (σₛ)** cross-sections. Beryllium is a good **reflector and moderator** primarily because of its high σₛ, NOT its low σₐ. Make sure you distinguish both.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Print This Section**

### A. Definitions to Memorize
- [x] **Superalloy** → Alloy retaining strength, creep, and oxidation resistance > 540 °C.
- [x] **γ′ phase** → Ni₃(Al, Ti), L1₂ ordered, coherent precipitate.
- [x] **γ″ phase** → Ni₃Nb, DO₂₂ ordered, BCT, dominant in Inconel-718.
- [x] **LMP constant** → C ≈ 20 for Ni-superalloys.
- [x] **Thermal neutron cross-section** → measured in **barns** (1 barn = 10⁻²⁴ cm²).
- [x] **Zircaloy-4** → Zr-1.5Sn-0.2Fe-0.1Cr, the **PWR cladding** standard.
- [x] **Stellite-6** → Co-28Cr-4W-1C, used for wear + corrosion in valves.
- [x] **ODS alloy** → Oxide Dispersion Strengthened (e.g., MA-956 with Y₂O₃).

### B. Numerical Constants to Remember
- [x] Zr σₐ = **0.18 b**, Fe σₐ = **2.6 b**, Be σₐ = **0.009 b**
- [x] Tₘ of W = **3422 °C**, Mo = **2623 °C**, Nb = **2477 °C**
- [x] Inconel-718 max service = **650 °C continuous**
- [x] Haynes-230 max service = **1150 °C**
- [x] TZM service = **~1400 °C**
- [x] Ti-6Al-4V density = **4.43 g/cm³** (half that of Ni-superalloys)
- [x] Larson-Miller constant $C = 20$ for steels and superalloys

### C. Strengthening Mechanism Order (Strongest to Weakest at High T)
1. **ODS (Y₂O₃)** – stable to 0.9 Tₘ
2. **γ′ precipitation** – Ni₃Al, resists coarsening
3. **γ″ precipitation** – Ni₃Nb, loses strength above 650 °C
4. **Solid-solution** – Co, W, Mo
5. **Carbide pinning** – grain boundaries

### D. Process Routes for Superalloys (in increasing capability)
- **Cast equiaxed** (Inconel-713C)
- **Directionally solidified (DS)**
- **Single crystal (SX)** (CMSX-4, PWA 1484)
- **SX with Re** (3rd/4th generation, CMSX-10)
- **ODS** (MA-6000)

### E. Why Each Reactor Material Is Chosen
- **Cladding (PWR):** Zircaloy-4 — low σₐ + corrosion resistance.
- **Cladding (LMFBR fast reactor):** 316 SS or HT9 — tolerates fast neutron flux.
- **Reflector:** Be or H₂O — high σₛ, low σₐ.
- **Pressure tube (PHWR):** Zr-2.5Nb — high strength + low σₐ.
- **Control rod:** Ag-In-Cd or B₄C — high σₐ.
- **Reactor pressure vessel:** SA-533B + 308L cladding — toughness.
- **First wall (fusion):** Be or W — plasma-facing, low sputtering.

### F. Frequently Asked KTU 2024 Topics (Board Frequency Analysis)
- ⭐⭐⭐ **Larson-Miller Parameter + numerical** (asked 4 of last 5 years)
- ⭐⭐⭐ **Inconel-718 composition + γ″ strengthening** (asked 3 of last 5 years)
- ⭐⭐⭐ **Zircaloy vs stainless steel in PWR** (asked 4 of last 5 years)
- ⭐⭐ **Creep equations (Norton-Bailey)** (asked 3 of last 5 years)
- ⭐⭐ **Stellite composition and applications** (asked 2 of last 5 years)
- ⭐ **Refractory metals (W, Mo, Nb) — properties and uses** (asked 1 of last 5 years)
- ⭐ **Hot corrosion vs oxidation** (asked 1 of last 5 years)

### G. Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing σₐ and σₛ — always specify which cross-section.
- ❌ Saying "Inconel-718 is γ′-strengthened" — it is **γ″-strengthened**.
- ❌ Mixing up Zircaloy-2 and Zircaloy-4 compositions.
- ❌ Stating "W is used in jet engines" without specifying the role (turbine or X-ray or filament).
- ❌ Forgetting to include the **annealed vs cold-worked** state of Be in mechanical property questions.
- ❌ Citing "high melting point" as the only reason for refractory metal selection.

### H. Quick Recall: Major Alloys in 30 Seconds
- **Inconel-718** → Ni-base, γ″, jet engines, rockets, **650 °C** limit.
- **Waspaloy** → Ni-base, γ′, turbine disks, **870 °C** limit.
- **Stellite-6** → Co-base, valve trim, wear.
- **Haynes-230** → Ni-Co-Cr-W, combustion cans, **1150 °C**.
- **Ti-6Al-4V** → aerospace + biomedical.
- **Zircaloy-4** → PWR cladding, low σₐ.
- **Zr-2.5Nb** → PHWR pressure tubes.
- **TZM** → Mo-base, die casting.
- **W-Re** → thermocouples up to 2300 °C.
- **C-103 (Nb-10Hf-1Ti)** → rocket nozzles.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organic Polymers and its Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Organic Polymers and its Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Organic Polymer?

An **organic polymer** is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of a large number of small repeating structural units called **monomers**, covalently bonded together in a long-chain or network structure. The word *polymer* originates from the Greek words *poly* (meaning "many") and *meros* (meaning "parts"). The process of joining monomers to form a polymer is called **polymerization**.

The repeat unit of a polymer is called a **mer**, and a single polymer chain is often called a **macromolecule** because of its giant size. Most engineering polymers are **organic**, meaning their backbone is based on a **carbon (C)** chain (often with hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, or chlorine atoms attached).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** A polymer is a high-molecular-weight compound (molecular weight typically ranging from **10,000 to 10,000,000 g/mol**) formed by the chemical combination of a large number of small molecules called monomers. The number of repeat units $(n)$ in a polymer chain is called the **Degree of Polymerization (DP)**.

### Classic Examples

| Polymer | Monomer | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| Polyethylene (PE) | Ethylene $(C_2H_4)$ | Plastic bags, bottles |
| Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) | Vinyl Chloride $(C_2H_3Cl)$ | Pipes, cables |
| Polystyrene (PS) | Styrene $(C_8H_8)$ | Disposable cups |
| Nylon 6,6 | Adipic acid + Hexamethylene diamine | Textile fibers |
| Bakelite | Phenol + Formaldehyde | Electrical switches |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Train" Analogy)

Imagine a **train** as a polymer molecule:

- Each **railway coach** = one **monomer** (small unit).
- The **coupling/linker** between coaches = the **covalent bond** formed during polymerization.
- The **entire train** = the **polymer chain**.
- The **number of coaches** in the train = the **Degree of Polymerization (DP)**.
- Different types of coaches (passenger, goods, sleeper) = **different monomers** forming a **copolymer**.

Just as a long train (high DP) has greater strength and structural stability than a short train, a polymer with a higher degree of polymerization generally exhibits **superior mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Polymers (Overview)

Polymers can be broadly classified into three orthogonal schemes — all are **KTU high-yield**:

### A. Based on Origin
- **Natural Polymers:** Found in nature (e.g., cellulose, starch, proteins, natural rubber, DNA).
- **Synthetic Polymers:** Man-made (e.g., polyethylene, PVC, nylon, Bakelite).

### B. Based on Thermal Response (KTU Favourite)
- **Thermoplastics:** Soften on heating and harden on cooling — **reversible**. (e.g., PE, PP, PVC, PS, Nylon)
- **Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets):** Undergo permanent cross-linking on heating — **irreversible**. (e.g., Bakelite, Epoxy, Melamine, Phenolic resins)
- **Elastomers:** Elastic, can be stretched and recover original shape. (e.g., Natural rubber, Neoprene, Silicone)

### C. Based on Polymerization Mechanism
- **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymers:** Formed by addition of monomers without loss of any byproduct. (e.g., PE, PP, PVC)
- **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymers:** Formed with elimination of small molecules like $H_2O$, $HCl$, or $NH_3$. (e.g., Nylon, Polyester, Bakelite)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Many students confuse *thermoplastics* and *thermosets*. Remember: Thermoplastics = **recyclable** (chains slide past each other); Thermosets = **non-recyclable** (chains permanently locked in 3D network).

---

## 1.4 Why Study Organic Polymers?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Organic polymers form the backbone of **modern engineering materials** — from aerospace composites to biomedical implants, from packaging to electronics. Understanding their structure-property relationship allows engineers to **select the right material for the right application**, predict service life, and design new polymeric materials.

Key engineering domains relying on polymers:
- **Aerospace:** Carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) composites
- **Automotive:** Polymer matrix composites to reduce weight
- **Biomedical:** Biodegradable polymers (PLA, PGA) for sutures and scaffolds
- **Electronics:** Polymer insulators, flexible displays
- **Civil:** PVC pipes, polymer-modified concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Degree of Polymerization (DP)

The **Degree of Polymerization (DP)** is defined as the **number of repeat units** in a single polymer chain. It is the most fundamental parameter defining a polymer.

For a homopolymer (made from one type of monomer):

$$DP = n = \frac{\text{Molecular Weight of Polymer } (M)}{\text{Molecular Weight of Repeat Unit } (M_0)}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Average molecular weight of the polymer chain
- $M_0$ = Molecular weight of a single repeat unit (mer)
- $n$ = Degree of Polymerization

**Example:** If polyethylene has $M = 280,000 \text{ g/mol}$ and $M_0 = 28 \text{ g/mol}$ (for $-CH_2-CH_2-$), then $DP = 10,000$ — meaning the chain has 10,000 repeat units.

---

## 2.2 Molecular Weight of Polymers

Unlike small molecules, polymers are **polydisperse** — meaning every chain in a sample has a slightly different molecular weight. Hence, we use **average molecular weights**.

### A. Number-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_n$)

This is the **statistical average** of molecular weights weighted by the **number of molecules** of each size.

$$\overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i \cdot M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

Where:
- $N_i$ = Number of molecules with molecular weight $M_i$
- $M_i$ = Molecular weight of species $i$

It is determined experimentally by techniques like **osmometry** and **end-group analysis**.

### B. Weight-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_w$)

This is the average weighted by the **mass (weight)** of molecules of each size.

$$\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i \cdot M_i^2}{\sum N_i \cdot M_i}$$

It is determined by **light scattering** and **sedimentation** methods. $\overline{M}_w$ is **always greater than or equal to** $\overline{M}_n$.

### C. Polydispersity Index (PDI)

The **Polydispersity Index** quantifies the breadth of the molecular weight distribution:

$$PDI = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$$

- $PDI = 1$ → **Monodisperse** (all chains equal — ideal, rarely achieved)
- $PDI > 1$ → **Polydisperse** (real polymers)
- $PDI \approx 1.5 - 2.0$ → typical for **addition polymers**
- $PDI \approx 2.0$ → typical for **condensation polymers**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two averages?** A simple numerical average underrepresents heavy chains because there are fewer of them. The weight-average gives more importance to heavier molecules, which dominate bulk properties like viscosity and toughness.

---

## 2.3 Polymerization Mechanisms (Detailed)

### A. Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerization

Occurs in three distinct stages:

1. **Initiation:** An **initiator** (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) decomposes to form a **free radical** $R^{\bullet}$ which attacks the double bond of a monomer.
2. **Propagation:** The active chain end rapidly adds more monomers in a chain reaction.
3. **Termination:** Two active chains combine (or disproportionate) to form a dead polymer chain.

**Key features:**
- No byproduct is released.
- Requires an initiator (radical, cationic, or anionic).
- Only monomers with $C=C$ (or $C \equiv C$) double bonds participate.
- Example: Ethylene $\rightarrow$ Polyethylene.

### B. Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization

- Any two monomer species (monomers, dimers, trimers, etc.) can react at any time.
- Each reaction **eliminates a small molecule** ($H_2O$, $HCl$, $NH_3$).
- Proceeds slowly, and **high molecular weight is achieved only at very high conversions** (>99%).
- Example: Hexamethylene diamine + Adipic acid $\rightarrow$ Nylon 6,6 + $H_2O$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carothers' Equation (Step-Growth Polymerization):** The degree of polymerization is related to the conversion $p$ (fraction of functional groups reacted):

$$DP = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

This means for $DP = 100$, we need $p = 0.99$ (**99% conversion**). This explains why step-growth polymers require extremely pure monomers.

---

## 2.4 Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)

The **glass transition temperature** is the temperature below which a polymer becomes hard, rigid, and glassy (with frozen segmental motion), and above which it becomes soft, rubbery, and flexible.

- Below $T_g$: Polymer is **glassy**, hard, brittle.
- Above $T_g$: Polymer is **rubbery**, flexible.
- For crystalline polymers, the **melting temperature** $T_m$ also exists.

A simplified empirical relationship (for symmetric polymers):

$$\frac{T_g}{T_m} \approx \frac{1}{2} \text{ to } \frac{2}{3} \text{ (in Kelvin)}$$

**Factors affecting $T_g$:**
- **Chain flexibility:** Rigid backbones (e.g., aromatic rings) → higher $T_g$
- **Side groups:** Bulky side groups → higher $T_g$ (steric hindrance)
- **Cross-linking:** Higher cross-linking → higher $T_g$
- **Plasticizers:** Lower $T_g$ (e.g., in PVC pipes)
- **Intermolecular forces:** Strong forces → higher $T_g$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degree of Polymerization | $DP = n = M / M_0$ | Basic polymer sizing |
| 2 | Number-Average MW | $\overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Osmometry, end-group analysis |
| 3 | Weight-Average MW | $\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Light scattering, sedimentation |
| 4 | Polydispersity Index | $PDI = \overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n$ | Always $\geq 1$ |
| 5 | Carothers' Equation | $DP = 1 / (1 - p)$ | Step-growth polymerization |
| 6 | $T_g / T_m$ Ratio | $T_g / T_m \approx 0.5 - 0.67$ | Empirical, in Kelvin |
| 7 | Glass Transition | Below $T_g$: glassy; Above $T_g$: rubbery | Defines service temperature |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above theoretical framework directly enables engineers to:

- **Predict mechanical strength:** Higher $M_w \rightarrow$ Higher tensile strength.
- **Predict processability:** Lower $M_w \rightarrow$ Easier melt processing.
- **Select service temperature:** Choose a polymer with $T_g$ well above/below operating temperature.
- **Control crystallinity:** Slower cooling → Higher crystallinity → Higher density, stiffness.
- **Design copolymers:** Combine two monomers to tune properties (e.g., SBR rubber, ABS plastic).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number-Average and Weight-Average Molecular Weights

### Setup

Consider a polymer sample containing $N_1$ molecules of molecular weight $M_1$, $N_2$ molecules of molecular weight $M_2$, ..., $N_i$ molecules of molecular weight $M_i$.

**Step 1: Total mass of species $i$.**

The mass contributed by all molecules of species $i$ is:

$$m_i = N_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 2: Total number of molecules in the sample.**

$$N_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i$$

**Step 3: Total mass of the sample.**

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 4: Define Number-Average Molecular Weight.**

The number-average is the total mass divided by total number of molecules:

$$\overline{M}_n = \frac{m_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\sum N_i \cdot M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

**Step 5: Define Weight (Mass) Fraction.**

The weight fraction of species $i$ is:

$$w_i = \frac{N_i \cdot M_i}{\sum N_j \cdot M_j}$$

**Step 6: Express $\overline{M}_n$ in terms of $w_i$.**

Since $N_i = \dfrac{w_i \cdot \sum N_j \cdot M_j}{M_i}$, substitution yields the alternate form:

$$\overline{M}_n = \frac{1}{\sum \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}}$$

**Step 7: Define Weight-Average Molecular Weight.**

The weight-average gives each molecule a weight proportional to its own mass:

$$\overline{M}_w = \sum w_i \cdot M_i = \frac{\sum N_i \cdot M_i^2}{\sum N_i \cdot M_i}$$

**Step 8: Substitution to obtain the same in terms of $N_i$.**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the appropriate factor:

$$\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i \cdot M_i^2}{\sum N_i \cdot M_i}$$

> **Logic:** The square of $M_i$ in the numerator gives heavier molecules **proportionally more influence** on the average. This is why $\overline{M}_w \geq \overline{M}_n$ always.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Molecular Weight Calculation

**Problem:** A polymer sample has the following distribution:

| Species | $N_i$ (molecules) | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 200 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 50 | 100,000 |

Find $\overline{M}_n$, $\overline{M}_w$, and PDI.

### Step 1: Compute $\overline{M}_n$

$$\overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

Compute numerator:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum N_i M_i &= (100)(10{,}000) + (200)(50{,}000) + (50)(100{,}000) \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 + 10{,}000{,}000 + 5{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 16{,}000{,}000 \text{ (g/mol · molecules)}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum N_i &= 100 + 200 + 50 = 350 \text{ molecules}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{M}_n = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{350} = 45{,}714.3 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 2: Compute $\overline{M}_w$

$$\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

Compute numerator:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum N_i M_i^2 &= (100)(10{,}000)^2 + (200)(50{,}000)^2 + (50)(100{,}000)^2 \\
&= (100)(10^8) + (200)(25 \times 10^8) + (50)(10^{10}) \\
&= 1 \times 10^{10} + 50 \times 10^{10} + 50 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 101 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 1.01 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{M}_w = \frac{1.01 \times 10^{12}}{16 \times 10^6} = 63{,}125 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 3: Compute PDI

$$PDI = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \frac{63{,}125}{45{,}714.3} \approx 1.38$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $PDI > 1$, the polymer is **polydisperse** (typical of addition polymers). The higher value of $\overline{M}_w$ compared to $\overline{M}_n$ indicates the presence of a few high-molecular-weight chains that strongly influence bulk properties like melt viscosity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Carothers' Equation

**Problem:** In a step-growth polymerization of Nylon 6,6, conversion $p = 0.98$. Find the Degree of Polymerization.

### Step 1: Apply Carothers' Equation

$$DP = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$DP = \frac{1}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50$$

> **Insight:** At 98% conversion, $DP$ is only 50. To reach $DP = 100$ (a useful engineering polymer), we need:

$$DP = 100 \Rightarrow 1 - p = 0.01 \Rightarrow p = 0.99 \text{ (99% conversion)}$$

This shows why step-growth polymerization requires extremely pure monomers and efficient byproduct removal.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property → Structure Relationship

| Property | Effect of Increasing Chain Length (Higher DP) | Effect of Cross-Linking | Effect of Crystallinity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (up to a point) | ↑ Increases |
| Elongation at Break | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases |
| Glass Transition $T_g$ | Slight ↑ | ↑ Increases | — |
| Melt Viscosity | ↑ Sharply increases | Polymer may not melt | ↑ Increases |
| Chemical Resistance | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases |
| Density | ↑ Slightly | ↑ Slightly | ↑ Increases |
| Transparency | — | — | ↓ Decreases (more crystalline = opaque) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
