## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a nanomaterial. What is the significance of the 1–100 nm range?**

**Model Answer:**
A nanomaterial is a material having at least one dimension in the range of **1–100 nm**, where size-dependent properties (quantum confinement, surface effects) differ significantly from bulk. The lower bound of **1 nm** corresponds to the size of a small cluster of atoms (~50 atoms), below which the material loses its "condensed phase" character. The upper bound of **100 nm** is the threshold below which size-induced properties become observable. Above 100 nm, the fraction of surface atoms becomes negligible (< 1%), and bulk behaviour dominates.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four differences between nanomaterials and bulk materials.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Bulk | Nanomaterial |
|---|---|---|
| Surface-to-volume ratio | Low | Very high (∝ 1/d) |
| % of surface atoms | Negligible | Significant (up to 50% for 5 nm) |
| Band gap | Fixed | Size-tunable |
| Melting point | Standard | Lower (melting point depression) |
| Mechanical strength | Normal | Higher (Hall–Petch) |
| Reactivity | Low | Very high (catalysis) |

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**

**Q. (a)** What are nanomaterials? Classify them based on dimensions with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A nanomaterial is a material that has at least one external dimension in the size range of **1–100 nm** and exhibits size-dependent properties markedly different from the bulk. This definition is per ISO/TS 80004 standards adopted by KTU.

**Classification (5 marks):**

| Class | Dimensions < 100 nm | Examples | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0D** | All 3 dimensions | Quantum dots (CdSe, ZnS), Au nanoparticles, fullerenes | LEDs, solar cells, bio-imaging |
| **1D** | 2 dimensions | Carbon nanotubes, Ag nanowires, Si nanorods | Transistors, sensors, composites |
| **2D** | 1 dimension | Graphene, MoS₂ nanosheets, h-BN, clay platelets | Electronics, barriers, catalysis |
| **3D** | Internal structure | Polymer nanocomposites, nanocrystalline metals | Structural materials, packaging |

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the expression for quantum confinement energy of an electron in a spherical nanoparticle of radius R, using the infinite spherical well model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(Provide the full derivation already shown in **Section 3.3** above. Step-by-step: Schrödinger equation → separation of variables → boundary conditions → $kR = n\pi$ → final result $E_n = \dfrac{n^2 h^2}{8 m^* R^2}$.)

Add this concluding remark: The result shows that the energy of the confined electron increases as $1/R^2$, predicting that smaller nanoparticles will have a **larger effective band gap** and emit/absorb light at **higher energies (blue-shifted)**. This is the foundation of size-tunable optical properties in quantum dots.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the surface-to-volume ratio effect in nanomaterials. Show that the surface atom fraction in an FCC nanoparticle of diameter d is approximately $4a/d$, where a is the lattice parameter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual explanation (3 marks):** When a material's size is reduced to the nanometre scale, the fraction of atoms located on the surface becomes comparable to or greater than that in the bulk interior. Surface atoms have unsaturated/dangling bonds, leading to enhanced chemical reactivity, high surface energy, and altered mechanical/electronic behaviour. The surface-to-volume ratio scales as $1/d$, so as diameter decreases, surface area increases dramatically.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes. The final result is:

$$\frac{N_s}{N_{total}} = \frac{3a}{2d} \approx \frac{4a}{d}$$

For a 5 nm gold nanoparticle (FCC, $a = 0.408$ nm): $N_s/N_{total} \approx 4(0.408)/5 = 32.6\%$ of atoms are on the surface.

---

**Q. (b)** Distinguish between top-down and bottom-up approaches for nanomaterial synthesis. Give two examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|---|---|---|
| Starting material | Bulk solid | Atoms/molecules/ions |
| Process | Breaking/slicing | Building/assembling |
| Size control | Moderate (limited by tool) | Excellent (atomic precision) |
| Defect density | High (mechanical damage) | Low |
| Energy cost | Often high | Variable |
| Examples | Ball milling, photolithography, sputtering, etching | CVD, sol-gel, chemical reduction, self-assembly, hydrothermal |

**Examples:**
- **Top-down:** (i) High-energy ball milling of Cu to produce nanocrystalline Cu powder; (ii) Photolithography to pattern transistor gates below 10 nm.
- **Bottom-up:** (i) Sol-gel synthesis of SiO₂ nanoparticles from tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS); (ii) Chemical reduction of HAuCl₄ with citrate to produce Au nanoparticles (Turkevich method).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in $R$ vs. $d$:** Many students write $E = h^2/(8mR^2)$ and substitute the **diameter** by mistake, getting an answer off by a factor of 4. **Always double-check** whether the formula uses radius or diameter.
> 2. **Forgetting the Coulomb term in $E_g^{nano}$:** A full mark answer includes the *negative* Coulomb correction $1.8e^2/(4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r R)$. Writing only the kinetic term loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing 0D, 1D, 2D definitions:** A *nanosheet* is 2D (one dimension ≤ 100 nm), not 1D. Many students confuse this in classification tables.
> 4. **Confusing Bohr radius with atomic radius:** $a_B$ is a *material property* (e.g., 4.9 nm for InSb, 5.3 nm for CdSe), not a universal constant.
> 5. **No units in numerical problems:** Always state numerical values with **units** (eV, nm, m⁻¹). Marks are deducted even if the numerical answer is correct.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

- **Definition (1–100 nm):** Below 1 nm = cluster of atoms; above 100 nm = bulk. The 1–100 nm range is where size effects dominate.
- **Surface-to-volume ratio:** $\dfrac{A}{V} = \dfrac{6}{d}$ for a sphere. Universal scaling: $A/V \propto 1/L$.
- **Surface atom fraction:** $\dfrac{N_s}{N_{total}} \approx \dfrac{4a}{d}$ for FCC. At 5 nm, ~30–60% of atoms are surface atoms.
- **Quantum confinement energy:** $E_n = \dfrac{n^2 h^2}{8 m^* R^2}$ — scales as $1/R^2$, increases as particle shrinks.
- **Effective band gap:** $E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{h^2}{8R^2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{m_e^*}+\dfrac{1}{m_h^*}\right) - \dfrac{1.8e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r R}$
- **Bohr exciton radius ($a_B$):** Critical length scale. When $R \lesssim a_B$, quantum confinement is *strong*.
- **Classification:** 0D (all 3 nm) → quantum dots; 1D (2 nm) → nanotubes/nanowires; 2D (1 nm) → nanosheets/graphene; 3D (internal) → nanocomposites.
- **Synthesis routes:** Top-down (ball milling, lithography — high defects, large scale); Bottom-up (CVD, sol-gel — atomic control, fewer defects).
- **Key property changes in nanomaterials:** Higher strength (Hall–Petch), lower melting point, blue-shifted optical absorption, enhanced catalytic activity, altered electrical conductivity.
- **Key term pairs to distinguish:** Quantum dot ≠ quantum well; Bohr radius ≠ lattice parameter; bulk material ≠ nanostructured bulk.
- **Constants to memorize:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
- **Real-world examples to mention in answers:** Carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene, CdSe quantum dots, TiO₂ in sunscreen, Ag nanoparticles in antibacterial coatings, Pt nanoparticles in fuel-cell catalysts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Corrosion

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion** is the destructive and irreversible physico-chemical interaction (reaction) between a metal and its surrounding environment, which results in the degradation of the material's mechanical, optical, or structural properties. The driving force is the thermodynamic tendency of the metal to revert to its more stable, lower-energy oxide/compound form (its ore state).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, corrosion is essentially an **electrochemical oxidation-reduction (redox) process** in which the metal undergoes oxidation (loss of electrons) at the **anode**, and a corresponding reduction (gain of electrons) occurs at the **cathode**, accompanied by the flow of electrons through the metal and ions through the electrolyte.

Mathematically, this is represented as:
$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-} \quad \text{(Anodic reaction / Oxidation)}$$

$$2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2} \quad \text{(Cathodic reaction / Reduction in acidic medium)}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of corrosion as **"nature's recycling process."** When we extract metals like iron from their ores (iron oxide), we are spending a huge amount of energy to "purify" them. But nature does not like this pure state. So, it tries to send the metal back to its original ore form through corrosion. In short, **corrosion is the metals trying to "go home" to their natural, low-energy oxide state.**

Another simple analogy: A bicycle left out in the rain slowly develops rust spots. This is the metal "sacrificing" itself to the environment to achieve chemical stability. The rust (Fe₂O₃) is essentially the same compound as the iron ore (Fe₂O₃) from which the iron was originally extracted.

## 1.3 Classification of Corrosion

| Type | Environment | Mechanism | Example |
|------|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **Chemical (Dry) Corrosion** | Absence of electrolyte (high-temp gases, O₂) | Direct chemical attack | Oxidation of iron in furnace |
| **Electrochemical (Wet) Corrosion** | Presence of electrolyte (moisture, salts) | Galvanic cell formation | Rusting of iron in water |
| **Galvanic Corrosion** | Two dissimilar metals in electrolyte | Potential difference | Cu-Fe pipe joint |
| **Pitting Corrosion** | Localized passive layer breakdown | Autocatalytic pits | Stainless steel in Cl⁻ |
| **Intergranular Corrosion** | Grain boundaries attacked | Precipitate depletion zone | Stainless steel weld zones |

## 1.4 Economic and Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> According to NACE International estimates, the global annual cost of corrosion is **~$2.5 trillion USD**, equivalent to roughly **3-4% of the world's GDP**. Hence, corrosion science is critical in KTU-aligned engineering disciplines — Civil (rebar in concrete), Mechanical (boiler tubes, pipelines), Chemical (reactor vessels), and Marine (ship hulls).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Mechanism of Corrosion

The corrosion of a metal (say, **iron in water containing dissolved O₂**) proceeds through four mandatory steps:

1. **Anode Reaction (Metal Dissolution):** The metal atoms lose electrons and dissolve as ions.
$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

2. **Electron Flow through the Metal:** The liberated electrons travel through the metallic bulk to the cathode site (these sites can be impurities, second-phase particles, or areas of different composition).

3. **Cathode Reaction (Reduction):** Electrons are consumed by species in the electrolyte. Common cathodic reactions include:
   - **Hydrogen evolution (in acidic medium):** $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}\uparrow$
   - **Oxygen reduction (in neutral/alkaline medium):** $O_{2} + 2H_{2}O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$
   - **Oxygen reduction (in acidic medium):** $O_{2} + 4H^{+} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 2H_{2}O$
   - **Metal ion reduction:** $M^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow M$

4. **Ion Migration through Electrolyte:** Metal ions migrate from anode; OH⁻ or other anions migrate to anode to form corrosion products (rust: Fe₂O₃·xH₂O).

## 2.2 Why Does Corrosion Occur? (The Thermodynamic 'Why')

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental driving force is the **Gibbs Free Energy Change ($\Delta G$)** of the oxidation reaction. If $\Delta G < 0$, corrosion is **spontaneous** and proceeds without external energy. Most metals have a strong negative $\Delta G$ for oxide formation, which is why almost all metals corrode in oxygen-rich environments.

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol**
- $E_{cell}$ = cell potential (V)

If $E_{cell} > 0$, then $\Delta G < 0$ and corrosion is thermodynamically favorable.

## 2.3 The Nernst Equation (EMF Series Application)

The single most important equation for predicting corrosion tendency. It gives the electrode potential under non-standard conditions:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$$

Or in the metallic form (for $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$):

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln [M^{n+}]$$

At standard temperature (25°C = 298 K), substituting $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K and $F = 96,485$ C/mol:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [M^{n+}]$$

## 2.4 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (Corrosion Rate Calculation)

**First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Second Law:** The mass of different substances liberated by the same quantity of electricity is proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.

**Corrosion Rate (CR) Expression:**

$$CR \text{ (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $W$ = weight loss (mg)
- $D$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $A$ = exposed area (in²)
- $t$ = exposure time (hours)
- 534 = unit conversion constant (for mpy)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use | Units |
|---------|-----------|-----|-------|
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [M^{n+}]$ | Electrode potential under non-std. conditions | Volts (V) |
| Cell Potential | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Predicting galvanic corrosion tendency | V |
| Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ | Spontaneity of corrosion | J/mol |
| Faraday's First Law | $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal dissolved | grams |
| Corrosion Rate (mpy) | $CR = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Engineering corrosion rate | mils/year |
| Corrosion Current (Tafel) | $I_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Electrochemical measurement | Amperes |
| Polarization Resistance | $R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ | Linear polarization method | Ohms (Ω) |

## 2.6 Forms (Types) of Corrosion - Engineering Perspective

1. **Galvanic Corrosion:** Occurs when two dissimilar metals are electrically connected in an electrolyte. The more active metal (anode) corrodes faster; the more noble metal (cathode) is protected.
2. **Pitting Corrosion:** Highly localized, produces deep pits. Common in stainless steels attacked by Cl⁻ ions. Most insidious because it causes failure with little mass loss.
3. **Crevice Corrosion:** Occurs in stagnant micro-environments (gaskets, bolt heads) where differential aeration/concentration cells form.
4. **Intergranular Corrosion:** Selective attack at grain boundaries due to chromium carbide precipitation in stainless steel (sensitization).
5. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC):** Combined action of tensile stress and corrosive medium (e.g., brass in ammonia).
6. **Erosion Corrosion:** Acceleration of corrosion by mechanical abrasion of the protective film.
7. **Hydrogen Embrittlement:** Atomic H diffuses into metal, causing loss of ductility and brittle fracture.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - **Marine Industry:** Ship hulls use cathodic protection with zinc anodes.
> - **Oil & Gas:** Underground pipelines coated with 3LPE (3-layer polyethylene) plus impressed current cathodic protection.
> - **Reinforced Concrete:** Epoxy-coated rebar plus calcium nitrite admixtures prevent rebar corrosion.
> - **Boilers:** Deaerators remove dissolved O₂; oxygen-scavenging chemicals (hydrazine) are added.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Detailed, KTU Style)

We begin with the **thermodynamic Gibbs-Helmholtz relationship** for a reversible electrochemical cell:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Where $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

For a general reversible electrode reaction:
$$Ox + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons Red$$

The electrical work done by the cell is:
$$W_{elec} = nFE$$

And the relationship between Gibbs free energy and cell potential is:
$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad ; \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

Substituting these into the first equation:
$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Dividing both sides by $-nF$:
$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

For the oxidation $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$, the reaction quotient is $Q = [M^{n+}]$, so:
$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln [M^{n+}]$$

Or equivalently, using the convention $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln [M^{n+}]$ (for reduction direction):

Now converting from natural log to base-10 log:
$$\ln x = 2.303 \log x$$

So:
$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{nF} \log [M^{n+}]$$

At $T = 298$ K, substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) and $F = 96,485$ C/mol:
$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.0591 \text{ V}$$

Therefore the **Nernst Equation (25°C form)** is:
$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [M^{n+}]$$

This is the universally-used form in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of Faraday's First Law (Mass Loss Equation)

Consider an electrode reaction:
$$M^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow M$$

Charge passed through the cell:
$$Q = I \cdot t \quad \text{(Coulombs)}$$

Number of moles of electrons transferred:
$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

Number of moles of metal deposited (1 mole of M requires n moles of e⁻):
$$n_{M} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Mass of metal deposited:
$$m = n_{M} \times M = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Defining **Electrochemical Equivalent** $Z = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}$:
$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

This proves the First Law of Electrolysis.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A steel pipe of area 100 cm² is exposed to seawater for 30 days. The weight loss is 0.85 g. Calculate the corrosion rate in (a) mpy and (b) mm/year. Given: Density of steel = 7.86 g/cm³.

**Step 1:** Convert given data to consistent units.
- $W = 0.85$ g $= 850$ mg
- $A = 100$ cm² $= 100 / 6.4516 = 15.50$ in²
- $t = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hours

**Step 2:** Apply the mpy formula.
$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$$

Substituting values:
$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \times 850}{7.86 \times 15.50 \times 720}$$

Numerator:
$$534 \times 850 = 453,900$$

Denominator:
$$7.86 \times 15.50 \times 720 = 87,702.6$$

Final value:
$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{453,900}{87,702.6} = 5.175 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 3:** Convert mpy to mm/year.
$$1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/year}$$

$$CR_{mm/y} = 5.175 \times 0.0254 = 0.1314 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Result:** The corrosion rate is **5.175 mpy (≈ 0.13 mm/year)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting Corrosion Protection

| Method | Best For | Cost | Lifespan | Maintenance |
|--------|----------|------|----------|-------------|
| Cathodic Protection (Sacrificial Anode) | Small structures, ships | Low | 5-15 yrs | Periodic replacement |
| Cathodic Protection (Impressed Current) | Long pipelines, tanks | Medium-High | 20+ yrs | DC power source |
| Protective Coatings (Paint) | Atmospheric exposure | Low | 5-10 yrs | Recoating needed |
| Hot-Dip Galvanizing | Structural steel | Medium | 30-50 yrs | Minimal |
| Inhibitor Addition | Closed systems (boilers) | Low | System lifetime | Continuous dosing |
| Material Selection (Alloys) | Critical service | High | 20+ yrs | Minimal |
| Anodic Protection | Strongly oxidizing media | Medium | System lifetime | Reference electrode |

## 3.5 Pourbaix Diagram Interpretation (Conceptual)

The **Pourbaix diagram** is a plot of electrode potential (E) vs. pH, showing the thermodynamically stable phase of a metal. It has three regions:
- **Immunity:** Metal is stable; no corrosion occurs.
- **Corrosion:** Metal dissolves as ions; corrosion active.
- **Passivation:** Stable oxide/hydroxide layer forms; corrosion rate very low.

For iron, in the pH range 9-13 and at moderately positive potentials, Fe is passive (forms Fe₂O₃/Fe(OH)₃ layer). This is the principle behind anodic protection of steel in alkaline media.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Electrochemical Corrosion Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anode Site - Metal Dissolution] -->|Fe → Fe2+ + 2e-| B[Metal Bulk - Electron Pathway]
    B -->|e- flow| C[Cathode Site - Reduction]
    C -->|O2 + 2H2O + 4e- → 4OH-| D[Electrolyte - Ion Migration]
    D -->|OH- moves to anode| A
    A -->|Fe2+ + 2OH- → FeOH2| E[Corrosion Product - Rust Fe2O3·xH2O]
```
