# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define corrosion. Distinguish between chemical corrosion and electrochemical corrosion.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Corrosion is the destructive and irreversible degradation of a metal due to chemical or electrochemical reactions with its environment.
- **Chemical Corrosion (1 mark):** Occurs in the absence of a liquid electrolyte; takes place by direct chemical attack (e.g., oxidation of metals in dry O₂, high-temperature attack by gases like SO₂, H₂S).
- **Electrochemical Corrosion (1 mark):** Occurs in the presence of a liquid electrolyte (moisture, salt solution). Involves separate anodic (oxidation) and cathodic (reduction) reactions with electron flow through the metal and ion migration through the electrolyte (e.g., rusting of iron in water).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is the significance of the EMF series and the galvanic series in corrosion prediction?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **EMF Series (1.5 marks):** Lists standard electrode potentials of pure metals in their standard states. The metal with the more negative potential (e.g., Mg, Zn) acts as the anode and corrodes preferentially when coupled with a more noble metal. **Limitation:** It does not account for the influence of passivation, oxide films, or non-standard conditions.
- **Galvanic Series (1.5 marks):** A practical ordering of metals and alloys in a specific environment (typically seawater). It includes alloys and their passive-state behavior. **Use:** When two metals are coupled in a real environment, the one higher in the galvanic series (more noble) is protected, while the one lower (more active) corrodes. It is more reliable for engineering design than the EMF series.

## 5.2 Part B Question - Choice A (14 Marks Total)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** Choice A: 
(a) Explain the mechanism of electrochemical corrosion of iron in a neutral aqueous medium with dissolved oxygen, giving the relevant anodic and cathodic reactions. (7 marks)
(b) Discuss the various methods of corrosion control with a focus on cathodic protection (sacrificial anode and impressed current methods). (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Q3(a) - 7 marks

The mechanism proceeds in **four distinct stages** (each stage ~1.5-2 marks):

**Stage 1 - Anodic Dissolution (1.5 marks):**
At the anodic site (e.g., impurity, stressed area), iron atoms lose electrons and dissolve:
$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-} \quad (E^{\circ} = -0.44 \text{ V})$$

**Stage 2 - Electron Transport (1 mark):**
The liberated electrons travel through the bulk of the iron to the cathodic sites (where dissolved O₂ is present).

**Stage 3 - Cathodic Reduction (1.5 marks):**
At the cathode, dissolved O₂ in the neutral water is reduced:
$$O_{2} + 2H_{2}O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-} \quad (E^{\circ} = +0.40 \text{ V})$$

The overall cell potential is $E_{cell} = 0.40 - (-0.44) = +0.84$ V (highly spontaneous).

**Stage 4 - Product Formation (Rust) (1.5 marks):**
The Fe²⁺ ions react with OH⁻ ions and O₂ to form hydrated ferric oxide (rust):
$$4Fe^{2+} + 8OH^{-} + O_{2} + 2H_{2}O \rightarrow 2Fe_{2}O_{3} \cdot 3H_{2}O \text{ (Rust)}$$

The presence of dissolved salts (NaCl in seawater) accelerates the process by increasing electrolyte conductivity and disrupting protective films.

**Conclusion (1.5 marks):** This mechanism is the basis of rust formation in atmospheric, underwater, and underground corrosion of steel structures, pipelines, ship hulls, and rebar in concrete.

### Model Solution for Q3(b) - 7 marks

**Cathodic Protection (CP) - Overview (1 mark):**
CP is an electrochemical method that converts the entire metal structure into the cathode of an electrochemical cell, thus preventing the anodic dissolution (corrosion) reaction.

**Method 1: Sacrificial Anode Method (3 marks):**
- A more electrochemically active metal (Zn, Mg, or Al) is electrically connected to the steel structure to be protected.
- The sacrificial anode (e.g., Zn) is preferentially oxidized:
$$Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$
- These electrons flow to the steel, suppressing the Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ reaction.
- **Applications:** Ship hulls, offshore platforms, underground water tanks, small pipelines.
- **Advantages:** No external power source required; simple; suitable for low-resistivity environments.
- **Disadvantages:** Periodic replacement of anodes; limited driving voltage (~0.25 V for Zn-Fe); unsuitable for high-resistivity soils.

**Method 2: Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) (3 marks):**
- An external DC power source (rectifier) is used, with the negative terminal connected to the structure and the positive terminal to an inert anode (graphite, titanium, scrap iron).
- The DC current forces the structure potential to a more negative value, suppressing anodic dissolution.
- **Applications:** Long cross-country pipelines, marine platform pilings, storage tank bases.
- **Advantages:** Can protect large structures; adjustable driving voltage; long-lasting anodes.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires continuous power; needs regular monitoring; higher initial cost.

**Comparison summary must be tabulated for full marks.**

## 5.3 Part B Question - Choice B (14 Marks Total - Alternative)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]** Choice B:
(a) With a neat diagram, explain the differential aeration corrosion cell. How does it lead to corrosion under deposits and water-line corrosion? (7 marks)
(b) Derive the Nernst equation and apply it to calculate the electrode potential of a copper electrode immersed in 0.01 M CuSO₄ solution at 25°C. Given: $E^{\circ}_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Q4(a) - 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Differential aeration corrosion occurs when the same metal is exposed to different oxygen concentrations in an electrolyte, creating a corrosion cell.

**Mechanism with Diagram Description (3 marks):**
Consider a horizontal iron rod partially immersed in water:
- The **area just below the waterline (less O₂)** becomes the **anode**:
$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$
- The **area at the waterline (more O₂ exposure)** becomes the **cathode**:
$$O_{2} + 2H_{2}O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$$
- The metal preferentially corrodes **just below the waterline** → this is the classic **water-line corrosion**.

**Corrosion under deposits (2 marks):**
- A sand particle, rust scale, or dirt deposit on a metal surface creates a shielded area (less O₂) underneath.
- The shielded area acts as the anode; the surrounding metal (more O₂) acts as the cathode.
- Severe localized pitting occurs beneath the deposit.

**Crevice corrosion** is a special case of differential aeration in gasket joints, lap joints, and bolt threads.

**Engineering examples (1 mark):** Heat exchanger tubes with scale deposits, riveted plates in old ships, bolted joints in marine service.

### Model Solution for Q4(b) - 7 marks

**Derivation of Nernst Equation (3.5 marks):**

Starting from the thermodynamic relation:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

For an electrochemical cell, the electrical work is:
$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

Substituting:
$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Dividing by $-nF$:
$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

For the half-reaction $Cu^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Cu$, $Q = \frac{1}{[Cu^{2+}]}$:
$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{1}{[Cu^{2+}]}$$
$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln [Cu^{2+}]$$

Converting $\ln$ to $\log$ and substituting $T = 298$ K:
$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [Cu^{2+}]$$

**Numerical Application (3.5 marks):**

Given: $E^{\circ} = +0.34$ V, $n = 2$, $[Cu^{2+}] = 0.01$ M = $10^{-2}$ M.

$$E = 0.34 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.01)$$

$$\log(0.01) = -2$$

$$E = 0.34 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2)$$

$$E = 0.34 + (-0.0591)$$

$$\boxed{E = +0.2809 \text{ V}}$$

This is less positive than the standard potential, as expected for a more dilute solution (Le Chatelier's principle).

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Mistakes in Corrosion Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the half-cell reaction species:** Students often write $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + 3e^{-}$ instead of $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$. In neutral/aqueous corrosion, **Fe²⁺ is the primary product** (Fe³⁺ forms only via subsequent oxidation).
> 2. **Sign convention errors in Nernst equation:** The sign of the logarithm term must be carefully tracked. Always re-derive from $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$ in the exam to avoid sign flips.
> 3. **Confusing EMF series with Galvanic series:** EMF series is for **pure metals in standard states**; Galvanic series is for **metals and alloys in real environments** (e.g., seawater). The KTU examiner awards marks only when both are explained with examples.
> 4. **Not drawing the corrosion cell diagram:** In 7-mark mechanism questions, **a labeled diagram is mandatory**. Without it, expect a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Cathodic protection confusion:** Students often write that the "anode is protected" - it is the **STRUCTURE that is protected** by making it the **cathode**. The sacrificial anode **sacrifices itself**.
> 6. **In mpy calculations, unit conversion errors:** Watch for cm² to in² conversion: $1$ in² $= 6.4516$ cm². Wrong conversion = wrong answer = 0 marks.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Corrosion** is fundamentally an **electrochemical oxidation-reduction (redox) process**, with metal dissolution at the anode and a reduction reaction at the cathode.
- **Driving Force:** Thermodynamic tendency of the metal to return to its oxide/ore state, measured by negative $\Delta G$ and positive $E_{cell}$.
- **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [M^{n+}]$ at 25°C. Used to calculate electrode potentials under non-standard conditions.
- **Faraday's Law:** $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$; used to calculate mass loss and corrosion rate.
- **Corrosion Rate Units:** mpy (mils per year) and mm/year; conversion: 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year.
- **Galvanic Series** > EMF series for engineering design, because it accounts for passivation and real-environment behavior.
- **Eight Forms of Corrosion:** Uniform, Pitting, Crevice, Intergranular, SCC, Erosion, Dealloying, Hydrogen Embrittlement.
- **Protection Methods (4 categories):**
  1. **Cathodic Protection:** Sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg, Al) and Impressed Current.
  2. **Anodic Protection:** For metals that form passive films (e.g., steel in HNO₃, stainless steel in H₂SO₄).
  3. **Coatings:** Metallic (galvanizing, electroplating), Organic (paints, epoxies), Conversion (chromating, phosphating).
  4. **Inhibitors:** Anodic (chromates, nitrites), Cathodic (ZnSO₄, polyphosphates), Mixed (organic amines).
- **Differential Aeration Corrosion** is responsible for **water-line corrosion** and **corrosion under deposits**.
- **Pourbaix Diagrams (E vs pH)** predict regions of immunity, corrosion, and passivation - critical for material selection.
- **Common SRO questions in KTU:** Nernst equation derivation + numerical (frequent), Cathodic protection diagram + explanation, Pitting corrosion mechanism.
- **Numerical Skill Required:** Be fluent in mpy ↔ mm/yr conversion, Faraday's law, and the $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n}\log[M^{n+}]$ calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types, Mechanism and Factors Influencing Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Different Types, Mechanism and Factors Influencing Corrosion # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CORROSION** is the gradual destruction or deterioration of metals and alloys by chemical or electrochemical reactions with their surrounding environment. It is essentially the reverse process of extraction of metals from their ores — the metal tends to revert back to its thermodynamically stable oxidized state (oxide, hydroxide, or salt form). As per the KTU Board Examiner convention, corrosion is categorized broadly into two primary classes: | Type | Synonym | Driving Force | |---|---|---| | **Chemical (Dry) Corrosion** | Direct Chemical Attack | Direct chemical reaction with gases like O₂, SO₂, H₂S (no liquid electrolyte) | | **Electrochemical (Wet) Corrosion** | Galvanic / Immersed Corrosion | Electrochemical cells formed in presence of an electrolyte | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of corrosion like a "rust tax" on metals.** > Just like a wooden chair left out in monsoon Kerala rains slowly rots and returns to soil, an iron gate exposed to humid coastal air rusts and returns to iron oxide — the same compound (Fe₂O₃) found in natural iron ore. Metals are essentially *stored sunlight* (energy), and corrosion is the slow *release* of that energy back to nature. ### A Simple Everyday Picture: Imagine you have two iron nails — one in a sealed glass jar with silica gel (perfectly dry), and one in an open coastal tray where salty humid air touches it daily. - **Dry nail** → stays shiny for months. - **Coastal nail** → develops reddish-brown rust within weeks. The **second case is electrochemical corrosion**, where thin films of moisture (electrolyte) + dissolved oxygen act as the "crime scene" for rusting. The dry case is *chemical corrosion*, requiring high temperatures (e.g., boiler tubes reacting with hot O₂). --- ## 1.3 The Eight Major Forms of Corrosion (KTU Board Focus) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU syllabus explicitly lists **Eight Principal Forms of Corrosion** — these are *high-yield* and frequently appear in 14-mark ESE questions. 1. **Uniform (General) Corrosion** — even attack across entire exposed surface. 2. **Galvanic (Two-Metal) Corrosion** — when two dissimilar metals are electrically coupled in an electrolyte. 3. **Pitting Corrosion** — localized, deep, narrow cavities (most dangerous form). 4. **Crevice Corrosion** — attack within stagnant micro-gaps (gaskets, rivet joints). 5. **Intergranular Corrosion** — preferential attack along grain boundaries (e.g., weld decay in SS-304). 6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — combined action of tensile stress + corrosive medium. 7. **Erosion Corrosion** — accelerated attack due to mechanical abrasion + chemistry. 8. **Hydrogen Embrittlement** — atomic H diffuses into metal, causing brittleness and cracking. --- ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | **E°** | Standard Electrode Potential | **0.00 V** (SHE reference) | | **ρ (resistivity of electrolyte)** | Controls galvanic current | **Ω·cm** | | **PBR (Pilling–Bedworth Ratio)** | Volume of oxide / volume of metal consumed | **1 < PBR < 2** is protective | | **β (Tafel slope)** | Tafel constant | **~0.03 to 0.12 V/decade** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Electrochemical Mechanism of Wet Corrosion (Anodic–Cathodic Half-Cells) Wet corrosion is fundamentally an **electrochemical cell** — four components are mandatory: > [!NOTE] > **The Four Horsemen of Corrosion (Evans' Elements):** > 1. **Anode** — where metal dissolves (oxidation, M → M^n+ + ne⁻). > 2. **Cathode** — where reduction occurs (e.g., H⁺, O₂ reduction). > 3. **Electrolyte** — ionic conductor (moisture, salt solution). > 4. **Electrical connection** — metallic path for electron flow (the metal itself or external wire). ### 2.1.1 Anodic Reaction (Universal) $$ \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^- $$ The metal atom loses electrons and enters the electrolyte as cations. *This is the actual "loss" of metal.* ### 2.1.2 Cathodic Reactions (Three Variants — Board Favourite) **(a) Hydrogen Evolution Type** — in strongly acidic, de-aerated solutions: $$ 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2 \uparrow $$ **(b) Oxygen Absorption Type (Neutral / Alkaline)** — common in salt water, moist air: $$ \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^- $$ **(c) Oxygen Absorption Type (Acidic)** — common in aerated acid solutions: $$ \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ ### 2.1.3 Complete Example: Rusting of Iron in Neutral Aqueous Medium $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Anode:} \quad & \text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- \\ \text{Cathode:} \quad & \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^- \\ \text{Overall:} \quad & 2\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe(OH)}_2 \\ \text{(further oxidation:)} \quad & 4\text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3 \\ \text{(dehydration:)} \quad & 2\text{Fe(OH)}_3 \longrightarrow \underbrace{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot x\text{H}_2\text{O}}_{\text{RUST (Hydrated Ferric Oxide)}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Rust" is therefore a hydrated ferric oxide** — Fe₂O₃·xH₂O, a flaky, porous, non-protective coating. This is why iron continues to corrode once rusting starts. --- ## 2.2 Chemical (Dry) Corrosion Mechanism Three sub-classes are board-relevant: | Sub-type | Mechanism | Example | |---|---|---| | **Oxidation by O₂** | M + O₂ → MO | Fe → FeO/Fe₂O₃ at high T | | **Corrosion by SO₂** | Metal + SO₂ + O₂ → MSO₄ | Industrial atmosphere attack | | **Corrosion by H₂S** | Metal + H₂S → MS + H₂ | Refinery furnace tubes | > [!NOTE] > **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** decides whether the oxide layer protects or destroys the metal: > - **PBR < 1** → porous, non-protective (e.g., Mg, Ca — alkali metals). > - **1 < PBR < 2** → **protective** (e.g., Al, Cr, Ti — forms adherent passive film). > - **PBR > 2** → flaky, spalls off (e.g., Fe — that's why iron rust falls off). --- ## 2.3 Detailed Treatment of Major Corrosion Forms ### 2.3.1 Galvanic Corrosion - Occurs when **two metals of different electrode potentials** are electrically connected in an electrolyte. - The **more active (anodic)** metal corrodes preferentially. - Severity ↑ with: **(i) larger potential difference**, **(ii) larger cathode:anode area ratio**, **(iii) higher electrolyte conductivity**. ### 2.3.2 Pitting Corrosion - Localized, autocatalytic attack producing deep cavities. - Most aggressive when **anode:cathode area ratio is very small** (e.g., a tiny scratch is the anode, the entire surface is the cathode). - Common in **stainless steels in chloride environments** (marine-grade SS). ### 2.3.3 Intergranular Corrosion - Attack concentrated at grain boundaries due to **chromium carbide (Cr₂₃C₆) precipitation** in austenitic stainless steels, depleting Cr in the boundary zone ("**sensitization**"). - Solution: use **low-carbon grade (SS-304L)** or **stabilized grade (SS-321 with Ti, SS-347 with Nb)**. ### 2.3.4 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC) - Requires **three simultaneous conditions**: susceptible alloy + specific corrosive environment + sustained tensile stress. - "**Caustic embrittlement**" of boiler steel is a classic SCC example (NaOH + residual stress). --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Application | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode** | Driving EMF of corrosion cell | **V** (volts) | | 2 | **ΔG° = −nFE°cell** | Spontaneity; if ΔG° < 0, corrosion is thermodynamically favourable | **J/mol** | | 3 | **E = E° − (RT / nF) ln a** | **Nernst Equation** | **V** | | 4 | **E = E° − (0.0591 / n) log₁₀ a** (at 25°C) | Simplified Nernst for board problems | **V** | | 5 | **log₁₀(i) = log₁₀(i₀) + (η / β)** | Tafel equation (η = overpotential, β = Tafel slope) | **A/cm²**, **V** | | 6 | **CR = (K × W) / (A × T × D)** | Corrosion Rate (K = 8.76 × 10⁴ for mm/yr) | **mm/yr** | | 7 | **PBR = W·ρM / (n·ρoxide·AM)** | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | dimensionless | | 8 | **ΔE for galvanic series** | Larger ΔE ⇒ faster galvanic corrosion | **V** | | 9 | **η_cathodic = (β_c / 2.303) log(i_corr / i₀)** | Butler–Volmer (high-field branch) | **V** | | 10 | **Caustic concentration ≈ 10% NaOH + stress** | Critical condition for caustic embrittlement | **wt%** | > [!WARNING] > **Examination Pitfall:** When calculating E°cell, **always subtract the more negative (anode) electrode potential from the more positive (cathode) potential**. Students frequently reverse the sign and lose full marks. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance - **Oil & Gas:** H₂S + CO₂ + Cl⁻ environments cause SSC, pitting, and HIC in subsea pipelines. - **Marine (Kerala Coast):** Atmospheric salt + humidity ⇒ severe galvanic & pitting corrosion on fishing boats and offshore platforms. - **Power Plants:** Boiler tubes suffer from caustic embrittlement and oxidation corrosion. - **Aerospace:** Stress corrosion cracking of Al-alloy fuselage panels is a critical safety concern. - **Civil Engineering:** Reinforcement bars (rebar) corrode in concrete when chloride threshold is crossed ⇒ spalling of concrete cover. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation (Board-Level, Step-by-Step) **Statement:** The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium electrode potential of a half-cell at any temperature and ionic activity. ### Setup Consider the general reversible half-cell reaction: $$ aA + ne^- \rightleftharpoons bB $$ ### Step 1: Relate Gibbs free energy to the equilibrium constant From thermodynamics, the change in Gibbs free energy for the reaction under non-standard conditions is: $$ \Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q $$ where $Q = \dfrac{a_B^{\,b}}{a_A^{\,a}}$ is the reaction quotient and $a_X$ is the activity of species $X$. ### Step 2: Substitute electrochemical work term The electrical work done is $W_{elec} = -nFE$, and at constant $T$, $P$: $$ \Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ $$ ### Step 3: Combine and isolate E $$ \begin{aligned} -nFE &= -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q \\ \Rightarrow \quad E &= E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Convert natural log to log₁₀ and substitute constants At $T = 25^\circ\text{C} = 298\ \text{K}$: $$ \frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.02569\ \text{V} $$ And since $\ln Q = 2.303 \log_{10} Q$: $$ \boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{at } 25^\circ\text{C}} $$ ### Step 5: Application — Calculate the EMF of a Daniell Cell Reaction: Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu Given: $[\text{Zn}^{2+}] = 0.01\ \text{M}$, $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 1.0\ \text{M}$. Standard potentials: $E^\circ_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.763\ \text{V}$, $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.337\ \text{V}$. $$ \begin{aligned} E^\circ_{cell} &= E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode} = 0.337 - (-0.763) = 1.100\ \text{V} \\ n &= 2,\quad Q = \frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \frac{0.01}{1.0} = 0.01 \\ E_{cell} &= 1.100 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log_{10}(0.01) \\ &= 1.100 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2) \\ &= 1.100 + 0.0591 = 1.1591\ \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** As [Zn²⁺] decreases (more corrosion), the cell EMF *increases*, driving the reaction more aggressively. This is the Nernst foundation of corrosion thermodynamics. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) **Statement:** PBR quantifies whether the oxide layer formed on a metal will be protective or non-protective. ### Step 1: Define mass and molar volume relationships Consider oxide formation: $$ xM + \frac{y}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow M_x\text{O}_y $$ ### Step 2: Volume of metal consumed $$ V_M = \frac{x \cdot A_M}{\rho_M} $$ where $A_M$ = atomic mass of metal, $\rho_M$ = density. ### Step 3: Volume of oxide formed $$ V_{oxide} = \frac{(x A_M + y A_O)}{\rho_{oxide}} $$ where $A_O = 16$ g/mol. ### Step 4: Form the ratio $$ \boxed{\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_M} = \frac{(x A_M + y A_O)\cdot \rho_M}{x \cdot A_M \cdot \rho_{oxide}}} $$ ### Step 5: Numerical Example — Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃) - $A_{Al} = 27$, $\rho_{Al} = 2.7\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $x = 2$ - $A_O = 16$, $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.95\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $y = 3$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PBR}_{Al_2O_3} &= \frac{(2 \times 27 + 3 \times 16) \times 2.7}{2 \times 27 \times 3.95} \\ &= \frac{(54 + 48) \times 2.7}{54 \times 3.95} \\ &= \frac{102 \times 2.7}{213.3} = \frac{275.4}{213.3} \approx 1.29 \end{aligned} $$ **PBR ≈ 1.29 → lies between 1 and 2 → PROTECTIVE layer** ✓ (this is precisely why Al is "passive" against further corrosion). ### Step 6: PBR for Fe₂O₃ on Iron - $A_{Fe} = 56$, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.87$, $x = 2$ - $\rho_{Fe_2O_3} = 5.24$, $y = 3$ $$ \text{PBR}_{Fe_2O_3} = \frac{(2 \times 56 + 3 \times 16) \times 7.87}{2 \times 56 \times 5.24} = \frac{160 \times 7.87}{586.88} = \frac{1259.2}{586.88} \approx 2.15 $$ **PBR ≈ 2.15 > 2 → NON-PROTECTIVE, spalls off** (rust flakes off, exposing fresh iron — endless corrosion). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law **Statement:** Mass loss of a corroding metal can be related to the current density of the corrosion cell. ### Step 1: Faraday's Law of Electrolysis $$ m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot A_M}{n \cdot F} $$ where $m$ = mass of metal dissolved (g), $I$ = anodic current (A), $t$ = time (s). ### Step 2: Define current density $$ i = \frac{I}{A} \Rightarrow I = i \cdot A $$ where $A$ is the exposed area in cm². ### Step 3: Substitute and define corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ $$ m = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot A_M}{n \cdot F} $$ ### Step 4: Convert to corrosion rate in mm/year $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CR (mm/yr)} &= \frac{m}{A \cdot \rho_M \cdot t} \times 10 \quad \text{(unit conversion)} \\ &= \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A_M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_M} \times (3.154 \times 10^9) \quad \text{where } i_{corr} \text{ in A/cm}^2 \\ &\approx \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot A_M}{n \cdot \rho_M} \quad \text{(mm/yr)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **The constant 0.00327** emerges from converting seconds-per-year ($3.154 \times 10^7$) and cm³/mm³ ($10^{-3}$). Memorize this number for board problems. ### Step 5: Worked Example A steel surface ($A_M = 55.85$, $\rho = 7.87$, $n = 2$) corrodes with $i_{corr} = 10\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$. $$ \text{CR} = \frac{0.00327 \times (10 \times 10^{-6}) \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87} = \frac{1.826 \times 10^{-6}}{15.74} = 1.16 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{mm/yr} $$ Wait — this is unrealistically low. Let's recompute with a realistic $i_{corr} = 100\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$ (typical for active corrosion): $$ \text{CR} = \frac{0.00327 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mm/yr} $$ For heavily corroding systems ($i_{corr} \sim 1\ \text{mA/cm}^2$): $\text{CR} \approx 0.116\ \text{mm/yr}$ — realistic and actionable. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: EMF of Galvanic Couple — Why Small Anodes Fail Catastrophically ### Step 1: Total corrosion current in a galvanic cell The total current is governed by the mixed-potential theory: $$ I = \frac{E^\circ_c - E^\circ_a}{R_e + R_m} $$ where $R_e$ = electrolyte resistance, $R_m$ = metallic path resistance. ### Step 2: Current density at the anode $$ i_a = \frac{I}{A_a} = \frac{E^\circ_c - E^\circ_a}{A_a (R_e + R_m)} $$ ### Step 3: The Key Insight — Inverting the anode area If $A_a$ is very small (e.g., a rivet, a scratch, a coating defect) and $A_c$ is large, then $i_a$ becomes **enormous**, causing intense localized attack → **pitting corrosion**. ### Step 4: Numerical comparison Suppose $E^\circ_c - E^\circ_a = 0.5\ \text{V}$, $R_e + R_m = 100\ \Omega$. | Case | $A_a$ (cm²) | $i_a$ (A/cm²) | Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | Equal areas | 10 | 0.0005 | Mild general attack | | Small anode (scratch) | 0.01 | **0.5** | **Severe pitting** | This single mathematical relationship is **why a small scratch on coated steel under salt spray can perforate the sheet in weeks**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Electrochemical Corrosion Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture This Mermaid schematic maps the complete interaction topology of an electrochemical corrosion cell, equivalent in clarity to a textbook hand-drawn figure. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[ANODE
Metal Surface
M dissolves to M^n+] nodeB[ELECTROLYTE
Aqueous ionic medium
H2O with dissolved ions] nodeC[CATHODE
Inert region
Reduction site] nodeD[METALLIC PATH
Electron conductor
The metal body itself] nodeE[ANODIC REACTION
Fe -- Fe2+ + 2e-
Metal ion enters solution] nodeF[CATHODIC REACTION
O2 + 2H2O + 4e- -- 4OH-
Oxygen reduction] nodeG[RUST FORMATION
Fe2+ + 2OH- -- FeOH2
4FeOH2 + O2 + 2H2O -- 4FeOH3
2FeOH3 -- Fe2O3 xH2O] nodeA -- oxidation --> nodeE nodeE -- releases electrons --> nodeD nodeD -- electron flow --> nodeC nodeC -- consumes electrons --> nodeF nodeF -- generates OH- --> nodeB nodeB -- supplies H2O and O2 --> nodeF nodeB -- carries Fe2+ ions --> nodeG nodeG -- deposits --> nodeA ``` **Interpretation:** - Solid arrows trace the **electron path** (through metal) and **ion path** (through electrolyte). - Rust forms at the **anode–electrolyte interface**, precipitating as Fe(OH)₂ → Fe(OH)₃ → hydrated Fe₂O₃. - The metallic path closes the circuit via the bulk metal — no external wire needed. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Classification Tree of Corrosion Types ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeRoot[CORROSION] nodeChem[CHEMICAL DRY CORROSION] nodeElec[ELECTROCHEMICAL WET CORROSION] nodeOx[Oxidation by O2] nodeSO2[Corrosion by SO2] nodeH2S[Corrosion by H2S] nodeOther[Other gases CO2 Cl2] nodeUni[Uniform General] nodeGal[Galvanic] nodePit[Pitting] nodeCrev[Crevice] nodeIG[Intergranular] nodeSCC[Stress Corrosion Cracking] nodeEro[Erosion] nodeHE[Hydrogen Embrittlement] nodeRoot --> nodeChem nodeRoot --> nodeElec nodeChem --> nodeOx nodeChem --> nodeSO2 nodeChem --> nodeH2S nodeChem --> nodeOther nodeElec --> nodeUni nodeElec --> nodeGal nodeElec --> nodePit nodeElec --> nodeCrev nodeElec --> nodeIG nodeElec --> nodeSCC nodeElec --> nodeEro nodeElec --> nodeHE ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Galvanic Corrosion Mechanism (Two-Metal Couple) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeZn[ZINC ANODE
Eo = minus 0.763 V
More active
CORRODES] nodeFe[IRON CATHODE
Eo = minus 0.440 V
More noble
PROTECTED] nodeSalt[SALT WATER ELECTROLYTE
NaCl solution
Ion conductor] nodeWire[METALLIC CONTACT
Direct physical coupling] nodeAnodeR[Anodic reaction:
Zn -- Zn2+ + 2e-] nodeCatR[Cathodic reaction:
O2 + 2H2O + 4e- -- 4OH-] nodeZn -- gives up electrons --> nodeWire nodeWire -- electrons flow to --> nodeFe nodeFe -- accepts electrons --> nodeCatR nodeCatR -- consumes O2 --> nodeSalt nodeSalt -- supplies H2O and O2 --> nodeCatR nodeZn -- releases Zn2+ --> nodeAnodeR nodeAnodeR -- enters --> nodeSalt ``` **Engineering Insight:** This is the principle of **cathodic protection by sacrificial anodes** used in ship hulls, buried pipelines, and offshore platforms — zinc blocks are bolted onto steel to deliberately corrode and protect the structural steel. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Pitting Corrosion — Autocatalytic Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart[Initial Surface Defect
or Coating Break] nodeAnode[Tiny Anode at Pit Bottom
Metal dissolves M -- Mn+ + ne-] nodeCathode[Large Cathode Area
Surrounding intact surface] nodeO2Red[O2 reduction:
O2 + 2H2O + 4e- -- 4OH-] nodeClMig[Cl minus ions migrate into pit
for charge neutrality] nodeMCl[Metal chloride MCln
hydrolyses] nodeAcid[Hydrolysis produces H+
pH drops inside pit 1 to 3] nodeAccel[Acidic + concentrated Cl
accelerates further anodic dissolution] nodeSelf[Autocatalytic Self-Propagation
Pit grows deeper and deeper] nodeStart --> nodeAnode nodeAnode --> nodeClMig nodeClMig --> nodeMCl nodeMCl --> nodeAcid nodeAcid --> nodeAccel nodeAccel --> nodeSelf nodeCathode --> nodeO2Red nodeO2Red -- supplies OH minus --> nodeSelf ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitting is self-accelerating** because the pit interior becomes acidic (pH can fall to 1–2) while the surrounding cathodic area stays near-neutral. This is why pitting is the **most dangerous and unpredictable** form of corrosion in chemical plants and marine structures. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define corrosion. Distinguish between chemical (dry) and electrochemical (wet) corrosion.** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** Corrosion is the gradual deterioration of metals by chemical or electrochemical reactions with their environment, converting the metal back to its thermodynamically stable oxide form. **Distinction (2 marks):** | Feature | Chemical (Dry) Corrosion | Electrochemical (Wet) Corrosion | |---|---|---| | Medium | Direct contact with gases (O₂, SO₂, H₂S) at high temperature | Presence of aqueous electrolyte (moisture, salt solution) | | Mechanism | Direct chemical reaction, no charge transfer across phases | Involves anodic dissolution + cathodic reduction with ion transport | | Examples | Oxidation of iron at high T, sulfidation in refineries | Rusting of iron in humid air, corrosion of marine structures | | Electrochemical cell | Absent | Present (anode, cathode, electrolyte, metallic path) | --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is Pilling–Bedworth ratio? How does it determine the protective nature of an oxide film?** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) is the ratio of the volume of the oxide formed on a metal to the volume of the metal consumed in producing that oxide. $$ \text{PBR} = \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal consumed}}} $$ **Protective Behaviour (2 marks):** - **PBR < 1** → Oxide volume is less than metal volume; the film is porous and non-protective (e.g., Mg, Ca — alkali metals form powdery oxides). - **1 < PBR < 2** → Oxide volume slightly exceeds metal volume; the film is **continuous, adherent and protective** (e.g., Al, Cr, Ti — these are the "self-passivating" metals). - **PBR > 2** → Oxide volume far exceeds metal volume; the film is bulky, cracks and spalls off (e.g., Fe forms flaky rust — the most cited failure case). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Both options provided below.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams and relevant equations, explain the **electrochemical mechanism of wet corrosion of iron in neutral aqueous medium** (oxygen absorption type). Derive the overall rust formation reaction. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the mechanism, characteristic features, and preventive measures of **pitting corrosion** in stainless steels. Why is pitting considered the most dangerous form of localized corrosion? --- ### **Model Answer — Question A (a)** **Step 1: Setup the electrochemical cell (2 marks)** Wet corrosion of iron in neutral aerated water requires the four components: - **Anode:** Iron surface (active sites) - **Cathode:** Adjacent iron surface (impurity sites like Fe₃C, pearlite, or oxide inclusions) - **Electrolyte:** Thin film of moisture containing dissolved O₂ and CO₂ - **Metallic path:** The bulk iron body itself **Step 2: Anodic half-reaction (1 mark)** $$ \text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- $$ Iron is oxidized from its metallic (zero) state to ferrous (+2) ions, which dissolve into the moisture film. **Step 3: Cathodic half-reaction (1 mark)** — Oxygen absorption in neutral medium: $$ \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^- $$ Dissolved oxygen accepts the electrons released at the anode. This is the **rate-determining step** because O₂ solubility in water is low. **Step 4: Combine the two half-reactions (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Anode (×2):} \quad & 2\text{Fe} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe}^{2+} + 4e^- \\ \text{Cathode:} \quad & \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^- \\ \text{Overall:} \quad & 2\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe(OH)}_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Further oxidation to rust (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Intermediate:} \quad & 4\text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3 \\ \text{Dehydration:} \quad & 2\text{Fe(OH)}_3 \longrightarrow \underbrace{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot x\text{H}_2\text{O}}_{\text{Hydrated ferric oxide — RUST}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Diagram and concluding statement (1 mark)** Refer to Section 4.1 diagram. Conclude with: "Rust is non-protective because PBR > 2, hence iron continues to corrode." --- ### **Model Answer — Question A (b)** **Step 1: Definition and occurrence (1 mark)** Pitting corrosion is a form of highly localized attack producing deep, narrow cavities (pits) on otherwise passive metal surfaces. It is most commonly observed on **stainless steels (especially SS-304, SS-316)** in **chloride-containing environments** (marine, deicing salts, bleach). **Step 2: Initiation mechanism (2 marks)** Pitting initiates at microscopic surface heterogeneities: - Scratches, surface roughness - Inclusions (MnS in stainless steels are notorious initiation sites) - Coating defects - Localized breakdown of the passive Cr₂O₃ film The **critical pitting potential (E_pit)** must be exceeded for pit nucleation. **Step 3: Autocatalytic propagation mechanism (2 marks)** (refer Section 4.4 diagram) - Inside pit (anode): Metal dissolves → M^n+ + ne⁻ - Cl⁻ ions migrate into pit to maintain charge neutrality → MClₙ forms. - Hydrolysis: MClₙ + H₂O → M(OH)ₙ + HCl (pH drops to 1–2 inside pit). - Aggressive acidic + chloride-rich environment accelerates further dissolution. - Cathodic reaction (O₂ reduction) occurs on the surrounding large passive surface. **Step 4: Characteristic features (1 mark)** - Pit depth-to-width ratio > 1 (deep, narrow holes) - Small anode:large cathode area ratio → extremely high anodic current density - Pits often covered by a semi-permeable cap of corrosion product - Penetration rate can be **mm per month**, leading to perforation. **Step 5: Preventive measures (1 mark)** - Use **Mo-containing stainless steels** (SS-316, 2–3% Mo raises pitting resistance). - Maintain **PREN** (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number) > 25 for marine service: PREN = %Cr + 3.3×%Mo + 16×%N. - Avoid stagnant chloride solutions, ensure proper draining. - Cathodic protection, controlled hydrodynamics. - Avoid surface contamination during fabrication. **Conclusion:** Pitting is dangerous because it is **visually undetectable** (small surface opening hiding massive subsurface cavity), can lead to **catastrophic perforation** of pressure vessels or pipelines, and is **autocatalytic** — once started, it accelerates. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the mechanism of **galvanic corrosion**. Discuss the factors that influence the rate and severity of galvanic corrosion, giving two real-world engineering examples. **(b) [7 Marks]** Define **intergranular corrosion**. Explain **weld decay in austenitic stainless steel (SS-304)** with reference to chromium carbide precipitation, sensitization, and remedies. --- ### **Model Answer — Question B (a)** **Step 1: Definition (1 mark)** Galvanic corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals with different electrode potentials are placed in electrical contact in the presence of an electrolyte. The more active (anodic) metal corrodes preferentially while the more noble (cathodic) metal is protected. **Step 2: Mechanism with diagram (2 marks)** — refer to Section 4.3 diagram. For a Zn–Fe couple in salt water: - **Anode (Zn):** Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (zinc dissolves) - **Cathode (Fe):** O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (oxygen reduced on iron) - **Electron flow:** From Zn through metallic contact to Fe - **Ion flow:** Zn²⁺ enters electrolyte; OH⁻ migrates towards anode - **Net product:** White Zn(OH)₂ / ZnO deposit near the joint **Step 3: Factors influencing severity (2 marks)** | Factor | Effect on Galvanic Corrosion Rate | |---|---| | **Electrode potential difference (ΔE)** | Larger ΔE → stronger driving force → faster corrosion | | **Relative area (anode:cathode)** | Small anode / large cathode → extremely high anodic current density → severe localized attack | | **Electrolyte conductivity** | Higher conductivity → greater current flow → faster corrosion (seawater is more aggressive than fresh water) | | **Temperature** | Higher T → faster kinetics → faster corrosion | | **Polarization behaviour** | Cathodic polarization (e.g., H₂ overpotential) can reduce severity | | **Distance between metals** | Closer the couple → less electrolyte resistance → more current | **Step 4: Engineering examples (2 marks)** 1. **Ship hull protection** — Zinc sacrificial anodes bolted to steel hull. Zinc (E° = −0.76 V) corrodes preferentially over steel (E° = −0.44 V), protecting the hull. Replaced periodically. 2. **Buried pipelines** — Coupled to Mg anodes via impressed-current or sacrificial-anode cathodic protection systems. Prevents external soil-side corrosion of carbon steel pipes. 3. *(Bonus)* Copper-aluminium joints in plumbing → galvanic corrosion if not insulated with dielectric unions. --- ### **Model Answer — Question B (b)** **Step 1: Definition (1 mark)** Intergranular corrosion (IGC) is a localized attack in which the corrosion proceeds preferentially along the grain boundaries of an alloy, while the bulk grains remain relatively unattacked. The metal often loses its mechanical integrity and fractures intergranularly under minimal stress. **Step 2: Weld decay in SS-304 (3 marks)** SS-304 contains ~18% Cr and ~8% Ni. When heated to **450–850 °C** (e.g., during welding — the HAZ "heat-affected zone"), chromium reacts with carbon (typically 0.05–0.08%) to precipitate **chromium carbide Cr₂₃C₆** along the grain boundaries: $$ 23\text{Cr} + 6\text{C} \longrightarrow \text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6 $$ This depletes the region adjacent to the grain boundary of chromium, dropping it **below the critical 12% Cr required for passive film formation**. The chromium-depleted zones become **anodic** and preferentially attacked by corrosive media → intergranular corrosion. This phenomenon is called **sensitization**, and the resulting failure mode is called **weld decay** (because the HAZ is the most affected). **Step 3: Mechanism outline (1 mark)** ``` Grain interior | Cr-depleted zone (~10% Cr) | Grain boundary Cathode | Anode | Cr23C6 ppt Protected | Dissolves | Inert ``` **Step 4: Remedies (2 marks)** 1. **Reduce carbon content below 0.03%** → Use **SS-304L (Low Carbon)** grade → insufficient C for carbide formation. 2. **Add carbide stabilizers** → Use **SS-321 (Ti-stabilized)** or **SS-347 (Nb-stabilized)**. Ti and Nb have higher affinity for C than Cr, forming TiC/NbC preferentially, leaving Cr in solution to maintain passivity. 3. **Solution annealing** at 1050–1100 °C followed by rapid quenching → dissolves carbides back into the matrix. 4. Avoid prolonged exposure to sensitization temperature range (450–850 °C). --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Corrosion Questions (as observed in KTU valuation scripts):** > > 1. **Confusing the oxygen absorption type** — Students write the acidic medium equation (O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂O) when the question demands the neutral medium equation (O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻). **−2 marks penalty.** > 2. **Forgetting to state "Rust is hydrated Fe₂O₃·xH₂O"** — Examiners allocate one full mark for this exact phrase in any rusting question. > 3. **Not labeling the anode/cathode clearly in diagrams** — A diagram without explicit labels earns zero marks on the figure question. > 4. **Galvanic corrosion: writing "more reactive" instead of "more anodic / more active"** — Use the proper electrochemical vocabulary. > 5. **Pilling–Bedworth: not stating the critical range "1 < PBR < 2"** — Students quote PBR < 1 or PBR > 2 cases but skip the protective range. > 6. **Intergranular corrosion: not mentioning "chromium depletion below 12%"** — This is the precise mechanism; vague answers lose 2–3 marks. > 7. **In Nernst equation derivation, not converting ln to log₁₀** — Final answer must be in the form **E = E° − (0.0591/n) log Q**. > 8. **Not balancing the overall cell reaction** — Charge and mass balance are checked strictly. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Fire High-Yield Facts (must-memorize):** > > - Rust = **Fe₂O₃·xH₂O** (hydrated ferric oxide), NOT pure Fe₂O₃. > - Two universal laws: **(1) Anode always dissolves.** **(2) Cathode is always protected.** > - Anodic reaction (universal): **M → Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻**. > - Three cathodic reactions: **Hydrogen evolution (acidic, no O₂)**, **O₂ absorption neutral/alkaline (O₂ + 2H₂O)**, **O₂ absorption acidic (O₂ + 4H⁺)**. > - Pilling–Bedworth protective range: **1 < PBR < 2**. > - Galvanic corrosion: severity ↑ with **increasing ΔE**, **decreasing anode area**, **increasing electrolyte conductivity**. > - **Pitting corrosion**: most dangerous — small opening, deep penetration, autocatalytic. > - **Weld decay** in SS-304: caused by **Cr₂₃C₆** precipitation in 450–850 °C HAZ → Cr depletion below 12%. > - Solutions: **SS-304L** (low C), **SS-321** (Ti), **SS-347** (Nb). > - **Caustic embrittlement** = SCC of boiler steel due to NaOH + residual stress. > - **Cathodic protection** by sacrificial anodes (Zn, Mg, Al) protects steel structures. > - **Nernst equation at 25°C:** E = E° − (0.0591/n) log₁₀ Q. > - **Faraday's corrosion rate constant** for mm/yr: **0.00327**. > - **PREN** = %Cr + 3.3×%Mo + 16×%N; for marine service PREN > 25. > - **Stress corrosion cracking** requires 3 simultaneous conditions: susceptible alloy + specific corrodent + sustained tensile stress. > - **Hydrogen embrittlement** caused by atomic H entering metal lattice → loss of ductility, brittle fracture. > - **Erosion corrosion** accelerated by high fluid velocity + suspended particles + corrosive medium. > - **Crevice corrosion** occurs in stagnant zones (gaskets, lap joints, under deposits).

topics.title: Corrosion Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Prevention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Corrosion Prevention

**Corrosion Prevention** refers to the systematic application of engineering methods, material modifications, and electrochemical techniques employed to suppress, retard, or completely eliminate the degradation of metals caused by chemical or electrochemical interaction with their surrounding environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Corrosion prevention encompasses the strategic selection of materials, application of protective coatings, electrochemical control (cathodic and anodic protection), use of chemical inhibitors, and design optimization to minimize the thermodynamic driving force and kinetic rate of corrosion reactions.

In more rigorous thermodynamic terms, corrosion prevention involves shifting the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) of a metal to a region where the anodic dissolution reaction is kinetically impeded, or alternatively, by physically isolating the metal from the corrosive electrolyte.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Raincoat for Your Body**
>
> Imagine your skin is made of metal. When you step out into a rainstorm (corrosive environment), the raindrops (electrolyte + oxygen) slowly wear you down. Corrosion prevention is like wearing:
> - A **raincoat** (Coatings: paint, galvanizing, polymer films)
> - An **umbrella** (Design modifications: avoiding water pooling)
> - A **chemical spray that repels water** (Inhibitors: chromates, phosphates)
> - A **grounded wire draining the harmful charge** (Cathodic Protection: sacrificial anodes)
> - A **completely different skin material** (Material Selection: stainless steel instead of mild steel)
>
> Just as no single method is perfect for every storm, the engineer must choose the most economical and effective combination for a given environment.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

The economic impact of corrosion is staggering. Studies estimate that **3–5% of a nation's GDP** is lost annually due to corrosion and its associated maintenance. Effective corrosion prevention:
- **Extends asset life** of bridges, pipelines, ships, and aircraft.
- **Ensures public safety** by preventing catastrophic structural failures.
- **Reduces environmental contamination** from leaking tanks and pipes.
- **Conserves natural resources** by reducing the need for metal replacement.

The key physical constant involved in electrochemical corrosion prevention is **Faraday's Constant, F = 96,485 C/mol** (often approximated as **96,500 C/mol** in engineering calculations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Corrosion Prevention Methods

Corrosion prevention strategies are broadly classified into **five principal categories** based on their mechanism of action:

### 2.1.1 Material Selection and Alloying
- The most fundamental approach is to choose a metal or alloy that is inherently resistant to the specific service environment.
- **Examples:** Stainless steels (Fe-Cr-Ni) for oxidizing environments, Monel (Ni-Cu) for hydrofluoric acid, Titanium for seawater, Inconel for high-temperature oxidation.
- **Alloying for passivation:** Adding chromium (>10.5%) to iron forms a stable, self-healing passive Cr₂O₃ film.

### 2.1.2 Protective Coatings
Coatings act as a **physical barrier** between the metal substrate and the corrosive medium.

| Type | Examples | Mechanism |
|------|----------|-----------|
| **Metallic (Anodic)** | Galvanizing (Zn on Fe), Cadmium plating | Sacrificial dissolution protects base metal |
| **Metallic (Cathodic)** | Tinning (Sn on Fe), Chrome plating, Ag plating | Noble metal barrier; fails if scratched |
| **Organic** | Paints, varnishes, epoxies, polyurethanes | Physical barrier + sometimes inhibitive pigments |
| **Inorganic** | Vitreous enamel, cement coatings, chromate conversion | Chemical conversion + barrier |
| **Conversion Coatings** | Anodizing (Al), Phosphating, Bluing (steel) | Surface chemistry modification |

### 2.1.3 Cathodic Protection (CP)
This is the **most industrially significant** electrochemical method. The metal to be protected is made the **cathode** in an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing the anodic dissolution reaction.

- **Method 1 — Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) CP:** A more active metal (e.g., **Zn, Mg, Al**) is electrically connected to the structure. The anode preferentially corrodes.
- **Method 2 — Impressed Current CP (ICCP):** An external DC power source drives current from an inert anode (e.g., graphite, Ti-MMO) to the structure, forcing it cathodic.

### 2.1.4 Anodic Protection (AP)
The opposite of cathodic protection. The metal is forced into the **passive region** of its anodic polarization curve by applying a controlled positive potential. Only applicable to metals that exhibit passivity (e.g., stainless steel in H₂SO₄, Fe in concentrated HNO₃).

### 2.1.5 Corrosion Inhibitors
Chemical substances added in small concentrations to the corrosive environment to reduce the corrosion rate.

- **Anodic Inhibitors:** Chromates, nitrites, molybdates — form protective films on anodic sites.
- **Cathodic Inhibitors:** Polyphosphates, zinc salts — suppress cathodic reaction.
- **Mixed Inhibitors:** Amines, mercaptans, benzotriazole — adsorb on metal surface.
- **Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI):** Dicyclohexylamine nitrite — used in packaging.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EQUATIONS FOR KTU BOARD EXAMS** — Memorize the units carefully!

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (mass of metal reacted) | W in g, I in A, t in s |
| 2 | $CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate in mils per year | D in g/cm³, A in in², T in hr |
| 3 | $CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate in mm/year | D in g/cm³, A in cm², T in hr |
| 4 | $I_{req} = i_c \cdot A$ | Required cathodic protection current | i_c in A/m², A in m² |
| 5 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | Nernst Equation (25 °C) | E in Volts |
| 6 | $\eta = \beta \log \dfrac{i}{i_0}$ | Tafel Equation (overpotential) | η in V |
| 7 | $\text{Efficiency} (\%) = \dfrac{W_{o} - W_{i}}{W_{o}} \times 100$ | Inhibitor/Coating Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_p = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr}(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Linear Polarization Resistance (Stern-Geary) | Ω·cm² |

**Key Constants:**
- **Faraday's Constant: F = 96,485 C/mol ≈ 96,500 C/mol**
- **1 Faraday = Charge on 1 mole of electrons = 1 mole of Ag deposited from Ag⁺**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Underground pipelines:** Cathodic protection with Mg anodes; cost-effective for cross-country oil/gas lines.
- **Marine structures:** Impressed current CP with Ti-MMO anodes for ship hulls and offshore platforms.
- **Storage tanks:** Internal coatings + cathodic protection for crude oil tanks.
- **Reinforced concrete:** Cathodic prevention using Ti anodes embedded in concrete to stop rebar corrosion.
- **Chemical industry:** Anodic protection for sulfuric acid storage tanks (carbon steel with AP is 5x cheaper than stainless steel).
- **Boilers and cooling towers:** Phosphonate and zinc-based inhibitors in water treatment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's Law for Cathodic Protection

Faraday's First Law states that the mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

**Starting with the fundamental relationship:**

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

This is the total charge in Coulombs. The number of moles of electrons transferred is:

$$n_{e^-} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

For a metal undergoing the reaction $M^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow M$, **1 mole of metal requires n moles of electrons**. Therefore, the number of moles of metal deposited or dissolved is:

$$n_{M} = \dfrac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

The mass of metal reacted is obtained by multiplying by the atomic mass M:

$$W = n_M \cdot M = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

This is the working equation for **all cathodic protection current calculations** in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Cathodic Protection Current Requirement

**Problem:** A buried steel pipeline of length 50 km and diameter 60 cm is to be protected using the sacrificial anode method. The required cathodic current density is $i_c = 10 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.010 \text{ A/m}^2$. Calculate the total current required and the number of magnesium anodes needed, if each anode can deliver 0.5 A.

**Step 1: Calculate the surface area to be protected**

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.60 \text{ m} \times 50{,}000 \text{ m}$$

$$A = 3.1416 \times 30{,}000 = 94{,}248 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total current required**

$$I_{req} = i_c \cdot A = 0.010 \text{ A/m}^2 \times 94{,}248 \text{ m}^2$$

$$I_{req} = 942.48 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the number of anodes needed**

$$N = \dfrac{I_{req}}{I_{anode}} = \dfrac{942.48}{0.5} = 1885 \text{ anodes}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Corrosion Rate Calculation (Faraday's Law)

**Problem:** A zinc specimen of area 10 cm² was exposed to a corrosive solution for 30 days. The weight loss was 0.8 g. Calculate the corrosion current density. (Given: Density of Zn = 7.13 g/cm³, M = 65.4 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,500 C/mol)

**Step 1: Convert time to seconds**

$$t = 30 \text{ days} = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2{,}592{,}000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Apply Faraday's Law to find corrosion current I_corr**

From $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$, rearranging:

$$I_{corr} = \dfrac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{t \cdot M}$$

$$I_{corr} = \dfrac{0.8 \times 2 \times 96{,}500}{2{,}592{,}000 \times 65.4}$$

$$I_{corr} = \dfrac{154{,}400}{169{,}516{,}800} = 9.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate current density**

$$i_{corr} = \dfrac{I_{corr}}{A} = \dfrac{9.11 \times 10^{-4}}{10 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.911 \text{ A/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Calculate corrosion rate in mpy for verification**

$$CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

With T = 30 × 24 = 720 hr, A = 10 cm² = 1.55 in²:

$$CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \times 0.8}{7.13 \times 1.55 \times 720} = \dfrac{427.2}{7957.5} = 0.0537 \text{ mpy}$$

This is a very low corrosion rate, indicating excellent service conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cathodic vs Anodic Protection

| Parameter | Cathodic Protection (CP) | Anodic Protection (AP) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Driving Force** | Suppresses anodic reaction by external current | Drives metal into passive region |
| **Current Direction** | Current flows INTO the protected metal | Current flows OUT OF the protected metal |
| **Applicable Metals** | All metals (especially active ones) | Only passivating metals (Cr, Ni, Ti, SS) |
| **Current Required** | High (mA/m² to A/m²) | Very low (μA/m² range) |
| **Electrolyte** | Soil, seawater, neutral solutions | Aggressive acids (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄) |
| **Application** | Pipelines, ships, tanks, rebar | Sulfuric acid tanks, chemical reactors |
| **Equipment** | Sacrificial anodes OR rectifier + inert anode | Potentiostat + reference electrode + cathode |
| **Risk if Power Fails** | Rapid corrosion resumes | Slow drift out of passive region |
| **Cost** | Lower capital cost | Higher capital cost, lower operating cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define cathodic protection. Differentiate between sacrificial anode and impressed current cathodic protection methods.**

**Model Answer:**

**Cathodic Protection (CP):** It is an electrochemical method of corrosion prevention in which the metal to be protected is made the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing its anodic dissolution reaction. This is achieved by supplying electrons from an external source.

| Feature | Sacrificial Anode CP | Impressed Current CP |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Driving Force | Galvanic potential difference | External DC rectifier |
| Current Source | Active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) | DC power supply |
| Anode | Consumed (sacrificed) | Inert (graphite, Ti-MMO) |
| Best for | Small, localized structures | Large, long pipelines |
| Voltage | Self-generated (~0.2–0.5 V) | Adjustable (up to 50 V) |
| Cost | Lower installation, higher anode replacement | Higher installation, low operating |

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What are corrosion inhibitors? Classify them with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Corrosion Inhibitors** are chemical substances added in small concentrations (typically 1–500 ppm) to a corrosive environment to reduce the rate of metal dissolution without significantly changing the concentration of the bulk electrolyte.

**Classification:**

1. **Anodic Inhibitors** — Form protective oxide films on anodic sites. *Examples: Chromates (CrO₄²⁻), Nitrites (NO₂⁻), Molybdates (MoO₄²⁻).*

2. **Cathodic Inhibitors** — Suppress cathodic reaction by forming insoluble precipitates. *Examples: Polyphosphates, Zinc salts (ZnSO₄), Calcium bicarbonate.*

3. **Mixed Inhibitors** — Adsorb on the metal surface, blocking both anodic and cathodic sites. *Examples: Amines, Mercaptans, Benzotriazole, Thiourea.*

4. **Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI)** — Volatile compounds that protect metals in enclosed spaces. *Examples: Dicyclohexylamine nitrite (DICHAN).*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the sacrificial anode method of cathodic protection. Mention the criteria for selecting the sacrificial anode material. **7 Marks**

**(b)** A buried pipeline of length 2 km and diameter 25 cm is protected by impressed current cathodic protection. The minimum current density required is 15 mA/m². If a rectifier of 30 V and 25 A is available, calculate the number of rectifiers required and the daily power consumption in kWh. Assume rectifier efficiency = 80%. **7 Marks**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Sacrificial Anode Method:**

In this method, a more electrochemically active metal (the anode) is electrically connected to the metal structure to be protected. The active metal preferentially corrodes, sacrificing itself to protect the structure.

**Schematic:**
```
    Zn (Anode) ——————— Fe (Cathode/Structure)
         \                  /
          \                /
        Soil/Electrolyte
   Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (Oxidation at anode)
   O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (Reduction on Fe)
```

**Criteria for selecting sacrificial anode material:**

1. **More negative electrode potential** than the protected metal (e.g., Zn, Mg, Al are more active than Fe).
2. **High electrochemical capacity** (ampere-hours per kg).
3. **Uniform corrosion behavior** (no localized pitting).
4. **High current efficiency** (most of the current goes into protective action).
5. **Low cost and easy availability.**
6. **Non-toxic and environmentally safe.**

Common materials: **Magnesium (most active, used in high-resistivity soils), Zinc (used in seawater), Aluminum alloys (marine applications).**

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Surface area of pipeline**

$$A = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.25 \times 2000 = 1570.8 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Total current required**

$$I_{req} = i_c \times A = 0.015 \times 1570.8 = 23.56 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Number of rectifiers required**

Since each rectifier provides 25 A:

$$N = \dfrac{I_{req}}{I_{rectifier}} = \dfrac{23.56}{25} = 0.94 \approx 1 \text{ rectifier}$$

**Step 4: Daily power consumption**

Power output required:
$$P_{out} = V \times I = 30 \times 23.56 = 706.8 \text{ W}$$

Considering 80% efficiency, power input:
$$P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{0.80} = \dfrac{706.8}{0.80} = 883.5 \text{ W}$$

Daily energy:
$$E = P_{in} \times 24 \text{ hr} = 883.5 \times 24 = 21{,}204 \text{ Wh} = 21.2 \text{ kWh}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is anodic protection? Explain its working principle with a polarization diagram. Mention its advantages and limitations. **7 Marks**

**(b)** A zinc plate of 20 cm × 15 cm area was exposed to a 0.1 N H₂SO₄ solution for 8 hours. The weight loss observed was 0.5 g. Calculate the corrosion current and corrosion current density. Given: Atomic weight of Zn = 65.4, Valency = 2, Density = 7.13 g/cm³. **7 Marks**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Anodic Protection:**

**Definition:** Anodic protection is an electrochemical corrosion prevention technique in which the metal to be protected is made the **anode** and its potential is controlled within the **passive region** of its anodic polarization curve using a potentiostat.

**Working Principle (Polarization Diagram):**
The anodic polarization curve of a passivating metal has three regions:
- **Active region (A→B):** Rapid metal dissolution.
- **Passive region (B→C):** Protective oxide film forms; current drops to a low value ($i_{pass}$).
- **Transpassive region (C→D):** Film breaks down; oxygen evolution or pitting.

The potentiostat holds the metal at a fixed potential within the **passive region (B–C)**, where a thin, adherent, self-healing oxide film (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel) forms, drastically reducing corrosion rate.

**Advantages:**
1. Extremely low current requirement (μA range).
2. Ideal for highly aggressive acids (H₂SO₄, HNO₃).
3. Can be applied to complex geometries.
4. Long life — passive film is self-healing.

**Limitations:**
1. Applicable only to passivating metals (Cr, Ni, Ti, SS).
2. Cannot be used in non-conductive or low-conductivity media.
3. High capital cost (potentiostat + reference electrode).
4. Risk of accelerated corrosion if potential drifts into active or transpassive region.

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert time to seconds**

$$t = 8 \text{ hr} = 8 \times 3600 = 28{,}800 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Area of zinc plate**

$$A = 20 \times 15 = 300 \text{ cm}^2 = 300 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.03 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law to find corrosion current**

From $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$:

$$I = \dfrac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{t \cdot M} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 2 \times 96{,}500}{28{,}800 \times 65.4}$$

$$I = \dfrac{96{,}500}{1{,}883{,}520} = 0.0512 \text{ A} = 51.2 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4: Calculate corrosion current density**

$$i_{corr} = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{0.0512}{0.03} = 1.71 \text{ A/m}^2$$

**Step 5 (Optional): Verify by calculating corrosion rate in mm/yr**

$$CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 0.5}{7.13 \times 300 \times 8} = \dfrac{43.8}{17{,}112} = 2.56 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm/yr}$$

This is a very low rate, consistent with passivation behavior in H₂SO₄.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing seconds, hours, and days. Always convert time to the unit consistent with the formula used. Most KTU board answers lose 1–2 marks here.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Cathodic and Anodic Protection:** Students frequently mix up the current direction and potential shift. Memorize: **CP shifts potential negative; AP shifts potential positive (into passive region).**
> 
> 3. **Forgetting Rectifier Efficiency in Power Calculations:** If a rectifier efficiency of 80% is given, always compute $P_{in} = P_{out}/\eta$ before multiplying by time. Skipping this step costs 1 full mark.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Sign of Electrode Potential:** Sacrificial anodes must have a **more negative (more active)** electrode potential than the protected metal. Not "more positive."
> 
> 5. **Nernst Equation Constants:** Use **0.0591** only for **25 °C (298 K)**. For other temperatures, use $\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{0.0257 \cdot T(K)}{n}$ volts.
> 
> 6. **Sketching Mistakes in CP Diagrams:** Always label the **anode, cathode, electrolyte, electron flow, and ion flow** with arrows. Half-mark deductions are common for unlabeled diagrams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Quick Reference**

### ⚡ Core Concepts (Must Know)

- **Corrosion Prevention = Making the metal the cathode, OR isolating it from the environment, OR changing the environment.**
- The **5 main methods** are: **M**aterial selection, **C**oatings, **E**lectrochemical (CP/AP), **I**nhibitors, **D**esign.
- **Cathodic Protection** = Make the metal a **cathode** by supplying electrons.
- **Anodic Protection** = Make the metal an **anode** in the **passive region** (only for passivating metals).

### 📐 Master Formulas

- **Faraday's Law:** $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ with **F = 96,500 C/mol**
- **Corrosion Rate (mpy):** $CR = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ (D in g/cm³, A in in², T in hr)
- **Corrosion Rate (mm/yr):** $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ (D in g/cm³, A in cm², T in hr)
- **CP Current:** $I_{req} = i_c \times A$ (i_c typically 10–50 mA/m² for steel in soil)
- **Power:** $P = V \times I / \eta_{rectifier}$ (remember efficiency correction)

### 🧪 Material Specifications

- **Sacrificial Anodes:** Mg (–1.55 V, soil), Zn (–0.76 V, seawater), Al (–1.10 V, marine).
- **Inert Anodes (ICCP):** Graphite, high-silicon cast iron, **Ti-MMO** (mixed metal oxide).
- **Inhibitor Examples:** Chromates, nitrites, polyphosphates, amines, benzotriazole.

### 🎯 KTU Exam Traps

- Always check whether to use **F = 96,500** or **96,485** — both are accepted in KTU.
- **Galvanizing protects steel even if scratched** (because Zn is anodic). **Tinning fails if scratched** (because Sn is cathodic to Fe).
- **Anodic protection is NOT used in soil or seawater** — only in conductive acid solutions.
- For **part (a) sketch questions**, always show: **anode, cathode, electrolyte, electron path, ion path, and labels.**
- **Nernst equation temperature coefficient:** 0.0591 at 25 °C; for every 1 °C change, the factor changes by $0.000198/n$.

### 🔁 Memory Trick: "**MICE-D**"

- **M** — Material selection
- **I** — Inhibitors
- **C** — Coatings
- **E** — Electrochemical (CP & AP)
- **D** — Design modifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always include a **labeled diagram** (worth 2–3 marks), a **derivation** (worth 3–4 marks), and a **numerical** (worth 3–4 marks), with a **comparison table or conclusion** (worth 1–2 marks). Time management: spend ~10 min on part (a) and ~15 min on part (b) for a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inhibitors and their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Inhibitor:** A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations to an environment (typically 0.1% to 1% by weight), effectively **reduces the corrosion rate of a metal exposed to that environment** without significantly reacting with the bulk medium or altering the metal's bulk composition.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT412 — Material Science, Module 4: Corrosion), inhibitors are classified as one of the three principal **corrosion control methodologies**, alongside:
1. **Material selection** (choosing noble alloys)
2. **Design modification** (geometric and environmental isolation)
3. **Electrochemical protection** (inhibitors, coatings, cathodic/anodic protection)

The efficiency of an inhibitor is quantified by the formula:

$$\text{IE \%} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$$

where $CR_0$ is the corrosion rate without inhibitor and $CR_i$ is the corrosion rate with inhibitor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Inhibitors form a recurring **12-mark weightage** in Module 4, with emphasis placed on (a) classification, (b) mechanism of action, and (c) industrial applications such as cooling water systems, oil & gas pipelines, and acid pickling baths.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Medicine for Metals" Concept

Imagine a human body submerged in a hostile, acidic, oxygen-rich atmosphere (analogous to a metal in an electrolyte). The body begins to deteriorate — this is **corrosion**. Now, a doctor prescribes a small dose of **medicine** that travels through the bloodstream and protects the body's vital organs from decay. The medicine does not change the body itself; it merely forms a **protective shield** on the tissues and slows down the damage.

**A corrosion inhibitor works exactly this way:**

| Human Body Analogy | Corrosion Inhibitor Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hostile atmosphere (pollution, microbes) | Corrosive electrolyte (acid, salt water) |
| Body deterioration | Metal dissolution (anodic reaction) |
| Medicine | Corrosion inhibitor molecule |
| Shield on tissues | Protective film on metal surface |
| Dosage (mg) | Concentration (ppm or %) |
| Effectiveness period | Inhibitor longevity / reapplication interval |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Just as a single paracetamol tablet cannot cure a chronic disease, an inhibitor must be **continuously present** in the environment to maintain its protective effect. Once it is consumed (e.g., by adsorption-desorption cycles or chemical degradation), corrosion resumes.

## 1.3 Why Inhibitors? — The Engineering Need

In many engineering scenarios, alternative protection methods are impractical:

- **Cathodic protection** requires a sacrificial anode (impractical for moving equipment like ships' hulls in all conditions).
- **Coatings** fail in aggressive acid solutions used in industrial cleaning (pickling).
- **Material substitution** (e.g., switching to titanium) is prohibitively expensive for large-scale infrastructure like buried pipelines.

**Inhibitors** solve these problems by:
- Requiring **no structural modification** to the system.
- Working at **low concentrations** (cost-effective).
- Adapting to **complex geometries** (internal surfaces of boilers, distillation columns).
- Being **reversible** (can be removed if downstream processes require bare metal surfaces, e.g., before electroplating).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Corrosion Inhibitors

Inhibitors are classified along **three orthogonal axes** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### Axis 1: Electrochemical Mechanism
1. **Anodic Inhibitors** — suppress the anodic reaction ($M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$)
2. **Cathodic Inhibitors** — suppress the cathodic reaction ($O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ or $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$)
3. **Mixed Inhibitors** — suppress both reactions simultaneously

### Axis 2: Chemical Nature
1. **Inorganic inhibitors** — chromates, phosphates, nitrites, molybdates, arsenates
2. **Organic inhibitors** — amines, imidazolines, benzotriazoles, Schiff bases

### Axis 3: Physical State / Application Mode
1. **Liquid-phase inhibitors** — added directly to the corrosive medium
2. **Vapour-phase inhibitors (VPI)** — volatile solids that sublime and protect enclosed spaces
3. **Interfacial inhibitors** — film-forming compounds that adsorb on the metal

## 2.2 Anodic Inhibitors — Detailed Mechanism

Anodic inhibitors (also called **passivators**) act by **shifting the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) in the noble (positive) direction**, typically by forming a thin, adherent, and insoluble oxide/hydroxide film over the anodic sites.

**Examples and their protective films:**

| Inhibitor | Anodic Reaction Suppressed | Protective Film Formed |
|---|---|---|
| Sodium chromate ($Na_2CrO_4$) | Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ | $Cr_2O_3$ / $\gamma$-FeOOH (mixed) |
| Sodium nitrite ($NaNO_2$) | Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ | $\gamma$-Fe₂O₃ (passive film) |
| Sodium molybdate ($Na_2MoO_4$) | Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ | $MoO_3$ + Fe₂O₃ |
| Sodium tungstate ($Na_2WO_4$) | Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ | $WO_3$ / FeWO₄ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** "Dangerous inhibitors" — If the concentration of an anodic inhibitor falls **below a critical threshold**, it accelerates localized corrosion (pitting). This is the **"the inhibitor that ate the pipe"** problem in industry. Chromates are being phased out globally under **REACH (EU)** and **EPA (USA)** regulations due to carcinogenicity.

## 2.3 Cathodic Inhibitors — Detailed Mechanism

Cathodic inhibitors suppress the cathodic reaction by either:
- **Reducing the cathodic area** (precipitation inhibitors), or
- **Limiting the diffusion of reactants** to the cathodic sites (oxygen scavengers).

**Three sub-categories:**

#### (a) Cathodic Precipitates
React with the alkalinity produced at the cathodic site to form insoluble hydroxides that blanket the cathode:

$$Zn^{2+} + 2OH^- \rightarrow Zn(OH)_2 \downarrow$$

$$Ca^{2+} + 2HCO_3^- + 2OH^- \rightarrow CaCO_3 \downarrow + 2H_2O$$

#### (b) Oxygen Scavengers
Remove dissolved $O_2$ from neutral/alkaline systems before it can participate in the cathodic reaction:

$$2Na_2SO_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 2Na_2SO_4$$

$$N_2H_4 + O_2 \rightarrow N_2 \uparrow + 2H_2O \quad \text{(hydrazine, industrial use)}$$

#### (c) Cathodic Poisons
Arsenic, antimony, and bismuth compounds adsorb on cathodic sites and block hydrogen evolution. **Used in acidic pickling to prevent hydrogen embrittlement.**

## 2.4 Mixed Inhibitors

Mixed inhibitors adsorb over the **entire metal surface** (both anodic and cathodic zones), forming a uniform film that blocks electrolyte access. The displacement in $E_{corr}$ is typically **less than ±85 mV** — this is the diagnostic criterion for a mixed inhibitor on an Evans diagram.

**Common organic mixed inhibitors:**
- **Amines** (primary, secondary, tertiary)
- **Imidazolines** — extensively used in oil & gas pipelines
- **Quaternary ammonium salts**
- **Schiff bases** (reaction products of amines + aldehydes)
- **Benzotriazole (BTA)** — the gold standard for copper protection

## 2.5 Vapour Phase Inhibitors (VPI)

VPI are **volatile organic salts** that sublime slowly, vaporize, and migrate to the metal surface where they hydrolyze to release protective anions (typically nitrite or carbonate). They are used for **enclosed-space protection** (export packaging, storage of precision parts, military equipment).

**Classic VPI examples:**

| VPI Compound | Volatility | Decomposition Product | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dicyclohexylammonium nitrite (DICHAN) | Moderate | $NO_2^-$ | Historic; replaced due to toxicity |
| Cyclohexylamine carbonate (CHC) | High | $CO_3^{2-}$ | Modern, low-toxicity |
| Benzotriazole (BTA) | Low | BTAH | Copper alloys in transformers |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description / Boundary Condition | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $IE \% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$ | Inhibitor efficiency from weight loss or Tafel data | Dimensionless % |
| 2 | $IE \% = \dfrac{R_c(R_c^0)^{-1} - 1}{1} \times 100$ | From polarization resistance ($R_p$) data | Dimensionless % |
| 3 | $\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0}$ | Surface coverage fraction ($0 \le \theta \le 1$) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ | **Langmuir isotherm** (linear form, plot $C/\theta$ vs $C$) | mol/L |
| 5 | $\theta = \dfrac{K_{ads} \cdot C}{1 + K_{ads} \cdot C}$ | **Langmuir isotherm** (non-linear form) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\theta = K_{ads} \cdot C^n$ | **Freundlich isotherm** (empirical, $0 < n < 1$) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$ | Standard free energy of adsorption | kJ/mol |
| 8 | $CR_0 = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate from weight loss (ASTM G1) | mmpy or mpy |
| 9 | $E_{corr}$ shift $\le \pm 85$ mV | Criterion for "mixed" inhibitor classification | mV vs SCE |
| 10 | $\eta_{inh} = \dfrac{i_{corr}^0 - i_{corr}^{inh}}{i_{corr}^0} \times 100$ | Inhibition efficiency from Tafel slopes | Dimensionless % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
