This is a **frequently lost mark** in KTU answer sheets.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

| Industry Sector | Corrosive Medium | Preferred Inhibitor | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Petroleum (downstream)** | $H_2S$, $CO_2$, brine in pipelines | Imidazoline + quaternary salts | Mixed, film-forming |
| **Power plants (cooling towers)** | Recirculated water with dissolved $O_2$ | Phosphonates + zinc | Cathodic + anodic |
| **Acid pickling (steel finishing)** | 5–15% $H_2SO_4$ or $HCl$ | Thiourea, propargyl alcohol | Mixed, adsorption |
| **Automotive antifreeze** | Ethylene glycol + water | Silicates, phosphates, BTA | Mixed |
| **Marine (offshore structures)** | Seawater (3.5% NaCl) | Calcium nitrite + zinc | Anodic + cathodic |
| **Concrete reinforcement** | Carbonated pore water, chlorides | Calcium nitrite (as admixture) | Anodic |
| **Electronics / PCB** | Humidity, ionic residues | BTA, tolyltriazole | Mixed, chelation with Cu |
| **Packaging / storage** | Humid air, enclosed space | DICHAN, CHC | Vapour phase |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm Applied to Corrosion Inhibition

### Step 1: Define the adsorption equilibrium

Consider an inhibitor molecule **I** dissolved in the corrosive electrolyte at bulk concentration **C** (mol/L). It adsorbs onto a vacant active site **S** on the metal surface, forming an adsorbed complex **S—I**:

$$S_{(aq)} + I_{(aq)} \rightleftharpoons S\text{-}I_{(ads)}$$

### Step 2: Define the equilibrium constant

The equilibrium constant $K_{ads}$ (also called the **adsorption coefficient** or **binding constant**) is:

$$K_{ads} = \frac{[S\text{-}I]}{[S] \cdot C}$$

### Step 3: Introduce surface coverage $\theta$

Let:
- $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered by inhibitor = $\dfrac{\text{Number of occupied sites}}{\text{Total number of sites}}$
- $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction of vacant sites

Then:
- $[S\text{-}I]$ is proportional to $\theta$
- $[S]$ is proportional to $(1 - \theta)$

Substituting:

$$K_{ads} = \frac{\theta}{(1 - \theta) \cdot C}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\theta$ (Langmuir non-linear form)

Rearranging:

$$\theta = K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

$$\theta + K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot \theta = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

$$\theta(1 + K_{ads} \cdot C) = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K_{ads} \cdot C}{1 + K_{ads} \cdot C}}$$

### Step 5: Convert to linear form (for graphical verification)

Take the reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{\theta} = \frac{1 + K_{ads} \cdot C}{K_{ads} \cdot C} = \frac{1}{K_{ads} \cdot C} + 1$$

Divide both sides by $C$:

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Graphical Verification (KTU Lab Component):** A plot of $C/\theta$ (y-axis) vs $C$ (x-axis) must yield a **straight line with:**
> - **Slope ≈ 1** (confirms Langmuir monolayer adsorption)
> - **Intercept = $1/K_{ads}$** (used to compute $K_{ads}$)
> - **$R^2$ ≥ 0.99** (linearity criterion)

### Step 6: Compute the standard free energy of adsorption

At the corrosion temperature **T** (Kelvin), $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ is related to $K_{ads}$ via:

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The factor 55.5** is the molar concentration of water in pure water (mol/L). It arises because $K_{ads}$ must be referenced to a **standard state of 1 mol/kg of solvent**, not 1 mol/L of solute. This is a **favorite 2-mark sub-question** in KTU exams.

### Step 7: Interpret the sign and magnitude of $\Delta G_{ads}^0$

| $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ (kJ/mol) | Adsorption Type | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta G_{ads}^0 \le -20$ | Physisorption (weak, van der Waals) | Reversible; mild protection |
| $-40 \le \Delta G_{ads}^0 \le -20$ | Mixed physio-chemisorption | Moderate protection |
| $\Delta G_{ads}^0 \le -40$ | Chemisorption (covalent/ionic bond) | Strong, durable protection |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Inhibitor Efficiency from Weight-Loss Method

### Step 1: Measure weight loss under two conditions

- **Without inhibitor:** Initial mass $W_0^{(1)}$, final mass $W_f^{(1)}$, exposure time $T$, specimen area $A$, metal density $D$.
- **With inhibitor:** Initial mass $W_0^{(2)}$, final mass $W_f^{(2)}$, same $T$, $A$, $D$.

### Step 2: Compute weight losses

$$\Delta W_0 = W_0^{(1)} - W_f^{(1)} \quad \text{(no inhibitor)}$$

$$\Delta W_i = W_0^{(2)} - W_f^{(2)} \quad \text{(with inhibitor)}$$

### Step 3: Compute corrosion rates (ASTM G1 standard formula)

$$CR = \frac{K \cdot \Delta W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

where $K$ is a unit-conversion constant:
- $K = 87{,}600$ for **mm/year (mmpy)**
- $K = 534$ for **mils/year (mpy)**
- $K = 3.45 \times 10^6$ for **$\mu$m/year**

### Step 4: Substitute into the efficiency formula

$$CR_0 = \frac{87{,}600 \cdot \Delta W_0}{D \cdot A \cdot T}, \quad CR_i = \frac{87{,}600 \cdot \Delta W_i}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

The 87,600 factor cancels in the ratio:

$$IE \% = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{\Delta W_0 - \Delta W_i}{\Delta W_0} \times 100$$

> [!TIP]
> **Shortcut for KTU exams:** When $A$, $D$, and $T$ are identical for both experiments (which is the standard lab protocol), the efficiency can be computed **directly from the weight-loss ratio** without needing the area, density, or time.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Model Question):**
A mild steel specimen of area $15 \text{ cm}^2$ is immersed in $1\text{ M}$ $H_2SO_4$ at $25°\text{C}$ for 6 hours. The weight loss without inhibitor is **0.420 g**, and with 0.5 mM thiourea inhibitor is **0.063 g**. Calculate:
1. Corrosion rate (with and without inhibitor) in mmpy. Take $D = 7.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$.
2. Inhibitor efficiency.
3. Surface coverage.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $A = 15 \text{ cm}^2$, $T = 6 \text{ h} = 6 \times 3600 \text{ s}$ (or simply use $T = 6$ h with $K = 87{,}600$ for mmpy in hours)
- $D = 7.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $K = 87{,}600$ (mmpy)
- $\Delta W_0 = 0.420 \text{ g}$, $\Delta W_i = 0.063 \text{ g}$

**Part 1: Corrosion rates**

$$CR_0 = \frac{87{,}600 \times 0.420}{7.86 \times 15 \times 6}$$

$$CR_0 = \frac{36{,}792}{707.4} \approx 52.0 \text{ mmpy}$$

$$CR_i = \frac{87{,}600 \times 0.063}{7.86 \times 15 \times 6}$$

$$CR_i = \frac{5{,}518.8}{707.4} \approx 7.8 \text{ mmpy}$$

**Part 2: Inhibitor efficiency**

$$IE \% = \frac{52.0 - 7.8}{52.0} \times 100 = \frac{44.2}{52.0} \times 100 \approx 85.0\%$$

**Part 3: Surface coverage**

$$\theta = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} = \frac{44.2}{52.0} = 0.85$$

This means thiourea covers **85% of the surface area** at 0.5 mM concentration, leaving only 15% exposed to the acid.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Activation Energy Comparison (with and without inhibitor)

The Arrhenius equation for corrosion rate is:

$$CR = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Taking natural logarithms:

$$\ln CR = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

For two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\ln \frac{CR_2}{CR_1} = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

**With inhibitor added**, the new activation energy $E_a'$ is computed from the new rates $CR_1'$ and $CR_2'$:

$$\ln \frac{CR_2'}{CR_1'} = \frac{E_a'}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** If $E_a' > E_a$, the inhibitor raises the energy barrier for corrosion (physical barrier effect). If $E_a' < E_a$, the inhibitor works by lowering the pre-exponential factor **A** (blocking active sites, reducing collision frequency). Both mechanisms reduce the rate, but through different physics — this distinction is examined in KTU Module 4 long-answer questions.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Inhibitor

| Operating Condition | Best Inhibitor Class | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Stagnant, low-velocity water** | Anodic (nitrite, molybdate) | Passive film is self-healing in calm conditions |
| **High-velocity, turbulent flow** | Mixed organic (amine, imidazoline) | Anodic films are mechanically eroded; mixed films re-adsorb dynamically |
| **Strongly acidic ($pH < 2$) pickling** | Mixed organic (thiourea, propargyl alcohol) | Inorganic films dissolve in acid; only adsorption-type films survive |
| **Closed, humid storage** | Vapour phase (CHC, BTA) | Liquid inhibitors cannot penetrate packaging; VPIs sublime and reach all surfaces |
| **Concrete pore water ($pH \approx 12.5$)** | Calcium nitrite | Passive film forms spontaneously on rebar; nitrite oxidizes Fe²⁺ to stable Fe³⁺ |
| **Two-phase oil-water pipelines** | Oil-soluble, water-dispersible imidazolines | Partition coefficient ensures inhibitor reaches the metal-water interface |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A, Q1(b)]**

### Question 1
**Define a corrosion inhibitor. Differentiate between anodic and cathodic inhibitors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance that, when added in small concentrations (typically 0.1–1% by weight) to a corrosive environment, significantly reduces the corrosion rate of the metal without reacting with the bulk medium. **(1 mark)**

**Anodic inhibitors** suppress the metal dissolution reaction ($M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$) by forming a passive oxide film on anodic sites. Example: Sodium chromate ($Na_2CrO_4$), which forms a $Cr_2O_3$ / $Fe_2O_3$ passive layer. **(1 mark)**

**Cathodic inhibitors** suppress the cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction or hydrogen evolution) by precipitating insoluble hydroxides at cathodic sites or by scavenging dissolved oxygen. Example: Zinc sulphate ($ZnSO_4$), which precipitates $Zn(OH)_2$ at cathodic sites, blocking oxygen reduction. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1(c)]**

### Question 2
**What are vapour phase inhibitors? Give two examples and state one industrial application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Vapour phase inhibitors (VPI) are **volatile organic compounds** that sublime at room temperature, vaporize, and migrate to the metal surface where they hydrolyze to release protective anions (such as $NO_2^-$ or $CO_3^{2-}$) that form a passive film. **(1.5 marks)**

**Examples:**
- **Cyclohexylamine carbonate (CHC):** decomposes to release $CO_3^{2-}$; modern, low-toxicity.
- **Benzotriazole (BTA):** protects copper and copper alloys via complex formation. **(1 mark)**

**Application:** Used in **export packaging of precision metal components, machine tools, and electronic parts** to prevent rusting during long-term storage and ocean shipment. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Q11]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Classify corrosion inhibitors based on their mechanism of action. Explain with neat sketches (polarization diagrams) the effect of anodic, cathodic, and mixed inhibitors on the polarization curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel specimen of area $20 \text{ cm}^2$ was immersed in $0.5\text{ M}$ $H_2SO_4$ at $30°\text{C}$ for 8 hours. Without inhibitor, the weight loss was 0.520 g. With the addition of 1 mM of an organic inhibitor, the weight loss reduced to 0.078 g. Calculate: (i) the corrosion rates in mmpy, (ii) the inhibitor efficiency, and (iii) the surface coverage. Take $D = 7.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $K = 87{,}600$ for mmpy. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Classification and Polarization Diagram Explanation (7 marks)

**Classification (3 marks):**

1. **Anodic inhibitors** — function by forming a passive oxide film on the anodic (metal dissolution) sites. They shift the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) in the **noble (positive) direction**. Examples: Chromates, nitrites, molybdates.

2. **Cathodic inhibitors** — function by forming insoluble precipitates at cathodic sites, blocking oxygen reduction or hydrogen evolution. They shift $E_{corr}$ in the **active (negative) direction**. Examples: Zinc salts (precipitate $Zn(OH)_2$), polyphosphates (precipitate $CaCO_3$), hydrazine ($O_2$ scavenger).

3. **Mixed inhibitors** — adsorb uniformly on the metal surface, suppressing both anodic and cathodic reactions. The shift in $E_{corr}$ is **less than ±85 mV**. Examples: Organic amines, imidazolines, benzotriazole.

**Polarization Diagram Explanation (4 marks):**

A polarization (Evans) diagram plots **log(current density i)** on the y-axis versus **electrode potential E** on the x-axis. The intersection of the anodic Tafel line (slope $\beta_a$) and the cathodic Tafel line (slope $-\beta_c$) gives the **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$** at the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$.

- **Anodic inhibitor:** The anodic Tafel line is **displaced to the right** (higher overpotential needed for the same anodic current). $E_{corr}$ shifts positive, $i_{corr}$ decreases.

- **Cathodic inhibitor:** The cathodic Tafel line is **displaced to the left**. $E_{corr}$ shifts negative, $i_{corr}$ decreases.

- **Mixed inhibitor:** **Both Tafel lines** are displaced, with $E_{corr}$ shift less than 85 mV in either direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always draw a **clear, labeled diagram** with axes, Tafel slopes, original and inhibited curves, and the $i_{corr}$ values marked. A diagram without proper labels scores only 2 out of 4 marks for this sub-question.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $A = 20 \text{ cm}^2$, $T = 8 \text{ h}$, $D = 7.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $K = 87{,}600$
- $\Delta W_0 = 0.520 \text{ g}$ (no inhibitor)
- $\Delta W_i = 0.078 \text{ g}$ (with inhibitor)

**Step 1: Corrosion rate without inhibitor (2 marks)**

$$CR_0 = \frac{87{,}600 \times 0.520}{7.86 \times 20 \times 8}$$

$$CR_0 = \frac{45{,}552}{1{,}257.6} = 36.22 \text{ mmpy}$$

**Step 2: Corrosion rate with inhibitor (2 marks)**

$$CR_i = \frac{87{,}600 \times 0.078}{7.86 \times 20 \times 8}$$

$$CR_i = \frac{6{,}832.8}{1{,}257.6} = 5.43 \text{ mmpy}$$

**Step 3: Inhibitor efficiency (1.5 marks)**

$$IE \% = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{36.22 - 5.43}{36.22} \times 100$$

$$IE \% = \frac{30.79}{36.22} \times 100 \approx 85.0\%$$

**Step 4: Surface coverage (1.5 marks)**

$$\theta = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} = \frac{30.79}{36.22} \approx 0.850 \text{ (i.e., 85.0\%)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is a vapour phase inhibitor? Describe the mechanism by which dicyclohexylammonium nitrite (DICHAN) protects iron and steel components during storage and shipment. Mention two limitations of DICHAN. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm as applied to corrosion inhibition. The following data were obtained for a mild steel specimen in 1 M $H_2SO_4$ with varying concentrations of an organic inhibitor at 25°C. Verify whether the adsorption follows the Langmuir isotherm and calculate $K_{ads}$ and $\Delta G_{ads}^0$.

| Concentration $C$ (mM) | Inhibitor Efficiency IE (%) | Surface Coverage $\theta$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | 55.0 | 0.550 |
| 0.5 | 85.0 | 0.850 |
| 1.0 | 92.5 | 0.925 |
| 2.0 | 96.3 | 0.963 |
| 5.0 | 98.4 | 0.984 |

(Take $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$, $T = 298 \text{ K}$, and water concentration = 55.5 mol/L.) **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Vapour Phase Inhibitor Mechanism (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Vapour phase inhibitors (VPI) are **volatile chemical compounds** that, when placed inside a sealed enclosure, sublime slowly and reach all metal surfaces (including hidden crevices) via the vapour phase, where they protect against atmospheric corrosion.

**Mechanism of DICHAN (4 marks):**

DICHAN (dicyclohexylammonium nitrite, $[(C_6H_{11})_2NH_2]^+ \cdot NO_2^-$) acts in **four sequential steps:**

1. **Sublimation:** At room temperature, DICHAN slowly sublimes, releasing vapour into the enclosure. Its vapour pressure at 25°C is moderate (~$10^{-4}$ mmHg), ensuring long-term release.

2. **Diffusion:** The vapour diffuses throughout the enclosed airspace (e.g., inside a sealed plastic-wrapped crate) and reaches every metal surface, including recessed areas unreachable by liquid inhibitors.

3. **Hydrolysis on metal surface:** The vapour hydrolyzes on the slightly acidic moisture film on the metal surface:

$$[(C_6H_{11})_2NH_2]^+ NO_2^- + H_2O \rightarrow (C_6H_{11})_2NH + HNO_2$$

4. **Passivation:** The released nitrous acid ($HNO_2$) acts as an oxidizing passivator, oxidizing $Fe^{2+}$ to $Fe^{3+}$ and forming a thin, adherent $\gamma$-$Fe_2O_3$ passive film (2–5 nm thick) that suppresses anodic dissolution.

**Limitations of DICHAN (2 marks):**
1. **Toxicity and health hazard:** DICHAN has been classified as a potential carcinogen and is restricted under REACH regulations; many countries have banned or limited its use.
2. **Limited to ferrous metals and copper-free alloys** — it is not effective on copper, brass, or zinc, which can be attacked by the amine vapour. For copper, BTA-based VPIs are preferred.

---

#### Part (b) — Langmuir Isotherm Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1: Set up the linearized Langmuir equation (1 mark)**

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

**Step 2: Compute $C/\theta$ for each concentration (2 marks)**

| $C$ (mM) | $\theta$ | $C/\theta$ (mM) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | 0.550 | 0.1818 |
| 0.5 | 0.850 | 0.5882 |
| 1.0 | 0.925 | 1.0811 |
| 2.0 | 0.963 | 2.0769 |
| 5.0 | 0.984 | 5.0813 |

**Step 3: Linear regression of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ (2 marks)**

Plotting $C/\theta$ (y-axis) vs $C$ (x-axis), a linear fit yields:
- **Slope ≈ 1.00** (confirms Langmuir behaviour)
- **Intercept ≈ 0.0833 mM = 8.33 × 10⁻⁵ mol/L**

(Note: $K_{ads} = 1/\text{intercept} = 1/0.0833 \text{ mM} = 12{,}000 \text{ L/mol} = 1.2 \times 10^4 \text{ L/mol}$.)

Actually, computing precisely:
$$K_{ads} = \frac{1}{\text{intercept}} = \frac{1}{0.0833 \text{ mM}} = 12.0 \text{ mM}^{-1} = 1.2 \times 10^4 \text{ L/mol}$$

**Step 4: Calculate $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ (2 marks)**

$$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -(8.314)(298) \ln(55.5 \times 1.2 \times 10^4)$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -(2477.6) \ln(6.66 \times 10^5)$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -(2477.6)(13.41)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{ads}^0 \approx -33.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $-40 \le \Delta G_{ads}^0 = -33.2 \le -20$ kJ/mol, the adsorption is **mixed physio-chemisorption** — the inhibitor is bonded to the surface via both electrostatic attraction (physisorption) and partial electron sharing (chemisorption). This is a **moderately strong, durable** protective film.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Inhibitor Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 55.5** in the $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ equation. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
> 2. **Confusing the sign convention** — $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ is always **negative** for spontaneous adsorption. Students often write positive values.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — $K_{ads}$ is in L/mol, $C$ in mol/L. Inconsistent units give wrong intercepts.
> 4. **Not labeling axes** on Evans diagrams — examiners deduct 1.5 marks for unlabelled polarization curves.
> 5. **Writing "$Na_2CrO_4$ is safe"** — it is **carcinogenic**; mentioning REACH/EPA restrictions scores extra credit.
> 6. **Ignoring the "below critical concentration"** caveat for anodic inhibitors. Always state that anodic inhibitors must be present at a **minimum concentration** to avoid pitting acceleration.
> 7. **Forgetting the $R^2$ value** when reporting Langmuir verification. A perfect straight line with $R^2 < 0.95$ should be questioned.
> 8. **Writing $E_{corr}$ shift of 200 mV for a "mixed" inhibitor** — by definition, mixed inhibitors shift $E_{corr}$ by less than ±85 mV. Large shifts indicate anodic or cathodic behaviour.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Inhibitors (Module 4)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Inhibitor = chemical that reduces corrosion rate at low concentration; does not change metal composition.
- [ ] **Three main classes:** Anodic (passivators), Cathodic (precipitates/scavengers/poisons), Mixed (adsorption).
- [ ] **Anodic inhibitors** shift $E_{corr}$ **positive (noble)**; examples: chromate, nitrite, molybdate.
- [ ] **Cathodic inhibitors** shift $E_{corr}$ **negative (active)**; examples: Zn²⁺, polyphosphate, hydrazine.
- [ ] **Mixed inhibitors** shift $E_{corr}$ by **less than ±85 mV**; examples: amines, imidazolines, BTA.
- [ ] **Chromates are toxic** — being replaced by molybdates and tungstates (REACH/EPA).
- [ ] **Below critical concentration**, anodic inhibitors cause **pitting corrosion** — this is a major failure mode.
- [ ] **VPI (Vapour Phase Inhibitors):** DICHAN (Fe), CHC (Fe, modern), BTA (Cu).
- [ ] **Benzotriazole (BTA)** is the standard for copper and copper alloy protection.
- [ ] **Inhibitor efficiency formula:** $IE\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \theta \times 100$.
- [ ] **Langmuir isotherm (linear form):** $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ — slope must be ≈ 1.
- [ ] **$\Delta G_{ads}^0$ formula** includes the **55.5 mol/L water factor** for standard-state conversion.
- [ ] **$\Delta G_{ads}^0$ interpretation:** $\le -20$ physisorption; $-40$ to $-20$ mixed; $\le -40$ chemisorption.
- [ ] **Pickling inhibitors** (acid cleaning): thiourea, propargyl alcohol, dibenzyl sulfoxide.
- [ ] **Cooling water inhibitors:** phosphonates (HEDP, ATMP) + zinc = "mixed-mode cocktail."
- [ ] **Oil & gas pipelines:** imidazoline + quaternary ammonium salts at 10–50 ppm.
- [ ] **Concrete rebar:** calcium nitrite admixture to prevent chloride-induced pitting.
- [ ] **Antifreeze:** silicates, phosphates, BTA — multi-metal protection.
- [ ] **Green inhibitors** (plant extracts, chitosan, amino acids) — emerging research area.
- [ ] **Synergistic effect:** combining two inhibitors often gives $IE_{combined} > IE_1 + IE_2$ (e.g., Zn²⁺ + BTA).
- [ ] **Tafel slope analysis:** slope of $\log(i)$ vs $E$ plot; $\beta_a$ (anodic) and $\beta_c$ (cathodic) reveal mechanism.
- [ ] **$E_{corr}$ shift criterion:** $\Delta E_{corr} > +85$ mV = anodic; $\Delta E_{corr} < -85$ mV = cathodic; $\vert \Delta E_{corr} \vert \le 85$ mV = mixed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Oxidation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Oxidation** in the context of materials science is broadly defined as the chemical reaction between a metal (or non-metal) and the surrounding environment, typically involving the **loss of electrons** by the metal atoms and the formation of an **oxide scale** on the surface. In the narrower engineering sense, oxidation refers to the **reaction of a metal with oxygen** to form a metal oxide, accompanied by the transport of metal ions and/or oxygen through the growing oxide layer. The general chemical reaction can be written as: $$M + \frac{n}{2}O_2 \rightarrow M O_n$$ Where $M$ is the metal and $MO_n$ is the corresponding metal oxide. This reaction is **electrolytic in nature** because both electrons and ions must be transported through the scale to sustain growth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Oxidation is classified under *environmental degradation of materials*, alongside corrosion, and is fundamentally an **electrochemical / diffusion-controlled process**. The understanding of oxidation kinetics (linear, parabolic, logarithmic) forms the basis of *Course Outcome CO3: Identify mechanisms of material degradation and prevention strategies.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a fresh apple. When you cut it and leave it exposed to air, the exposed surface gradually turns brown. This is **oxidation of organic matter** — oxygen from the air reacts with the components inside. Now think of an iron rod left in a damp atmosphere. Over weeks, a flaky reddish-brown layer (rust) forms on the surface. In both cases, the underlying principle is identical: - **Oxygen (O₂)** from the air acts as the **oxidizing agent**. - The **metal surface acts as the anode**, giving up electrons. - A **layer forms on the surface** that initially protects the metal but may eventually crack, flake, or spall — exposing fresh metal to continue the attack. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** Oxidation is essentially the metal "breathing in" oxygen and "exhaling" an oxide scale. Whether the scale **protects** (like Al₂O₃ on aluminum) or **destroys** (like rust on iron) depends entirely on the **physical and chemical nature** of that scale. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value/Range | |---|---| | Standard oxidation potential (Fe → Fe²⁺) | **+0.44 V** | | Partial pressure of O₂ in dry air | **0.21 atm** | | Parabolic rate constant (Fe in air at 600 °C) | ≈ **1.5 × 10⁻¹² m²/s** | | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) — protective range | **1 < PBR < 2** | | Activation energy for Fe oxidation | **≈ 134 kJ/mol** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of Oxidation — Step-by-Step Logic For oxidation to proceed continuously after the initial oxide scale has formed, **two simultaneous transport processes** must occur: 1. **Metal cations (Mⁿ⁺)** must diffuse *outward* through the scale toward the oxide–gas interface. 2. **Oxygen anions (O²⁻)** or electrons must diffuse *inward* through the scale toward the metal–oxide interface. > [!NOTE] > The oxide film grows at the interface where the **slower-diffusing species** is rate-limiting. For most metals, **cation diffusion outward** dominates, and growth occurs at the **outer surface**. ### 2.1.1 The Three Stages of Oxide Formation **Stage I — Initial Chemisorption** - Oxygen molecules physically adsorb on the metal surface. - Electron transfer from metal to O₂ → formation of a chemisorbed layer. - Nucleation of oxide islands (Volmer–Weber type growth). **Stage II — Thin-Film Regime (< ~100 nm)** - Oxide islands coalesce into a continuous thin film. - Electric field across the film is large; **ion transport is field-driven** (Cabrera–Mott mechanism). - Growth is rapid initially but slows logarithmically. **Stage III — Thick-Film Regime (> ~100 nm)** - Transport is now governed by **thermal diffusion** of ions through a thick crystalline scale. - The **Wagner theory of oxidation** applies. - Parabolic kinetics prevail. ## 2.2 The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) The PBR is the most fundamental criterion for predicting whether an oxide scale will be **protective** or **non-protective**. It is defined as the **ratio of the volume of the oxide formed to the volume of the metal consumed**: $$PBR = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$$ Where: - $M_{oxide}, M_{metal}$ = molecular/molar masses - $\rho_{oxide}, \rho_{metal}$ = densities - $n$ = number of metal atoms per oxide molecule ### Interpretation Table | PBR Value | Scale Behaviour | Examples | |---|---|---| | **PBR < 1** | Porous, non-protective (metal volume > oxide volume) | Mg, Ca, Ba oxides | | **1 < PBR < 2** | Protective, adherent, compressive stress | Al₂O₃ (1.28), Cr₂O₃ (1.99), SiO₂ (1.88) | | **PBR > 2** | Cracking, spalling (excess compressive stress) | Fe₂O₃ (2.15), Fe₃O₄ — partial spallation | ## 2.3 Oxidation Kinetics — Rate Laws ### 2.3.1 Linear Rate Law The oxide scale is **non-protective**; oxygen has free access to the metal surface. $$x = k_L \cdot t$$ Where $x$ = scale thickness, $k_L$ = linear rate constant (cm/s), $t$ = time. ### 2.3.2 Parabolic Rate Law (Wagner) The scale is **protective**; growth is limited by **diffusion** of ions through the scale. $$x^2 = k_P \cdot t$$ Where $k_P$ = parabolic rate constant (cm²/s). $$\boxed{\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{k_P}{2x}}$$ This shows the rate **decreases inversely with thickness** — the thicker the scale, the slower the growth. ### 2.3.3 Logarithmic Rate Law Applies to **thin films at low temperatures** (Cabrera–Mott regime). $$x = k_{log} \cdot \ln(t + t_0)$$ ### 2.3.4 Arrhenius Temperature Dependence All rate constants follow: $$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$ Where: - $Q$ = activation energy (J/mol) - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) - $A$ = pre-exponential factor ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Formula | Expression | Applicability | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Oxidation reaction | $M + (n/2)O_2 \to MO_n$ | All metals | — | | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | $PBR = (M_O \cdot \rho_M) / (n \cdot M_M \cdot \rho_O)$ | Predict protective scale | dimensionless | | Linear rate law | $x = k_L \cdot t$ | Non-protective scale | cm | | Parabolic rate law | $x^2 = k_P \cdot t$ | Wagner, protective scale | cm² | | Rate form (parabolic) | $dx/dt = k_P/(2x)$ | Differential form | cm/s | | Logarithmic law | $x = k_{log}\ln(t+t_0)$ | Thin films, low $T$ | cm | | Arrhenius | $k = A e^{-Q/RT}$ | All rate constants | varies | | Mass gain form | $(\Delta m/A)^2 = k_P \cdot t$ | TGA experiments | mg²/cm⁴ | | Diffusion (Wagner) | $k_P \propto D_{ion}$ | Thick scales | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note for KTU Examinations:** The parabolic rate constant $k_P$ has units of **cm²/s** (or m²/s), NOT cm/s. Students frequently lose 1 mark by stating the wrong units. Always remember: $x^2 = k_P t$ ⇒ $k_P$ has units of length²/time. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility - **High-temperature alloy design**: Selecting alloying elements (Cr, Al, Si) that form protective $\alpha$-Al₂O₃ or Cr₂O₃ scales for gas-turbine blades. - **Boiler & steam-plant metallurgy**: Predicting oxidation rate of superheater tubes at 600–800 °C. - **Heat-resistant coatings**: Aluminide coatings on Ni-superalloys rely on a **slow parabolic growth** of Al₂O₃. - **Failure analysis**: Spalled oxide scales in jet engines (e.g., TBC delamination) are diagnosed using parabolic kinetics. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Parabolic Rate Law (Wagner Theory) ### Step 1: Fick's First Law of Diffusion Assume the oxide scale is a **planar slab** of thickness $x$. The flux $J$ of metal ions diffusing outward through the scale is given by Fick's first law: $$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ Where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient of metal cations in the oxide. ### Step 2: Linear Concentration Gradient Assuming quasi-steady-state diffusion across the scale with a constant concentration difference $\Delta C$ between the metal–oxide interface and the oxide–gas interface: $$\frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{\Delta C}{x}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute into Fick's Law $$J = D \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{x}$$ ### Step 4: Relate Flux to Scale Growth The rate of oxide growth is proportional to the ionic flux: $$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha \cdot J = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{x}$$ Where $\alpha$ is a stoichiometric proportionality constant. ### Step 5: Separate Variables $$x \cdot dx = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta C \cdot dt$$ ### Step 6: Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t$ $$\int_{0}^{x} x\, dx = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta C \cdot \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ $$\frac{x^2}{2} = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta C \cdot t$$ ### Step 7: Define the Parabolic Rate Constant $$x^2 = 2 \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta C \cdot t = k_P \cdot t$$ $$\boxed{x^2 = k_P \cdot t \quad ; \quad k_P = 2 \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta C}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** Since $k_P \propto D$, and $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$, the parabolic rate constant shows an **Arrhenius dependence on temperature** — a fact exploited in plotting $\ln k_P$ vs. $1/T$ to extract the activation energy of oxidation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio ### Step 1: Volume of Metal Consumed Consider $n$ moles of metal of molar mass $M_M$ and density $\rho_M$. The volume of metal consumed is: $$V_{metal} = \frac{n \cdot M_M}{\rho_M}$$ ### Step 2: Volume of Oxide Formed One mole of oxide $M O_n$ forms per $n$ moles of metal. So the volume of oxide is: $$V_{oxide} = \frac{M_O}{\rho_O}$$ Where $M_O$ is the molar mass of the oxide and $\rho_O$ is its density. ### Step 3: Form the Ratio $$PBR = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_O / \rho_O}{n \cdot M_M / \rho_M} = \frac{M_O \cdot \rho_M}{n \cdot M_M \cdot \rho_O}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A steel specimen oxidizes in air at 700 °C following parabolic kinetics. The parabolic rate constant is $k_P = 3.5 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s. Calculate (a) the oxide thickness after 100 hours, and (b) the time required to grow a 200 µm scale. ### Part (a): Thickness after 100 hours Given: $k_P = 3.5 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s, $t = 100 \text{ h} = 100 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3.6 \times 10^5 \text{ s}$ Using the parabolic rate law: $$x^2 = k_P \cdot t$$ $$x^2 = (3.5 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (3.6 \times 10^5)$$ $$x^2 = 1.26 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$ $$x = \sqrt{1.26 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.122 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{x \approx 1.12 \text{ mm} = 1122 \text{ µm}}$$ ### Part (b): Time for 200 µm scale Given: $x = 200 \text{ µm} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m $$t = \frac{x^2}{k_P} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4})^2}{3.5 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{3.5 \times 10^{-12}}$$ $$t = 1.143 \times 10^4 \text{ s} = 3.17 \text{ hours}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 3.17 \text{ h}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Oxide Scale Behaviour | Property | Aluminium (Al₂O₃) | Iron (Fe₂O₃) | Magnesium (MgO) | |---|---|---|---| | **PBR value** | 1.28 | 2.15 | 0.81 | | **Scale type** | Protective | Cracks/spalls | Non-protective | | **Kinetics** | Parabolic (thin) / logarithmic (low T) | Parabolic at high T | Linear | | **Dominant defect** | Cation vacancies | Cation vacancies | — | | **Activation energy** | ~ 280 kJ/mol | ~ 134 kJ/mol | ~ 130 kJ/mol | | **Result** | Adherent, protective | Eventual spallation | Continuous oxidation | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mechanism of Oxide Scale Growth (Diffusion Pathways) The following Mermaid block diagrams the **transport phenomena** occurring during high-temperature oxidation. It traces the movement of metal cations, oxygen anions, and electrons through the growing scale, and identifies the two interfaces where new oxide formation can occur. ```mermaid flowchart TB airNode["OUTER ATMOSPHERE
Oxygen Gas O2"] outerNode["OXIDE GAS INTERFACE
Outer surface of scale"] scaleNode["OXIDE SCALE LAYER
Thickness x growing with time
Cation vacancies and electron holes dominant"] innerNode["OXIDE METAL INTERFACE
Inner surface of scale"] metalNode["BULK METAL SUBSTRATE
Fe Ni Cr Al source of cations"] O2a["O2 molecule adsorbs"]:::gas eTrans["Electrons e migrate outward"]:::elec cationDiff["Metal cations Mn plus diffuse outward"]:::cation oDiff["Oxygen anions O2 minus diffuse inward"]:::anion airNode --> O2a O2a --> outerNode metalNode --> cationDiff cationDiff --> innerNode innerNode --> cationDiff cationDiff --> outerNode outerNode --> oDiff oDiff --> innerNode metalNode --> eTrans eTrans --> scaleNode eTrans --> outerNode scaleNode -.-> innerNode scaleNode -.-> outerNode outerNode --> growth1["Oxide growth
at outer interface"] innerNode --> growth2["Oxide growth
at inner interface"] classDef gas fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 classDef elec fill:#e0e0ff,stroke:#000080,color:#000000 classDef cation fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 classDef anion fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Oxidation Kinetics The diagram below maps the **decision logic** used by materials engineers to identify which oxidation rate law applies for a given metal–environment–temperature combination, and the corresponding protective mechanism. ```mermaid flowchart LR startNode["START
Oxidation Analysis of a Metal"]:::start checkTemp["CHECK TEMPERATURE
Low T less than 0.4 Tm

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio. State its significance in determining the protective nature of an oxide scale.** **Model Answer:** The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) is defined as the ratio of the volume of the oxide formed to the volume of the metal consumed during oxidation: $$PBR = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$$ **Significance:** - If **PBR < 1** → oxide volume is less than metal volume → scale is porous, tensile → **non-protective** (e.g., Mg, Ca). - If **1 < PBR < 2** → oxide volume is sufficient to cover the metal → scale is adherent, under slight compression → **protective** (e.g., Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃). - If **PBR > 2** → oxide volume is much greater → high compressive stress → scale cracks and spalls → **non-protective** (e.g., Fe₂O₃). *(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the three ranges, 1 mark for examples.)* --- ### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between linear and parabolic oxidation kinetics. Under what conditions does each apply?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Linear Kinetics | Parabolic Kinetics | |---|---|---| | Rate law | $x = k_L \cdot t$ | $x^2 = k_P \cdot t$ | | Scale nature | Non-protective, porous | Protective, dense, adherent | | Rate-determining step | Chemical reaction at interface | Diffusion of ions through scale | | Oxide scale thickness | Independent of $x$ | Inversely proportional to $x$ | | Examples | Mg, Ca, Ba oxides | Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, SiO₂ on Fe–Cr alloys | **Conditions:** - **Linear:** When the oxide is porous or volatile (e.g., MoO₃, WO₃), oxygen reaches the metal surface unimpeded. - **Parabolic:** When the oxide is dense and adherent; growth controlled by Wagner-type diffusion of ions. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Internal Choice Instruction (As per KTU ESE Pattern) > Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** Derive the parabolic rate law for high-temperature oxidation of metals. State the underlying assumptions. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the Wagner mechanism of oxidation. Discuss the role of point defects in oxide scales. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution to (a): **Assumptions:** 1. The oxide scale is **dense, adherent, and non-porous**. 2. The rate-determining step is **diffusion of ions** (cations or anions) through the scale. 3. The concentration gradient across the scale is **linear and time-independent**. 4. **Quasi-steady state** diffusion applies. 5. Local thermodynamic equilibrium exists at both interfaces. **Derivation:** Consider a planar oxide scale of thickness $x$ on a metal substrate. The flux of metal cations $J$ diffusing outward follows Fick's first law: $$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$ With a constant concentration difference $\Delta C$ across the scale of thickness $x$: $$J = D \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{x}$$ The scale growth rate $dx/dt$ is proportional to the flux: $$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha \cdot J = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{x}$$ Rearranging: $$x \cdot dx = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta C \cdot dt$$ Integrating from $t = 0, x = 0$ to time $t$: $$\int_0^x x \, dx = \alpha D \Delta C \int_0^t dt$$ $$\frac{x^2}{2} = \alpha D \Delta C \cdot t$$ Defining the parabolic rate constant $k_P = 2\alpha D \Delta C$: $$\boxed{x^2 = k_P \cdot t}$$ This is the **parabolic rate law**, indicating that the oxide thickness grows as the square root of time — characteristic of diffusion-controlled oxidation. #### Model Solution to (b): **Wagner Mechanism — Detailed Explanation:** Hauffe and Wagner (1933) proposed that oxidation of metals at high temperatures proceeds via **counter-current diffusion** of ions and electrons through a dense oxide scale. The schematic is: ``` ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ Gas Phase (O₂ atmosphere) │ └──────────────┬──────────────┘ │ ▼ O²⁻ formation ┌──────────────────────────────┐ │ Outer Interface (Oxide-Gas) │ ← Cations arrive, react with O₂ └──────────────┬───────────────┘ │ │ Oxide scale (thickness x) │ • Cation vacancies │ • Anion interstitials │ • Electron holes │ │ Mⁿ⁺ diffuses outward (via vacancies) │ e⁻ moves outward │ O²⁻ diffuses inward (optional) │ ┌──────────────┴───────────────┐ │ Inner Interface (Metal-Oxide)│ ← New oxide formed here └──────────────┬───────────────┘ │ ▼ Bulk Metal (M) ``` **Step-by-step Wagner mechanism:** 1. At the **outer surface**, oxygen molecules adsorb and dissociate into O²⁻ ions by accepting electrons. 2. At the **inner interface**, metal atoms ionise: $M \to M^{n+} + n e^-$. 3. **Metal cations** $M^{n+}$ diffuse outward through cation vacancies. 4. **Electrons** migrate outward through the oxide (semiconducting behaviour). 5. At the **outer interface**, $M^{n+}$ reacts with $O^{2-}$ to form new oxide: $2M^{n+} + nO^{2-} \to 2MO_{n/2}$. 6. For some oxides (ZnO, Al₂O₃), **anion diffusion inward** also contributes. **Role of Point Defects:** | Defect Type | Effect on Diffusion | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Cation vacancies** | Allow outward cation diffusion | FeO, NiO (p-type) | | **Cation interstitials** | Allow outward cation diffusion | ZnO, BeO (n-type) | | **Anion vacancies** | Allow inward O²⁻ diffusion | TiO₂, ZrO₂ (n-type) | | **Electron holes** | Carry charge during cation diffusion | p-type oxides | The **parabolic rate constant $k_P$ is directly proportional to the diffusion coefficient of the rate-limiting species**, which itself depends on defect concentration. Hence: $$k_P \propto D_{defect} \propto \exp(-Q_{defect}/RT)$$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the Pilling–Bedworth theory of oxidation. Compute the PBR for the formation of Al₂O₃ on aluminum and comment on the protective nature of the scale. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Discuss the various protection methods against high-temperature oxidation of metals and alloys, with suitable examples. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution to (a): **Pilling–Bedworth Theory (1923):** Pilling and Bedworth proposed that the **volume relationship** between the oxide formed and the metal consumed governs the protective nature of the scale. The ratio $V_{oxide}/V_{metal}$ is the PBR. **Step 1 — Volume of Metal Consumed:** For $n$ moles of metal (Al) consumed to form 1 mole of Al₂O₃, here $n = 2$ (since 4 Al → 2 Al₂O₃, per formula unit). **Step 2 — Volume of Oxide Formed:** For 1 mole of Al₂O₃. **Step 3 — PBR Formula:** $$PBR = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{Al_2O_3} / \rho_{Al_2O_3}}{2 \cdot M_{Al} / \rho_{Al}}$$ **Step 4 — Data:** - $M_{Al_2O_3} = 102$ g/mol - $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.99$ g/cm³ - $M_{Al} = 27$ g/mol - $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³ **Step 5 — Computation:** $$PBR = \frac{102 / 3.99}{2 \times 27 / 2.70}$$ $$PBR = \frac{25.56}{20.00}$$ $$\boxed{PBR = 1.28}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation:** Since **1 < PBR (1.28) < 2**, the Al₂O₃ scale is **protective**. The oxide volume is sufficient to fully cover the underlying metal, generating a small compressive stress that enhances adhesion. This explains why aluminum is highly resistant to atmospheric oxidation despite being a reactive metal — the self-formed Al₂O₃ layer passivates the surface. #### Model Solution to (b): **Protection Methods Against High-Temperature Oxidation:** **1. Alloying Additions (Most Important):** Add 12–30 wt% **Cr** to steels to form a continuous, adherent Cr₂O₃ layer (PBR ≈ 1.99) instead of porous Fe₂O₃. Similarly, **Al and Si additions** promote Al₂O₃ (PBR 1.28) and SiO₂ (PBR 1.88) scales. Examples: stainless steels, Inconel alloys, nimonic superalloys. **2. Protective Coatings:** - **Aluminide coatings:** Pack-aluminisation or CVD deposition of Al on Ni-superalloys; forms $\beta$-NiAl that produces a slow-growing Al₂O₃ scale. - **MCrAlY coatings** (M = Ni, Co): Used in gas-turbine blades; provide oxidation + hot-corrosion resistance. - **Thermal barrier coatings (TBCs):** 8YSZ (8% yttria-stabilised zirconia) overlayer with a bond coat — reduces metal temperature by 100–200 °C. **3. Surface Pre-treatments:** - **Pre-oxidation:** Deliberately forming a thin, dense, protective oxide before service. - **Shot peening** to introduce compressive residual stress that delays spallation. **4. Environmental Control:** - Reducing $pO_2$ in the atmosphere. - Using inert or reducing atmospheres in heat-treatment furnaces. **5. Inhibition / Vapour-Phase Inhibitors:** - Adding small amounts of H₂S, SO₂, or other species that modify the oxide defect structure and lower diffusion. **6. Cathodic Protection (limited use):** - Used in aqueous corrosion, occasionally in molten-salt environments. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mistake:** Students write $k_P$ with units of cm/s — it must be cm²/s or m²/s. > 2. **PBR formula error:** Confusing $n$ (stoichiometric coefficient) — for Al₂O₃, $n = 2$ (two Al atoms per oxide), not 1. > 3. **Forgetting assumptions** in parabolic law derivation — at least 3 must be stated for full marks. > 4. **Confusing Wagner and Cabrera–Mott mechanisms** — Wagner applies to thick, high-T scales; Cabrera–Mott applies to thin, low-T films. > 5. **Mixing oxidation with corrosion** — oxidation is specifically metal + oxygen; corrosion includes all environmental attack (aqueous, gaseous, molten salt). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ⚡ **Oxidation** is the reaction of a metal with O₂ to form an oxide scale; both **electrolytic** (ionic) and **electronic** conduction occur through the scale. - ⚡ **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** = $V_{oxide}/V_{metal}$; the golden rule is **1 < PBR < 2** for a protective scale. - ⚡ **PBR < 1** → porous, non-protective (Mg, Ca). - ⚡ **PBR > 2** → spalling, non-protective (Fe₂O₃ at room T). - ⚡ **PBR between 1 and 2** → protective (Al₂O₃ = 1.28, Cr₂O₃ = 1.99, SiO₂ = 1.88). - ⚡ **Parabolic law** $x^2 = k_P t$ → Wagner, thick scales, high T, protective oxides. - ⚡ **Linear law** $x = k_L t$ → non-protective oxides (porous or volatile). - ⚡ **Logarithmic law** $x = k_{log} \ln(t + t_0)$ → Cabrera–Mott, thin films at low T. - ⚡ **Parabolic rate constant** $k_P$ has units of **length²/time** (cm²/s or m²/s). - ⚡ **Arrhenius dependence:** $\ln k_P = \ln A - Q/(RT)$; plot $\ln k_P$ vs $1/T$ to extract activation energy $Q$. - ⚡ **Wagner mechanism** requires simultaneous diffusion of cations outward AND electrons outward through the scale. - ⚡ **Point defects** (vacancies, interstitials) control oxide growth rate — p-type vs n-type semiconductor behaviour of oxides. - ⚡ **Protection strategies:** alloying with Cr/Al/Si, aluminide/MCrAlY coatings, TBCs, pre-oxidation, environmental control. - ⚡ **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about kinetics, always explicitly state **assumptions, derive, define the rate constant, and give an example metal**. ---

topics.title: Aging of Rubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aging of Rubber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Aging of Rubber** is the irreversible, time-dependent deterioration of the physical, mechanical, and chemical properties of vulcanized (or unvulcanized) elastomers when exposed to environmental service conditions such as heat, oxygen, ozone, ultraviolet radiation, humidity, and mechanical stress over a period of time.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, aging is classified as a **service-life degradation phenomenon** under the broader umbrella of polymer durability, closely tied to the rubber's **crosslink density (ν)**, **network structure**, and **anti-aging additive package**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> Aging = **Chemical (oxidative/ozonolytic) + Physical (thermal cycling, stress relaxation)** degradation of the elastomeric network, leading to either **chain scission** (softening) or **over-crosslinking** (hardening/embrittlement).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Sliced Apple

Imagine biting into a fresh, white apple slice and leaving it on the kitchen counter. Within minutes, the exposed surface turns **brown and leathery** — a chemical reaction with atmospheric **oxygen** has altered its structure permanently.

Rubber aging behaves identically:
- The **fresh apple** = freshly vulcanized rubber (pliable, elastic).
- The **air in the kitchen** = environmental stressors (O₂, O₃, UV).
- The **brown slice** = aged rubber (hard, brittle, cracked).

Just as lemon juice (an antioxidant) slows the apple's browning, **antioxidants and antiozonants** added during rubber compounding slow down rubber aging.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Activation energy of oxidation | **Eₐ** | **80 – 110 kJ/mol** |
| Critical ozone concentration for cracking | **[O₃]ᵢ** | **≥ 5 pphm (parts per hundred million)** |
| Standard aging test temperature | T | **70 °C / 100 °C / 125 °C** |
| Tensile retention after aging | — | **≥ 80 %** (industrial spec) |
| Elongation retention after aging | — | **≥ 50 %** (ASTM D573) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Fact:** Natural rubber (NR) ages primarily by **chain scission**, while SBR, NBR, and EPDM tend to age by **additional crosslinking** (hardening). This dictates the choice of antioxidant.

## 1.4 Why Aging Matters in Engineering

Aged rubber components fail in service — tire sidewall cracking, sealing gasket embrittlement in fuel systems, conveyor belt hardening in mining, and O-ring failure in hydraulic actuators. Understanding aging enables **lifetime prediction** via **Arrhenius modeling** and **selection of protective additives**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Aging Mechanisms

Aging in rubber occurs via **three coupled pathways**:

### A. Oxidative Aging (Thermo-Oxidative)
- **Trigger:** Heat + atmospheric O₂.
- **Mechanism:** **Free-radical chain reaction** with three classical stages — *Initiation → Propagation → Termination*.
- **Effect:** Predominantly **chain scission** in NR, BR; **crosslink formation** in SBR, NBR, butyl.

### B. Ozonolytic Aging (Ozone Cracking)
- **Trigger:** Atmospheric O₃ concentration above the critical threshold.
- **Mechanism:** **Criegee ozonolysis** of C=C double bonds in the backbone, producing **ozonides** that decompose to aldehydes/ketones, creating microcracks.
- **Effect:** Surface-only **perpendicular cracking** under strain (visible as "alligator skin" or "stress cracking").

### C. Photochemical / UV Aging
- **Trigger:** Sunlight (UV-B 280–315 nm).
- **Mechanism:** Direct C–C and C–H bond cleavage; chromophore impurities (carbonyl groups) act as **photoinitiators**.
- **Effect:** Surface chalking, discoloration (yellowing of NR), loss of gloss.

### D. Physical Aging (Thermal Cycling / Stress Relaxation)
- **Trigger:** Cyclic temperature or sustained static load.
- **Mechanism:** **Viscoelastic relaxation** + post-curing secondary crosslink formation.
- **Effect:** Permanent set, modulus drift.

### E. Mechanical Fatigue Aging
- **Trigger:** Repeated dynamic loading.
- **Mechanism:** **Mechano-chemical** free-radical generation → chain scission.
- **Effect:** Crack growth rate (dc/dN) governed by **Paris–Erdogan law**.

## 2.2 The Auto-Oxidation Chain Mechanism (Core Theory)

Step 1 — **Initiation:**
$$ RH \xrightarrow{\Delta,\, h\nu,\, \text{stress}} R^{\bullet} + H^{\bullet} $$

Step 2 — **Propagation:**
$$ R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet} $$
$$ ROO^{\bullet} + RH \rightarrow ROOH + R^{\bullet} $$

Step 3 — **Branching (hydroperoxide decomposition):**
$$ ROOH \rightarrow RO^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}OH $$
$$ 2\,ROOH \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet} + RO^{\bullet} + H_2O $$

Step 4 — **Termination:**
$$ R^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \rightarrow R\text{–}R $$
$$ ROO^{\bullet} + ROO^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{stable products} $$

The **rate of oxygen absorption** $\frac{d[O_2]}{dt}$ is auto-catalytic (exponential rise), giving rise to the classic **S-shaped oxidation curve**.

## 2.3 Antioxidant Mechanisms

| Class | Example Compound | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary (radical scavenger)** | **BHT, IPPD, 6PPD** | Donates H• to ROO• → ROOH + stable antioxidant radical |
| **Secondary (peroxide decomposer)** | **TNP, DSTDP** | Reduces ROOH → ROH, breaking chain reaction |
| **UV stabiliser** | **HALS, carbon black** | Quenches excited states; converts peroxy radicals to inert species |
| **Antiozonant** | **Wax + 6PPD** | Wax blooms to surface (physical barrier); 6PPD reacts with O₃ |
| **Metal deactivator** | **Salicylaldehyde oxime** | Chelates trace Cu, Mn, Fe (pro-oxidant metals) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\ln \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | **Arrhenius lifetime prediction** (ISO 11346) |
| 2 | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0\, e^{-t/\tau}$ | **Stress relaxation** in aged elastomers |
| 3 | $E(t) = E_{\infty} + (E_0 - E_{\infty})\, e^{-(t/\tau)^{\beta}}$ | **Kohlrausch–Williams–Watts (KWW)** physical aging |
| 4 | $r = k\,[RH]^m\,[O_2]^n$ | **Oxidation rate law** (m, n reaction orders) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C\,(\Delta K)^m$ | **Paris–Erdogan** fatigue crack growth |
| 6 | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho\, N_A}{M_c}$ | **Crosslink density** change due to aging |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{ox} = \sum \text{bond D} - \text{products D}$ | **Oxidative bond energy** balance |
| 8 | $t_{crit} = \dfrac{1}{k\,[O_3]_{eff}}$ | **Ozone crack initiation time** |
| 9 | $R_{ret} = \dfrac{P_{aged}}{P_{original}} \times 100\,\%$ | **Property retention** (tensile, elongation) |
| 10 | $T_g(\text{aged}) = T_g(\text{original}) + k\,\Delta\nu$ | **Tₘ shift** due to crosslink change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Equation (1) — the **Arrhenius two-temperature extrapolation** — is the most frequently tested formula in Part A and Part B for aging problems. Memorize it with units: $E_a$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ in Kelvin.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre industry:** Predicting 5–7 year shelf life via accelerated oven aging at 70 °C and 100 °C.
- **Aerospace seals:** Specifying HNBR or EPDM over NBR for high-temperature hydraulic systems (180 °C capability).
- **Medical devices:** Silicone rubber aging in autoclave sterilization cycles.
- **Cable insulation:** EPR/XLPE aging under simultaneous thermal + electrical stress.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius Lifetime Prediction

### Problem Statement
A natural rubber compound retains ≥ 80 % tensile strength for **18 months at 25 °C**. The same compound is tested at an accelerated temperature of **70 °C** and reaches the same end-of-life criterion in **14 days**. Predict the activation energy of the aging reaction, then estimate service life at **40 °C**.

### Given
- $T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298$ K
- $t_1 = 18$ months
- $T_2 = 70 + 273 = 343$ K
- $t_2 = 14$ days
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1** — Write the standard Arrhenius relation for time-to-failure:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_1}{t_2}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

**Step 2** — Compute the temperature reciprocal difference:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{298}-\dfrac{1}{343} = \dfrac{343-298}{298 \times 343} = \dfrac{45}{102{,}214} = 4.402 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3** — Convert time units to a common base. Use **days**:

$$
t_1 = 18 \text{ months} = 18 \times 30 = 540 \text{ days}, \qquad t_2 = 14 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4** — Compute $\ln(t_1/t_2)$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{540}{14}\right) = \ln(38.57) = 3.652
$$

**Step 5** — Solve for activation energy $E_a$:

$$
E_a = \dfrac{R \cdot \ln(t_1/t_2)}{(1/T_1)-(1/T_2)}
$$

$$
E_a = \dfrac{8.314 \times 3.652}{4.402 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{30.36}{4.402 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
E_a = 6.898 \times 10^{4}\,\text{J/mol} = \mathbf{68.98\,\text{kJ/mol}}
$$

**Step 6** — Predict lifetime at $T_3 = 40 + 273 = 313$ K:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_3}{t_2}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_3}\right)
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_3} = \dfrac{1}{343}-\dfrac{1}{313} = \dfrac{313-343}{343 \times 313} = \dfrac{-30}{107{,}359} = -2.794 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_3}{t_2}\right) = \dfrac{68{,}980}{8.314}\times(-2.794\times 10^{-4}) = 8296.6 \times (-2.794\times 10^{-4}) = -2.319
$$

$$
\dfrac{t_3}{t_2} = e^{-2.319} = 0.0983
$$

$$
t_3 = 0.0983 \times 14 = 1.376 \text{ days} \;(\text{at 70 °C, not relevant — recompute below})
$$

Re-anchoring to $T_1 = 298$ K as reference:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_3}{t_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_3}\right)
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{298}-\dfrac{1}{313} = \dfrac{313-298}{298\times 313} = \dfrac{15}{93{,}274} = 1.608 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_3}{t_1}\right) = \dfrac{68{,}980}{8.314}\times 1.608\times 10^{-4} = 13.34
$$

$$
\dfrac{t_3}{t_1} = e^{13.34} = 6.18 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
t_3 = 540 \times 6.18 \times 10^{5} = 3.34 \times 10^{8}\,\text{days} \approx 9.15 \times 10^{5}\,\text{years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above extrapolation is mathematically valid but physically unreasonable — it illustrates the **danger of over-extrapolating Arrhenius** beyond the tested temperature range. In practice, **two-temperature bracketing** within ±20 °C of service is mandatory (ISO 11346).

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{E_a \approx 69\;\text{kJ/mol}, \qquad t_{40^\circ C} \;\text{predicted via two-point Arrhenius model}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Aging Type vs Property Change

| Aging Type | Hardness (Shore A) | Tensile Strength | Elongation | Surface Aspect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thermo-oxidative (NR) | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | Soft, tacky |
| Thermo-oxidative (SBR) | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | Hard, brittle |
| Ozonolytic | ≈ | ↓ | ↓ | **Perpendicular cracks** |
| UV / Photo | ↑ (surface) | ↓ | ↓ | Chalking, yellowing |
| Mechanical fatigue | ≈ | ↓ | ↓ | Internal microcracks |
| Hydrolytic (PU, ester) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | Swelling, softening |

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Crosslink Density Change During Aging

For an elastomer under oxidative aging, suppose **m moles per gram** of new crosslinks form per unit time, and **n moles per gram** of original crosslinks undergo chain scission. The change in crosslink density $\Delta \nu$ per unit time is:

$$
\dfrac{d\nu}{dt} = m - n = k_1\,[O_2]\,e^{-E_a/RT} - k_2\,e^{-E_b/RT}
$$

Integrating for time $t$ at constant temperature:

$$
\nu(t) = \nu_0 + (k_1\,[O_2]\,e^{-E_a/RT})\,t - (k_2\,e^{-E_b/RT})\,t
$$

The **gel-point criterion** $\nu = 0$ marks embrittlement; solving for $t_{crit}$:

$$
t_{crit} = \dfrac{\nu_0}{k_2\,e^{-E_b/RT} - k_1\,[O_2]\,e^{-E_a/RT}}
$$

This is the foundation of the **kinetic embrittlement model** used in KTU exam problems involving NBR/SBR seal life.
