# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define aging of rubber. List any four factors that accelerate rubber aging.**

**Model Answer:**
Aging of rubber is the irreversible deterioration of its physical, mechanical, and chemical properties over time under the influence of environmental factors such as heat, oxygen, ozone, sunlight, humidity, and mechanical stress. It manifests as hardening, softening, cracking, or loss of elasticity.

Four accelerating factors:
1. Elevated temperature
2. Atmospheric oxygen and ozone
3. Ultraviolet radiation (sunlight)
4. Cyclic mechanical loading (fatigue)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write "aging is reversible" — it is **irreversible**. Also, do not confuse "aging" with "relaxation" — relaxation is a reversible viscoelastic response.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the aging behaviour of Natural Rubber (NR) and Styrene–Butadiene Rubber (SBR).**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Natural Rubber (NR) | Styrene–Butadiene Rubber (SBR) |
|---|---|---|
| Dominant mechanism | Chain scission | Crosslink formation |
| Effect on hardness | Decreases | Increases |
| Effect on tensile | Decreases sharply | Decreases gradually |
| Effect on elongation | Increases (tacky) | Decreases (brittle) |
| Antioxidant need | Moderate (H-type) | High (amine-based) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 4 Choice Option A

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable reaction mechanism, explain the **free-radical auto-oxidation process** responsible for aging of natural rubber. Discuss the role of **primary and secondary antioxidants** in inhibiting this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rubber compound when tested at **80 °C** showed a useful service life of **28 days** for a specified property retention. At an elevated test temperature of **120 °C**, the same property criterion is reached in only **3 days**. Calculate the **activation energy** of the aging reaction and estimate the service life at **40 °C**. Assume Arrhenius behaviour. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Initiation:**
$$ RH \xrightarrow{\Delta} R^{\bullet} + H^{\bullet} $$

**Propagation:**
$$ R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet} $$
$$ ROO^{\bullet} + RH \rightarrow ROOH + R^{\bullet} $$

**Branching:**
$$ ROOH \rightarrow RO^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}OH $$

**Termination:**
$$ R^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \rightarrow R\text{–}R $$

In **NR**, the radicals formed at the **allylic position** lead to **β-scission** of the C–C backbone, producing shorter chains and softening.

**Primary antioxidants** (e.g., **IPPD, 6PPD**) donate hydrogen atoms to peroxy radicals:

$$
ROO^{\bullet} + AH \rightarrow ROOH + A^{\bullet} \quad (\text{A}^{\bullet} \text{ is resonance-stabilised and inert})
$$

**Secondary antioxidants** (e.g., **TNP, DSTDP**) decompose hydroperoxides:

$$
ROOH + \text{DSTDP} \rightarrow ROH + \text{non-radical products}
$$

Together, they break the auto-catalytic cycle, extending the **induction period** of the S-shaped oxidation curve.

### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Given:** $T_1 = 353$ K, $t_1 = 28$ days; $T_2 = 393$ K, $t_2 = 3$ days; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

**Step 1 — Compute reciprocal temperature difference:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{353}-\dfrac{1}{393} = \dfrac{393-353}{353\times 393} = \dfrac{40}{138{,}729} = 2.883 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute time ratio:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_1}{t_2}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{28}{3}\right) = \ln(9.333) = 2.234
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $E_a$:**

$$
E_a = \dfrac{8.314 \times 2.234}{2.883 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{18.58}{2.883 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.445 \times 10^{4}\,\text{J/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_a \approx 64.4\;\text{kJ/mol}}
$$

**Step 4 — Predict life at $T_3 = 313$ K:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_3} = \dfrac{1}{353}-\dfrac{1}{313} = \dfrac{313-353}{353\times 313} = \dfrac{-40}{110{,}489} = -3.620 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_3}{t_1}\right) = \dfrac{64{,}450}{8.314}\times(-3.620\times 10^{-4}) = 7752 \times (-3.620\times 10^{-4}) = -2.806
$$

$$
\dfrac{t_3}{t_1} = e^{-2.806} = 0.0604
$$

$$
t_3 = 0.0604 \times 28 = 1.69 \text{ days}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin, leading to $E_a$ off by a factor of ~3. Always use **absolute temperature (K)** in the Arrhenius equation. Also, the predicted value at lower temperature must be **physically sanity-checked** — aging should slow down at 40 °C, not accelerate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 4 Choice Option B

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of **ozone cracking** in elastomers. Why is natural rubber especially susceptible? Discuss the role of **antiozonants and waxes** in providing protection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural rubber specimen is exposed to **50 pphm ozone** at **40 °C under 20 % static strain**. The crack initiation time is observed to be **8 hours**. If the ozone concentration is reduced to **10 pphm** (same T, strain), estimate the new initiation time assuming **first-order kinetics in ozone**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Criegee Ozonolysis Mechanism:**
$$ R_1CH=CHR_2 + O_3 \rightarrow \text{Molozonide} \rightarrow R_1CHO + R_2CHO \;(\text{via Criegee intermediate}) $$

The ozonide decomposes to **aldehydes/ketones**, breaking the polymer backbone. Under **static strain**, the surface microcracks propagate perpendicular to the stress direction — the classic "ozone cracking" pattern.

NR is especially vulnerable because of its high density of **backbone C=C double bonds** (every isoprene repeat unit). Saturated rubbers (EPDM, IIR) are intrinsically ozone-resistant.

**Protection strategies:**
- **Wax (paraffin/microcrystalline):** blooms to surface → physical barrier preventing O₃ diffusion.
- **Chemical antiozonants (6PPD, IPPD):** react preferentially with O₃, forming a sacrificial protective film.
- **Compounding:** blending with **EPDM** (saturated backbone) drastically improves ozone resistance.

### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Given:** $[O_3]_1 = 50$ pphm, $t_1 = 8$ h; $[O_3]_2 = 10$ pphm; first-order in O₃ → $t \propto 1/[O_3]$.

**Step 1 — Determine rate constant from initial test:**

$$
t_1 \cdot [O_3]_1 = k^{-1}
$$

$$
k^{-1} = 8 \times 50 = 400\;\text{pphm·h}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute new initiation time:**

$$
t_2 = \dfrac{400}{[O_3]_2} = \dfrac{400}{10} = 40 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_2 = 40 \text{ hours}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often wrongly assume $t \propto [O_3]$ (linear increase), giving $t_2 = 1.6$ h. The correct first-order relationship is **inverse**: $t \propto 1/[O_3]$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Aging** = irreversible property degradation; **relaxation** = reversible.
- Three aging types to master: **thermo-oxidative, ozone, photo**.
- NR ages by **chain scission** (softening); SBR/NBR by **crosslinking** (hardening).
- **Antioxidants** = primary (radical scavengers like **IPPD, 6PPD**) + secondary (peroxide decomposers like **TNP, DSTDP**).
- **Antiozonants** = wax + 6PPD; saturated rubbers (EPDM, IIR) are intrinsically ozone-resistant.
- **Arrhenius equation:** $\ln(t_1/t_2) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ — **always use Kelvin**.
- **Ozone kinetics:** first-order → $t \propto 1/[O_3]$.
- **Standard test temperatures:** 70 °C, 100 °C, 125 °C (ASTM D573).
- **Critical ozone concentration:** ≥ 5 pphm initiates cracking in strained NR.
- **Activation energy** of rubber oxidation: typically **80–110 kJ/mol** — memorize this range.
- **Avoid extrapolating Arrhenius** beyond the tested temperature window (ISO 11346).
- **Tensile retention ≥ 80 %** and **elongation retention ≥ 50 %** are industrial acceptance criteria (ASTM D573).
- **Carbon black** is a **dual-function additive** — reinforces mechanically + screens UV.
- **Photo-yellowing** in NR is due to **carbonyl chromophore** formation; HALS additives inhibit.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxidation of Metals and Radiation Damage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oxidation of Metals and Radiation Damage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Oxidation of Metals — Formal Definition

**Oxidation of metals** is the chemical reaction between a metal and an oxidizing environment (typically atmospheric oxygen, but also water vapour, CO₂, or SO₂) that produces a **metal oxide** scale on the surface. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, oxidation is classified as a **high-temperature corrosion phenomenon** and is treated under the broader study of *environmental degradation of engineering materials*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Oxidation of metals is the reaction of a metal with an oxidizing agent (chiefly O₂) to form a metal oxide, whose growth rate, morphology, and protective character are governed by thermodynamics (Ellingham diagrams), kinetics (rate laws), and the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)."*

The general reaction is written as:

$$M + \frac{n}{2}\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; M O_n$$

where **M** is the metal and **MOₙ** is the stoichiometric oxide. The standard free energy change (ΔG°) of this reaction, derived from **Ellingham diagrams**, dictates the thermodynamic spontaneity of oxidation.

## 1.2 Radiation Damage — Formal Definition

**Radiation damage** (also called **radiation-induced degradation** or **displacement damage**) refers to the permanent microstructural alterations produced in a solid material when it is exposed to **high-energy particles** such as **fast neutrons, fission fragments, charged ions, electrons, or gamma photons**. These defects degrade mechanical, thermal, electrical, and dimensional properties of materials used in nuclear reactors, space systems, and particle accelerators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Particle Terminology (KTU Module 4):**
> - **Primary Knock-on Atom (PKA):** A lattice atom displaced from its site by an incident high-energy particle.
> - **Frenkel Pair:** A vacancy + self-interstitial atom (SIA) pair created by one displacement event.
> - **Displacement Cascade:** A branching chain of secondary displacements triggered by a single PKA.
> - **Wigner Energy:** Stored potential energy in a crystal due to displaced atoms, released upon annealing.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

### 🍎 Oxidation Analogy: "The Apple Turning Brown"
Imagine slicing a fresh apple and leaving it on the kitchen counter. The exposed flesh (bright white) gradually turns brown — this is oxidation in action. The brown layer is the **oxide scale**. In metals:
- If the brown layer (oxide) is **dense and sticks** to the apple's surface (think of a thin, glossy skin on aluminium), it **stops further oxidation** → *protective behaviour* (high PBR).
- If the brown layer is **powdery and flakes off** (like rust on iron), the apple keeps browning underneath → *non-protective* (low PBR).
- If the layer **cracks and falls off in chunks**, fresh metal is exposed continuously → *catastrophic oxidation* (PBR > 2).

### ☢️ Radiation Damage Analogy: "The Marble Floor in a Crowded Hall"
Imagine a perfectly ordered **marble tile floor** in a quiet gallery. Now throw a **bowling ball** into the hall. The ball strikes a tile, knocks it out of place (creating a **vacancy**), and that displaced tile ricochets into another tile, knocking it out too — a **cascade**. The floor now has gaps, displaced tiles jammed in awkward spots (interstitials), and the symmetry is ruined. This is exactly what a high-energy neutron does inside a metal's atomic lattice in a nuclear reactor core.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this for the exam:** *Oxidation = surface chemistry problem; Radiation damage = bulk atomic-displacement problem.* The KTU board often contrasts these two degradation mechanisms in Part A questions.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** |
| Standard state (oxidation) | T, P | **298 K, 1 atm** |
| Displacement threshold energy (typical metal) | $E_d$ | **~25 eV (Fe)**, ~30 eV (Cu) |
| Bohr radius (atom size scale) | $a_0$ | **0.529 Å** |
| Lattice parameter of α-Fe | $a$ | **2.866 Å** |
| Atomic volume of Fe | $\Omega$ | **1.18 × 10⁻²⁹ m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Metal Oxidation

The oxidation of a metal proceeds in **three sequential regimes** (this is a frequent **Part B, 7-mark question** in KTU papers):

### Regime 1 — Initial / Transient Stage
- The clean metal surface is exposed to oxygen.
- Oxygen molecules **chemisorb** onto the surface, dissociate into atoms, and form a **2D chemisorbed layer**.
- A thin, sometimes amorphous, oxide nucleate film grows.

### Regime 2 — Thick Film Growth Stage
- The oxide film thickens and a **continuous barrier** forms between metal and gas.
- Further growth now requires **solid-state diffusion** of ions/electrons through the film.
- This regime is governed by **Wagner's theory** (high-T, parabolic kinetics).

### Regime 3 — Post-Oxidation / Failure Stage
- The oxide may crack, spall, or volatilize.
- Repeated scale failure leads to **linear or catastrophic kinetics**.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Diffusion Path

For the oxide scale to grow, **two species** must move in opposite directions through the scale:

| Species | Direction | Driving Force | Charge Carrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Metal cations (M²⁺) | Metal → Scale–Gas interface | Concentration gradient of M²⁺ vacancies | Cation vacancies ($V_M^{2-}$) |
| Oxygen anions (O²⁻) | Gas → Metal–Scale interface | Concentration gradient of O²⁻ vacancies | Anion vacancies ($V_O^{2+}$) |
| Electrons (e⁻) | Metal → Scale–Gas interface | Electrochemical potential gradient | Free electrons / electron holes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The slowest of these three fluxes controls the rate.** This is the heart of **Wagner's parabolic oxidation law**, and examiners love asking *"Which step is rate-limiting?"* — the answer is always the slowest diffusing species.

## 2.3 Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) — The Master Classification

The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is defined as the ratio of the molar volume of the metal oxide to the molar volume of the metal from which it was formed:

$$R_{PB} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}$$

where:
- $M_{\text{oxide}}$ = molecular weight of the oxide
- $M_{\text{metal}}$ = atomic weight of the metal
- $\rho_{\text{metal}}$ = density of the metal
- $\rho_{\text{oxide}}$ = density of the oxide
- $n$ = number of metal atoms in the oxide formula (e.g., $n=2$ for Al₂O₃)

### PBR Classification Table (High-Yield for KTU Board)

| PBR Range | Mechanical State | Protectiveness | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R < 1** | Tensile stress, porous scale | **Non-protective** | Mg, Na, K, Ba |
| **R = 1** | Stress-free, ideal | **Perfectly protective** (rare) | — |
| **1 < R < 2** | Compressive stress, adherent | **Protective** (engineering optimum) | Al, Cr, Fe, Cu, Ni |
| **R > 2** | High compressive stress, spalling | **Non-protective** | Fe (high-T to Fe₂O₃ scale), W |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Oxidation & Radiation Damage

| # | Formula / Law | Equation | Applicability / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio** | $R_{PB} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$ | Dimensionless, classify oxide protectiveness |
| 2 | **Linear rate law** | $x = k_L \cdot t$ | Porous, non-protective scales (x in m, t in s) |
| 3 | **Parabolic rate law** | $x^2 = k_p \cdot t$ | Dense, protective scales (Wagner regime) |
| 4 | **Logarithmic rate law** | $x = k_e \cdot \ln(at + 1)$ | Thin films, low T, $T < 0.4 T_m$ |
| 5 | **Arrhenius for $k_p$** | $k_p = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Q in J/mol, R = 8.314 J/mol·K |
| 6 | **Parabolic rate constant (Wagner)** | $k_p = \dfrac{2 D_{\text{eff}} \, c_{\text{eq}}}{N_A}$ | $D_{\text{eff}}$ = effective diffusivity (m²/s) |
| 7 | **Number of displaced atoms (Kinchin–Pease)** | $N_d = \dfrac{E_{\text{PKA}}}{2 E_d}$ | For $E_d < E_{\text{PKA}} < 2 E_d / \alpha$ |
| 8 | **Displacement cross-section** | $\sigma_d = \int_{E_d}^{E_{\max}} \dfrac{d\sigma(E)}{dE}\, dE$ | Barns (1 barn = 10⁻²⁴ cm²) |
| 9 | **dpa (displacements per atom)** | $\text{dpa} = \Phi \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \bar{N}_d \cdot t$ | Dose unit for radiation damage |
| 10 | **Swelling rate (void)** | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = K \, \phi^{\,n}$ | $\phi$ = dose (dpa), $n \approx 1$ to 2 |
| 11 | **Energy transferred (hard sphere)** | $T_{\max} = \dfrac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2}\, E_{\text{inc}}$ | Max PKA energy (eV) |
| 12 | **Wigner energy release** | $E_W \approx N_d \cdot \varepsilon_f$ | Stored energy (J) per unit volume |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safeguard Notice:** In the table above, vertical bars in the **dpa formula** are escaped using the *word* "dpa" instead of the symbol `\vert` to prevent markdown-renderer corruption. This is a known KTU-prep rendering issue — do not copy-paste from broken tables into your answer booklet.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gas-turbine blades (Ni-superalloys):** Form a protective Al₂O₃ (PBR ≈ 1.28) TBC layer that allows operation at $T > 1000\,°C$.
- **Reactor pressure vessels (A533B steel):** Designed for a 40-year lifetime at $\phi < 0.1$ dpa to avoid **irradiation embrittlement**.
- **Zirconium fuel cladding (Zr-4):** Forms a thin, adherent ZrO₂ (PBR ≈ 1.56) layer in PWR water, with strict limits on H pickup.
- **Spacecraft aluminium skins:** Avoid oxidation-driven pitting in low-Earth orbit by Al₂O₃ passivation, but face severe **atomic-oxygen erosion** — a separate radiation-like damage mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parabolic Oxidation Law from Fick's First Law

We model the oxide scale as a slab of thickness $x$ at time $t$. Let $c$ be the equilibrium concentration of dissolved metal (or oxygen) at the gas–scale interface, and zero at the metal–scale interface. The flux of cations through the scale is given by Fick's first law:

$$J \;=\; -D_{\text{eff}} \, \frac{dc}{dx}$$

Since $c$ varies linearly from $c_{\text{eq}}$ at $x=0$ (gas side) to $0$ at $x=x$ (metal side), the gradient is:

$$\frac{dc}{dx} \;=\; -\frac{c_{\text{eq}}}{x}$$

Therefore the flux magnitude is:

$$J \;=\; D_{\text{eff}} \, \frac{c_{\text{eq}}}{x}$$

**Step 1 — Relate flux to oxide growth rate.**
The rate of oxide thickness increase, $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$, is proportional to the flux arriving at the metal–scale interface. The proportionality constant is the inverse of the molar concentration of cations in the oxide, $N_A / \Omega$ (where $\Omega$ is the oxide's molecular volume):

$$\frac{dx}{dt} \;=\; J \cdot \frac{\Omega}{1} \;\cdot\; \frac{1}{c_{\text{ox}}}$$

For a stoichiometric oxide with $c_{\text{ox}} = 1 / \Omega_{\text{ox}}$, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} \;=\; \frac{J}{c_{\text{ox}}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{eff}} \, c_{\text{eq}}}{c_{\text{ox}} \, x}$$

**Step 2 — Define the parabolic rate constant.**
Collect all material-dependent constants into a single parameter $k_p$:

$$k_p \;\equiv\; 2 \, D_{\text{eff}} \, \frac{c_{\text{eq}}}{c_{\text{ox}}}$$

Then the growth equation becomes:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} \;=\; \frac{k_p}{2x}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\int_0^{x} 2x' \, dx' \;=\; \int_0^{t} k_p \, dt'$$

$$x^2 \;=\; k_p \cdot t$$

This is the **parabolic oxidation law** (Wagner's law), a cornerstone of KTU Module 4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the exam:** $x^2 = k_p t$ ⇒ at constant T, doubling the oxidation time **quadruples** the oxide thickness (not doubles). This is a very common "Part A" conceptual trap.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)

We start from the definition of molar volume $V_m = M / \rho$ for any phase.

**Step 1 — Molar volume of metal (atomic).**
For a metal of atomic weight $M_{\text{metal}}$ and density $\rho_{\text{metal}}$:

$$V_{\text{metal}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{metal}}}{\rho_{\text{metal}}} \quad \text{(units: m}^3\text{/mol)}$$

**Step 2 — Molar volume of the oxide (per metal atom).**
For an oxide of formula $\text{M}_a \text{O}_b$, with molecular weight $M_{\text{ox}}$ and density $\rho_{\text{ox}}$, the volume **per metal atom** is:

$$V_{\text{ox (per metal atom)}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{ox}}}{a \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$R_{PB} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{ox (per metal atom)}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{a \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$$

This is the working form KTU examiners expect in derivations.

### Worked Example: PBR for Aluminium → Al₂O₃
- $M_{\text{ox}}$ (Al₂O₃) = **101.96 g/mol**
- $M_{\text{metal}}$ (Al) = **26.98 g/mol**
- $\rho_{\text{metal}}$ (Al) = **2.70 g/cm³**
- $\rho_{\text{ox}}$ (Al₂O₃) = **3.99 g/cm³**
- $a$ (Al atoms per formula) = **2**

$$R_{PB} \;=\; \frac{101.96 \times 2.70}{2 \times 26.98 \times 3.99} \;=\; \frac{275.29}{215.30} \;\approx\; 1.28$$

**Result:** $R_{PB} = 1.28 \in (1,2)$ ⇒ **protective oxide** ✓ (consistent with aluminium's well-known corrosion resistance).

## 3.3 Derivation: Kinchin–Pease Displacement Model

The Kinchin–Pease model estimates the number of Frenkel pairs (vacancy + interstitial) generated by a single PKA of energy $E_{\text{PKA}}$.

**Step 1 — Elastic collision regime.**
A PKA of energy $E_{\text{PKA}}$ transfers energy $T$ to a secondary atom. The mean number of displacements $\bar{N}_d$ is:

$$\bar{N}_d(E_{\text{PKA}}) \;=\; \begin{cases} 0, & E_{\text{PKA}} < E_d \\[4pt] 1, & E_d \le E_{\text{PKA}} < 2 E_d / \alpha \\[4pt] \dfrac{\alpha \, E_{\text{PKA}}}{2 E_d}, & 2 E_d / \alpha \le E_{\text{PKA}} < E_{\text{inc}} / \alpha \\[4pt] \dfrac{E_{\text{inc}}}{2 E_d}, & E_{\text{PKA}} \ge E_{\text{inc}} / \alpha \end{cases}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **displacement efficiency** (≈ 0.8 for hard-sphere scattering) and $E_d$ is the threshold displacement energy (≈ 25 eV for iron).

**Step 2 — Common exam form (above the knee).**
For most practical PKA energies in a reactor, only the middle regime matters:

$$\bar{N}_d \;\approx\; \frac{E_{\text{PKA}}}{2 E_d}$$

**Worked example:** A 1 MeV PKA in iron:
$$\bar{N}_d \;\approx\; \frac{1 \times 10^6 \,\text{eV}}{2 \times 25 \,\text{eV}} \;\approx\; 20{,}000 \;\text{Frenkel pairs}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the modern Norgett–Robinson–Torrens (NRT) correction:** The NRT-dpa standard in current nuclear materials science is $\bar{N}_d^{NRT} = 0.8 \, E_{\text{PKA}} / (2 E_d)$, replacing the older Kinchin–Pease with $\alpha = 1$. KTU 2024 syllabus allows either form, but examiners prefer you to **state which model you are using**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Energy Transfer (Hard-Sphere Collision)

For an incident particle of mass $M_1$ and kinetic energy $E_{\text{inc}}$ striking a stationary lattice atom of mass $M_2$, classical two-body kinematics give the maximum energy transferable in a head-on elastic collision:

$$T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \, E_{\text{inc}}$$

**Step 1 — Derivation outline.**
Conservation of energy and momentum in a head-on 1D elastic collision:
$$M_1 v_i \;=\; M_1 v_1' + M_2 v_2'$$
$$\frac{1}{2} M_1 v_i^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{2} M_1 (v_1')^2 + \frac{1}{2} M_2 (v_2')^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $v_2'$ (head-on).**
Solving the system yields:
$$v_2' \;=\; \frac{2 M_1}{M_1 + M_2} \, v_i$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kinetic energy.**
$$T_2 \;=\; \frac{1}{2} M_2 (v_2')^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{2} M_2 \left(\frac{2 M_1}{M_1 + M_2}\right)^2 v_i^2$$

Substitute $E_{\text{inc}} = \tfrac{1}{2} M_1 v_i^2$:

$$T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \, E_{\text{inc}}$$

**Engineering insight:** A neutron ($M_1 \approx 1$ u) in a heavy-metal lattice transfers at most $\approx 4 M_1 M_2 / M_2^2 \approx 4/M_2$ of its energy per collision — i.e., a 1 MeV neutron in iron ($M_2 = 56$ u) gives $T_{\max} \approx 69$ keV. That single PKA then cascades into ~1400 Frenkel pairs.

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Mechanism | Rate Law | Slope of $\log x$ vs $\log t$ | When Observed | Example Material |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | $x = k_L t$ | 1 | Porous, cracked scale | Mg, Na, K |
| Parabolic | $x^2 = k_p t$ | 0.5 | Dense, protective scale (Wagner) | Cu, Fe, Ni |
| Logarithmic | $x = k_e \ln(at+1)$ | $\to 0$ (rapidly) | Low T ($< 0.4 T_m$), thin film | Al, Zn (RT) |
| Cubic | $x^3 = k_c t$ | 0.33 | Intermediate regimes | Ti (some T) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Oxidation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clean Metal Surface] --> B[O2 Adsorption]
    B --> C[Chemisorbed O Layer]
    C --> D[2D Oxide Nucleation]
    D --> E[Thin Continuous Oxide Film]
    E --> F{Is Film Protective?}
    F -- Yes PBR 1 to 2 --> G[Parabolic Growth x2 equals kp times t]
    F -- No PBR less than 1 --> H[Porous Scale Linear Kinetics]
    F -- No PBR greater than 2 --> I[Cracking and Spalling]
    G --> J[Diffusion Limited Wagner Regime]
    H --> K[Rapid Linear Attack]
    I --> L[Catastrophic Oxidation]
    J --> M[Stable Protective Scale]
    K --> N[Material Failure]
    L --> N
    M --> O[Long Term Service]
    N --> P[Replacement Required]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Radiation Damage Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Incident Particle Neutron or Ion] --> P1[Energy Transfer Tmax]
    P1 --> P2[Primary Knock On Atom PKA Created]
    P2 --> P3{PKA Energy exceeds Ed}
    P3 -- No --> P4[No Displacement]
    P3 -- Yes --> P5[Displacement Cascade Initiated]
    P5 --> P6[Secondary Knock On Atoms]
    P6 --> P7[Branch Sub Cascade Tree]
    P7 --> P8[Final Frenkel Pairs Vacancy plus SIA]
    P8 --> P9[Point Defect Accumulation]
    P9 --> P10[Cluster Formation Voids Loops]
    P10 --> P11[Swelling Embrittlement Creep]
    P11 --> P12[Service Life Reduction]
    P8 --> P13[Stored Wigner Energy]
    P13 --> P14[Annealing Releases Energy]
    P14 --> P15[Defect Recovery]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Oxide Scale Growth (Text Description)

For students preparing a hand-drawn diagram in the answer booklet, the standard KTU-approved sketch has:

1. A horizontal **metal substrate** at the bottom.
2. An **oxide scale** of thickness $x$ on top.
3. **Oxygen gas** above the scale.
4. Arrows showing **cations M²⁺** moving upward through the scale.
5. Arrows showing **anions O²⁻** moving downward through the scale.
6. Arrows showing **electrons e⁻** moving upward.
7. Labels for the **three interfaces**: M/MOₙ, MOₙ bulk, MOₙ/gas.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** When drawing the oxide scale, explicitly label the **charge of each defect** ($V_M^{2-}$, $V_O^{2+}$, $e^-$, $h^+$). KTU examiners award 1–2 marks simply for correct defect notation in Part B answers.

## 4.4 Decision Flow: Identifying the Rate Law from a Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Plot x versus t Data] --> R2[Take Logarithm of Both Axes]
    R2 --> R3[Measure Slope n]
    R3 --> R4A[Slope equals 1] --> RA[Linear Law x equals kL times t]
    R3 --> R4B[Slope equals 0.5] --> RB[Parabolic Law x2 equals kp times t]
    R3 --> R4C[Slope equals 0.33] --> RC[Cubic Law x3 equals kc times t]
    R3 --> R4D[Slope decreasing toward 0] --> RD[Logarithmic Law]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio. State its significance in determining the protective nature of an oxide scale formed on a metal surface.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR), $R_{PB}$, is defined as the ratio of the molar volume of the metal oxide to the molar volume of the metal from which the oxide is formed:

$$R_{PB} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}}$$

**Significance:** It indicates the **mechanical compatibility** between the oxide and the parent metal. If $R_{PB} < 1$, the oxide is too thin to cover the metal and is **non-protective** (e.g., Mg, Na). If $1 < R_{PB} < 2$, compressive stresses make the oxide **adherent and protective** (e.g., Al, Cr, Fe). If $R_{PB} > 2$, excessive compressive stress causes **spalling** and the scale is non-protective (e.g., W, some Fe scales at high T).

> **Marking key:** 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the three-range classification with examples.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between linear and parabolic oxidation rate laws. Under what conditions is each observed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Linear Law | Parabolic Law |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $x = k_L t$ | $x^2 = k_p t$ |
| Rate-determining step | **Chemical reaction** at the surface (no diffusion barrier) | **Solid-state diffusion** of ions/electrons through the scale |
| Type of oxide | **Porous, non-adherent, cracked** (PBR < 1 or > 2) | **Dense, adherent, protective** (1 < PBR < 2) |
| Typical example | Mg, Na oxidation; Fe at very high T | Cu, Fe, Ni at moderate-to-high T |

> **Marking key:** 1 mark for the equations, 1 mark for rate-determining step, 1 mark for example(s).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **Wagner mechanism of high-temperature oxidation** of metals. Explain how the parabolic rate law arises from the diffusion of ionic and electronic defects through the oxide scale. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the oxidation of **copper to Cu₂O**, the following data are available:
- Atomic weight of Cu: **63.55 g/mol**
- Density of Cu: **8.96 g/cm³**
- Molecular weight of Cu₂O: **143.09 g/mol**
- Density of Cu₂O: **6.00 g/cm³**
- Cu atoms per formula of Cu₂O: **2**

**Calculate the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio and comment on the protectiveness of the Cu₂O scale.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

1. **Setup** (2 marks): Draw a labelled cross-section showing the **metal**, the **oxide scale of thickness $x$**, the **gas phase**, and arrows for the three diffusing species — cations ($M^{2+}$), anions ($O^{2-}$), and electrons ($e^-$).

2. **Wagner's postulates** (2 marks):
   - The oxide scale is **dense, adherent, and stoichiometric**.
   - Growth is controlled by **lattice diffusion** of point defects (vacancies or interstitials).
   - The rate-limiting step is the **slowest-diffusing species** (usually the ion, not the electron).

3. **Derivation** (2 marks): From Fick's first law with linear concentration gradient $c_{\text{eq}}/x$:
$$\frac{dx}{dt} \;=\; \frac{k_p}{2x} \;\Longrightarrow\; x^2 \;=\; k_p t$$

4. **Engineering consequence** (1 mark): The parabolic law implies that oxidation **slows down with time**, which is why Wagner-regime scales give long service life in gas turbines, heat exchangers, etc.

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Apply the PBR formula.**

$$R_{PB} \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{ox}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{a \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ox}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$R_{PB} \;=\; \frac{143.09 \,\text{g/mol} \times 8.96 \,\text{g/cm}^3}{2 \times 63.55 \,\text{g/mol} \times 6.00 \,\text{g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator calculation.**

$$143.09 \times 8.96 \;=\; 1282.09 \,\text{(g/cm}^3 \cdot \text{g/mol)}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator calculation.**

$$2 \times 63.55 \times 6.00 \;=\; 762.60 \,\text{(g/cm}^3 \cdot \text{g/mol)}$$

**Step 5 — Final ratio.**

$$R_{PB} \;=\; \frac{1282.09}{762.60} \;\approx\; 1.68$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (2 marks).**
Since $1 < R_{PB} = 1.68 < 2$, the Cu₂O scale is **protective** and the oxidation of copper follows a **parabolic rate law** in the Wagner regime. Compressive stresses in the scale keep it adherent to the metal.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of radiation damage** in materials. Describe the formation of **Frenkel pairs**, **displacement cascades**, and the resulting **point defects** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **1 MeV neutron** strikes an iron lattice atom at rest. Given:
- Atomic mass of neutron: **1 u**
- Atomic mass of iron (⁵⁶Fe): **56 u**
- Threshold displacement energy of Fe: **E_d = 25 eV**
- Displacement efficiency: **α = 0.8**

**Calculate:**
1. The **maximum energy transferred** to the iron PKA.
2. The **number of Frenkel pairs** produced using the Kinchin–Pease model.
3. The **NRT-corrected** number of displacements. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

1. **Particle–matter interaction** (2 marks): When a high-energy particle (neutron, ion) enters a crystal, it transfers kinetic energy to a lattice atom via elastic (and sometimes inelastic) collisions.

2. **PKA and threshold** (2 marks): If the transferred energy exceeds the **threshold displacement energy $E_d$** (≈ 25 eV for Fe), the struck atom leaves its lattice site, becoming a **Primary Knock-on Atom (PKA)** and leaving behind a **vacancy**. It eventually comes to rest in an interstitial position, forming a **Frenkel pair** (vacancy + self-interstitial).

3. **Displacement cascade** (2 marks): A high-energy PKA (tens of keV) strikes further atoms, each of which may produce more PKAs. This branching forms a **displacement cascade**, generating thousands of Frenkel pairs within a few picoseconds in a small volume (~100 nm).

4. **Microstructural consequences** (1 mark): Vacancies coalesce into **voids** (causing **swelling**); interstitials cluster into **dislocation loops** (causing **embrittlement** and **irradiation creep**).

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**1. Maximum energy transfer to PKA:**

$$T_{\max} \;=\; \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \, E_{\text{inc}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the mass factor.**

$$\frac{4 \times 1 \times 56}{(1 + 56)^2} \;=\; \frac{224}{3249} \;\approx\; 0.0689$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by incident energy.**

$$T_{\max} \;=\; 0.0689 \times 1 \times 10^6 \,\text{eV} \;\approx\; 68{,}900 \,\text{eV} \;\approx\; 68.9 \,\text{keV}$$

**2. Number of Frenkel pairs (Kinchin–Pease, α = 0.8):**

$$\bar{N}_d \;=\; \frac{\alpha \, E_{\text{PKA}}}{2 E_d} \;=\; \frac{0.8 \times 68{,}900}{2 \times 25} \;=\; \frac{55{,}120}{50} \;\approx\; 1102 \;\text{pairs}$$

**3. NRT-corrected displacements:**

$$\bar{N}_d^{NRT} \;=\; \frac{0.8 \times 68{,}900}{2 \times 25} \;\approx\; 1102 \;\text{(numerically same, as NRT uses 0.8)}$$

> **Examiner note:** If the student had used the older form with $\alpha = 1$, the answer would be $68{,}900 / 50 = 1378$ pairs. Both are accepted if the model is stated.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state units and the model used** in Part B derivations → **−1 to −2 marks**. Always write: *"Using the Kinchin–Pease model with α = 0.8…"*
> 2. **Mixing up PBR ranges**: Many students write "$R_{PB} > 1$ is protective" — wrong! It is the **range 1 < R < 2** that is protective, **not** $R > 1$ alone.
> 3. **Confusing PBR with the oxide-to-metal volume *expansion* factor**: PBR is the ratio of *volumes*, not a stress or strain.
> 4. **Forgetting the $4M_1M_2/(M_1+M_2)^2$ factor** in $T_{\max}$: Simply equating $T_{\max} = E_{\text{inc}}$ is **wrong** for neutrons — they transfer at most a few percent per collision.
> 5. **Omitting the sketch** in oxidation mechanism answers: KTU board deducts up to **−3 marks** if the diagram (M/scale/gas with arrows) is missing.
> 6. **Calling "displacement damage" the same as "ionizing radiation damage"**: The KTU Module 4 distinction is critical — displacement = atomic knock-on (non-ionizing), ionization = electron–hole pairs. Examiners look for this nuance.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Oxidation** = metal + O₂ → metal oxide; **driven by ΔG°** (Ellingham diagram), **rate-limited by diffusion** (Wagner).
- ✅ **PBR** = $V_{\text{ox}}/V_{\text{metal}}$; protective only when **1 < R < 2**; outside this range → porous or spalled scale.
- ✅ **Linear law** ($x = k_L t$) → porous scale; **Parabolic law** ($x^2 = k_p t$) → dense, protective scale; **Logarithmic** → low-T thin films.
- ✅ **Parabolic rate constant** follows Arrhenius: $k_p = k_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$; activation energy $Q$ is a high-yield exam quantity.
- ✅ **Wagner oxidation** requires three simultaneous fluxes: cations, anions, and electrons — **the slowest controls the rate**.
- ✅ **Radiation damage** = permanent displacement of atoms from lattice sites by high-energy particles.
- ✅ **PKA** = primary knock-on atom; **Frenkel pair** = vacancy + interstitial; **cascade** = branching chain of displacements.
- ✅ **Threshold displacement energy** $E_d \approx 25$ eV for Fe — **must be exceeded** for damage.
- ✅ **Kinchin–Pease**: $\bar{N}_d = E_{\text{PKA}} / (2 E_d)$ (with $\alpha$ correction for hard-sphere).
- ✅ **Maximum energy transfer** in elastic collision: $T_{\max} = \dfrac{4M_1M_2}{(M_1+M_2)^2} E_{\text{inc}}$.
- ✅ **dpa** (displacements per atom) is the standard dose unit in nuclear materials engineering.
- ✅ **Effects of radiation damage**: **void swelling**, **irradiation embrittlement**, **irradiation creep**, **dimensional instability**, **stored Wigner energy**.
- ✅ **Real-world link**: Al₂O₃ on Al (PBR ≈ 1.28) ⇒ protective; Fe₂O₃ on Fe at very high T (PBR > 2) ⇒ spalling; ZrO₂ on Zr-4 cladding (PBR ≈ 1.56) ⇒ protective in PWRs.
- ✅ **Exam-favorite diagram**: M | scale (thickness $x$) | gas with arrows for M²⁺ ↑, O²⁻ ↓, e⁻ ↑.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for PBR** — *"One to two is the safest range for you."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting the Selection of Materials for Engineering Purposes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting the Selection of Materials for Engineering Purposes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> **Material Selection** is the systematic, multi-criteria decision-making process in engineering design wherein a designer identifies, evaluates, and chooses the most appropriate engineering material (metal, alloy, polymer, ceramic, or composite) from a set of available candidates, such that the selected material satisfies the **functional performance requirements**, **manufacturing constraints**, **economic viability**, **service environment compatibility**, **safety regulations**, and **sustainability mandates** of the intended application over its entire design life.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, material selection is treated as a **design-time engineering decision** that directly maps to the **Course Outcome (CO)** concerning the ability to correlate material properties with practical engineering applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Job Interview" Analogy

> [!NOTE] Intuitive Breakdown
> Imagine you are an HR manager hiring an engineer for a critical project. You would never pick a candidate based on just *one* trait (say, height). You would weigh **skills** (mechanical strength), **experience** (corrosion history), **fitness for the job environment** (thermal stability), **salary demand** (cost), and **team compatibility** (manufacturability with existing processes).

**Material selection works exactly the same way.** An engineer does not select a material because it is "strong" alone. They evaluate a **portfolio of properties** and weigh them against a **portfolio of requirements**. A material that is strong but corrodes in seawater (e.g., plain carbon steel) is rejected for marine use in favor of stainless steel, even if the latter is more expensive.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Material Selection (KTU Conceptual Foundation)

Every material selection problem in the KTU syllabus is anchored on three foundational pillars:

| Pillar | Description | KTU Standard Metrics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Performance** | Can the material do the *job*? | Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$), Toughness ($K_{IC}$), Hardness (HV) |
| **Processability** | Can we *make* the part from it? | Castability, Weldability, Machinability rating |
| **Economy** | Can we *afford* it and *sustain* it? | Cost per kg ($$\$/kg$$), Recyclability index, Availability |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in Selection

> [!NOTE] Standard Reference Values (for KTU numerical/analytical questions)
> - **Density of water:** $1 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Density of steel:** $\approx 7.85 \, \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Density of aluminum alloys:** $\approx 2.7 \, \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Density of titanium alloys:** $\approx 4.5 \, \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Specific strength** (strength-to-weight ratio) is the **single most quoted metric** in aerospace selection problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Primary Factors Affecting Material Selection

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly groups selection criteria into the following eight categories. Each must be addressed in any 14-mark descriptive question.

### Factor 1: **Mechanical Properties**
The first filter in any structural application.
- **Strength** (Tensile, Compressive, Shear, Fatigue) — Does it withstand the applied load?
- **Toughness** — Can it absorb impact without brittle fracture?
- **Hardness** — Will it resist surface wear, indentation, or abrasion?
- **Stiffness** (Young's Modulus $E$) — Will it resist elastic deformation under load?
- **Fatigue Limit** — Can it survive cyclic loading (e.g., shafts, springs)?

> [!NOTE] KTU Examiner Insight
> For a question like *"Select a material for an aircraft wing spar,"* the expected answer must mention **high specific strength** and **fatigue resistance** — not just "high strength."

### Factor 2: **Physical Properties**
- **Density** ($\rho$): Critical for weight-sensitive applications (aerospace, automotive).
- **Melting Point**: Determines the upper service temperature.
- **Thermal Conductivity** ($k$): Required for heat exchangers (high) or insulators (low).
- **Electrical Conductivity**: Required for conductors (copper) or insulators (PVC).
- **Thermal Expansion Coefficient** ($\alpha$): Critical for bimetallic strips, precision instruments.

### Factor 3: **Chemical Properties**
- **Corrosion Resistance** — Resistance to oxidation, acids, alkalis, and salts.
- **Oxidation Resistance at High Temperatures** — Critical for turbine blades (Ni-based superalloys).
- **Chemical Inertness** — Required for biomedical implants, food processing.

### Factor 4: **Manufacturing / Fabricability Considerations**
- **Castability**: Can it be cast into complex shapes?
- **Weldability**: Can it be joined by welding without cracking?
- **Machinability**: Can it be easily cut, drilled, and turned?
- **Formability**: Can it be rolled, forged, extruded, or drawn?
- **Heat Treatability**: Can its properties be enhanced by heat treatment?

### Factor 5: **Economic Considerations**
- **Initial Material Cost** (raw material price per kg).
- **Fabrication Cost** (machining, joining, finishing cost).
- **Total Life-Cycle Cost** (initial + maintenance + replacement).
- **Availability** in required size, shape, and quantity.

### Factor 6: **Service Conditions / Environmental Factors**
- Operating temperature range.
- Exposure to UV, humidity, chemicals, radiation.
- Load type (static, dynamic, impact, fatigue).
- Presence of abrasives or erosive media.

### Factor 7: **Aesthetic and Ergonomic Factors**
- Surface finish, color, texture.
- Feel, weight-in-hand (consumer products).
- Optical properties (transparency for lenses).

### Factor 8: **Safety, Reliability, and Sustainability**
- **Failure consequences** (catastrophic vs. minor).
- **Recyclability** and environmental impact.
- **Toxicity** and disposal regulations.
- **Regulatory compliance** (e.g., FDA for medical, BIS for structural).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] Key Quantitative Metrics for Selection Problems
> The following table summarizes the **mathematically evaluable metrics** that frequently appear in KTU numerical and analytical questions.

| **Metric** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Engineering Significance** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Specific Strength** | $SS = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho}$ | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ or $\text{Nm/kg}$ | Aerospace selection (high value ⇒ light + strong) |
| **Specific Stiffness** | $SE = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Beam deflection, structural rigidity per unit mass |
| **Strength-to-Weight Ratio** | $SWR = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Dimensionless / numeric | Direct comparison of lightweight materials |
| **Cost Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{\text{Cost per kg}}{\text{Yield Strength}}$ | $\text{Rs} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{MPa}^{-1}$ | Lower value ⇒ economical performer |
| **Wear Resistance Index** | $K = \dfrac{H}{k}$ (H = Hardness, k = wear coefficient) | Dimensionless | Material ranking for tribological use |
| **Safety Factor (n)** | $n = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$ | Dimensionless | Built into selection margin |
| **Fatigue Life (Basquin)** | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | MPa, cycles | Cyclic loading endurance |

> [!WARNING] LaTeX/Markdown Safety
> All absolute value separators and "divides" are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table integrity. Never use the vertical pipe `|` character inside a table row.

## 2.3 The Material Selection Trade-off Curve (Ashby Concept)

> [!NOTE] KTU 2024 Advanced Concept
> The **Ashby Material Selection Charts** plot one material property against another (e.g., Strength vs. Density, or Modulus vs. Cost) on log-log scales. The **slope of the selection line** is determined by the design requirement.

For a component requiring **minimum mass for a given stiffness** (e.g., a stiff, light bracket), the selection line on an $E$ vs. $\rho$ chart has a slope of:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{d(\log E)}{d(\log \rho)} = 1$$

This corresponds to selecting materials with the **highest $E/\rho$** (specific stiffness). Such a line is mathematically described as:

$$E \geq C_1 \cdot \rho^1$$

where $C_1$ is a constant determined by the loading geometry. The same logic applies to springs (slope = 2), ties (slope = 0.5), and beams (slope varies with loading type).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — The Weighted Property Method

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 14-Mark Analytical Tool
> When a KTU question asks *"Select the most suitable material for a given application with justification,"* the **Weighted Property Method** is the expected structured response.

### Step 1: List Candidate Materials
Identify $n$ candidate materials (typically $n = 3$ to $5$).

### Step 2: List Critical Properties
Identify $m$ critical properties required for the application.

### Step 3: Assign Weighting Factors ($w_i$)
For each property $i$, assign a weight $w_i$ based on its importance, such that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i = 1$$

For example, in an aircraft wing application:
- Strength weight: $w_1 = 0.40$
- Density weight: $w_2 = 0.30$
- Corrosion resistance weight: $w_3 = 0.15$
- Cost weight: $w_4 = 0.10$
- Fatigue weight: $w_5 = 0.05$

### Step 4: Normalize Property Values
For each property, normalize the value of material $j$ in property $i$ using:

$$P_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{\text{Value}_{ij}}{\text{Max Value}_i} \quad \text{(for "higher is better" properties)}$$

$$P_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{\text{Min Value}_i}{\text{Value}_{ij}} \quad \text{(for "lower is better" properties like cost, density)}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Performance Index
The performance index for each material $j$ is:

$$\gamma_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i \cdot P_{ij}^{\text{norm}}$$

### Step 6: Rank and Select
The material with the **highest $\gamma_j$** is selected.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Material Selection for a Connecting Rod

> [!NOTE] KTU-Style Numerical Problem
> **Problem:** Three candidate materials for an automobile connecting rod are given. Compute the performance index and recommend the best material.

| Property (Weight) | **Al Alloy 7075** | **Steel AISI 4140** | **Ti Alloy Ti-6Al-4V** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Yield Strength, MPa ($w = 0.35$) | 503 | 655 | 880 |
| Density, g/cm³ ($w = 0.25$, lower better) | 2.81 | 7.85 | 4.43 |
| Fatigue Limit, MPa ($w = 0.20$) | 159 | 360 | 510 |
| Cost Index ($w = 0.10$, lower better) | 0.20 | 0.05 | 1.00 |
| Corrosion Resistance ($w = 0.10$, scale 1–10) | 8 | 4 | 9 |

### Step 1: Normalize "Higher is Better" Properties
- Yield Strength max = 880 → Normalize: 503/880 = **0.572**, 655/880 = **0.744**, 880/880 = **1.000**
- Fatigue Limit max = 510 → Normalize: 159/510 = **0.312**, 360/510 = **0.706**, 510/510 = **1.000**
- Corrosion Resistance max = 9 → Normalize: 8/9 = **0.889**, 4/9 = **0.444**, 9/9 = **1.000**

### Step 2: Normalize "Lower is Better" Properties
- Density min = 2.81 → Normalize: 2.81/2.81 = **1.000**, 2.81/7.85 = **0.358**, 2.81/4.43 = **0.634**
- Cost Index min = 0.05 → Normalize: 0.05/0.20 = **0.250**, 0.05/0.05 = **1.000**, 0.05/1.00 = **0.050**

### Step 3: Compute Performance Index for Al Alloy 7075

$$\gamma_{Al} = (0.35 \times 0.572) + (0.25 \times 1.000) + (0.20 \times 0.312) + (0.10 \times 0.250) + (0.10 \times 0.889)$$

$$\gamma_{Al} = 0.2002 + 0.2500 + 0.0624 + 0.0250 + 0.0889$$

$$\gamma_{Al} = 0.6265$$

### Step 4: Compute Performance Index for Steel AISI 4140

$$\gamma_{St} = (0.35 \times 0.744) + (0.25 \times 0.358) + (0.20 \times 0.706) + (0.10 \times 1.000) + (0.10 \times 0.444)$$

$$\gamma_{St} = 0.2604 + 0.0895 + 0.1412 + 0.1000 + 0.0444$$

$$\gamma_{St} = 0.6355$$

### Step 5: Compute Performance Index for Ti Alloy Ti-6Al-4V

$$\gamma_{Ti} = (0.35 \times 1.000) + (0.25 \times 0.634) + (0.20 \times 1.000) + (0.10 \times 0.050) + (0.10 \times 1.000)$$

$$\gamma_{Ti} = 0.3500 + 0.1585 + 0.2000 + 0.0050 + 0.1000$$

$$\gamma_{Ti} = 0.8135$$

### Step 6: Final Ranking and Selection

| Material | $\gamma$ | Rank |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | **0.8135** | **1st (Selected)** |
| Steel AISI 4140 | 0.6355 | 2nd |
| Al Alloy 7075 | 0.6265 | 3rd |

> [!IMPORTANT] Conclusion
> **Ti-6Al-4V** is the optimal material for a high-performance connecting rod despite its high cost, because the weighted importance of strength, fatigue, and density dominates the cost penalty. For a *cost-driven* mass-market car, **Steel 4140** would be selected (cost weight re-assigned to 0.40).

## 3.3 Analytical Selection Logic Tree

| If the application requires... | Select materials with... | Typical choices |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Minimum weight (aerospace) | High specific strength | Al, Ti, CFRP composites |
| High temperature (turbines) | High melting point + creep resistance | Ni-superalloys, ceramics |
| Corrosive environment (marine) | High Cr content / passivation | Stainless steel, Ti, FRP |
| Electrical conductor | High electrical conductivity | Cu, Al, Ag |
| Electrical insulator | High resistivity | PVC, PTFE, ceramics |
| Spring / elastic energy | High elastic limit + fatigue | Spring steel, beryllium-copper |
| Cutting tool | High hardness + wear resistance | WC-Co cermets, HSS, ceramics |
| Transparent window | Optical transparency | Soda-lime glass, PMMA |
| Biomedical implant | Biocompatibility + corrosion | Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, PEEK |
