# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]
**Q: List any four factors that affect the selection of materials for engineering applications. Briefly explain the role of "specific strength" in aerospace material selection.**

**Model Answer:**

The four primary factors affecting material selection are:
1. **Mechanical Properties:** Strength, toughness, hardness, fatigue resistance — the material must withstand applied loads without failure.
2. **Physical Properties:** Density, melting point, thermal and electrical conductivity — these determine suitability for thermal, electrical, or weight-sensitive applications.
3. **Chemical Properties:** Corrosion and oxidation resistance — critical for components exposed to aggressive environments.
4. **Economic Factors:** Cost of raw material, fabrication, and life-cycle maintenance.

**Role of Specific Strength in Aerospace:**
Specific strength is defined as the ratio of ultimate tensile strength to density:

$$SS = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho}$$

In aerospace applications, every additional kilogram of structural weight reduces payload capacity and increases fuel consumption. Therefore, materials with **high specific strength** are preferred because they deliver high load-bearing capacity per unit mass. Typical examples include **aluminum alloys (e.g., Al 7075)**, **titanium alloys (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V)**, and **carbon-fiber-reinforced polymers (CFRP)**.

> [!NOTE] (3 marks: 1.5 marks for factors + 1.5 marks for specific strength explanation)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]
**Q: Explain why "corrosion resistance" is a critical selection factor for materials used in marine and chemical process industries. Give two examples of corrosion-resistant materials.**

**Model Answer:**

Corrosion resistance is critical in marine and chemical process industries because:
- Components are continuously exposed to **aggressive ions** (Cl⁻ in seawater, H⁺ in acids).
- Failure due to corrosion can lead to **catastrophic accidents, environmental contamination, and economic loss**.
- Frequent replacement of corroded parts increases **downtime and maintenance cost**.

**Mechanism of Protection:** Corrosion-resistant materials form a **passive oxide layer** on their surface that prevents further reaction. For example, stainless steel (e.g., AISI 316) forms a Cr₂O₃ passive film; titanium forms a TiO₂ layer.

**Two examples:**
1. **Austenitic Stainless Steel (AISI 316L):** Contains Mo for resistance to chloride pitting; used in chemical tanks, marine propellers.
2. **Titanium (Grade 2 / Ti-6Al-4V):** Excellent resistance to seawater and chlorides; used in submarine hulls, heat exchangers, desalination plants.

> [!NOTE] (3 marks: 1.5 marks for explanation + 1 mark each for examples)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 4 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **mechanical, physical, and chemical properties** that govern the selection of engineering materials. Support your answer with **two suitable engineering examples** for each property category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **weighted property method** (analytical matrix), explain how an engineer would systematically select a material for an **automotive crankshaft**. List at least four candidate materials and four key properties. Show the performance index calculation for at least two materials. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Mechanical Properties (2.5 marks):**
These properties determine a material's response to applied forces.
- **Strength (Tensile/Yield/Fatigue):** Capacity to withstand load without permanent deformation or fracture. Example: **AISI 1040 steel** for axles; **Al 7075** for aircraft structural members.
- **Toughness:** Ability to absorb energy and plastically deform before fracture. Example: **Mild steel** for crash-resistant car frames (as opposed to brittle cast iron).
- **Hardness:** Resistance to indentation and wear. Example: **Through-hardened EN31 steel** for ball bearings; **WC-Co cermet** for cutting tool inserts.
- **Fatigue Limit:** Endurance under cyclic loading. Example: **Spring steel (AISI 5160)** for automotive leaf springs; **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V** for jet engine compressor blades.

**2. Physical Properties (2.5 marks):**
These determine thermal, electrical, magnetic, and mass-related behavior.
- **Density (Low):** Required for weight-sensitive applications. Example: **Al alloy 6061** for aircraft fuselage skin; **Magnesium AZ91** for laptop housings.
- **High Thermal Conductivity:** Required for heat exchangers. Example: **Pure copper** for condenser tubes; **Al 1100** for cooking utensils.
- **Electrical Conductivity:** Example: **Pure Cu (IACS 100%)** for power cables; **Al 1350** for overhead transmission lines.
- **Low Thermal Expansion:** Example: **Invar (Fe-36Ni)** for precision instrument components; **Carbon-fiber composites** for space structures.

**3. Chemical Properties (2 marks):**
- **Corrosion Resistance:** Ability to resist degradation in aggressive environments. Example: **AISI 316L stainless steel** for marine fasteners; **PTFE (Teflon)** for chemical storage tanks.
- **Oxidation Resistance at High Temperatures:** Example: **Inconel 718 (Ni-Cr-Fe superalloy)** for gas turbine blades; **Silicon carbide (SiC)** for rocket nozzle liners.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify Application Requirements (1 mark):**
An automotive crankshaft operates under:
- High cyclic fatigue loading (10⁷–10⁸ cycles per vehicle life).
- Torsional and bending stresses.
- Lubricated sliding contact with bearings.
- Moderate temperatures (80–150 °C).
- Must be cost-effective for mass production.

**Step 2 — List Candidate Materials (1 mark):**

| Candidate | Grade | Reason for Inclusion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | AISI 1045 Carbon Steel | Industry standard, cheap, hardenable |
| 2 | AISI 4140 Alloy Steel | Higher fatigue strength, Cr-Mo toughening |
| 3 | AISI 4340 Ni-Cr-Mo Steel | Ultra-high strength, aerospace grade |
| 4 | Ductile Cast Iron (SG Iron) | Good damping, cheaper |

**Step 3 — List Key Properties and Assign Weights (1 mark):**

| Property | Weight ($w_i$) | Direction |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Fatigue Strength | 0.40 | Higher is better |
| Toughness ($K_{IC}$) | 0.25 | Higher is better |
| Cost | 0.20 | Lower is better |
| Machinability | 0.10 | Higher is better |
| Wear Resistance | 0.05 | Higher is better |
| **Total** | **1.00** | — |

**Step 4 — Tabulate Property Values and Normalize (2 marks):**

| Property (Weight) | 1045 Steel | 4140 Steel | 4340 Steel | SG Iron |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Fatigue Strength, MPa ($w = 0.40$) | 280 | 480 | 600 | 220 |
| Toughness, MPa√m ($w = 0.25$) | 50 | 65 | 80 | 35 |
| Cost Index, $/kg ($w = 0.20$, lower better) | 0.80 | 1.20 | 2.50 | 0.60 |
| Machinability Rating % ($w = 0.10$) | 60 | 55 | 40 | 70 |
| Wear Resistance (HV) ($w = 0.05$) | 200 | 280 | 350 | 180 |

**Normalize to "Higher is Better" convention** (Cost is inverted):

| Normalized | 1045 | 4140 | 4340 | SG Iron |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Fatigue (max = 600) | 0.467 | 0.800 | 1.000 | 0.367 |
| Toughness (max = 80) | 0.625 | 0.813 | 1.000 | 0.438 |
| Cost (min = 0.60, inverted) | 0.750 | 0.500 | 0.240 | 1.000 |
| Machinability (max = 70) | 0.857 | 0.786 | 0.571 | 1.000 |
| Wear (max = 350) | 0.571 | 0.800 | 1.000 | 0.514 |

**Step 5 — Compute Performance Index for AISI 1045 (1 mark):**

$$\gamma_{1045} = (0.40 \times 0.467) + (0.25 \times 0.625) + (0.20 \times 0.750) + (0.10 \times 0.857) + (0.05 \times 0.571)$$

$$\gamma_{1045} = 0.1868 + 0.1563 + 0.1500 + 0.0857 + 0.0286$$

$$\gamma_{1045} = 0.6074$$

**Step 6 — Compute Performance Index for AISI 4140 (1 mark):**

$$\gamma_{4140} = (0.40 \times 0.800) + (0.25 \times 0.813) + (0.20 \times 0.500) + (0.10 \times 0.786) + (0.05 \times 0.800)$$

$$\gamma_{4140} = 0.3200 + 0.2033 + 0.1000 + 0.0786 + 0.0400$$

$$\gamma_{4140} = 0.7419$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\gamma_{4140} > \gamma_{1045}$ (and similarly for 4340/SG Iron), **AISI 4140 alloy steel** is the recommended cost-optimized choice. If a high-performance racing application is considered (cost weight reduced to 0.05), **AISI 4340** wins due to its superior fatigue and toughness.

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning
> Common student mistakes on this question:
> - **Forgetting to invert "lower is better" properties** like cost and density during normalization. Examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark.
> - **Weights not summing to 1.0** — this immediately invalidates the index. Always verify $\sum w_i = 1$.
> - **Listing only properties without values and weights** — descriptive answers without numerical support lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Picking the most expensive material blindly** — you must justify the choice based on the computed $\gamma$, not intuition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — KTU 2024 Pattern, Module 4]

**Q: (a)** Describe the **Ashby material selection methodology** for engineering design. Explain how a **log-log property chart** (e.g., Young's modulus vs. Density) is used to identify an optimum material class for a **stiff, lightweight beam**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Select the most appropriate material for each of the following applications, justifying your choice on the basis of at least **three selection factors**: **(i)** Gas turbine blade, **(ii)** Submarine hull, **(iii)** Cutting tool for machining steel, **(iv)** Polymer for a flexible cable insulation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Ashby's Methodology (3.5 marks):**
Developed by **Prof. Michael Ashby (University of Cambridge)**, the Ashby method provides a **systematic, chart-based framework** for materials selection that addresses the limitations of single-property comparison. The procedure consists of four steps:

1. **Translation:** Convert the design requirements into a **function** (e.g., "store elastic energy per unit mass") and **constraints** (e.g., "must not yield under service load").
2. **Screening:** Eliminate materials that violate the constraints (e.g., melting point too low, corrosion unacceptable).
3. **Ranking:** Use an **objective function** (also called the **material index**) to rank remaining candidates. For a stiff, light beam, the index is $E^{1/3}/\rho$ (derived from deflection analysis of a beam under bending).
4. **Supporting Information:** Apply additional criteria — cost, availability, manufacturability, environmental impact — to finalize the selection.

**Log-Log Chart Application (3.5 marks):**
On a **Young's modulus ($E$) vs. Density ($\rho$)** chart plotted on **log-log axes**:
- Materials appear as **"bubbles"** occupying distinct regions.
- **Metals** cluster in the mid-range (steel at $E \approx 200$ GPa, $\rho \approx 7.85$ g/cm³; aluminum at $E \approx 70$ GPa, $\rho \approx 2.7$ g/cm³).
- **Ceramics** and **CFRP composites** lie in the upper-left region (high $E$, low $\rho$) — ideal for stiff lightweight design.
- **Polymers** and **elastomers** occupy the lower-right region (low $E$, low–medium $\rho$).

A **selection line** of slope = 1 (for minimum-mass, minimum-deflection beam) is drawn through the chart. Materials lying **above and to the left** of the line satisfy the index. **CFRP, carbon fiber, and SiC ceramics** are typically the winners, with **Ti alloys** as a strong metallic alternative.

> [!NOTE] The slope is obtained by writing the design equation: deflection $\delta = k \cdot \frac{\rho^{a}}{E^{b}} \cdot L^{c}$, and minimizing mass for a given $\delta$ yields the slope of the selection line.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

| Application | Selected Material | Justification (3 Factors) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Gas Turbine Blade** | **Inconel 718 (Ni-Cr-Fe superalloy)** or single-crystal Ni-superalloys | 1. **High temperature strength** — retains strength above 900 °C due to $\gamma'$ (Ni₃Al) precipitation. 2. **Oxidation/corrosion resistance** — Cr and Al form stable oxide layers. 3. **Creep resistance** — solid-solution and precipitation hardening resist time-dependent deformation. |
| **(ii) Submarine Hull** | **HY-80 / HY-100 high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steel** for conventional submarines; **Ti-6Al-4V** for deep-diving research subs | 1. **High yield strength (≥ 550 MPa for HY-80)** — resists hydrostatic collapse at depth. 2. **Toughness at low temperature** — resists brittle fracture in cold deep water. 3. **Weldability** — required for fabricating large hull sections. |
| **(iii) Cutting Tool for Steel** | **WC-Co cemented carbide** or **Al₂O₃+TiC ceramic** or **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** | 1. **High hardness (HV 1500–2000 for WC-Co)** — resists abrasive wear at the cutting edge. 2. **High hot hardness** — maintains edge integrity at 600–800 °C cutting temperatures. 3. **Chemical inertness** — minimizes diffusion wear against steel workpiece. |
| **(iv) Flexible Cable Insulation** | **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)** for general purpose; **XLPE (Cross-linked polyethylene)** for high voltage; **PTFE** for high-temperature | 1. **High electrical resistivity ($\rho_e \approx 10^{14}$ Ω·m)** — provides insulation. 2. **Flexibility and toughness** — allows repeated bending without cracking. 3. **Flame retardancy (especially PVC)** — meets safety standards for building and appliance wiring. |

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert
> - **(i) Turbine blade:** Students often write "stainless steel" — this is **wrong**. Plain stainless steel loses strength above 600 °C; only Ni-superalloys or ceramics are suitable. **Marks deducted: 1–2.**
> - **(ii) Submarine hull:** Writing "cast iron" is a critical error — cast iron is **brittle** and cannot survive underwater shock loading. Must specify **tough HSLA steel**.
> - **(iii) Cutting tool:** Writing "high-speed steel (HSS)" is acceptable for low-speed tools but **not** for modern high-speed steel machining. Examiner may deduct a mark for not mentioning cemented carbide or ceramic.
> - **(iv) Cable insulation:** Writing "rubber" without specifying the type loses a mark. Always name the specific polymer grade (PVC, XLPE, EPR, PTFE).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4
> - **Definition:** Material selection is a multi-criteria decision process; no single property dominates.
> - **Three Pillars:** **Performance + Processability + Economy** — always structure your answer around these.
> - **Eight Factors:** (1) Mechanical, (2) Physical, (3) Chemical, (4) Manufacturing, (5) Economic, (6) Service Environment, (7) Aesthetic, (8) Safety/Sustainability.
> - **Key Quantitative Metrics:** Specific strength = $\sigma_{UTS}/\rho$; Specific stiffness = $E/\rho$; Cost index = cost/$\sigma_y$.
> - **Ashby Method:** Translate → Screen → Rank (using material index) → Support with extra criteria.
> - **Weighted Property Method:** Always normalize values; ensure $\sum w_i = 1.0$; higher $\gamma$ wins.
> - **Log-log charts** with selection line of slope derived from the design equation is the **Ashby hallmark**.
> - **Aerospace →** Ti, Al, CFRP (high specific strength). **Marine →** SS, Ti, FRP (corrosion). **Turbine →** Ni-superalloys, ceramics (heat). **Cutting →** WC-Co, ceramics, CBN (hardness).
> - **Common Errors to Avoid:**
>   * Picking a material on cost alone (always justify with multiple factors).
>   * Forgetting to invert "lower is better" properties in normalization.
>   * Listing factors without giving engineering examples.
>   * Confusing corrosion resistance with oxidation resistance.
> - **High-Yield Examples for 14-mark answers:**
>   * Aircraft wing spar → CFRP / Al 7075 / Ti-6Al-4V
>   * Marine propeller → Mn-bronze / AISI 316 / Ni-Al-bronze
>   * Gas turbine blade → Inconel 718 / CMSX-4 single crystal
>   * Artificial hip implant → Ti-6Al-4V / Co-Cr-Mo / PEEK
>   * Cookware → Al (conductivity) / SS 304 (corrosion + aesthetics)
>   * Spacecraft re-entry tile → SiC / C-C composite / HRSI tiles
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Suitable Materials for Construction in Chemical Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT412 - MATERIAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Suitable Materials for Construction in Chemical Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Selection (Chemical Industry Construction):** The systematic engineering process of identifying and specifying engineering materials — metals, alloys, polymers, ceramics, and composites — that can safely, economically, and reliably withstand the combined attack of **chemical corrosion**, **mechanical stress**, **thermal cycling**, and **pressure loading** encountered during the design service life of process equipment, piping, and storage vessels in a chemical plant.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based framework, material selection is treated as a **multi-objective design optimization problem** where the candidate material must simultaneously satisfy four constraint families:

1. **Chemical compatibility** (corrosion resistance to process fluid)
2. **Mechanical integrity** (strength, toughness, fatigue life)
3. **Thermal stability** (creep, thermal shock, oxidation)
4. **Fabricability & economics** (weldability, availability, cost per kg)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Suit of Armor" Principle**
> Imagine you are a knight going into battle. You would not wear a paper armor in a fire-breathing dragon's lair, nor a heavy iron suit while swimming across a river. The armor (material) must be chosen based on the **enemy** (corrosive chemical), the **environment** (temperature, pressure), and the **duration of the fight** (service life).
> In the chemical industry, choosing the *cheapest* material (mild steel) for a hydrochloric acid tank is like wearing a paper armor against a dragon — the equipment fails catastrophically. Choosing *expensive* titanium for a water pipeline is like wearing titanium armor to mow the lawn — economically wasteful. **Material selection is the art of finding the right armor for the right battle.**

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Weightage Module

In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 of PECHT412 (Material Science) directly maps to **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Select appropriate engineering materials for chemical process equipment based on service conditions."* 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics Used in Industry:**
> - **Corrosion Rate (CR):** measured in **mm/year (mpy)** — industry-accepted safe limit is **< 0.1 mm/year**
> - **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN):** dimensionless, must be **> 40** for chloride service
> - **Design Life:** typically **15–30 years** for chemical plant equipment
> - **Temperature Range:** cryogenic (−196 °C) to high-temp (1100 °C) crackers

## 1.4 Classification of Materials Used in Chemical Industry

| Class | Examples | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon & Low-Alloy Steels | SA-516, SA-387, A106 | Pressure vessels, pipelines |
| Stainless Steels | SS 304, SS 316, SS 904L, Duplex 2205 | Reactors, heat exchanger shells |
| Nickel Alloys | Monel 400, Inconel 625, Hastelloy C-276 | Concentrated acid service, HF |
| Reactive Metals | Titanium Gr.2, Zirconium, Tantalum | Wet HCl, HNO₃, H₂SO₄ |
| Non-Metals (Polymers) | PTFE, HDPE, PVDF, FRP | Lined pipes, tanks, gaskets |
| Ceramics & Glass | Glass-lined steel, Acid-proof bricks | H₂SO₄ concentrators |
| Composites | FRP, Carbon-Carbon, GFRP | Aggressive halide service |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Pillar Material Selection Framework

A KTU examiner expects students to know that selection is **never based on a single parameter**. The decision follows a structured funnel:

### Pillar 1 — Corrosion Resistance Analysis
- Identify the **corrodent** (acid, alkali, salt, oxidizer, organic).
- Determine the **corrosion form**: uniform, pitting, crevice, SCC, galvanic, erosion-corrosion.
- Refer to **iso-corrosion diagrams** (e.g., sulfuric acid concentration vs. temperature for SS 316).
- **Acceptance Rule:** Corrosion rate must be **< 0.5 mm/year** for the design life.

### Pillar 2 — Mechanical Property Envelope
- Verify the material's **yield strength (σ_y)** and **ultimate tensile strength (σ_u)** at the **design temperature** (not room temperature).
- For pressure vessels, use **ASME Sec. VIII** allowable stress values.
- Check **impact toughness** (Charpy V-notch) — must be **> 27 J** at minimum design metal temperature (MDMT) to avoid brittle fracture.

### Pillar 3 — Thermal & Pressure Stability
- **Creep regime:** Above **0.4 T_m** (melting temperature in Kelvin), long-term rupture strength governs. For example, SS 304 cannot be used above **800 °C** in continuous service.
- **Thermal expansion mismatch** between shell and lining must be considered for glass-lined reactors.

### Pillar 4 — Fabrication & Joining
- **Weldability** is critical. High-carbon steels (> 0.25% C) need preheat; austenitic SS is prone to **sensitization** in the 450–850 °C range.
- **Formability**, **machinability**, and availability in standard plate/pipe forms.

### Pillar 5 — Economics & Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)
$$\text{LCC} = C_{\text{capital}} + C_{\text{installation}} + C_{\text{maintenance}} + C_{\text{downtime}} + C_{\text{replacement}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Rule of Thumb:** If a slightly more expensive alloy extends service life from 5 to 20 years, the LCC is almost always lower.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion Rate (CR) | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | mm/year | Mass-loss coupon test |
| 2 | Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | dimensionless | Chloride pitting resistance |
| 3 | Design Wall Thickness (Cylinder) | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot E - 0.6 P} + c$ | mm | ASME Sec. VIII |
| 4 | Creep Rupture (Larson-Miller) | $LMP = T \left[ C + \log(t_r) \right]$ | °R·h | High-temp design |
| 5 | Thermal Stress (Cladding Mismatch) | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$ | MPa | Lined equipment |
| 6 | Galvanic Series Potential | $\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | V | Bimetallic corrosion |
| 7 | Stress Corrosion Cracking (K_ISCC) | $K_I \leq K_{ISCC}$ | MPa·√m | SCC design |
| 8 | Design Stress | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ (n = 1.5 to 2.0) | MPa | Safety factor design |

> **Where in the table above:** $W$ = mass loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (cm²), $T$ = time (h), $P$ = pressure, $R$ = radius, $E$ = joint efficiency, $c$ = corrosion allowance, $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coefficient, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, the **"material selection chart"** developed by **Mike Ashby** is widely used. It plots **Strength (MPa)** vs. **Density (g/cm³)** or **Strength vs. Modulus** with property envelopes. For chemical industry, Ashby charts are extended to include **corrosion resistance** as a third axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always mention **two numerical constraints + one economic reason** in any material selection answer. A single-property answer (e.g., "SS 316 because it resists chloride") fetches only partial marks; stating "SS 316 over SS 304 because PREN = 25 vs. 18, and both are cheaper than Inconel 625 for ambient-temperature seawater service" fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Mass-Loss Test (ASTM G1)

A coupon of structural steel (density 7.85 g/cm³) weighing initially 25.600 g is exposed to a process stream for 720 hours. After cleaning, the final mass is 24.850 g. The exposed surface area is 28.0 cm². Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/year.

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
- $W = m_i - m_f = 25.600 - 24.850 = 0.750 \text{ g} = 750 \text{ mg}$
- $D = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $A = 28.0 \text{ cm}^2$
- $T = 720 \text{ h}$
- Constant $K = 87.6$ (for mm/year with these units)

**Step 2 — Apply the standard corrosion rate formula:**

$$\text{CR (mm/year)} = \frac{K \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 750}{7.85 \times 28.0 \times 720}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$7.85 \times 28.0 \times 720 = 7.85 \times 20160 = 158{,}256$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$87.6 \times 750 = 65{,}700$$

**Step 6 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{65{,}700}{158{,}256} = 0.415 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> Since 0.415 mm/year **> 0.1 mm/year**, this carbon steel is **NOT acceptable** for long-term service. Options: (a) upgrade to a stainless steel, (b) add corrosion allowance of 4 mm for a 10-year life, or (c) apply a coating/lining. The third option is usually the most economical for large vessels.

## 3.2 Derivation: PREN Calculation and Material Ranking

A new candidate alloy contains: 22.0% Cr, 5.0% Mo, 0.18% N. Compare its chloride pitting resistance with SS 316 (17% Cr, 2.5% Mo, 0.05% N) and Super Duplex 2507 (25% Cr, 4.0% Mo, 0.30% N).

**Step 1 — Recall the PREN formula:**

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate for the new alloy:**

$$\text{PREN}_{new} = 22.0 + 3.3 \times 5.0 + 16 \times 0.18$$

$$= 22.0 + 16.5 + 2.88 = 41.38$$

**Step 3 — Calculate for SS 316:**

$$\text{PREN}_{316} = 17 + 3.3 \times 2.5 + 16 \times 0.05 = 17 + 8.25 + 0.80 = 26.05$$

**Step 4 — Calculate for Super Duplex 2507:**

$$\text{PREN}_{2507} = 25 + 3.3 \times 4.0 + 16 \times 0.30 = 25 + 13.2 + 4.80 = 43.00$$

**Step 5 — Tabulate and rank:**

| Material | PREN | Suitability for Seawater |
|---|---|---|
| SS 316 | 26.05 | Marginal, SCC risk |
| New Alloy (22-5) | 41.38 | Excellent |
| Super Duplex 2507 | 43.00 | Excellent (premium) |

**Step 6 — Decision logic:** Both the new alloy and Super Duplex pass the **PREN > 40** rule. Cost comparison now becomes decisive.

## 3.3 Worked Problem — Selecting Material for a Sulfuric Acid Storage Tank

**Service Conditions:**
- Fluid: 98% H₂SO₄ (concentrated)
- Temperature: 60 °C
- Pressure: Atmospheric
- Volume: 50 m³

**Step 1 — Consult iso-corrosion data:** Carbon steel is suitable for **> 65% H₂SO₄** at **< 80 °C**. Reference: standard isocorrosion charts.

**Step 2 — Check mechanical need:** Atmospheric pressure → thin-walled tank → carbon steel SA-516 Gr.70 is acceptable.

**Step 3 — Check water dilution risk:** A leak that dilutes acid below 65% concentration will cause severe attack on carbon steel. 

**Step 4 — Verify with formula — Corrosion allowance:**

$$c = \text{CR} \times \text{Design Life} = 0.5 \text{ mm/year} \times 20 \text{ years} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Final selection:** Carbon steel SA-516 Gr.70 with **10 mm corrosion allowance**, internally inspected every 2 years, and an emergency dilution sensor. **LCC is lowest among all options.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Selection Funnel (Decision Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Service Conditions] --> B[Identify Corrodent and Concentration]
    B --> C{Corrosion Rate < 0.5 mm/year?}
    C -- No --> D[Consider Lined CS or Upgrade Alloy]
    C -- Yes --> E[Check Mechanical Properties at Design Temp]
    E --> F{Creep Regime?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use High Temp Alloy: Inconel / SS 321]
    F -- No --> H[Check Fabrication and Weldability]
    H --> I{Weldable Without Cracking?}
    I -- No --> J[Use LCC or Low-Carbon Grade]
    I -- Yes --> K[Compute Life Cycle Cost LCC]
    K --> L{LCC within 15 percent of Cheapest?}
    L -- No --> M[Reconsider or Add Lining]
    L -- Yes --> N[Final Material Selected]
    D --> E
    G --> K
    J --> H
    M --> K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Material Selection for a Chemical Reactor

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Process Fluid Chemistry]
        I2[Operating Temperature]
        I3[Operating Pressure]
        I4[Service Life Required]
    end

    subgraph SelectionEngine
        S1[Corrosion Database Lookup]
        S2[Mechanical Property Module]
        S3[Thermal Stability Module]
        S4[Fabrication Compatibility Module]
        S5[LCC Calculator]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Base Metal Specification]
        O2[Corrosion Allowance Value]
        O3[Welding Procedure Requirement]
        O4[Inspection and Maintenance Plan]
    end

    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S2
    I2 --> S3
    I3 --> S2
    I3 --> S3
    I4 --> S5
    S1 --> O1
    S2 --> O1
    S3 --> O1
    S4 --> O3
    S5 --> O2
    S5 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Application Mapping: Material-to-Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reactor Vessel] -->|SS 316L| B[Heat Exchanger Tubes]
    B -->|Titanium Gr.2| C[Cooling Water Side]
    A -->|Glass-Lined CS| D[Concentrated HCl]
    E[Agitator Shaft] -->|Hastelloy C-276| F[Wet Chlorine]
    G[Storage Tank] -->|Carbon Steel + Lining| H[Caustic Soda]
    I[Piping] -->|FRP / GRE| J[Brine Service]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any four factors that govern the selection of materials for chemical process equipment.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Chemical compatibility** with the process fluid (corrosion rate, pitting, SCC).
2. **Mechanical properties** at operating temperature and pressure (yield strength, toughness).
3. **Thermal stability** including creep resistance and thermal expansion behavior.
4. **Fabrication characteristics** such as weldability, formability, and availability.
5. **Economics** — total life-cycle cost (LCC) including capital, maintenance, and downtime.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define PREN. What is the minimum PREN recommended for seawater service?**

**Model Answer:**
**Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)** is an empirical ranking index used to compare the chloride pitting resistance of stainless steels.

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

For seawater service, a **PREN ≥ 40** is recommended, typically requiring super-duplex or super-austenitic grades.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the systematic procedure for selecting a material for the shell of a reactor handling 70% H₂SO₄ at 120 °C and 5 bar pressure. Discuss the role of carbon steel, SS 316, and Hastelloy C-276 with reference to iso-corrosion diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-mm-thick carbon steel plate (density 7.85 g/cm³) loses 0.420 g in a 30-day corrosion test with an exposed area of 20 cm². Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/year. Is the material acceptable for a 15-year design life with a 6 mm corrosion allowance? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Define service envelope:** 70% H₂SO₄, 120 °C, 5 bar.

**Step 2 — Consult iso-corrosion diagram for carbon steel:** At 70% concentration and 120 °C, carbon steel shows CR ≈ **1.0–1.5 mm/year** — exceeds the **0.5 mm/year** limit.

**Step 3 — Consult iso-corrosion diagram for SS 316:** At 70% H₂SO₄ and 120 °C, SS 316 shows CR ≈ **0.3–0.8 mm/year** — borderline, not preferred.

**Step 4 — Hastelloy C-276 behaviour:** Excellent resistance (< 0.05 mm/year) over the entire H₂SO₄ concentration range up to the boiling point. **Most suitable but expensive.**

**Step 5 — Decision:** For batch / non-critical service, use **carbon steel with a 15–20 mm corrosion allowance** and periodic UT thickness monitoring. For continuous critical service, use **Hastelloy C-276** clad on a carbon-steel base.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- $W = 0.420 \text{ g} = 420 \text{ mg}$
- $D = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $A = 20 \text{ cm}^2$
- $T = 30 \times 24 = 720 \text{ h}$
- $K = 87.6$

**Step 1 — Apply formula:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 420}{7.85 \times 20 \times 720}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$7.85 \times 20 \times 720 = 113{,}040$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$87.6 \times 420 = 36{,}792$$

**Step 4 — Compute CR:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{36{,}792}{113{,}040} = 0.325 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Step 5 — Material consumed in 15 years:**

$$\text{Loss} = 0.325 \times 15 = 4.88 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Verdict:** Since **4.88 mm < 6 mm corrosion allowance**, the material **is acceptable** for the 15-year design life, with a residual safety margin of 1.12 mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare carbon steel, austenitic SS 304, and titanium Grade 2 for service in hot concentrated caustic (NaOH) solution. Recommend the most suitable with justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch (or functional diagram), describe the function of a **corrosion allowance** in pressure-vessel design. A vessel is designed for a 25-year life with CR = 0.25 mm/year. If the base metal thickness from pressure design is 18 mm, compute the total ordered plate thickness. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

| Material | Behaviour in Hot NaOH | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon Steel | Stress corrosion cracking (caustic embrittlement) above 50 °C | Not safe for hot service |
| SS 304 | Resistant up to ~100 °C; suffers SCC in concentrated NaOH > 150 °C | Limited use |
| Titanium Gr.2 | Excellent resistance to hot NaOH up to boiling point; immune to SCC | **Recommended** |

**Decision:** For hot concentrated NaOH at > 150 °C, **Titanium Gr.2** is the most suitable material, justified by zero SCC susceptibility, low corrosion rate (< 0.01 mm/year), and reasonable LCC.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Function of corrosion allowance:** It is the **extra thickness** added to the pressure-derived thickness to compensate for uniform metal loss during service, ensuring the vessel retains its structural integrity throughout its design life.

**Step 2 — Given:** Design life = 25 years, CR = 0.25 mm/year, pressure thickness = 18 mm.

**Step 3 — Corrosion allowance calculation:**

$$c = 0.25 \times 25 = 6.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total plate thickness:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = 18 + 6.25 = 24.25 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{order } 26 \text{ mm (next standard size)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Material Selection Answers:**
> 1. **Naming the material without numerical justification** — Always quote CR (mm/year), PREN, or allowable stress values.
> 2. **Ignoring temperature derating** — A material selected for room-temperature strength often fails at 600 °C. Always re-check at design temperature.
> 3. **Forgetting weldability and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** — Many SCC failures originate from welds because sensitization was overlooked.
> 4. **Single-criterion answer** — A 14-mark question expects discussion of **chemistry + mechanics + fabrication + economics** in a structured way.
> 5. **Using outdated materials** — Refer to current ASME/ASTM designations (e.g., SA-516 Gr.70, not "mild steel").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Material selection is multi-objective:** never justify on a single property.
- [ ] Always compute **corrosion rate (mm/year)** from mass-loss data using $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 W}{DAT}$.
- [ ] **PREN > 40** is the safe threshold for chloride/seawater service.
- [ ] Carbon steel is **NOT** safe for HCl, dilute H₂SO₄ (< 65%), HF, and strong alkalis at high T.
- [ ] **Austenitic SS (304/316)** suffers SCC in chloride + tensile stress environments.
- [ ] **Duplex SS** offers higher strength, better SCC resistance, and lower Ni cost.
- [ ] **Hastelloy, Inconel, Monel** are reserved for hot, concentrated, or mixed-acid service.
- [ ] **Titanium** is the metallurgist's choice for wet HCl, chlorinated organics, and oxidizing acid service.
- [ ] **Non-metals (PTFE, FRP, glass-lined steel)** are cost-effective alternates for highly aggressive fluids.
- [ ] Always add a **corrosion allowance** ($c = \text{CR} \times \text{Design Life}$) on top of pressure-derived thickness.
- [ ] **LCC (Life-Cycle Cost)** is the industry's true selection metric — not the per-kg purchase price.
- [ ] For high-temperature service above **0.4 T_m**, use the **Larson-Miller Parameter** for creep design.
- [ ] Refer to **ASME Sec. VIII / Sec. II** for allowable stress values at design temperature.
- [ ] Always perform a **galvanic compatibility check** when two different metals are coupled.
- [ ] Remember the **Mnemonic — "CATCH-4":** **C**hemistry, **A**llowable stress, **T**emperature, **C**ost, **H** (Hall of weldability).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
